Salomon Rothschild in Vienna became Metternich's standing financial counsellor; their correspondence is in the Austrian state archives, independent of the family's own records, and shows the relationship was political as well as financial. Salomon was advising the architect of post-Napoleonic Europe on the financial implications of his political decisions, and the architect was taking the advice. James in Paris became so embedded in French politics that the July Monarchy of Louis-Philippe was called, semi-seriously by contemporaries, *la monarchie Rothschild*. The Rothschild courier network functioned as the de facto European intelligence service for any government willing to pay for access.

The transaction that exposes the system most cleanly is the one the antisemitic literature has not been able to twist, because it does not need twisting. It is in Hansard.

On 3 August 1835, the British government finalised a loan of fifteen million pounds with a syndicate led by Nathan Mayer Rothschild and his brother-in-law Sir Moses Montefiore. The Chancellor of the Exchequer told the House of Commons that day that the money was needed to pay slave-owners under the Slavery Abolition Act of 1833. That Act had freed eight hundred thousand enslaved people across most of the British Empire, exempting India and several protectorates. To persuade the slave-owning class to permit the legislation, the British state had promised them twenty million pounds in compensation for their lost human property. Twenty million pounds was forty per cent of the British Treasury's annual revenue at the time and five per cent of British GDP. It was the largest single peacetime payment of public money in British history. Not one penny of it went to the freed people. The Act also required the freed people to spend several more years as "apprentices" — working without pay for their former owners — before being released into a labour market in which they owned nothing.

The Rothschild–Montefiore syndicate raised fifteen million in cash by issuing three new series of British government bonds, called the West India Loan in Hansard. The remaining five million was paid directly in government stock. The syndicate's commission was a twenty-five-year annuity of £101,875 per year. The Rothschilds sub-underwrote much of the loan to other investors. The bonds went into Britain's permanent national debt, were rolled over and consolidated across the next century and three quarters, and were finally redeemed by Her Majesty's Treasury on 1 February 2015 — one hundred and eighty years after the original loan. British taxpayers paid for the compensation of British slave-owners across nine generations. The last instalment was paid in 2015 by people who could not have known they were paying it, because the British state never announced what the debt service was for. The University College London project *Legacies of British Slave-ownership*, working from the Compensation Commission records, has mapped the more than forty thousand individual British slave-owners who received the payouts. They estimate that ten to twenty per cent of the wealthy of present-day Britain can be traced to direct or indirect descent from these compensation payments. The fortunes of dozens of major British families — politicians, peers, members of cabinet across generations, names in the *Times* this week — were built on the proceeds of that loan and on the slave labour the loan retrospectively monetised. The Rothschilds underwrote it. The Bank of England cleared the payments. The British state passed the cost to its people. The freed slaves and their descendants, in Britain and the Caribbean, received nothing.

This is what the Rothschild machine did. The same architecture handled France's indemnity to Germany after the Franco-Prussian War in 1871, allowing the French state to rebuild on Rothschild credit while Bismarck consolidated the unified German Empire on the proceeds. The same architecture financed Disraeli's seizure of a controlling British stake in the Suez Canal Company in 1875 — a Rothschild loan organised across a single weekend that bypassed Parliament, gave Britain effective control of the route to India, and made the Khedive of Egypt a debtor to the British state for the rest of his reign. The same architecture financed the Brazilian Empire, the Egyptian state, the Vatican, the Mexican Empire, half a dozen Italian republics, the Belgian state, and the Portuguese Crown. By the 1830s the family held effective veto power over any major sovereign borrowing in Europe. By the 1870s they held effective veto power over any major sovereign borrowing on earth.

This is conspiracy in the original sense of the word. *Con-spirare*, to breathe together. A coordinated multi-generational partnership of five brothers and their descendants, sharing intelligence in real time across five capitals, allocating their pooled capital collectively to decide which sovereigns to fund and which to deny, exercising structural influence over the political and military decisions those sovereigns could afford to make. The authorised histories, written with the family's cooperation, frame this coordination as commercial prudence. The antisemitic literature inflates the same coordination into mystical control. Both framings serve specific interests. What is in the independent record — the state papers of the governments the family dealt with, the parliamentary records of the loans they underwrote, the Foreign Office correspondence soliciting their intelligence — is that the Rothschilds exercised the kind of structural power over sovereign finance that no banking house had held before them, that they maintained it deliberately through the five-city partnership, and that they used it to maximise the returns of the partnership. The British state paid them to underwrite the compensation of slave-owners. The Austrian state paid them to underwrite the post-Napoleonic restoration. The French state paid them to underwrite a war indemnity that funded German unification. The Egyptian state paid them to underwrite the Suez transaction that ended Egyptian sovereignty over the canal. In each case, the public paid the interest, and the family received the commission.

The family also gave money on a vast scale to Jewish charity, financed hospitals and schools, lobbied for Jewish emancipation in country after country, and used their political access to oppose the antisemitic legislation of the nineteenth century where they could. These facts are true and they are not a defence. Andrew Carnegie also built libraries. John D. Rockefeller also funded medical research. The Sacklers also endowed museums. The function of the philanthropy in each case is the same — to launder the source of the capital into the public good, to convert money extracted from a system into reputation deposited in another. Philanthropy is not the test of a banking house. Conduct in the bond market is. Conduct in the loan syndicate is. Conduct toward the public whose taxes service the debt is. By those tests the Rothschilds extended the design invented in Amsterdam and London into something larger than its inventors had imagined — a permanent multi-national private intelligence and capital network that could outrun any sovereign, fund any sovereign, refuse any sovereign, and survive any sovereign's collapse. Every subsequent private bank with global ambitions has copied the model: pooled capital across borders, private intelligence superior to governments, no transaction refused on grounds other than profit, the public left holding the debt. The Rothschilds are the bridge between the Bank of England's design and the Goldman Sachs of the present. The line runs directly through their five-city partnership.

By 1850 the centre of gravity had begun to shift. The Rothschilds had financed European governments. The next century would not be about European governments. It would be about something the European powers themselves had created on the far side of the Atlantic, and could no longer control. The new mask was being put on in a country that had fought a war of independence against the Bank of England, that had spent its first half-century arguing with itself about whether to have a central bank at all, and that would settle the argument in 1913 by importing the Bank of England's design wholesale and dressing it up as something new.

The Americans called it the Federal Reserve. They were not the first to think of it. They were the last.


---

# Chapter 3: The Conspirators Named

The previous two chapters have described, in broad strokes, the building of the modern financial state — the Dutch East India Company in 1602, the Bank of Amsterdam in 1609, the Glorious Revolution of 1688, the Bank of England in 1694, the Rothschild network through the nineteenth century. What those chapters did not do, and what this one will do, is name the people in the rooms.

The reason for the pause is methodological. The official histories tend to describe these institutions as if they assembled themselves — "the VOC was founded," "the Bank of England was established," "the Financial Revolution occurred." The grammatical voice is passive because the active voice would require naming the men who did it. The men who did it had names. The men who did it had specific business interests, specific political offices, specific personal investments in the institutions they were chartering. In every case, the institutions were chartered by the men who would profit from them, often by the same men, in the same rooms, in the same week. This is not a structural accident. It is the original deliberate design, executed by named conspirators on the public record. The previous chapters described what they built. The record describes who they were.

## I. Amsterdam, 1602–1609

The Dutch East India Company was not "founded by the States General of the Netherlands." It was forced into existence by one man, against the wishes of most of the pre-existing trading companies who would be merged into it. His name was Johan van Oldenbarnevelt. He was born in 1547 in Amersfoort, studied law at Leuven, Bourges, Heidelberg, and Padua, fought as a volunteer in the relief of Haarlem and Leiden during the early stages of the Eighty Years' War against Spain, and in 1586 became Land's Advocate of Holland — the senior political office of the largest province in the Dutch Republic. The Land's Advocate held no executive power on paper. The Republic was deliberately constructed without a central executive. In practice, for thirty-two years, Van Oldenbarnevelt was the prime minister of the Dutch Republic, because the Republic could not function without someone who was actually willing to make decisions, and he was.

He decided to merge the six existing pre-companies — small joint-stock ventures funding voyages to the East Indies — into a single corporate body chartered by the state, capitalised with a permanent base of public subscription, granted a monopoly on Asian trade and the sovereign powers of war, treaty, coinage, and execution. He did this in negotiation with Prince Maurice of Nassau, the stadtholder, who controlled the army; with the merchants of Amsterdam, who held the largest pre-company stakes and would dominate the new company's board; and with the States General, which he controlled. The charter was granted on 20 March 1602. The IPO subscription opened in August. Eleven hundred and forty-three Dutch citizens put in money. Six hundred and forty thousand pounds sterling-equivalent of capital was raised. The world's first joint-stock corporation, with the world's first publicly tradeable shares, began trading on the world's first stock exchange the same year.

Van Oldenbarnevelt did the same thing again seven years later. In 1609, with the Twelve Years' Truce with Spain just signed, he supported the city of Amsterdam in founding the Wisselbank — the world's first public deposit bank, run by commissioners drawn from the city's burgomasters and merchant council, housed in the basement of the city hall under the offices of the men who governed the city. By 1620 it had over twelve hundred account holders and the VOC had its primary clearing account there. Van Oldenbarnevelt had now built, inside two decades, both halves of the architecture that would define modern capital — the joint-stock corporation and the public deposit bank — and had handed both to the same circle of Amsterdam merchant families who held the city government, the company's board, and the bank's commissions in interlocking memberships.

The Republic killed him for it. The pretext was theological — a religious dispute between Arminians and Counter-Remonstrants — but the underlying conflict was political. Van Oldenbarnevelt had built an oligarchic state in which the merchant-class regents of Holland held effective power, and Prince Maurice had decided he wanted that power back. On the night of 29 August 1618 Maurice had Van Oldenbarnevelt arrested. He was tried by a packed tribunal. On 13 May 1619, at the age of seventy-one, he was beheaded in the Binnenhof in The Hague. His final reported words to the executioner: "Make it short, make it short." The man who had built the corporate-financial state was killed by the prince he had armed.

What remained after he died, however, was the architecture. The VOC kept running. The Wisselbank kept running. The Amsterdam Stock Exchange kept running. The merchant oligarchy — the regent class, the same hundred families across generations who held the city councils, the bank's commissions, the VOC's board, and the States General's votes — kept running them. By the 1620s the merchant elite of Amsterdam, sitting in overlapping positions on the bank and the company and the city, were the wealthiest concentrated power in Europe outside Spain. The architecture had outlived its architect. That was the architecture's whole point.

## II. London, 1688

Eighty years later, a small group of English nobles invited a Dutch prince to invade their country.

The document is in the National Archives at Kew, catalogued as State Papers 8/1/224. It is dated 30 June 1688. It is written in code, with numbered ciphers replacing the names of the signatories. The signatories, decoded, are:

- **Charles Talbot, 12th Earl of Shrewsbury**, who hosted the meeting at his London residence on the night the letter was signed.
- **William Cavendish, 4th Earl of Devonshire**, later 1st Duke of Devonshire, owner of Chatsworth.
- **Thomas Osborne, 1st Earl of Danby**, former Lord High Treasurer under Charles II, later 1st Duke of Leeds.
- **Richard Lumley, 2nd Viscount Lumley**, later 1st Earl of Scarbrough.
- **Henry Compton, Bishop of London**, the only clergyman in the group.
- **Edward Russell**, naval officer, cousin of William the Conqueror's namesake nobles, later 1st Earl of Orford and Admiral of the Fleet.
- **Henry Sidney**, William of Orange's principal English agent, who drafted the letter and was running an intelligence network on William's behalf in London. Later 1st Earl of Romney.

Six peers and a bishop. They are remembered in English historiography as the "Immortal Seven." A more accurate term is conspirators. The act they committed was, in the precise legal meaning of the words of the Treason Act 1351, *compassing the death of the King* — they wrote to a foreign head of state asking him to invade England with a foreign army, depose the reigning monarch, and place the monarch's daughter and her foreign husband on the throne. The signatures were in code because discovery would have meant the scaffold. They were not discovered, the invasion succeeded, and within twelve months James II was in exile in France and William and Mary were jointly crowned at Westminster Abbey under terms set by Parliament. The conspirators were rewarded with peerages, offices, and forfeited Catholic estates. None of them were ever tried.

The point is not that they were wrong to depose James II. James II had real defects of judgement and the Catholic absolutism the Stuarts were drifting into would have produced its own catastrophes. The point is the precise mechanism by which the deposition was carried out. Six nobles and a bishop, meeting in secret in a London townhouse, decided to bring in a foreign army to remove a king. They sent their decision to the foreign prince by courier. The foreign prince came with fifteen thousand men. The country accepted the result because the alternative was civil war. The political settlement that emerged — the Bill of Rights 1689, the supremacy of Parliament, the Protestant succession — is celebrated as the foundation of British constitutional liberty. It was, in its inception, a conspiracy by named men against the standing monarch, executed by foreign military force, ratified after the fact by the institutions the conspirators then controlled. The Glorious Revolution was the original successful coup d'état against a legitimate European sovereign in the modern era. The official histories soften this by calling it bloodless, glorious, and necessary. The first two are mostly true. The third is what the conspirators wanted the country to believe afterwards.

## III. The Whig Junto

What the Glorious Revolution actually delivered, beyond a new king, was the political faction that would build the British financial state. It was called the Whig Junto. It was made up of four men, sometimes five.

**John Somers, Baron Somers** (1651–1716): Lord Keeper of the Great Seal from 1693, Lord Chancellor from 1697. Co-drafter of the Bill of Rights 1689. The Junto's principal legal architect.

**Charles Montagu, 1st Earl of Halifax** (1661–1715): Lord of the Treasury from 1692, Chancellor of the Exchequer from 1694, First Lord of the Treasury from 1697. The Junto's financial architect. The man who put the Bank of England through Parliament.

**Edward Russell, 1st Earl of Orford** (1653–1727): same Edward Russell who had been one of the Immortal Seven. Now First Lord of the Admiralty, Treasurer of the Navy, in charge of the British fleet that was about to be paid for with the new financial instruments his colleagues were building. The same man who had invited William over was now running the navy that William's new state needed to fund.

**Thomas Wharton, 1st Marquess of Wharton** (1648–1715): the Junto's party manager, the operator who delivered votes in Parliament.

Sometimes **Robert Spencer, 2nd Earl of Sunderland** (1641–1702) is counted as the fifth — the master fixer who had served James II, switched sides during the Revolution, and engineered the Junto's installation in government.

These five men, between 1693 and 1700, ran the British state. They had a portrait commissioned of themselves in 1710 by John James Baker — the only known group portrait of the Junto — which now hangs at Tate Britain. They are shown gathered on a grand terrace, a Black servant pulling back a heavy velvet curtain to reveal the garden beyond. The painting was commissioned by Edward Russell. It is a self-image of an oligarchy comfortable with the fact that the country was now theirs to run.

What they did with that control, in financial terms, was the following. In 1693 they introduced the first English government bonds — long-term public debt secured against parliamentary tax pledges rather than against the personal credit of the monarch. In 1694 they chartered the Bank of England. In 1695 they pushed through the controversial recoinage scheme. In 1696 they founded the Board of Trade. In 1697 Montagu, as First Lord of the Treasury, introduced Exchequer Bills as a parallel form of short-term government debt. Across the same years they negotiated peace terms with France that institutionalised Britain's emerging credit advantage. They enriched themselves directly along the way. In 1699 and 1700 Somers, Russell, and Montagu were granted extensive Irish forfeited estates by Parliament — Irish lands confiscated from Catholic nobility loyal to James II, handed by Parliament to the men who had organised James's removal. The granting of forfeited estates to the conspirators became sufficiently controversial that Parliament passed a Resumption Act in 1700 to reverse some of the grants. The grants had already been received.

Charles Montagu, while Chancellor of the Exchequer, in May 1694, **personally subscribed two thousand pounds of his own money to the Bank of England's founding capital**, in the same month he was pushing the charter through Parliament. The man chartering the bank put his own money into the bank he was chartering. This is publicly documented in the History of Parliament Trust biography of Montagu. It is not contested. It would in any modern jurisdiction be a textbook conflict of interest punishable as fraud. In 1694 it was the foundation of the British financial state. The principle established was that the people who run the state are also the people who fund the state's borrowing, and that the state's borrowing is therefore arranged on terms favourable to the people who run the state. This principle has not been modified in the three hundred and thirty years since.

## IV. The Houblons

Below the Junto, in the founding share register of the Bank of England on 21 June 1694, are the names of the men who put in the actual money. The largest single subscriber was the Crown: William and Mary jointly invested ten thousand pounds, the maximum the charter permitted any single subscriber to hold. Several private individuals matched that maximum. The most important of them was Sir John Houblon.

Houblon was the third son of James Houblon, a London merchant whose own father had fled Lille in 1568 as a Huguenot refugee from the Spanish Catholic persecution of French Protestants in the Low Countries. The family had landed in London with a name, a religion, and the skill of long-distance trade, and had spent eighty years building one of the great City merchant houses, the Portugal Merchants, who handled English trade with Lisbon, Cadiz, and the Mediterranean. By the 1680s the Houblons were one of the wealthiest commercial families in London. They were also Whigs. They had supported William's invasion. They had backed the constitutional settlement. They were rewarded.

In October 1689, eleven months after William's arrival in London, John Houblon was knighted by the new king at the Guildhall mayoralty feast. By 1694 he was an Alderman, a Lord Commissioner of the Admiralty appointed by William, Master of the Grocers' Company, and an elder of the French Protestant Church in Threadneedle Street. On 21 June 1694, he subscribed ten thousand pounds to the Bank of England. On 27 July 1694, when the Bank's Royal Charter was sealed, he was elected the first Governor of the Bank of England. On 29 September 1695, while still serving as Governor of the Bank, he was elected Lord Mayor of London. He continued to serve as Lord Commissioner of the Admiralty throughout. The first Governor of the Bank of England was simultaneously responsible for the Royal Navy's accounts, the City of London's government, the principal Huguenot congregation's spiritual leadership, and one of the great livery companies. He was, in any meaningful sense, the principal civilian official of the British state in the same years that he was also the principal officer of the Bank that was lending the state its money.

His brother **James Houblon**, an MP for the City of London, was also a founding director of the Bank. His younger brother **Abraham Houblon** was a founding director, and would serve as Governor of the Bank himself from 1703 to 1705. The three Houblon brothers held the Bank's Governor's chair across two of its first three terms, and held directorships through most of its first thirty years. Their cousins held further directorships. Their commercial partners — **Sir Theodore Janssen**, **Sir James Bateman**, **John Lordell**, **Samuel Lethieullier**, **John Ward**, **James Denew** — held the rest of the founding directorate. The first court of directors of the Bank of England was, with very limited exceptions, a single extended family of Huguenot-descended City merchants who had backed the Glorious Revolution and now ran the bank that financed its consequences.

The Houblons did not stop at financing. Anne Houblon, Abraham's daughter, married Henry Temple in 1703. Their grandson, Henry Temple, became the 2nd Viscount Palmerston. His grandson, the 3rd Viscount Palmerston, became Prime Minister of the United Kingdom from 1855 to 1858 and again from 1859 to 1865 — the same man who ordered the Second Opium War against China, who supervised the British response to the Indian Rebellion of 1857, who ran the British state through the high imperial decades. The Houblons of the Bank of England produced, by direct descent through marriage, the imperial prime minister of the high British nineteenth century. The same family. The same money. The same political project, extended across five generations.

## V. The pattern, named

Step back from the names and look at the pattern.

In Amsterdam in 1602, the prime minister of the Dutch Republic forced the creation of the world's first joint-stock corporation, in collaboration with the stadtholder who controlled the army and the Amsterdam merchant oligarchy who controlled the city. The corporation got sovereign powers, a state-backed monopoly, and a public share offering that drew capital from across society. The merchant oligarchy got the directorships. Seventeen years later the prime minister was executed by the stadtholder, but the corporation kept running because the merchant oligarchy who had been installed in its directorships were now the actual rulers of the city, the bank, and the company simultaneously. Same families, overlapping seats.

In London in 1688, six peers and a bishop committed treason against the standing king by inviting a foreign army to depose him. The invasion succeeded. The conspirators became, within six years, the Whig Junto that ran the British state. In 1694 the Junto chartered the Bank of England — a private corporation that lent the state its money and was permitted to issue currency against the loan — and the Chancellor of the Exchequer who pushed the charter through Parliament put two thousand pounds of his own money into the bank he was chartering. The first Governor of the bank was simultaneously the Lord Mayor of London, an Admiralty Commissioner, and the senior officer of the Huguenot merchant community. His brothers were directors. His niece by marriage produced the line that produced a Prime Minister. The same families. Overlapping seats. The pattern from Amsterdam, transferred whole.

Across the nineteenth century the Rothschild family, as the previous chapter described, extended the same architecture across borders. Five brothers in five capitals, pooled capital, a private intelligence network superior to any government, decisions made jointly about which sovereigns to fund and which to deny. By the 1830s they held effective veto power over major sovereign borrowing in Europe. They were not the cause of the system. They were the heirs of the system. The system had been built in Amsterdam by Van Oldenbarnevelt and the merchant regents, transferred to London by William of Orange and the Immortal Seven and the Whig Junto, capitalised by the Houblons and their Huguenot circle, and was waiting for a family with the right combination of cousins in the right capitals to extract from it the rents the original architects had only dreamed of.

This is what is meant by *con-spirare*, breathing together. Not a single conspiracy with a single committee. A continuous architecture of named conspirators across four centuries, each generation installing instruments their predecessors had designed, each generation enriching themselves directly from the institutions they were chartering, each generation rewarded with peerages and forfeited estates and inheritances in return for the political work of getting the next instrument through. It is the longest-running corporate-state hybrid project in human history. The men in the rooms had names. They have always had names. The official histories soften this by writing in the passive voice. The plain language is that the financial state was built by named men acting in their own financial interest while holding the offices that were supposed to be regulating their conduct, and that the institutions they built were designed to preserve their successors' ability to do the same thing.

What the United States would do in the nineteenth century, and what the Federal Reserve would do in 1913, was not a departure from this pattern. It was its replication on a continental scale, by a new generation of named men, in new rooms, building the same machine. The Americans tried, harder than anyone before them, to break the pattern. They failed. The next chapter is about the men who made them fail.


---

# Chapter 3: The American Argument, 1790–1836

## I.

The United States was founded by men who had read everything. Some of them had read the same books and reached the same conclusions, and some of them had read the same books and reached opposite ones. The argument that defined the first half-century of American politics was an argument between two men who had read Adam Smith, who had read Locke and Montesquieu and Hume, who had served in the same revolution against the same king, who had sat together in the same cabinet under Washington — and who disagreed, and irreconcilably, about whether the new country should import the British financial system or refuse it. The argument was won, in the short term, by the man who wanted to import it. The argument was relitigated, repeatedly and bloodily, for the next hundred and twenty-two years, until the Federal Reserve Act of 1913 buried it under a different name.

Alexander Hamilton was thirty-three years old when George Washington appointed him the first Secretary of the Treasury of the United States in September 1789. He was a brilliant, ambitious, slightly disreputable West Indian immigrant of uncertain parentage who had read his way out of obscurity through the libraries of New York and the artillery of the Continental Army. He had spent the years of his political adolescence watching the American government under the Articles of Confederation fail to pay its soldiers, fail to service its war debts, fail to fund its operations, and nearly fail to exist. He had drawn from this experience an entirely consistent set of conclusions, which he laid out across three documents submitted to Congress between 1790 and 1791: the *Report on Public Credit*, the *Report on a National Bank*, and the *Report on Manufactures*. They are among the most important documents in American economic history. Their central argument is simple. The United States cannot be a serious nation without a permanent national debt, a private central bank, a federal taxing power adequate to service the debt, and a protected manufacturing base. The model for all four was Great Britain.

Hamilton was explicit. The *Report on a National Bank*, submitted in December 1790, used the Bank of England's 1694 charter as the working template. Read the documents side by side, as later economic historians have done, and the architectural lift is unmistakable. A privately owned joint-stock bank chartered by the state. The bank holds the government's deposits. The bank lends the government money. The bank issues banknotes that circulate as currency. The government pays interest. The shareholders draw a dividend. The instrument is identical. Hamilton called this the *American* national bank because it was on American soil. In every other respect it was the institution William Paterson had proposed to William of Orange ninety-six years earlier.

The bill was introduced in Congress in January 1791. The opposition was led, in the cabinet, by Thomas Jefferson, the Secretary of State; in the House, by James Madison, who had drafted much of the Constitution four years earlier. The opposition's arguments were of two kinds. The first was constitutional. Madison and Jefferson held that the Constitution did not grant Congress the power to charter a corporation, that the Tenth Amendment reserved unenumerated powers to the states or to the people, that the Necessary and Proper Clause did not extend to creating a private banking monopoly, and that Hamilton's interpretation of "necessary" — as meaning useful or conducive rather than strictly indispensable — would dissolve the limits on federal power altogether. Jefferson's *Opinion on the Constitutionality of the Bill for Establishing a National Bank*, dated 15 February 1791, made the case in writing for Washington. It was, and remains, the foundational text of strict constructionism in American constitutional theory.

The second kind of argument was political-economic, and it is the one this chapter is interested in. Jefferson believed — and stated, in correspondence and in policy memoranda across the next twenty-five years — that Hamilton's bank would do four things. It would concentrate wealth in the hands of a small class of speculators and creditors. It would tie the federal government's revenue stream to the maintenance of that class's interests, because the government would now owe them money continuously. It would shift the centre of gravity of the new republic from agrarian production to mercantile and financial extraction. And it would, over time, produce the same political class the British had: a moneyed oligarchy attached to an instrument of public debt, voting for the wars and the policies that enlarged the debt and enlarged its income. Jefferson had been the American minister in Paris through the 1780s; he had watched the *ancien régime* implode under the weight of debt it could not service. He had seen what the British system produced from the receiving end. He did not want it on this side of the Atlantic.

Hamilton's reply to Jefferson's constitutional argument was the doctrine of implied powers. The Constitution gave Congress the power to lay taxes, borrow money, regulate commerce, and provide for the common defence. A national bank was a means to those ends. Therefore the Necessary and Proper Clause authorised it. Washington — who had spent six painful weeks reading both opinions, asking Edmund Randolph's, asking Madison's, walking the new federal capital site on the Potomac — signed the bill on 25 February 1791. The Bank of the United States opened for business in Philadelphia on the 12th of December that year. Its capital was ten million dollars, two million of it owned by the federal government and eight million by private subscribers. The IPO was, at the time, the largest in American history. The institution that resulted was the largest corporation in the country and would remain so for the duration of its charter.

Within months, two things had happened that would define the politics of the next century. The first was that the federal government's revenue requirements — driven by the interest on the assumed state debts that Hamilton had bundled into the new federal debt — required new taxes. Hamilton's preferred instrument was an excise tax on domestically distilled spirits. The whiskey tax fell, almost exclusively, on small farmers in western Pennsylvania, Virginia, and the Carolinas, for whom distilling grain into whiskey was the only economically rational way to get their crop to market across the Appalachians. Armed resistance built across 1791, 1792, 1793. By the summer of 1794 it had reached open insurrection in western Pennsylvania. Washington personally led a federalised militia of thirteen thousand men across the mountains — the only time in American history that a sitting president has commanded troops in the field — and put the Whiskey Rebellion down without a meaningful battle. The new federal government had demonstrated that it would collect the taxes that serviced the debt that paid the Bank's shareholders, and that it would use military force against its own citizens to do so. That was the message. The farmers in the western counties had read the message correctly. The pattern would repeat.

The second thing was that the Bank's stock began to attract foreign capital almost immediately. The subscription books had been opened in July 1791. American capital was thin; British capital was deep, sterling-denominated, and looking for yield. By the early 1800s, British holders controlled a steadily growing share of the Bank's private stock; by the time the recharter debate opened in 1810, contemporary estimates put British ownership at two-thirds of the private capital — about five and a third million dollars out of the eight million in private hands. The Bank's charter forbade foreign shareholders from voting, but the dividends still went where the shares went, and the dividends were paid out of taxes on Americans. The Bank that Hamilton had built to make the United States independent of foreign finance had, within twenty years, become a vehicle through which American tax revenue was paid as a continuous yield to investors in the City of London. Jefferson, by this point retired to Monticello, did not need to write a pamphlet about it. The recharter debate in Congress wrote the pamphlet for him.

Hamilton himself did not live to see the recharter. He had been killed on 11 July 1804 in a duel with Aaron Burr at Weehawken, New Jersey, at the age of forty-seven. By 1810 his Federalist Party was a regional rump. The Democratic-Republicans — Jefferson's and Madison's coalition, the heirs of the original opposition — held both houses of Congress and the presidency. But the Bank had also accumulated, across twenty years, its own coalition: state banks that depended on its discounting, merchants who held its notes, federal officials who used it as the Treasury's fiscal agent, and the foreign holders who quietly lobbied the British government to pressure the American one on behalf of their dividends. The recharter fight became one of the closest legislative contests of the early republic.

The House voted on 24 January 1811. The motion to renew the charter was defeated, sixty-five to sixty-four, by a single vote. The Senate voted on 20 February. The result was a seventeen-seventeen tie. Vice-President George Clinton of New York — Jefferson's old Anti-Federalist ally, an enemy of Madison and an enemy of Hamilton's banking project from its founding — cast the tie-breaking vote against renewal. The Bank of the United States closed its doors on 3 March 1811, the day before its charter expired. The largest corporation in America ceased to exist. Stephen Girard, a French-born Philadelphia merchant who had become one of the wealthiest men in the country, bought the building, most of the stock, and the cashier's staff, and reopened the next year as Girard Bank — a private institution, no longer a national one. The bank's foreign shareholders, including most of the British holders, were paid out their stock at par in specie over the next eighteen months. The gold and silver flowed across the Atlantic.

The war came almost immediately. By June 1812 the United States was fighting Britain again, and almost at once it discovered what Jefferson had warned about from the opposite direction. Without a national bank, the federal government could not borrow at scale. The Treasury issued small denominations of paper Treasury notes that depreciated rapidly. State banks, freed from the discipline of the national bank, proliferated wildly — by 1816 there were over two hundred and forty of them, issuing their own notes at their own discount rates. Most suspended specie payments altogether after the British burned Washington in 1814. The currency of the United States was, by the end of the war, a chaos of competing private paper, much of it worthless. The federal government could neither pay its soldiers reliably nor service its debt. By 1815, the Republican party that had killed the First Bank had reversed itself. Madison — who had led the constitutional opposition to the First Bank in 1791 — signed the charter of the Second Bank of the United States in April 1816.

The Second Bank was the First Bank scaled up. Its capital was thirty-five million dollars. Its charter was again for twenty years. Its structure was identical in essentials to the institution Hamilton had designed: a private corporation with the federal government as a minority shareholder, holding government deposits, issuing currency, lending to the Treasury, serving as the fiscal agent of the United States. The arguments against it from 1791 were rehearsed again, by the same factions in different costumes. The arguments for it from 1791 prevailed, because the experience of 1812 to 1815 had demonstrated what the absence of the instrument produced. The lesson Madison drew, and the lesson the country drew with him, was a lesson the British had drawn in 1694 and the Dutch had drawn in 1609: once you are inside the system of permanent state debt and circulating bank paper, you cannot easily get out. The instruments produce the conditions in which the instruments become indispensable. To dismantle them is to invite the chaos that justifies rebuilding them.

This is the part of the American story that is consistently underplayed in the standard histories, which prefer to present the bank fight as a question of constitutional interpretation. It was not, primarily, a constitutional question. It was a question about which class of Americans would own the country's future. The constitutional argument was the form. The substance was: would the United States be a society of independent landholding producers, as Jefferson wanted, or a society organised around a financial centre, as Hamilton wanted. The Bank of the United States was the instrument that would decide the question, because whoever held the Bank held the credit system, and whoever held the credit system held the conditions under which farmers could borrow, merchants could expand, manufacturers could build, and the federal government could function. The question was about who.

Hamilton's answer, by 1791, had been built into law. Jefferson's answer would not return to power until Andrew Jackson, fourteen years later, decided to kill the Bank a second time and discovered, as Madison had discovered, what the absence of the instrument produced. The Jackson story comes next. But the underlying point of the period from 1791 to 1816 is that the British design had been transplanted to American soil, that it had been rejected once and re-imposed almost immediately, and that the political class which depended on its survival was now, in the second decade of the nineteenth century, a recognisable American oligarchy with names that recur across generations: Biddle, Astor, Girard, Brown, Baring (through their American agents), Willing, Hamilton (through his successors), Morris (through the wreckage of his bankruptcy). The men who had attended the Constitutional Convention had argued about whether the new country should have such a class. The men who attended the recharter debate of 1816 took it as a fact. The argument had moved from whether to how much.

The next thirty years would test how much.


---

# Chapter 4: America, and the Last Bank

The American war of independence was, at the level the colonists themselves understood, a war against the Bank of England. The taxes the colonists refused to pay — on tea, on stamped paper, on sugar, on tobacco — were taxes set in London by a Parliament financing a war debt that had been issued by the Bank of England to fight the Seven Years' War. The colonists were the collateral for a debt they had not contracted. They had also, in the years immediately preceding the Revolution, been forbidden by the Currency Act of 1764 from issuing their own colonial paper currency, because the Bank of England's directors did not want competition in the colonies for the sterling instruments they controlled. Benjamin Franklin, in the testimony he gave to Parliament in 1766, named this directly: the colonies had been prosperous when they issued their own scrip; the prohibition of colonial paper money was a primary cause of the unrest that followed.

The Revolution was won. The Constitution was drafted. The new Republic faced the same question every successor state faces: what to do about the financial architecture of the regime it had just overthrown. The men in the new American government split immediately, and they split along a line that would define American political life for a century and a quarter. The line was: should the United States build itself a Bank of England, or should it not.

## I. Hamilton's bank

The man who wanted the Bank of England was Alexander Hamilton. He had been born in 1755 or 1757 in the British West Indies, on the island of Nevis, illegitimate son of a Scottish father and a half-French half-British mother. He came to the mainland colonies as a teenager, educated himself, fought in the Revolution as Washington's aide-de-camp, drafted much of the *Federalist Papers* with Madison and Jay, and was appointed by Washington as the first Secretary of the Treasury in September 1789. He was thirty-four years old. He spent the next four years building the United States a financial architecture identical, in every essential, to the British one he had grown up watching.

In December 1790 he submitted to Congress a *Report on a National Bank*. The bank he proposed was a private corporation, capitalised at ten million dollars, with twenty per cent of the stock owned by the federal government and eighty per cent by private investors. The bank would hold the government's deposits, manage its debt, and issue paper currency backed by the deposits. The model, as Hamilton openly stated, was the Bank of England. It would be modelled on the Bank of England in capital structure, in operating principle, and in the political relationship to the state. The eighty per cent private ownership would, as in London, be drawn from the merchant and financial class of the country's largest cities — Philadelphia, New York, Boston — and a significant share would be sold to foreign investors in Britain and the Netherlands.

The opposition was led by Thomas Jefferson, then Secretary of State, and James Madison, then in the House. Their arguments were technical and constitutional on the surface and political at the foundation. The technical argument was that the Constitution did not enumerate a power for Congress to charter corporations, and that under what would shortly become the Tenth Amendment, powers not enumerated were reserved to the states. The political argument was that the Bank Hamilton was proposing would do in America what the Bank of England had done in Britain: concentrate the country's credit in a small circle of urban financiers, tie the federal government to those financiers' interests, and place a private oligarchy at the centre of the public finance. Jefferson described the entire banking industry, in a private letter, as "an infinity of successive felonious larcenies." Madison wrote in the House that the Constitution conferred no power to incorporate any such body.

Hamilton answered with a fifteen-thousand-word essay, *On the Constitutionality of a National Bank*, dated 23 February 1791. He invoked the doctrine of implied powers under the "necessary and proper" clause of Article I, Section 8. Washington signed the bill two days later. The First Bank of the United States opened in Philadelphia on 12 December 1791 with a twenty-year charter. The initial public offering in July 1791 sold out in less than an hour, setting off the first speculative bubble in American securities. Many of the initial shareholders were foreign — British and Dutch — though the charter denied them voting rights. The cabinet split between Hamilton and Jefferson over the bank became the founding fracture of American politics, producing the Federalist and Democratic-Republican parties and structuring every American political fight for the next thirty years.

In 1804, Hamilton was killed in a duel with Aaron Burr on the cliffs of Weehawken, New Jersey. He was forty-seven years old. The duel was political as well as personal, and Burr — the sitting Vice President of the United States — had been one of the principal opponents of Hamilton's bank in New York. With Hamilton dead and the Federalists out of power, the Bank's recharter in 1811 was rejected by the Senate by a single vote. The First Bank closed on 3 March 1811.

## II. Madison's reversal, Jackson's veto

The War of 1812 destroyed the Madisonian opposition to a national bank. Without a central financial agent, the federal government could not borrow the state banks suspended specie payments, the currency fragmented, and the war was financed by emergency expedients that nearly broke the Treasury. By 1816, Madison, who had once written the constitutional case against Hamilton's bank, signed the bill chartering the Second Bank of the United States. The charter was twenty years. The capital was thirty-five million dollars. The structure was identical to the First Bank, just larger.

The Second Bank had a difficult first few years and a competent middle decade under its third president, Nicholas Biddle, a Philadelphia patrician who had been a literary prodigy, edited the Lewis and Clark journals, and ran the Bank from 1822 with what contemporaries described as imperial confidence. Under Biddle the Bank stabilised the currency, restrained the state banks' note issues by forcing them to redeem in specie, and produced significant federal dividends. It also, under Biddle, became something the First Bank had never quite become — an explicit political actor. Biddle made loans to favoured politicians, retained editors and lawyers on the Bank's payroll to defend its interests, and distributed Bank funds to influence elections. The Bank's correspondence, published in 1919, is a record of routine political bribery conducted through a federally chartered private corporation. Biddle did not regard any of this as scandalous. He regarded it as the management of the institution's political environment, and he believed that the public interest was identical to the Bank's interest because the Bank's interest, properly run, was identical to a stable currency.

He met his opposite in 1828, when Andrew Jackson was elected President.

Jackson had grown up frontier-poor in the Carolinas, lost his entire family to the Revolutionary War by the age of fourteen, fought the British at the Battle of New Orleans, and in 1804, before he was famous, lost most of his fortune when paper notes from a Philadelphia merchant he had accepted in a land deal became worthless after the merchant's bank failed. Jackson never trusted paper money again. He never trusted bankers. He never trusted the East Coast financial class, which he regarded — accurately — as an aristocracy of money equivalent to the British aristocracy of birth his generation had fought to overthrow. He came to the Presidency hating the Second Bank of the United States and intending to destroy it.

Biddle, advised by Henry Clay and Daniel Webster, decided to force the issue. He submitted a bill to recharter the Bank in January 1832, four years before the existing charter was due to expire, calculating that Jackson would not dare veto it in an election year. The bill passed both houses of Congress. Webster and Clay had been right that Jackson would face political risk in vetoing it. They were wrong about what he would do. On 10 July 1832, Jackson sent his veto message to the Senate. It was written primarily by his Attorney General Roger Taney and his Kitchen Cabinet adviser Amos Kendall, but the political instincts and many of the central phrases were Jackson's. It is one of the most important political documents in American history. It is also, on a careful reading, the most explicit American statement of what a central banking system actually does.

The veto message attacked the Bank in detail. It noted that the Bank was owned by a small circle of wealthy stockholders, many of them foreign — Jackson's tally was that of the Bank's thirty-five million in stock, eight million was held by British and Dutch investors. It noted that the Bank's profits, paid out as dividends, flowed largely to those stockholders, including the foreign ones, draining specie from the United States to Europe at a rate of two to five million dollars per year. It noted that the Bank's twenty-five directors included five appointed by the government and twenty elected by the stockholders, with the foreign shareholders excluded from voting only by an internal arrangement that could be changed at will. It noted that the Bank had been used to enrich its owners at the expense of farmers, mechanics, and labourers. And it stated, in a phrase that became the political slogan of a generation: the Bank's powers were used "to make the rich richer and the potent more powerful."

The veto was sustained. Jackson ran for re-election on the Bank issue and won by a landslide, four-to-one in electoral votes against Henry Clay. The next year, 1833, he ordered the federal deposits removed from the Bank and placed in state-chartered "pet banks." Biddle responded by contracting credit sharply across the country, creating an artificial recession that he hoped would force Jackson to back down. Biddle wrote to allies that he intended to teach the President "that pressure cannot affect the Bank, although it may affect every other interest in the community." The Bank War became economic warfare against the United States by its own central bank, conducted by a man who believed his private corporation's interest was synonymous with the public good. Jackson did not back down. Biddle was eventually forced to reverse the contraction. The Bank's federal charter expired in 1836. Biddle rechartered it as a Pennsylvania state bank. It collapsed in 1841.

On 30 January 1835, while the Bank War was still in progress, a British-born house painter named Richard Lawrence approached Jackson on the East Portico of the Capitol and pulled the triggers of two pistols at point-blank range. Both pistols misfired. Jackson — sixty-seven years old, recovering from a respiratory illness — beat Lawrence to the ground with his cane. It was the first known assassination attempt on a sitting President of the United States. Lawrence was tried, found insane, and confined for the rest of his life. He had claimed at trial to be the rightful King of England and to believe Jackson was preventing his accession. Jackson, characteristically, believed Lawrence had been hired by his Bank enemies. The book does not claim he was right. The book notes that he was the first American president to survive an assassination attempt during a fight with a central bank, and that the next two presidents to fight major monetary battles — Lincoln and McKinley — would not survive theirs.

## III. The greenback experiment

For seventy-seven years after Jackson's veto, the United States had no central bank. It had instead a fragmented system of state banks, each issuing its own paper notes, plus the Treasury. The system was unstable, prone to panics, and inadequate for a continental industrialising economy. The Panic of 1837, the Panic of 1857, the Panic of 1873, the Panic of 1893, and finally the Panic of 1907 each broke the economy in turn. There was, however, no central bank for those panics to bail out — and as the panics receded, the country recovered without the kind of permanent debt-financed dependence on a private banking institution that the British had built.

The most important demonstration of an alternative came under Abraham Lincoln. The Civil War was the most expensive thing the United States had ever done — eventually around three and a half billion dollars, against a pre-war annual federal budget of around sixty-five million. Lincoln's Treasury Secretary Salmon P. Chase initially tried to finance the war through bond sales to private banks. The banks offered loans at rates Lincoln considered usurious — reportedly as high as thirty-six per cent in some accounts. Lincoln turned instead to a solution his friend Colonel Edmund Dick Taylor of Chicago proposed: have the federal government simply print its own currency, declare it legal tender, and use it to pay the army and the contractors directly.

The First Legal Tender Act was passed on 25 February 1862. It authorised one hundred and fifty million dollars in United States Notes — "greenbacks," from the green ink on the reverse. The notes were not redeemable in gold or silver. They were not borrowed from any private bank. They were issued directly by the Treasury and made legal tender by act of Congress. Two more Legal Tender Acts followed in July 1862 and March 1863. By war's end, four hundred and fifty million dollars in greenbacks had been issued. Roughly a quarter of the Union war effort was financed not by debt to private banks but by sovereign fiat currency, issued at no interest cost, backed by nothing but the credit of the United States and the willingness of the population to accept it as money.

The greenbacks worked. The Union won the war. The dollar held up — there was wartime inflation, but the currency did not collapse, and after the war the greenbacks circulated as ordinary money alongside coin. The lesson the greenbacks demonstrated was the lesson the Bank of England had been hiding for a century and a half: a sovereign government does not need to borrow its own currency from private bankers at interest. It can simply issue the currency. The private intermediary is a choice, not a necessity. The choice exists because the private intermediary profits from it. If the government issues the currency directly, the private intermediary does not get paid.

This was understood by the parties to the question. The American financial class — Wall Street, the New York banks, the agents of the European banking houses — opposed the greenbacks throughout the war and lobbied continuously for their withdrawal. The New York banks had wanted to be the ones lending the Union the money at interest; Lincoln had cut them out by going directly to the printing press. The political question after the war was whether the country would continue to issue its own sovereign currency, or revert to borrowing from the banks. The financial interests had a clear preference. The documented record is the policy response.

Abraham Lincoln was assassinated by John Wilkes Booth on 14 April 1865, six days after the surrender at Appomattox. The book does not claim that the assassination was carried out by banking interests, because documented does not establish that. The book does record the following sequence of facts. Lincoln was killed on 14 April 1865. Eleven months and twenty-eight days later, on 12 April 1866, Congress passed the Contraction Act, beginning the systematic withdrawal of greenbacks from circulation. The Resumption Act of 1875 completed the process, taking the United States fully back onto the gold standard by 1 January 1879. The greenback experiment was unwound within fourteen years of its progenitor's death. The American financial class, which had opposed the greenbacks throughout the war, had the policy reversed within a generation. The Treasury once again became dependent on private bank credit to fund federal operations. The president who had demonstrated that the United States did not need to borrow its currency from private bankers was dead, and within months of his death the institutions he had inconvenienced were dismantling his policy. The book draws no further conclusion. The reader can.

## IV. Morgan, the Panic of 1907, and the National Monetary Commission

By 1900 the United States had spent thirty years on the gold standard and was the largest industrial economy on earth. It had no central bank. Its currency was, in practice, run by J.P. Morgan, the senior partner of the firm that bore his name, from his office on the corner of Wall Street and Broad. Morgan was sixty-three years old in 1900, the son of a successful merchant banker, trained in London at the firm that became Morgan Grenfell. He was personally responsible, on at least three occasions in the 1890s, for bailing the United States Treasury out of liquidity crises by syndicating gold loans on behalf of the federal government. The American state, the most powerful industrial state on earth, was a personal client of a single private banker.

In October 1907 a failed attempt by F. Augustus Heinze and Charles W. Morse to corner the stock of the United Copper Company triggered a bank run on the Knickerbocker Trust Company in New York. The panic spread. Banks across the country suspended payments. The stock market collapsed. The Treasury Secretary, George Cortelyou, deposited federal funds in the New York banks to try to stabilise the system, but the federal Treasury did not have the resources to manage a crisis of that scale.

J.P. Morgan did. On Sunday, 3 November 1907, Morgan summoned the leading bankers of New York to his Madison Avenue library, locked the doors, and refused to let them leave until they had agreed to pool resources to save the system. By the small hours of Monday morning they had agreed. The crisis was contained. A private banker, on his own initiative, in his own house, had saved the financial system of the United States by personally compelling other private bankers to put up the money. The point Morgan made — quietly, deliberately, in the months that followed — was that this had been the last time he or anyone else would do it. The country needed an institution. The country needed a central bank.

In May 1908 Congress passed the Aldrich-Vreeland Act, creating the National Monetary Commission. The Commission was chaired by Senator Nelson W. Aldrich of Rhode Island. Aldrich was the senior Republican in the Senate, the chairman of the Finance Committee, the man known privately as the "General Manager of the Nation." His daughter Abby had married John D. Rockefeller Jr. in 1901. He was, by family connection, the link between the Senate and the Rockefeller fortune. He was put in charge of designing the American central bank.

For two years the Commission toured European central banks — the Bank of England, the Banque de France, the Reichsbank, the Banca d'Italia — interviewing officers and collecting data. By the autumn of 1910 the Commission had eighteen members and could not agree on a plan. Aldrich decided to draft the plan himself, with a small group of advisers, in private, away from the Commission and away from the press. He chose six men, including himself. He chose where they would meet. He chose the security arrangements. He chose the cover story.

## V. Jekyll Island, November 1910

On the evening of 22 November 1910, six men boarded a private rail car at a Hoboken, New Jersey, station, one at a time, under instructions from Aldrich to use only their first names. The men were the following.

**Nelson W. Aldrich**, United States Senator from Rhode Island, chairman of the Senate Finance Committee, chairman of the National Monetary Commission, senior Republican leader of the Senate. He was known in Washington as the "General Manager of the Nation." In 1901 his daughter Abby Aldrich had married John D. Rockefeller Jr., merging the senior Republican leadership of the Senate directly into the largest private fortune in the world. The bill Aldrich was about to draft would benefit, in the first instance, the bank his daughter's father-in-law owned.

**Arthur Shelton**, Aldrich's private secretary. The keeper of papers.

**A. Piatt Andrew**, Assistant Secretary of the Treasury under President William Howard Taft. A Harvard economist who had been recruited to give the operation institutional cover from inside the federal government. He was the United States Treasury's man in the room.

**Henry P. Davison**, senior partner at J. P. Morgan & Co. Personally J. P. Morgan's right hand, the man Morgan trusted to manage the firm's political business. It was Davison who had been the operational fixer during the 1907 Panic three years earlier, and Davison who, when the rail car reached the Brunswick station and the reporters were waiting on the platform, walked out and spoke to them until they dispersed and the cover story held.

**Frank A. Vanderlip**, president of the National City Bank of New York. National City Bank was the principal Rockefeller bank. It would become Citibank, and eventually Citigroup. Vanderlip was a former Treasury official who had crossed over to the private side. He was the senior officer of the Rockefeller financial empire in the room.

**Paul M. Warburg**, partner at Kuhn, Loeb & Co. of New York. Kuhn, Loeb was the American merchant bank of the German-Jewish banking dynasties — the Warburgs of Hamburg, the Schiffs of Frankfurt, the Loebs, the Speyers, the Seligmans. The interlocking marriages are worth naming directly. Paul Warburg had married Nina Loeb, daughter of Solomon Loeb, one of the founders of Kuhn, Loeb. Paul's brother Felix Warburg had married Frieda Schiff, daughter of Jacob Schiff — the head of Kuhn, Loeb. Jacob Schiff had married Therese Loeb, Solomon Loeb's other daughter. Paul Warburg's other brother, Max Warburg, ran the M. M. Warburg banking house in Hamburg, which would shortly be financing the German Imperial government's preparation for the First World War. Paul Warburg, in the room at Jekyll Island, was simultaneously the brother-in-law of the head of Kuhn, Loeb in New York, the brother of the head of M. M. Warburg in Hamburg, and the principal technical architect of the central bank that would soon be financing the Allied side of the war his brother was financing on the German side. Warburg had been writing essays on American banking reform since 1907, all of them arguing that the United States should adopt the German Reichsbank model. At Jekyll Island, that is what he drafted.

Six men. The Aldrich political machine, the Rockefeller bank, the Morgan bank, the Kuhn-Loeb-Warburg-Schiff network, the United States Treasury, and a private secretary to keep the papers. By Vanderlip's own later admission, they represented, between them, a quarter of the wealth of the world. They were not six independent bankers. They were the senior officers of three interlocking dynastic banking networks, plus the senior Senate Republican whose daughter had married into the wealthiest of those networks, plus the Treasury's man. The Jekyll Island Club, where they were going, was a private resort co-owned by Morgan himself and by other members of the same circle — the Vanderbilts, the Astors, William Rockefeller, J. P. Morgan. The participants would have the resort to themselves. The customary club staff had been given two weeks' paid leave. Replacement servants brought from the mainland did not know who any of the guests were.

The cover story was a duck-hunting trip. Reporters had been told as much. When the party arrived at the Brunswick, Georgia, train station, Davison spoke to the journalists waiting on the platform. The journalists dispersed. The secret of the journey was kept. The men spent the next ten days drafting the bill that would, with modifications, become the Federal Reserve Act of 1913.

The participants were instructed, before arrival, to use only their first names. Vanderlip and Davison went further: they adopted entirely new first names, becoming "Wilbur" and "Orville" — after the Wright brothers, on a joke that they were "always right." Vanderlip and Davison kept calling each other Wilbur and Orville for years afterwards. The group later referred to themselves as the "First Name Club." This is not folklore. It is in Vanderlip's autobiography, *From Farmboy to Financier*, published in 1935. Vanderlip is the same man who, describing his own behaviour in November 1910, wrote: "I was as secretive — indeed, as furtive — as any conspirator."

The men worked, by Vanderlip's account, "the most intense period of work that I have ever had." Warburg, schooled in the German Reichsbank model, did most of the technical drafting. Aldrich provided the political shape. The disagreements were tactical: Aldrich wanted a single centralised institution on the European model; Warburg argued that American politics would not accept that and the institution needed to look decentralised, with twelve regional reserve banks, even if the policy authority was centralised. Warburg's tactic won. The plan that emerged from Jekyll Island was a Reserve Association of America — a private banking institution dressed in the public language of regional federation, with the actual control vested in the major member banks, which were the banks the six men represented.

The "discovery, we knew, simply must not happen," Vanderlip later wrote. Discovery did not happen. The participants returned to New York. The plan was published by Aldrich under his own name in January 1911. The bill — known as the Aldrich Plan — went to Congress in 1912. It died there. Not because the plan was bad, and not because the political opposition was real. It died because the political theatre required it to die under the Republican label so it could pass under the Democratic one.

The point that needs naming here is the one the official histories soften. The 1912 election was won by Woodrow Wilson on an explicit anti-Wall-Street platform. The progressive Democrats had spent two decades attacking exactly the kind of money trust the Aldrich Plan represented. William Jennings Bryan had said publicly: "Big financiers are back of the Aldrich currency scheme." This was true. The Aldrich Plan was the money trust's bill. Wilson ran against it. Wilson won. And then thirteen months later Wilson signed the same bill into law.

The mechanism of the switch was Colonel Edward M. House, Wilson's senior political adviser. House had spent the 1912 campaign as the bridge between the Wilson campaign and the Wall Street circle. He had personally written a novel in 1912, *Philip Dru: Administrator*, in which a benevolent dictator imposes a graduated income tax and a central bank on the United States — published anonymously while he was simultaneously running Wilson's political operation. House would later, in 1921, found the Council on Foreign Relations with Paul Warburg as one of its founding members. The relationship was not concealed in retrospect. House and Warburg corresponded directly throughout the drafting of the Federal Reserve Act. House arranged the meetings between Warburg and Carter Glass, the Democratic congressman who introduced the eventual bill. The Democratic alternative to the Aldrich Plan was the Aldrich Plan with the Democratic Party as its political surface, and Colonel House had ensured the substitution before Wilson was sworn in.

Vanderlip stated this plainly later. "Although the Aldrich Federal Reserve Plan was defeated when it bore the name Aldrich," he wrote in his autobiography, "nevertheless its essential points were all contained in the plan that was finally adopted." Carter Glass, when challenged in 1922, denied copying the Aldrich Plan and called the suggestion the greatest misconception ever projected in the Senate Chamber. Glass was lying. The bill was the same. The two-party theatre of opposition was theatre. The senior leaderships of both parties had been brought into the same plan through Colonel House before the political "debate" began, and the public arguments in 1912 and 1913 about who was for and against the central bank were arguments about whose name went on the front of the bill, not about whether the bill would pass.

This is what is meant by capture. The opposition party, running on opposition to the money trust, was already negotiating the money trust's bill before the election. The Aldrich Plan did not lose. Aldrich's name lost. The plan was preserved intact, attached to a different politician, and passed by the same Congress that had refused it under Republican sponsorship a year earlier.

This is a conspiracy. The word is the precise legal and historical description. Six men met in secret, in a private resort on a remote island, under false names, with the staff sent away and replacement servants brought in who did not know their identities, to draft legislation that would bind three hundred and fifty million Americans across the next century. They wrote about it themselves, decades later, in language they themselves chose: secretive, furtive, conspiratorial. They then arranged, through Colonel House, for the same legislation to be passed under the political brand most likely to make it acceptable to the public — the party that had run against it. The Federal Reserve was not founded by Congress in any meaningful sense. It was founded by six men on Jekyll Island in November 1910, ratified by a Congress that had been pre-arranged to ratify it, signed by a President whose senior adviser had spent the previous two years arranging the signing. The country was not consulted. The country was the security pledged against the loan, just as the British public had been in 1694, just as the Bandanese had been in 1621.

## VI. The Titanic, as an aside

There is a story, retailed widely in the years since the rise of the internet, that the sinking of the RMS *Titanic* on the night of 14–15 April 1912 was engineered by J.P. Morgan to eliminate three wealthy opponents of the Federal Reserve plan: John Jacob Astor IV, Benjamin Guggenheim, and Isidor Straus. Morgan owned the parent company. Morgan was booked on the maiden voyage and cancelled at the last minute. The three men died. The Federal Reserve Act passed eight months later. The story is so clean that millions of people have repeated it.

The book has checked it. The structural facts are real. Morgan did own the International Mercantile Marine, which owned the White Star Line, which owned the *Titanic*. He was booked into one of the most expensive suites for the maiden voyage. He did cancel. The three men named did die. The Act did pass that December. None of this is in dispute.

The central claim is, however, the wrong way round. Isidor Straus was not an opponent of the Federal Reserve plan. He was a public supporter of it. The *New York Times* of 16 October 1911, page 10, ran the headline ISIDOR STRAUS URGES NEW BANKING PLAN; REPLIES TO J.J. HILL'S ATTACK ON THE NATIONAL RESERVE ASSOCIATION SCHEME — the National Reserve Association being the formal name of the Aldrich Plan, the plan drafted on Jekyll Island a year earlier. Two days later, on 18 October, the *Times* ran another piece on Straus defending the same plan. On 25 October Straus wrote to the *Times* himself, complaining that the paper was being insufficiently supportive of "a great cause that needs all the help it can muster." This was not a man being killed to remove him from the path of the central bank. This was a man campaigning for it.

The connection goes further back. The historian Murray Rothbard — a fierce opponent of the Federal Reserve, no friend to mainstream consensus — documents in *The Progressive Era* that in January 1906 Jacob Schiff of Kuhn, Loeb pushed the New York Chamber of Commerce to set up a special committee on banking reform. The committee's members included Frank A. Vanderlip — one of the six men who would meet on Jekyll Island four years later — and Isidor Straus, who was a personal friend of Schiff. Their March 1906 report called for a central bank "similar to the Bank of Germany." Straus was, in other words, on the founding committee of the central-bank movement. He had been part of the project for six years before he died. He was an insider in the same circle as Vanderlip, Schiff, and through them Warburg.

Astor and Guggenheim, the other two named in the meme, left no documented public position on the Federal Reserve in any direction. Astor was a real estate magnate; Guggenheim, a mining heir. Neither had a public role in the banking fight. Neither has been shown to have opposed the plan. The meme's three "opponents" consist of one demonstrable supporter on record and two men with no documented stake in the question either way.

The book mentions all of this for the same reason it spent a chapter on the documented secrecy of Jekyll Island and a chapter on the named Whig Junto: the discipline cuts both ways. Where conspiracy is documented, the book names it. Where a popular conspiracy story collapses under primary-source examination, the book says so. The *Titanic* story collapses. The most prominent supposed victim was campaigning publicly for the Aldrich Plan in the *New York Times* six months before he died. The real opposition to the Federal Reserve was not at the bottom of the Atlantic. It was on the floor of the House of Representatives. That is where this chapter goes next.

## VII. The bill passes — and the opposition that was actually destroyed

The Federal Reserve Act passed the House on 22 December 1913 by 298 votes to 60 and the Senate on 23 December by 43 to 25. President Woodrow Wilson signed it the same day. Congress had been in session through the Christmas recess; the bill was rushed to a vote at a time when many members had gone home for the holidays. The Speaker of the House and the Senate Majority Leader had cleared the way. The Republican opposition was scattered. The Democratic populist wing, which had spent two decades attacking exactly this kind of institution, was given the political surface — twelve regional banks, government appointment of the central board — that Warburg's tactical drafting had designed for exactly this purpose.

The opposition that existed was real, articulate, and overwhelmed. Sixty members of the House voted against. The most prominent of them was **Charles A. Lindbergh Sr.**, Republican of Minnesota, father of the aviator. Lindbergh had spent the previous two years running a separate investigation through the Pujo Committee — named for Louisiana Democrat Arsène Pujo — which had documented the interlocking directorates of J.P. Morgan, Rockefeller's National City Bank, and First National Bank as a single concentrated banking power. Lindbergh's own 1913 book, *Banking and Currency and the Money Trust*, had laid out, before the Federal Reserve Act was passed, precisely how the proposed system would centralise the power the Pujo Committee had just documented. On the floor of the House on the day of the vote, Lindbergh rose and said: "This Act establishes the most gigantic trust on earth. When the President signs this Act, the invisible government by the Money Power, proven to exist by the Money Trust Investigation, will be legalized. The new law will create inflation whenever the trusts want inflation... From now on, depressions will be scientifically created."

Lindbergh was not believed. He was mocked as a crank for the rest of his career. He died in 1924. His grandson — Charles Lindbergh Jr., the aviator's twenty-month-old son — was kidnapped from the family home in New Jersey on 1 March 1932 and found murdered six weeks later, in what remains one of the most notorious unsolved crimes of the twentieth century. The book does not connect the kidnapping to the family's monetary politics, because no serious historian has done so.
