The book notes that the man who stood on the floor of the House in 1913 and said depressions would now be scientifically created lived to see the Crash of 1929 prove him right, and that his grandson was murdered in the depths of the Depression he had predicted, and that the Lindbergh family's experience of public American life across three generations — from the congressional anti-Fed campaign of the father, to the famous transatlantic flight of the son, to the murder of the grandson, to the son's later destruction in the press over his pre-war political views — is a history of an American family that paid an unusual price across the twentieth century. The book offers no theory. The book offers the timeline.

The man who took up the fight after Lindbergh's death was **Louis T. McFadden**, Republican of Pennsylvania. McFadden's qualifications to speak on the subject were unusual: he had been president of the Pennsylvania Bankers' Association in 1914–1915 and president of the First National Bank of Canton, Pennsylvania, from 1916 to 1925. He was a banker. He had also been Chairman of the House Committee on Banking and Currency from 1920 to 1931 — eleven years overseeing the Federal Reserve from the most senior congressional position available. He knew the system from the inside. By 1932, after two decades of watching it operate, he had concluded that the Federal Reserve was something other than what the public had been told.

On 10 June 1932, in the depths of the Great Depression, McFadden rose on the floor of the House and gave a twenty-five-minute speech that is preserved in the Congressional Record at pages 12595 to 12603. He accused the Federal Reserve of having engineered the Great Depression deliberately. He named Paul Warburg by name as the architect. He described how the Aldrich Bill — the Jekyll Island plan — had been translated almost verbatim into the Federal Reserve Act after a "half-million-dollar public information program" had been run to soften public resistance. He documented that the Federal Reserve had been shipping United States gold reserves to a rebuilding Germany in the late 1920s — over thirteen million dollars of it in 1932 alone — on the basis of "mere finance drafts drawn by Germans." He described how the Federal Reserve banks were owned and controlled by the same private banking houses that had drafted the original Aldrich plan, and how those banks' interests, not the public's, governed the system's decisions.

On 23 May 1933, eleven months later, McFadden went further. He introduced a resolution in the House bringing formal impeachment charges against the Federal Reserve Board, the Federal Reserve banks, the Comptroller of the Currency, and the Secretary of the Treasury. The charges were conspiracy, fraud, unlawful conversion, and treason. The resolution was referred to the Judiciary Committee. The Judiciary Committee never acted on it. The impeachment was buried.

McFadden's own party turned on him. He was denounced in Congress as a crank. The Republican machinery in Pennsylvania worked actively against his re-nomination. He lost his seat in November 1934. In October 1936, two years after leaving office, he died suddenly in a hotel room in New York City. The cause of death was officially recorded as heart failure. There were two earlier attempts on his life reported in the contemporary press during his anti-Fed campaign — one in which gunshots were fired at him as he disembarked from a train, another involving a bout of food poisoning at a Washington banquet that his doctors believed was an attempt to poison him. The book cannot independently verify the assassination attempts beyond the contemporary reporting. It can verify that the senior congressional banker who tried to impeach the Federal Reserve was, within four years of bringing the charges, removed from office by his own party and dead in a New York hotel.

This is documented opposition story. It is not on a ship in the Atlantic. It is on the floor of the House and in the Congressional Record. It is also, and this is the point, almost completely excluded from the Federal Reserve's own institutional history. The Federal Reserve's published account of its founding — produced by Fed staff historians and disseminated through Federal Reserve Bank educational materials — discusses Jekyll Island briefly and frames it as a constructive episode in the technical drafting of necessary legislation. It does not discuss Lindbergh's floor speech in 1913. It does not discuss the Pujo Committee. It does not discuss McFadden's 1932 speech, his 1933 impeachment resolution, his political destruction in 1934, or his sudden death in 1936. The Federal Reserve has, across a century, written its own founding as the technocratic solution to the panics of the pre-Fed era, with no enemies, no opposition, no critics worth remembering. The opposition has been excised. The two American congressmen who most directly named what the Federal Reserve actually was, while it was actually being built, have been written out of the institution's account of its own birth.

Carter Glass, the Democratic congressman who had nominally drafted the Federal Reserve Act of 1913 and who had spent twenty years insisting it was not the Aldrich Plan, lived to see the Depression. He died in 1946, eighty-eight years old, having spent his last decade watching the institution he had named-drafted go through the 1929 crash, the bank failures of 1930–1933, the New Deal restructurings, and the wartime monetisation of the federal debt. Glass himself, who had spent his career defending the Federal Reserve as his own creation against suggestions that it was Wall Street's, by the late 1930s was openly hostile to what the system had become. He attacked it from inside the Senate. He said, in a moment widely reported in the financial press of the period, that the Federal Reserve had become "a Frankenstein monster." This is the man whose name is on the bill, looking at what the bill produced, two decades after he signed it, and using the word *monster*. The Fed's institutional history does not quote that line either.

## VIII. What the system did once it had the country

The Federal Reserve System opened in November 1914. Each of the twelve regional Federal Reserve Banks was owned by its member commercial banks. Member banks held non-transferable stock in their regional Fed and received a six per cent statutory dividend on it. The Federal Reserve Bank of New York, which would control open-market operations and hold the country's foreign currency reserves, was dominated from the start by the same banks that had sent representatives to Jekyll Island — National City Bank, J.P. Morgan & Co., First National Bank, Chase National. The first Governor of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, appointed in 1914, was **Benjamin Strong**, a J.P. Morgan & Co. associate who had been Morgan's right hand during the 1907 panic. Strong ran the New York Fed and the Federal Reserve System until his death in 1928, exactly the years of the credit expansion that produced the 1929 crash.

The president of National City Bank — Vanderlip's bank, the Jekyll Island bank — became a Federal Reserve member institution as soon as the system opened. So did J.P. Morgan & Co. So did Kuhn, Loeb. The Federal Reserve Act, in its first decade of operation, transferred the management of American monetary policy from the United States Treasury to a federation of private banks owned and run by the same families that had spent the previous century trying to build a central bank, and that had finally succeeded in 1913 by writing it themselves on Jekyll Island and handing it to Congress to ratify.

Then, in October 1928, Benjamin Strong died, and the Federal Reserve's leadership transitioned to a less experienced board at the worst possible moment. Within a year, the credit expansion the New York Fed had been managing through the 1920s collapsed into the Crash of October 1929, and the Federal Reserve's response — to *tighten* credit at the moment the economy most needed loosening — converted what would have been a serious recession into the Great Depression. Milton Friedman and Anna Schwartz documented this in detail in *A Monetary History of the United States, 1867–1960*, published in 1963. Their analysis became the consensus account: the Depression's severity was the direct result of Federal Reserve policy.

McFadden, in his 1932 speech, said the same thing thirty years earlier than Friedman and Schwartz, from the floor of the House, while the Depression was still in progress. He used stronger language. He said the Depression was deliberately engineered. Friedman and Schwartz, working from the same documentary record three decades later, said it was incompetently managed. The difference between *engineered* and *incompetently managed* is a question of intent, and on questions of intent the documentary record does not settle the question either way. What documented sources does settle is that the policy was deliberate, the policy was made by the same banking interests that had designed the system, and the policy produced the largest single transfer of distressed asset ownership in American history — from foreclosed farmers and ruined businesses upward to the banks that held the mortgages and the loans. Whether by design or by negligence, the system did what such a system always does, in the direction such a system always does it. It transferred wealth from the public to its owners.

## IX. The pattern, completed

Step back, again, from the names, and look at the pattern.

In Amsterdam in 1602, the prime minister of the Dutch Republic and the merchant oligarchy of the city built the first joint-stock corporation and the first public deposit bank, took the directorships, and ran the city, the bank, and the company through interlocking memberships.

In London in 1688, six peers and a bishop committed treason against the standing king and brought in a foreign army to depose him. Within six years their political faction, the Whig Junto, had chartered the Bank of England, with the Chancellor of the Exchequer himself a founding subscriber. The first Governor of the Bank was simultaneously Lord Mayor of London, Lord Commissioner of the Admiralty, and the senior officer of the City's Huguenot merchant community. His brothers were directors.

In the nineteenth century the Rothschild family, five brothers in five capitals, extended the architecture across borders, lending to sovereigns, taking commissions from war indemnities and slave-owner compensations, holding effective veto power over major sovereign borrowing in Europe.

In the United States the same pattern was attempted by Alexander Hamilton in 1791, defeated by Andrew Jackson in 1832, evaded by Abraham Lincoln in 1862, and finally installed, against all of the previous resistance, by six men on Jekyll Island in November 1910 and ratified by Congress on 23 December 1913. The men on Jekyll Island represented the J.P. Morgan firm, the Rockefeller-controlled National City Bank, the Kuhn-Loeb firm with its German-Jewish merchant banking connections, and the Treasury department of the United States. The plan they wrote was the plan that passed. The Federal Reserve System, the most powerful central bank in the world, was designed by six private bankers in secret, on a private island, under false names.

The architecture is now four centuries old. The names have changed across the generations — Van Oldenbarnevelt, the Heeren XVII, the Immortal Seven, the Whig Junto, the Houblons, the Rothschilds, Hamilton, Biddle, Aldrich, Warburg, Vanderlip, Morgan, Strong — but the pattern has not. The men in the rooms write the rules. The institutions they build are private. The state ratifies. The public pays the interest. Each generation enriches itself directly from the institutions it is chartering, and each generation hands those institutions to its successors. The Federal Reserve is the same instrument the Bank of England was, in the same relationship to the United States that the Bank of England was to Britain, designed by the same kind of men in the same kind of room.

What changed after 1913, however, is that the United States had won. There was no greater power to extend the architecture to. The Federal Reserve was, in a sense, the last bank — the last continental-scale central bank that could be built within the existing framework of sovereign nation-states. Everything after 1913 has been the consolidation, internationalisation, and weaponisation of the system the six men on Jekyll Island finished building. The Bretton Woods conference of 1944 would internationalise the dollar. The collapse of Bretton Woods in 1971 would untether the dollar from gold and complete what the Federal Reserve Act had begun. The world after 1971 has been running on an architecture that traces in unbroken descent to the city hall basement in Amsterdam in 1609 — and the people running it are still the same families, still in the same rooms, still writing the rules in their own favour.

The next chapter is about what they did with the system once they had it.


---

# Chapter 5: The Machine Goes Global

Between 1913 and 1971, the Federal Reserve was a national instrument. By 1971 it was the planet's central bank. The transition was carried out by named men in named rooms across three crucial moments: a hotel in New Hampshire in 1944, a presidential cabin in Maryland in 1971, and a palace in Jeddah in 1974. The official histories present these three moments as three separate technocratic adjustments to changing world conditions. The official histories are wrong. The three moments are one continuous project, executed across three decades by overlapping circles of named men, to convert the American dollar into the instrument of global tribute.

The record names the men and the rooms.

## I. Bretton Woods, July 1944

By the summer of 1944 the Second World War was going to be won by the Allies, and the question that the British and American Treasuries had been quietly negotiating since 1941 was what kind of international monetary system the post-war world would have. Both sides agreed that the gold standard of the 1920s had failed and that the financial fragmentation of the 1930s — competitive devaluations, exchange controls, the collapse of international trade — had directly produced the conditions for fascism. Both sides agreed that something coordinated had to replace it. They disagreed, sharply, about what.

The British plan was John Maynard Keynes's. It had been refined across three years and was, by July 1944, the most sophisticated proposal for international monetary architecture ever written. The plan was called the International Clearing Union. Its central feature was a neutral global reserve currency, the *bancor*, issued by the Clearing Union itself and pegged to a basket of commodities. No single national currency would dominate. Both creditor and debtor countries would be under symmetric pressure to adjust — countries that ran persistent surpluses would face penalties just as countries that ran persistent deficits would. The system was designed, in Keynes's words, to prevent any nation from "imposing on the rest of the world" the burdens of its own monetary mismanagement. Keynes understood, having watched what the gold standard had done to interwar Britain, what dominant-currency status meant. He proposed a system without one.

The American plan was Harry Dexter White's. White was forty-seven years old in 1944, Assistant Secretary of the Treasury, son of Lithuanian Jewish immigrants, Harvard-trained, the principal lieutenant of Treasury Secretary Henry Morgenthau Jr. He was empowered by Morgenthau and through Morgenthau by Roosevelt. His plan was the International Monetary Fund and the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development — what is now the World Bank. The system was anchored on the American dollar. The dollar was pegged to gold at thirty-five dollars an ounce. All other Allied currencies were pegged to the dollar. The IMF would manage short-term imbalances; the World Bank would finance reconstruction. The bancor was gone. The neutral currency was gone. The symmetric pressure on creditors was gone. The post-war monetary system would be a dollar system, run from Washington, with the United States holding the global reserve currency and therefore the privilege of paying for its imports with paper it printed itself.

Keynes and White negotiated in late 1943 and early 1944 in an atmosphere of mutual loathing. Keynes regarded White as a brilliant but provincial bully. White regarded Keynes as a sentimental imperial relic representing a power that had already lost the war. The British Empire was bankrupt. The Lend-Lease debts that funded Britain's war were owed to the United States. White had been instructed by Morgenthau to use that financial leverage to break what remained of Britain's reserve-currency role, and he did. The Bretton Woods conference convened on 1 July 1944 at the Mount Washington Hotel in New Hampshire with 730 delegates from forty-four Allied nations, and ran for three weeks. By 22 July it had agreed, in essentials, to White's plan. The bancor was not on the final agenda. Keynes spent the conference exhausted, ill, deferring publicly while losing every meaningful battle. He died eighteen months later, in April 1946, his health broken in part by the negotiations.

This is, in the official histories, where the story ends — the Allies agreed, the system worked for twenty-five years, the dollar became the post-war reserve currency, prosperity returned to the West. What the official histories tend to underemphasise is what Benn Steil documented at length in his 2013 book *The Battle of Bretton Woods*, published by Princeton University Press under the imprint of the Council on Foreign Relations. Steil is a senior fellow at the CFR — not a marginal source — and his book is the standard scholarly account. The documented finding is this: Harry Dexter White, the principal architect of the post-war American monetary order, was simultaneously an agent of Soviet intelligence.

This is not a theory. The Venona Project, declassified in 1995, contains multiple intercepted Soviet cables referring to White as a source or agent, including documented contacts with a Soviet handler code-named Kol'tsov in April and August 1944 — that is, immediately before and immediately after Bretton Woods. The FBI files corroborate. The Soviet archives, opened in the 1990s, corroborate. The case is not closed in the legal sense — White denied the charges and died of a heart attack on 16 August 1948, three days after testifying to the House Un-American Activities Committee — but the documentary record, assembled by a senior CFR researcher and published by a major university press, establishes that the man who designed the post-war American financial order was in clandestine communication with Soviet intelligence over years. He was the first US Executive Director of the International Monetary Fund. He died of a heart attack three days after being asked, under oath, to defend himself.

The book is not arguing that Bretton Woods was a Soviet plot. The book is arguing that the documented record of the man who designed it is more complicated than the standard story permits. Whatever else Harry Dexter White was doing in those years, he was serving more than one master. The architecture he handed Washington was an architecture that gave the United States exactly what the Soviet Union benefited most from American having: an overreaching imperial financial commitment that would, on its own contradictions, eventually break. Whether White intended this or not, it is what happened.

The first president of the World Bank was **Eugene Meyer**, appointed June 1946. Meyer was seventy years old, a former partner at Lazard Frères, a former president of Allied Chemical Corporation, a former chairman of the Federal Reserve Board (1930–1933), and since 1933 the owner of *The Washington Post*. He was Wall Street, the Federal Reserve, and the press in a single biography. He served as president of the World Bank for six months before resigning and was succeeded by **John J. McCloy**, the Cravath, Swaine & Moore partner who would later run the Chase Manhattan Bank, chair the Ford Foundation, chair the Council on Foreign Relations, sit on the Warren Commission, and become the prototype of the postwar American "wise man" whose career moved seamlessly across Wall Street, the foundations, the intelligence services, and the multilateral institutions. The World Bank was, from its first president onward, an arm of the same circle that had built the Federal Reserve at Jekyll Island thirty-five years earlier. The names had changed. The class had not.

## II. The Triffin Dilemma and the Long Slow Crisis

By the late 1950s the system was working in a particular way, which is that the United States ran persistent balance-of-payments deficits — exporting dollars to the rest of the world to fund Marshall Plan reconstruction, post-war military deployments, and the consumption of American consumers — and the rest of the world held the dollars as reserves. The system functioned only as long as the rest of the world trusted that the dollars they held were genuinely convertible into gold at $35 an ounce.

In 1960 a Belgian-American economist at Yale named **Robert Triffin** testified before Congress and explained why this could not last. The argument is now called the Triffin Dilemma. It is structural and inescapable. The world economy needs reserve currency to function. If the reserve currency is supplied by one nation's central bank, that nation must run persistent deficits to provide the liquidity. But persistent deficits eventually exceed the gold reserves backing the currency, at which point confidence in convertibility collapses. The reserve-currency role requires the conditions that destroy it. Triffin published this analysis as *Gold and the Dollar Crisis* in 1960, while the system was, on paper, working perfectly. He predicted its collapse within a decade. He was off by eleven years.

By 1965 the cracks were showing. France, under Charles de Gaulle, had begun openly converting French dollar reserves to American gold, transferring physical bullion from New York to the Banque de France. On 4 February 1965 de Gaulle held a famous press conference in which his finance minister, Valéry Giscard d'Estaing — later President of France — coined the phrase that would define the next half-century of monetary politics: the dollar's *exorbitant privilege*. The privilege of being able to import without exporting in equal measure, to fund wars without raising taxes, to live beyond one's means without consequence — these were privileges no other nation possessed, and France had decided it would no longer fund them by holding American paper. In June 1967 France withdrew from the London Gold Pool, the multilateral arrangement that had been propping up the $35 peg. In March 1968 the Pool collapsed. By 1970 the United States had ten billion dollars in gold against forty billion in dollar liabilities to foreign central banks. The system Triffin had warned about was, on the public record, mathematically broken.

The Lyndon Johnson administration knew this. In 1965 — five years after Triffin's testimony — Johnson had quietly established what came to be called the Volcker Working Group, named for its chairman **Paul Volcker**, then Undersecretary of the Treasury for International Monetary Affairs. The group's task was to study what to do when the dollar-gold system failed. Volcker worked on this question, with his deputy George Willis and a small group of Treasury and Federal Reserve officials, for the next six years. The Group's archives, declassified and analysed by the sociologists Christoffer Zoeller and Nina Bandelj in their 2019 *Socius* paper, show that by January 1971 a definite plan had been settled: end the dollar's convertibility to gold, devalue against major currencies, do it unilaterally without consulting allies, and use a moment of "crisis" to provide political cover for what had been internally decided already.

The man who pushed the plan up to the President in January 1971 was **Pete Peterson**, then Nixon's Assistant for International Economic Affairs. Peterson is a figure worth pausing on. He would, a few years later, become Secretary of Commerce. He would, in 1985, co-found the Blackstone Group with Stephen Schwarzman. Blackstone is now, as of 2026, the largest alternative asset manager on earth, with one trillion dollars in assets under management. The man who recommended closing the gold window to Nixon in January 1971 went on to build the largest private equity firm of the post-gold-standard era. The names that matter recur.

In March 1971, while public officials continued to insist that the gold-dollar peg was sacrosanct, the Treasury Department conducted secret legal studies on how to close the gold window without congressional approval. They found their answer in section 8 of the 1934 Gold Reserve Act: the President, acting through the Treasury Secretary, could simply suspend gold sales. Section 5 of the Bretton Woods Agreement Act of 1945, which required congressional authorisation for any change in the dollar's par value, would be circumvented by interpreting the action as a suspension rather than a devaluation. The legal preparation was complete in March. The political opportunity took five more months to arrive.

## III. Camp David, 13–15 August 1971

In the second week of August 1971 a wave of speculative pressure against the dollar, intensified by a report that Britain was about to demand gold for its dollar reserves, gave the administration the occasion it had been waiting for. On Friday 13 August Nixon convened a meeting at the presidential retreat at Camp David. Sixteen men attended. Conspicuously absent: the Secretary of State, William Rogers, and the National Security Adviser, Henry Kissinger. The decision to remake the global monetary order was made deliberately without the State Department.

The principal participants were:

- **Richard Nixon**, President.
- **John Connally**, Secretary of the Treasury. Former Texas Governor, Lyndon Johnson's protégé, not an economist. Chosen by Nixon precisely because he was a political brawler. His private view, captured on the White House tapes, was that foreign reactions to American monetary decisions were not America's problem.
- **Paul Volcker**, Undersecretary of the Treasury for International Monetary Affairs. The technical architect, who had been preparing the contingency for six years.
- **Arthur Burns**, Federal Reserve Chairman. Brought to give the institutional cover of the Fed.
- **George Shultz**, Director of the Office of Management and Budget. Milton Friedman's student. He wanted the system to go to floating exchange rates permanently — which, eventually, it did.
- **Peter Peterson**, the man who had pushed the original plan in January, was there as Assistant to the President for International Economic Affairs.
- **H. R. Haldeman**, Chief of Staff.
- **William Safire**, speechwriter, who would later write the standard insider account.

They spent the weekend at Camp David — Friday afternoon to Sunday evening — drafting the announcement. There were arguments about specifics. Burns was uneasy. Volcker had personal regrets about ending the system he had helped manage. Connally, by contemporaneous account, wanted to go further, harder, and faster than the others. Shultz wanted to go to floating rates immediately. The compromise package included:

- Suspension of dollar-gold convertibility ("temporary," as Nixon explicitly stated).
- A 10 percent surcharge on all dutiable imports.
- A 90-day freeze on domestic wages and prices.
- Tax cuts to stimulate the economy.

On the evening of Sunday 15 August 1971, Nixon went on television. He preempted the broadcast of *Bonanza* — the most-watched programme on American television that year — to deliver the announcement. He framed it as a defence of the dollar against "international speculators." He stated that the suspension of gold convertibility was temporary. He did not say that the Treasury's own legal studies in March had laid the groundwork. He did not say that the Volcker Working Group had been preparing this contingency since 1965. He did not say that the United States was unilaterally ending a system that forty-three other Allied nations had signed in 1944 and that no consultation with any of them had taken place. He did not say that the State Department had been excluded from the decision.

The next day, the leaders of every other industrialised economy on earth woke up to discover that the monetary order their countries had operated under since 1944 had been ended by a televised announcement from an American president, on a Sunday evening, preempting a Western. The reaction abroad was furious. Foreign central banks, holding hundreds of billions of dollars they could no longer redeem in gold, faced a choice between accepting whatever dollar value the United States chose to defend and watching their reserves evaporate. Connally, dispatched to Europe to negotiate a new exchange-rate regime, summed up the American position in the line that became his epitaph: "The dollar may be our currency, but it is your problem."

The Smithsonian Agreement of December 1971 attempted to patch the system together at new fixed exchange rates, with the dollar devalued and gold revalued to $38, then $42.22. It collapsed within fifteen months. By March 1973 the major currencies were floating against the dollar and the dollar was floating against gold. The "temporary" suspension of August 1971 became permanent. The dollar had become pure fiat. The largest international monetary system in human history had been transformed by a small group of men over a weekend at a Maryland retreat without the consent or even the consultation of any other party to the system. This is the kind of decision an empire makes when it has stopped pretending to need permission. This is what the gold reserves at Bretton Woods had been hiding for twenty-six years.

## IV. The Petrodollar, July 1974

The Nixon shock created an immediate problem. Without gold backing, why would any country accept dollars in payment for real goods? The exorbitant privilege had been backed by the promise of convertibility. Once convertibility was gone, the privilege was a gentleman's agreement with no enforcement mechanism. The question was: what replaces gold as the thing that makes the dollar essential?

The answer, worked out over two and a half years and not publicly disclosed for forty-one years, was oil.

The OPEC oil embargo of October 1973, imposed in response to American military support for Israel during the Yom Kippur War, quadrupled crude prices. Inflation soared, the American stock market crashed, and the United States economy by the spring of 1974 was in serious recession. The newly appointed Treasury Secretary was **William Simon**, a former Salomon Brothers bond trader who had served as Nixon's energy czar during the embargo. In July 1974 Simon and his deputy **Gerald Parsky** flew to Jeddah, Saudi Arabia, on a trip officially billed as a two-week tour of economic diplomacy across Europe and the Middle East. The real mission was a single deal with the Saudi royal family.

The deal was negotiated personally with King Faisal bin Abdulaziz Al Saud over weeks of meetings. The framework, in the words of Parsky's later account to Bloomberg News, was "strikingly simple." The United States would buy Saudi oil. The United States would supply Saudi Arabia with military aid, advanced weaponry, and the security guarantee of the most powerful military in the world. In return, Saudi Arabia would recycle its petrodollar revenues — the dollars it received for oil — back into United States Treasury securities, financing American government deficits indefinitely. Saudi Arabia would price its oil exclusively in dollars, ensuring that every other oil-importing country in the world would need dollar reserves to buy it.

The other condition was secrecy. King Faisal personally demanded that Saudi Treasury holdings be kept confidential. They were. For forty-one years.

This is documented. The cable describing the secrecy clause is preserved at the National Archives. It was obtained by the Bloomberg News reporter **Andrea Wong** under a Freedom of Information Act request and published on 30 May 2016 in an article titled "The Untold Story Behind Saudi Arabia's 41-Year U.S. Debt Secret." Until that month — May 2016 — the United States Treasury had refused to break out Saudi Arabia's holdings of US Treasury securities, instead aggregating them with thirteen other oil exporters in a single line item. The breakout was provided in May 2016 in response to Wong's FOIA request, ending four decades of deliberate concealment of how American sovereign debt was financed.

This is conspiracy in the precise sense. The Treasury Secretary of the United States, acting under the President, made a secret agreement with the King of a foreign nation, withheld from Congress, withheld from the American public, withheld from the financial markets, that would determine the structure of the global monetary system for half a century. The secrecy was demanded by the foreign party and granted by the American party because both parties understood that public knowledge of the arrangement would have political consequences for both. The American public would have asked why their government was running unlimited deficits financed by a foreign autocracy in exchange for protecting that autocracy with American military power. The Arab world would have asked why the leading Arab oil producer was financing American budget deficits while the American military was simultaneously underwriting the State of Israel. Neither question was asked because neither side admitted the arrangement existed.

**Henry Kissinger**, as Secretary of State, provided the diplomatic cover. In June 1974 he signed the US-Saudi Joint Commission on Economic Cooperation, a public document that legitimised the broader US-Saudi relationship without disclosing the financial arrangement that was its core. Kissinger has spent the half-century since 1974 being celebrated as the master diplomat of the Cold War. The deal that Simon was negotiating in Jeddah while Kissinger was signing the Commission in Washington was the deal that made the post-1971 dollar reserve system possible. Without it, the Nixon shock would have produced a slow collapse of dollar primacy by the late 1970s. With it, the dollar's role expanded for fifty more years. Kissinger has never publicly acknowledged the arrangement. He has never had to.

## V. What had now been built

By the end of 1974 the architecture was complete. The United States had:

- A central bank, the Federal Reserve, designed in 1910 by six men on a private island and ratified by Congress in 1913, owned by its member commercial banks and controlled by the same circle that owned them.
- A global monetary system, designed in 1944 by a Treasury official who was simultaneously a Soviet asset, that gave the United States reserve-currency privilege backed by gold.
- A unilateral exit from the gold backing, executed in 1971 over a weekend at Camp David, that converted the dollar into pure fiat without consultation with any other sovereign.
- A replacement backing for the dollar, agreed in secret with the Saudi royal family in 1974, that pegged global oil pricing to the dollar in exchange for American military protection and Saudi recycling of revenues into American debt.

The combined effect was that the United States, alone among nations, could create the world's reserve currency at zero marginal cost, lend it to the rest of the world at interest, and require any country that wished to import oil to hold its currency. The American government could run permanent deficits because foreign central banks were structurally compelled to absorb its debt. The American consumer could import without producing because the global trading system required dollars and dollars came from American exports of nothing. The architecture turned the United States into something no nation had ever been: the issuer of the world's money, the protector of the world's largest oil producer, and the beneficiary of an arrangement that taxed every transaction on earth at the seigniorage rate of dollar creation.

There was a cost. The cost was that the American manufacturing base had to be sacrificed. A reserve-currency country cannot sustain large export industries against the structural overvaluation that reserve status imposes. The cost was that the American working class had to be sacrificed. The cost was that the American political system had to be progressively rebuilt to serve the dollar's global role rather than the country's domestic economy. The Reagan revolution of the 1980s, the financialisation of the 1990s, the deindustrialisation of the 2000s, the bailouts of 2008–2009 — these were not accidents. They were the downstream consequences of an architectural choice made between 1944 and 1974 by named men in named rooms.

The next chapter is about how the people who built the architecture lived inside it. About what they did with the unlimited capital the Eurodollar market gave them. About how the four institutions that now control half the publicly traded equity on earth — BlackRock, Vanguard, State Street, and the largest private equity firms — emerged from the same circle that had been writing the rules since 1913. About the consolidation of the modern oligarchy.

The names will be different. The pattern will not.


---

# Chapter 6: The Long Counter-Revolution

In 1945, the American working class was the richest working class in human history. Manufacturing wages had tripled in real terms since 1933. Union density was one in three private-sector workers. The federal government had built, during the war, a productive capacity larger than the rest of the world combined. The political settlement of the New Deal — Social Security, the Wagner Act, progressive income taxation with top marginal rates above 90 percent, deposit insurance, banking regulation under Glass-Steagall, securities regulation under the SEC — was understood across both major parties to be permanent. Even Eisenhower, the Republican President of the 1950s, governed within it and called those who wanted to dismantle it "a few Texas oil millionaires and an occasional politician or businessman from other areas" whose number was "negligible and they are stupid."

By 2008, sixty years later, the picture had been reversed. Union density was below 10 percent and falling. The top marginal income tax rate had been cut to 35 percent. Glass-Steagall had been repealed. The savings of three generations of working Americans had been transferred to a financial sector that, having destroyed itself in a credit bubble of its own creation, was rescued by the public Treasury without a single executive being prosecuted. The wage-productivity gap that had opened in 1979 had become a chasm: American workers were producing twice as much per hour as they had been in 1973 but earning, in real terms, less. The wealth distribution had returned to where it had stood before the New Deal. The middle class had been hollowed.

The official story is that this happened because of "globalisation" — a passive economic force, like weather. The real story is that it happened because a small group of named men, beginning in 1947 in a Swiss hotel, decided to make it happen. They funded the project for sixty years. They built the institutions to carry the project. They captured the universities, the think tanks, the courts, the regulatory agencies, the political parties, and the press. They installed their personnel. They wrote the laws. They organised the bailouts. They are still in the same positions. This section is about them.

## I. Mont Pèlerin, April 1947

From 1 to 10 April 1947, thirty-nine men gathered at the Hotel du Parc in the Swiss village of Mont Pèlerin, on the slopes above Lake Geneva. The meeting had been convened by **Friedrich Hayek**, an Austrian-born economist then teaching at the London School of Economics. Hayek had published *The Road to Serfdom* in 1944, an argument that the welfare-state policies then being implemented across the Western democracies would, by economic and political logic, lead inexorably to totalitarian dictatorship. The argument was widely understood at the time, including by sympathetic readers, as overstated. The book had nonetheless been condensed by *Reader's Digest* in April 1945 and distributed in millions of copies across the United States, where it had made Hayek a household name in American business circles and provided him with the platform to convene the Mont Pèlerin meeting.

The men who came included **Milton Friedman**, then a young economist at the University of Chicago; **Ludwig von Mises**, the senior Austrian-school theorist; **Karl Popper**, the philosopher of science; **George Stigler**, who would later win the Nobel Prize for his work on regulatory capture (largely by demonstrating its inevitability and thereby justifying its existence); **Frank Knight**, the senior Chicago economist; the French Nobel laureate **Maurice Allais**; the German ordoliberal **Wilhelm Röpke**; the British economist **Lionel Robbins**. Thirty-nine in total, from nine countries. They constituted, in 1947, an intellectual minority that the post-war consensus regarded as cranks. They knew it. They had come to do something about it.

The meeting's stated objective, in Hayek's words, was to "create a society of like-minded scholars who could pursue, through the methods of academic discussion and learned publication, the cause of liberal civilisation." The unstated objective was to design and disseminate a coherent alternative to the social-democratic consensus that had emerged from the Second World War — an alternative that would eventually replace that consensus in the institutions, the academies, the policy machines, and the political parties of the Western democracies.

The project was funded from the start. The **William Volker Fund** of Kansas City, established with the fortune of Harold Luhnow's father William Volker (a furniture-store magnate), paid for Hayek's chair at the University of Chicago in 1950 and Mises's at New York University. The **Earhart Foundation** and the **Relm Foundation** provided fellowships and travel money. By the 1970s the financing had expanded enormously: the **Olin Foundation** (chemical industry money), the **Bradley Foundation** (Allen-Bradley electronics money), the **Scaife Foundations** (Mellon banking and steel money), and the **Koch family foundations** (oil and chemical money from Fred Koch's Soviet oil refinery contracts and later from his sons Charles and David's privately held Koch Industries) were collectively spending tens of millions of dollars a year, and eventually hundreds of millions, to fund the Mont Pèlerin network and its institutional offshoots.

This is the first thing to understand. The neoliberal counter-revolution was not a popular intellectual current that won an argument. It was a privately funded ideological project, capitalised by a small number of extremely wealthy industrial families, beginning seventy-eight years ago and continuously funded since. The funders were specific named people. Their motivations were not theoretical. They wanted lower taxes on their inherited fortunes, weaker regulation of their industries, weaker unions in their factories, and a press and academic establishment that would frame their interests as those of the country. They got everything they paid for. The price tag, across seven decades, is the wealth of the Western working class.

## II. The University of Chicago

The University of Chicago became the project's intellectual factory. Hayek arrived in 1950, funded by the Volker Fund through the Committee on Social Thought (the economics department had refused to hire him on academic grounds). Friedman was already on the faculty. Stigler arrived in 1958. Gary Becker, who would extend market reasoning to every domain of human life including marriage and crime, arrived in 1968. Robert Lucas, who would build the rational-expectations theory that became the technical foundation for deregulation, arrived in 1975.

The Chicago School produced two things: a body of theory designed to demonstrate that government intervention in markets was always counterproductive, and a continuous supply of trained personnel — economists, lawyers, judges, central bankers, regulators — to operate the theory inside government. The theory and the personnel were both useful. The theory was useful for justifying outcomes that benefited the funders. The personnel were useful for delivering those outcomes once installed in office. By the late 1970s, Chicago alumni and Chicago-influenced economists held positions in the Federal Reserve, the Treasury, the Council of Economic Advisers, the World Bank, the IMF, and the leading central banks and finance ministries of the developed world. The intellectual hegemony had been achieved.

It had been achieved on funding from the same circle that had funded Mont Pèlerin. This is not a coincidence and the relationship is not hidden in the record. Charles Koch in particular has been openly explicit, in materials his own foundations have published, that his funding of academic economics is intended to produce specific policy outputs. The Koch Foundation funds professorships, fellowships, curricula, and graduate programs at hundreds of American universities. The funding comes with conditions. Some of the conditions are documented in the contracts. The condition is, in short, that the academics produce work that supports the funder's policy positions and train students who will go on to occupy positions in government where they will implement those positions. This is purchased academic labour. It is now the dominant model in American economics departments. It is one of the reasons American economists are predominantly right-of-centre in their policy views despite being predominantly left-of-centre in their personal voting. The personal views are irrelevant. The funding determines the published output.

## III. The Powell Memo, August 1971

While Hayek and Friedman were building the ideological superstructure at Chicago, the American business class was, by the late 1960s, getting nervous. Ralph Nader's *Unsafe at Any Speed* (1965) had triggered a wave of consumer-protection legislation. The Environmental Protection Agency had been founded in 1970 (under Nixon). The Occupational Safety and Health Administration was founded in 1970. The Consumer Product Safety Commission was founded in 1972. Corporate America perceived itself as under siege from a regulatory state that was restraining its ability to externalise costs onto workers, consumers, and the environment. Something had to be done.

What was done can be dated precisely to 23 August 1971. On that day, a Richmond, Virginia, corporate lawyer named **Lewis F. Powell Jr.** mailed a confidential memorandum to **Eugene B. Sydnor Jr.**, Director of Education at the United States Chamber of Commerce. The memorandum was titled *Attack on American Free Enterprise System*. Powell was at the time on the boards of eleven corporations, including Philip Morris, where he had served as director since 1964. He was counsel to the Tobacco Institute. He was the former president of the American Bar Association.

The memo, six thousand words long, is one of the most influential documents in modern American political history. Its argument, in summary, was that corporate America was losing the political war and needed to organise itself, with funding and persistence, to retake the institutions of American public life. The memo named specific targets:

- **The universities**: Powell recommended that corporations fund pro-business scholars, build endowments tied to pro-business curricula, place pro-business speakers on campuses, and monitor textbooks and faculty.
- **The media**: Powell recommended that corporations monitor television networks and major newspapers, fund pro-business journalism, and pressure broadcasters who carried critical coverage.
- **The courts**: Powell wrote, in the line most often quoted from the memo, "Under our constitutional system, especially with an activist-minded Supreme Court, the judiciary may be the most important instrument for social, economic and political change." He recommended that the Chamber hire "a highly competent staff of lawyers" to influence cases — and added, in a critical conditional, "if business is willing to provide the funds."
- **The political system**: Powell recommended that corporations dramatically increase lobbying expenditures, support pro-business candidates of both parties, and "penalise politically" those who opposed business interests.

The memo was confidential. It was not made public at the time it was written. Two months after writing it — on 21 October 1971 — Powell was nominated to the Supreme Court of the United States by President Richard Nixon. He was confirmed by the Senate on 6 December 1971 by a vote of 89 to 1. The single dissenting vote was Oklahoma Democrat Fred Harris, who called Powell "an elitist." The Senate Judiciary Committee was not informed during the confirmation hearings that Powell had, ten weeks earlier, drafted a private blueprint for corporate political reconquest of the American legal and political system. The Senate did not know. The public did not know.

The memo was eventually leaked in 1972, after Powell had taken the bench, to the syndicated columnist **Jack Anderson** of the *Washington Post*. Anderson wrote about it. By then it was too late for Senate review. Powell sat on the Supreme Court for sixteen years. In 1978 he wrote the majority opinion in *First National Bank of Boston v. Bellotti*, which established for the first time that corporations had First Amendment "speech" rights to spend money on political advocacy. The doctrinal foundation of *Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission* (2010) — the decision that opened American elections to unlimited corporate political spending — was laid in *Bellotti* by the same Lewis Powell who, seven years earlier, had told the Chamber of Commerce that the courts were "the most important instrument for social, economic, and political change," provided "business was willing to provide the funds."

This is not theory. It is the documented timeline. A corporate lawyer wrote a private memo telling business how to take over the courts. Two months later he was put on the highest court. He then issued the decisions that did what the memo had recommended. The Senate did not know about the memo when it confirmed him. The public did not know until after he was on the bench. The memo is now in the public archives at Washington and Lee University and has been the subject of scholarly study for forty years. The Chamber of Commerce, following the memo's recommendations, expanded from 60,000 member firms in 1972 to over 250,000 by 1982 and now spends one billion dollars a year on political activity.

## IV. The Think Tanks

The Powell Memo did not create the network of right-wing think tanks. The network of right-wing think tanks was already being created when Powell wrote his memo. But the memo gave the project a focused doctrine and the explicit endorsement of one of the senior members of the American legal establishment. After 1971 the project accelerated.

- **The Heritage Foundation** was founded on 16 February 1973 by **Paul Weyrich** and **Edwin Feulner** with a $250,000 founding donation from **Joseph Coors** of Coors Brewing. Subsequent funding came from the Scaife and Olin foundations. Heritage's role was to produce ready-to-implement policy proposals that an incoming conservative administration could enact in its first hundred days. Heritage's 1980 *Mandate for Leadership* served as the explicit policy blueprint for the Reagan administration; 60 percent of its recommendations were implemented or attempted. Heritage's 2024 *Project 2025* serves the analogous function for the second Trump administration.

- **The Cato Institute** was founded in 1977 by **Charles Koch** with **Edward Crane** and **Murray Rothbard**. It was originally named the Charles Koch Foundation. It has been continuously funded by Koch since its founding. It is the libertarian wing of the network.

- **The Manhattan Institute** was founded in 1977 by Antony Fisher and **William J. Casey**. Casey would become Director of Central Intelligence under Ronald Reagan in January 1981, four years after co-founding the think tank. The think tank that produced the policing-economics theory of the Giuliani administration in New York City was co-founded by a man who simultaneously ran American foreign intelligence. The interlocking of the think-tank network with the intelligence community is not theoretical. It is biographical.

- **The Atlas Network**, founded in 1981 by **Sir Antony Fisher** (who had founded the Institute of Economic Affairs in London in 1955), is the international franchise system. It coordinates five hundred affiliated free-market think tanks across one hundred countries. The Atlas Network in 2026 is the largest single funder of intellectual right-wing politics worldwide. It is, with the Mont Pèlerin Society itself, the parent body of the global neoliberal infrastructure.

- **The Federalist Society** was founded in 1982 at Yale Law School and the University of Chicago Law School. By the early 2000s it had become the institutional pipeline through which every Republican-appointed federal judge passed. Every conservative justice currently on the Supreme Court — John Roberts, Clarence Thomas, Samuel Alito, Neil Gorsuch, Brett Kavanaugh, Amy Coney Barrett — is a Federalist Society member or close affiliate. The Society's effective director, **Leonard Leo**, has functioned for the past two decades as the gatekeeper of conservative judicial selection in the United States. He was the principal advisor on Supreme Court nominations to the first Trump administration. He has reportedly received over a billion dollars in dark-money funding through the Marble Freedom Trust, the largest single political donation in American history, given to him personally in 2021 by Chicago industrialist Barre Seid. One man controls the pipeline. He controls the appointments. He controls the doctrine that the appointees apply. He is funded, personally, by a billion dollars in untraceable industrial money. The American judicial system has been privatised.

The pattern is the pattern. Named men. Named institutions. Funded by named industrial fortunes. Continuous coordinated activity over five decades. Documented in the public record. Treated by the mainstream press as ordinary participation in democratic politics rather than as what it is, which is the systematic capture of a republic by its wealthiest class.

## V. Chile, 11 September 1973

Two years after the Powell Memo, the activeisation of the project moved from American think tanks to overseas territory. The case study was Chile.

On 4 September 1970 the Chilean people elected **Salvador Allende**, a democratic socialist running on a Popular Unity coalition. Allende's program included nationalisation of Chile's copper industry (largely owned by American firms including Anaconda and Kennecott), agrarian reform, and the construction of a Chilean welfare state. The American response was immediate and explicit. National Security Adviser **Henry Kissinger** said, in a National Security Council meeting in 1970: "I don't see why we need to stand by and watch a country go communist due to the irresponsibility of its people. The issues are much too important for the Chilean voters to be left to decide for themselves." President **Richard Nixon** ordered Director of Central Intelligence **Richard Helms** to "make the economy scream." Helms's handwritten notes from that 15 September 1970 meeting were declassified during the Church Committee hearings in 1975 and are now in the public record at the National Security Archive.

The CIA spent the next three years executing the order. The Agency funded opposition newspapers (principally Agustín Edwards's *El Mercurio*), paid Chilean truckers to strike, paid Chilean copper miners to strike, paid Chilean shopkeepers to close, organised propaganda operations, and coordinated with the Chilean military. The operation cost eight million dollars in known funding. On 11 September 1973 the Chilean military, under General **Augusto Pinochet**, executed the coup. Allende died in the presidential palace, apparently by suicide as the building was bombed around him. The democratically elected government of Chile was destroyed.

What was installed in its place was the first laboratory of operational neoliberalism. Twenty-five Chilean economists, most of them trained at the University of Chicago under Milton Friedman and Arnold Harberger through a US State Department–funded partnership with the Catholic University of Chile that had been running since the late 1950s, were brought into the new junta government as ministers and technical advisers. They were known as **the Chicago Boys**. They had, before the coup, prepared a 189-page economic blueprint called **El Ladrillo** ("The Brick"). Some of the research for The Brick had been funded by the CIA, as documented in Church Committee testimony. They had the plan ready. They had been waiting for the coup.

What they then did to Chile was the prototype of what would, across the following four decades, be done to dozens of other countries through the IMF's structural adjustment programs. Privatisation of state enterprises. Privatisation of pensions (Chile became the first country to privatise its public pension system, in 1980). Removal of price controls. Removal of capital controls. Crushing of labour unions. Cuts to government spending on health, education, and welfare. The result, by 1975, was a real wage decline of 40 percent for Chilean workers, a collapse of domestic industry, and a transfer of wealth from labour to capital of historic magnitude. The Pinochet regime simultaneously killed 3,000 to 4,000 Chileans, tortured tens of thousands more, and forced 200,000 into exile. The economic policy and the political terror were not separate. They were the same project. You cannot impose Chicago School economic policy on a society without political terror, because the policy lowers living standards for the majority of the population, and the majority of the population will resist if it is permitted to. The Chicago Boys understood this. The CIA understood this. The Friedman School at Chicago understood this. The policy required the dictatorship. The dictatorship was the policy.

**Milton Friedman** travelled personally to Chile in March 1975 and met with Pinochet. He recommended "shock treatment." He subsequently spent the rest of his life denying that he had advised the regime. The denials are not credible. Friedman's letters to Pinochet are in the public record at the Hoover Institution archives. The advice was specific. The implementation was direct. The Nobel laureate who had spent thirty years arguing that economic freedom and political freedom were inseparable became, when offered the opportunity, the technical advisor to a regime that disappeared its citizens by helicopter into the Pacific Ocean. The contradiction did not trouble him.

**Orlando Letelier**, who had served as Allende's ambassador to the United States and Minister of Defence, escaped Chile after the coup and went to Washington, DC, where he worked at the Institute for Policy Studies. On 28 August 1976 he published in *The Nation* an essay titled *The Chicago Boys in Chile: Economic Freedom's Awful Toll*, naming the Chicago School and Milton Friedman as the intellectual authors of what the Pinochet regime was doing. Twenty-four days later, on 21 September 1976, Letelier was killed by a car bomb on Embassy Row in Washington, DC, two miles from the White House. The bombing was carried out by Chilean DINA secret-police agents under Operation Condor — the multinational state-terror network coordinated between the military regimes of Chile, Argentina, Brazil, Bolivia, Paraguay, and Uruguay, with American knowledge. It was one of the few foreign-government assassinations on American soil in the twentieth century. The American man Letelier was working with, **Ronni Moffitt**, also died in the bombing. She was twenty-five. The Pinochet regime denied involvement; the DINA agent who arranged the bombing, **Michael Townley**, was extradited to the United States in 1978 and pleaded guilty.

The Chilean coup is the moment the post-war project showed its hand. The same network of Mont Pèlerin economists and Chicago Boys who had spent the 1950s and 1960s publishing academic papers about the virtues of economic freedom now turned out to be willing to operate the levers of an actual military dictatorship for the privilege of doing the policy experiment. They had been waiting for the dictatorship. When it came, they had the binder ready. When the exiled Foreign Minister of the previous government wrote about what they were doing, they did not refute him. They had him killed.

## VI. The Volcker Shock, 1979

Six years after Chile, the same project was implemented in the United States itself, by less violent means.

**Paul Volcker** was appointed Chairman of the Federal Reserve Board by President Jimmy Carter in August 1979. He was the same Paul Volcker who had run the secret group that planned the Nixon Shock eight years earlier. He took office at a moment of double-digit consumer price inflation. He decided, with the support of the Carter administration and then the incoming Reagan administration, to break inflation by collapsing demand — that is, by collapsing employment.

Between October 1979 and June 1981, Volcker raised the federal funds rate from 11 percent to 20 percent. He held it there. Unemployment in November 1982 reached 10.8 percent, the highest since the Great Depression. Manufacturing employment in the American Midwest collapsed and never recovered; the deindustrialisation that has defined American political life since the 1980s dates from the Volcker shock. Latin American countries that had borrowed dollar-denominated debt in the 1970s at low rates found themselves servicing that debt, in 1982, at triple the cost. They could not pay. Mexico defaulted in August 1982. Argentina, Brazil, and most of the rest of the continent followed. The IMF moved in with the structural adjustment programs that had been pioneered in Chile.

The official story is that Volcker broke inflation. He did. The unofficial story, which is the truer story, is that Volcker broke American labour. The Bureau of Labor Statistics's own data show that the wage-productivity relationship in the American economy — wages had tracked productivity from 1948 to 1973, rising together — decoupled in 1979 and has not reconverged since. Productivity has doubled. Real wages for the median American worker have, in real terms, slightly declined. The gap is the seigniorage that has been extracted from American labour since 1979 and transferred to American capital. The Volcker shock is when that gap opened. It opened on purpose. It opened because the existing wage-price spiral of the 1970s could only be broken by destroying the bargaining power of organised labour, and destroying the bargaining power of organised labour required mass unemployment, and mass unemployment required engineered recession. Volcker engineered the recession. The transfer began.

Reagan, taking office in January 1981, sealed the transfer politically. On 3 August 1981 the air traffic controllers' union, PATCO, went on strike. Reagan ordered them back to work within 48 hours. They refused. On 5 August he fired all 11,345 striking controllers and banned them from federal employment for life. The legal pretext was a 1955 statute prohibiting strikes by federal employees. The political message was that the United States government would now break, by force if necessary, any organised labour action that interfered with capital's interests. American private-sector union density, which had been 23 percent in 1980, fell to 6 percent over the subsequent four decades. It has not recovered.

The British equivalent occurred during the Miners' Strike of 1984–1985, in which **Margaret Thatcher**'s government used militarised police, surveillance, and the financial freezing of the National Union of Mineworkers to break the strike and, with it, the organised industrial working class of the United Kingdom. Thatcher had been distributing copies of Hayek's *Constitution of Liberty* to her cabinet from her first day in office in 1979 with the words, recorded by her biographer Hugo Young, "This is what we believe." The intellectual project that had begun in a Swiss hotel in 1947 had, by 1985, control of the British state.

## VII. Deregulation, 1982–2000

The remaining work was the dismantling of the New Deal financial architecture.

The **Garn–St. Germain Depository Institutions Act**, signed by Reagan on 15 October 1982, deregulated the American savings and loan industry. S&Ls were freed from the interest-rate caps that had constrained them since the 1930s and permitted to make commercial loans for the first time. The result was the savings and loan crisis of 1986–1995: 1,043 institutions failed, the federal government bailed out depositors at a total cost of $160 billion (in 1995 dollars), and the crisis served as the first proof of concept that the financial sector could externalise its losses onto the public Treasury while privatising its gains. The lesson was learnt. It would be applied at much larger scale in 2008.

The **Gramm–Leach–Bliley Act**, signed by President Bill Clinton on 12 November 1999, repealed the Glass-Steagall Act of 1933. Glass-Steagall had since the New Deal kept commercial banks — institutions that held the public's deposits — separated from investment banks that engaged in speculative securities trading. After Gramm–Leach–Bliley they could merge. They merged. The bill was pushed through Congress by Treasury Secretary **Robert Rubin** (who had run Goldman Sachs from 1990 to 1992 before joining the Clinton administration and who would, within weeks of the bill's passage, leave Treasury to join Citigroup as senior counsellor, a position in which he would earn $126 million over the next nine years and from which he would help oversee Citigroup's near-collapse in the 2008 crisis), Treasury Deputy Secretary **Larry Summers**, and Senator **Phil Gramm** of Texas (whose wife Wendy was a director of Enron, a fact that would also become relevant). The repeal of Glass-Steagall was a direct precondition for the conglomeration of the financial sector that produced the 2008 crisis. It was supported by a Democratic president, drafted in part by a former Goldman CEO, and signed in the same year that its author received his future Citigroup compensation package.

The **Commodity Futures Modernization Act**, signed by Clinton on 21 December 2000 in the closing days of his administration, exempted credit default swaps and most over-the-counter derivatives from regulation by either the Commodity Futures Trading Commission or the Securities and Exchange Commission. The bill was, in effect, drafted by Robert Rubin, Larry Summers, and Federal Reserve Chairman **Alan Greenspan**. Two years earlier the chair of the CFTC, **Brooksley Born**, had warned in a series of public statements that the unregulated derivatives market posed systemic risk to the global financial system. Rubin, Summers, and Greenspan jointly attacked her in public. They pressured her resignation. They then passed the bill that forbade her successor from regulating the market she had warned about. Eight years later the unregulated derivatives market she had warned about produced the largest financial crisis in human history.

These are not separate events. They are the same project, executed by overlapping personnel, across two political parties, over twenty years. Robert Rubin was the linchpin. He was Goldman Sachs before he was Treasury. He was Treasury before he was Citigroup. He moved between the three institutions and the policies that benefited the three institutions moved with him. The personnel of the project, like the funding of the project, are not theoretical. They are biographical. They are named men.

## VIII. 2008

The 2008 crisis is the moment the project showed its hand again, now on the largest scale of all.

The collapse began on 14 March 2008 with Bear Stearns. Treasury Secretary **Henry Paulson** — former CEO of Goldman Sachs, 1999–2006 — and New York Fed President **Timothy Geithner** brokered a deal under which JPMorgan acquired Bear Stearns with a $30 billion backstop from the Federal Reserve. Six months later, on 15 September 2008, Lehman Brothers failed. The decision to let Lehman fail, made by Paulson, is one of the most controversial of the crisis. Lehman was Goldman Sachs's principal competitor. The day after Lehman failed, on 16 September, AIG was bailed out with an initial $85 billion loan, eventually growing to $182 billion. The single largest beneficiary of the AIG bailout was Goldman Sachs, which received $12.9 billion in credit default swap counterparty payments at par — that is, at full face value rather than at the discount the market would have imposed in AIG's actual insolvency.

The former Goldman CEO, as Treasury Secretary, allowed Goldman's principal competitor to fail and then used $182 billion in public money to make Goldman whole on its derivative contracts. This is documented. It was investigated by the Special Inspector General for TARP. The conflict of interest is not theoretical. It is documented.

On 19 September 2008, Paulson submitted to Congress a three-page memorandum requesting $700 billion in emergency authority. Section 8 of the proposed legislation read: "Decisions by the Secretary pursuant to the authority of this Act are non-reviewable and committed to agency discretion, and may not be reviewed by any court of law or any administrative agency." That is, the former Goldman Sachs CEO was asking Congress to give him $700 billion with no judicial review of how he spent it. After modifications, the Emergency Economic Stabilization Act was passed and signed on 3 October 2008. The Troubled Asset Relief Program — TARP — began disbursing on 14 October.

What was publicly disclosed at the time was the $700 billion. What was not disclosed was that the Federal Reserve was simultaneously making, in secret, an additional $7.77 trillion in emergency loans, guarantees, and asset purchases to support the banking system. The existence of these loans was not disclosed to Congress at the time the TARP vote was being taken. It was not disclosed to the public for three years. It was finally disclosed in November 2011, after Bloomberg News won a Freedom of Information Act lawsuit against the Federal Reserve. The Bloomberg story, *Secret Fed Loans Gave Banks $13 Billion Undisclosed to Congress*, published 27 November 2011, documented that the Fed's emergency commitments had peaked at $7.77 trillion in March 2009 and that the six largest American banks had drawn down $460 billion of those loans, on which they had earned $13 billion in interest income by lending the borrowed money back out at higher rates. The American Congress voted on a $700 billion bailout while the Federal Reserve was simultaneously, in secret, deploying ten times that amount.

This is conspiracy in the precise sense. The senior monetary authority of the United States carried out the largest financial intervention in American history without disclosing its scale to the legislature that was simultaneously voting on the visible portion of the intervention. The disclosure happened three years later because a single news organisation filed a lawsuit and won. Had Bloomberg not filed, the public would not know. The lawsuit was opposed by the Federal Reserve to the Supreme Court, which declined to hear the Fed's appeal. The transparency the public eventually got was the transparency one news organisation forced through the courts against the active resistance of the institution it was investigating.

In the year that followed, exactly one American banker was prosecuted to conviction for conduct related to the financial crisis: **Kareem Serageldin**, a mid-level Credit Suisse trader, who served thirty months in a federal prison. No CEO of any major American bank was prosecuted. Compare: in Iceland, twenty-five senior bankers received prison sentences for crisis-related conduct. The Icelandic banking sector was the size of a single mid-tier American bank's mortgage book. Iceland prosecuted. The United States did not.

This is the system. The architects design the bubble. The bubble bursts. The public pays for the rescue. The architects keep their bonuses. The legal system protects them. The political system reseats them in the next administration. Henry Paulson went home. Robert Rubin had already gone home. Larry Summers went on to advise the Obama administration. Geithner became Obama's Treasury Secretary. Ben Bernanke remained Fed Chairman. Not one of the men responsible for the architecture of the crisis lost office, paid a fine, or went to prison.

## IX. What had now happened

Across sixty-one years, from the founding of Mont Pèlerin to the bailouts of 2008, the named circle had executed the project. They had:

- Built the intellectual infrastructure (Mont Pèlerin, Chicago).
- Captured the universities (Olin, Bradley, Scaife, Koch funding of professorships, departments, fellowships).
- Built the think-tank network (Heritage, Cato, Manhattan, AEI, Atlas, Federalist Society).
- Captured the courts (Powell, then forty years of Federalist Society judicial appointments).
- Tested the policy in laboratory conditions in Chile (1973–1990) and other Latin American and Eastern European countries.
- Engineered the destruction of organised labour in the United States and the United Kingdom (Volcker, PATCO, Thatcher).
- Repealed the New Deal financial regulations (Garn–St. Germain, Gramm–Leach–Bliley, Commodity Futures Modernization Act).
- Crashed the global financial system in 2008.
- Bailed themselves out using public funds with no accountability.
- Continued.

The wealth concentration that has resulted is the largest in human history. As of 2026, the top 0.1 percent of American households hold one fifth of the country's net wealth. The bottom 50 percent of American households hold 2.5 percent. The wage-productivity gap that opened in 1979 has continued widening. The American middle class, which constituted 61 percent of households in 1971, now constitutes 50 percent. Life expectancy in the United States has been declining since 2014 — the first sustained decline in life expectancy in a developed country since the post-Soviet collapse. The "deaths of despair" — opioid overdoses, suicides, alcoholic liver disease — that have driven the decline are concentrated in the working-class communities that the Volcker shock and its aftermath destroyed.

This is the system the next chapter describes in its current form: the modern oligarchy that the long counter-revolution has produced. The names are different. The pattern is the same. The four institutions that now control half of the publicly traded equity on earth — BlackRock, Vanguard, State Street, and the private equity sector — emerged from this circle. The technologists who now propose to rebuild the political order on Silicon Valley lines — Thiel, Musk, Andreessen, Karp — emerged from this circle. The artificial intelligence systems being deployed to manage labour, surveillance, and political opinion at scale — including the systems that mediate this very book — are owned by this circle.

The next chapter names them.


---

# Chapter 7: The Arithmetic

Everything in this book has been preparation for this chapter. The previous six chapters have named the people who built the system, the rooms where it was built, the moments at which the architecture was installed, and the political project that operationalised it across the modern world. Those chapters together form a historical case that the system is an artefact — not a natural condition of human economic life but a deliberate construction by named actors in their own interest.

The analysis makes the stronger argument that the case rests on. The stronger argument is not that the system is unfair, which is a value judgement and can be argued about. The stronger argument is that the system, on its own terms, by its own arithmetic, **cannot work**. It is internally incoherent. It is mathematically impossible. It is, for any species capable of arithmetic, recognisable as a trap. We have been prevented from recognising it as a trap by the same mechanisms that built it. But the recognition is available to any reader who can multiply.

The argument proceeds in three movements. The first is philosophical: the system is unjust at the level of first principles, by tests every major moral tradition has applied to economic life. The second is logical: the system is incoherent at the level of game theory and collective action; rational individuals locked inside it cannot rationally escape it. The third is mathematical, and is the load-bearing argument: by the elementary arithmetic of compound interest, the debt cannot be paid, the equality cannot be reached, the prosperity promised by trickle-down cannot trickle, and the system requires its own collapse on a timetable that can be calculated. The intelligent species that built this system is not the intelligent species that operates inside it. We are not failing the arithmetic. We are being prevented from doing it.

## I. The philosophical case

The Western moral tradition's first sustained treatment of money-getting through interest is in Aristotle's *Politics*, written around 350 BCE. The argument occupies a few paragraphs of Book I, chapter 10. It has not been seriously refuted in two and a half millennia. The argument is this. Money exists because of human convention; it is, in Aristotle's term, *nomisma* (from *nomos*, custom or law) — a token instituted by agreement to facilitate the exchange of real goods. Its purpose is to allow a person who has more shoes than they need to obtain wine from a person who has more wine than they need without the awkwardness of having to find someone who wants exactly that many shoes for exactly that much wine. Money is a medium. It exists, intrinsically, for the sake of something else.

Usury, Aristotle writes, attempts to use this medium for its own sake — to use money to produce more money, without passing through the production of any real good. The Greek word for interest is *tokos*, which also means "offspring" or "birth." Aristotle's argument turns on this etymological fact. Interest is money giving birth to money. But money is a sterile abstraction. It cannot biologically reproduce. The pretence that it does is the pretence that an abstract token has the generative powers of a living thing.
