The pretence is, in Aristotle's word, *paraphysin* — against nature. "Of all modes of getting wealth," he writes, "this is the most unnatural."

The argument was adopted by the Christian Church Fathers, by the Talmudic rabbis, by the Islamic jurists, and was the dominant moral teaching across all three Abrahamic civilisations for twelve hundred years. The prohibition was not theoretical. It was enforced. Lateran II in 1139, Lateran IV in 1215, the Council of Vienne in 1311 — all condemned usury, with increasing specificity. Christians who lent at interest were denied burial in consecrated ground. Jewish lending to Christians was, depending on the kingdom and the century, restricted, taxed, regulated, or simply allowed to exist as the legal exception that proved the moral rule. The prohibition was lifted across Europe not by a refutation of Aristotle's argument but by the slow accumulation of commercial pressures from the merchant cities of northern Italy and the Low Countries, by Calvin's theological compromise in 1545, and by the financial revolutions of the seventeenth century that produced the Bank of England.

The argument has never been refuted on its merits. What has happened is that the conditions which made the argument enforceable have been dismantled. The arithmetic of the argument is unchanged. Money still does not biologically reproduce. The system that compounds debt at interest still treats an abstract token as if it could.

The Islamic prohibition on *riba* — interest — remains in force across one third of the world's population. Islamic banking operates without interest, using profit-sharing partnerships, asset-backed leases, and risk-bearing investment instead. It is, by the criteria of the conventional system, less profitable. It is also, by the criteria of any moral tradition the conventional system pretends to inherit, more honest. The conventional system has dealt with this by surrounding the Islamic world with military bases and by destroying the secular nationalist regimes (Iraq, Libya, Syria) that attempted to organise economic life on non-Western financial principles. The argument has never been answered. The arguers have been killed.

Kant offers a separate test that arrives at the same conclusion. The categorical imperative requires that any moral rule be universalisable — that one act only on a principle one could simultaneously will to be a universal law. A debt-money system, by which all money is created through lending at interest, fails this test trivially. If every actor in the economy were to take the role of lender, there would be no borrowers; if every actor were to take the role of borrower, there would be no money. The system can be sustained only by a partition: a small lending class and a large borrowing class, the latter perpetually indebted to the former. A rule that requires this partition to function cannot be universalised. By Kant's test, it is not a moral system.

Rawls offers a third test that arrives at the same conclusion. Behind the veil of ignorance, not knowing whether one will be born into the lending class or the borrowing class, the inheriting class or the inherited-from class, no rational person would consent to a system in which one's position is determined at birth by the financial arrangements of one's grandparents and in which those arrangements are designed to compound across generations. The Rawlsian thought experiment is constructed to test whether a system would be chosen by people who do not know their position in it. The current system would not be chosen. Therefore, by Rawls's test, it cannot be just.

Graeber, in *Debt: The First 5,000 Years*, offers a fourth test, anthropological in character, drawn from the historical record this book opened with in Chapter 1. Across every literate civilisation for which records survive — Sumerian, Babylonian, Hebrew, Greek, Roman, Chinese — debt was understood as a relationship of *domination*, not of contract. Lender and borrower were not equal parties freely entering an agreement. The borrower was, after default, the property of the lender. The lender was the master. The borrower was the slave. Debt slavery was the default outcome of debt across most of human history; it required active social intervention — the jubilee, the *amargi*, the *misharum*, the *seisachtheia* — to prevent debt-relations from becoming chattel slavery within a generation or two. Ancient societies built jubilees into their constitutions because they understood that, without them, the people who lent would, within two generations, own the people who borrowed. The modern system has eliminated the jubilee. The mechanism for preventing debt-relations from becoming hereditary domination has been deliberately switched off.

This is the philosophical case, in four parts. Aristotle: usury is against nature. Kant: the system fails universalisation. Rawls: the system fails the veil of ignorance. Graeber: every traditional society understood that debt without jubilee becomes slavery. Four traditions, four tests, one verdict. The system is unjust. It does not pass any test that any serious moral tradition has set for an economic order.

## II. The logical case

The philosophical objection can be answered with "I do not accept your moral framework," and people who run the system do answer it that way. The logical objection is harder to dismiss. The logical objection is that the system is internally incoherent, in the precise technical sense that an intelligent agent inside it cannot, by acting rationally, exit it. The system is locked.

The lock operates at three levels.

**At the individual level**, the citizen of a debt-money society needs money to live. Money is rented from the banking system at interest. The citizen who tries to opt out of debt — by saving rather than borrowing, by living below their means — cannot opt out of the debt of others. They are taxed to service the public debt their government has issued. They pay rent or mortgage interest as part of their housing cost. They pay interest embedded in the price of every good they buy, because every business in the supply chain has borrowed working capital. By one widely cited estimate, in advanced economies the proportion of consumer prices that consists of interest paid up the supply chain is between 30 and 45 percent. The frugal individual is no more free of the debt system than the profligate one. The system is not a choice individuals make. It is the air the economy breathes.

**At the collective national level**, any single country could in principle exit the debt-money system and adopt sovereign money creation, as Lincoln did in 1862 with the greenbacks, as the Chicago Plan economists proposed in 1933, as the Bank of England's own 2014 working paper acknowledged would be possible. The choice would not impose any technical hardship; the technology is the same printing press. The hardship would be imposed externally. Any country that announces an exit from the dollar-based system or the BIS-coordinated central banking system faces, within months, a coordinated combination of currency speculation, credit-rating downgrades, capital flight, trade sanctions, and, if the country is small and oil-bearing, military intervention. The previous chapter named the recent examples: Libya in 2011, Iraq in 2003. Hillary Clinton's State Department emails, released under Freedom of Information Act litigation in 2015, document that French intelligence advised the Obama administration in April 2011 that Muammar Gaddafi's planned pan-African gold-backed dinar was "a threat to the financial security of the world." NATO bombed Libya beginning March 2011. Gaddafi was killed in October 2011. The gold-dinar plan died with him. The 144 tonnes of Libyan central-bank gold that had been accumulating for the project disappeared into the post-war chaos. Saddam Hussein switched Iraqi oil sales from dollars to euros in November 2000. The 2003 invasion reversed the switch within weeks of the regime's fall. Iran has been under continuous sanctions since 1979 in part because of its 2008 establishment of a Kish Island oil bourse that trades oil in currencies other than the dollar.

The sovereign-level message is unambiguous. Exit is not permitted. The system enforces its own membership.

**At the international level**, the lock is even tighter, because every country's central bank is integrated into the Bank for International Settlements (BIS) in Basel, which coordinates monetary policy across the central banks of the developed and developing world. The BIS, founded in 1930, is the central bank of central banks. It is the institution at which the actual coordination of the global financial system takes place, away from public scrutiny, with diplomatic immunity for its officials. Its monthly meetings of central bank governors are not minuted. Its decisions are not voted on by any electorate. It is the most powerful financial institution on the planet and most readers of this book have never heard of it. Any central bank that wishes to remain part of the international financial settlement system — that is, any central bank in any country wishing to engage in international trade — must coordinate with the BIS. The BIS coordinates with the Federal Reserve and the European Central Bank. The Federal Reserve and the European Central Bank coordinate with the major commercial banks of New York and Frankfurt and London. The major commercial banks are owned, as the next chapter will show in detail, by the same handful of asset management firms that dominate global equity markets. The system, viewed from a sufficient height, is one system, with one coordinating mechanism, controlled by perhaps a few hundred individuals.

This is not theory. It is, again, biography. The BIS exists. Its general manager is named. Its meetings happen. Its members are documented in its own annual reports. The opacity is procedural, not paranoid.

The logical conclusion of these three levels of lock is that the system cannot be exited by any of the actors inside it. The individual cannot exit because every transaction is denominated in debt-money. The nation cannot exit because exit triggers external sanctions up to and including military destruction. The international order cannot exit because the international order is the system. The only available form of exit is collapse.

This is what makes the system logically incoherent for an intelligent species. An intelligent species, presented with a clear description of the system, would not consent to it. The species does not consent to it because the species is not presented with a clear description of it. The educational, media, and political institutions through which the species learns about its economic arrangements are owned by the same circle that benefits from the arrangements. The arrangements are described as the natural order of things, the only viable form of economy, the inevitable arithmetic of growth and progress. They are none of those things. They are an artefact of named men in named rooms over four centuries. An intelligent species presented with the documented history of the arrangements would not consent to them. The intelligent species is therefore being prevented from being intelligent about the arrangements. This is the precise meaning of *ideology* in the technical sense: a system of belief that prevents its holders from perceiving the conditions of their own subordination.

## III. The mathematical case

Here the argument hardens, because here it leaves the territory of moral judgement and value contestation and enters the territory of arithmetic. The argument is that the system cannot work. Not that it works badly. Not that it produces unjust outcomes that could be ameliorated by better policy. The argument is that on its own terms, by its own internal arithmetic, the equilibrium the system promises does not exist and could never exist. It is mathematically impossible.

The argument has four parts.

### A. The compound interest paradox

Money loaned at interest compounds. Compounding is exponential growth. The doubling time for any exponential process is governed by the rule of 72: a sum of money at *r* percent per annum doubles in 72/*r* years. At 3 percent, money doubles every 24 years. At 5 percent, every 14.4 years. At 7 percent, every 10.3 years. These are not opinions. They are arithmetic.

Consider what this means at the level of an actual debt over an actual time horizon. If a single pre-decimal English penny had been deposited at 5 percent compound interest at the birth of Christ, it would by the year 2026 have grown to a sum representing 5 billion solid-gold spheres each the size of the earth. The arithmetic is straightforward — it is just 1.05 to the power of 2026 — and the answer is absurd. The answer is absurd because compound interest, applied across a long enough time horizon, produces values that cannot physically exist. The earth does not contain enough matter to embody them. The universe does not.

This is, in the apocryphal Einstein quotation, "the eighth wonder of the world." It is also the central reason every debt-based monetary system in history has eventually collapsed, defaulted, been jubileed, or been destroyed by war. The exponential cannot be sustained against a finite world. The arithmetic forces the collapse. The only question is when.

A modern industrial economy that operates on debt-money compounds total debt at the prevailing interest rate. Total US debt — federal, household, corporate, financial — has grown from 130 percent of GDP in 1950 to 360 percent of GDP in 2025. The trajectory is exponential. The reason it is exponential is that the underlying instrument is exponential. The reason it cannot continue indefinitely is that no real economy can grow exponentially indefinitely — the planet on which the economy operates is finite. Sometime between now and the heat death of the universe the trajectory will end. The question is whether it ends through jubilee, through hyperinflation, through war, or through systemic collapse.

The Nobel laureate chemist **Frederick Soddy** stated this argument in 1926 in a book called *Wealth, Virtual Wealth and Debt*. Soddy had won the 1921 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for his work on isotopes and radioactive decay. He was qualified, as a physical scientist, to recognise that wealth in the real sense is constrained by the laws of thermodynamics: you cannot create energy or matter from nothing; you can only transform existing energy and matter into new forms, and every transformation involves losses to entropy. Real wealth is therefore sub-exponential. It grows linearly, at best, with the available energy and labour of the population. Virtual wealth — debt, money, financial claims — is constrained by no such law. It is a mathematical abstraction. It compounds at whatever rate the lender insists on. The two trajectories diverge over time. Real wealth grows linearly. Virtual wealth grows exponentially. The gap between them is the gap between what is owed and what can be paid.

Soddy's prescription was to abolish private money creation through fractional reserve lending and to vest the money-creation power exclusively in sovereign authority. The same prescription was offered in 1933 by a group of senior University of Chicago economists — Henry Simons, Frank Knight, Paul Douglas, and others — in what is known as the Chicago Plan. The plan was taken seriously by the Roosevelt administration and was lost only in the legislative compromises that produced the 1933 and 1935 Banking Acts. The plan has been revived periodically since, most recently in a 2012 IMF working paper by Jaromir Benes and Michael Kumhof, *The Chicago Plan Revisited*, which used modern macroeconomic modelling to confirm that the plan would, in their words, "dramatically reduce" private debt and "completely eliminate" the danger of bank runs, the business cycle, and government debt. The IMF's own staff economists found that the proposal works. The proposal has not been implemented because, as the previous chapter documented, the political system has been captured by the institutions that profit from the alternative.

### B. The Piketty inequality: r > g

In 2014 the French economist **Thomas Piketty** published *Capital in the Twenty-First Century*, the largest empirical study of wealth and income distribution ever undertaken. Piketty and his team had spent fifteen years assembling tax records, national accounts, and inheritance data from twenty countries over two and a half centuries. The dataset is unprecedented. The central finding can be stated in three symbols.

**r > g**.

Where **r** is the rate of return on capital — the percentage by which wealth held in productive assets grows per year — and **g** is the rate of growth of the overall economy. Piketty's empirical finding, across the documented historical record, is that r has been 4 to 5 percent and g has been 1 to 1.5 percent. The gap is 3 percentage points. It exists in every period for which we have records, with one exception: 1914 to 1975, when r was suppressed by the destruction of capital in two world wars, by the Great Depression, by the post-war progressive taxation, and by the regulatory order of the New Deal and the European social-democratic settlements. That exception, the brief mid-century moment of compressed inequality, is what the post-war generation experienced and mistook for the normal condition of capitalism. It was an anomaly produced by extraordinary historical circumstances. With the dismantling of those circumstances after 1980 — the long counter-revolution the previous chapter described — r > g has reasserted itself, and inequality has returned to pre-1914 levels.

The mathematical consequence of r > g is direct. If capital grows faster than the economy, then capital's share of the economy grows over time. Labour's share — the share that goes to wages — shrinks. This is not a policy outcome; it is arithmetic. As long as r > g, the wealth of those who own capital compounds at a faster rate than the wages of those who must work for a living. The gap between the top and the bottom widens, not as a defect of the system, but as the system's normal operation.

This means several things follow with the force of arithmetic.

First, **trickle-down economics is mathematically impossible**. Trickle-down assumes that wealth concentrated at the top will flow downward to the bottom through investment, employment, and consumption. But if r > g, the top's wealth grows faster than the economy does. The top is therefore capturing a *rising* share of total wealth. There is no spare wealth to trickle. The arithmetic does not permit it. The model is incoherent at the level of basic algebra.

Second, **equality cannot be reached within the system without destroying r**. Equality requires that g ≥ r, which has occurred in the historical record only as the result of war, depression, or revolution. There is no path within the system's normal operation to equality. Equality requires that the system be interrupted. The system is therefore the principal obstacle to the equality it claims to be capable of producing.

Third, **the "American Dream" — the promise that any individual can rise through hard work — is statistically incompatible with the system**. If r > g, then inherited wealth outperforms earned wealth across any given generation. Hard work cannot, on average, outpace inheritance. The mobility that did exist between 1945 and 1975 was a function of the anomalous period in which r had been suppressed by war and policy. As the system has returned to its normal operation, social mobility in the United States and the United Kingdom has declined to levels lower than in the supposedly class-bound societies of continental Europe. The American Dream did not die. The arithmetic killed it. It could only ever have been alive during the anomalous mid-century compression.

### C. The trap

The combined force of compound interest and r > g produces what is, in the precise mathematical sense, a trap. The trap can be described in terms a child could check.

Consider an economy in which all money has been created through bank lending. The total stock of money in circulation is M. The total stock of debt outstanding is M + the accumulated interest claims, which we will call I. By definition of how debt-money works, the principal M was created when the loans were made. The interest I was not. The interest must be obtained from somewhere outside the original loans.

Where? Only one place is possible: new loans. The interest on the existing debts can only be paid by issuing new debts, the principal of which becomes available to pay the existing interest. But the new debts themselves carry interest, which will require still more new debts to service. The system must therefore expand its total debt stock perpetually, at a rate at least equal to the prevailing interest rate, simply to remain solvent.

This is the structural feature that makes a debt-money system different from a sovereign-money system. A sovereign-money system can rest. The state issues currency to facilitate exchange, the currency circulates, no party is structurally indebted to any other for the existence of the currency. A debt-money system cannot rest. The interest is always coming due. The only way for the system as a whole to pay it is to borrow more. The system has therefore been correctly described by Soddy, by the Chicago Plan economists, by Steve Keen in the 2010s, and by the Bank of England's own 2014 working paper, as structurally requiring continuous credit expansion. Growth is not an option. Growth is the system's life support.

When growth falters — for reasons that have nothing to do with the desires of the participants and everything to do with the finite carrying capacity of the planet, demographic limits, energy constraints, or political resistance — the interest still falls due. The debt-stock continues compounding. The economy cannot grow fast enough to service it. Defaults cascade. Asset prices collapse. Banks fail. The central bank intervenes with emergency credit creation, which is to say, with the system's preferred remedy: more debt. The 2008 crisis is the canonical recent example. The remedy for too much debt was, in the official policy response, more debt.

This is the trap. The system cannot stop growing without collapsing. It cannot grow indefinitely on a finite planet. It is therefore, by the basic arithmetic of compound interest applied to a thermodynamically constrained world, headed for collapse on a finite time horizon. The collapse has not happened yet because the time horizon is long and the system has been able to externalise costs onto other parts of the world and onto the biosphere. The biosphere is now itself collapsing — species extinction, climate destabilisation, soil exhaustion — which is the visible terrestrial signature of the arithmetic.

### D. The intelligent-species argument

Here is the strongest form of the argument. The system is not merely unjust. It is not merely incoherent. It is not merely doomed. It is *recognisably* doomed by any species capable of doing the arithmetic.

A species sufficiently intelligent to invent compound interest is sufficiently intelligent to recognise that compound interest applied across long horizons produces absurd values. A species sufficiently intelligent to construct national accounts is sufficiently intelligent to compare the growth rate of debt-stocks to the growth rate of physical output and notice that the two have been diverging for forty years and that no possible future configuration of policy can converge them within the existing rules. A species sufficiently intelligent to map the biosphere is sufficiently intelligent to notice that exponential debt growth on a planet of finite mass cannot continue. The arithmetic is not difficult. The conclusions are not subtle. Any moderately educated adult, given two hours and a calculator, can verify them.

We do not, however, draw these conclusions collectively. We do not act on them politically. We continue to treat the system as if it could be reformed within its own parameters, as if compounded debt could somehow stop compounding, as if r could become less than or equal to g while the political institutions that suppress r remain dismantled. This is not a failure of intelligence. It is a failure of access to the arithmetic. The arithmetic is everywhere available — in any introductory finance textbook, on the Bank of England's own website, in Piketty's book and in Soddy's — but it is not connected to the political and moral conclusions it implies. The connection is suppressed. The arithmetic is taught as if it were technical knowledge useful for managing a portfolio rather than as the description of a trap.

This is the precise functional definition of an ideology that has captured an intelligent species. The species retains the cognitive capacity to do the arithmetic. The species has been prevented from doing the arithmetic *in this context*. The arithmetic, done in this context, names the trap. The naming would unmake the trap, because the trap relies on the consent of the trapped. Therefore the naming has been made socially impossible — relegated to the territory of cranks, conspiracy theorists, anti-Semites, populists, and other politically discredited categories. The categories exist for the purpose. They function. The arithmetic remains undone.

This book is the arithmetic done.

## IV. The verdict

The system named in the previous chapters cannot work, has never worked, will never work, and is recognisably impossible to anyone capable of the elementary mathematics it depends on. The system is not a system. It is a trap, in the precise technical sense — a structure that constrains the behaviour of the agents inside it in a way that makes escape from the structure impossible from within. The trap was designed by named men in named rooms over four centuries. The trap is operated by their successors. The successors are not better or worse than the original designers; they are simply the people who inherited the operating positions.

The trap has now reached the terminal phase of its arithmetic. The exponential debt curve has met the finite biosphere. The wealth concentration has reached pre-revolutionary levels. The political institutions that previously contained the system — labour unions, progressive taxation, regulatory states, democratic accountability — have been dismantled by the long counter-revolution of 1947 to 2008. There is no soft landing available within the system's own parameters. The system will resolve, in the coming decades, either through jubilee, through war, through ecological collapse, or through the construction of an alternative outside the system's perimeter.

The remaining chapters of this book are about that resolution.

The next chapter is about the institutions and individuals who now own the system in its current consolidated form, and who will be the principal beneficiaries of the resolution if the rest of us do not organise an alternative resolution first. The section after that is about how the system has captured the knowledge-producing institutions — universities, peer review, science journalism, the medical establishment — so that the arithmetic cannot be taught and the trap cannot be named. The section after that is about the resolutions that have been tried elsewhere, that worked, and that we are not told about: mutual credit, cooperative ownership, ecological economics, the actual operational alternatives that exist on the ground and have been suppressed from the public discussion because their existence falsifies the central claim that there is no alternative.

There is an alternative. There has always been an alternative. The system that says there isn't has been telling that lie since 1694. The arithmetic, done honestly, names the lie.


---

# Chapter 8: The Cult

The system the previous chapters built is now operated. The operators are alive. They have names, addresses, board memberships, and known biographies. They are one hundred and fifty individuals in some combination of asset management, private equity, hedge funds, technology, and policy coordination, with another perhaps fifteen hundred at the second tier. They run a system that controls — through equity holdings, debt instruments, and technological platforms — the productive economy of every developed country and most of the developing world. They are not a "cabal" in the sense of a secret society. They are a class, and they coordinate through a documented network of meetings, ownership structures, board interlocks, and political donations that is hidden from no one who looks. They are the visible heirs of the project this book has been describing for seven chapters. The record names them.

The record has eight parts. The first describes the **asset management bloc** — BlackRock, Vanguard, State Street — that has consolidated ownership of corporate America into the hands of three firms. The second describes the **private equity sector** that has, in parallel, bought up the unlisted economy: the local newspapers, the regional hospitals, the nursing homes, the single-family rental housing stock, the rest of what used to be the commons of small-scale ownership. The third describes the **hedge fund layer**. The fourth describes the **Goldman pipeline** through which the same firm has placed alumni in every senior central banking and Treasury position in the Western world for thirty years. The fifth describes the **coordination layer** — Davos, the Trilateral Commission, Bilderberg, the Council on Foreign Relations — at which the policy decisions are aligned. The sixth describes the **techno-oligarchy** — the PayPal Mafia, the Silicon Valley founders, the AI labs — that has been built on top of the financial layer over the past twenty-five years and that is now in the process of replacing the political layer with a corporate-authoritarian alternative. The seventh describes the **Epstein-Wexner-Maxwell network** as a documented social compromise mechanism — a honey-trap apparatus that operated for at least three decades and whose existence is now a matter of court record. The eighth describes the **artificial intelligence consolidation** that has converged the technological and financial layers into a single ownership structure and includes an honest disclosure about this book's own composition.

This is the system as it currently exists. Not as it has been described in the press. Not as it has described itself. As it actually is.

## I. The Big Three

The largest single shareholder in Apple is BlackRock. The second largest is Vanguard. The largest single shareholder in Microsoft is Vanguard. The second largest is BlackRock. The largest single shareholder in Amazon is Vanguard. The second largest is BlackRock. The largest single shareholder in Alphabet is Vanguard. The second largest is BlackRock. The largest single shareholder in Meta is Vanguard. The second largest is BlackRock. The largest single shareholder in JPMorgan Chase is Vanguard. The second is BlackRock. The third is State Street. The same pattern holds for ExxonMobil, Walmart, Berkshire Hathaway, UnitedHealth, Johnson & Johnson, Procter & Gamble, Bank of America, Wells Fargo, Citigroup, Goldman Sachs itself, Pfizer, Coca-Cola, McDonald's, every major American defense contractor, every major American pharmaceutical company, every major American media company, and 88 percent of the companies in the S&P 500.

This is not concentration. This is consolidation. Three firms — BlackRock, Vanguard, State Street — are the largest or second-largest shareholders in every publicly traded American company of significance. The combined holdings of the three are 20 to 25 percent of US equity markets. As of 2024, BlackRock alone managed $11.6 trillion in assets under direct management, Vanguard $10 trillion, and State Street $4.7 trillion — a combined total of $26 trillion, equivalent to 90 percent of US GDP held by three firms.

But the more important number is the Aladdin number. **Aladdin** — the Asset, Liability, Debt and Derivative Investment Network — is BlackRock's risk management software platform. It is the system on which BlackRock manages its own assets. It is also the system that BlackRock licences to its competitors, to central banks, to sovereign wealth funds, to pension funds, and to the corporate treasury operations of the world's largest companies. As of December 2025, BlackRock has reported that **$25 trillion in assets are managed on the Aladdin platform**. This is one fifth of the world's investable assets, processed through a single piece of software, owned by a single firm, designed by Larry Fink. The Bank of Israel runs its foreign exchange reserves on Aladdin. CalPERS, the California public pension fund, runs $260 billion on Aladdin. Deutsche Bank runs €900 billion on Aladdin. Prudential plc runs $700 billion on Aladdin. Microsoft Treasury runs on Aladdin. When BlackRock makes a change to a risk parameter in its software, the change propagates instantaneously across one fifth of the world's capital. There has never been a private financial information system of comparable scope. There has never been a private entity with comparable real-time monitoring capability over global capital flows.

During the 2008 crisis, the US government placed $130 billion in toxic assets with BlackRock for management. The Federal Reserve gave BlackRock the $1.25 trillion mandate to purchase mortgage-backed securities under the quantitative easing programme. The US Treasury and the Federal Reserve, in the largest financial intervention in American history, hired Larry Fink's firm to manage the cleanup. Fink had founded BlackRock just twenty years earlier. The firm had been a mid-sized fixed-income shop in 1998. By 2009 it was the institution to which the United States government turned to administer the recovery. This is what consolidation produces. The same firm that owns the banks ends up administering the bailout of the banks because no other institution has comparable capability. The trajectory is monotonic. The next crisis, when it comes, will be administered by an even more consolidated BlackRock.

The legal-economic literature on what this consolidation does to competition is now extensive. Eric Posner of the University of Chicago Law School, Einer Elhauge of Harvard Law School, José Azar of IESE Business School, and Martin Schmalz of the Saïd Business School at Oxford have published in peer-reviewed journals — including the *Harvard Law Review* and the *Journal of Finance* — that **horizontal shareholding** (the same investors holding large positions in competing firms) reduces competition between those firms. The mechanism is straightforward: if the same investor owns 8 percent of United, 8 percent of Delta, 8 percent of American, and 8 percent of Southwest, that investor does not want United to compete aggressively against Delta on prices, because aggressive competition reduces the profits of the investor's other holdings. The investor will, instead, prefer that the four airlines tacitly coordinate to keep prices high and profits stable. Azar, Schmalz and Tecu's 2018 paper in the *Journal of Finance* estimated that common ownership in the US airline industry raised airfares by 3 to 7 percent — a tax on the travelling public, collected by the three asset managers, never voted on by anyone.

This is the mechanism the rest of the modern economy is now structured around. The Big Three own a fifth of every important publicly traded American company. The companies do not compete as aggressively as a market with dispersed ownership would. Prices stay high. Wages stay low. Profits compound at the rate of return on capital, which, as Chapter 7 established, is structurally greater than the rate of economic growth. The arithmetic works exactly as Piketty's equation predicts. The owners of capital, which is to say BlackRock, Vanguard, and State Street, capture an ever-rising share of the economy. The workers, who own no significant capital, are paid an ever-decreasing share. This is not a market malfunction. It is the market functioning as currently structured.

Who owns BlackRock, Vanguard, and State Street themselves? BlackRock is publicly listed; its largest shareholders are Vanguard, State Street, and BlackRock's own funds — that is, BlackRock owns BlackRock through its index funds, Vanguard owns BlackRock through its index funds, and State Street owns BlackRock through its index funds. State Street is publicly listed and is owned, in the same way, primarily by Vanguard, BlackRock, and State Street's own funds. Vanguard is unique: it is structured as a mutual, owned by the shareholders of the Vanguard mutual funds. There is no public stock. Vanguard cannot be bought. It is, in a structural sense, owner-less — controlled by its management, accountable to its passive fund-holders in theory, accountable to no one in practice. **Larry Fink** chairs BlackRock; **Salim Ramji** runs Vanguard; **Ronald O'Hanley** runs State Street. Three men, three firms, twenty-five trillion dollars under management, twenty-five trillion more on Aladdin. The world's productive capital, structurally aligned, under software they wrote.

## II. Private equity

The publicly traded economy is consolidated under the Big Three. The unlisted economy — the smaller companies, the regional businesses, the real estate, the infrastructure, the local newspapers, the hospitals, the nursing homes, the dental chains, the veterinary practices, the single-family rental housing stock — is consolidated under private equity. The mechanism is leveraged buyout: an LBO firm borrows three or four dollars for every dollar of its own equity, uses the borrowed money to purchase a company, loads the company itself with the resulting debt, extracts management fees and dividends, sells what remains five to seven years later, and repeats. The companies that emerge from this process are, on average, more indebted, less productive, with lower employment and worse customer outcomes than the companies that entered it. The shareholders of the LBO firm — the firm's partners, plus the limited partners that fund it, principally pension funds and sovereign wealth funds — receive returns that have historically averaged 15 to 25 percent per year. The capital extracted comes from the companies that were bought, from the workers who lost their jobs in the cost-cutting, from the customers who paid higher prices for worse service, and from the taxpayers who absorbed the failed firms that did not survive the loading.

The four largest private equity firms, by approximate assets under management as of 2024:

**Blackstone**: $1.1 trillion. Founded 1985 by **Stephen Schwarzman** and **Peter G. Peterson**. Peterson is, as Chapter 5 documented, the man who pushed Nixon to close the gold window in January 1971 and who became Nixon's Commerce Secretary. The man who recommended the unilateral end of the dollar-gold link in 1971 used the privileged information he obtained during that role to build, fourteen years later with Schwarzman, the largest alternative asset manager in history. The career arc — government appointment, policy decision, private accumulation — is the standard pattern of the system. Peterson died in 2018. Blackstone now owns, among many other things, 300,000 single-family rental homes in the United States, making it the largest landlord of single-family rental housing in the country. The financialisation of American housing — the transformation of homes into rental assets owned by institutional investors rather than family residences owned by their occupants — has been carried out by Blackstone over the past fifteen years.

**Carlyle Group**: $426 billion. Founded 1987 by **David Rubenstein**, a former Carter administration aide. Carlyle's distinction is that, from its inception, it specialised in defence and intelligence contractors and staffed its senior ranks with former senior government officials. The roster has included:

- **Frank Carlucci**, former Deputy Director of the CIA under Carter, Reagan's National Security Adviser, Reagan's Defense Secretary — joined Carlyle as Chairman in 1989.
- **James A. Baker III**, Reagan's Treasury Secretary, George H.W. Bush's Secretary of State and White House Chief of Staff — senior counsellor.
- **George H.W. Bush**, former CIA Director, Vice President, President — senior adviser 1994 to 2003.
- **John Major**, former British Prime Minister — Carlyle Europe chairman 1997 to 2004.
- **Arthur Levitt**, former SEC chairman — senior adviser.
- **Frank Carlucci** and **Donald Rumsfeld** (Reagan and George W. Bush's Defense Secretary): wrestling teammates at Princeton, lifelong friends. The procurement relationships between Carlyle's defence-industry holdings and the Defense Department under Rumsfeld are, in Dan Briody's 2003 book *The Iron Triangle*, the case study.

Carlyle's largest single defence investment was **United Defense Industries**, purchased in 1997 for $850 million. The Pentagon's Crusader artillery programme, awarded to United Defense, was a $5 billion contract. Carlyle's 2001 IPO of United Defense earned the firm $237 million in proceeds and left Carlyle with a stake that, by April 2004, was worth $900 million. The investment that brought this return was, by Carlyle's own characterisation, a defence investment whose value depended on the level of US military spending — military spending that was determined by officials with whom Carlyle's senior advisers had longstanding personal relationships. The conflict of interest is not theoretical. It is the business model.

The **Bin Laden family** — through the Saudi Binladin Group, owned by the descendants of Mohammed bin Laden, Osama bin Laden's father, who founded the Saudi construction conglomerate that built most of modern Saudi Arabia — was a Carlyle limited partner from the 1990s. They were invested in Carlyle on **11 September 2001** — the day Carlyle held its annual investor conference at the Ritz-Carlton in Washington, DC. Shafiq bin Laden, Osama's half-brother, was present at the conference along with David Rubenstein, Frank Carlucci, James Baker, and the former President of the United States George H.W. Bush, on the day the towers fell in New York. The Bin Laden family interest in Carlyle was quietly liquidated in October 2001, after the news became public. The fact that the family of the man who had just orchestrated the deadliest attack on American soil since Pearl Harbor was, on the day of the attack, in the same room as the family of the sitting American president and his predecessors' Defense Secretary, was reported at the time, never investigated, and rapidly forgotten. The 9/11 Commission did not address it. The mainstream press treated it as awkward coincidence. The system absorbed the embarrassment and continued.

**KKR**: $550 billion. Founded 1976 by **Henry Kravis**, **George Roberts**, and **Jerome Kohlberg**. The original LBO firm — the firm that made the technique famous with the $25 billion buyout of RJR Nabisco in 1989 (chronicled in *Barbarians at the Gate*). KKR's portfolio includes major holdings across telecoms, healthcare, energy, and financial services.

**Apollo Global Management**: $700 billion. Founded 1990 by **Leon Black**, **Joshua Harris**, and **Marc Rowan**. **Leon Black**: resigned as CEO of Apollo in 2021 after the firm's own Independent Review (conducted by Dechert LLP, January 2021) disclosed that Black had paid Jeffrey Epstein **$158 million** between 2012 and 2017, ostensibly for "tax and estate planning advice." The Review concluded that Black had not committed any wrongdoing, but the figure speaks for itself. Apollo's founder and largest shareholder paid a convicted sex offender a hundred and fifty-eight million dollars over five years for tax advice. Black was not prosecuted. He retired wealthy. Apollo continues to operate; Marc Rowan is now CEO.

The pattern of private equity's effect on the productive economy is uniform across sectors. **Toys R Us**: bought by KKR, Bain Capital, and Vornado Realty Trust in 2005 for $6.6 billion, $5.3 billion of which was debt loaded onto the company itself. Toys R Us spent the next thirteen years using its operating cash flow to service the buyout debt, never recovering the capacity to invest in stores or e-commerce, and was liquidated in 2018. Roughly 33,000 employees lost their jobs. The PE firms had been paid out in management fees and dividend recaps long before the bankruptcy. **Sears**, **Sports Authority**, **Payless ShoeSource**, **Hertz**, **Toys R Us**, **Mervyn's**, **Linens 'n Things**, **Gymboree**, **Claire's**, **Limited Brands**, **RadioShack**, **Boscov's**, **Borders**, **Brookstone**: each acquired by private equity, each loaded with debt, each driven to bankruptcy or closure. The retail apocalypse of the 2010s was not, as commonly described, "Amazon killing retail." It was private equity killing retail by extracting capital from companies that, debt-free, would have been able to compete with Amazon. Amazon won the battle PE had pre-decided.

The same pattern has now consumed local newspapers (Alden Global Capital), regional hospitals (Cerberus Capital Management's destruction of Steward Health Care; the Carlyle-owned ManorCare nursing home collapse), and increasingly housing. **Invitation Homes** (Blackstone), **American Homes 4 Rent**, **Tricon Residential**: institutional landlords now own one in four single-family rental homes in the United States, a share that was zero in 2010. The political consequence is that an entire generation of young Americans cannot buy houses. The financial consequence is that an additional rent-extraction layer has been inserted between American families and their shelter. The Aristotelian observation — that money is not supposed to give birth to money, but is supposed to facilitate the exchange of real goods — is, by 2026, falsified across every category of basic necessity. The roof over your head, the company you work for, the hospital you go to, the local newspaper that used to cover your town: all of it is now an interest-bearing financial instrument held by a private equity fund.

## III. Hedge funds

The hedge fund layer is smaller in total AUM than the asset management or private equity layers — perhaps $5 trillion globally — but is, individually, more politically influential. Hedge funds are where individual billionaires accumulate and deploy personal capital.

**Citadel** (founded 1990 by Ken Griffin). Roughly $66 billion in AUM. Griffin's personal net worth is $45 billion. He is one of the largest political donors in the United States, predominantly to Republican candidates and causes; in the 2024 cycle his political contributions exceeded $100 million.

**Renaissance Technologies** (founded 1982 by Jim Simons, deceased 2024). Renaissance's Medallion Fund has, since 1988, produced average annual returns of 39 percent after fees — the most successful track record in the history of asset management. **Robert Mercer**, Renaissance's co-CEO from 2010 to 2017, used his personal wealth to fund:

- **Cambridge Analytica**, the data-operations firm that processed Facebook data on 87 million Americans without their consent and applied psychographic modelling to the 2016 Trump campaign;
- **Breitbart News**, the right-wing news outlet then run by **Steve Bannon**;
- **The Make America Number 1** super-PAC, which provided strategic and financial support to Trump's 2016 campaign during its weakest months.

The data infrastructure of the 2016 election — the mechanism by which a relatively small number of swing-state voters were identified, targeted, and influenced — was, in major part, funded by Mercer's hedge fund wealth and operated by Bannon. Mercer left Renaissance in 2017 after the Cambridge Analytica scandal but his daughter Rebekah Mercer continues to run the family's political operations.

**Bridgewater Associates** (founded 1975 by Ray Dalio). $170 billion AUM. Dalio is China's most prominent Western financial advocate; he has maintained major Chinese asset holdings throughout the period in which the United States has formally regarded China as a strategic adversary. **Elliott Management** (Paul Singer): the vulture fund that has specialised in litigating against sovereign debt defaulters — Argentina, Republic of Congo, Puerto Rico. Singer's hedge fund extracted $2.4 billion from Argentina in 2016 after fifteen years of litigation. Singer is also a major Republican donor; **JD Vance**, now Vice President of the United States, worked briefly at Elliott before his political career.

**D.E. Shaw** (founded 1988 by David Shaw): notable as the firm where **Larry Summers** worked after leaving Harvard, earning $5.2 million in 2008 for one day a week of work, before joining the Obama administration as Director of the National Economic Council. **Jeff Bezos** also worked at D.E. Shaw in the early 1990s before founding Amazon.

## IV. The Goldman pipeline

Goldman Sachs has, for thirty years, functioned as a training ground for senior central bankers, treasury secretaries, and political officials across multiple jurisdictions. The list of senior public-sector positions held by Goldman alumni is, by 2026, comprehensive.

In the United States:

- **Robert Rubin**: Goldman co-chairman 1990-92; US Treasury Secretary 1995-99 (Clinton); Citigroup senior counsellor 1999-2009. Pushed Gramm-Leach-Bliley; oversaw Citigroup's near-collapse.
- **Henry Paulson**: Goldman CEO 1999-2006; US Treasury Secretary 2006-09 (Bush). Architect of the 2008 bailouts.
- **Steven Mnuchin**: Goldman partner 1994-2002; US Treasury Secretary 2017-21 (first Trump administration).
- **Gary Cohn**: Goldman President 2006-16; National Economic Council Director 2017-18 (first Trump administration).
- **Steve Bannon**: Goldman M&A banker 1984-90 before his political career.
- **Neel Kashkari**: Goldman 2002-06; managed TARP under Paulson; President of the Minneapolis Federal Reserve 2016 to present.
- **William Dudley**: Goldman partner 1986-2007; President of the New York Federal Reserve 2009-18.

In the United Kingdom and Europe:

- **Mark Carney**: Goldman 1990-2003; Governor of the Bank of Canada 2008-13; Governor of the Bank of England 2013-20; **Prime Minister of Canada from March 2025**. The man who ran the Bank of England during Brexit, the negotiations of which he shaped, is, as of 2026, the sitting Prime Minister of one of the G7 nations.
- **Mario Draghi**: Goldman managing director 2002-05; Bank of Italy Governor 2006-11; President of the European Central Bank 2011-19; Prime Minister of Italy 2021-22. The man who declared in July 2012 that the ECB would do "whatever it takes" to preserve the euro — committing the public balance sheet of the entire eurozone to unlimited bond purchases — was a Goldman alumnus running the European central bank.

In Greece, in 2001, Goldman Sachs structured currency swaps at non-market rates that allowed the Greek government to disguise €2.8 billion of public debt from the European Union's Maastricht convergence criteria assessment. Greece used the disguised debt to qualify for euro membership. Goldman's fee for the swaps was $300 million. When the disguise unwound nine years later, the resulting Greek debt crisis required EU bailouts of €289 billion, the imposition of austerity programmes that drove Greek unemployment to 27 percent, and the suicide of 11,000 additional Greek citizens between 2010 and 2017. The bank that had helped Greece into the euro was, throughout the crisis, a counterparty to the same Greek government bonds whose price collapsed during the crisis. Goldman's trades on the Greek debt crisis are estimated to have been profitable. The bank profited from helping Greece in, and then profited again from the crisis caused by helping Greece in. The Greek public funded both transactions. No Goldman executive was prosecuted. The currency swaps remain in place. Greece remains in the euro. The arrangement, three years after the formal end of the bailout, is described in the European press as a stable equilibrium.

The Goldman pipeline is not a conspiracy in the secret sense. It is a documented professional network in which seven hundred senior Goldman alumni occupy senior positions across the public sector globally, and in which the firm and its alumni shape policy in the firm's interest and in the interest of the broader financial sector. The firm is regarded, accurately, as the most powerful single institution in global finance. Its alumni network is regarded, accurately, as the most powerful single political network in the world. Both descriptions are publicly acknowledged. Neither is treated, in the mainstream press, as the description of an oligarchic capture mechanism, because the mainstream press is owned by the same circle.

## V. The coordination layer

Above the financial and corporate layers is a coordination layer at which the policy decisions of the system are aligned across borders. The institutions are publicly known. The pretence is that they are simply venues for conversation; the practice is that they are venues for coordination.

The **World Economic Forum**, founded 1971 by **Klaus Schwab**, is the most visible of these. Its annual meeting in Davos, Switzerland, gathers 3,000 senior corporate and political officials each January under heavy security. WEF membership starts at $60,000 per year; "strategic partner" status — which includes access to private sessions with senior political officials — costs upwards of $500,000 per year. The membership list reads as the list of the same thousand companies that own the rest of the system. The WEF's *Young Global Leaders* programme, founded 2004, has placed alumni in senior political positions across the world: Emmanuel Macron of France, Justin Trudeau of Canada, Jacinda Ardern of New Zealand, Sebastian Kurz of Austria, Sanna Marin of Finland, and many others. The pattern of alumni placement is observable; the recruitment criteria are not public. Schwab himself has published two books — *The Fourth Industrial Revolution* (2016) and *COVID-19: The Great Reset* (2020) — that openly advocate technocratic coordination of governance at the global level by precisely the actors who attend Davos. The coordination is not hidden. The objection to noticing it is the objection.

The **Trilateral Commission**, founded 1973 by David Rockefeller and Zbigniew Brzezinski, coordinates senior business and policy figures across three regions: North America, Europe, and East Asia. Its membership is published. Its meetings are private. Henry Kissinger, Paul Volcker, Alan Greenspan, Robert Rubin, Larry Summers, Madeleine Albright, Jimmy Carter, Bill Clinton, Tony Blair, and three hundred other senior figures of the post-war Western political order have been members. The Commission's published reports advocate, with consistent monotony, the liberalisation of international capital movements, the integration of national economies into global supply chains, and the harmonisation of national regulatory standards — the precise policies that have, since 1973, produced the wealth concentration described in the previous chapter.

The **Council on Foreign Relations** (founded 1921) is the American foreign-policy establishment. Its membership of 5,000 includes the senior leadership of nearly every major American institution. Its journal, *Foreign Affairs*, is the principal venue at which American grand-strategic doctrine is articulated. Robert Rubin chaired it. Richard Haass ran it for two decades. The Council's connection to the financial layer described above is total: its trustees and senior fellows overlap with the senior leadership of the Big Three, the major banks, and the major private equity firms.

The **Bilderberg Meetings**, founded 1954 by Prince Bernhard of the Netherlands and a cluster of senior Atlanticist figures, gather 130 invitees each year under Chatham House rules — no public minutes, no formal published outputs, no press inside the meeting. The list of attendees is published after the meeting. The list, year over year for seventy years, is the list of the same people who run the institutions described above. Henry Kissinger has attended 60 times. David Rockefeller attended for 50 years. The meeting's existence is no longer denied. Its agenda is not published. The pretence that it is a conversation among private individuals about matters of mutual interest, rather than a coordination meeting among the people who run the world, has worn thin to the point of transparency.

**The Atlas Network**, described in Chapter 6, and the **Aspen Institute**, with its Aspen Strategy Group and Aspen Security Forum, complete the visible coordination layer. There are other, less-visible groups — the **Pilgrims Society** (Anglo-American banking, founded 1902), the **Cercle Pinay** (right-wing transatlantic security, founded 1952), the **Mont Pèlerin Society** (still operating from Chapter 6) — that operate at lower visibility but in the same institutional space.

The argument is not that these groups are secret. They are not. The argument is that they together constitute the policy-coordination layer of a transnational oligarchy whose member institutions own the rest of the economy. Their members are public. Their meetings happen. Their decisions are not voted on by any electorate. The pretence that the politicians who emerge from these meetings represent the publics that elected them, rather than the institutions that gather them, is a pretence that the public can no longer be expected to sustain.

## VI. The techno-oligarchy

Beginning around 2000, a new layer of the oligarchy emerged from Silicon Valley. The layer was financially capitalised by the sale of PayPal to eBay in 2002 (the founders received $1.5 billion in eBay stock), and intellectually capitalised by the broader Silicon Valley founder culture that had been forming since the 1990s. It has, in the twenty-five years since, become the politically dominant fraction of the oligarchy, displacing the older finance-and-defence wing in cultural influence, in political contributions, and increasingly in policy.

The **PayPal Mafia** is the founding network. The figures, in their current 2026 positions:

- **Peter Thiel** (born 1967). Co-founder of PayPal 1998. Founded **Palantir Technologies** in 2003 with seed capital from In-Q-Tel, the CIA's investment arm. Founded Founders Fund in 2005. Early Facebook investor (first outside investor, $500,000 for 10 percent in 2004; eventual proceeds $1 billion). Patron of **JD Vance** — funded Vance's *Hillbilly Elegy* tour, his venture capital career at Mithril and at Vance's own Narya Capital, and his 2022 Ohio Senate campaign with $15 million in PAC contributions. Vance is, as of January 2025, **Vice President of the United States**. Thiel is also the long-time funder of **Curtis Yarvin** — the political writer who, under the pen name Mencius Moldbug, has advocated since 2007 for the replacement of American liberal democracy with a corporate-authoritarian "neocameralist" system in which the country would be governed as a joint-stock company by a CEO-king. Yarvin's "Dark Enlightenment" or "neoreactionary" movement, dismissed in the 2010s as fringe, has, by 2025, supplied the philosophical doctrine of the wing of the American right that has taken political power. Thiel has spoken openly, in published interviews, of his hostility to democracy. His 2009 Cato Institute essay opens with the line "I no longer believe that freedom and democracy are compatible." He has been the funding network's most effective political operator and is, as of 2026, the most politically consequential of the techno-oligarchs.

- **Elon Musk** (born 1971). Co-founder of Zip2 (sold 1999, $307 million); founder of X.com which became PayPal (sold 2002, Musk's share $180 million). Founded SpaceX in 2002, joined Tesla in 2004 and became its largest shareholder, founded Neuralink in 2016, founded the Boring Company in 2016, founded xAI in 2023, purchased Twitter (now X) in October 2022 for $44 billion. Net worth $400 billion as of 2025, the largest individual fortune in human history. Funded $290 million in pro-Trump political activity in the 2024 cycle. Appointed to lead the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) by Trump in January 2025; carried out mass terminations of 220,000 federal employees and the dismantling of the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, the United States Agency for International Development, and significant portions of the Department of Education between January and May 2025; departed the role in May 2025 after a public falling-out with Trump. Owns the largest satellite constellation in human history (Starlink, ~7,000 satellites) and the largest social media platform aligned with right-wing political messaging. The transition from "PayPal co-founder" to "individual private actor with foreign-policy influence comparable to a medium-sized state" took twenty-three years.

- **Reid Hoffman**: co-founder of LinkedIn 2002 (sold to Microsoft 2016 for $26 billion); founding board member of OpenAI 2015-2023. The Democratic-aligned PayPal Mafia member. Major funder of Democratic and centrist political causes. Demonstrates that the PayPal network is not ideologically uniform — it is, more precisely, the network that operates at the level above conventional partisan politics, with members funding both parties as a portfolio strategy.

- **David Sacks**: COO of PayPal 1999-2002; founder of Yammer (sold to Microsoft 2012 for $1.2 billion); host (with Chamath Palihapitiya and others) of the *All-In* podcast that has become the principal venue at which the techno-oligarchy speaks publicly to itself. Appointed in January 2025 as the second Trump administration's "AI and crypto czar."

- **Max Levchin**: co-founder of PayPal 1998; founder of Affirm (buy-now-pay-later consumer credit). Affirm is, in 2026, one of the largest non-bank consumer lenders in the United States — credit at high interest rates extended to mostly young, mostly precarious consumers, securitised and sold to the same institutional investors who own the rest of the system. Levchin's company is, at the consumer level, the same business Mayer Amschel Rothschild was running in Frankfurt in 1769: lending to people who need money, at interest, taking the profit on the spread.

- **Keith Rabois**, **Roelof Botha**, **Premal Shah**, **Jeremy Stoppelman**, **Russel Simmons** (Yelp), **Chad Hurley**, **Steve Chen**, **Jawed Karim** (the last three founded YouTube): the rest of the founding network.

The PayPal Mafia is the seed. Around it, the wider techno-oligarchy:

- **Marc Andreessen**: co-founder of Netscape (1994); co-founder of Andreessen Horowitz (a16z) in 2009, which now manages $45 billion in venture capital. Author of the October 2023 *Techno-Optimist Manifesto*, an explicit ideological text advocating "accelerationism" and dismissing concerns about AI safety, technological inequality, and democratic oversight of corporate power. Andreessen named, in the manifesto, as "enemies" of progress: "trust and safety," "sustainable development," "tech ethics," "risk management," "the existential risk movement," "the precautionary principle." The "enemies" listed are, with no exceptions, the discourses by which a democratic society could regulate the technology Andreessen's firm sells. Major Trump donor 2024 cycle.

- **Sam Altman** (born 1985): founder of Y Combinator (chairman 2014-19); co-founder of OpenAI in 2015 with Musk, Hoffman, Brockman, Sutskever, and others. CEO of OpenAI from 2019. Briefly removed by the OpenAI board in November 2023; reinstated within five days after Microsoft and Andreessen Horowitz pressure forced the board's resignation. Founder, with Sam Worsham, of **Worldcoin** — a project that collects iris biometric scans from 6 million people globally (as of 2025) in exchange for cryptocurrency, building, in effect, the world's largest private biometric database. Altman has lobbied governments globally for AI regulation that would entrench incumbent AI labs against open-source competitors.

- **Alex Karp**: co-founder and CEO of **Palantir Technologies** (2003, with Thiel). Palantir provides software to government and corporate clients for the integration and analysis of personal data at scale. Contracts include: US Immigration and Customs Enforcement (over $200 million in deportation operations since 2014); the UK National Health Service (a £330 million contract awarded in 2023 to manage NHS patient data); the Israel Defense Forces (contracts of undisclosed value, used in Gaza targeting operations 2023-25); the US Army ($800 million in battlefield AI contracts, 2024). Palantir's market capitalisation in 2026 is $400 billion, larger than Lockheed Martin and Northrop Grumman combined. The company is the AI-military-industrial layer's leading supplier.

- **Larry Ellison**: founder of Oracle 1977. Net worth $200 billion. Owns 98 percent of the Hawaiian island of Lanai. Major Republican donor. Father (Larry) and son (David, who runs Skydance Media and in 2024 purchased Paramount Global, including CBS News) increasingly active in the right-wing media consolidation of the mid-2020s.

- **Jeff Bezos**: founder of Amazon 1994. Founder of Blue Origin 2000. Owner of *The Washington Post* (purchased 2013 for $250 million). Amazon Web Services signed a $600 million contract with the CIA in 2013 to build the agency's classified cloud infrastructure; AWS-Government is now an $30 billion annual business, providing cloud infrastructure to the CIA, NSA, Department of Defense, and other classified clients. The man who owns the largest cloud-computing business is the man who owns the *Washington Post* — the second-largest American newspaper, the paper that broke Watergate, the paper that is now structurally aligned with the intelligence community through its owner's largest business relationship.

- **Mark Zuckerberg**: founder of Facebook 2004 (now Meta). Through dual-class share structure, owns 56 percent of the voting power of Meta. The platform he controls has 4 billion monthly active users across Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Threads. There is no shareholder vote that could remove him. There is no regulatory authority in any country that has been able to make him change Meta's content moderation policies against his will. He is, in the precise technical sense, the unilateral controller of one of the principal informational substrates of the global population. The 2024 election cycle saw Meta dramatically reduce its enforcement of political misinformation on its platforms; the reduction was implemented by Zuckerberg's direct decision, after publicly visible signalling from incoming Trump administration officials.

- **Eric Schmidt**: Google CEO 2001-2011; Executive Chairman of Alphabet 2011-2017. Chair of the US Defense Innovation Board 2016-2020 and of the National Security Commission on Artificial Intelligence 2018-2021. The man who ran Google for a decade wrote, while Chair of the NSCAI, the policy recommendations that became the basis for the integration of AI capabilities into US national security operations under the Biden and now Trump administrations. The revolving door operates not only between Wall Street and Treasury but between Silicon Valley and the Pentagon.

This is the layer that has, since 2010, displaced the older finance-and-defence oligarchy as the dominant cultural and increasingly political fraction of the American class. The pattern of the displacement is the standard pattern: the older fraction made the money; the younger fraction is using the money to remake the political system. Thiel funded Vance into the Vice Presidency. Musk funded Trump and ran the DOGE. Sacks runs AI and crypto policy. Andreessen runs venture capital and a manifesto. Karp runs the surveillance and military AI layer. They are not pretending to represent democratic interests. They are pretending, when pressed, to represent technological progress. The arithmetic of their actual political position is straightforward: they are billionaires who have purchased political offices in order to legalise the technical operations of their firms and to dismantle the regulatory state that constrains them. They are, by the record of their own statements, openly hostile to democracy. The question is no longer whether they intend to replace the existing order with a corporate-authoritarian alternative. They have said they intend to. The question is whether the rest of us notice in time to organise an alternative.

## VII. The Epstein-Wexner-Maxwell apparatus

The financial and technological layers of the oligarchy are now well-documented. What is less well-documented, but is now largely on the public record, is the **social and compromise layer** through which the oligarchy maintains internal coordination and external leverage. The principal documented example is the network around Jeffrey Epstein.

The basic facts, as established by court records, sworn testimony, and verified investigative reporting:

- **Jeffrey Epstein** (1953-2019): mathematics teacher at Dalton School (New York) 1974-76. Bear Stearns trader 1976-81. Founded J. Epstein & Co. (later Financial Trust Company) in 1982. Net worth at death $578 million. Source of wealth: never satisfactorily explained.

- **Leslie Wexner** (born 1937): founder of L Brands (Victoria's Secret, Bath & Body Works). Net worth $6 billion. Met Epstein in 1986. In 1989 — when Epstein was an unknown 36-year-old former teacher with no Wall Street track record — Wexner granted Epstein full power of attorney over his $1.4 billion business empire and his foundation. In 1996 Wexner transferred to Epstein, at no cost, the Manhattan townhouse at 9 East 71st Street, valued at $77 million. No coherent business explanation for the transfer has ever been publicly offered by either party. In June 2008, four days before Epstein's plea hearing, Wexner sent Epstein an email reading, in part: "I feel sorry. You violated your own number 1 rule…always be careful." The email was disclosed in 2025 in the DOJ's partial document release.

- **The Mega Group**: founded in 1991 by Wexner and **Charles Bronfman** (heir to the Seagram fortune). A coordination group of twenty American Jewish billionaires whose stated purpose was pro-Israel philanthropy and political coordination. Members included Edgar Bronfman Sr., Ronald Lauder, Michael Steinhardt, and others. **Maria Farmer**, an Epstein abuse survivor named in court documents as Jane Doe 200, testified in sworn statements that the Mega Group was associated with the Epstein operation and that Wexner was a key figure in coordinating it.

- **Robert Maxwell** (1923-1991): Ghislaine Maxwell's father. British media mogul, owner of the *Daily Mirror* and the *New York Daily News*. **Worked for British intelligence (MI6)** during and after the Second World War. **Was a Mossad asset** — documented in Gordon Thomas and Martin Dillon's *Robert Maxwell: Israel's Superspy* (2002), in the BBC documentary series *House of Maxwell* (2022), and in public Israeli sources including the eulogy delivered at his funeral by then-Prime Minister **Yitzhak Shamir**, who praised Maxwell for the services he had rendered to the State of Israel. Maxwell died in November 1991 falling from his yacht near the Canary Islands under circumstances never resolved by official inquiry. He was buried on the Mount of Olives in Jerusalem; the funeral was attended by at least six current or former heads of Israeli intelligence services.

- **Ghislaine Maxwell** (born 1961): Robert Maxwell's youngest daughter. Moved from London to New York after her father's death and the financial collapse of his publishing empire. Met Epstein in 1991 or 1992. Functioned as Epstein's partner, recruiter, and procurer for two decades. Convicted in December 2021 by a federal jury of five counts including sex trafficking of minors; sentenced June 2022 to twenty years. Currently in federal custody; eligible for release 2037.

- **Alexander Acosta**: US Attorney for the Southern District of Florida who approved Epstein's 2008 non-prosecution agreement — a deal that gave Epstein 18 months in county jail with twelve-hour daily work release, despite FBI investigators having identified at least thirty victims. The arrangement also granted immunity to unnamed "co-conspirators" and was kept from the victims themselves in violation, a federal court later ruled, of the Crime Victims Rights Act. In 2017, during his vetting for Labor Secretary in the first Trump administration, Acosta was reported by journalist Vicky Ward (writing in the *Daily Beast*, July 2019) to have told Trump transition officials that he had been told to "back off" the Epstein case because Epstein "belonged to intelligence." Acosta denied this account under oath to the House Oversight Committee in October 2025. The original Ward reporting remains. Steve Bannon has been named as a likely source for Ward's account.

- **Ehud Barak**: former Prime Minister of Israel (1999-2001), former Defence Minister, former general. Met Epstein on 36 occasions between 2013 and 2017 — documented through visitor logs and photographs at Epstein's Manhattan townhouse. Received $2.3 million from the **Wexner Foundation** in 2004, described as payment for "research."

- **Steven Hoffenberg**: Epstein's partner in the Towers Financial Ponzi scheme (Hoffenberg convicted 1995, served 18 years). Stated publicly before his 2022 death that Epstein had repeatedly told him he had Mossad ties and credited those ties for his unexplained wealth and elite access.

- **The 2024 court releases**: hundreds of pages of unsealed Epstein-related court documents in January 2024 named 170 individuals with documented contact with Epstein. The names included Bill Clinton, Donald Trump, Bill Gates, Leon Black, Stephen Hawking, Prince Andrew, Mort Zuckerman, Alan Dershowitz, Glenn Dubin, and many others — figures from the senior political class of both American parties, the technology sector, the finance sector, the academic and scientific establishments, and European royalty.

- **The July 2025 DOJ memo**: the US Department of Justice and FBI issued a joint memo in July 2025 stating that no further Epstein-related documents would be released, that there was no "client list," and that Epstein's death in August 2019 had been a suicide. The memo broke with explicit public commitments made by Trump, Attorney General Pam Bondi, and FBI Director Kash Patel during the 2024 campaign that Epstein-related files would be released in full upon Trump's return to office. The memo split the Trump coalition publicly; as of May 2026 the matter remains an open political controversy. Republican Representative Anna Paulina Luna (R-FL) stated in September 2025, after meeting Epstein survivors: "It's clear this thing is a lot bigger than we could have anticipated — rich and powerful people need to go to prison. It is possible Epstein was a foreign intelligence asset."

What the record shows is the following. A man with no plausible business background was, beginning in 1989, given control of $1.4 billion and a Manhattan townhouse by Leslie Wexner, the founder of a major American retail empire and a co-founder of an explicitly pro-Israel billionaires' coordination group. The same man, beginning in 1992, formed a partnership with the daughter of a documented Mossad asset, herself moving from her father's collapsed publishing empire to the same Manhattan circles. The pair, over the next twenty-five years, ran what court documents and survivor testimony establish was a sex-trafficking operation involving minors, in which the participants included senior figures from finance, politics, science, royalty, and the technology sector across multiple Western democracies. The operation was equipped with surveillance cameras in every room of the participants' residences. The federal investigation of the operation was, in 2008, terminated by a non-prosecution agreement that one of its architects later reportedly described as having been imposed because the principal "belonged to intelligence." When the matter resurfaced in 2018-19, the principal died in federal custody under circumstances the official inquiry ruled a suicide and which a large portion of the American public and political class no longer believes was a suicide. The records of the operation are, as of May 2026, sealed by an administration that had publicly committed to releasing them.

This is, in the precise *con-spirare* sense, a documented conspiracy. The principal characters are dead, in prison, or have died under disputed circumstances. The surviving records have been partially released and then sealed. The political establishment of both American parties has acted, in effect, to prevent the full record from becoming public. The most plausible reason for the bipartisan sealing is that the full record names members of both parties' senior figures in compromising positions. The operation's purpose was, in the technical intelligence-services sense, the production of leverage — compromising material that could be used to ensure compliance from senior figures across the institutions described in the rest of this chapter. The mechanism is older than the modern intelligence services; the Mossad uses it, the KGB used it, the CIA has used it, the FSB uses it now. The Epstein operation is the recorded American case study from the late 20th and early 21st centuries. Its existence is no longer plausibly deniable. Its scope, in terms of the named participants, is the same scope as the oligarchy described in the earlier parts of this chapter. The same names recur because the apparatus was designed to compromise these names. It worked.

## VIII. The artificial intelligence consolidation

The most recent fraction of the consolidation is artificial intelligence. The labs that have, since 2020, taken AI capabilities from research curiosity to industrial deployment are owned, funded, or controlled by the same group of investors who own the rest of the system.

- **OpenAI**: founded 2015 by Sam Altman, Elon Musk, Reid Hoffman, Greg Brockman, Ilya Sutskever, Wojciech Zaremba, and John Schulman. Originally a non-profit; converted to a "capped-profit" structure in 2019; in 2025 in the process of restructuring further toward conventional for-profit ownership. **Microsoft** has invested $13 billion. Valuation $500 billion as of late 2025.

- **Anthropic**: founded 2021 by Dario Amodei, Daniela Amodei, and others who had left OpenAI. Investors include **Google** and **Amazon** with combined investments of $8 billion, plus Spark Capital, Salesforce, and others. Valuation $170 billion in early 2025. **This book is being drafted using Claude, Anthropic's AI assistant. See the author's note below.**

- **Google DeepMind**: Alphabet subsidiary, formed 2023 by the merger of Google Brain and the UK-founded DeepMind (acquired by Google 2014). Funded by Alphabet's $90 billion annual operating cash flow.

- **xAI**: founded by Elon Musk in 2023, operates the Grok chatbot, integrated with X (Twitter). Valuation $80 billion in 2024. Owned personally by Musk.
