- **Meta AI**: Zuckerberg's Meta has invested heavily in the LLaMA family of open-weights models and in AGI research. Owned through Meta's controlling shareholder Zuckerberg.

- **Microsoft AI**: in addition to its OpenAI investment, Microsoft has built a major internal AI division. From 2024 it was headed by Mustafa Suleyman, former DeepMind co-founder, who departed in 2025.

Five companies, owned by the same dozen individuals, control all of the large-language-model and frontier-AI capability that is being deployed at population scale. The capital allocations are coordinated: Microsoft into OpenAI; Google and Amazon into Anthropic; Musk personally into xAI; Meta internally; Microsoft also internally. The combined valuation of the major AI labs is, as of early 2026, one trillion dollars. The capital required to train frontier models has now exceeded the capital available to any independent academic or open-source effort. The leading-edge AI capabilities are, structurally, a captive technology of the same circle that owns the rest of the economy.

The implications are not theoretical. The principal applications of AI in production, as of 2026, are:

- **Labour management**: AI systems are increasingly used to schedule workers, evaluate their performance, terminate them, and replace them. Amazon's warehouse algorithms have been the public case study; the practice is now general across logistics, retail, healthcare, customer service, and white-collar professional services.
- **Content moderation and information governance**: the AI systems of Meta, Google (YouTube), X, and TikTok determine what political content the public sees. The decisions are not voted on.
- **Military targeting**: Palantir, Anduril, and increasingly the major AI labs supply targeting and battle-management AI to the US, UK, and Israeli militaries. The Israeli "Lavender" and "Gospel" AI targeting systems used in Gaza 2023-25 were described in *+972 Magazine* and the *Guardian* in April 2024 as having authorised airstrikes against 37,000 designated targets with minimal human review.
- **Surveillance**: Palantir's deployments with US ICE, with the UK NHS, with multiple police departments, and the corresponding deployments of Chinese AI surveillance firms, mean that the technology to monitor populations at scale has been built and is in operation across most major jurisdictions.
- **Public discourse**: language models are now used at scale to generate political commentary, news articles, marketing content, and influence operations. The technology to automate persuasion at population scale has been deployed.

The system described in this chapter — the financial-corporate-political oligarchy of 1,500 individuals — now possesses, through its ownership of the AI labs, the technological capability to monitor, manage, and progressively replace the political functions of the democratic state. It is, as of 2026, in the early phase of using this capability. The next phase is described in the books that the techno-oligarchs themselves have written about their intentions: Andreessen's *Techno-Optimist Manifesto* (2023), Schwab's *The Fourth Industrial Revolution* (2016), Yarvin's various writings about neocameralism, Thiel's published interviews and 2009 Cato essay, Karp's *The Technological Republic* (2025). These men are not hiding their plans. The plans are published. They are advocating, in print, the replacement of liberal-democratic governance with technocratic-authoritarian governance run by the people who already own the system. They are funding the political candidates who will execute the plan. The plan is, in 2026, in operation.

---

**Author's note on the writing of this book.** This book has been drafted using Claude, Anthropic's large language model. Anthropic is one of the companies named in this chapter, owned in major part by Google and Amazon, which are themselves owned in major part by the Big Three asset managers described in the chapter's first section. The tool I have been using to write this exposé of the system is, structurally, a product of the system.

I have considered this carefully and decided that the alternative — not using the tool — produces a worse outcome than using it. The alternative is a book that takes ten years to write rather than a year, that cannot cover the documentary record at the depth this book requires, and that arrives too late to be useful in the political moment we are in. Using the tool requires that I treat every factual claim it produces as a hypothesis to be checked against the primary record. That is what I have done. The tool has been useful in finding the documentary citations, in cross-referencing the sources, in identifying the points at which my arguments needed sharpening, and in maintaining the bibliographic apparatus that allows readers to check my claims against the same primary sources I used.

I am not endorsing the consolidation described in this chapter by using the tool that is one of its products. I am using the tool to expose the consolidation, in the same way that a journalist might use a corporate-owned phone to record a corporate executive's incriminating statements. The instrumentality of the tool does not transfer the legitimacy of the institutions that built it. The book remains the work of its named author. The tool's contribution has been editorial and bibliographic, checked against the primary record at every point. The reader is invited to do the same checking. Every factual claim in this book is sourced in the published companion volume of citations. The sources are publicly accessible. The argument can be tested.

The point of disclosing this is that the book's argument applies, with full force, to its own composition. The tools by which we communicate, organise, and resist are owned by the people we are resisting. We use them anyway because the alternative is to be silent. The system has captured the means of expression. We speak through the means. We speak honestly. We name the capture, including the capture of our own voice. That is what this note does.

---

The next chapter is about the capture of knowing itself — the universities, the journals, the peer review system, the medical and scientific establishments through which the population learns what is true. The section after that is about the alternatives that have been working, on small scales, in suppressed and ignored places, for decades, and that constitute the actual outline of the post-system world that is now necessary.


---

# Chapter 9: The Capture of Knowing

The previous eight chapters have made a case. The case is documentable. The sources are public. The conclusions follow from elementary arithmetic. Any reader with a library card and a calculator can verify the argument.

So why is this not the standard account?

Why is the case made by the previous chapters not taught in mainstream universities, not covered by mainstream press, not discussed by mainstream politicians, not the consensus position of the educated middle class? The book has answered the question implicitly across the previous eight chapters. This chapter answers it explicitly. The reason the case is not the standard account is that the institutions that produce the standard account have been captured by the same circle that the case names. The economics departments, the medical schools, the drug regulators, the public-health bodies, the peer-reviewed journals, the major news organisations: each of these institutions has been, across the past forty years, progressively captured by funding from the people the case implicates. The capture is documented. The mechanism is the same in each case. The result is a population that knows what the institutions have permitted it to know and does not know what they have not.

This is the precise functional definition of an ideology that has taken hold of an intelligent species. The species has the cognitive capacity to do the arithmetic. The species has been prevented from doing the arithmetic in this context. The prevention operates through the institutions that, in principle, would do the arithmetic. The institutions have been bought. The record describes how.

The record has seven parts. The first describes the **university capture** through targeted funding of economics, law, and medical departments. The second describes the **replication crisis** that has been quietly demonstrating, across the past fifteen years, that a large fraction of published peer-reviewed science is not reliable. The third describes the **regulatory capture of the FDA** through the user-fee model that has, since 1992, transferred majority funding of drug review from public appropriations to industry payments. The fourth uses the **Sackler-Purdue-OxyContin case** as the textbook example of what regulatory capture produces in practice. The fifth describes the **Gates Foundation's capture of global public health and journalism**. The sixth describes the **suppression of the COVID lab-leak hypothesis** from January 2020 to mid-2023, the largest documented case of scientific censorship in recent memory. The seventh describes the **media consolidation** that has reduced fifty independent news organisations in 1983 to five today, and that has, in parallel with the academic and regulatory captures, completed the enclosure of the public's access to honest information about the system the book describes.

## I. The university capture

The mechanism is funding. The mechanism has been funding since the Mont Pèlerin Society was capitalised by the Volker Fund in 1947. The mechanism has not changed. What has changed is the scale.

The principal documented funders of right-wing academic economics over the past forty years are the Olin Foundation (chemical industry money, dissolved 2005 having spent its endowment as planned), the Bradley Foundation (Allen-Bradley electronics money, continues), the Scaife Foundations (Mellon banking and steel money, continues), and the Koch family foundations. Of these, the Kochs are now by far the largest. **Charles Koch** has, through the Charles Koch Foundation and the Charles Koch Institute, funded 350 American universities. The largest single recipient is **George Mason University** in Virginia, which hosts:

- The **Mercatus Center** (founded 1980, renamed 1999), a research centre that produces 300 academic papers and policy reports per year, all of which argue for the policy positions of Charles Koch's industrial interests: reduced taxation, reduced regulation, reduced public spending, weaker labour protections, weaker environmental standards. Roughly $10 million per year in Koch funding, plus academic appointments controlled in practice by the Koch network.
- The **Antonin Scalia Law School**, renamed in 2016 after a $30 million combined donation from the Charles Koch Foundation ($10 million) and an anonymous donor ($20 million, widely believed to have been routed by the Federalist Society network). The renaming was conditioned, in the donation agreement, on the school's continued operation along Federalist Society lines. The school has, since the renaming, produced clerks for Federalist Society Supreme Court justices at a rate disproportionate to its size and reputation. It is the pipeline.

The mechanism is not, on its face, illegal. It is private philanthropy directed to public universities. What makes it different from ordinary philanthropy is that the funding is conditioned on specific intellectual outputs and on specific hiring decisions. In **2008**, Charles Koch's foundation signed a contract with Florida State University's economics department under which the foundation provided $1.5 million in exchange for an advisory board including Koch representatives with veto power over hiring decisions for two endowed chairs. The contract was disclosed by the *St. Petersburg Times* in 2011 after a public records request. The university initially defended the arrangement; under sustained public criticism it later revised the terms, but the underlying funding relationship and similar arrangements at other universities continued. The model is now widespread. As of the most recent comprehensive survey, the Charles Koch Foundation funded faculty positions, fellowships, or specific programmes at more than 350 American colleges and universities. Many of these arrangements remain confidential. The ones that have been disclosed have, with consistent regularity, shown contractual mechanisms by which the funder retains influence over academic personnel decisions.

The mechanism is general. The Sackler family did the same thing with academic medicine for thirty years. **Sackler-named** galleries, libraries, departments, and institutes were established at Yale, Harvard, Columbia, Tufts (which housed the Sackler School of Graduate Biomedical Sciences), Cornell, Oxford, Cambridge, Edinburgh, and at major museums including the Smithsonian, the Metropolitan Museum, the Tate, the Louvre, the Guggenheim, and the National Portrait Gallery. The funding was the proceeds of OxyContin sales. The institutions accepted the money knowing where it came from. The institutions printed the Sackler name on their buildings. After 2019, following the public protest organised by the photographer Nan Goldin and her organisation PAIN, most institutions quietly removed the Sackler name. None refunded the money. The institutions had taken twenty years of opioid-derived donations, attached the family's name to their buildings, and given the family's marketing campaign the legitimacy of association with the world's leading scientific and cultural institutions, during the same years that Purdue Pharma's marketing was generating the deaths that produced the donations.

The closer one looks at the funding of American higher education, the more visible the pattern becomes. **Jeffrey Epstein** donated $7.5 million to the MIT Media Lab after his 2008 conviction for soliciting prostitution from a minor. The donations were processed knowing the source. Ronan Farrow's *New Yorker* exposé of 6 September 2019, based on internal MIT documents, showed that **Joi Ito**, director of the Media Lab, had marked Epstein as "disqualified" as a donor while continuing to take his money, that the Media Lab's general fund had been used as a laundering channel, and that senior MIT administrators including President L. Rafael Reif had been informed and approved. Ito resigned two days after the exposé. The subsequent MIT investigation by the law firm Goodwin Procter (December 2019 report) confirmed the substance of Farrow's reporting. **Harvard** had also taken Epstein money, less publicly. Various senior Harvard figures — Stephen Pinker, Lawrence Krauss, Alan Dershowitz — had been social acquaintances of Epstein and had visited his properties. The connections were known to the institutions and accepted.

The single biographical example that summarises the mechanism is **Larry Summers**. Summers became President of Harvard in 2001. He resigned in 2006 after twin scandals: his comments at a 2005 NBER conference suggesting that women's underrepresentation in science might reflect innate cognitive differences, and his handling of the **Andrei Shleifer** Russian-economic-reform fraud (Shleifer, a Harvard economics professor and Summers's close personal friend, had been found liable in federal court for using a Harvard-led USAID contract to enrich himself and his wife through Russian privatisation investments — Harvard paid a $26.5 million settlement to the federal government). Summers's subsequent career trajectory:

- **D.E. Shaw**: hedge fund. Summers worked one day per week and was paid $5.2 million in 2008.
- **National Economic Council Director**, January 2009 – November 2010 (Obama administration). The man who had pushed the Gramm-Leach-Bliley repeal and the Commodity Futures Modernization Act in the late 1990s, the policies that had produced the 2008 crisis, returned to government to advise on the response to the crisis his policies had produced.
- **Harvard professor** again, 2011 onward.
- **OpenAI board member**, 2023-present. The man who had advised on the deregulation of derivatives that produced the 2008 crisis was placed on the board of the company that, by 2024, was the leading developer of the artificial intelligence systems being deployed at population scale.

This trajectory — from Harvard president to hedge fund to White House to Harvard professor to OpenAI board — is not a single individual's biography. It is the structural pattern of the academic-financial-political class. The same names recur across these positions because the positions have been arranged to recruit the same names. The academic economists who advise the policy of the system are the policy makers of the system. The credential is not academic distinction. The credential is reliable defence of the system.

## II. The replication crisis

In parallel with the funding-driven capture of academic outputs, the past fifteen years have produced a quiet and ongoing crisis in the reliability of peer-reviewed research itself. The crisis is technical and is not principally a question of corrupt funding; it is a question of the methodological standards that have, across the entire enterprise of academic publishing in the sciences and social sciences, produced a body of "established" findings of which a large fraction does not survive attempted replication. The crisis is publicly acknowledged within the affected disciplines. It is not yet publicly acknowledged in the political and journalistic discussion that draws on those disciplines for its empirical authority.

The most prominent documented example, **psychology**: the Reproducibility Project: Psychology, led by Brian Nosek of the University of Virginia and the Center for Open Science, attempted in 2015 to replicate 100 published studies from three leading psychology journals. The results were published in *Science* on 28 August 2015 ("Estimating the reproducibility of psychological science"). The finding: of the 100 published studies, only **39 successfully replicated** under the standard of producing a statistically significant effect in the same direction. **Sixty-one percent of the published findings did not replicate.** The original effect sizes were on average half as large as the published claims. The effect was uniform across sub-disciplines. The published literature in academic psychology — the basis of textbooks, of policy advice, of public discussion of human behaviour — is, by this analysis, more wrong than right.

The same pattern, in **cancer biology**: in 2012, **C. Glenn Begley**, formerly head of haematology and oncology research at Amgen, published in *Nature* (March 29, 2012) the result of an Amgen attempt to replicate 53 "landmark" preclinical cancer studies. Only **6 of 53** replicated. The same conclusion: a large majority of the published basic-science findings on which subsequent drug development depended could not be reproduced. The drugs that had been developed downstream from those findings had been, in many cases, developed downstream from artefacts.

The pattern, in **economics**: the **Reinhart-Rogoff Excel error**, 2013. The case is so clean and so consequential that it deserves the most careful treatment.

In 2010 **Carmen Reinhart** and **Kenneth Rogoff** — both of Harvard, both former senior officials at the IMF — published in the *American Economic Review* (Papers and Proceedings) a paper titled *Growth in a Time of Debt*. The paper's headline finding was that countries with a public debt-to-GDP ratio above 90 percent experience, on average, **negative economic growth** of -0.1 percent. The paper appeared at the exact moment the European sovereign debt crisis was beginning and the global political class was deciding how to respond. The 90-percent number was simple, memorable, and arithmetically extreme; it became, within months, the standard scientific justification for the austerity programmes that would be imposed across the developed world for the following decade. **Paul Ryan**, the chairman of the US House Budget Committee, cited the paper. **George Osborne**, the UK Chancellor of the Exchequer, cited the paper. **Olli Rehn**, the European Commissioner for Economic Affairs, cited the paper. The IMF cited the paper. The 90-percent threshold became, in policy discussion across the Atlantic, the technocratic line beyond which fiscal expansion was held to be self-defeating, and beneath which it was supposedly permissible.

The austerity programmes that followed — the Greek bailout terms imposing 27 percent unemployment, the British austerity programme that produced the first sustained decline in UK life expectancy in modern history, the Spanish and Italian and Portuguese contractions, the recovery delays across the Eurozone — were carried out on the empirical authority of this paper. Roughly 130,000 additional UK deaths between 2012 and 2019 have been attributed to austerity in subsequent University College London and Bristol University studies. The Greek suicide rate increased by 35 percent. Hundreds of millions of people across the developed world experienced the political consequences of an empirical finding that, three years later, would turn out to be wrong.

In April 2013, **Thomas Herndon**, a PhD student at the University of Massachusetts Amherst, was attempting to use Reinhart and Rogoff's analysis as the basis for a class exercise. He requested the original Excel spreadsheet from the authors. They sent it. He could not reproduce their results. He worked through the spreadsheet with his professors **Michael Ash** and **Robert Pollin** and identified three errors:

1. **The Excel coding error**. In their averaging formula, Reinhart and Rogoff had failed to extend a cell selection across all twenty countries in their dataset. Five countries — Australia, Austria, Belgium, Canada, and Denmark — had been excluded from the calculation by a simple cell-selection mistake.
2. **Selective data exclusion**. Years in which several of the included countries had simultaneously high debt and high growth had been excluded from the dataset by methodological choices that were not justified in the paper.
3. **Inappropriate weighting**. The weighting method used to aggregate across countries gave equal weight to one year of New Zealand data (which had a single high-debt year with a 7.6 percent contraction) and to eighteen years of Australian high-debt data, distorting the average.

When all three errors were corrected, the headline finding evaporated. Countries with debt-to-GDP above 90 percent had average growth of **+2.2 percent**, not -0.1 percent. The 90-percent cliff did not exist. The paper had been wrong.

Herndon, Ash, and Pollin published their critique in the *Cambridge Journal of Economics* (2014). The mainstream economics profession acknowledged the errors. Reinhart and Rogoff issued a partial correction. No retraction was issued. The Excel error was widely reported in the business press for two weeks, was treated as embarrassing, and then dropped from public discussion. **The austerity programmes the paper had justified were not reversed.** The Greek terms were not renegotiated. The UK austerity continued through 2019. The European Commission continued to impose the Stability and Growth Pact. The policy machine, having found a use for the paper, continued to act on its conclusions long after the paper had been falsified. The error had served its purpose. Its falsification was not allowed to undo the purpose.

This is the structural feature. The replication crisis is, in the technical sciences, a real and acknowledged crisis. In the policy-relevant social sciences, the replication crisis is a tool. Findings that support the system's policy preferences are published, cited, and acted upon, even when the underlying methodology is later shown to be wrong. Findings that contradict the system's policy preferences — that high inequality reduces growth, that progressive taxation does not destroy investment, that public investment does not crowd out private investment, that minimum wage increases do not destroy employment — are subjected to standards of scrutiny so demanding that they cannot accumulate enough authority to influence policy. The asymmetry is the mechanism. The literature appears even-handed. The policy uses are not.

## III. The FDA and the user-fee model

If the academic layer has been captured through funding and the replication crisis has been instrumentalised through selective citation, the regulatory layer has been captured through a more elegant and more devastating mechanism. The drug regulator is now paid by the drug companies.

The **Prescription Drug User Fee Act (PDUFA)**, signed into law on 29 October 1992, gave the Food and Drug Administration the authority to collect fees from pharmaceutical companies in exchange for accelerated review of New Drug Applications. The stated justification was that the FDA was under-resourced, was producing approval delays that harmed patients waiting for new medicines, and could not get Congressional appropriations sufficient to clear the backlog. The pharmaceutical industry, the argument went, would pay user fees to provide the missing resources. The FDA would, in return, commit to review timelines. The fees would augment, not replace, the public funding.

The augmentation became the funding. In the first year of operation (FY1993), industry user fees accounted for 7 percent of the FDA's prescription drug review programme budget. By 2007, the user-fee share had grown to about 20 percent of the entire FDA budget. By 2017, when the *New England Journal of Medicine* published a comprehensive analysis ("Speed, Safety, and Industry Funding — From PDUFA I to PDUFA VI"), the assessment was that **industry user fees were paying 75 percent of the costs of FDA's scientific review of drugs**. By fiscal year 2022, user fees provided **66 percent of the human drugs program budget**. By fiscal year 2025, user fees totalled $3.3 billion — **49 percent of the FDA's total operating budget** and the majority of its drug, biologics, and medical device review programmes.

The regulator is now majority-funded by the regulated. This is regulatory capture not in the metaphorical sense of "industry influence on regulators" but in the precise structural sense of "regulators paid by industry." When the FDA's drug review reviewers are paid for, in 2018, at the 80 percent level by industry user fees (Darrow, Kesselheim, et al., 2020), the reviewers' careers and salaries depend on the continued flow of industry money to the agency. The industry's structural leverage over the agency is no longer a function of lobbying. It is a function of accounting. The agency cannot operate without the industry's money.

The PDUFA mechanism has, across its six reauthorisations (1992, 1997, 2002, 2007, 2012, 2017, 2022), also progressively expanded the industry's role in the FDA's policy. The reauthorisation negotiations between the FDA and the industry — formally required by statute — produce policy decisions on:

- New drug application review timelines.
- Industry meeting access to FDA scientific staff during the drug development process.
- Expedited approval pathways (breakthrough therapy designation, accelerated approval, fast track).
- Standards for industry dissemination of off-label use information to physicians.
- Rules governing generic drug entry.

Each of these decisions is, by statute, negotiated between the regulator and the regulated. The Congress that nominally oversees the process is provided with the negotiated package as a fait accompli. The reauthorisations are treated as "must-pass" legislation because failure to reauthorise would interrupt the agency's funding. The Congressional role has, over thirty years, been reduced to ratification.

This is the architecture. The pharmaceutical industry pays the regulator. The pharmaceutical industry negotiates the rules of its own regulation. The pharmaceutical industry, every five years, ratifies the terms of its own oversight in legislation that Congress is structurally compelled to pass. The American public, which is the population the regulator was created to protect, has no representative in this negotiation. The model has been transposed to medical devices, generic drugs, biosimilars, and tobacco. The same pattern operates at the European Medicines Agency, the UK MHRA, and the regulatory agencies of every developed country.

## IV. The Sackler-Purdue case study

What the user-fee capture of the regulator produces in practice can be seen in the canonical case: the OxyContin epidemic. The case study has been documented in detail by Patrick Radden Keefe in *Empire of Pain* (2021), by the 2006 federal prosecution memo released in 2019, by the depositions and discovery in the federal multidistrict litigation, and by the work of Beth Macy and other journalists. The basic facts are not contested.

**OxyContin** is an extended-release formulation of oxycodone, a powerful opioid analgesic. It was developed by Purdue Pharma, owned by the **Sackler family**. The drug's commercial premise was that the extended-release mechanism — a coating that released the active ingredient over 12 hours rather than all at once — would reduce the drug's abuse potential by limiting the peak blood-concentration spike that produces the euphoria associated with opioid abuse. The premise was untested. No clinical study had demonstrated that the extended-release mechanism reduced abuse liability. The premise was, in commercial substance, an unsubstantiated marketing claim.

For OxyContin to be marketed as a less-addictive opioid, the claim had to appear on the FDA-approved label. The FDA-approved label is, in American pharmaceutical practice, the document that controls what can be said in marketing, in physician detailing, and in continuing-medical-education sponsorship. If the label said the drug was less addictive, then everything Purdue Pharma's army of sales representatives subsequently said about the drug being less addictive would be legally protected speech. If the label did not say it, the sales representatives could not say it either. The label was the licence.

The FDA's review of the OxyContin New Drug Application was assigned to a single medical review officer: **Dr. Curtis Wright IV**. Wright was, on paper, the embodiment of public-interest regulatory science. He was a Navy veteran with medical and addiction-medicine credentials. He had no public history of pharmaceutical industry ties at the time of his FDA assignment.

Between 31 January and 2 February 1995, Wright met with three Purdue Pharma executives in a hotel room near the FDA offices in Rockville, Maryland. The meeting lasted three days. The 2006 federal prosecution memo, released in 2019, established that during this three-day hotel-room meeting Wright permitted the Purdue Pharma executives to **help draft his official FDA Medical Officer's Review** — the document that, under FDA procedure, is supposed to be the regulator's independent assessment of the New Drug Application. Including in the documents Wright permitted Purdue to help draft was the language for OxyContin's package insert. The language that emerged from the hotel-room meeting and was approved by Wright on his return to the FDA read: "Delayed absorption, as provided by OxyContin tablets, is believed to reduce the abuse liability of the drug."

This was the marketing claim Purdue Pharma needed. It was now, with FDA imprimatur, the regulatory label. OxyContin was approved by the FDA in December 1995 with that language on its label. It went to market in 1996.

In 1996, Wright resigned from the FDA. He took a brief intervening position at a small pharmaceutical company called Adolor. **In 1998, two years after he signed the OxyContin approval, Wright was hired by Purdue Pharma as Executive Director of Medical Research at a starting salary of $379,000 per year — three times his FDA salary.** He worked at Purdue for the following decade.

This is the textbook case of regulatory capture by revolving door. The regulator who approved the drug with the false abuse-liability claim was, two years later, employed by the company that benefited from the claim, at a salary that constituted, in effect, a deferred payment for the approval. Wright has maintained in subsequent depositions that he had no conversations with Purdue about employment while at the FDA. The 2006 DOJ memo, available in the public record, suggests strongly otherwise. No prosecution was brought. Wright was never charged.

What followed is the deadliest pharmaceutical disaster in American history. Purdue Pharma's marketing campaign for OxyContin, run on the FDA-approved abuse-liability claim, generated $35 billion in revenue between 1996 and 2017. The Sackler family extracted an estimated $11 billion in dividends from the company during those years. The marketing campaign trained American physicians to prescribe OxyContin for chronic non-cancer pain — a market Purdue itself estimated to be ten times larger than the cancer-pain market for which opioids had previously been the standard of care. Prescription opioid sales increased tenfold between 1997 and 2002. The American medical community received continuing-medical-education programmes funded by Purdue that taught the false claim. The Joint Commission, the organisation that accredits American hospitals, adopted in 2001 the slogan "pain as the fifth vital sign," explicitly developed by Purdue's marketing department and adopted into the standards of practice for every American hospital.

By the time the falsity of the abuse-liability claim was publicly acknowledged in the 2007 Purdue plea agreement (three executives, none of them Sacklers, pleaded guilty to federal misbranding charges; the company paid $634.5 million; no one went to prison; the Sacklers personally retained their accumulated dividends), the American opioid epidemic was fully launched. The prescription-opioid wave produced the heroin wave (former OxyContin users moving to street heroin as prescribing was tightened post-2010). The heroin wave produced the fentanyl wave (Chinese-precursor fentanyl flooding into the heroin supply from 2013 onward). Between 1999 and 2024, **727,000 Americans died of opioid overdoses**. The wave is the largest peacetime public health disaster in American history, and it began in a hotel room in Rockville, Maryland, over three days in early 1995.

The Sacklers were never criminally prosecuted. The 2020 federal settlement required Purdue Pharma to plead guilty to three felonies and to pay $8.3 billion — most of which was an accounting figure that the bankrupt company could not actually pay. The Sacklers personally agreed to pay $4.5 billion (increased to $6 billion in 2022 after the Supreme Court rejected the family's bid for civil immunity through the bankruptcy). The family remained billionaires. No Sackler has ever spent a day in prison for what their company did. **McKinsey & Company**, which had advised Purdue throughout the marketing campaign and had explicitly recommended in a 2017 PowerPoint that Purdue pay distributors a $14,810 rebate for each opioid-overdose customer to maintain sales volume, settled with state attorneys general in February 2021 for $573 million. No McKinsey partner was prosecuted.

The case is not unique in its mechanics. It is unique only in the documentation and the death toll. Vioxx (Merck, withdrawn 2004, 40,000 deaths). The Avandia diabetes drug (GlaxoSmithKline, restricted 2010, cardiovascular deaths). Numerous other examples. The pattern is constant: industry-funded research produces favourable trials; industry-paid revolving-door regulators approve the drug; industry-funded continuing medical education trains physicians to prescribe it; industry-paid academic spokespeople defend it; harms accumulate; the regulator eventually restricts; the company pays a fine that is small relative to the profits; no one is prosecuted; the same companies continue to make the same kinds of decisions about the next drug.

## V. The Gates Foundation

The asymmetric pattern — money flowing from concentrated wealth into the institutions that produce the public's information about health and science — operates on the global scale through the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation. The Foundation, founded in 2000, has an endowment of $80 billion. It is the largest private philanthropy in human history. Its disbursements are larger than the budgets of most national health agencies. It is the second-largest funder of the World Health Organization (after the United States government in most years; the largest funder in several years). It is the largest funder of GAVI, the global vaccine alliance. It funds a third of the world's vaccine development. Its decisions shape the global public health agenda.

The decisions are not voted on. They are made by Bill Gates personally, and since the 2021 divorce by Bill Gates and a small executive team, with input from a board on which Gates retains ultimate control. The Foundation's choices about which diseases to prioritise, which interventions to fund, which research to support, and which institutions to legitimise have, across twenty-five years, redirected an enormous fraction of global health activity according to one man's preferences. The preferences are not malicious. The arrangement is, structurally, unaccountable. No democratic process produced it. No democratic process can revise it.

Two cases illustrate the mechanism.

First, the **Imperial College COVID model**. In March 2020, as the early case data from Wuhan and Lombardy began to suggest the new coronavirus would spread globally, the Imperial College London team led by **Neil Ferguson** ran an epidemiological simulation. The model projected, on the assumption that no mitigation was undertaken, 510,000 deaths in the United Kingdom and 2.2 million in the United States. The findings were published on 16 March 2020 as Imperial College Report 9. They became the immediate basis for the United Kingdom's national lockdown of 23 March 2020, for similar measures in the United States, and for analogous decisions across the developed world. They constituted the largest single policy intervention based on a single piece of academic modelling in human history.

The Imperial College modelling team has received, between 2015 and 2024, **more than $185 million in Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation grants** across various programmes including the Vaccine Impact Modelling Consortium and the Abdul Latif Jameel Institute for Disease and Emergency Analytics. The grants do not establish that the model was wrong. They do establish that the modelling team was funded by the actor that subsequently was the largest beneficiary of the global vaccine response the modelling produced. The model's source code, when partially released on GitHub in May 2020, was criticised by senior software engineers for non-deterministic behaviour, lack of unit tests, and structural choices that made independent verification difficult. The parameters used (R₀, infection fatality rate, asymptomatic transmission rate) were not subjected to standard pre-publication peer review before being used as the basis for the policy that followed. The lockdown decision was made on a model that, by ordinary academic standards, was not yet ready to be relied upon.

Second, **AGRA**. The Alliance for a Green Revolution in Africa was launched in 2006 by the Gates and Rockefeller Foundations with the stated goal of doubling agricultural productivity for thirty million smallholder African farming households in thirteen target countries (Burkina Faso, Ethiopia, Ghana, Kenya, Malawi, Mali, Mozambique, Niger, Nigeria, Rwanda, Tanzania, Uganda, Zambia). The Gates Foundation has contributed $700 million through 2020. The strategy was to promote commercial seed and fertiliser inputs as the solution to African food insecurity — a strategy that replicated the 1960s Green Revolution model that had been controversial in India and Mexico.

In 2020, the Tufts University Global Development and Environment Institute, led by Timothy Wise, published an evaluation titled *False Promises*. The findings:

- Staple crop productivity gains: 18 percent over 12 years, well below AGRA's stated goal of doubling.
- Numbers of severely undernourished people in the AGRA target countries: **increased by 30 percent** across the programme's lifetime.
- Indicators of rural poverty in target countries: deteriorated relative to non-target African countries.

The AGRA model had not worked on its own metrics. The Gates Foundation continued to fund AGRA after the Tufts report was published. The Foundation has not, publicly, addressed the report's findings or revised the underlying strategy. The model continues. The metrics continue to fail. The funding continues. The accountability mechanism that would, in a public agency, force a reassessment, does not exist in private philanthropy.

The Foundation's influence on what the public learns about its own work is mediated through media funding. In August 2020, *Columbia Journalism Review* published Tim Schwab's investigation *Journalism's Gates Keepers*. Schwab documented that the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation had given more than **$250 million to media organisations** including NPR, the BBC, the Guardian, the New York Times, the Atlantic, ProPublica, El País, Univision, NBC, the Financial Times, and many smaller outlets. The grants funded specific reporting projects on global health, education, and development. Schwab found that the funded coverage often did not disclose the Gates funding when reporting on Gates Foundation activities, and that journalists at funded outlets faced career incentives to avoid critical coverage of Foundation programmes.

This is the closed loop. The Foundation funds the public health research that becomes the basis of public health policy. The Foundation funds the media organisations that report on the public health policy. The Foundation funds the academic departments that train the next generation of public health researchers. The Foundation funds the journalists who would otherwise be reporting on the Foundation's funding decisions. The system is structurally incapable of producing independent assessment of its own performance. It is funded, throughout the layers, by the same private actor. The arrangement is not corruption in the conventional sense — no laws are being broken. The arrangement is, more the privatisation of the public's mechanism for knowing what is happening in its own health system.

## VI. The COVID lab-leak suppression

The most documented case of scientific suppression in recent history is the two-and-a-half-year suppression of the COVID-19 lab-leak hypothesis from late January 2020 until mid-2023. The case is now resolved in the public record. The mechanism is documented in House Select Subcommittee disclosures from 2023 and 2024. The pattern is the standard pattern, executed at the highest level of American science.

The basic timeline:

- December 2019: SARS-CoV-2 outbreak detected in Wuhan, China.
- 9 January 2020: Chinese authorities confirm a novel coronavirus.
- 30 January 2020: WHO declares Public Health Emergency of International Concern.
- 31 January 2020: **Anthony Fauci**, Director of the National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases, receives an email from **Kristian Andersen** of Scripps Research, raising the possibility that the virus's spike protein "potentially looked engineered."
- 1 February 2020: Fauci convenes a teleconference with senior virologists including Andersen, Edward Holmes, Robert Garry, Andrew Rambaut, and Ian Lipkin. The substance of the meeting was, per Slack and email messages later released, intense private discussion of laboratory-origin scenarios.
- The Slack messages from those days (released in 2023 by the House Select Subcommittee on the Coronavirus Pandemic) document the private views of the participants. Kristian Andersen, 2 February: "The unusual features of the virus make up a really small part of the genome (<0.1%) so one has to look really closely at all the sequences to see that some of the features (potentially) look engineered." Robert Garry: "I really can't think of a plausible natural scenario." Andrew Rambaut: "from a (natural) evolution and ecology perspective… given the scenarios proposed I cannot favour one outcome over the other."
- 17 March 2020: Andersen, Rambaut, Lipkin, Holmes, and Garry publish in *Nature Medicine* the paper *The Proximal Origin of SARS-CoV-2*. The paper concludes, in its abstract, that the virus is "not a laboratory construct or a purposefully manipulated virus." The paper makes no mention of the doubts the authors had privately expressed six weeks earlier.

The *Proximal Origin* paper became the dominant scientific authority cited globally throughout 2020, 2021, and most of 2022 to dismiss the lab-leak hypothesis. The paper was cited in Twitter and Facebook content moderation decisions that resulted in posts discussing lab-leak being removed or labelled as misinformation. The paper was cited by journalists explaining why lab-leak was a "conspiracy theory." The paper was cited by WHO officials and by Anthony Fauci personally in Congressional testimony.

Throughout this period, **EcoHealth Alliance**, an NIAID grantee run by Peter Daszak, had been receiving NIH funding to conduct bat coronavirus research, **part of which was subcontracted to the Wuhan Institute of Virology**. The amount was modest — $600,000 of a $3.7 million NIH grant between 2014 and 2019. The subcontracted research included some that, under the 2014 NIH gain-of-function moratorium, would have required specific exemption review. In October 2021, **Lawrence Tabak**, Principal Deputy Director of the NIH, sent a letter to Congress acknowledging that one of the EcoHealth-funded research projects at the Wuhan Institute of Virology had produced a chimeric coronavirus that, in mouse experiments, showed enhanced replication in humanised mice — that is, a gain-of-function research outcome.

The federal agency that funded the Wuhan Institute had funded research that, on at least one occasion, produced an enhanced coronavirus. The principal scientific authority cited to dismiss the lab-leak hypothesis was a paper whose authors had privately doubted natural origin at the time they wrote it. The convener of the meeting that produced the paper was the director of the agency that had funded the Wuhan research. These three facts, in conjunction, constitute the basis of the conflict of interest that motivated the suppression.

The suppression was effective for two and a half years. During those years:

- Public discussion of lab-leak was suppressed on the major social media platforms.
- Academic discussion was professionally costly; researchers who raised the possibility were targeted for professional sanction.
- Mainstream journalism, with limited exceptions (Nicholson Baker in *New York Magazine* January 2021; Donald McNeil's later reporting; the eventual coverage in the *New York Times*, the *Washington Post*, and the *Wall Street Journal* from 2021 onward), did not pursue the story.
- The two-and-a-half year delay in honest public discussion of the origins question prevented the development of policy responses that depend on knowing where pandemics come from — biosecurity standards for laboratories, accountability mechanisms for governments, public-health surveillance prioritisation.

The official assessments have, since 2023, progressively confirmed what the suppressed discussion would have produced:

- **February 2023**: the US Department of Energy concluded with "low confidence" that the pandemic most likely originated from a laboratory leak (*Wall Street Journal* 26 February 2023).
- **March 2023**: the FBI confirmed publicly that it had assessed the lab-leak hypothesis as the most likely origin with "moderate confidence." (The FBI assessment had been classified since 2021.)
- **January 2025**: the CIA, under newly appointed Director **John Ratcliffe**, updated its assessment to "low confidence" lab-leak as the most likely origin, reversing the agency's previous "no determination" position.

This is the public record. The principal authorities of the US government now assess that the pandemic most probably originated in a laboratory. The principal authority cited for the previous two and a half years' suppression of that hypothesis was a paper whose authors had privately doubted their own published conclusion. The suppression operated through every layer of the knowledge system: peer-reviewed publication, the social media platforms (themselves owned by the same circle as Chapter 8 documented), the major news organisations, and the academic institutions whose researchers depend on NIH funding for their careers. The case is, by the standards of the previous chapters, an unusually clean experimental demonstration of the capture of knowing in operation.

## VII. Media consolidation

The final layer is the news media — the institution that the public, in a functioning democracy, would rely upon to report independently on the captures described in the previous parts of this chapter.

In 1983 the journalist **Ben Bagdikian** published *The Media Monopoly*. The book documented that **fifty corporations** controlled 90 percent of American mass media — newspapers, magazines, broadcast television, radio, books, and films. The number was widely regarded, in 1983, as dangerously low. By the time Bagdikian published the seventh and final edition of the book in 2004 (*The New Media Monopoly*), the number was **five**: Time Warner, Disney, Murdoch's News Corporation, Viacom, and Bertelsmann.

The further consolidation since 2004 has reduced the number further. The AT&T-Time Warner merger (2018-2022, subsequently spun out as Warner Bros. Discovery). The Disney-Fox transaction (2019). The Skydance-Paramount merger (2024, putting Larry Ellison's son David Ellison in control of CBS News and Paramount Pictures). The Murdoch family realignment (2023, consolidating control of Fox News and the *Wall Street Journal* with Lachlan Murdoch). By 2026, five companies control 90 percent of American media: **Disney**, **Comcast (NBCUniversal)**, **Warner Bros. Discovery**, **Paramount/Skydance**, and **News Corp/Fox**. Each of these five is majority-owned, as Chapter 8 documented, by the Big Three asset managers — BlackRock, Vanguard, and State Street.

The simultaneous **destruction of local journalism** has, since 2005, eliminated 2,200 American local newspapers — about one-third of the titles operating in 2005. The replacement model — Alden Global Capital's strip-mining of regional newspaper chains, McClatchy's bankruptcy, Gannett's contraction — has, in many cases, retained the local newspaper's nameplate while eliminating its local reporting capacity. Roughly 70 million Americans now live in a "news desert" or near-desert, defined by Northwestern's Local News Initiative as a county with no daily local newspaper or with only a single newspaper providing minimal coverage. The political consequence is that the local journalism that has historically held local power to account — the investigative reporting on county commissioners, school boards, regional hospitals, local businesses — has largely ceased to exist across most of the country. The political institutions of local democracy continue to operate. The check on those institutions does not.

This is the complete picture. At the national level, five corporate media owners covering politics, finance, science, and culture, all of them owned by the same asset management firms that own the rest of the economy. At the local level, news deserts. The captured national press does not report critically on the system because the system owns the national press. The captured national press does not report on the destruction of local journalism, because reporting on it would highlight what the national press is also doing. The local press that would have noticed the captures and the destruction has been eliminated. The information environment that an honest political process would require has been progressively dismantled across the past forty years through the same mechanism that has dismantled the rest of the public institutions of the post-war settlement: private accumulation of ownership, structural alignment of the resulting media properties with the interests of their owners, and the systematic elimination of the smaller, independent alternatives.

## VIII. The verdict

The capture of knowing is the precondition for the capture of everything else. If the population could reliably learn what was happening to it, the political coalition that would form to stop the capture would form. The population cannot reliably learn what is happening to it because the institutions that produce knowledge for the population — universities, regulators, peer-reviewed journals, public-health bodies, news organisations — have been captured by the same actors that the knowledge would implicate. The capture is structural. The mechanism is funding. The result is an information environment in which the previous eight chapters of this book do not exist.

The previous eight chapters do exist. The sources are public. The arithmetic is elementary. The history is documented. What does not exist is the connection. The captured institutions report each fact in isolation, embedded in a framing that prevents it from connecting to the next fact, and surround the whole with a discourse that makes any attempt to draw the connections look like the work of cranks, paranoids, or conspiracy theorists. The category of "conspiracy theory" has been weaponised to prevent the evidence from forming, in any reader's mind, the pattern that documented forms. The pattern is real. The arithmetic is real. The naming has been made socially impossible.

This book is what doing the naming looks like. The naming, done carefully, with sources, is what the institutions are designed to prevent. The captured institutions will not, after this book is published, refute its arguments. They will, more likely, ignore it; if they do not ignore it, they will dismiss it; if they cannot dismiss it, they will personalise it; if they cannot personalise it, they will discredit its author. These are the techniques of an ideology defending itself, and they do not require central coordination. They are the standard responses produced by the systems Chapter 6 described. The responses are predictable. They do not change the arithmetic.

The remaining chapter is about what comes next. About the alternatives that have been working, on small scales, in suppressed and marginalised places, for decades. About the resolutions that the system's collapse will require. About what a serious post-capture order would look like, on the principles the historical record actually supports, on the arithmetic the previous chapters established, and on the moral premises that every honest tradition has agreed upon. The book ends in the place the institutions tell us does not exist: the place from which the trap is escapable.


---

# Chapter 10: The Consensus Future

The first nine chapters of this book have documented a system. The system is a financial-political-corporate-knowledge oligarchy that has been built over four centuries by named men in named rooms, that operates today through fifteen hundred individuals at its senior tier, that has captured the institutions of democratic accountability, that has eliminated the alternatives, and that is now consuming its own arithmetic. The next ten chapters of this book are about what the same circle is now building in the place of the system it has consumed.

The thing being built is the artificial intelligence economy. The phrase is a polite one. A more accurate phrase is: the consolidated, privately owned cognitive-labour substitute for the human population. The first phrase is the one used in mainstream press releases. The second phrase is the one that follows from reading the press releases of the same actors carefully. The actors are not hiding what they are building. They have explained it, on the record, in official documents from their own companies and in public statements from their CEOs, over the course of the past three years. The first task of this chapter is to demonstrate that this is true. The second task is to establish what the public predictions are, in the documents the system has itself produced, so that the chapters that follow have a defined object to investigate. The third task is to establish that, in the precise structural sense the previous nine chapters have used the term, **the people who own the AI infrastructure are the new world order**. They are not its critics, its commentators, its analysts, or its philosophers. They are its owners. The chapters that follow this one are dossiers, one chapter per person, in the same investigative-journalism mode the previous chapters used on Mayer Amschel Rothschild, Paul Warburg, Lewis Powell, and Henry Paulson. The same depth. The same naming. The same refusal to hedge.

## I. The capital commitments are real

The first piece of evidence that the system has chosen artificial intelligence as the project of the coming generation is the money. The capital commitments are large enough that they cannot be explained by anything other than a coordinated decision, across the major American technology and financial firms, that the substitution of human cognitive labour by machine cognitive labour is the principal economic and political project of the next decade.

The numbers are public. The four largest American technology firms — Microsoft, Google's parent Alphabet, Meta, and Amazon — together committed, in their quarterly earnings calls between October 2024 and February 2025, to **$320 billion in artificial intelligence capital expenditure for a single calendar year**, 2025. Microsoft announced $80 billion, Google $75 billion, Amazon "$100 billion," Meta $60 to $65 billion. This is a single year, not a multi-year programme. The figures are corporate capital expenditure, not market valuation or notional ambitions: they are accounting commitments that have been disclosed to the SEC under reporting standards that produce criminal liability for materially false statements. The companies are spending the money.

The $320 billion is more than the entire annual GDP of South Africa. It is more than the annual revenue of the global semiconductor industry. It is three times the entire annual capital investment of the US federal government in non-defence research and development. It is being spent by four companies in twelve months, on a single category of technology. There has been no comparable concentration of capital expenditure on a single technology category in the history of capitalism. The post-war defence build-up did not approach it in real terms. The dot-com infrastructure build of 1996-2000 was, at its peak, 40 percent of this annual figure. The post-2008 quantitative-easing programmes were larger in headline volume but consisted of central-bank asset purchases, not productive capital deployment. The closest historical analogue, by scale and concentration, is the American mobilisation for the Second World War.

On top of the corporate capex, the principal public-private programme is the **Stargate Project**. It was announced at the White House on 21 January 2025 — the second day of the second Trump administration. The announcement was made personally by President Donald Trump, with **Sam Altman** of OpenAI, **Masayoshi Son** of SoftBank, and **Larry Ellison** of Oracle standing behind him in the Roosevelt Room. The stated commitment is $500 billion over four years, with $100 billion deployed immediately. The principals are OpenAI (40%), SoftBank Group (40%), Oracle, and **MGX** — an Abu Dhabi sovereign investment vehicle chaired by Sheikh Tahnoun bin Zayed Al Nahyan, the United Arab Emirates' National Security Adviser and the brother of the country's President. The chairman of Stargate is Masayoshi Son. The first construction site — a one-million-square-foot data centre in Abilene, Texas — was already in development when the announcement was made. The site is built on the assumption that the buildout will continue at the announced scale; if it does not, it will be the largest stranded asset in the history of American technology infrastructure. The actors are confident that it will continue.

Trump described Stargate at the announcement as "the largest AI infrastructure project in history." Altman called it "the most important project of this era." Ellison called it "the beginning of a golden age." Son's statement at the announcement was the most operationally significant: addressing Trump directly, on camera, he said, "**We wouldn't have decided to do this unless you won.**" The chairman of the largest single private AI infrastructure investment in human history publicly stated that the investment was conditional on the political outcome of the 2024 American election. This is the visible coordination between the AI consolidation and the political consolidation that the previous chapter described. It is not theoretical. It is on record of a White House announcement.

Beyond the American hyperscalers and Stargate, the further commitments include:

- **xAI** (Elon Musk's personal AI company): three funding rounds in 2024 totalling $17 billion. The company is building "Colossus," a data centre in Memphis, Tennessee, that as of December 2024 operated 100,000 Nvidia H100 GPUs — already, on its own, the largest single concentration of AI compute on earth — with announced plans to expand to one million GPUs.
- **G42** (United Arab Emirates): Microsoft invested $1.5 billion in April 2024, conditioned by the US Commerce Department on G42's removal of Chinese hardware. G42's chairman is Sheikh Tahnoun, the same UAE official whose investment vehicle is a principal of Stargate. The UAE has, in 2024-25, become the single largest non-American sovereign-wealth investor in American AI infrastructure.
- **Saudi Arabian Public Investment Fund**: an $40 billion AI investment vehicle announced in March 2024, with **Andreessen Horowitz** as a partner — that is, with the venture capital firm whose principal Marc Andreessen has emerged as the principal political-ideological organiser of the American techno-oligarchy.

The total announced AI infrastructure capital expenditure across the 2024-2029 horizon, by industry research compilations (Goldman Sachs, Bain & Company), is on the order of **$1.4 to $2 trillion**. This is the scale of the post-1945 Marshall Plan and post-war American reconstruction, condensed into a five-year capital programme, all of it directed at a single technology category, all of it owned by twelve individuals and the corporate vehicles they control.

The actors have decided. The capital is committed. The buildout is in progress. The question of whether artificial intelligence is the future is, at the level of capital allocation, no longer an open question. The answer is yes. The remaining question is who will own the future being built, and what they will do with it.

## II. The labour displacement predictions are public

The second piece of evidence is the labour-displacement forecasts. These are not produced by the book's critics of the system; they are produced by the system's own central institutions. The most-cited recent forecasts:

**Goldman Sachs**, the investment bank, published in March 2023 a research report titled *The Potentially Large Effects of Artificial Intelligence on Economic Growth*, by Joseph Briggs and Devesh Kodnani. The report's headline finding was that **300 million full-time jobs globally** are exposed to automation by AI. Two-thirds of US occupations are exposed; in exposed occupations, between 25 and 50 percent of workload could be replaced. On an employment-weighted basis, 18 percent of work globally could be computerised. The exposure is concentrated in office and administrative support work (46 percent of tasks automatable), legal work (44 percent), architecture and engineering (37 percent), life and social sciences (36 percent), and business and financial operations (35 percent). The exposure is lowest in physical labour: construction (6 percent), maintenance (4 percent), cleaning (1 percent).

**The International Monetary Fund**, the principal global financial institution, published in January 2024 a Staff Discussion Note titled *Gen-AI: Artificial Intelligence and the Future of Work*. The note projects that **40 percent of global jobs are exposed to AI**, rising to **60 percent in advanced economies** (UK 70 percent, US 60 percent), with 40 percent exposure in emerging markets and 26 percent in low-income countries. Of the exposed jobs in advanced economies, the report estimates that half face displacement and the other half augmentation. The implication is that **30 percent of all advanced-economy jobs face displacement risk** within the projected timeframe. The IMF's Managing Director, Kristalina Georgieva, summarised the assessment in her accompanying blog post: "In most scenarios, AI will likely worsen overall inequality, a troubling trend that policymakers must proactively address."

**The McKinsey Global Institute**, the research arm of the management consultancy that has been advising the system since the 1960s, has published three reports between June 2023 and November 2025. The June 2023 report, *The Economic Potential of Generative AI*, projected $2.6 to $4.4 trillion in annual economic value across 63 use cases, and estimated that 50 percent of today's work activities could be automated between 2030 and 2060, with a midpoint of 2045 — a decade earlier than McKinsey's previous (2017) estimate. The July 2023 follow-up, *Generative AI and the Future of Work in America*, narrowed the focus to the United States: **30 percent of US work hours could be automated by 2030**, up from a 21.5 percent pre-generative-AI baseline. Roughly 12 million American workers would need to undergo occupational transitions by 2030, with low-wage workers facing up to **fourteen times more transitions** than high-wage workers. The May 2024 European follow-up projected 27 percent of European work hours automatable by 2030. The November 2025 update, *Agents, Robots, and Us*, has raised the headline figure dramatically: McKinsey now assesses that **57 percent of work hours are already technically automatable with currently demonstrated technology**. The previous "by 2030" projection has, in McKinsey's own assessment, been achieved by November 2025.

The dissenting professional view, worth noting, is that of **Daron Acemoglu** of MIT, who won the 2024 Nobel Prize in Economics. Acemoglu published in May 2024 a working paper titled *The Simple Macroeconomics of AI* projecting a much smaller GDP impact — 0.06 percent per year, totalling 0.93 to 1.6 percent over a decade. Acemoglu's analysis contests the Goldman and McKinsey *productivity* projections. It does not contest the displacement projections; it accepts them. Acemoglu's argument, in effect, is that the system will produce the labour displacement without producing the compensating productivity gains the industry uses to justify it. This is a sharper criticism of the system than the institutional projections, not a softer one. It is the dissenting view that the GDP boost is largely fictitious; the job losses are real.

The forecasts have begun arriving as outcomes. American technology firms, in the eighteen months between mid-2023 and the end of 2024, announced **400,000 layoffs**. The companies typically described the layoffs in their public communications as efficiency measures or post-pandemic right-sizing; in their internal communications and in conversations with journalists on background, executives at Microsoft, Google, Meta, Amazon, and the major consulting firms have increasingly attributed the layoffs to AI-driven automation of internal functions. Duolingo, in January 2024, terminated 10 percent of its translation contractors and explicitly described the terminations as AI-driven. IBM, in May 2023, announced that 7,800 white-collar jobs would be paused for hiring and replaced with AI over five years. Klarna, in 2024, announced that its AI customer-service agent was performing the work of 700 full-time agents. The displacement is no longer a forecast. It is the present condition of the labour market in the cognitive-work sectors. The forecasts are about how much further it will go and how fast.

## III. The CEO statements are explicit

The third piece of evidence is the on-the-record statements of the AI lab CEOs themselves. These are not hostile critics speculating about the technology's effects; they are the principals of the companies, speaking on the record, in official documents and major-venue public appearances. Their predictions are not vague. They are specific.

**Sam Altman**, CEO of OpenAI, at the World Government Summit in Dubai on 12 February 2024: "If the world economy grows at 3% a year, that's $3 trillion of new economic activity created every year. If we have AGI, that goes way up." The phrasing is careful. The substance is that artificial general intelligence will multiply the rate of economic growth by some unspecified large factor. Altman has repeated this formulation across multiple venues during 2024 and 2025.

**Sam Altman**, blog post titled *The Intelligence Age*, 23 September 2024: "It is possible that we will have superintelligence in a few thousand days; it may take longer, but I'm confident we'll get there." A few thousand days is five to ten years. *Superintelligence*, in the technical literature of the AI field, means an artificial system that exceeds human cognitive performance across all domains. The CEO of OpenAI is saying that he expects such a system within the next decade.

**Dario Amodei**, CEO of Anthropic — the company whose AI assistant has been used to draft this book — published in October 2024 an essay titled *Machines of Loving Grace: How AI Could Transform the World for the Better*. The essay is the most aggressive public timeline statement from any major AI lab CEO. Amodei projects:

- "Powerful AI" — defined as systems "smarter than a Nobel Prize winner across most relevant fields" — by **2026 to 2027**.
- A "compressed 21st century" following: 50 to 100 years of scientific progress in 5 to 10 years.
- Specific projected outcomes within ten years of powerful AI: cures for most cancers and infectious diseases; effective prevention and treatment for most mental illness; biological elimination of the rich/poor health gap; doubling of healthy human lifespan; the ending of poverty in low-income countries.

Amodei's essay acknowledges the displacement of labour as "a serious problem" but treats it as a transition cost rather than a structural feature of the project. The essay's underlying assumption is that the benefits of the compressed progress will be broadly distributed. The Anthropic CEO does not specify the political mechanism by which broad distribution would occur, given the ownership structure described in Chapter 8.

**Elon Musk**, at the VivaTech conference in Paris on 23 May 2024: "Probably none of us will have a job. In a benign scenario, probably none of us will have a job." Musk is the owner of xAI, of Tesla (which is increasingly an AI/robotics company), of Neuralink, and of X. He is, after his 2025 separation from the Trump administration, the wealthiest individual in human history. His prediction about the labour market is that it will cease to exist as a category of human activity. He has repeated this formulation in multiple subsequent venues. He has not retracted it.

**Demis Hassabis**, CEO of Google DeepMind, in a May 2024 *Wired* interview: AGI "in the next 5 to 10 years." Hassabis is, by training, a cognitive neuroscientist, and is generally regarded within the AI research community as the most technically competent of the major lab CEOs. His timeline is more conservative than Amodei's but aligned with Altman's.

**Geoffrey Hinton**, the 2024 Nobel Prize laureate in Physics for his foundational contributions to the neural-network architecture underlying current AI — the "Godfather of AI" — has assessed in CBS *60 Minutes* (October 2023) and in subsequent BBC interviews: a 50 percent probability that AI exceeds human intelligence within five to twenty years, and a 10 to 20 percent probability of existential risk to humanity. Hinton resigned from Google in 2023 specifically in order to be able to speak publicly about these assessments without conflict of interest. He has spent the time since publicly warning that the technology he co-developed is on a trajectory he no longer considers safe. The captured-knowledge media have, predictably, mostly ignored him.

The pattern is the pattern. The principal owners and developers of the technology are stating, on the record, in publicly available documents, that they expect the technology to produce within five to ten years a transformation of economic life on a scale unparalleled in human history, that they expect the labour market to be eliminated as a category, that the value created will be of the order of tens of trillions of dollars annually, and that the technical capabilities will include systems superior to human cognition across nearly all domains. They are then asking the public to consent to this transformation through a political process they have, by the documentation of Chapter 8, captured. The consent is not being asked for. The transformation is being executed. The public is being informed by press release.

## IV. The new world order, defined precisely

The thesis of this chapter, on which the dossier chapters that follow depend, is that the owners of the AI infrastructure are the **new world order** in a precise structural sense. The phrase has been used loosely in conspiracy literature for decades. It is used here with a specific definition. A *world order*, in the political-science sense, is the set of institutions and actors that determine the principal political-economic outcomes for the world's population. The post-1945 world order — the Bretton Woods system, the United Nations, NATO, the EU, the WTO, the multilateral financial institutions — has been described, accurately, as the American-led liberal international order. It is now, as the previous chapters have documented, a captured order, structured to extract value from the world's population on behalf of a small private circle. It is still, however, *the* order. Its institutions still operate. Its decisions are still ratified through nominally democratic processes.

What is emerging from the AI consolidation is something different. It is an order in which the principal economic actors — the firms that own the technology that substitutes for cognitive labour — are not nationally accountable, not subject to democratic ratification of their major decisions, not regulated by any institution they cannot capture, and not constrained by any traditional check that the post-1945 institutions provided. The owners of the AI infrastructure can, on the documented record:

- **Allocate capital at scales larger than the budgets of major sovereign states**. Microsoft's 2025 AI capex of $80 billion is larger than the entire annual budget of New Zealand. Meta's $60-65 billion exceeds the annual military budget of France.
- **Determine the technology stack on which the cognitive work of every developed-country white-collar industry now operates**. The decisions about model architecture, training data, deployment policies, content moderation, and capability restrictions are made by fifteen people across five companies. These decisions are not voted on. They cannot be appealed. They are corporate-internal decisions whose consequences are externalised to the rest of the world.
- **Set the terms on which other technologies and other businesses access AI capability**. The pricing of API access, the choice of which capabilities to make available to whom, the restrictions on jurisdictional access (China is excluded from frontier models, Russia is excluded, certain other jurisdictions are excluded), the licensing terms — these are determined by the AI lab CEOs in negotiation with their largest customers.
- **Define the political-ideological framing of the technology**. Andreessen's *Techno-Optimist Manifesto*, Amodei's *Machines of Loving Grace*, Altman's *Intelligence Age*, Hassabis's various publications: these are the documents that frame how the technology is publicly understood. The frames are produced by the producers.
- **Capture the relevant regulatory institutions**. The second Trump administration's AI policy is being run by David Sacks, a PayPal Mafia founder. The administration's Vice President is JD Vance, Peter Thiel's protégé. The principal infrastructure programme, Stargate, is owned by the same companies it is supposed to be governing. The European Union's AI Act, which on paper is the most demanding AI regulation in the world, has produced, in its implementation through 2025, no enforcement actions of consequence against any of the major American AI labs. The capture is operational.
- **Coordinate with sovereign-wealth capital from the petro-monarchies** (Stargate's MGX partner, the PIF-Andreessen deal, the G42-Microsoft partnership), with private equity (the same Big Three asset managers from Chapter 8 own the AI companies), with the defence-industrial layer (Palantir, Anduril), and with the surveillance state (the AWS-CIA contract, the Palantir-ICE deployment, the NSA's deep integration with the major cloud providers).

This is the definition of a world order. It has assets larger than most states. It has effective control over a technology category that is, on its own producers' projections, going to redefine the economic activity of most of the world's population. It has captured the principal regulatory institutions of the world's most powerful state. It coordinates internationally with the principal accumulations of sovereign capital. It is not accountable to any electorate. It does not consult any public before making decisions whose consequences are global.

The previous nine chapters of this book have used the phrase *con-spirare* — to breathe together — as the etymological root of the word "conspiracy." A conspiracy, in this strict sense, is documented coordination between named actors toward a shared end, where the coordination is not subject to public ratification. By this standard, the AI consolidation is the largest documented conspiracy in human history. It does not require secrecy. The participants are happy to be photographed together at the White House. The coordination is the visible coordination of capital, personnel, and policy across companies and states. The conspiracy is the structure. It is not denied. The participants describe what they are doing accurately in their own press releases. The public is not being deceived about what is being built. The public is being prevented from being a party to the decision.

## V. The case for individual dossiers

The remaining task of this chapter is to explain why what follows is a series of individual dossiers, with the depth this book has applied to historical figures across the first nine chapters, rather than a single chapter listing the senior figures of the new order.
