The argument is straightforward. The first nine chapters demonstrated, with biographical specificity, that the post-1600 financial system was built by named men. Mayer Amschel Rothschild had a biography that, when fully understood, illuminates what the Rothschild banking firm became. Paul Warburg had a biography — including the specific German banking apprenticeship at M.M. Warburg in Hamburg, his arrival in New York in 1902, his marriage into the Loeb-Kuhn family, his participation in the Jekyll Island meeting of November 1910 — that illuminates what the Federal Reserve Act of 1913 became. Henry Paulson had a biography — Goldman CEO 1999-2006, Treasury Secretary 2006-2009, decision-maker on the AIG bailout that funnelled $12.9 billion to Goldman through credit default swap counterparty payments — that illuminates what the 2008 bailouts became. The biographical specificity is not biographical drama for its own sake. It is the mechanism by which the abstract claim "the system was built by named men with specific interests" becomes the documented claim "these specific men, with these specific interests, made these specific decisions in these specific rooms."

The same standard, applied to the AI consolidation, requires the same depth on each principal. The principals are not interchangeable. **Peter Thiel** is not a generic "Silicon Valley billionaire." He has a specific biography — born 1967 in Frankfurt, moved to South Africa during the apartheid period as a child, schooled in Swakopmund (then in South African-administered South West Africa, now Namibia), undergraduate at Stanford (where he co-founded the *Stanford Review* and co-authored *The Diversity Myth*), Stanford Law, a brief Manhattan derivatives-trading career, the founding of Confinity in 1998 that became PayPal, the founding of Palantir in 2003 with CIA In-Q-Tel seed money, the early investment in Facebook in 2004, the founding of Founders Fund in 2005, the long-term funding of Curtis Yarvin and the neoreactionary movement from 2010, the 2016 Trump endorsement at the Republican National Convention, the $15 million in PAC funding for JD Vance's 2022 Ohio Senate race, the multiple published essays and interviews in which he has explicitly stated his belief that freedom and democracy are incompatible, the role in Donald Trump's vice-presidential selection process in 2024, and his current position as the most politically consequential of the techno-oligarchs. This biography is not redundant detail. It is the evidence that demonstrates the structural argument. The man who funded the political career of the sitting Vice President of the United States, who funded the philosophical movement that has supplied the ideological framework of the second Trump administration, and who co-founded the surveillance company that operates the American immigration enforcement system and the Israeli targeting system used in Gaza, is not a generic figure. He is a specific historical actor with a specific trajectory. The trajectory is now, in 2026, beginning to determine the shape of the political institutions the rest of us live inside.

**Elon Musk** is similarly not generic. He has a specific biography — born 1971 in Pretoria, son of **Errol Musk**, a South African engineer who has publicly stated that he owned, during the 1980s, a half-share in a Zambian emerald mine (the family wealth of the man who is now the richest individual in human history therefore traces, in part, to apartheid-era southern African mineral extraction at a time when most of the world had imposed sanctions on the region) — and a specific subsequent trajectory through Zip2, X.com (renamed PayPal after the Confinity merger), Tesla, SpaceX, Neuralink, the Boring Company, the Twitter purchase, xAI, the falling-out and rapprochement with Trump in 2024, the DOGE role January-May 2025, and the post-DOGE reorientation toward direct political organisation through the America Party. The biographical details matter because they ground the structural claim. The man with $400 billion in net worth, who personally operates the largest satellite constellation in human history (Starlink, ~7,000 satellites in low Earth orbit), the largest social media platform aligned with right-wing political messaging (X), and the leading private space-launch capability (SpaceX), is not a generic billionaire. He is a specific person with a specific family history with specific political effects.

The same logic applies to **Sam Altman** (born 1985, Stanford dropout, Loopt founder, Y Combinator president, OpenAI co-founder with Musk and Hoffman in 2015, the November 2023 board removal and five-day reinstatement, the Worldcoin iris-scanning project, the Stargate role, the documented family allegations from his sister Annie Altman that the book cannot honestly avoid given its commitment to the same standard of investigation it has applied to the Sackler family); to **Marc Andreessen** (Netscape co-founder, Mosaic before that, the Andreessen Horowitz fund founding 2009, the political conversion from Obama-Democrat to MAGA-aligned during 2023-24, the *Techno-Optimist Manifesto*, the explicit listing of "trust and safety" and "tech ethics" as enemies); to **Reid Hoffman**, **David Sacks**, **Max Levchin**, and the rest of the PayPal Mafia second tier; to **Jeff Bezos** (D.E. Shaw, Amazon, AWS-CIA, the *Washington Post*, the recent political reorientation); to **Mark Zuckerberg** (Harvard, Facebook 2004, the 56% voting share, the 2024 content-moderation rollback, his sister-in-law's role in his pro-Trump pivot); to **Eric Schmidt** (Google, the Defense Innovation Board, the NSCAI chair, the role bridging Silicon Valley to the Pentagon); to **Larry Ellison** (Oracle, the Lanai purchase, the Israel-defence connections, the son David Ellison's Skydance-Paramount acquisition); to **Bill Gates** (Microsoft origins, the antitrust case 1998-2001, the Foundation 2000, the WHO funding, the Epstein relationship 2011-2019, the divorce 2021, the current AI investments); to **Alex Karp** (Palantir, the In-Q-Tel funding, the ICE/NHS/IDF contracts, *The Technological Republic* published 2025); to **Dario and Daniela Amodei** (OpenAI alumni, Anthropic founding 2021, the Google-Amazon investment structure, the safety-focused branding question that the book cannot honestly avoid given that Anthropic's tool is drafting this book); to **Masayoshi Son** (SoftBank, the Vision Fund, the WeWork disaster, the Saudi PIF relationship, the Stargate chairmanship); and to the **Gulf sovereign-wealth layer** — Sheikh Tahnoun bin Zayed (UAE), Mohammed bin Salman (Saudi Arabia), and the post-petrodollar accumulation that is now flowing into AI infrastructure.

Each of these requires a dossier. Each dossier requires the same standards the book has applied to its earlier subjects. The standard is: the record. The family background where documented. The financial history where documented. The political activity where documented. The ideological statements in the subject's own words, with citations. The structural role in the system where documented. No hedging. No softening. No false balance. No parroting of the media accounts. The book has spent nine chapters establishing that the previous standard of investigative journalism — the one that prevails in the institutions — produces accounts of these figures that systematically minimise their political role, treat their statements about democracy and labour displacement as eccentric private opinions rather than corporate-strategic documents, and refuse to draw the connections between the financial-political layer of Chapter 8 and the technological layer of the chapters that follow this one. The book has nine chapters of evidence that this standard of journalism is captured. The remaining chapters apply the alternative standard.

There is one further note before the dossiers begin. Several of the figures profiled in the chapters that follow have, in their public communications, attempted to distinguish themselves from the rest. Andreessen has presented himself as the free-speech accelerationist against the corporate-authoritarian wing. Amodei has presented Anthropic as the safety-focused responsible actor against the unregulated competition. Hoffman has presented himself as the Democratic-aligned counterweight to the Trump-aligned right wing of the network. These self-presentations will be examined in their respective chapters and assessed against documented sources. The book's preliminary position, to be tested chapter by chapter, is that these are differentiations of style rather than of substance. The figures are all members of the same fifteen-person circle. They all own equity in the same set of companies. They all attend the same conferences. They all fund the same political candidates, often the same candidates by different routes. They all benefit from the same outcomes. The differentiations of branding mask a near-total alignment of interest.

The section that follows this one is the first of the dossiers. It is about Peter Thiel. He is first because, in the assessment of the author of this book — based on the record of who funded what, who placed whom in which political position, and whose ideological framework supplies the current programme of the second Trump administration — he is the most politically consequential of the techno-oligarchs as of 2026. The dossier covers his family origins in Frankfurt and South Africa, his education and ideological formation, his founding of Palantir and its current operational role, his relationship with Curtis Yarvin and the neoreactionary movement, his sponsorship of JD Vance, his hostility to democracy in his own words, and his position in the architecture of the new order.

The man who said, in his 2009 Cato Institute essay, "I no longer believe that freedom and democracy are compatible," is now, by the operation of the political mechanisms he has spent fifteen years funding, in the position of effective regent over the second Trump administration's policy agenda.


---

# Chapter 11: Peter Thiel

There is in 2026 no single private citizen of the United States whose personal biography has produced more political consequence than that of Peter Andreas Thiel. The sitting Vice President of the United States, James David Vance, is a Peter Thiel project from 2011 onward — Thiel introduced Vance to venture capital, Thiel introduced Vance to Trump, Thiel funded Vance's Senate campaign at the largest scale in American history, Thiel lobbied Trump for Vance's vice-presidential nomination. The principal data-surveillance company of the American federal government, Palantir Technologies, is a Peter Thiel company — Thiel co-founded it in 2003, Thiel's $30 million of personal capital bankrolled the first years, the CIA's venture-capital arm In-Q-Tel provided the second tranche of funding, Thiel remains the company's chairman and largest shareholder. The principal philosophical movement of the American post-democratic right — the so-called Dark Enlightenment or neoreactionary tendency around Curtis Yarvin — has been, since at least 2013, funded and intellectually sponsored by Peter Thiel. The principal recent demonstration of what private wealth can do to American journalism when it disapproves of a media outlet — the secret funding of *Bollea v. Gawker* — was executed by Peter Thiel. The 2016 endorsement of Donald Trump at the Republican National Convention by a major Silicon Valley figure, opening the long process by which the technology industry was reorganised around the Trump-MAGA project, was the endorsement of Peter Thiel.

What follows is a dossier on the man. The standard is the standard the book has applied throughout — full investigative depth, family and ancestral context where documented, financial history, ideological statements in the subject's own words, structural role in the system. The book takes the position, on the basis of the public record assembled here, that Peter Thiel is the principal architect of the American political transition described in the previous chapter — that the new world order, in its specifically American operational form, runs through this one man's biography in a way that no other living figure's biography matches. He is not the wealthiest. He is not the most famous. He is the most consequential.

## I. Origins: Frankfurt, Cleveland, Swakopmund

Peter Andreas Thiel was born on 11 October 1967 in Frankfurt am Main, West Germany. His father was Klaus Friedrich Thiel (1938-2022), a chemical engineer; his mother was Susanne Thiel; he had a brother, Patrick. The family moved to Cleveland, Ohio, in 1968, when Peter was one year old, after Klaus applied for American citizenship.

In the early 1970s — the precise date is not in the public record but the move appears to have occurred around 1971-1972 — Klaus Thiel left the United States and moved his family to southern Africa. He had taken employment with a chemical-engineering contractor working on the development of a uranium-mining operation in what was then South African-administered South West Africa, in the territory that would become independent Namibia after the 1990 transition. The mine was **Rössing**, near the German colonial coastal town of Swakopmund, in the Erongo region. It was, by the mid-1970s, the largest open-pit uranium mine in the world.

The Rössing mine matters for the purpose of understanding Peter Thiel because of three documented facts about it.

The first fact is that the mine operated in **direct contravention of United Nations resolutions**. The United Nations General Assembly had revoked South Africa's mandate over South West Africa in 1966 (Resolution 2145), and the United Nations Council for Namibia issued Decree No. 1 in 1974, which prohibited any extraction of Namibian natural resources by any party without the Council's permission. UN Security Council Resolution 366 of 1974 demanded South Africa's withdrawal from Namibia and the cessation of its illegal occupation. South Africa ignored all of these. The Rössing mine, operated by the British-Australian mining conglomerate Rio Tinto Zinc with minority participation from the pre-revolutionary Iranian government and offtake agreements with German, Japanese, and American utilities, continued throughout the 1970s and 1980s in defiance of the UN framework. It was, in international law, an illegal extraction operation on occupied territory. Klaus Thiel's employer was conducting the chemical-engineering work for that operation.

The second fact is that the mine **supplied uranium to apartheid South Africa's nuclear weapons programme**. The South African government, between 1971 and 1989, developed and built six nuclear devices, with a seventh under construction at the time of the programme's voluntary dismantlement in 1989-1990. The fissile material for those devices came principally from the uranium extracted at Rössing, processed at the South African Y-Plant at Valindaba. The complete history of the South African nuclear weapons programme was declassified after the transition to majority rule in 1993; the Rössing connection to the weapons programme is now historical fact. Klaus Thiel was not, as far as the public record establishes, a senior scientist on the weapons programme itself. He was a chemical engineer in the chain that supplied the input material. He was, in the language of international sanctions, a sanctions buster: an employee of a contractor working on extraction in violation of UN decrees, where the output supplied apartheid South Africa's nuclear weapons programme.

The third fact is that the **Swakopmund community in which Peter Thiel attended school during the mid-1970s was, in the contemporary press record, a continuing centre of Nazi sympathy**. The *New York Times* reported in 1976 that residents of Swakopmund commonly greeted one another with "Heil Hitler" and the Nazi salute. South African journalist Phil van Niekerk has documented (in interviews with the *Namibian Sun* in 2025) that, into the 1980s, souvenir shops in Swakopmund sold mugs and other merchandise bearing Nazi swastikas. The town's German community was, in the historical context, descended from the German South West Africa colonial settler population — the population whose forebears had carried out the **Herero and Namaqua genocide** of 1904-1908 under the German Imperial administration, an event now formally recognised by the United Nations as the first genocide of the twentieth century. The community's persistence of Nazi-era political iconography into Peter Thiel's childhood is a documented historical fact. Peter Thiel attended a German-language school in Swakopmund. His school records are not public; the broader pattern of his subsequent ideological development is.

The Thiel family returned to the United States in 1977, settling in Foster City, California. Peter Thiel was nine years old. He had, in the seven years between his second birthday and his ninth, attended at least seven different elementary schools, the last several of them in southern Africa under the apartheid administration. Klaus Thiel died in 2022. He gave no public interviews about his work at Rössing; nor has Peter Thiel ever publicly discussed his father's professional history in detail, beyond the general statement that Klaus was "a chemical engineer."

The standard framing in mainstream Thiel biography is that the South African period of his childhood was unimportant to his subsequent development — that Peter Thiel was a precocious chess-playing maths Olympiad American kid who happened to spend a few years abroad. The framing is not credible. The man who would, three decades after his return from Swakopmund, write that "freedom and democracy are no longer compatible" and that "the extension of the franchise to women" had been a disaster for liberty was formed in a household whose paterfamilias was working on a sanctions-busting uranium operation supplying an apartheid nuclear weapons programme, in a German colonial community that had, within living memory of his childhood, continued to greet itself with the Hitler salute. The biography is not coincidental. The ideological formation has continuity with the family circumstances.

## II. Stanford and *The Diversity Myth*

Peter Thiel entered Stanford University as an undergraduate in 1985, studying philosophy. He completed his B.A. in 1989 and his J.D. from Stanford Law School in 1992. The period was formative in two specific ways.

First, in 1987, Thiel founded **The Stanford Review** — the right-wing student newspaper that would become, over the subsequent thirty years, the principal recruiting ground for what would become the PayPal Mafia. The Review was capitalised in part by the **Olin Foundation** (chemical industry money, the same Olin Foundation that Chapter 6 documented as the foundational funder of the Mont Pèlerin-to-Federalist Society academic pipeline) through the Collegiate Network — a programme established in 1979 specifically to fund right-wing campus publications. The Stanford Review's editorial trajectory through the late 1980s and early 1990s defined the ideological character of the Thiel circle: opposition to multiculturalism and identity politics, opposition to feminism, opposition to gay rights and to the AIDS-era public-health responses, hostility to the university administration, and a self-presentation as the rebellious dissident voice against a putatively coercive campus left. Subsequent Review editors included **David O. Sacks** (Thiel's *Diversity Myth* co-author, later PayPal COO, now Trump's AI and Crypto Czar) and **Keith Rabois** (the same Rabois whose 1992 outburst the *Diversity Myth* book would defend). The institutional continuity from the 1987 Olin Foundation-funded undergraduate newspaper to the 2025 Trump administration AI policy team is direct and traceable through named individuals.

Second, Thiel attended the seminars of **René Girard**, the French Catholic literary theorist whose theory of "mimetic desire" — the proposition that humans copy each other's desires, that mass behaviour is therefore best understood as a contagion phenomenon, and that civilisation depends on the scapegoating mechanism to dissipate the violence that mimetic rivalry produces — has been Thiel's principal philosophical influence. Thiel has cited Girard in every long-form interview he has given since the late 2000s. The Girardian framework, in Thiel's hands, supports an oligarchic-elitist conclusion: that mass democracy, by amplifying mimetic contagion, produces destructive scapegoating dynamics, and that wise leadership requires the ability of a small elite to stand outside the contagion. The conclusion is not Girard's own — Girard was, in his own theological commitments, a Catholic who emphasised Christianity's revelatory role in *exposing* the scapegoating mechanism. Thiel's appropriation of Girard for an elitist political programme is a selective reading. It is, however, the reading that has shaped Thiel's politics.

Thiel was also, during his Stanford years, in proximity to **Opus Dei**, the Catholic conservative organisation that maintained a presence at Stanford through Arne Panula, the priest who directed the Opus Dei University House at Stanford in the late 1980s. Thiel attended Opus Dei meetings regularly. The connection is significant because Opus Dei's influence — its preference for hierarchical institutions, its hostility to liberal Catholicism, its dense network of lay associates in conservative legal and political institutions — has supplied a large fraction of the personnel of the current American right, including (post-2010) Trump-administration figures such as Bill Barr, Leonard Leo, and the Federalist Society pipeline more broadly. Thiel's later sponsorship of JD Vance's 2019 conversion to Roman Catholicism brought Vance into the same Opus Dei-adjacent Catholic intellectual milieu.

In 1995, three years after his Stanford Law graduation, Thiel and David O. Sacks published *The Diversity Myth: "Multiculturalism" and the Politics of Intolerance at Stanford*, through the Independent Institute press. The book is a polemical attack on Stanford's multicultural curricular reforms of the late 1980s and the university administration's handling of campus speech controversies. Two specific passages in the book have received subsequent scrutiny and remain, in 2026, in the documentary record.

The first is the defence of **Keith Rabois**. In 1992, Rabois, a Stanford Law School student, stood outside the home of a Stanford resident assistant — the RA, Dennis Matthies, was the housing supervisor for Otero House on Stanford's campus — and shouted, repeatedly, "**Faggot! Faggot! Hope you die of AIDS!**" The university disciplined Rabois; he transferred to Harvard Law to complete his degree. *The Diversity Myth* presents the incident not as an act of bigotry but as legitimate political speech against a censorious administration, and devotes serious argument to Rabois's defence. Rabois went on, after Stanford, to become a central member of the PayPal Mafia — operating executive at PayPal, founding executive at LinkedIn, partner at Khosla Ventures, partner at Founders Fund. He is, in 2026, one of Thiel's closest long-term collaborators, and is the founder of the e-commerce company OpenStore. Thiel's published defence of Rabois's "Hope you die of AIDS" speech, in a book co-authored with the future Trump AI Czar, is in the record.

The second is the discussion of date rape. In a chapter critiquing what Thiel and Sacks call Stanford's "multicultural rape charge" framework, the authors address a 1991 case in which a 17-year-old Stanford freshman was raped while intoxicated. Thiel and Sacks wrote: "Although [the alleged perpetrator] was guilty of serving alcohol to an underage woman and taking advantage of her resulting lack of judgement, there was no sexual assault... Understandably, however, the woman regretted the whole incident afterwards." A separate passage, on the general question of intoxicated consent: "But since a multicultural rape charge may indicate nothing more than belated regret, a woman might 'realize' that she had been 'raped' the next day or even many days later. Under these circumstances, it is unclear who should be held responsible. If the alcohol made both of them do it, then why should the woman's consent be obviated any more than the man's? Why is all blame placed on the man?" When these passages were unearthed in October 2016 by *Guardian* journalist Julie Carrie Wong, Thiel issued a brief statement to Forbes calling the passages "insensitive, crudely argued" and saying he wished he had never written them. The retraction came eight days before the *Access Hollywood* tape was made public and three weeks before Thiel donated $1.25 million to Donald Trump's campaign — Trump being the candidate against whom twenty women would, by election day, have filed sexual assault allegations.

The *Diversity Myth* is, in 2026, twenty-one years old, and Thiel has formally distanced himself from parts of it. The book remains in print. Its co-author, David Sacks, is currently the most senior AI policymaker in the United States government. The Rabois incident's principal subject is, in 2026, the senior partner of OpenStore and a continuing close investment collaborator with Thiel. The personnel are the same personnel. The book's positions, in mature operational form, are now the staff positions of the second Trump administration.

## III. The PayPal years and the founding of the Mafia

In 1996, Thiel left a brief career as a derivatives trader at Credit Suisse Financial Products in New York and as a securities lawyer at Sullivan & Cromwell, returning to California to found Thiel Capital Management, a multi-strategy hedge fund. In December 1998, he co-founded **Confinity** with **Max Levchin**, a Ukrainian-American engineer he had met after Levchin had attended a Thiel lecture at Stanford. Confinity's initial product was Palm Pilot encryption software; the company pivoted in 1999 to digital payments under the brand name **PayPal**.

In March 2000, Confinity merged with **X.com**, an online bank founded by **Elon Musk**. The merged company traded for several months under the X.com name with Musk as CEO. Internal disagreements between Musk and the Thiel-Levchin faction over technical infrastructure (Musk wanted to migrate to Windows; Thiel and Levchin preferred Unix) and corporate direction came to a head in September 2000. While Musk was on a delayed honeymoon flight to Australia, the board removed him as CEO. Thiel was installed in his place. The company was renamed PayPal. Musk remained the largest shareholder and a board member; the personal animosity from the 2000 ouster has been a documented feature of the Thiel-Musk relationship across the subsequent quarter-century, though the two have continued to collaborate on shared political and business interests.

PayPal was acquired by eBay in October 2002 for $1.5 billion in stock. Thiel personally received $55 million in the transaction. The PayPal employee cohort had, by then, formed the dense personal network that has subsequently become known as the **PayPal Mafia**: Thiel, Musk, Levchin, **Reid Hoffman** (later LinkedIn), **David Sacks** (later Yammer, then the Trump administration), **Roelof Botha** (later Sequoia Capital), **Keith Rabois**, **Ken Howery** (later Founders Fund and US ambassador), **Luke Nosek** (Founders Fund), **Steve Chen / Chad Hurley / Jawed Karim** (later YouTube), **Premal Shah** (later Kiva), **Jeremy Stoppelman / Russel Simmons** (later Yelp), **Dave McClure** (later 500 Startups), **Jack Selby**, **Yishan Wong** (later Reddit CEO).

The PayPal Mafia is not a metaphor. It is a documented network of thirty individuals who emerged from a single mid-2000-2002 corporate environment and who have, across the subsequent quarter-century, repeatedly co-invested, co-founded, and politically coordinated. They organised, beginning in 2003, around Thiel as the senior partner. They co-founded or seeded Tesla, SpaceX, LinkedIn, Yelp, YouTube, Yammer, Palantir, Founders Fund, and dozens of secondary companies. They have, since 2016, increasingly converged on a shared right-wing political project — at the time of writing, the senior PayPal Mafia figures (Thiel, Musk, Sacks, Hoffman as the Democratic-aligned dissenter, Rabois, Howery) are among the most consequential political donors in the United States.

The structural significance is that what is sometimes presented in the press as a collection of independent Silicon Valley billionaires with divergent views is, in operational reality, a coordinated network with a twenty-five-year history of joint investment, joint hiring, joint political activity, and joint ideological development around a small set of core texts (Girard, *The Sovereign Individual*, Yarvin's *Unqualified Reservations*, *Zero to One*) that the principals all read and discuss. The network is the unit. Thiel is the senior partner.

## IV. Palantir and the surveillance project

In 2003, Thiel used proceeds from the PayPal sale to found **Palantir Technologies**. The name comes from the *palantíri* of Tolkien's *Lord of the Rings* — seven crystal balls used by the rulers of Númenor to see across distances and ages. The naming convention is significant: it is the explicit articulation of the company's purpose as elite surveillance, in the language of an old-aristocratic literary tradition. Thiel has been transparent about this naming choice from the outset.

The co-founders were **Alex Karp** (Thiel's Stanford Law classmate, then living in Frankfurt as a PhD philosophy student at the University of Frankfurt, where he had studied under Jürgen Habermas — a detail that has acquired ironic significance given Palantir's subsequent operational role), **Nathan Gettings** (a PayPal engineer), **Joe Lonsdale** (a Stanford undergraduate and *Stanford Review* editor at the time), and **Stephen Cohen** (also a Stanford undergraduate).

Thiel personally bankrolled **$30 million** of the initial capital. The first external institutional investment, in 2004, came from **In-Q-Tel** — the venture capital arm of the Central Intelligence Agency, established in 1999 under CIA Director George Tenet on the explicit model of having the intelligence community participate as a venture investor in the technology companies whose products it intended to use. In-Q-Tel invested $2 million in Palantir. The CIA was Palantir's first major customer.

The CIA's incumbent analytics platform in 2004 was the British i2 *Analyst's Notebook*. Palantir's Gotham product was designed to replace it. The replacement was accomplished over the following several years. By 2007-2008, Palantir's software had been integrated with the data systems of the FBI, the NSA, the US Army Special Operations Command, and, beginning in 2008, the Israeli Defense Forces. The expansion since has been continuous. By 2026, Palantir is the principal data-fusion platform of the American national-security state. Its software integrates databases across the CIA, FBI, NSA, DOD, ICE, DHS, IRS, HHS, SSA, and most of the major federal agencies. It supports the targeting decisions of the United States Special Operations Command in counter-terrorism strike operations. It has provided, since the early 2010s, the principal data infrastructure for the US Immigration and Customs Enforcement deportation programme — through its **Falcon** (case management) and **Investigative Case Management** systems. It is, in 2026, a contractor to the Israeli Defense Forces, where its software supports the **Lavender** and **Gospel** AI targeting systems that have been used to generate kill lists in the Gaza campaign of 2023-2026. It is, in the United Kingdom, the contractor for the National Health Service's **Federated Data Platform** — a £330 million contract awarded in November 2023 to consolidate the NHS's clinical data on a Palantir architecture.

Palantir.s federal contracts doubled in the first year of the second Trump administration — from $541 million in fiscal year 2024 to over $970 million by mid-2025. The US Army's overarching software contract with Palantir has a $10 billion ceiling. The integration of federal-agency data systems on Palantir infrastructure has been accelerated through Trump-administration executive orders, principally the March 2025 executive order on inter-agency data sharing, which provides the legal basis for previously siloed federal databases to be consolidated. The result, as of mid-2026, is that the American federal government's understanding of its own population is mediated principally through a single private company's software, owned and controlled by a private individual who has written, in print, that he no longer believes freedom and democracy are compatible.

This is not metaphor. It is operational reality. Peter Thiel is, in his capacity as chairman and principal shareholder of Palantir, the proprietor of the data-surveillance system that the United States government uses to identify, locate, and act against its own population. The same individual is the political sponsor of the sitting Vice President. The fusion of these two roles in one biography is what the term "regent" is for.

## V. Facebook and Founders Fund

In August 2004, Peter Thiel met **Mark Zuckerberg** through Sean Parker (Napster co-founder, then briefly Facebook president). Thiel invested $500,000 in Facebook in exchange for 10.2 percent of the company and a seat on the board. He was the first outside investor in Facebook; the investment is, in raw multiple terms, one of the most successful single venture investments in technology history. Thiel's $500,000, sold in tranches between 2012 and 2017, returned $1 billion. Thiel remained on the Facebook (later Meta) board until February 2022, when he stepped down to focus on political activity.

The Facebook investment matters not only for its return but for what it bought. Thiel had, between 2004 and 2022, eighteen years of direct board-level access to the company that became, in that period, the principal information-distribution infrastructure for three billion human beings. He was on the board during Facebook's transformation from a Harvard social network into a global political-information system. He was on the board during the 2016 Cambridge Analytica period (and was, per multiple subsequent reports, the board member most sympathetic to Cambridge Analytica's data-harvesting methodology, which Thiel viewed as legitimate). He was on the board during the 2017-2018 controversies over Russian influence operations on the platform. He was on the board during the 2020-2021 content-moderation decisions surrounding the 2020 election. He has not, in public statements, addressed the substance of these board-level decisions in detail. The board minutes are not public.

In 2005, Thiel founded **Founders Fund** with Ken Howery and Luke Nosek (both PayPal alumni). Founders Fund is the principal investment vehicle through which Thiel has, over the subsequent twenty years, exercised his influence on the technology industry. By 2026, the fund manages $11 billion across multiple funds. Its principal positions include SpaceX (a stake of 5 percent acquired beginning 2008), Palantir (a continuing position), Stripe, Anduril (the defence-technology company founded by Palmer Luckey in 2017), Airbnb (early stage), and historically Facebook. Founders Fund's portfolio and the broader Thiel-network investments now constitute, in aggregate, the most concentrated single-network exposure to the consolidating American technology and defence industries that any private actor has ever assembled.

Founders Fund's smaller and more politically targeted investments have, since 2013, included funding for Curtis Yarvin's Tlon Corporation — the corporate vehicle for the Urbit decentralised computing platform — about which more below.

## VI. The 2009 Cato essay

In April 2009, *Cato Unbound* — the online publication of the libertarian Cato Institute (itself a Koch-network institution, as documented in Chapter 6) — published Thiel's essay "**The Education of a Libertarian**." The essay is the most explicit on-the-record articulation of Thiel's political worldview. It is also the document that has, more than any other, shaped the subsequent ideological development of the post-democratic American right. The principal passages bear quotation.

The opening:

> "I remain committed to the faith of my teenage years: to authentic human freedom as a precondition for the highest good. I stand against confiscatory taxes, totalitarian collectives, and the ideology of the inevitability of the death of every individual. For all these reasons, I still call myself 'libertarian.' But I must confess that over the last two decades, I have changed radically on the question of how to achieve these goals. **Most I no longer believe that freedom and democracy are compatible.**"

The proximate cause of the incompatibility, in Thiel's account, is the expansion of the franchise:

> "The 1920s were the last decade in American history during which one could be genuinely optimistic about politics. Since 1920, **the vast increase in welfare beneficiaries and the extension of the franchise to women — two constituencies that are notoriously tough for libertarians — have rendered the notion of 'capitalist democracy' into an oxymoron.**"

The essay's prescription, given the incompatibility, was that libertarians should "escape" politics rather than reform it, via three frontiers: cyberspace, outer space, and seasteading. Thiel funded all three. PayPal was framed by Thiel, in his early statements about the company's purpose, as a cyber-currency project intended to escape national monetary sovereignty ("a new world currency, free from all government control"). Thiel was an early major outside investor in SpaceX (Musk's space-launch project). And Thiel funded the **Seasteading Institute** founded by Patri Friedman — Milton Friedman's grandson, David Friedman's son — in 2008, with seed capital and continuing support.

When the women-and-welfare passage produced public outcry, Cato Unbound published a follow-up note from Thiel: "While I don't think any class of people should be disenfranchised, I have little hope that voting will make things better." The clarification is not the retraction it appeared to be. It says that Thiel does not propose to remove anyone's vote; it does not retract the claim that the extension of the franchise to women has made capitalist democracy an oxymoron. The two propositions are consistent. Thiel believes the franchise should not be removed, *and* he believes its existence has rendered the system unworkable. His conclusion is therefore not to reform the franchise but to exit politics altogether — to construct private alternatives that are not subject to it.

The 2009 essay is, read carefully, the founding document of the post-democratic project that the rest of this chapter will trace. It states the diagnosis (democracy and freedom are incompatible because the democratic majority will redistribute), the prescription (exit rather than reform), and the strategy (private capital construction of alternative institutions outside the reach of democratic politics). Every subsequent Thiel project — Palantir, the Yarvin sponsorship, the Vance pipeline, the second Trump administration's policy agenda — is an operational implementation of this diagnosis. The essay was published in 2009. By 2026, the working programme is in place.

## VII. Curtis Yarvin and the neoreactionary funding

In 2007, an obscure San Francisco software engineer named Curtis Guy Yarvin began writing a blog under the pen name **Mencius Moldbug**. The blog, *Unqualified Reservations*, ran until 2014 and produced what is now treated as the founding corpus of the **Dark Enlightenment** or **neoreactionary (NRx)** movement — an explicitly anti-democratic, anti-egalitarian political philosophy that argues that the American liberal-democratic system is a failed experiment and should be replaced by an authoritarian alternative.

Yarvin's core doctrinal contribution is **neocameralism** — the proposal that nation-states should be reorganised as joint-stock corporations, with the country's territory and population as the corporation's assets, and a single "CEO" sovereign in the place of elected government. The corporate CEO would exercise the executive authority that, in the current system, is distributed across the President, Congress, the courts, the press, the universities, and what Yarvin calls "the Cathedral" — his term for the network of educational, media, and nonprofit institutions that, in his account, controls American discourse and is the principal obstacle to a return to good governance.

Yarvin has explicitly argued, in print, for the dissolution of American democracy. From his 2021 *Gray Mirror* writings: "Because the university is the heart of the old regime, it is absolutely essential to the success of any regime change that all accredited universities be both physically and economically liquidated." From his 2012 writings: "If Americans want to change their government, they're going to have to get over their dictator phobia." In a January 2025 *New York Times* interview, Yarvin argued that Franklin Roosevelt had, in his first inaugural, demanded absolute power from Congress, and that the precedent justified a contemporary demand by an executive for the same.

These are not subtle positions. They are, by the ordinary standards of American political discourse, fascist positions in the technical sense — positions that hold that liberal-democratic constitutional government should be replaced by a unitary executive operating outside the normal legal constraints. Yarvin is sometimes presented in the press as an idiosyncratic blogger; the documentary record establishes that he is a funded propagandist whose work is treated, by the most consequential individuals in the contemporary American right, as serious political theory.

The funding, as far as the public record can reconstruct it, has been:

- **Thiel personally** gave $100,000 to John Burnham (Yarvin's Tlon co-founder) in 2011.
- **Founders Fund** (Thiel's firm) invested in **Tlon Corporation**, the corporate vehicle for the Urbit decentralised-computing project that Yarvin co-founded in 2013. The investment has been continuous since.
- **Marc Andreessen** (subject of a subsequent chapter) has also personally invested in Tlon and has publicly described Yarvin as a "friend."

Yarvin's own description of the relationship to Thiel, in his June 2016 emails to Milo Yiannopoulos (subsequently published by BuzzFeed): "I watched the election at his house, I think my hangover lasted into Tuesday. **He's fully enlightened, just plays it very carefully.**" The word "enlightened," in the Yarvin lexicon, means having internalised the Dark Enlightenment doctrine — that liberal democracy is a failed experiment and that the proper political objective is its replacement. Yarvin has consistently maintained that Thiel is a fellow believer who, for tactical reasons, presents himself more conventionally in public.

The contemporary operational significance of the Yarvin doctrine, as of 2026:

- **JD Vance** discussed Yarvin's influence on his political thinking in a July 2024 podcast interview. Vance has been more circumspect since assuming the vice presidency, but the influence is on the public record.
- **Michael Anton**, the State Department Director of Policy Planning in the second Trump administration, has discussed Yarvin's ideas in his published writings. Yarvin has claimed publicly to have provided staffing recommendations to Anton.
- **Yarvin attended Trump's inaugural gala on 20 January 2025**, where *Politico* described him as an "informal guest of honor" due to his "outsize influence over the Trumpian right."
- **The DOGE programme** — Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency, operational from 20 January to late May 2025, which eliminated 220,000 federal positions, dismantled USAID, and accelerated the consolidation of federal data on Palantir infrastructure — is the form of Yarvin's "hard reboot" doctrine. The doctrine had been published in *Unqualified Reservations* between 2007 and 2014; the Trump-Musk-Vance administration implemented it from January 2025.

This is the evidence. A funded propagandist whose principal doctrine is the dissolution of American democracy has, since 2013, been the "house political philosopher" — Max Chafkin's phrase, in his 2021 Thiel biography *The Contrarian* — of the most senior Silicon Valley investment circle. That circle's political project, executed through the funding and installation of Trump-Vance into the executive branch, has proceeded to implement major elements of the propagandist's programme. The funding is Thiel's. The political execution is Vance's. The execution agent on the ground was Musk. The intellectual scaffolding is Yarvin's.

## VIII. The Gawker case

In late 2007, the gossip blog Gawker — specifically its tech-industry affiliate Valleywag, under writer Owen Thomas — published an article headlined "Peter Thiel is totally gay, people." The article was factually accurate. Thiel has not contested its factual claims. He had, before the article, lived a deliberately discreet personal life; after the article, he was out, although he did not give a heavy public interview on his sexuality until much later. He would, in 2016, speak at the Republican National Convention as an explicitly gay Republican — the first openly gay convention speaker since Jim Kolbe in 2000.

In 2012, Gawker editor A.J. Daulerio published a two-minute extract from a thirty-minute sex tape of the professional wrestler Terry Bollea (stage name Hulk Hogan) with Heather Clem, the wife of radio personality Bubba the Love Sponge. The tape had been recorded without Bollea's knowledge. The extract included ten seconds of explicit sexual content. Bollea sued Gawker for invasion of privacy, infliction of emotional distress, and infringement of personality rights, in Florida state court, in 2013.

What was not public at the time, and would not be public until *Forbes* magazine's Ryan Mac broke the story in May 2016, was that Bollea's lawsuit was being secretly funded by Peter Thiel. The arrangement had been brokered through Aron D'Souza, an Australian businessman who had been a Thiel associate since 2011. Thiel's funding — $10 million in legal expenses over the course of the lawsuit — paid for high-end Los Angeles litigation counsel (Charles Harder) that Bollea could not otherwise have afforded, and was specifically designed not just to win the case but to bankrupt Gawker.

In March 2016, the Florida jury awarded Bollea $140.1 million ($115 million compensatory, $25 million punitive). Gawker, unable to post the appeal bond, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in June 2016. Gawker founder Nick Denton personally filed for bankruptcy in August 2016. Gawker.com was shut down. Univision purchased the remaining Gawker properties (Deadspin, Jezebel, Gizmodo, Kotaku, Lifehacker) for $135 million.

Thiel called his role "one of my greater philanthropic things that I've done." The framing — that the destruction of a media organisation by a billionaire who had been written about critically by that organisation, executed through a secretly funded third-party lawsuit, was a philanthropic act — is the framing. The substance is that a private actor used a large fraction of his personal fortune to bankrupt a publication whose editorial decisions he disliked, by funding an unrelated party's lawsuit against it without disclosing that he was the funder.

The precedent has been applied repeatedly since. Mike Lindell's funding of various suits. The Murdoch family's litigation strategies. The Dominion v. Fox News settlement (different legal posture, but the underlying mechanic — the use of large-scale litigation funding to discipline media coverage — has now been generalised). The chilling effect on American investigative journalism is and documented. The press environment in which a small magazine or independent online publication could afford to investigate Silicon Valley figures, which existed when Gawker was reporting in the 2000s and early 2010s, no longer exists. The cost of being wrong, or of being right but unable to prove it to a satisfied jury under hostile litigation funding, is bankruptcy. The chilling effect is the point.

Thiel's funding of the Gawker case is the prototype of what private wealth, applied to the courts, can do to a free press. It is the journalism-discipline mechanism corresponding to the Yarvin doctrine on the academic side. The universities, in Yarvin's programme, are to be liquidated. The press, in Thiel's operational practice, is to be sued into bankruptcy. The combination produces the information environment in which the rest of the agenda can be pursued.

## IX. Trump 2016 and the post-*Access Hollywood* donation

Thiel had, before 2016, been a major Republican donor — to Ron Paul's 2012 primary campaign, to various PACs and candidates — but had not been a prominent national political figure. The 2016 cycle changed this.

On 21 July 2016, Thiel spoke from the stage of the Republican National Convention in Cleveland, endorsing Donald Trump and identifying himself as a proud gay Republican. The endorsement was, in its political context, significant principally because it signalled that Silicon Valley — which had been overwhelmingly Democratic-leaning in its public political positions through 2016 — contained a major dissenting wealth-and-power node that was prepared to be openly aligned with the Trump project.

On 8 October 2016, the *Access Hollywood* tape was released, showing Trump bragging about sexual assault. Several major donors paused or withdrew their support. The Trump campaign, going into the final month, faced a serious financial and political crisis.

On 15 October 2016, Thiel donated $1.25 million to Trump-affiliated PACs and the Trump campaign — a publicly disclosed donation, timed specifically to demonstrate continued support during the *Access Hollywood* fallout. The donation was, in the campaign's internal calculus, important not only for the money but for the signal: that one of the most prominent figures in the technology industry was prepared to be publicly identified with the Trump campaign at its lowest moment.

Trump won. In November-December 2016, Thiel was placed on Trump's transition executive committee. Per Trump's own subsequent public statements, Thiel personally chose which technology industry CEOs would be invited to the December 2016 Trump Tower "Tech Summit" — the highest-profile early signal of the relationship between Trump and Silicon Valley. The CEOs invited (Cook, Page, Sandberg, Bezos, Nadella, Rometty, Catz, Musk, Krzanich, Robbins, Schmidt, Karp) constituted, between them, 80 percent of American technology industry market capitalisation. The fact that Thiel made the invitation list is significant for two reasons. First, it placed Thiel — alone among private citizens — in the role of broker between the Trump administration and the American technology industry. Second, the resulting personal relationships, formed in the late 2016 transition period, have continued to operate across the subsequent decade. The pattern of senior technology figures attending Trump-administration events, donating to Trump-aligned political vehicles, and operationally cooperating with Trump-administration agencies that has developed in 2024-2026 has its origin in the December 2016 Trump Tower meeting that Thiel curated.

## X. The Vance project

In 2011, Thiel was invited to give a talk at Yale Law School. **James David Vance**, then a 27-year-old second-year law student at Yale, attended. Vance, by his own subsequent account in an essay for a Catholic literary journal, was "steered away from a career in big law and instead [toward] pursuing entrepreneurship" by the talk. Thiel and Vance kept in touch.

Vance graduated Yale Law in 2013. He worked briefly at Sidley Austin in Washington, then moved to San Francisco. He drafted the memoir that became *Hillbilly Elegy*, which was published in June 2016 and rapidly became a national bestseller — the canonical mainstream explanation of the white Appalachian-Rust-Belt working-class political shift to Trump.

In 2016, on the back of *Hillbilly Elegy*'s success, Thiel hired Vance as a principal at **Mithril Capital**, Thiel's global venture capital firm. By the subsequent reports of former Mithril colleagues to the *Wall Street Journal* (July 2024), Vance was rarely physically present at the Mithril offices; he spent most of his Mithril period promoting *Hillbilly Elegy*. No specific significant Mithril deals are publicly associated with Vance's work. The position was, a sponsor's stipend.

In 2017, Vance left Mithril for Steve Case's Revolution. In 2019, he left Revolution to co-found **Narya Capital** in Cincinnati, Ohio. The funding came from Thiel (at least 15 percent of the initial capital), **Eric Schmidt**, and **Marc Andreessen**. Narya invested principally in defence, AI, and Appalachian-region startups, including the indoor-agriculture company AppHarvest (which subsequently filed for bankruptcy in 2023 after multiple SEC investigations).

In 2019, Vance converted to **Roman Catholicism**. By his own account, Thiel was the principal influence on the conversion. Vance's adopted spiritual framework — the *ordo amoris* doctrine that he has subsequently used to justify "America First" hierarchies of obligation — is the Catholic intellectual framework adjacent to the Opus Dei circles Thiel has frequented since Stanford. Pope Francis, before his death in April 2025, publicly criticised Vance's interpretation of *ordo amoris*; Pope Leo XIV has continued the criticism. Vance has not retracted.

In private messages to a friend dated 2016 — disclosed in 2024 — Vance had described Trump as "an American Hitler" and called himself "a Never Trump guy." By 2021, this position had been completely reversed.

The reversal was executed across the following sequence:

- **February 2021**: Thiel personally introduces Vance to Trump at Mar-a-Lago.
- **March 2021**: Thiel donates $10 million to **Protect Ohio Values**, a super PAC supporting a potential Vance Senate run. Robert Mercer also contributes.
- **April 2021**: Vance announces interest in the Senate seat being vacated by Rob Portman.
- **May 2021**: Vance launches an exploratory committee.
- **1 July 2021**: Vance formally announces his Senate candidacy.
- **2022**: Thiel's total Senate-campaign contributions to Vance reach **$15 million** — the largest single-donor contribution to any individual Senate campaign in American history at that point.
- **April 2022**: Trump endorses Vance.
- **3 May 2022**: Vance wins the Republican primary with 32 percent.
- **8 November 2022**: Vance defeats Tim Ryan in the general election, 53-47.

By the time of his 2022 Senate victory, Vance was operationally a Thiel candidate. His political career had been funded by Thiel from before he entered politics. His introduction to the Republican coalition's principal had been brokered by Thiel. His state-level political infrastructure had been built with Thiel money. His personal-network conversion from "American Hitler" Never-Trumper to MAGA loyalist had occurred during the period in which his financial dependence on the Thiel network was deepest.

In 2024, when Trump began the selection process for his vice-presidential running mate, Thiel was among the principal external lobbyists for Vance. Per *New York Times* reporting, Thiel personally called Trump multiple times to advocate Vance. Elon Musk, David Sacks, and Tucker Carlson reinforced. Donald Trump Jr. and Eric Trump both backed Vance internally. On 15 July 2024, Trump announced Vance as the VP nominee. On 5 November 2024, Trump and Vance won the election. On 20 January 2025, Vance was sworn in as the 50th Vice President of the United States.

The Vice President of the United States, in 2026, is in every operational respect a continuation of Peter Thiel's biography. The Vice President was first interested in entrepreneurship by Thiel. The Vice President was hired into venture capital by Thiel. The Vice President's venture firm was capitalised by Thiel. The Vice President was introduced to the President by Thiel. The Vice President's Senate campaign was funded principally by Thiel. The Vice President's VP nomination was lobbied for by Thiel. The Vice President's intellectual framework — Catholic conservatism, post-liberal politics, the Yarvin neoreactionary doctrines — is the framework Thiel has cultivated across his Silicon Valley circle for two decades.

This is what a regent is. The medieval term denoted a figure who governed in the name of a monarch who was nominally sovereign but practically incapable of governing without the regent's direction. The Trump-Vance administration's economic, technological, and political agenda — Stargate, the DOGE programme, the Palantir-led federal-data consolidation, the deportation expansion through Palantir-supported ICE operations, the Catholic post-liberal social policy framework, the Yarvin-derived institutional-liquidation programme aimed at universities and the federal civil service — is in major part the agenda of Peter Thiel, executed by Donald Trump's administration through Peter Thiel's protégé.

## XI. The 2024 nominal disengagement

In March 2024, Peter Thiel gave an interview to *The Atlantic*'s Barton Gellman titled "Peter Thiel Is Taking a Break From Democracy." In the interview, Thiel told Gellman that he had decided not to donate directly to Trump's 2024 campaign — that he had become disappointed in the first Trump administration's chaotic execution and was sceptical of further direct political donations. Trump had personally called Thiel asking for $10 million in early 2024, Thiel said, and he had refused. The interview was widely reported across the spring and summer of 2024 as Thiel's "stepping back" from politics.

The reporting is misleading. The 2024 disengagement is a nominal disengagement that operates only in the most literally direct sense — Thiel did not, in 2024, make a personal donation to Trump's campaign committee. The structural Thiel influence over the 2024 cycle and the subsequent administration operated through:

- **JD Vance**'s vice-presidential candidacy, which Thiel had created and lobbied for.
- **David Sacks** as the principal Trump fundraiser within Silicon Valley — a Thiel PayPal Mafia colleague since 1998 and *Diversity Myth* co-author since 1995.
- **Marc Andreessen** as the principal organiser of the Silicon Valley pro-Trump shift — Thiel's long-standing investment partner and the man who has explicitly cited Curtis Yarvin as a friend and intellectual influence.
- **Palantir's** continuing role as the technical infrastructure of the federal surveillance state, and its dramatic contract expansion under the Trump-Vance administration.
- **Founders Fund's** continuing investment in companies whose growth is directly enabled by Trump-administration policy choices, particularly in the defence-technology layer (Anduril, etc.).

The pattern is the standard pattern. A senior figure makes a nominal public withdrawal from direct involvement while the influence continues through the network. The net financial effect of Thiel's "stepping back" from 2024 has been positive in the extreme: his personal net worth has grown by $20 billion since the November 2024 election, principally through the Palantir share-price appreciation that has followed from the federal-contract expansion. He gave up the appearance of direct political activity and gained an order of magnitude more financial benefit from the administration his protégé is serving in than direct donations could have purchased.

The "stepping back" is not the absence of influence. It is the protection of the influence from attribution.

## XII. The position in 2026

This is what Peter Thiel is, as of May 2026:

- The chairman and principal shareholder of **Palantir Technologies**, the company that operates the data-fusion infrastructure of the American national-security state, the American immigration enforcement system, and large portions of the Israeli military targeting infrastructure and the UK National Health Service's clinical data system. Palantir's federal contract revenue has doubled since the inauguration of the Trump-Vance administration, and the company is, on standard valuation methods, now worth in excess of $400 billion.
- The political sponsor of the **sitting Vice President of the United States**, James David Vance, whose career Thiel funded, mentored, and lobbied into the second-highest constitutional office.
- The principal funder, since at least 2013, of **Curtis Yarvin** — the political theorist whose anti-democratic doctrines are now operationally implemented by the Trump-Vance administration.
- The principal of **Founders Fund**, the venture capital firm whose portfolio includes the most consequential consolidating American technology and defence companies of the 2020s — SpaceX, Palantir, Stripe, Anduril, and many others.
- The senior figure of the **PayPal Mafia**, the thirty-person network whose junior members include the world's wealthiest individual (Musk), the Trump administration's AI and Crypto Czar (Sacks), and the principal of the LinkedIn-OpenAI Democratic-aligned wing (Hoffman).
- The author, in 2009, of the most influential public articulation of the post-democratic American right, the *Cato Unbound* essay declaring freedom and democracy incompatible.
- The funder, in 2013-2016, of the Gawker bankruptcy litigation that established the active template for billionaire-funded litigation as a discipline mechanism against American journalism.
- The principal beneficiary, in financial terms, of the second Trump administration's first eighteen months, with personal net worth growth of $20 billion since November 2024.

He has done all this without holding elected office, without holding a senior government appointment, and without giving a personal interview to a major American publication on the substance of his political project at any point since the 2009 Cato essay. He has constructed, around himself, an information environment in which his role is systematically minimised by the mainstream press — which is, by the documentation of Chapter 9, captured — and in which the knowledge institutions treat his statements about the incompatibility of freedom and democracy as eccentric private opinions rather than as the operational doctrine they have, since 2024, become.

This is the regent. The political institutions of the United States have been, in the operative respects that matter for the question of who governs and to what end, captured by a project whose principal architect is one specific 58-year-old private citizen, with a specific family history in apartheid-era southern African uranium mining, a specific intellectual formation in Stanford's right-wing student press of the late 1980s, a specific business trajectory through PayPal and Palantir, and a specific philosophical commitment, declared in print in 2009 and operationalised across the seventeen subsequent years, that the freedom worth preserving cannot coexist with the franchise his society's history extended to women in 1920.

The man who wrote that sentence is, in 2026, in the position of effective regent over the second Trump administration's policy agenda.

## XIII. The Palantir board and the founder voting trust

The previous sections have described Thiel's biography and political project. The investigative standard also requires investigation of the people who sit on Thiel's boards, run his funds, and serve as his operational lieutenants. The mechanism of accumulation has never been a single individual operating alone. It has been a small network of named individuals whose joint biographies are themselves the working form of the project.

The Palantir board, per the company's April 2025 proxy filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission, consists of seven directors. Three are Founders — Thiel as Chairman, Karp as CEO, Stephen Cohen as President. Four are nominally external — Alexander Moore, Alexandra Wolfe Schiff, Lauren Friedman Stat, and Eric Woersching. The composition of the "external" four is, on examination, the composition of a captured board.

**Alexander Karp** has been Palantir's CEO since 2004. Karp is, by background, Thiel's Stanford Law School classmate and a German philosophy PhD (University of Frankfurt, dissertation under Jürgen Habermas, the principal philosopher of the post-war German left). His public persona — long-haired, sweater-wearing, jiu-jitsu-practising, with a habitual rhetorical style of mixing Continental philosophy citations with American patriotic militarism — has been carefully constructed to obscure the fact that he is, the executive officer of the principal surveillance company of the American national-security state. Karp's 2025 book *The Technological Republic*, co-authored with Palantir's Head of Corporate Affairs Nicholas Zamiska, is the most explicit on-the-record articulation of the Palantir worldview: that the West is in civilisational decline, that Silicon Valley's hostility to defence work has been treason, that the proper response is the aggressive deployment of AI in military and security applications. The book has been reviewed favourably by the press outlets the book has documented and largely ignored by the limited remaining serious press. Karp's personal net worth in 2025 was $13.9 billion (Bloomberg).

**Stephen Cohen** has been Palantir's President since 2005. Cohen was, in 2003, a Stanford undergraduate when Thiel and Lonsdale recruited him as a co-founder. He has not given a public interview of any substance in twenty years. His net worth in 2025 was $5.6 billion.

**Alexander Moore** is the Palantir board's "outside" director who is most directly tied to the Thiel network. Moore is a partner at **8VC**, the venture capital firm founded by Palantir co-founder **Joe Lonsdale** after Lonsdale departed Palantir in 2009. Lonsdale is one of the most politically active members of the Thiel network — a major Republican donor, founder of the Cicero Institute (the right-wing state-policy think tank), and operationally embedded in the second Trump administration through his network of appointments. Moore's presence on the Palantir board is, the institutional bridge between Palantir and Lonsdale's 8VC portfolio — not an independent check on management.

**Alexandra Wolfe Schiff** is the daughter of the novelist **Tom Wolfe** (*The Bonfire of the Vanities*, *The Right Stuff*, *A Man in Full*, *The Painted Word*, *The Electric Kool-Aid Acid Test*). Tom Wolfe was, throughout his life, a politically conservative writer whose work was consistently sympathetic to the New York and Silicon Valley elites his novels nominally satirised. His daughter, before joining the Palantir board in July 2020, had worked as a *Wall Street Journal* reporter for seven years. Her resignation from the WSJ was announced on the same day as her appointment to the Palantir board. The journalism-ethics question — whether a working journalist can move directly from covering a beat that includes Palantir to receiving Palantir compensation — produced limited public discussion at the time and has produced none since. She has been on the board for nearly six years as of this writing. The relevant point for the book's purposes is structural: the Palantir board's "journalist" director is the daughter of a literary figure of the American conservative cultural circle, who left a major newspaper to join the board of a company whose surveillance products her former colleagues at that newspaper might reasonably have been investigating. The fence between the watchdog and the watched is, in the substance, removed.

**Lauren Friedman Stat** is the CEO of Stat and Co LLC; previously Chairwoman of Valkyrie Intelligence LLC; previously CEO of Figa Jewelry. Her professional background is not in technology, defence, or the regulated industries in which Palantir operates. **Eric Woersching** is a former General Catalyst partner now operating as a "private consultant for early-stage software companies." Neither director has the operational expertise to provide meaningful oversight of Palantir's activities.

The voting structure renders the entire board question secondary. Palantir has a three-class share structure (Class A, Class B, Class F). **All Class F supervoting shares — which carry 49.999999 percent of the total voting power on most matters — are held in a Founder Voting Trust** established by Thiel, Karp, and Cohen, with Wilmington Trust as trustee, that guarantees the three of them retain nearly 50 percent of all voting power regardless of how many of their personal shares they sell. The public-market shareholders who hold the Class A common stock are, providing capital to a company whose actual control they cannot influence. The board is a formality. The Founder Voting Trust is the governance.

**Shyam Sankar** is not a board member but is, the Palantir senior executive most consequential for the company's expansion in the 2020s. Sankar joined Palantir in 2006 as employee number 13. He is now Chief Technology Officer and Executive Vice President. Cornell electrical engineering, Stanford management science. He is, since July 2025, the fifth Palantir billionaire (after Thiel, Karp, Cohen, and Lonsdale). He also serves as Chairman of Ginkgo Bioworks — the synthetic-biology company that itself has major Pentagon and intelligence-community contracts — and as a Hudson Institute trustee. He has testified before the House Select Committee on Strategic Competition with the Chinese Communist Party and the Senate Armed Services Subcommittee on Cybersecurity. He is, structurally, the technical-operational interface between Palantir and the federal national-security apparatus. The Hudson Institute trusteeship is significant: Hudson is the principal Washington think tank advancing the China-confrontation policy that has, since 2018, become the bipartisan US foreign-policy consensus, and Palantir is the principal beneficiary of the defence-procurement expansion that consensus has authorised. The integration of Sankar's institutional roles is the integration of the policy advocacy and the contract recipient.

## XIV. The Founders Fund partner structure

Founders Fund, in 2026, manages $17 billion in committed capital across multiple funds. It is one of the three or four most consequential venture capital firms in the United States; its portfolio includes SpaceX, Palantir, Stripe, Anduril, Neuralink, Airbnb, and two hundred other companies. Its current general partners — the individuals who actually approve investments and sign cheques — are three: Thiel, Napoleon Ta, and Trae Stephens.

**Napoleon Ta** is, of the three, the figure least known to the public. He has been a Founders Fund partner since 2014, covers enterprise software and infrastructure, and is now one of the three named directors on Founders Fund Growth III (the firm's $4.6 billion fund closed in April 2025 — the largest in the firm's history). Ta has not given a heavy public interview; the active substance of his investment activity is documented principally through portfolio-company SEC filings.

**Trae Stephens** is the figure whose biography most directly demonstrates the integrated structure of the Thiel network. Born November 1983 in rural Ohio (thirty miles northeast of Cincinnati, in a log cabin his father built by hand), Stephens attended Georgetown University's School of Foreign Service, studying Arabic and Security Studies. After 9/11, he served in the **United States Intelligence Community** as a computational linguist, building Arabic and Persian name-matching systems for intelligence-agency use. He worked in the Washington office of then-Congressman **Rob Portman** of Ohio — the same Senate seat that **JD Vance** subsequently filled in 2022 with Thiel's funding. Stephens worked at the Embassy of Afghanistan in Washington in the period immediately following the US installation of Hamid Karzai's transitional government. In 2008 he joined **Palantir** as a forward-deployed engineer, working with intelligence-agency customers; he was at Palantir for six years.

In 2013, Thiel recruited Stephens to Founders Fund. After Trump's election in November 2016, Stephens was appointed to the **Trump transition team for the Defense Department**, a role from which he transitioned to the **Defense Innovation Board** under the chairmanship of **Eric Schmidt**. In April 2017 — five months after Trump's inauguration — Stephens co-founded **Anduril Industries** with Palmer Luckey, Matt Grimm, Joe Chen, and Brian Schimpf. The same individual who had served on Trump's Defense transition team and on the Defense Innovation Board advising the Pentagon on procurement reform was, simultaneously, a Founders Fund partner and the executive chairman of the defence-technology startup positioned to benefit from the procurement reforms his board recommended. The arrangement would, by the ordinary standards of public-private conflict of interest, have been disqualifying. In operational substance, it was the explicit design.

By 2025, Anduril was a $30 billion company with a $1+ billion annual revenue run rate, a $20 billion ten-year Army contract awarded in 2025, the Project Maven AI targeting contract, the Air Force Collaborative Combat Aircraft programme, and a position alongside Palantir as one of the two principal Silicon Valley-origin defence contractors. Stephens became a billionaire in June 2025 after Anduril's Series G funding round. He has, simultaneously, served as a commissioner of the Atlantic Council Commission on Software-Defined Warfare — the precise policy-advisory body whose recommendations shape the Pentagon procurement decisions that benefit his portfolio.

Stephens is also, since 2024, the co-founder of **Valinor Enterprises**, a "company of companies" — an incubator structure that produces lean subsidiary defence-technology companies under a unified Valinor corporate umbrella. Valinor's founding team is sourced from **Palantir, Anduril, and Helsing** (the German AI-defence startup). Its CEO is Julie Bush, a former Palantir senior vice president; its other co-founders are General Catalyst's Paul Kwan and Red Cell Partners' Grant Verstandig. Valinor participates in Anduril's Lattice Partner Program and is a member of the Defense Industrial Base Consortium. In one year, Valinor has launched ten product companies. The model — a Founders Fund partner who is also Anduril's executive chairman building an incubator structure whose products integrate with Anduril's own ecosystem and benefit from the procurement preferences he himself helped design as a Defense Innovation Board commissioner — is the integration of the financial, operational, and policy layers in one biography.

Stephens is a **devout Christian**. His wife **Michelle Stephens** founded the Christian nonprofit **ACTS17**, which describes its mission as "ministering to elites" — that is, to senior figures in Silicon Valley, finance, and government. ACTS17 has funded **Peter Thiel's 2025 lecture series on the antichrist** (the lectures, delivered in San Francisco in autumn 2025, are widely reported as the most extended public articulation of Thiel's recent religious-political worldview, which holds that the contemporary left is the working vehicle of an apocalyptic adversary). ACTS17 has also funded events featuring **Garry Tan** (Y Combinator CEO) and **Pat Gelsinger** (former Intel CEO). The structural significance is that the wife of the senior Founders Fund defence-investments partner runs a religious nonprofit funding the senior partner's apocalyptic-political lecture series and the parallel events of two of the most consequential Silicon Valley executives. The institutional integration extends from the corporate boardrooms to the religious institutions to the political donor circles. The same individuals appear at every level.

**Scott Nolan** is a Founders Fund partner who, before joining the firm, worked at SpaceX, where he helped develop the Merlin engine systems and the Dragon capsule. In 2025 he co-founded **General Matter**, a new nuclear-fuel startup that, in the firm's own framing, is "the third part of a trilogy" of Founders Fund-incubated defence-adjacent companies — Palantir, Anduril, and now General Matter. The progression — from surveillance software (Palantir) to autonomous-weapons systems (Anduril) to nuclear-fuel production (General Matter) — is the explicit Founders Fund strategic doctrine that the previous chapters' rentier-extraction history is now extending into the most regulated and most consequential industries of national security.

**Other current and former Founders Fund partners** include:

- **Lauren Gross** — partner and COO since the firm's founding; the sole female partner; leads all non-investment activities (legal, finance, operations).
- **Sam Blond**, **Joey Krug** (former Co-CIO of Pantera Capital and co-founder of Augur, the crypto prediction-market protocol), **Jennifer Campbell**, **Matias Van Thienen**, **Ryan Petersen** (founder of Flexport, the logistics company) — junior partners covering various sectors.
- **Mike Solana** — Chief Marketing Officer; founder of **Pirate Wires**, the right-wing tech-political online publication that is, in 2026, the principal house-publication of the Thiel-aligned technology right. Solana's editorial direction shapes how the network's positions are communicated to its principal audience. Pirate Wires has been a consistent advocate for the Trump administration's tech-industry policy and a consistent critic of the *New York Times*, the academic institutions, and the broader captured-press environment.

**Former partners** include the founding members who have since departed:

- **Ken Howery** — PayPal co-founder, Founders Fund co-founder, former US Ambassador to Sweden 2019-2021 (first Trump administration). The Ambassador appointment is itself significant: it placed a Thiel-network insider in a senior US diplomatic post during the first Trump administration, contributing to the institutional bridges that the network has subsequently used to navigate the second.
- **Luke Nosek** — PayPal co-founder, Founders Fund co-founder. Left Founders Fund in 2017 to found **Gigafund** with **Stephen Oskoui**. Gigafund's principal positions are in **SpaceX, Neuralink, and the Boring Company** — that is, the Musk portfolio. Nosek remains on the SpaceX board. Gigafund is, the Thiel-network capital vehicle that has been used to maintain Thiel-network involvement in Musk's companies after the formal Thiel-Musk relationship cooled following the 2000 PayPal succession dispute.
- **Sean Parker** — Napster co-founder, briefly president of Facebook (2004-2005), joined Founders Fund 2006. Major political donor; historically more Democratic-aligned than the rest of the network, though his recent contributions have spread across parties.
- **Brian Singerman** — joined 2008, partner 2011, moved to emeritus status December 2024. Was the lead Anduril investor at Founders Fund. Now raising **GPx**, a separate $500 million fund.
