By his own claim, his Founders Fund investments returned an average of 18.3× invested capital — more than triple the venture-capital industry average.
- **Keith Rabois** — the *Diversity Myth* "Hope you die of AIDS" Rabois. PayPal executive; LinkedIn founding executive; Square; Khosla Ventures partner; Founders Fund partner 2019-2024; returned to Khosla in 2024. Founder of OpenStore.
- **Bruce Gibney** — author of *A Generation of Sociopaths* (2017), a polemical critique of the Baby Boomer generation that argues the post-1945 generation has been uniquely destructive to American institutions through its narcissistic political choices.
- **Cyan Banister** — PayPal alumna. Among the more conservative-libertarian voices in the network.
- **Kevin Hartz** — Eventbrite co-founder.
- **Geoff Lewis** — founded Bedrock Capital after departing.

The Founders Fund partner roster is, across two decades, a stable network with limited turnover. The same thirty individuals have, since 2005, executed the firm's investment decisions, populated its portfolio-company boards, and provided the lateral movement between Founders Fund, Palantir, Anduril, SpaceX, the Trump administration, the Defense Innovation Board, the Atlantic Council, the Hudson Institute, and the other institutional bridges through which the network operates.

## XV. Mithril Capital and the secondary Thiel vehicles

Beyond Founders Fund, Thiel operates **Mithril Capital Management**, a smaller global venture vehicle founded in 2012 with **Ajay Royan** as managing partner. Mithril's distinguishing feature is its global mandate — Founders Fund focuses on US technology; Mithril invests internationally. At its peak Mithril managed $1 billion.

Royan is the principal operating partner. Yale undergraduate, previous career at Royal Bank of Canada. He and Thiel relocated Mithril from San Francisco to Austin, Texas, in 2018-2019 — a relocation that produced a 2019 lawsuit from former partner **Crystal McKellar** alleging improprieties in the handling of investor capital during the move. McKellar (Yale Law graduate; sister of the actress Danica McKellar) settled out of court but went on to be a public source for subsequent reporting on Mithril's operations. McKellar is also the source — speaking on the record to the *Wall Street Journal* and *Fortune* in July 2024 — for the documented reports that **JD Vance**, during his 2016-2017 tenure as a Mithril principal, "was never once seen in the office."

The Mithril structure is, in 2026, diminished from its peak — much of its capital and many of its operational functions have migrated back to Founders Fund and other Thiel-aligned vehicles. The significance of Mithril is principally to establish the structural fact: the JD Vance career began with a position at a Thiel vehicle whose operational substance — by the documented testimony of his former colleague — was that of a sponsor's stipend rather than working venture-capital employment. The Mithril partnership was, for Vance, the financial bridge between the *Hillbilly Elegy* career and the subsequent Narya-Senate-Vice-Presidency trajectory.

**Thiel Capital LLC** is Thiel's personal investment vehicle, distinct from Founders Fund and Mithril. It is the corporate vehicle through which Thiel manages his personal portfolio outside the firms' institutional structures. **Blake Masters** — Thiel's *Zero to One* co-author — served as Thiel Capital's COO from 2015 to 2022, before his Senate candidacy in Arizona. Thiel Capital's portfolio is private; its activities are documented principally through SEC Form 13D and 13G filings when its positions exceed disclosure thresholds in public companies.

## XVI. The political-operative layer

Beyond the formal investment partners, the Thiel network operates through a layer of political operatives, intellectual-property propagandists, and religious-cultural figures whose roles are not principally financial but who function as the network's interface with electoral politics, with the press, and with the broader cultural environment in which the technology operates.

**Blake Masters**: Stanford Law graduate (2012), Thiel's co-author on *Zero to One* (the book is, structurally, Masters's classroom notes from Thiel's 2012 Stanford "Computer Science 183: Startup" course, edited and published with Thiel as the lead author). Masters was COO of Thiel Capital and the Thiel Foundation from 2015 to 2022. He ran for the US Senate in Arizona in 2022 with **$20 million** of Thiel money — the second-largest Thiel candidate sponsorship after the $15 million Vance investment. Masters lost to Mark Kelly 51.4% to 46.5%. He subsequently ran for a US House seat in Arizona in 2024 and lost the primary. As of 2026 he has returned to investment activity. The Masters operation is the verified case in which the Thiel sponsorship model worked less successfully than the parallel Vance operation — Masters was a less able candidate in a more competitive state, and the same scale of funding did not produce the same electoral outcome. The point is structurally instructive: the Thiel sponsorship model is not a guaranteed success; it requires the right candidate, the right state, and the right cycle. In 2022, Vance was the right candidate; Masters was not. The model adapts.

**Patri Friedman**: Co-founder of the **Seasteading Institute** (2008), with Thiel as the seed funder. Friedman is **Milton Friedman's grandson** and **David Friedman's son** — the genealogy is itself documentation of the intellectual continuity from the Mont Pèlerin Society of 1947 to the seasteading-libertarianism of the 2010s and 2020s. Patri Friedman is now also the principal of **Pronomos Capital**, a charter-cities investment vehicle that Thiel funds. The charter-cities project — the proposition that economic-zone enclaves with private governance can be developed in host countries (Honduras, Próspera) outside the normal legal frameworks of the host state — is the operational continuation of the seasteading idea after the seasteading idea proved technically and politically infeasible. The doctrine is consistent across the Thiel network: construct alternatives to democratic governance through private capital.

**Aron Ping D'Souza**: Australian lawyer based in London. Thiel's intermediary for the 2013-2016 Gawker bankruptcy funding. Since 2023, founder and president of **The Enhanced Games**, the pro-doping athletic competition scheduled for Las Vegas, 21-24 May 2026, where performance-enhancing drugs are permitted under medical supervision. The Enhanced Games has been backed by Thiel, by **German biotech billionaire Christian Angermayer** (founder of Atai Life Sciences), by **Balaji Srinivasan**, and since February 2025 by **Donald Trump Jr.** through **1789 Capital** (the Trump Jr.-aligned fund operated with Omeed Malik and Chris Buskirk).

The Enhanced Games is structurally significant beyond its own modest scale. Per Wired's reporting on Angermayer's investment rationale: "AI will create more leisure time and boost demand for sports entertainment" combined with the development of "performance-enhancing drugs to the largest pharmaceutical market in history." The strategic objective is to use elite athletic competition as a marketing demonstration for a subsequent consumer-pharmaceutical-enhancement business — the same investment thesis that, applied to other categories, has produced the Atai Life Sciences psychedelic-pharmaceutical portfolio. The Enhanced Games is, in this sense, not principally an athletic competition. It is a long-running advertising and regulatory-arbitrage operation designed to normalise the consumer use of currently controlled enhancement substances by demonstrating their safety in elite athletes under medical supervision, with the eventual commercial objective of a mass-market business.

D'Souza had previously co-founded the Australian financial services firm Sargon (2015, with Phillip Kingston); Sargon collapsed into administration in 2020 amid governance and liquidity scandals. The pattern in D'Souza's biography is consistent: financial-services entrepreneurship combined with cause-based publicity operations (Gawker, Enhanced Games), with Thiel as the senior partner and the financial-services side increasingly subordinated to the political and cultural objectives the cause operations serve.

**Aubrey de Grey**: British biogerontologist. Founder of the Methuselah Foundation (longevity research). Recipient of $3.5 million in Thiel funding between 2009 and the present. De Grey's signature claim — that the first human to live to one thousand years old may already be sixty — is a recurring talking point of Thiel's at conferences. The longevity-research investment thesis is connected to the broader Thiel-network worldview: that the wealthy and powerful should, through advanced biotechnology, escape biological constraints on human lifespan that have historically applied uniformly to all classes. The investment is the implementation of the elite-exit doctrine in the biological domain, paralleling the seasteading-and-charter-cities project in the political domain.

**Curtis Yarvin (Mencius Moldbug)** — already covered in detail in Section VII above.

**Christian Angermayer**: German biotech billionaire, founder of Atai Life Sciences (psychedelic medicines; IPO'd 2021). Has co-invested with Thiel in multiple ventures (Enhanced Games, Compass Pathways, and the broader Thiel-aligned biotech portfolio). Angermayer's role is the German bridge: he supplies European capital to the Thiel-aligned American portfolio, and his political activity in Europe — particularly his lobbying for pharmaceutical and economic policy changes aligned with the broader Thiel-network doctrine — extends the network's influence beyond the United States.

**Mike Solana**: Founder of **Pirate Wires** and Chief Marketing Officer of Founders Fund. The principal in-house political-publicist of the Thiel network. Pirate Wires is, in 2026, the most-read right-wing tech-political online publication; its editorial line — hostility to the *New York Times*, to "woke" institutions, to most mainstream press coverage of Silicon Valley, and warm support for the second Trump administration's tech-industry policy — shapes the political education of the network's principal audience of younger Silicon Valley professionals and right-wing political operatives.

**Garry Tan**: President and CEO of Y Combinator (since 2023). Not formally a Thiel lieutenant but operationally aligned. Major Republican political donor ($1 million to a San Francisco mayoral-recall PAC in 2024). Y Combinator under Tan's presidency has dramatically shifted its political orientation rightward; Yarvin and other Thiel-aligned figures have been featured at its events. Tan was the subject of multiple controversies in December 2023 for posting graphic threats against San Francisco progressive city supervisors on X. Through ACTS17 (the Trae-Stephens-related Christian nonprofit), Tan is also networked with the Thiel religious-cultural milieu.

## XVII. The unifying pattern

The composition is the message. The Palantir board, the Founders Fund partners, the Mithril operatives, the political-operative layer, and the cultural-religious nonprofits all draw their personnel from a single network of fifty individuals who have known each other, invested with each other, attended each other's events, married each other's friends, and coordinated politically across the past twenty-five years. The network is the structure. The structure has produced the political outcomes the previous sections of this chapter have documented.

The captured-press framing of these figures presents them as separate billionaires with separate companies and separate views. The documentary record establishes that they are, a single network with a senior partner, an investment committee, a small set of foundational doctrines (Girardian mimetic theory, *The Sovereign Individual* exit doctrine, Yarvin neoreactionary corporate-monarchism, post-liberal Catholic *ordo amoris* hierarchies), and a twenty-five-year track record of joint action toward shared political objectives.

The objective, in 2026, is now achieved. The senior partner is in the position of regent. The network's protégés occupy the second-highest constitutional office, multiple senior administrative appointments, the principal AI-policy positions, the principal financial-policy positions, and the relevant defence-procurement positions. The network's portfolio companies receive the principal contracts. The network's intellectual-property propagandists set the terms of public political debate within the constituency that matters for the network's continued operation.

This is what the regent has built. It is not built in any single individual's biography. It is built across the network's biography. The book has, in this chapter, traced as much of that network as documented sources permits. The remaining chapters will trace, with the same standards, the other senior figures whose biographies extend and complement the Thiel trajectory.

The section that follows is about another man, also formed in apartheid-era southern Africa, also a member of the PayPal Mafia, also operating in 2026 at the technological frontier where the new world order's labour-displacement substrate is being built. **Elon Reeve Musk** is, in his personal style and in many of his policy positions, the opposite of the cerebral and reclusive Thiel. He has been, since 2022, the public face of the political project Thiel has constructed in the shadows.


---

# Chapter 12: Elon Musk

The previous chapter described Peter Thiel as the regent — the figure whose biography most directly explains the political project the new world order is executing. This section is about the figure whose biography most directly explains its public face. **Elon Reeve Musk** is, in 2026, the wealthiest individual in human history. Forbes estimates his personal net worth at $852 billion. He has, since 2022, conducted himself as the public protagonist of the political-technological project Thiel has constructed in private. He has owned the principal real-time information system of the English-speaking political world (X, formerly Twitter). He has operated the principal satellite-internet constellation (Starlink, 7,000 satellites in low Earth orbit, 60 percent of all active satellites). He has built the principal private space-launch capability (SpaceX). He has constructed the largest single concentration of artificial-intelligence compute on earth (xAI's Colossus data centre, 100,000 H100 GPUs operational by December 2024, with a planned expansion to one million). He has served, from January to May 2025, as the principal executor of the Trump administration's restructuring of the American federal government (DOGE). He has, in a single biography, integrated more of the active components of the new world order than any other living individual.

The captured-press framing of Musk presents him as an idiosyncratic genius — a charismatic engineer-entrepreneur whose extraordinary individual abilities explain his extraordinary individual outcomes. The documentary record does not support this framing. The Musk biography is the biography of a man whose family wealth derived from apartheid-era southern African mineral extraction, whose maternal grandfather was the Canadian leader of a banned anti-democratic political movement that emigrated to South Africa in 1950 specifically because the post-1948 apartheid regime aligned with his racial politics, whose paternal record includes an engineering business that constructed infrastructure for the apartheid military, whose principal corporate vehicles have been founded by other people and acquired by Musk after the fact (Tesla by Eberhard and Tarpenning; the X.com / Confinity merger), whose business successes have depended at every crisis point on US government contracts awarded in the final weeks of presidential administrations (the December 2008 NASA contract that saved both SpaceX and Tesla), and whose current political position is the joint product of personal capital, accumulated network capital, and the explicit operational alignment with the Thiel network the previous chapter described. The analysis applies the same standards to Musk that Chapter 11 applied to Thiel: family and ancestral context, education and formation, the founding fictions, the corporate trajectory, the ideology in his own words, the political activity, the board members and structural partners, the current position. There is no softening for the figure the press has spent two decades describing as a technical visionary. The documentary record is the documentary record.

## I. Origins: Pretoria, Saskatchewan, the Haldeman line

Elon Reeve Musk was born on 28 June 1971 in Pretoria, capital of the Transvaal province of the Union of South Africa, in the twenty-third year of the formal apartheid regime established by the 1948 election of the National Party. He was the eldest child of **Errol Graham Musk** (born 25 May 1946, Pretoria) and **Maye Musk** née Haldeman (born 19 April 1948, Saskatchewan, Canada). His siblings were Kimbal James Musk (born 20 September 1972) and Tosca Musk (born 1974).

The paternal line is South African of English descent. Errol's father Walter Henry James Musk was a South African World War II veteran; his mother Cora Amelia Robinson was an Englishwoman from Liverpool. Errol studied electromechanical engineering at the University of Pretoria — the principal Afrikaner-language university and the institutional centre of the apartheid intellectual establishment — and built a major engineering consulting and property-development business in Pretoria from the late 1960s through the 1980s. His business undertook, in his own subsequent characterisation to biographer Walter Isaacson, "large projects such as office buildings, retail complexes, residential subdivisions, and an air force base." The air force base is the salient detail. Errol Musk's engineering business worked on infrastructure for the **South African Air Force**, the air force of the apartheid state, during the period of the apartheid regime's domestic security operations and its destabilisation of the southern African region (the war in Angola, the support of RENAMO in Mozambique, the cross-border raids into Zambia, Botswana, Lesotho, and Zimbabwe).

He also owned, at peak, "at least half a share in an emerald mine" and, in 1986, acquired rights to the output of three Zambian emerald mines near Lake Tanganyika. Errol's verbatim explanation, in his interviews with Walter Isaacson for the 2023 biography, of why the mining operation was not formally registered: "**If you registered it, you would wind up with nothing, because the Blacks would take everything from you.**" The quotation is in the biographer's record, in Errol's own words, and has not been retracted. It is a clean statement of the white-supremacist worldview that produced both the apartheid regime and the regional extraction economy that operated under and around it. The phrase is the operational definition of the world Elon Musk's father inhabited and from which his family wealth derived.

By the time of Errol and Maye's 1979 divorce, Errol owned (per Maye's own memoir) "two homes, a yacht, a plane, five luxury cars, and a truck." Maye's memoir characterised the marriage as abusive and described an incident in which Errol appeared at a neighbour's house with a knife looking for her during the divorce proceedings. Elon and his brother Kimbal subsequently moved to live primarily with their father from around 1980, when Elon was ten — a decision Elon has, in adult interviews, described as one he regrets. In a 2017 *Rolling Stone* profile, Elon called his father "a terrible human being" and "almost every evil thing you could think of, he has done."

The pattern of the Errol Musk biography continued past Elon's departure from South Africa. In the early 1990s, Errol (then 45) married **Heide Bezuidenhout**, a 25-year-old he described in subsequent interviews as "one of the best looking women I've ever seen in my life." Heide had a four-year-old daughter from a previous relationship, **Jana Bezuidenhout**. Errol thereby became Jana's stepfather when she was four. He subsequently entered into a romantic and sexual relationship with his stepdaughter when she was an adult, and **fathered two children with his own stepdaughter**. The relationship is documented in multiple interviews Errol has himself given to the *Sunday Times* of London and to South African press over the past decade. Elon has publicly distanced himself from his father; Errol has continued to give interviews discussing the family's private affairs.

In November 2025, Errol gave a CNN interview with reporter Donie O'Sullivan for a documentary on the supposed "white genocide" narrative that has, since February 2025, become a Trump-administration foreign-policy talking point. In the interview, Errol stated that the United States is "doomed" because it will have a "majority non-white population" within twenty years. Asked what that would mean, he said: "You want to go back to the jungle?" He denied that black South Africans had been oppressed under apartheid and credited the white minority with "feeding" the black population and giving them work. The interview is publicly available; the quotations are verbatim from the broadcast and the transcript.

The maternal line is more politically consequential than the paternal one. **Joshua Norman Haldeman**, Elon's maternal grandfather (1902-1974), was American-born and trained as a chiropractor in Iowa. He emigrated to Canada in the 1920s and built a chiropractic practice in Saskatchewan. In the 1930s and 1940s he became politically active. He was the **national chairman of Technocracy Incorporated in Canada** — the Canadian branch of the Technocracy Movement, an anti-democratic political movement that proposed replacing democratic governments with rule by engineers and technical experts according to "scientific" management principles. The Canadian Technocracy Movement was **outlawed under the War Measures Act in 1940** as a subversive organisation; Haldeman was imprisoned for several months for his role in continuing its activity. After the war he ran for the Canadian Parliament on the **Social Credit Party** ticket — a populist movement whose founder William Aberhart had been openly antisemitic and which Haldeman aligned with on its anti-banking and anti-Jewish-finance positions.

In **1950 — two years after the National Party's election in South Africa and the formal establishment of apartheid — Haldeman emigrated with his family to South Africa**. The emigration was explicitly political. Haldeman had concluded, in his published writings of the late 1940s, that South Africa under the new National Party government represented a model of "proper" racial and political organisation. He spent the next twenty-four years in South Africa, where he flew his private aircraft across the southern African region, searched for the mythical "Lost City of the Kalahari," and continued to publish on political topics. His published positions across his lifetime included: opposition to democratic government as such; advocacy for racial segregation as a permanent civilisational principle; antisemitic conspiracy theories about "international Jewry" controlling banking and politics; opposition to fluoridation, vaccination, and other public-health measures as elements of an antidemocratic globalist conspiracy. He died in 1974, when Elon was three.

**Winnifred "Wyn" Fletcher Haldeman** (1914-2012), Elon's maternal grandmother, survived her husband by thirty-eight years and was, by Elon's own account, an important figure in his childhood. Per **Errol Musk's own statement** to biographers — and this is the relevant attribution, since Errol is not a hostile source on his ex-wife's parents — Wyn and Joshua Haldeman were **"fanatical" in favour of apartheid, and supported Nazism**. The statement is in Errol's own words, in the public record, and has not been retracted. Wyn told Elon many stories of his grandfather's exploits in his childhood, including slideshows of Joshua's South African and African travels. The grandparents who shaped Elon's formative imaginative world were, on the testimony of his own father, fanatical supporters of apartheid and Nazi sympathisers.

The standard captured-press framing of the Musk family origins is that Elon "grew up under apartheid" in a context-free generality. The documentary record establishes much more: that the paternal-line wealth was directly tied to apartheid-era infrastructure construction and regional mineral extraction, that the paternal grandfather and great-grandfather actively benefited from the apartheid system, that the maternal grandfather emigrated to South Africa specifically because of apartheid, that the maternal grandmother was publicly documented (per Elon's own father) a fanatical pro-apartheid Nazi sympathiser, and that the family conversations that formed Elon's childhood imagination featured the maternal grandfather's African adventures as their content. The biography is not coincidental. The ideological formation is consistent with the family circumstances in which it occurred.

## II. Bryanston, Pretoria, and the 1989 departure

Elon attended Waterkloof House Preparatory School in his early years, then Bryanston High School in the Johannesburg area, then Pretoria Boys' High School for his final years. The South African school system of the 1980s was, by law, racially segregated for white pupils; Elon's schools were all-white, English-medium, fee-paying institutions. The schools were attended by the children of the white professional and business class — the population that ran the apartheid state's economy.

Elon has, in adult interviews, described being severely bullied as a child, including an incident in which he was hospitalised after being thrown down a flight of stairs. The bullying narrative has been a central component of the personal-biography branding that the press has used to present him as a sympathetic outsider rather than as a member of the privileged class. The documented record does not contradict the bullying; it does establish that the bullying occurred in the context of the privileged-class school system that produced both the bullies and the bullied as members of the same racial and economic stratum.

In 1989, aged seventeen, Elon emigrated to Canada using his mother's Canadian citizenship. The proximate reason has been documented variously in his own subsequent accounts: to access better economic opportunity (his framing in some interviews), to escape his father (his framing in others), and **to avoid mandatory South African Defence Force conscription** (his framing in still others — the conscription, which he would have had to begin shortly after his eighteenth birthday, would have meant two years' service in the apartheid military, which by 1989-1990 was deployed in the township security operations and the cross-border raids that defined the regime's final years). The avoidance of apartheid military service is the most defensible element of the Musk family biography for the period; it does not, however, imply opposition to the regime more broadly. Many white South African families in 1989 understood that the apartheid system was approaching collapse and made provision for their children's emigration as economic insurance. The Musk family was such a family.

Elon spent two years (1990-1992) at Queen's University in Kingston, Ontario, before transferring to the **University of Pennsylvania**, where he completed dual bachelor's degrees in physics (College of Arts and Sciences) and economics (Wharton School). He graduated in 1995. He enrolled in a PhD programme in materials science and applied physics at **Stanford University** in August 1995 and **withdrew after two days** to pursue the company that would become Zip2.

## III. The founding fictions: Zip2, X.com, and the Tesla settlement

Three of the four principal corporate vehicles of Elon Musk's wealth were not founded by him in the sense the term is ordinarily used. Zip2 was a joint venture with his brother Kimbal. X.com was an Elon Musk founding but merged into Confinity (Thiel and Levchin's pre-existing company) and was later renamed PayPal — Musk's tenure as CEO of the merged company lasted six months before the board removed him. Tesla was founded by Martin Eberhard and Marc Tarpenning in July 2003; Musk became Chairman in February 2004 as a Series A investor and began publicly describing himself as a "founder" only after he pushed Eberhard out in late 2007. Only SpaceX is Musk's own foundation in the unambiguous sense.

**Zip2** was founded in December 1995 by Elon and Kimbal Musk with initial investor Greg Kouri as "Global Link Information Network," providing online city guides to American newspapers. The initial funding included $28,000 from **Errol Musk** — by Elon's own 2019 statement on X, "10% of a ~$200k angel funding round." The acknowledgement is significant because Musk has, at various other points, denied any meaningful paternal financial contribution to his early companies. The documented record is that Errol Musk's apartheid-era emerald-and-engineering wealth provided seed capital, in proportions Elon has subsequently minimised, for the first Musk corporate vehicle.

In 1996, Mohr Davidow Ventures invested $3 million in Zip2, displacing Elon as CEO and installing him as CTO. The displacement was, by all accounts, deeply frustrating to him and has been retrospectively presented in his biographies as the formative experience that taught him to retain controlling positions in subsequent ventures. In February 1999, Compaq acquired Zip2 for **$307 million in cash**. Elon received $22 million gross; Kimbal received $15 million.

**X.com** was founded in March 1999 with $12 million of Elon's Zip2 proceeds, along with co-founders Harris Fricker, Christopher Payne, and Ed Ho. X.com was an online bank — one of the early entrants into what would become online financial services. In March 2000, X.com merged with **Confinity** (Peter Thiel and Max Levchin's company, which had been developing PayPal as a cryptography and payments service). Elon became CEO of the combined entity, initially branded under the X.com name.

In **September 2000**, while Elon was on a delayed honeymoon flight to Australia with his first wife Justine Wilson, **the board (led by Thiel and Levchin) voted to remove him as CEO**. Thiel was installed in his place. The company was renamed PayPal. Musk remained the largest individual shareholder and a board member but was not the working executive. In October 2002, eBay acquired PayPal for $1.5 billion. Musk received **$175 million gross** — the capital that would fund the next phase of his career. The September 2000 ouster has been a documented source of long-running tension between Musk and Thiel; the two have, however, continued to collaborate on shared political and business projects through the Founders Fund-Gigafund structure that links Thiel's network to Musk's companies, the joint funding of Vance, and the coordination in the second Trump administration.

The most consequential founding fiction is **Tesla**. The captured-press framing of Tesla, repeated in every mainstream profile of Musk over the past twenty years, presents him as the founder of the company that revolutionised electric vehicles. The documentary record establishes much less.

**Tesla Motors was incorporated on 1 July 2003 in San Carlos, California, by Martin Eberhard and Marc Tarpenning.** Eberhard and Tarpenning had previously co-founded NuvoMedia (the company that built the Rocket eBook, the first secure internet-delivered e-book reader, in 1998). They financed the first year of Tesla themselves, then sought venture capital. **In February 2004 — eight months after Tesla's incorporation — Elon Musk led the company's Series A funding round**, contributing $6.5 million of the $7.5 million round and becoming Chairman of the Board. JB Straubel joined in May 2004 as the fifth employee.

For the next three years, Eberhard was CEO. The Tesla Roadster was developed under his leadership. In November 2007, **Musk, as Chairman, pushed Eberhard out** as CEO. The Fortune December 2007 reporting documented that "chairman Elon Musk had asked Eberhard to leave." Tarpenning departed in 2008. Musk became CEO. He began publicly describing himself as a "founder" of the company.

In **June 2009**, Eberhard sued Musk for libel, slander, and breach of contract, alleging that Musk had pushed him out and was publicly disparaging him. The complaint specifically objected to Musk's "founder" representations. In **September 2009 the case was settled**. The terms of the settlement, as subsequently confirmed by NBC Bay Area's reporting and by Eberhard himself in a 2021 CNBC interview, granted **Musk, JB Straubel, and Ian Wright the right to call themselves "co-founders"** of Tesla, in addition to Eberhard and Tarpenning. Eberhard was bound by an NDA preventing him from directly discussing the ouster.

**Elon Musk's status as a "co-founder" of Tesla is, in legal substance, a settlement term in a defamation lawsuit, not a historical fact.** The settlement converted a chairman who had become CEO after an internal management dispute into a person legally entitled to call himself a co-founder of a company that had been founded by other people two years before he had any involvement with it. The biographical record was, by the terms of an NDA-bound settlement, retroactively rewritten. The captured-press accounts of Tesla's history have, with limited exceptions, accepted the post-settlement framing. The actual founding date of Tesla is July 2003. The actual founders are Eberhard and Tarpenning. The actual first three Tesla employees included Straubel and Wright. Musk was the Series A investor who became Chairman and subsequently took over.

## IV. SpaceX: the one genuine founding, and the contract that saved everything

**Space Exploration Technologies Corporation** is the corporate vehicle that Musk did, in the unambiguous sense, found. He incorporated SpaceX in May 2002 in El Segundo, California, with $100 million of his PayPal-sale proceeds. He was sole founder; the early employees joined as employees, not as co-founders.

The company's stated purpose was to develop low-cost reusable rockets capable of human space flight. The technical execution between 2002 and 2008 was, by the standards of the rocketry industry, extraordinarily ambitious — and nearly fatal. The Falcon 1 launches of March 2006, March 2007, and August 2008 all failed. By August 2008 SpaceX had $30 million remaining of Musk's $100 million original capital, three launch failures on its record, and no commercial customers committed beyond contingent orders subject to successful launches. Tesla was simultaneously near collapse in the 2008 financial crisis. Both companies were, by Musk's own subsequent characterisations, weeks from bankruptcy.

The salvation came in two stages. The first stage was the **28 September 2008** successful fourth Falcon 1 launch — the technical demonstration that the company could deliver a payload to orbit. The second stage, and the more important, was the **23 December 2008** announcement that NASA had awarded SpaceX a **$1.6 billion Commercial Resupply Services contract** for cargo transport to the International Space Station. The contract was awarded by NASA Administrator Mike Griffin in the final weeks of the George W. Bush administration. Without it, SpaceX would have been bankrupt within months and would have taken Tesla with it (because Musk's personal liquidity depended on SpaceX's survival).

The 2008 NASA contract is, in retrospect, the single most consequential decision in the documented history of the American space industry. It moved $1.6 billion of public capital into a private company that had, on its operating record, demonstrated one successful rocket launch out of four attempts. The contract was the foundation of every subsequent SpaceX commercial success — the Crew Dragon astronaut-transport contract ($2.6 billion, 2014); the Lunar Starship contract ($2.9 billion, 2021, increased to $4.2 billion in 2024); the various intermediate launch contracts; and, most recently, the Starshield military variant of Starlink ($1.8 billion classified contract with the National Reconnaissance Office, disclosed in 2024). By 2024, total US government contracts to SpaceX exceeded **$22 billion**, per *Washington Post* analysis. The contracts continue.

The two operational programmes that have, since 2019, transformed SpaceX from a launch contractor into a strategic geopolitical actor are **Starlink** and **Starshield**.

**Starlink** is the satellite-internet constellation that SpaceX has deployed in low Earth orbit since 2019. By mid-2025, 7,000 active Starlink satellites are in orbit — the largest satellite constellation in human history, comprising 60 percent of all active satellites worldwide. The constellation provides commercial internet service to consumers in 100 countries. It also provides operational connectivity to military and government users, most consequentially the Ukrainian armed forces in the Russo-Ukrainian war.

The Starlink-Ukraine operational record is the documented case in which a private satellite-internet system has been used to determine the outcome of national-level military operations at the discretion of a private individual. In **September 2022**, Musk personally restricted Ukrainian use of Starlink to prevent a Ukrainian naval-drone attack on the Russian Black Sea Fleet in occupied Crimea. The restriction was disclosed by Musk biographer Walter Isaacson in September 2023; the details were subsequently reported by CNN, the *New York Times*, and the *Washington Post*. In Musk's own account, he restricted the service after a private conversation with the Russian ambassador in which he was told that a Ukrainian strike on the Crimean Bridge could trigger a Russian nuclear response. The factual basis of the threat assessment was, at minimum, contested; the consequence was that a private American citizen made a personal decision to restrict a NATO ally's military operations against a NATO adversary during an active war, on the basis of a private conversation with a representative of the adversary government. The decision was not made through any constitutional or treaty mechanism. It was made by Elon Musk.

**Starshield**, disclosed in 2024, is the military variant of Starlink that SpaceX provides to the US national-security community under classified contracts. The principal customer is the National Reconnaissance Office; the disclosed contract value is $1.8 billion. The Starshield programme provides, in effect, a private-sector parallel to the US government's national-reconnaissance satellite infrastructure — a parallel that is owned by Musk personally through his SpaceX shareholding.

## V. The other companies, briefly

**Neuralink** (founded 2016, with seven other co-founders) is the brain-computer-interface company. First human implant January 2024 (patient Noland Arbaugh). Multiple subsequent implants through 2025. The animal-trial period prior to FDA approval was the subject of multiple Reuters investigations documenting unusually high mortality among test animals and internal complaints about premature transition to human trials. The company is, in 2026, on the FDA's regulatory radar; its long-term viability depends on whether the medical-device safety record can be established in a population of human implant recipients large enough to be statistically meaningful.

**The Boring Company** (founded 2016) is the tunnel-construction venture. Its only fully operational deployment is the Las Vegas Convention Center loop. The originally proposed Hyperloop projects have not materialised. The company's valuation has declined from a 2022 peak of $5.7 billion to a current estimate of $1 billion.

**xAI** (founded April 2023) is the artificial-intelligence company. Funding rounds in 2024 totalled $17 billion across three tranches, with participants including Andreessen Horowitz, Sequoia, the Saudi Public Investment Fund, Qatar Investment Authority, and others. The principal asset is the **Colossus** data centre in Memphis, Tennessee — by December 2024, 100,000 Nvidia H100 GPUs operational, with a planned expansion to one million. Colossus is, on its own, the largest single concentration of AI compute on earth. The principal product is **Grok**, the large-language-model assistant integrated into X. In March 2025, **xAI acquired X Corp** (the entity Musk had created from the 2022 Twitter purchase) in an all-stock transaction valuing X at $33 billion and xAI at $80 billion, for a combined entity valued at $113 billion. The merger consolidated Musk's AI and social-media holdings under a single corporate structure and much reduced his personal exposure to the original Twitter debt.

**X Corp** (formerly Twitter) is the company Musk purchased in October 2022 for $44 billion. The acquisition is documented in detail below.

## VI. The Twitter purchase

In April 2022, Musk announced an offer to acquire Twitter for $54.20 per share — a price chosen, on the public record of Musk's own statements, partly for its incorporation of the "420" cannabis-culture meme. The total purchase price was $44 billion. The offer was initially refused by Twitter's board; Musk then announced he was withdrawing the offer; Twitter sued in Delaware Chancery Court to enforce the original agreement; the court was scheduled to rule against Musk; Musk completed the purchase under court pressure on **27 October 2022**.

The financing structure of the Twitter purchase is itself a documented part of the case the book is making about the integration of the Musk operation with the broader new-world-order capital structure. The purchase was financed by:

- **$13 billion in bank debt**, syndicated by Morgan Stanley, Bank of America, Barclays, MUFG, Société Générale, Mizuho, and BNP Paribas. The debt has subsequently been impossible to syndicate at par to ordinary debt buyers; the banks are sitting on large unrealised losses against the debt's face value (estimates as of mid-2025 suggest the debt trades at 70-80 cents on the dollar). The banks' exposure has been a documented constraint on their other lending activities.
- **$33.5 billion in equity**, comprising Musk's personal contribution ($25 billion, much of it sourced from Tesla margin loans) and a co-investor group including:
  - The **Saudi Public Investment Fund** through Prince Alwaleed bin Talal, who rolled over his pre-existing 5 percent Twitter stake (worth $1.9 billion) — making Saudi sovereign capital among the largest single investors in the post-Musk X.
  - **Larry Ellison** (Oracle Chairman), who personally invested $1 billion.
  - **Sequoia Capital** ($800 million).
  - **Andreessen Horowitz** ($400 million).
  - **Binance** ($500 million).
  - **Qatar Holding** ($375 million).
  - **VyCapital** (a UAE-based fund, $700 million).

The Twitter purchase, in capital-structure terms, integrated Musk's personal control of the principal real-time English-language information system with major Gulf sovereign-wealth participation, the principal Silicon Valley right-wing venture-capital firms, and the world's largest cryptocurrency exchange (Binance, whose founder CZ subsequently pled guilty to US federal anti-money-laundering violations in November 2023). The investor list is the active map of the new-world-order coalition that the previous chapter described.

Within weeks of the acquisition, Musk laid off 6,000 of Twitter's 8,000 employees — 75 percent of the workforce. He dissolved the Trust and Safety Council. He reinstated previously banned accounts including Donald Trump. He replaced the verified-blue-check system with a paid subscription model that removed the principal authentication mechanism for journalists, public officials, and other verified-identity users. He relaxed content moderation across the platform, with documented effects on the prevalence of harassment, hate speech, and coordinated disinformation campaigns (multiple peer-reviewed studies, 2023-2025, have measured the increases). The platform's advertising revenue dropped by 50 percent in the year following the purchase as major advertisers paused spending.

## VII. The 2024 political turn and the $290 million

Through 2018, Musk had presented himself politically as a centrist-libertarian who voted Democratic in presidential elections (he has stated in various interviews that he voted for Hillary Clinton in 2016 and for Joe Biden in 2020). Through 2022 he claimed to be politically independent. From late 2022 onward — coinciding with the Twitter purchase — his political activity shifted decisively rightward.

The 2024 cycle was the working turning point. Musk:

- **Endorsed Donald Trump** for the 2024 Republican nomination on 13 July 2024, hours after the assassination attempt on Trump in Butler, Pennsylvania.
- **Funded America PAC** with a documented **$291 million in personal contributions** across the 2024 cycle — the largest single-donor expenditure in any American election in history.
- **Held campaign rallies for Trump** in Pennsylvania and other swing states in the final weeks of the campaign, including the documented offer of $1 million daily prizes to randomly selected swing-state voters who signed his America PAC's petition (an arrangement that prompted Department of Justice review for potential federal election-law violation; no charges were filed before the Trump-administration DOJ closed the matter in February 2025).
- **Used X / Twitter** to amplify pro-Trump content systematically. Multiple peer-reviewed analyses (Stanford Internet Observatory, NYU CSMaP, MIT Election Lab) have documented the algorithmic preferentiation of Musk's own posts and of right-wing content generally on the platform during the 2024 cycle.

After Trump's election on 5 November 2024 and inauguration on 20 January 2025, Musk's role transitioned from campaign donor to operational executive — the **Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE)** position discussed in Section VIII below.

## VIII. DOGE: January–May 2025

On **20 January 2025 — inauguration day — Trump signed an executive order** creating the Department of Government Efficiency, with Elon Musk designated as a "Special Government Employee" (SGE) leading the effort. The SGE designation is a statutory category that permits temporary executive-branch employment for up to 130 days per twelve-month period without requiring Senate confirmation or comprehensive financial-disclosure compliance. Musk's appointment under this category was the explicit legal mechanism by which the largest single private contractor to the federal government (through SpaceX, Tesla, and Starshield) was placed in operational control of a programme to restructure federal agency operations — an arrangement that, by ordinary conflict-of-interest standards, would have been disqualifying. The SGE category was specifically chosen because it bypassed those standards.

DOGE's stated mandate, in Musk's original public communications, was to **reduce federal spending by $2 trillion**. The target was subsequently revised downward — to $1 trillion in early February 2025, then to "$150-200 billion in confirmed savings" by April 2025, then to a final claimed total that has been disputed by multiple independent analyses showing the actual savings (net of additional costs incurred) to be much smaller or negative.

DOGE's documented operational actions, January-May 2025:

- **Roughly 220,000 federal positions** eliminated or restructured through Reductions in Force (RIFs), deferred resignation programmes, terminations, and related mechanisms. The figure represents 9 percent of the federal civilian workforce.
- **USAID** dismantled. The agency's $40 billion annual aid programme was either cancelled, frozen, or transferred to State Department control with reduced effective spending. The dismantling proceeded despite multiple federal court orders requiring continuation of specific programmes; the administration argued executive authority over USAID's operational structure.
- **Department of Education** functions transferred to other agencies, with the announced eventual goal of eliminating the Department.
- **Consumer Financial Protection Bureau** operationally crippled through funding freezes and staffing reductions.
- **Office of Personnel Management (OPM)** data integration with Palantir architecture. The cross-agency federal employee database, payroll data, and personnel security data were consolidated on a Palantir-hosted infrastructure under the March 2025 executive order on inter-agency data sharing. The consolidation was, by some independent analyses, the most consequential operational achievement of DOGE — it provided the technical foundation for subsequent administration policy actions including the federal-workforce loyalty assessments that followed the May 2025 executive order on civil service reform.
- **Treasury payment system access**. DOGE personnel were granted read access to the Treasury Bureau of the Fiscal Service (BFS) payment system that processes $5 trillion in federal disbursements annually. The access was the subject of multiple federal lawsuits (filed by state attorneys general and federal-employee unions) in February-March 2025, several of which produced injunctions limiting the access. The injunctions were partially overturned on appeal in April 2025.

DOGE personnel were drawn predominantly from young engineers at Musk's companies — Tesla, SpaceX, X, xAI, Neuralink, the Boring Company. The most-reported figures included **Steve Davis** (president of the Boring Company and Musk's longtime operational lieutenant), Brad Smith, Jordan Wick, Akash Bobba, Edward Coristine, Luke Farritor, Gautier Cole Killian, and fifty other engineers granted federal credentials via the SGE / DOGE structure. Several of the DOGE staff were under 25 years old; several had no prior federal-government experience; at least two had documented histories of online activity (including pseudonymous racist posting) that, under normal federal hiring procedures, would have produced security-clearance disqualifications. The DOGE personnel were placed in operational control of access to federal payment systems, personnel data, and inter-agency communications.

On **28 May 2025**, Musk formally departed DOGE after his statutory 130-day SGE maximum expired. The DOGE programme has continued under other personnel; Musk's personal involvement formally ended.

The DOGE period is significant for the book's argument for two reasons. First, it was the operational implementation of the Yarvin neoreactionary doctrine — the "hard reboot" of the federal government, the institutional liquidation of the civil service, the corporate-restructuring framework applied to the state apparatus — that the previous chapter documented as the Thiel network's intellectual programme. The implementation agent was Musk; the intellectual framework was Yarvin's. Second, it placed in federal-data and federal-payment-system access a young, screened-by-loyalty rather than by qualification cadre of engineers from Musk's own companies, who exited federal employment (where the action could be subject to FOIA, oversight, and review) with their knowledge of the systems intact and with the structural relationships they had built across the federal workforce. The DOGE alumni network is, in 2026, an additional layer of the Musk-network political-operational apparatus.

## IX. The break with Trump

In **late May and early June 2025**, the working relationship between Musk and Trump broke down publicly. The proximate cause was Musk's opposition to Trump's "One Big Beautiful Bill" reconciliation legislation, which Musk criticised on X on 3 June 2025 as a "disgusting abomination" that violated Trump's stated commitments to fiscal discipline.

The dispute escalated rapidly. On **5 June 2025**, Musk posted on X that Trump appeared in the "Epstein files" (the documents in the federal government's possession relating to Jeffrey Epstein's network of contacts and victims), asserting that this was the "real reason" the files had not been fully released. Trump responded by threatening on Truth Social to terminate Musk's federal contracts — a threat that, given SpaceX's $22 billion in cumulative federal contracts and the dependency of NASA, the National Reconnaissance Office, and the Department of Defense on SpaceX capabilities, was understood by both parties to be unenforceable as a near-term action.

The break was patched over the subsequent weeks but the personal relationship has not been restored. In **July 2025, Musk launched the America Party** as an electoral vehicle independent of both major parties. The party is in 2026 still in its organisational phase; it has not yet contested elections. Musk has stated his intention to use the America Party to support candidates in 2026 House and Senate races who align with the post-DOGE policy programme — that is, with the active doctrine he implemented during his five months as SGE.

The Trump-Musk falling-out is significant principally because it demonstrates the structural fact that the new world order is not a unified executive command structure. It is a coalition of overlapping but distinct power centres, each with its own interests and its own operational capacity, that have aligned for the specific political project of the 2024-2028 period. The personal animosities and tactical disagreements between Musk and Trump, between Musk and Thiel (going back to September 2000), between Vance and Musk (since the June 2025 break), are real and operational. They do not, however, threaten the policy outcomes — the AI capital deployment, the labour-displacement programme, the Palantir-federal-data integration, the surveillance-state expansion, the Gulf-sovereign-wealth investment flows — that the coalition is jointly executing. The coalition can have internal disputes about the distribution of spoils and the credit for outcomes without those disputes affecting the outcomes themselves. The arrangement is the standard arrangement of oligarchic coalitions throughout the historical record the book has been documenting.

## X. The SpaceX board and the Thiel-Musk bridge

SpaceX, as a private company, has not been required to disclose its board composition in the public detail that public companies must. The currently known board, per various disclosures and press reporting through 2025, includes:

- **Elon Musk** — CEO, Chief Engineer, and Chairman of the Board.
- **Gwynne Shotwell** — President and Chief Operating Officer since 2008. Engineering background (Northwestern, Aerospace Corporation). Shotwell is, by all operational accounts, the executive who actually runs SpaceX's day-to-day operations — Musk's role is more strategic and external. She has been the principal interface between SpaceX and the federal-government customer base, including the negotiations of the 2008 NASA contract, the subsequent NASA contracts, and the Department of Defense contracts. Shotwell's personal share of SpaceX is undisclosed; her wealth at the company's late-2024 valuation of $350 billion is estimated at $2-3 billion.
- **Antonio Gracias** — Founder of Valor Equity Partners, a Chicago-based private equity firm. Long-time Musk ally; was on the Tesla board 2007-2021 (left after the SEC settlement that removed Musk as Tesla Chairman). Reportedly part of the DOGE inner circle in 2025. Valor Equity holds major SpaceX positions through multiple funds.
- **Steve Jurvetson** — Founding Partner of Future Ventures (formerly DFJ — Draper Fisher Jurvetson). Early Tesla and SpaceX investor through DFJ in the mid-2000s. The Draper family (Tim Draper, William Henry Draper III, William Henry Draper Jr.) is one of the genealogically deepest American venture-capital lineages — Bill Draper Jr. was a founder of the firm Sutter Hill in the 1950s — and the Draper-Jurvetson relationship to Musk is one of the early-investor relationships that has continued for two decades.
- **Luke Nosek** — PayPal co-founder, Founders Fund co-founder, now Gigafund principal. **This is the structural link between SpaceX and the Thiel network.** Nosek left Founders Fund in 2017 specifically to found Gigafund with Stephen Oskoui, with the principal stated purpose of maintaining the Thiel-network capital position in SpaceX, Neuralink, and the Boring Company at a time when the Thiel-Musk personal relationship had cooled. Gigafund's positions in the Musk portfolio are the documentation of the proposition that the Thiel network has continued to participate in Musk's companies despite the personal animosity dating to September 2000.
- **Donald Harrison** — Google executive. Adds the Google-network bridge.

The SpaceX board is, a closed circle of six individuals who have known each other and worked together for two decades. There is no public-market shareholder voting structure. There is no SEC-disclosure requirement for the board's compensation, decision-making, or governance processes. SpaceX's $350 billion valuation (as of late 2024 secondary-market transactions) is, in this sense, the largest privately held company in the world by valuation — and one of the most opaque in its governance.

## XI. The Tesla board and the Murdoch bridge

Tesla, as a public company, has been required to disclose its board composition. The current board per the 2025 proxy statement:

- **Robyn Denholm** — Chair since November 2018. Australian, formerly CFO of Telstra. Denholm was installed as Chair after the August 2018 SEC settlement that removed Musk as Tesla Chairman (Musk paid $20 million personally; Tesla paid $20 million additionally). Denholm has, during her chair tenure, sold $700 million of personal Tesla stock — a documented pattern of executive sales that has been the subject of multiple shareholder-derivative lawsuits.
- **Elon Musk** — CEO and director.
- **Kimbal Musk** — Elon's brother. Restaurant entrepreneur (The Kitchen Restaurant Group; Big Green nonprofit). On the Tesla board since 2004 — the longest non-Musk continuous service. Also serves on the SpaceX board. Major Tesla shareholder.
- **James Murdoch** — Son of Rupert Murdoch, former CEO of 21st Century Fox, current principal of Lupa Systems. Joined the Tesla board in 2017. James Murdoch is, in his own political activity, more liberal than the rest of his family — he has been a major Democratic donor since 2019 — but his presence on the Tesla board is structurally significant for what it represents: the working integration of the Tesla / Musk financial vehicle with the Murdoch family media empire. The Murdoch empire (Fox News, the Wall Street Journal, the New York Post) has been, since 2015, the principal media-coalition partner of the political project the previous chapter described. The Tesla-Murdoch board link is one of the structural bridges between the technology layer (Musk) and the media layer (Murdoch).
- **JB Straubel** — Tesla "co-founder" per the 2009 settlement. CEO of Redwood Materials (battery recycling). Rejoined the Tesla board in 2023.
- **Ira Ehrenpreis** — Managing partner of DBL Partners, a venture capital firm with major Tesla and SolarCity positions. Has been a Musk-network investor since the mid-2000s.
- **Joe Gebbia** — Airbnb co-founder. Joined the Tesla board in 2022. Subsequently appointed by Trump in January 2025 to the federal Office of Personnel Management board — a documented DOGE-network appointment that placed an Airbnb-Tesla-network figure inside the federal-employee management apparatus that DOGE was simultaneously restructuring.
- **Kathleen Wilson-Thompson** — Former Walgreens executive.

The Tesla board exhibits the standard captured-board characteristics the previous chapter documented at Palantir: a Chair with limited operational independence from the controlling shareholder, a family member of the controlling shareholder, the controlling shareholder himself, a network of long-time co-investors, and a small number of nominally independent directors whose biographies do not include the expertise to provide meaningful oversight. The voting structure is not as extreme as Palantir's Founder Voting Trust — Tesla does not have dual-class shares — but Musk's 13 percent personal stake, combined with the proxy-voting alignment of Kimbal, Straubel, the institutional shareholders, and the various Musk-network funds, has consistently produced governance outcomes favourable to Musk's preferences.

## XII. The xAI partners and the AI-network bridge

xAI is privately held; the board and executive structure are less fully disclosed than the public companies. The known senior personnel include:

- **Elon Musk** — CEO.
- **Igor Babuschkin** — Co-founder; formerly research scientist at Google DeepMind.
- **Toby Pohlen** — Co-founder; formerly Google DeepMind.
- **Christian Szegedy** — Co-founder; formerly Google Brain. Author of major foundational neural-network architecture papers.
- **Greg Yang** — Co-founder; mathematician, theoretical-deep-learning researcher, formerly Microsoft Research.
- **Tony Wu** — Co-founder; formerly Google.
- **Jimmy Ba** — Co-founder; deep-learning researcher, formerly University of Toronto and Google Brain (Adam optimiser co-author).
- Several other co-founders drawn from OpenAI, DeepMind, and Google Brain.

xAI's investor base, per the documented 2024 funding rounds, includes Andreessen Horowitz (a major commitment from the firm's growth funds), Sequoia, Saudi PIF, Qatar Investment Authority, Vy Capital, Fidelity, and others. The investor list is the same investor list as the Twitter purchase — the operational coalition of the new world order's AI-and-information infrastructure investments.

## XIII. The Errol Musk dimension and the structural pattern

The treatment of Elon Musk's family circumstances has been criticised, in advance and in similar instances elsewhere, on the ground that the actions of a person's parents should not be attributed to the person. The criticism is legitimate in principle and is accepted. The analysis does not argue that Elon Musk is responsible for his father's expressed views on race or for his maternal grandfather's pro-apartheid Nazi sympathies. He is not. He has, documented, distanced himself from his father (the 2017 "terrible human being" characterisation in *Rolling Stone* is the principal documentation) and he has not, in his own published statements, expressed pro-apartheid or pro-Nazi positions in their explicit form.

What the family record establishes is something distinct from personal responsibility for ancestors' views. It establishes the documented social-economic context from which the individual's wealth and ideological formation emerged. Elon Musk's family was not, in the apartheid-era southern African context, a marginal or dissenting family. It was a member of the white professional and business class that built the apartheid economy and benefited materially from it. The paternal-line wealth was the product of engineering contracts (including an air force base for the apartheid military) and regional mineral extraction (the Zambian emerald operations) conducted within and benefiting from the apartheid economic structure. The maternal line included a grandfather who emigrated to South Africa specifically because of apartheid and a grandmother who, on the testimony of the subject's own father, was a fanatical apartheid supporter and Nazi sympathiser whose stories shaped the subject's childhood imagination.

These facts do not, by themselves, predict the subject's adult political positions. They do, however, provide the social-economic context in which those positions developed. The subject is, in 2026, the wealthiest individual in human history. He has, in his adult political activity, deployed large portions of his wealth to support political movements (the second Trump administration, the America Party, the various right-wing X / Twitter content-amplification programmes, the documented financial support of European right-wing parties including Germany's AfD which Musk endorsed publicly in late 2024 and the UK Reform Party) whose core ideological commitments include the rollback of immigration, the restoration of explicit racial hierarchies in policy, and the major weakening of civil-rights protections that the post-apartheid and post-Civil-Rights generation has been the principal beneficiary of. The subject's adult political activity is, the continuation of the family's political-economic tradition in a different institutional environment.

No position is taken on the subject's psychological motivations. The trajectory is documented.

## XIV. The current position in 2026

This is what Elon Musk is, as of May 2026:

- The **wealthiest individual in human history**, with a personal net worth estimated by Forbes at $852 billion (February 2026). The wealth is concentrated in:
  - **Tesla** (~13 percent stake, valued at $200 billion based on the late-2025 share price).
  - **SpaceX** (~42 percent stake, valued at $200 billion based on the late-2024 $350 billion secondary-market valuation, with subsequent appreciation through 2025).
  - **xAI / X** (~50 percent post-merger stake, valued at $60 billion based on the March 2025 merger valuation, with subsequent funding-round revisions).
  - Neuralink, Boring Company, and various smaller positions.
- The **operator of the largest satellite constellation in human history** (Starlink, ~7,000 satellites, ~60 percent of all active satellites in orbit), and the principal contractor for the US military's classified satellite-internet programme (Starshield).
- The **owner of the principal real-time English-language information system** (X / Twitter), which he merged into xAI in March 2025 to create a single privately-controlled vehicle for AI development and information distribution.
- The **founder of the America Party** (July 2025), an electoral vehicle intended to support post-DOGE-doctrine candidates in the 2026 House and Senate cycles and beyond.
- A **former federal executive officer** (DOGE, January-May 2025) who, in his 130-day SGE tenure, eliminated or restructured 220,000 federal positions, dismantled USAID, consolidated federal personnel data on Palantir infrastructure, and trained a cadre of fifty young engineers in the active systems of the federal government, who are now alumni of the DOGE programme available for subsequent deployment.
- A **principal recipient of US government contracts**, with total cumulative SpaceX, Tesla, and Starshield federal-contract revenue exceeding $38 billion through fiscal year 2024 and expanding under the Trump-Vance administration.
- The **principal Silicon Valley political donor of the 2024 cycle**, with $291 million in personal contributions to America PAC and related vehicles — the largest single-donor expenditure in any American election in history.
- An **active political endorser of right-wing and far-right movements internationally**, including the German AfD (publicly endorsed late 2024), the UK Reform Party, and various other European movements that have been on the documented programme of building a transatlantic political alliance around immigration restriction, "anti-woke" cultural politics, and the rollback of post-1945 civil-rights frameworks.
- A **subject of multiple federal investigations**, several of which were closed by the second Trump administration after the election (SEC investigations into Twitter purchase disclosures, Department of Transportation investigations into Tesla Autopilot safety, FTC investigations into X data practices). The closures of investigations targeting the principal financial backer of the administration are themselves documented in the federal-register record and are a part of the working quid-pro-quo the 2024 cycle produced.

The biography is, in 2026, the form of the new world order's public face. The previous chapter described the regent. The record has described the executor — the figure through whose visible activity the regent's project is implemented in plain view. The two biographies are not interchangeable; they are complementary. Thiel built the network; Musk demonstrates its operation. The combination is what the system the previous chapters documented has produced as its leadership in the 2020s.




---

# Chapter 13: Sam Altman

**Samuel Harris Altman** wants to scan the irises of every human being on earth. That is not a paraphrase. His company **Worldcoin / Tools for Humanity**, co-founded with Alex Blania, proposes to assign each scanned individual a unique cryptographic identifier in a global biometric database. He is simultaneously, in 2026, the CEO of **OpenAI** — the company whose ChatGPT product, released in November 2022, introduced large-language-model artificial intelligence to the global population. He is the architect, with Masayoshi Son and Larry Ellison, of the **Stargate Project** — the $500-billion AI infrastructure programme announced as the centrepiece of the second Trump administration's first-day policy announcements. Through OpenAI's $500 billion October 2025 valuation, he runs the most valuable artificial-intelligence company in human history.

He is also the subject of documented public allegations of sexual abuse made by his own sister Annie Altman, including a federal lawsuit filed in January 2025 — allegations the press has declined to engage at the depth it engages with similar allegations against figures more politically convenient to expose. His company was the subject of the most-public corporate governance crisis in Silicon Valley's history (the November 2023 board ouster and five-day reinstatement). His former research collaborator and copyright-suit witness, Suchir Balaji, died in disputed circumstances in San Francisco in November 2024. His network of professional relationships runs through the Stanford-Y-Combinator-OpenAI-Microsoft pipeline that has supplied large fractions of the personnel driving the contemporary AI industry.

The press has treated Altman more sympathetically than it has treated Thiel or Musk — partly because of his more diplomatic public manner, partly because of his earlier Democratic-aligned political donations. The sympathy does not soften the standard here. The record is the record.

## I. Origins: St. Louis, the Gibstine line, and a family in dispute

Samuel Harris Altman was born on 22 April 1985 in Chicago, Illinois, and was raised in **St. Louis, Missouri**, in the affluent inner suburb of **Clayton**. He was the eldest of four children of **Jerry Altman** and **Connie Gibstine**.

**Jerry Altman** (deceased May 2018) was a real estate broker and lawyer in St. Louis. He worked principally in commercial real estate transactions in the St. Louis metropolitan area. He was, by the standards of the professional-class Jewish community in the inner-suburb-Clayton context, a successful but not extraordinary figure. The family was comfortable, not rich, in the period of Sam's childhood.

**Connie Gibstine** is a dermatologist; Stanford-trained; practising in St. Louis. She is, in the family's public-facing biography, the higher-earning parent through Sam's childhood and the figure who provided most of the household's professional stability after Jerry's death in 2018.

The family is Jewish; the children were raised in the Reform Jewish tradition of the St. Louis suburban community. The Altman household placed heavy emphasis on intellectual achievement; all four children attended elite private schools and pursued professional careers in technology and finance.

The siblings are themselves operational figures in the contemporary technology economy:

- **Jack Altman** (b. 1986) — entrepreneur and investor; founder and CEO of **Lattice**, an HR software company founded 2015 and valued at $3 billion. Jack is also an active early-stage investor; his portfolio includes numerous Silicon Valley startups, frequently in coordination with Sam's OpenAI Startup Fund positions.
- **Max Altman** (b. early 1990s) — founder of **Saga Ventures**, an early-stage venture capital firm. Max has also operated as an angel investor and is part of the Altman-network venture capital structure that has, since 2018, been one of the nodes of the Y Combinator alumni network.
- **Annie Altman** (b. 1994) — writer, musician, podcaster. The youngest of the four children; nine years younger than Sam. The host of the *humanly* podcast and the *Mercury Stardust Show*. The principal public figure of dispute within the family.

The Altman family is, an intra-family economic network of major scale. Sam controls OpenAI's $500 billion valuation directly; Jack controls Lattice's $3 billion valuation directly; Max operates Saga Ventures; the three brothers' combined economic activity places the family among the most consequential single-family Silicon Valley operations of the post-2020 period. Annie has been, since 2018-2020, **excluded from the family's economic structure** — by her own public account, after her father's 2018 death, her three brothers and mother managed the family estate in a manner she has alleged improperly withheld from her assets she was entitled to. The dispute over the estate is one of the threads of the broader family dispute that has, since 2021, become public.

## II. The Annie Altman allegations — what the public record establishes

The standard, per Guidance Section 9 item 10, is that where a subject's own family member has, in public, on the record, made serious allegations against the subject, the allegations are documented in the family member's own words, in proportion to the seriousness and the public-record status. The truth of the allegations is not adjudicated beyond what documented sources permit. No details are invented. Nothing is omitted.

The Annie Altman allegations are in the public record at the following levels:

**2021** — Annie posted publicly on Twitter (now X) that she had been **"sexually, physically, emotionally, verbally, financially, and technologically abused"** by her brother Sam and her brother Jack. The post specified that she had experienced abuse "beginning around the time" of her early childhood; she asked anyone else with similar experiences to contact her about pursuing legal action collectively. The post is publicly archived and remains on X.

**2021-2024** — Annie published additional statements on her podcast, on Substack, and on X documenting her allegations and her separate dispute with the family over financial matters. Her published statements consistently identified Sam as the principal perpetrator and Jack as a participant; she identified her brothers as the parties responsible for what she has called the financial deprivation she experienced after her father's 2018 death.

**6 January 2025** — Annie filed a **federal lawsuit, *Annie Altman v. Samuel Altman*, in US District Court for the Eastern District of Missouri**. The complaint alleged that Sam committed acts of **rape, molestation, sodomy, and battery** on Annie "several times a week" for nearly a decade, beginning around the time when Annie was three years old and continuing while she was a minor and Sam was an adult. The complaint sought damages of more than $75,000 plus legal fees and alleged she had suffered post-traumatic stress disorder, lost wages, and "a loss of enjoyment of a normal life" as a result of the alleged abuse. The complaint is on the federal court's public docket.

**7 January 2025** — Sam Altman, with his mother Connie and brothers Jack and Max, issued a **joint statement on X** denying the allegations. The statement called the allegations "utterly untrue" and characterised them as evolving "drastically over time." The statement also asserted that Annie had previously accused family members of "improperly withholding our father's 401k funds, hacking her wifi, and 'shadowbanning' her from various websites including ChatGPT, Twitter, and more." The family statement characterised Annie's broader behaviour as evidence of mental-health instability and said that the family had "no choice but to address" the lawsuit publicly.

**Subsequent reporting in January 2025** — *TechCrunch* (Maxwell Zeff, 7 January 2025) reported additional context from Sam Altman's follow-up posts on X: that Annie had been offered money, gifts, and a house by Sam over the years; that the family had described her personal life as "unstable," with Annie having moved twenty times in a single year; and that Jerry's 2018 death had been a "tipping point" for Annie's mental health, in the family's characterisation. The *New York* magazine 2023 profile of Sam (cited in the *TechCrunch* reporting) had previously documented the estrangement and the family's representation of Annie's situation in similar terms.

**March 2026** — A federal judge in the Eastern District of Missouri **dismissed Annie Altman's lawsuit on statute-of-limitations grounds**. The dismissal was procedural, not on the merits of the underlying allegations. Annie filed an amended complaint in the subsequent period. The legal process is, as of May 2026, ongoing.

No determination is made beyond the public record. The allegations are exactly as serious as Annie has made them; the family's denial is exactly as categorical as the joint statement made it; the dismissal was on procedural grounds, not on the merits; the amended complaint is in the docket. The reader is entitled to that information in the same depth applied to documented public allegations against figures more politically convenient to expose. That is the standard. The Altman family disputes the allegations entirely. Annie Altman has maintained them publicly and in federal court since 2021. The dispute is recorded, in the form in which the public record permits.

What the book observes about the broader pattern, in the structural-analysis terms it has applied to other figures: the captured-press treatment of these allegations has been much less engaged than the captured-press treatment of structurally similar allegations against figures of the political right. The same outlets that produced multiple investigations of Donald Trump's documented sexual-assault allegations, that produced years of coverage of the Cardinal McCarrick case, that engaged extensively with the *Surviving R. Kelly* documentary, have, in the Altman case, principally restricted themselves to single-cycle news coverage of the lawsuit filing and dismissal. The pattern is consistent with the broader structural argument the book has been making about the captured-press constituencies' selective application of investigative depth. Annie Altman is not a politically convenient figure for the captured-press constituency to investigate. Her brother is the principal architect of the AI build-out that the captured-press constituency has, on the public record of major-outlet news coverage, framed as the necessary technological future. The structural alignment between the captured-press constituency and the subject's interests has produced exactly the coverage outcome the previous chapters predicted.

## III. Education and ideological formation
