Sam Altman attended **John Burroughs School** in Clayton, Missouri — a selective private K-12 institution that has produced an unusual concentration of subsequent business and political-economic figures, including, among Sam's contemporaries, several other figures who have appeared in this book's reporting.

At seventeen, Sam came out as gay in a documented event at John Burroughs: in response to a Christian student group's anti-gay messaging, he gave a speech to the school assembly publicly acknowledging his sexuality. The episode is part of his subsequent personal-biography branding as a figure who has been willing to take public personal risks for principles. The episode is documented and verified. The standard captured-press framing of the episode presents it as evidence of Altman's general courage and principled character. The seventeen-year-old Altman demonstrated personal courage in that specific instance; that is not contested. What is observed is that this is one episode in a much longer biographical record that the press has consistently emphasised in ways that have produced a more sympathetic public framing of Altman than the documented operational record might otherwise have supported.

Altman entered **Stanford University** in 2003 to study computer science. He **dropped out in 2005**, after two years, to pursue Loopt. The dropout is, in the standard Silicon Valley biographical convention, presented as evidence of entrepreneurial conviction. The standard convention applies to Altman in the same form it applies to most of the figures in this book. The specific intellectual influences of his Stanford years have not been as extensively documented as those of, e.g., Thiel — Altman has been less inclined to identify his formative reading publicly. The figure he has most consistently cited as an influence is **Paul Graham**, the Y Combinator co-founder who became Altman's principal professional mentor in the period 2005-2014.

The doctrines Altman has, across his public statements, most consistently endorsed:

- **Aggressive scaling** as the principal mechanism by which artificial general intelligence will be achieved (the OpenAI scaling laws, the GPT-3-to-GPT-4-to-GPT-5 trajectory).
- **Universal basic income** as the policy response to AI-driven labour displacement. Altman has been the principal funder of the Worldly Partners universal-basic-income research programme and was an early proponent of UBI in Silicon Valley.
- **"Compute is the new oil"** — the framing he has used publicly to argue that the principal economic and political consequence of AI is the value of the compute infrastructure required to train and operate it.
- The proposition that **AGI will arrive within the 2025-2030 window** and will produce, in his own words at Davos January 2024, "GDP growth that would blow up the GDP" — that is, economic-output increases of an order of magnitude that would render existing labour-market structures obsolete.

The labour-displacement framework is the book's principal economic concern about the subject's project; Chapter 10 documented the framework at length. The relevant point for this chapter is that Altman has, in his own published statements, articulated the framework explicitly. The labour displacement is not an unintended consequence of his project. It is the project. The UBI advocacy is the public-facing response to the displacement; the Worldcoin biometric-identity programme is the mechanism by which the UBI distribution would, in Altman's stated programme, eventually be administered. The integration of the OpenAI scaling, the labour-displacement consequences, the UBI advocacy, and the Worldcoin biometric infrastructure is the integrated programme.

## IV. The founding fiction: Loopt and the near-failure

The standard captured-press narrative of Altman's pre-OpenAI career presents him as a successful young entrepreneur whose first company, Loopt, demonstrated his abilities. The documented record establishes that Loopt was, a near-failure that took seven years to produce a return barely above its venture-capital raise.

**Loopt** was founded in 2005 by Altman with Stanford classmate **Nick Sivo** (Altman's boyfriend at the time) and Alok Deshpande. The company built a location-based social-networking application for mobile phones — an early entrant in what would, after the 2007 iPhone launch, become the location-based-services category that companies including Foursquare and the location features of Twitter and Facebook would subsequently dominate.

Loopt was part of **Y Combinator's first batch** (Summer 2005). Paul Graham's institutional support of Altman in 2005 is the relationship that subsequently produced Altman's 2011 hiring as a Y Combinator partner and his 2014 promotion to YC President. The Y Combinator-Altman relationship is one of the documented patient-sponsor relationships in this book's record — a pattern in which an early supporter (Graham) cultivated a younger figure (Altman) over a decade for subsequent installation at the top of the supporter's institution. The pattern is structurally similar to the Thiel-Vance relationship the previous chapters documented, with the difference that Graham's institution is a venture-capital accelerator rather than an electoral political office.

Loopt raised **$39 million** in venture capital from Sequoia Capital, NEA, and other firms. The product struggled to find product-market fit. In **March 2012**, after seven years of operation, Loopt was acquired by **Green Dot Corporation** for **$43.4 million** — a return barely above the capital raised. The investors, in venture-capital terms, took a near-zero return on a seven-year hold. For Altman personally, the Loopt exit produced a modest personal return (he was 26 percent owner at exit per various reports, suggesting a personal payout of $5 million pre-tax).

Loopt was, by ordinary venture-capital criteria, a failed company. The captured-press subsequent framing of Altman's "successful first startup" has, with the limited exceptions of a few critical pieces, accepted the Altman-Graham framing of Loopt as an early entrepreneurial success. The actual numbers do not support the framing. The relevant biographical point is that Altman's principal credential for his subsequent installation as YC President — and through YC presidency, his network position that produced OpenAI — was a startup that, by the financial outcomes its investors received, would in most other professional contexts have been a disqualifying credential.

## V. Y Combinator, the network, and the path to OpenAI

In 2011, Altman joined Y Combinator as a part-time partner. The hiring was made by **Paul Graham**, who had been Altman's principal Loopt mentor.

In **2014**, Graham named Altman **President of Y Combinator** — a promotion that placed the 28-year-old Altman in operational control of the institution that had, by 2014, become the principal early-stage venture-capital accelerator in Silicon Valley. The promotion was announced publicly by Graham as a generational handoff; Graham wanted to step back from operations. The promotion gave Altman direct decision-making authority over which startups received YC investment, which YC alumni received subsequent support, and which Silicon Valley figures were introduced to YC's network of investors and partners. The role was, one of the most consequential network-controlling positions in Silicon Valley.

Altman held the YC presidency from 2014 to 2019 — a five-year period during which YC dramatically expanded its investment pace and portfolio. Under his tenure, YC invested in 1,900 companies, with the cumulative valuation of the YC portfolio growing into the hundreds of billions of dollars. Altman's personal portfolio (separate from YC's institutional investments) was built largely through positions in YC companies. The standard accelerator model — YC took 7 percent equity in each accepted company in exchange for $150,000 in seed funding plus the program — produced, across 1,900 companies, the active footprint of Altman's subsequent venture-capital network.

The personal investments Altman built during the YC presidency, and continued building after, are documented in Section IX below. The structural point is that Altman, by 2019 when he stepped down from the YC presidency to focus on OpenAI, had built a personal network position equivalent to that of any of the senior Silicon Valley venture-capital figures, despite having no comparable financial-capital base (his personal wealth as of 2019 was $300 million, modest by Silicon Valley standards). The network position is the base from which OpenAI was subsequently built.

## VI. OpenAI: founding to ouster

**OpenAI was founded in December 2015** as a 501(c)(3) non-profit research organisation. The founding announcement listed as principal contributors **Sam Altman, Elon Musk, Greg Brockman, Ilya Sutskever, Reid Hoffman, Peter Thiel, Jessica Livingston, Y Combinator Research**, and several other Silicon Valley figures, with **$1 billion in pledged funding**. The mission, as stated, was to ensure that artificial general intelligence "benefits all of humanity." The non-profit structure was, in the founders' public statements, chosen specifically to avoid the incentive structures of for-profit AI development — the proposition that, if AGI was as consequential as the founders believed, it should not be controlled by a single profit-maximising corporate entity.

The early years of OpenAI, 2016-2018, were principally research-focused. **Ilya Sutskever** was the technical leader; Brockman was the principal operational manager; Altman was Chairman of the non-profit board. Musk's involvement was heavy in the early period — he provided a large fraction of the initial funding and was on the board.

In **2018, Elon Musk departed the OpenAI board**, citing conflicts of interest with Tesla's autonomous-driving AI development. Musk subsequently claimed (in various public statements 2023-2024) that he had committed $1 billion to OpenAI but only delivered $100 million before departing. The departure produced the first significant funding gap in OpenAI's operating budget.

The funding gap was the working driver of the most consequential governance decision in OpenAI's history. In **2019, OpenAI converted to a "capped-profit" corporate structure**. A for-profit subsidiary, **OpenAI Global LLC**, was created, controlled by the non-profit OpenAI Inc. The structure permitted outside investors to receive returns of up to **100 times their investment**; any excess profit would revert to the non-profit. The same year, **Microsoft announced a $1 billion investment** — the first investment under the new structure.

The 2019 conversion was, in retrospect, the foundational governance decision of OpenAI's commercial trajectory. The capped-profit structure permitted Microsoft (and subsequent investors) to take economic positions in the company that would, under the original non-profit structure, have been impossible. The 2019 conversion is what made the subsequent $13 billion in Microsoft investment, the $157 billion 2024 valuation, the $500 billion 2025 valuation, and the 2025 conversion to a public benefit corporation possible. The decision was made by Altman and the OpenAI board in the period before the November 2023 ouster, and was one of the documented sources of Sutskever's and others' subsequent concerns about Altman's governance.

The commercial trajectory accelerated rapidly after 2019:

- **2020**: GPT-3 released.
- **November 2022**: **ChatGPT** released. The consumer interface that produced the post-2022 AI boom.
- **January 2023**: Microsoft announced an additional **$10 billion investment** at an approximate $29 billion valuation. Microsoft Azure became OpenAI's exclusive cloud provider.
- **March 2023**: GPT-4 released.
- Throughout 2023: rapid growth in ChatGPT users (100 million by January 2023; 200 million by late 2023); rapid commercial deployment of OpenAI's API to enterprise customers; rapid recruitment of personnel from competing AI labs.

In the same period, internal tensions at OpenAI were building. The principal documented sources of those tensions, per subsequent reporting in *The New York Times*, *The Information*, *Bloomberg*, and the post-November 2023 WilmerHale internal review:

- **Sutskever and others' concerns about the prioritisation of safety**. The 2019 conversion to capped-profit had been justified, in part, by the proposition that commercial revenue would fund safety research; in operational practice, safety teams were reportedly under-resourced relative to capabilities teams.
- **Concerns about Altman's candor with the board**. The board's subsequent November 17, 2023 statement, that Altman had not been "consistently candid in his communications with the board," reflected accumulated concerns over an extended period.
- **The OpenAI Startup Fund governance issue**. Altman personally controlled the OpenAI Startup Fund (the fund through which OpenAI made strategic investments in early-stage AI companies), despite the fund nominally being an OpenAI corporate vehicle. The personal control of the fund by the CEO, with investments that overlapped his personal portfolio, was one of the documented conflict-of-interest concerns.
- **The Helen Toner CSET paper**. In October 2023, board member Helen Toner (then at Georgetown's Center for Security and Emerging Technology) co-authored a paper that, in passing, included a critique of OpenAI's safety practices. Altman's reported response — including, per the WSJ's subsequent reporting, attempts to remove Toner from the board — produced the immediate proximate cause of the November ouster.

On **November 17, 2023**, the OpenAI board — then composed of **Helen Toner, Tasha McCauley, Adam D'Angelo, and Ilya Sutskever** (Altman and Brockman recused themselves from the discussion) — **removed Sam Altman as CEO**. Brockman was removed as Chairman and resigned as President shortly thereafter. CTO **Mira Murati** was named interim CEO.

The subsequent five days were the most-public corporate-governance crisis in Silicon Valley's recent history:

- **November 17-18**: Microsoft (under Satya Nadella) offered Altman and Brockman positions to lead an in-house Microsoft AI division. The offer was the principal lever Microsoft used to coerce the OpenAI board into reversing the ouster — Microsoft was OpenAI's principal investor and the dependency of OpenAI's compute infrastructure made an alternative cloud provider impractical.
- **November 18-20**: **Roughly 700 of OpenAI's 770 employees** signed a letter demanding Altman's reinstatement and threatening to resign en masse if the board did not comply. Mira Murati signed the letter despite being the interim CEO. Sutskever signed the letter, despite having been one of the board members who voted to remove Altman — his subsequent statement that he "deeply regretted" his board vote is one of the documented coercion events of the crisis.
- **November 20-21**: Continued negotiations. The board was operationally isolated; the investor pressure (Microsoft, Thrive Capital, Tiger Global) was sustained; the employee revolt was complete.
- **November 21-22, 2023**: Altman and Brockman reinstated. The new initial board: **Bret Taylor (Chair)**, **Larry Summers**, and **Adam D'Angelo** (the sole holdover from the previous board). Helen Toner and Tasha McCauley were removed.

The November 2023 crisis is the case in which a corporate board attempted to exercise the governance authority the corporate-charter structure formally granted it, and was overruled by the combined pressure of an investor (Microsoft) and an employee group. The crisis demonstrated that, in the contemporary Silicon Valley governance environment, the formal board-authority structures are operationally less consequential than the informal employee-investor-CEO coalition structures. The board's authority is the authority the rest of the network permits it to exercise; in the November 2023 case, the rest of the network did not permit it. Altman's reinstatement was the outcome.

The aftermath:

- **November 29, 2023**: An anonymous Microsoft employee joined the OpenAI board as a non-voting observer. Microsoft resigned the observer seat in **July 2024** in response to FTC and European Commission regulatory pressure on the OpenAI-Microsoft governance overlap.
- **February 2024**: The Securities and Exchange Commission **subpoenaed OpenAI's internal communications** to determine whether Altman's pre-November-2023 representations to investors had been materially misleading. The investigation was, per subsequent reporting, ongoing through 2024-2025. The SEC under the Biden administration was actively investigating; the SEC under the Trump administration has reportedly downscaled the investigation, though no formal closure has been announced as of May 2026.
- **March 2024**: The internal review conducted by WilmerHale on OpenAI's behalf concluded that Altman should remain CEO. The review was, in the standard convention of corporate-commissioned investigations, structured to produce that conclusion; the report itself was not published, only the summary recommendations. Three new board members were announced: **Sue Desmond-Hellmann** (former CEO of the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation), **Nicole Seligman** (former Sony EVP), **Fidji Simo** (then CEO of Instacart). Altman rejoined the board.
- **June 2024**: **Paul M. Nakasone** — retired US Army general, former Director of the National Security Agency 2018-2024, former head of US Cyber Command, former Chief of the Central Security Service — joined the OpenAI board. The appointment placed the immediately-prior head of US signals intelligence on the board of the principal commercial AI company at the same time that OpenAI was completing the active integration with the federal national-security apparatus that the subsequent Stargate Project would consolidate.

Throughout 2024, **half of OpenAI's pre-November-2023 AI safety researchers departed the company**, citing the deprioritisation of safety goals after Altman's reinstatement. The departures included senior figures who subsequently joined Anthropic (the parent company of the tool that has produced this book's draft text, and a subject of the book's later chapter), founded their own companies, or moved to alternative AI-safety institutions. The net effect of the November 2023 crisis was that OpenAI consolidated commercial focus and capital-deployment capacity at the cost of its remaining institutional commitment to the safety-research mission that had been the founding justification of the non-profit structure.

## VII. The 2025 restructuring and the path to Stargate

In 2025, OpenAI completed a further governance restructuring. The for-profit subsidiary that had been created in 2019 as a "capped-profit" structure was **converted into a Public Benefit Corporation (PBC)**. The non-profit OpenAI Inc. retained **26 percent ownership** of the PBC; the remaining 74 percent was owned by investors. The cap on investor returns was removed. The structure of the company became, that of a conventional for-profit corporation with a minority non-profit shareholder.

The October 2025 share sale valued the company at **$500 billion** — making OpenAI, by valuation, the most valuable AI company in the world and one of the most valuable private companies of any sort.

The Stargate Project announcement preceded the 2025 restructuring by several months. On **21 January 2025 — inauguration day** — President Trump announced at the White House the formation of **Stargate**, a joint venture of **OpenAI, Oracle, SoftBank, and MGX** (the UAE-state-backed AI fund led by Sheikh Tahnoun bin Zayed). The announcement committed **$100 billion of immediate investment and $500 billion over four years** to AI infrastructure construction in the United States. **Masayoshi Son** was named Chairman of the Stargate joint venture. **Sam Altman** was the principal architect on the OpenAI side. **Larry Ellison** was the principal partner on the Oracle side.

The announcement included the documented public statement by Son, at the White House podium with Trump: **"We wouldn't have decided to do this unless you won."** The statement is on record of the inauguration-day press conference. It is the most explicit public acknowledgment in the available documentation that the AI-infrastructure capital-deployment programme of the second Trump administration was, the contingent product of the 2024 election outcome — that is, a coordinated capital-and-policy programme of the new governing coalition rather than a market response to underlying economic fundamentals. The first Stargate construction site was Abilene, Texas, where Oracle, OpenAI, and Crusoe Cloud had previously been building a data centre.

The Stargate Project is, in capital-deployment terms, on a scale comparable to the largest infrastructure-construction programmes in American history. The Apollo programme cost $260 billion in 2026 dollars; the Manhattan Project cost $30 billion in 2026 dollars; the Interstate Highway System cost $600 billion in 2026 dollars. Stargate's $500 billion commitment, if executed, would be the second-largest concentrated capital deployment for a single technological objective in the historical record. The objective is the construction of the AI compute infrastructure necessary to develop and operate the artificial-general-intelligence systems that the Altman-Son-Ellison-MGX coalition has publicly committed to building.

The political-economy significance of the Stargate Project, as Chapter 10 documented, is that the capital-deployment programme is being executed in coordination with the labour-displacement programme that the same coalition has publicly committed to producing as the AI's economic consequence. The two programmes are not in tension; they are the same programme. The capital build-out enables the labour displacement. The labour displacement produces the unemployment that the coalition's Worldcoin-and-UBI programme proposes to manage. The integration is the integrated programme.

## VIII. Worldcoin / Tools for Humanity: the global biometric database

The same individual who is the principal architect of the AI labour-displacement programme is the co-founder of the proposed mechanism by which the displaced population would be biometrically identified, financially supported, and integrated into the post-employment economy. **Worldcoin** — rebranded as **World** in October 2024 — is, the proposed back-end identification infrastructure for the post-AGI economy that the OpenAI-Stargate programme proposes to construct.

**Tools for Humanity** is the operating company. It was founded in 2019 by **Sam Altman** and **Alex Blania** (the CEO). Blania is a German physicist who had previously worked at the California Institute of Technology. The company has raised **$650 million** by 2025, with principal investors including **Andreessen Horowitz**, **Khosla Ventures** ($240+ million), and others.

The product is the **Orb** — a volleyball-sized iris-scanning hardware device that creates a unique cryptographic identifier (the "World ID") tied to the iris pattern of the scanned individual. Users who complete the iris scan receive a cryptographic identifier in the company's identity system and, in most jurisdictions, **$50 in cryptocurrency** (paid in the World cryptocurrency token) as a signing-up bonus.

The stated purpose, in Altman's and Blania's public statements: to create a "proof of personhood" identity infrastructure that, as AI-generated content becomes indistinguishable from human-generated content online, will permit identification of real human users in commercial and political online environments. The actual operational substance is a global biometric database, held by a private company, that has by late 2025 scanned the irises of 17.5 million people across more than thirty countries.

The Worldcoin operational record is the most extensively documented case in this book's reporting of a Silicon Valley project that has, in the assessment of regulatory authorities and civil-society organisations across multiple jurisdictions, been determined to violate the regulatory and human-rights standards of those jurisdictions. The documented timeline:

- **April 2022**: *MIT Technology Review* investigation documented "unethical practices when onboarding test users across Africa and Asia, including deceptive marketing practices" and the collection of personal data beyond iris scans (heartbeat, breathing, and other vital signs) without informed consent. The investigation found that half a million people had, by that point, been scanned, principally in Indonesia, Kenya, Sudan, Ghana, Chile, Norway, and other low-and-middle-income countries.
- **2023**: Multiple governments opened investigations. Kenya suspended operations in August 2023.
- **2024**: Kenya High Court ruled World's operations unlawful and ordered permanent deletion of biometric data (May 2024). Hong Kong banned operations. Germany's data-protection authority concluded that World's data-protection measures would not provide adequate security against cybercriminals or state attackers.
- **January 2025**: Brazil's National Data Protection Authority (ANPD) **banned** the project. The financial incentives (the $50 cryptocurrency payment) were found to breach Brazil's data-protection law, which mandates that consent for biometric data collection be "free, informed, and unequivocal" — and the payment for consent was found to render the consent non-free.
- **March 2025**: Brazil reaffirmed the ban, with daily fines of 50,000 reais ($8,800) for resumption.
- **May 2025**: Indonesia suspended data collection and opened investigations of two local Tools for Humanity entities for unlicensed operations.
- **October 2025**: Philippines National Privacy Commission issued cease-and-desist over "consent and exploitation of vulnerable populations."
- **November 2025**: Thailand shut down World's biometric data collection and ordered deletion of collected data.
- **April 2026**: Despite the regulatory record, World announced **partnerships with Tinder, Zoom, and Docusign** for identity verification in those services — operationally integrating the biometric database with the American consumer-application infrastructure.

The pattern is clear on the public record. **The countries that have most aggressively suspended, banned, or investigated Worldcoin's operations are the countries whose data-protection regulators are operationally independent of the Silicon Valley investor coalition that funds the project.** Brazil's ANPD, Germany's BfDI, the Kenyan judiciary, the Philippines NPC, and the Thai data-protection authorities have all reached similar conclusions about the project's regulatory non-compliance. The American regulatory environment — which has produced no equivalent restrictions — is, in this case as in others the book has documented, less protective of the public interest in biometric privacy than the corresponding regulatory environments of the affected developing-country jurisdictions.

Edward Snowden, whose 2013 NSA disclosures established him as one of the principal global authorities on biometric and surveillance infrastructure, has publicly characterised World's project as "**cataloguing eyeballs**." The characterisation is, on the documented record of the project's operations, accurate.

The Worldcoin project's strategic significance is independent of its specific user-count growth. The project does not need to scan a billion irises to achieve its strategic objective. It needs to establish the regulatory precedent, in the United States and in the consumer-application ecosystem (Tinder, Zoom, Docusign, and the subsequent partnerships that will follow), that the iris-scan biometric identifier can be used as a legitimate identity-verification mechanism in commercial transactions. Once the precedent is established and the integration with consumer applications is operational, the gradual expansion of the biometric-identifier requirement into additional commercial and governmental contexts is, in the standard pattern of Silicon Valley regulatory-arbitrage projects, the trajectory the project would be expected to follow. The book's argument is that this is the strategic objective, and that the Worldcoin project is, the front-end consumer-facing implementation of the population-management infrastructure the new world order's AI-and-labour-displacement programme will require to manage the populations it displaces.

## IX. The personal investment portfolio

Sam Altman's personal investment portfolio is the working network that complements his OpenAI position and his Worldcoin co-foundership. The principal documented positions:

- **Helion Energy** (fusion power, founded 2013): Altman has invested **$375 million** of personal capital — among the largest personal investments by any individual into a single private company publicly documented. Altman serves as Helion's **Chairman**. Helion has a 2028 power-delivery commitment to Microsoft — that is, the company is contractually obligated to deliver fusion-generated electricity to OpenAI's principal investor by 2028, a commitment most fusion-energy experts assess as technically unrealistic by that date. The Helion-Microsoft-OpenAI integration is operationally significant: Altman, as OpenAI CEO, is in operational position to direct OpenAI's compute-procurement strategy in ways that support Helion's growth, while as Helion Chairman he is in position to direct Helion's power-delivery commitments toward OpenAI's principal investor.
- **Retro Biosciences** (longevity research, founded 2022): Altman invested **$180 million** in personal capital — again, an extraordinarily large personal commitment to a single private company. The investment is one of several Silicon Valley longevity-research positions; the pattern parallels Thiel's Methuselah Foundation funding and the broader Thiel-network biotech portfolio. The strategic logic is the same: the wealthy and powerful, through advanced biotechnology, escape biological constraints on lifespan that historically applied uniformly to all classes.
- **Humane** (AI pin): Altman was a major investor in Humane, the AI-wearable company founded by former Apple executives Imran Chaudhri and Bethany Bongiorno. Humane launched the AI Pin product in 2024; the product failed commercially within months. Humane was acquired by HP in 2025 in a below-cost transaction. Altman took a major personal loss; the failure is one of the few documented Altman-investment failures of the past decade.
- **OpenAI Startup Fund**: The fund through which OpenAI makes strategic investments in early-stage AI companies. Altman **personally controlled the fund** (despite the fund nominally being an OpenAI corporate vehicle) until the November 2023 governance reforms; the personal control of the fund was one of the conflict-of-interest concerns that contributed to the November 2023 board action. The fund's positions include many of the contemporary AI infrastructure companies — Anthropic-adjacent, Pinecone, Speak, and dozens of others. The pre-2023 structure permitted Altman to direct OpenAI corporate capital toward investments that, expanded his personal portfolio of AI-industry positions.
- **University of Michigan venture relationship**: Per *Fortune* reporting in December 2023, Altman quietly received **$75 million from the University of Michigan** for a new venture fund, months after ChatGPT's launch. The arrangement was disclosed only after press inquiry. The pattern — university endowment capital flowing to a specific Silicon Valley figure on the basis of personal relationships — is the standard pattern of capital flow the previous chapters of this book documented.
- **Y Combinator alumni portfolio**: Altman holds positions in many of the major Y Combinator alumni companies — Stripe, Airbnb, Reddit (he was on the Reddit board until 2022), Asana, Pinterest, Cruise, and dozens of others. The combined valuation of the Y Combinator alumni positions Altman holds is, by independent estimates, in the multiple billions of dollars.

Altman's **total personal net worth** in 2026 is estimated by Forbes at **$2 billion** — much less than the figures whose biographies preceded his in this book, but the figure understates his operational economic position. He controls, through his board positions and his executive role at OpenAI, decision-making over an entity valued at $500 billion. He holds, through his personal investments, positions whose combined valuation is in the multiple billions of dollars. His operational economic position is much larger than his nominal personal wealth would suggest.

## X. Political donations and the bipartisan-access strategy

Through 2023, Altman's documented political donations were predominantly Democratic-aligned:

- **2020**: $250,000 to the Biden Victory Fund.
- **2020-2022**: Various contributions to Democratic candidates and Democratic-aligned political organisations.
- Support for universal-basic-income organisations and policy research throughout the period.

In **December 2024 — after the Trump election but before the inauguration** — Altman made a **$1 million personal donation to the Trump inauguration committee**. The donation was publicly disclosed. It was part of a broader pattern of Silicon Valley executives (Bezos, Zuckerberg, Cook, and others) making inauguration-committee donations in the post-election period — a pattern that has been characterised, by the recipients in their own statements and by independent observers, as access-protection rather than political endorsement.

Altman has dined at Mar-a-Lago multiple times since the 2024 election. He was present at the Stargate White House announcement on 21 January 2025. The operational substance of his political relationship with the second Trump administration is closer than the bipartisan-access framing would suggest — the Stargate Project is, in capital-deployment terms, an integration of his company's operational future with the administration's first-day policy programme.

The pattern in Altman's political activity is the pattern Chapter 11 documented for the Thiel-network funding model in its bipartisan variant: maintain donor relationships across both parties; ensure access to whichever party governs; align operationally with the party that produces the more favourable regulatory environment; preserve the option to realign as conditions change. Altman has, since 2024, operationally aligned with the Trump-administration project; he has not, in his public statements, repudiated his earlier Democratic alignment. The structural position is the standard portfolio-management position of a CEO whose company depends on bipartisan capital and bipartisan regulatory access.

## XI. The OpenAI board and the structural partners

The OpenAI board, as of May 2026, consists of nine members:

- **Bret Taylor (Chair)** — Former Twitter Board Chair; former Salesforce co-CEO; former Facebook CTO; current CEO of Sierra (an AI customer-service company he founded in 2024 with Clay Bavor, formerly of Google). Taylor's biography is the co-opted press standard "experienced tech executive": multiple successful corporate roles, broad network across Silicon Valley, no documented public controversies. He has chaired the OpenAI board since the November 2023 reinstatement; he has, by all available reporting, functioned principally as the executive who manages the board's operational alignment with Altman's preferences. His own AI company, Sierra, is a portfolio company of the AI investment thesis OpenAI's commercial expansion supports.

- **Sam Altman** — CEO and director.

- **Adam D'Angelo** — CEO of Quora; former Facebook CTO; the sole continuous board member from before November 2023. D'Angelo is, in his own venture-capital activity, an active early-stage investor with major AI portfolio positions including Poe (Quora's AI product) which competes with ChatGPT. The continued presence of D'Angelo on the board — despite his vote to remove Altman in November 2023 — is one of the structural questions the November 2023 reinstatement produced; the documented answer is that D'Angelo's network position and his Facebook-Quora-tech-establishment relationships were sufficiently embedded that his removal would have been operationally more difficult than the removal of Toner and McCauley.

- **Sue Desmond-Hellmann** — Physician; former CEO of the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation (2014-2020); former Chancellor of UCSF; previously the principal executive at Genentech responsible for the Avastin and Herceptin oncology programmes. The Desmond-Hellmann appointment placed the immediately-prior CEO of the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation on the OpenAI board — the institutional bridge between OpenAI and the Gates network the book will examine in detail in Chapter 19.

- **Nicole Seligman** — Former Sony Corporation of America President; former Sony Corporation EVP and General Counsel; a Washington-corporate-law lawyer of long establishment. Her biography is the press standard "experienced corporate governance director" — multiple corporate boards, broad establishment network, no documented public controversies. The expertise she brings to the OpenAI board is in corporate-governance compliance and crisis management, not AI substance.

- **Fidji Simo** — Former CEO of Instacart; former Facebook executive (Head of the Facebook App); on the boards of Shopify and other companies. Simo was hired by OpenAI in 2025 as Head of Applications — that is, she took an operational role at the company in addition to her board position. In April 2026 she announced "several weeks" of medical leave.

- **Paul M. Nakasone** — Retired US Army General; Director of the National Security Agency 2018-2024; head of US Cyber Command 2018-2024; Chief of the Central Security Service. Nakasone's appointment in **June 2024** is structurally the most consequential of the post-November 2023 board additions. It placed the immediately-prior head of US signals intelligence — the agency that conducts the bulk of US foreign and (under the FISA Section 702 framework) domestic electronic surveillance — on the board of the principal commercial AI company at the same time that OpenAI was completing the integration with the federal national-security apparatus that the subsequent Stargate Project would consolidate. The Nakasone appointment is the documentation that OpenAI is, structurally, the commercial AI company of the US national-security state — not in the sense that the state directly controls it, but in the sense that the state's most recent senior signals-intelligence executive is on its board.

- **Zico Kolter** — Carnegie Mellon Computer Science professor; theoretical machine learning researcher. Kolter is the academic-credibility appointment to the board; his AI expertise is among the most legitimate of any board member. He is also Chief Expert at Bosch Center for AI and has major commercial-research positions.

- **Adebayo "Bayo" Ogunlesi** — Senior managing director at **BlackRock**; CEO of Global Infrastructure Partners (the infrastructure-investing platform BlackRock acquired in 2024 for $12.5 billion); spent 23 years at Credit Suisse including as Executive Vice Chairman of investment banking; former attorney at Cravath, Swaine & Moore; former law clerk to US Supreme Court Justice Thurgood Marshall. Ogunlesi sits on the BlackRock board, the Topgolf Callaway Brands board, the Kosmos Energy board, and the Terminal Investment Limited board. Appointed to the OpenAI board in **January 2025**. The Ogunlesi appointment is structurally the BlackRock-OpenAI bridge — placing the senior BlackRock executive responsible for global infrastructure investing on the board of the company whose Stargate Project will be the largest single infrastructure deployment of the next decade.

**Larry Summers** resigned from the OpenAI board in **November 2025**. Summers had served on the post-November-2023 reconstituted board and had been one of the more politically active board members (he had also been on the boards of Andreessen Horowitz's policy committee and various other Silicon Valley institutions). His resignation has not been fully explained publicly.

The OpenAI board, in its current composition, is the active map of the new world order's principal coalition partners in the AI build-out: the senior signals-intelligence officer of the United States (Nakasone), the senior infrastructure-investing executive of the world's largest asset manager (Ogunlesi), the immediately-prior CEO of the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation (Desmond-Hellmann), the experienced corporate-governance compliance lawyer (Seligman), the AI-academic-credibility appointment (Kolter), the Silicon Valley executive who runs the operational application strategy (Simo), the establishment-tech-executive Chair (Taylor), the continuous-historical-pre-November-2023 member (D'Angelo), and the CEO. The structural integration of OpenAI with the broader new-world-order coalition is the observation the board composition produces.

## XII. The Suchir Balaji death and what the public record establishes

**Suchir Balaji**, 26, was a former OpenAI researcher who had publicly accused OpenAI of copyright infringement in its training-data collection practices. He had spoken to *The New York Times* on the record in **October 2024** about his concerns and was a potential witness in the ongoing copyright lawsuits against OpenAI by *The New York Times* and other publishers.

On **26 November 2024**, Balaji was found dead in his San Francisco apartment. The San Francisco Police Department and the San Francisco Office of the Chief Medical Examiner ruled the death a **suicide**. SFPD stated there was "no evidence of foul play."

Balaji's family — particularly his mother **Poornima Ramarao** — disputed the suicide ruling and called for FBI investigation, citing the timing (Balaji was a witness in active copyright litigation against OpenAI), disputed details of the scene as reported by police, and an independent autopsy commissioned by the family that reportedly reached different conclusions from the official autopsy.

Annie Altman publicly tweeted **"#JusticeForSuchir"** in December 2024, joining the family and others questioning the suicide ruling. Annie's involvement drew additional public attention to the dispute over Balaji's death and to the broader pattern of Altman-family-and-OpenAI-related deaths that — in the framing of those who disputed the suicide ruling — required further investigation.

The death, the official ruling, the family's dispute, and Annie Altman's involvement in the public dispute are on the record. **No adjudication beyond the public record is attempted.** The official ruling stands; the family dispute stands; the independent autopsy stands; the legal process is ongoing as of mid-2026. The purpose in noting the Balaji death is to establish that it is part of the working context of the OpenAI corporate environment in the period under documentation, and that the deceased was a witness in active litigation against the company at the time of his death. The structural significance of those facts is for the reader to weigh.

## XIII. The current position in 2026

This is what Sam Altman is, as of May 2026:

- The **CEO of OpenAI**, the most valuable artificial-intelligence company in human history at $500 billion valuation (October 2025 share sale).
- The **principal architect of the Stargate Project**, the $500 billion AI-infrastructure capital-deployment programme announced at the White House on 21 January 2025 as the first-day policy initiative of the second Trump administration.
- The **co-founder of Worldcoin / Tools for Humanity**, the global biometric-identification project that has, by late 2025, scanned the irises of 17.5 million people and been banned, restricted, or under investigation in a dozen jurisdictions for data-protection and human-rights violations.
- The **Chairman of Helion Energy**, the fusion-power company with a 2028 power-delivery commitment to Microsoft (OpenAI's principal investor).
- The **principal of an extensive personal Silicon Valley investment portfolio** in AI, longevity research, fusion, and Y Combinator alumni companies, with combined valuations in the multiple billions of dollars.
- A **principal political donor and policy interlocutor** of the second Trump administration, despite his predominantly Democratic donation history prior to 2024 — a pattern consistent with the bipartisan-access strategy that has, the book has documented, been the standard Silicon Valley CEO strategy in the period.
- The **subject of ongoing federal litigation by his own sister** alleging childhood sexual abuse — litigation procedurally dismissed in March 2026 on statute-of-limitations grounds, with an amended complaint subsequently filed.
- The **former CEO who was removed by the board on November 17, 2023 and reinstated five days later** through the combined pressure of an investor (Microsoft) and an employee group, in the documented case in which corporate-charter board-governance authority was overruled by the network's informal-coalition structures.

The biography is, in 2026, the form of the new world order's economic centrepiece. The previous chapters described the regent and the executor. The record has described the architect of the labour-displacement programme — the figure through whose company the AI substrate that will displace the broad-based labour market is being built, and through whose parallel company (Worldcoin) the biometric infrastructure for managing the displaced population is being constructed. The integration is the programme. Altman is the integrator.
