# Chapter 14: Marc Andreessen

In October 2023, **Marc Lowell Andreessen** published a document called the *Techno-Optimist Manifesto*. In its published list of "Patron Saints" — the thinkers Andreessen credited as intellectual forebears of his worldview — the first name listed was **Filippo Tommaso Marinetti**, the founder of the Italian Futurist movement and a founding collaborator of the Italian Fascist project. The manifesto named as "enemies" the social-responsibility, trust-and-safety, tech-ethics, and risk-management functions that constitute the principal institutional defences against unchecked corporate power. The man who published this is the co-founder and senior General Partner of **Andreessen Horowitz** (commonly known as "a16z") — the venture-capital firm managing **$90 billion in assets**, the largest US venture-capital firm by capital under management. He sits on the board of **Meta** (since 2008). His firm holds positions across 1,076 portfolio companies. His firm's American Dynamism fund has been the principal capital channel for the Anduril-Shield-AI-Hadrian-Castelion defence-startup wave. His 2024 political-donor activity was the principal signal of the broader Silicon Valley right-wing political coordination.

His 1995 Netscape IPO is the historical event most commonly identified as the beginning of the dot-com bubble. His 2011 *Software Is Eating the World* essay is the foundational investment-thesis statement of his firm and of the broader Silicon Valley capital-class worldview. He is one of the most consequential figures in the contemporary American political-economy environment — not because of his technical contributions (which ended with Netscape) but because of the capital he directs and the ideology he has published to justify directing it.

## I. Origins: Cedar Falls, New Lisbon, and the working-class upbringing

Marc Lowell Andreessen was born on 9 July 1971 in **Cedar Falls, Iowa**. His family relocated shortly after his birth to **New Lisbon, Wisconsin**, a small agricultural town of 2,500 residents in west-central Wisconsin where he was raised.

His **father, Lowell Andreessen**, sold seeds for a small Wisconsin agricultural supply company. His **mother, Patricia Andreessen**, worked at the **Lands' End** mail-order catalog company, then headquartered in Dodgeville, Wisconsin. The family was of Scandinavian (principally Norwegian) descent and Lutheran religious background. The family was, by the standards of the small-town agricultural Wisconsin environment of the 1970s and 1980s, **working-class to lower-middle-class** — not poor, but not affluent. The household did not contain extensive book-collections, did not have major savings, and did not provide the educational-credential advantages that the children of the contemporary American professional class typically take for granted.

The contrast with every other figure in this book is real. Thiel's father was a chemical engineer at a Namibian uranium mine. Musk's father was a wealthy Pretoria businessman. Altman's parents were professional-class St. Louis Jews. Andreessen's parents sold seeds and packed catalog orders. Of the figures documented here, his family origins are the most genuinely modest. The meritocratic-ascent narrative is, in his case, more defensible than in any other.

The modest origins are on the record. The inference that modest origins entitle the destination is not accepted. The Wisconsin agricultural-supply worker's son who, having achieved Silicon Valley billionaire status, joins his wife in publicly opposing the construction of 130 affordable housing units near his Atherton estate (Section X below) is the same political-economy actor as the inherited-wealth Pretoria-born or apartheid-South-African-emigrant billionaires. The origins explain the trajectory. They do not legitimise the outcome.

## II. Mosaic and the founding fortune

Andreessen graduated from **New Lisbon High School** in 1989 and entered the **University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign**, where he studied computer science. The Illinois CS department in the late 1980s and early 1990s was among the strongest research programmes in the United States and was the institutional home of the **National Center for Supercomputing Applications (NCSA)** — one of five NSF-funded supercomputing centres established in the mid-1980s as part of the federal computing-infrastructure programme that produced, among other things, the early Internet backbone.

Andreessen worked at NCSA as an undergraduate beginning in 1992. With **Eric Bina**, a full-time NCSA staff programmer seven years his senior, Andreessen wrote **Mosaic** — the first widely-used graphical web browser. Mosaic's Unix version was released in **January 1993**; the Windows and Mac versions followed in subsequent months. Mosaic was the technology that opened the World Wide Web to mass use; before Mosaic, the Web was a text-based system used by a few thousand researchers, principally in physics and computer science. Within a year of Mosaic's release, the Web's user base had grown by two orders of magnitude.

The Mosaic codebase was, legally, the property of the **University of Illinois Board of Trustees** — Mosaic had been developed using NCSA's federal-funded computing infrastructure and Bina's salary was paid by NCSA. The University of Illinois subsequently licensed Mosaic to Spyglass Inc. for commercial development; Spyglass's Mosaic was the codebase Microsoft licensed for the original Internet Explorer 1.0 (1995).

Andreessen graduated from Illinois in 1993 and moved to Silicon Valley with the explicit intent of commercialising browser technology. The University of Illinois's ownership of Mosaic was the legal obstacle to that commercialisation; Andreessen's solution was to **write a competing browser from scratch**, with Bina and other NCSA alumni, in a new company that had no legal claim on the original Mosaic codebase. That new company was **Netscape Communications**.

## III. Netscape and the 1995 IPO

In April 1994, **Jim Clark** (the founder of Silicon Graphics, who had recently been pushed out of his own company) approached Andreessen with the proposal to form a new company to commercialise web-browser technology. Clark provided the capital and the executive credibility; Andreessen provided the technical expertise and the recruited team of NCSA alumni. The company was initially named **Mosaic Communications Corporation**; after the University of Illinois objected, it was renamed **Netscape Communications** in November 1994.

In **December 1994, Netscape Navigator 1.0** was released. The product was, in its initial release, given away free to non-commercial users — the explicit strategy was to capture the user base before competing browsers could establish themselves. Within a year, Navigator had captured **80 percent of the early web-browser market**.

On **9 August 1995, Netscape went public**. The IPO was the largest first-day stock pop in the history of American capital markets to that point. The stock opened at **$28 per share**; it closed at **$58.25**. The closing valuation of $2.9 billion was unprecedented for a company that had **never been profitable** and had been founded only sixteen months previously.

The Netscape IPO marks the beginning of the dot-com bubble. The bubble was the defining economic event of the late 1990s; its collapse in 2000-2002 was the defining crisis of the early 2000s; its broader consequences — the establishment of the Silicon Valley wealth class, the deregulatory alignment that class subsequently funded, the displacement of older industrial-economic models — are foundational to the political-economy environment this book describes.

Andreessen, at 24, was an instant centi-millionaire. The seed-salesman's son from New Lisbon was now a member of the global capital class. The subsequent twenty-eight years of his biography have been those of a member of that class.

The Microsoft response to Netscape's dominance — the bundling of Internet Explorer with Windows, the price war that eventually produced the free distribution of both browsers, and the subsequent **US v. Microsoft** antitrust case (1998-2001) — is the historical episode the book has documented elsewhere. The relevant point for this chapter is that **AOL acquired Netscape for $4.2 billion in stock in November 1998**; Andreessen received **$110 million** from the transaction. His personal liquidity from the Netscape sale, together with the Clark-network relationships he had built during the Netscape years, was the capital and network foundation from which all of his subsequent activity has proceeded.

## IV. The intermediate ventures and the corporate-board pipeline

In **1999**, Andreessen founded **Loudcloud** with **Ben Horowitz, Tim Howes, and In Sik Rhee** — three Netscape alumni who would, over the subsequent decade, become Andreessen's principal long-term professional collaborators. Loudcloud was a managed-services Internet hosting company — an early entrant in what would, after the post-2006 emergence of Amazon Web Services and the subsequent cloud-computing wave, become the cloud-services industry.

Loudcloud failed. The 2000-2002 dot-com collapse destroyed the customer base. In 2002, the hosting business was sold to **EDS** for $63.5 million — a fraction of the capital invested. The remaining software business was renamed **Opsware**.

In **2007, Hewlett-Packard acquired Opsware for $1.6 billion** — the second major liquidity event of Andreessen's career. The proceeds, combined with the Netscape money and intermediate investments, became the personal capital that the 2009 founding of Andreessen Horowitz would deploy.

Between the Netscape sale (1998) and the founding of a16z (2009), Andreessen accumulated the board seats that built the network:

- **eBay** (2008-2014) — relationships to Pierre Omidyar and Meg Whitman.
- **Facebook** (2008-present) — the most consequential seat. Andreessen has been on the Meta board since 2008, making him the longest-serving non-Zuckerberg director. The relationship is one of the deepest in Silicon Valley; Andreessen has been Zuckerberg's principal external advisor across Facebook's growth from 200 million users to four billion. The seat gives him direct decision-making influence over the world's dominant social-media platform.
- **Hewlett-Packard** (2009-2014) — placed him inside HP during its worst strategic failures, including the Léo Apotheker tenure and the **failed Autonomy acquisition** (2011, $11.1 billion purchase subsequently written down by $8.8 billion for accounting fraud — the largest corporate fraud of the period, producing multiple UK criminal prosecutions).

The board pipeline built the relationships. The firm that followed operationalised them.

## V. Andreessen Horowitz (2009-present): the firm

**Andreessen Horowitz** was founded in 2009 in Menlo Park by Marc Andreessen and Ben Horowitz. Initial fund size: $300 million. The founding thesis, published in Andreessen's August 2011 *Wall Street Journal* essay "**Why Software Is Eating the World**": that the dominant pattern of the early twenty-first century would be the displacement of incumbent industries by software-based competitors, and that the principal return on capital would come from capturing the rents that displacement produced.

As a description of 2009-2026, the thesis was correct. Retail (Amazon vs. department stores), advertising (Google and Meta vs. newspapers), entertainment (Netflix vs. cable), transportation (Uber vs. taxi medallions), hospitality (Airbnb vs. hotels), banking (fintech vs. traditional banks) — all displaced. Enormous returns to the displacers. Corresponding destruction of the displaced.

The thesis also produced the consequences this book has been documenting: capital concentrated in a handful of platform monopolies, labour markets hollowed out, regulatory capture insulating the monopolies from antitrust constraint, and a political-economic environment in which the platform-monopoly capital class funds the political project the previous chapters described. "Software is eating the world" was the most accurate investment thesis of the period. It was also the ideological framing that justified the eating.

The firm's growth has tracked the broader Silicon Valley capital expansion: 2009 ($300M), 2012 ($1.5B), 2014 ($1.5B), 2016 ($1.7B), 2019 ($2.75B plus dedicated biotech funds), 2022 ($9 billion across multiple funds), 2025 (additional growth funds), and **January 2026 — $15 billion raised across five new funds**, the firm's largest single fundraising. As of March 2026, the firm manages **at least $90 billion in assets** — the largest US venture-capital firm by capital under management.

The portfolio, per a16z's own listings as of November 2025: **1,076 companies**. The firm has, per OfficeChai's calculations, backed **32 unicorns since 2020**, more than any other venture-capital firm. The portfolio includes all of the consequential AI, crypto, defence-tech, fintech, and consumer-Internet companies of the period: **Airbnb, Coinbase, Facebook (early investment), Slack, Lyft, Pinterest, GitHub, Robinhood, Stripe, Instacart, Roblox, Anduril, Helion, OpenAI, xAI, Databricks, Mistral, Character.ai, Hadrian, Castelion, Apex Space, Flock Safety, Shield AI**, and 1,050 others.

## VI. The current partner structure (May 2026)

The general partners of Andreessen Horowitz, as of May 2026, are documented on the firm's own team page and in the firm's various fund-launch press materials. The current structure is **much larger and more vertical-specialised than the founding-era structure** — the firm has, over the period 2009-2026, evolved from a small partnership of three to five people into a multi-vertical investment platform with twenty general partners and a hundred operating-staff personnel.

The current general partners:

- **Marc Andreessen** — Co-founder, senior General Partner. Personally leads the strategic direction of the firm.
- **Ben Horowitz** — Co-founder, senior General Partner. The operational co-leader; author of *The Hard Thing About Hard Things* (2014) and *What You Do Is Who You Are* (2019), two of the most-read Silicon Valley business books of the period.
- **Jeff Jordan** — General Partner. Former CEO of OpenTable; former president of PayPal; former Disney executive. Eldercare and consumer-Internet specialism.
- **Chris Dixon** — General Partner. Leads the **a16z crypto** practice — a separate fund structure with **$7+ billion across multiple vintages**. Author of *Read Write Own* (2024) — the principal published statement of the a16z-network crypto-investment doctrine. Dixon is, the firm's principal interface with the Trump administration's cryptocurrency policy, including the firm's lobbying for the GENIUS Act stablecoin legislation passed in 2025.
- **Martin Casado** — General Partner. Infrastructure and AI; former co-founder of Nicira (the software-defined-networking company acquired by VMware for $1.26 billion in 2012). Stanford PhD in computer science. The firm's principal technical-credibility appointment in infrastructure investing.
- **Andrew Chen** — General Partner. Consumer Internet; author of *The Cold Start Problem* (2021) on network-effect business models.
- **Vijay Pande** — General Partner. Bio + health practice. Stanford PhD in physical chemistry; previously a Stanford professor. The firm's principal technical-credibility appointment in biotech investing.
- **Angela Strange** — General Partner. Fintech.
- **Alex Rampell** — General Partner. Fintech. Co-founder of TrialPay.
- **David Ulevitch** — General Partner. Co-leads the **American Dynamism** practice with Katherine Boyle. Former founder of OpenDNS (sold to Cisco for $635 million in 2015). The firm's principal operational manager of the defence-and-national-security investment portfolio.
- **Katherine Boyle** — General Partner. Co-leads American Dynamism. **Previously interned at Founders Fund and is, per her own published statements, explicitly influenced by Peter Thiel's thinking**. In 2016 joined General Catalyst where she helped fund Anduril, before joining a16z. **The principal operational bridge between a16z and the Thiel-network defence portfolio**. Her portfolio includes Hadrian (manufacturing in aerospace and defense), Apex Space (satellites), Castelion (hypersonic weapons), Anduril, Shield AI, and direct investments in Palantir.
- **Erin Price-Wright** — General Partner. American Dynamism fund (with Boyle and Ulevitch).
- **Connie Chan** — General Partner. Consumer technology; Chinese tech market expertise.
- **Jennifer Li** — General Partner. Growth.
- **David George** — General Partner. Growth fund.
- **Raghu Raghuram** — General Partner; joined October 2025; former CEO of VMware (2021-2023, during the Broadcom acquisition).
- **Erik Torenberg** — General Partner; joined 2025. Founder of Product Hunt, On Deck, Village Global, Turpentine. The principal "media/network" partner. Per Andreessen's own published statement on Torenberg's appointment: "We sometimes called ourselves 'a media company that monetizes via investing.'"
- **Sarah Wang** — General Partner. Growth.

Former partners include **Margit Wennmachers** (operating partner 2010-July 2025, now emeritus — the long-time head of the firm's marketing and communications operation), Frank Chen, Lars Dalgaard, and Scott Kupor (who served as the firm's first managing partner and as Chairman of the Small Business Administration Advisory Board under the first Trump administration).

The partner structure exhibits several distinctive features compared to the conventional venture-capital firm:

1. **Vertical specialisation** — the firm operates as multiple distinct funds (crypto, biotech, growth, American Dynamism, etc.), each with its own dedicated partners and capital pool. The structure is more similar to a multi-strategy hedge fund than to a traditional partnership-based venture firm.

2. **Media and content production** — the firm operates an extensive content programme, including podcasts, newsletters, essays, and books, that is, in Andreessen's own characterisation, the principal mechanism by which the firm signals its thesis and influences the broader Silicon Valley investment environment. The Erik Torenberg appointment in 2025 was the formal acknowledgment that the media operation is a core strategic asset of the firm, not a peripheral marketing function.

3. **Political-policy operation** — the firm maintains a major Washington-DC presence, with dedicated policy staff and an active lobbying programme. The policy operation has been most active in cryptocurrency policy (the GENIUS Act), AI policy (the rollback of the Biden October 2023 AI executive order), and antitrust policy (the post-2024 cycle reduction in FTC and DOJ Antitrust enforcement).

4. **Cultural Leadership Fund** — a separate fund structure (announced 2018) intended to connect "the greatest cultural leaders to the best new technology companies." The fund's principal investments are in Black-American-led companies and Black-American-aligned cultural institutions; it operates as the firm's principal diversity-and-inclusion-facing investment vehicle while the firm's principal economic activity has remained concentrated in white-and-male-led companies and partnerships.

## VII. The American Dynamism fund and the defence-tech integration

The **American Dynamism fund** is the most operationally consequential of the a16z vertical funds for the purposes of this book's argument. Founded in 2020 and formally launched as a dedicated fund in 2022, the American Dynamism programme deploys **$600 million in dedicated capital** specifically toward "companies that support the national interest" — that is, defence, aerospace, public safety, education, housing, supply chain, industrials, and manufacturing.

The leadership of the American Dynamism programme:

- **Katherine Boyle** (co-lead). Interned at Founders Fund. Identifies Thiel's thinking as a principal influence. Joined General Catalyst in 2016 and helped fund Anduril. Moved to a16z in 2020 to co-lead American Dynamism. Her career is the integration of the Thiel defence-investment doctrine with the a16z capital base.

- **David Ulevitch** (co-lead). Former founder of OpenDNS (sold to Cisco, 2015). At a16z since 2017. The firm's principal advocate for private capital supplanting the federal government as the lead funder of defence and infrastructure R&D.

- **Erin Price-Wright** (GP). Expanded the American Dynamism portfolio.

The American Dynamism portfolio, as of November 2025, includes **50 listed companies**. The principal positions:

- **Anduril Industries** — the Thiel-network defence-technology company Chapter 11 documented. a16z is one of the principal venture investors; Boyle is a board observer or director (the specific arrangement is private). The Anduril position is the working anchor of the American Dynamism portfolio.
- **Shield AI** — AI-powered autonomous drones for military applications; founded 2015 by Brandon Tseng and Ryan Tseng. a16z is a principal investor.
- **Hadrian** — manufacturing in aerospace and defence; founded 2020. The principal "American manufacturing renaissance" position in the portfolio.
- **Apex Space** — satellite bus manufacturing; founded 2022.
- **Castelion** — hypersonic weapons; founded 2022 by former SpaceX engineers. The most operationally explicit defence-weapons-development company in the portfolio.
- **Flock Safety** — automated license-plate recognition for police and private security; founded 2017. The principal domestic-surveillance position in the portfolio.
- **Skydio** — autonomous drones; founded 2014. Dual-use civilian and military applications.
- **Varda Space Industries** — in-space manufacturing; founded 2020 by Will Bruey (former SpaceX engineer).

The American Dynamism programme's stated public mission is the "reindustrialisation" of the American economy and the "national renewal" of American manufacturing capacity. The actual operational substance is the concentration of venture-capital deployment in defence-and-surveillance technology companies, in close coordination with the post-2024 Trump administration's defence-procurement and immigration-enforcement programmes. The November 2025 announcement that JPMorgan was committing **$1.5 trillion** to a "national resilience" lending programme — the financing facility through which American Dynamism portfolio companies would scale into the federal-contract pipeline — is the structural bridge between a16z's venture bets and the broader financial-sector capital the second Trump administration has mobilised.

American Dynamism is the venture-capital arm of the new world order's defence-and-surveillance build-out. Anduril is its principal product. JPMorgan is its principal banker. The second Trump administration is its principal customer.

## VIII. The Twitter / X investment and the xAI bridge

The clearest case of a16z's coordination with the broader coalition is the firm's participation in Musk's October 2022 Twitter acquisition. Chapter 12 documented the financing structure; a16z contributed **$400 million**.

By September 2024, per *Washington Post* analysis, **a16z had lost $288 million** against that commitment. In any normal venture firm, that loss triggers internal recrimination. At a16z, it was characterised as a "strategic investment" — political alignment with Musk, not financial return, was the point.

The logic was vindicated by the **March 2025 xAI-X merger**. The merger valued the combined entity at $113 billion and converted the underwater Twitter equity into a position in the merged Musk media-and-AI vehicle at a much higher implied valuation. a16z participated in the subsequent xAI funding rounds and expanded its position.

The Twitter-to-xAI arc is a financial position justified not by its expected investment return but by its expected political-cultural effect — structurally identical to Thiel's $10 million Gawker-destruction investment. The content-moderation rollback, the 2024 election amplification of right-wing content, the DOGE restructuring: a16z treated all of it as worth $288 million in paper losses.

## IX. The Techno-Optimist Manifesto

On **16 October 2023**, Andreessen published a **5,200-word essay** on the a16z website and on Substack, titled **"The Techno-Optimist Manifesto."** It is the most explicit ideological declaration produced by any senior Silicon Valley figure in the period this book covers. It is the document against which the programme of the new world order can most directly be measured.

The essay is divided into **15 parts**: Lies; Truth; Technology; Markets; The Techno-Capital Machine; Intelligence; Energy; Abundance; Not Utopia, But Close Enough; Becoming Technological Supermen; Technological Values; The Meaning Of Life; **The Enemy**; The Future; and **the Patron Saints of Techno-Optimism**. The phrase "We believe" appears **113 times** across the essay — the rhetorical structure is explicitly that of a religious-ideological declaration, in the form of a credal statement.

The content, in verbatim quotation from the published essay:

- "**We believe everything good is downstream of growth.**"
- "**There are only three sources of growth: population growth, natural resource utilization, and technology.**"
- "**We believe the global population can quite easily expand to 50 billion people or more, and then far beyond that as we settle other planets.**"
- "**We believe a universal basic income would turn people into zoo animals to be farmed by the state. Man was not meant to be farmed; man was meant to be useful, to be productive, to be proud.**"
- "**We are not victims, we are conquerors.**"
- "**We are not primitives, cowering in fear of the lightning bolt. We are the apex predator; the lightning works for us.**"
- "**Technology is the glory of human ambition and achievement, the spearhead of progress, and the realization of our potential.**"

The "**Enemy**" section is the most operationally significant. Andreessen names, as explicit "enemies" of techno-optimism: **"social responsibility," "trust and safety," "tech ethics," "risk management," "sustainability," "ESG" (environmental-social-governance corporate-responsibility frameworks), "stakeholder capitalism," "the precautionary principle," "the limits to growth,"** and the broader institutional infrastructure that has, across the post-1970 period, attempted to constrain the externalities of corporate activity. The single direct quotation: "**Our enemy is corruption, regulatory capture, monopolies, cartels.**" The framing is structurally inconsistent — Andreessen names as enemies both the regulatory-capture mechanisms that have enabled the monopolies he benefits from and the consumer-protection and worker-protection institutions that have, however imperfectly, constrained the monopolies. The inconsistency is not accidental; it is the standard rhetorical move by which platform-monopoly capital has framed its opposition to regulation as opposition to "regulatory capture by other parties."

The "**Patron Saints of Techno-Optimism**" section names **56 individuals** as the intellectual sources of the manifesto's worldview. The list includes the conventional pro-technology intellectual figures one would expect (Edison, Westinghouse, Carnegie, Hayek, Friedman, Buckminster Fuller). It also includes several figures whose inclusion is, more politically consequential:

- **Filippo Tommaso Marinetti** (1876-1944) — the founder of the Italian Futurist movement; author of the **1909 *Futurist Manifesto***; founding collaborator of the **Italian Fascist** project; co-author with Mussolini of the *Fascist Manifesto* of 1919. The Futurist movement explicitly celebrated war, violence, "the destruction of feminism," the burning of museums and libraries, the cult of speed and machinery, and the apex-predator/conqueror rhetoric the Andreessen manifesto closely echoes. The Futurists' subsequent role as the cultural-intellectual co-founders of Italian Fascism is part of the documented historical record. **Marinetti is the first specific historical figure named in the patron-saints list.**

- **Nick Land** — British philosopher; founder of **neoreactionary thought**; intellectual co-source of the **Curtis Yarvin / Mencius Moldbug** doctrines Chapter 11 documented. Land is the bridge between the academic NRx movement (which emerged from his work at Warwick University in the 1990s) and the Andreessen-network e/acc movement (which emerged from anonymous X accounts in 2022). The presence of Land in the patron-saints list is the explicit documentation that Andreessen's manifesto is, by the author's own attribution, part of the same intellectual lineage that produced the Yarvin / Vance political project the previous chapters documented.

- **James Burnham** (1905-1987) — American political theorist; author of *The Managerial Revolution* (1941). Burnham was a Trotskyist in the 1930s who became a foundational figure of post-war American conservatism; he was a principal collaborator of William F. Buckley at *National Review* and his elite-theory political doctrines (the proposition that all political systems are, oligarchies of managerial elites) are foundational to the Yarvin-Andreessen-network intellectual programme.

- **"Beff Jezos" and "Bayeslord"** — the **anonymous X accounts** credited with founding the effective-accelerationist (e/acc) movement in 2022. Their inclusion in the patron-saints list is the explicit acknowledgment that the e/acc movement, which Andreessen has publicly aligned with, is the operational ideological framework of the manifesto. (The Beff Jezos account was subsequently revealed in late 2023 to be that of **Guillaume Verdon**, a French-Canadian physicist who founded the AI compute company Extropic; he has subsequently appeared publicly under his own name.)

The Marinetti-Land-Burnham-e/acc lineage is, by Andreessen's own attribution, the intellectual genealogy of the manifesto. Founding-fascist Futurism of 1909. Elite-theory conservatism of mid-twentieth-century Burnham. Late-twentieth-century neoreaction of Land. Contemporary e/acc. The manifesto's political position is the synthesis of those four strands. This book's argument that the new world order's senior figures operate in an ideologically continuous tradition with the historical fascist and neoreactionary movements is, in Andreessen's case, a direct quotation of his own published patron-saint list.

The manifesto produced heavy public response:

- *Fortune*, *VentureBeat*, the *San Francisco Standard* ran the standard coverage: controversial but legitimate.
- Technology critics including Tom Coates, Jenny Stefanotti, and the author of the Global Nerdy blog identified the Marinetti-Futurist-Fascist lineage explicitly and called the manifesto fascist.
- The 70+ percent of Americans who, per the November 2023 Ipsos poll, favour establishment of standards to test and ensure the safety of artificial intelligence — and the 83 percent who "do not trust the companies developing AI systems to do so responsibly" — are the population whose values the manifesto names as "enemies."

The reader who finishes this chapter and reads the manifesto in full will not need further evidence of what the project is. Andreessen wrote it down.

## X. The Atherton housing opposition: the documented contradiction

The clearest documented case of the contradiction between Andreessen's published doctrine and his actual political-economic behaviour is the August 2022 Atherton housing-opposition incident.

The town of **Atherton, California** is, by repeated *Forbes* and *Bloomberg* analyses, **the most expensive zip code in the United States**. The town has 7,200 residents; the minimum residential lot size ranges from one-third of an acre to one acre; the median home price in 2022 was $7 million; the wealthiest individuals in the contemporary Silicon Valley capital class (including Andreessen, Eric Schmidt, the Google founders, and numerous others) maintain their primary residences there.

Under California state law, every California municipality is required to plan for accommodation of additional housing units in its "Housing Element" — the planning document that designates land for residential construction. Atherton's state-mandated 2031 housing-element target was 348 additional units. The town's planning department proposed, in mid-2022, a **modest rezoning to permit construction of 130 multifamily housing units by 2031** — that is, smaller apartment-style buildings rather than the exclusively single-family-on-large-lot construction that had been the town's historical pattern.

On **25 June 2022**, Marc Andreessen and Laura Arrillaga-Andreessen submitted a public-comment email to the Atherton mayor and city council. The email, with the subject line "**IMMENSELY AGAINST multifamily development!**", was authenticated by the Atherton planning office and first reported by *The Atlantic*. The verbatim quoted opposition:

> "I am writing this letter to communicate our IMMENSE objection to the creation of multifamily overlay zones in Atherton... Please IMMEDIATELY REMOVE all multifamily overlay zoning projects from the Housing Element which will be submitted to the state in July."

The email's further justification:

> "They will massively decrease our home values, the quality of life of ourselves and our neighbors and immensely increase the noise pollution and traffic."

After the Andreessens and 300 other Atherton residents submitted mostly negative feedback, **the town abandoned the multifamily overlay plan** and submitted alternative housing-element provisions involving only townhomes and accessory dwelling units (the "in-law unit" structures permitted within existing single-family residential properties). The state-mandated affordable-housing-construction target was thereby defeated through the public-comment process the wealthy residents had dominated.

The incident is documented contradiction with Andreessen's own published doctrine. In **April 2020, Andreessen had published an essay titled "It's Time to Build"** that criticised America's inability to build housing, infrastructure, and other physical capital. The verbatim quotation:

> "We can't build nearly enough housing in our cities with surging economic potential — which results in crazily skyrocketing housing prices in places like San Francisco, making it nearly impossible for regular people to move in and take the jobs of the future."

The 2020 essay had been celebrated, across the captured-press constituency, as the principal Silicon Valley statement of the YIMBY ("Yes In My Backyard") pro-housing-construction movement. The 2022 Atherton incident is the documented case in which Andreessen, who had publicly criticised America's inability to build housing, opposed the construction of 130 housing units in his own town.

The further context: in **October 2021 — ten months before the Atherton incident — the Andreessens had paid $177 million for a 7-acre Malibu estate**, a record price for a California residential transaction. The household's principal residence in Atherton is in a comparable price range. The public-comment opposition was therefore from a couple whose **combined real-estate holdings exceeded $250 million** and whose stated objection to the proposed 130 affordable housing units was that the units would "**massively decrease our home values**" — that is, the small fraction of their quarter-billion-dollar real-estate portfolio that was concentrated in the Atherton residence.

The doctrine: America cannot build because of NIMBY opposition. The behaviour: Andreessen is the NIMBY. The published critique of regulatory obstacles to housing construction does not apply to the wealthy resident's own neighbourhood. The doctrine is the programme of the working-and-middle-class voter who cannot afford housing. The behaviour is the programme of the billionaire who does not want apartment buildings near his estate.

## XI. The 2024 political turn

Through 2022, Andreessen had been a registered Democrat and a Democratic-aligned political donor. He had given to Hillary Clinton in 2016 and to Joe Biden in 2020. His firm had, on its various boards and policy committees, included Democratic-aligned figures (including Larry Summers in the OpenAI-policy-committee adjacency).

The shift began in **late 2022** with the cultural alignment around the Musk Twitter acquisition. It accelerated through 2023 with the publication of "Why AI Will Save the World" (June 2023), the Techno-Optimist Manifesto (October 2023), and the various podcast appearances and X posts that articulated the firm's increasingly explicit right-wing alignment.

The reasons for the shift, per Andreessen's own statements in the Joe Rogan interview of October 2024 and various subsequent appearances:

- **Biden administration antitrust enforcement**. The FTC under Lina Khan and the DOJ Antitrust Division under Jonathan Kanter had, in 2023-2024, opened multiple investigations of Big Tech companies that, threatened the platform-monopoly business models that a16z's portfolio depended on.
- **Biden administration cryptocurrency enforcement**. The SEC under Gary Gensler had, in 2023-2024, brought enforcement actions against multiple a16z crypto-portfolio companies (including Coinbase). The Bankman-Fried scandal had produced increased regulatory scrutiny of the entire crypto industry — a scrutiny that a16z's $7+ billion crypto portfolio was operationally exposed to.
- **The Biden October 2023 AI executive order**. The order had, in Andreessen's framing, created regulatory uncertainty for the AI portfolio.
- **The proposed "billionaire minimum income tax."** The Biden administration's proposed unrealised-capital-gains tax on billionaires would have affected Andreessen personally.

In **July 2024**, Andreessen and Horowitz publicly endorsed Donald Trump on their podcast *The Ben & Marc Show* episode "**Why We're Voting for Trump**." The endorsement was a public signal that the broader Silicon Valley right-wing turn that had been documented through 2023 was now electoral. The endorsement was followed by similar public statements from Sacks, Sequoia's Doug Leone, and various other senior Silicon Valley figures — the documented coordination of the venture-capital sector's 2024 political turn.

In **October 2024**, Andreessen appeared on the **Joe Rogan Experience** (episode 2208, 28 October 2024) for a three-hour interview. The interview was, by various estimates, listened to by 30-40 million people; it was the principal extended Silicon Valley-political-realignment communication of the cycle. Andreessen used the interview to articulate the "**Little Tech**" policy framework — the argument that the second Trump administration should align with Silicon Valley venture-capital "little tech" (a16z and its portfolio) against the Biden-administration "Big Tech" (Microsoft, Google, Apple, Meta). The framing established Andreessen as one of the principal Silicon Valley voices for the Trump policy programme and provided the intellectual structure within which the subsequent post-inauguration policy alignment was executed.

**2024 political contributions**, per FEC records: **$4.5 million to Trump-aligned PACs**, with additional contributions to "Little Tech" advocacy organisations and specific Republican Senate and House candidates aligned with the administration's policy programme. The figure is smaller than Musk's $291 million but, given that Andreessen's principal political effect was through the Joe Rogan and similar podcast appearances and through the firm's portfolio company-level political activity rather than through direct PAC donations, the documented monetary contribution understates the political effect.

## XII. The Arrillaga inheritance and the household economic position

Andreessen's personal-investment portfolio and the active economic position of the broader Andreessen household are enhanced by his marriage to **Laura Arrillaga-Andreessen** (born 1970, San Francisco), the daughter of **John Arrillaga** (1937-2022), the most consequential Silicon Valley commercial-real-estate developer of the post-1960 period.

John Arrillaga's firm **Peery Arrillaga** developed **12 million square feet of commercial real estate across Silicon Valley** between 1965 and 2010. The Peery Arrillaga buildings include the original campuses of **Cisco, Yahoo, Sun Microsystems, Tesla, Tibco**, and dozens of other consequential Silicon Valley companies. Arrillaga was, the principal physical landlord of Silicon Valley during the period of its emergence as the world's principal technology-industry geographic concentration. His personal net worth at death in 2022 was $4 billion.

Laura Arrillaga-Andreessen is the founder and Chair of the **Stanford Center on Philanthropy and Civil Society** — the Stanford academic centre that has, over the past two decades, been the principal institutional source of the contemporary "effective philanthropy" doctrine that the Bill Gates and Mark Zuckerberg foundations have operationally adopted. The Arrillaga family has been the principal donor to Stanford University's physical-campus development; the **Arrillaga Family Sports Center, the Arrillaga Family Dining Commons, the Arrillaga Plaza**, and numerous other major campus buildings are named for the family.

Laura Arrillaga-Andreessen inherited a large portion of her father's estate. The combined Marc-and-Laura Andreessen household economic position is therefore the operational combination of:

1. Marc's Silicon Valley venture-capital and corporate-board income (his a16z partnership share, his Meta director compensation, his other board fees and investments).
2. Laura's inherited real-estate-development wealth (the large portion of the $4 billion Arrillaga family fortune that she received).
3. The household's various jointly-held real-estate holdings (the Atherton residence, the $177 million Malibu estate, and others).

The household position is the joint product of late-twentieth-century Silicon Valley commercial-real-estate wealth and contemporary venture-capital wealth. The Arrillaga buildings are the physical infrastructure within which the Andreessen Horowitz portfolio companies operate.

The standard press framing presents Andreessen's position as the legitimate product of individual technical achievement (Mosaic, Netscape) and subsequent investment activity (a16z). The framing is not entirely wrong. It does, however, omit the structural fact that Andreessen's 2006 marriage integrated his personal wealth with a much larger inherited fortune that doubled or tripled the household's economic position. The "working-class kid made good" narrative is, post-2006, structurally misleading. The Andreessen household is an inherited-and-earned-wealth dynastic structure of the same scale as the dynastic-wealth structures the previous chapters documented.

## XIII. The current position in 2026

This is what Marc Andreessen is, as of May 2026:

- The **co-founder and senior General Partner of Andreessen Horowitz**, the largest US venture-capital firm by capital under management ($90+ billion AUM as of March 2026).
- The **co-author, with Ben Horowitz, of the principal Silicon Valley venture-capital response to the Biden administration's 2023-2024 regulatory programme**, including the public endorsement of Donald Trump in July 2024, the Joe Rogan interview of October 2024, and the "Little Tech" policy framework that has subsequently structured the second Trump administration's tech-industry-relationship doctrine.
- The **author of the October 2023 Techno-Optimist Manifesto**, the explicit ideological declaration that names as "enemies" the social-responsibility, trust-and-safety, tech-ethics, and risk-management institutional functions that have constrained corporate externalities during the post-1960 period, and that identifies as its intellectual patron saints the founding-fascist Marinetti, the neoreactionary Land, and the elite-theory conservative Burnham.
- The **longest-serving non-Zuckerberg director on the Meta board** (since 2008), with continuous decision-making influence over the principal global social-media platform.
- The **co-architect of the American Dynamism investment programme** that has, since 2020, been the principal venture-capital channel for the Anduril-Shield AI-Hadrian-Castelion defence-startup wave.
- The **member of an inherited-and-earned-wealth household** whose combined Marc-Andreessen-and-Laura-Arrillaga-Andreessen economic position is the integration of late-twentieth-century Silicon Valley commercial-real-estate-development wealth and contemporary venture-capital wealth.
- The **documented public opponent of affordable housing construction in his own neighbourhood**, despite his published criticism of America's inability to build housing — the documented contradiction between his published doctrine and his actual political-economic behaviour.

The previous chapters described the regent, the executor, and the integrator. The record has described the coordinator — the figure through whose firm the principal capital channels of the new world order's investment programme run, and whose published manifesto is the most explicit ideological declaration of the project the network has been executing.

# Chapter 15: Hoffman, Sacks, Levchin — and the Completion of the PayPal Mafia

Fifteen men worked at Confinity, X.com, or the merged PayPal during the 1999-2002 period. Those fifteen men subsequently founded or led the principal companies of the post-2002 Silicon Valley wave — Tesla, SpaceX, Palantir, LinkedIn, YouTube, Yelp, Yammer, Affirm, Slide — and the venture-capital firms that funded the next generation. The network is called the **PayPal Mafia**. The preceding chapters have dossiered its four senior figures: Thiel, Musk, Altman (adjacent), and Andreessen. Three remain. **Reid Garrett Hoffman**, **David Oliver Sacks**, and **Maksymilian Rafailovych "Max" Levchin** are the second tier of the network — the figures who have received less press attention than Thiel or Musk but whose biographies complete the map of how a single company's alumni captured the contemporary technology-industry power structure.

The commitment, per the dossier standard, is that the same investigative depth applies to every named principal regardless of political alignment. Hoffman is the principal Democratic-aligned figure of the PayPal Mafia — the documented case in which the co-opted press constituency that has been critical of Thiel, Musk, Sacks, and Andreessen has been much less critical of a structurally similar figure. The record on Hoffman is not softened because his political alignment is more comfortable for that constituency. The Hoffman dossier is at the same depth and applies the same standards as the Thiel dossier. The structural question — whether the bipartisan-and-partisan presentation of the Silicon Valley capital class is a genuine ideological diversity or a portfolio-management strategy to ensure the network wins regardless of which party governs — is asked and answered.

Sacks is the **conversion of the PayPal Mafia network into direct executive-branch policy authority** over the two industries (AI, cryptocurrency) in which the network's investment portfolio is most concentrated. Trump's 5 December 2024 announcement of Sacks as "White House AI and Crypto Czar" was the working acknowledgment that the network's policy-setting authority had been formalised through executive-branch appointment.

Levchin is the technical co-founder of PayPal and the founder of Affirm — the case of the post-PayPal financial-technology entrepreneurship that has produced one of the most consequential consumer-credit transformations of the post-2008 period. He is also the case of the late-Soviet Jewish emigration to the United States that produced large fractions of the post-1991 American technology and finance industries.

The three biographies are individual; the network is collective. The analysis treats both.

## I. Reid Hoffman: origins and the progressive household

**Reid Garrett Hoffman** was born on **5 August 1967** in **Stanford, California**, in the immediate geographic and cultural environment of Stanford University. His parents — **William Parker Hoffman Jr.** and **Deanna Ruth Hoffman** — were both **lawyers active in environmental and social-justice causes**. The household was, by all available documentation, politically progressive, intellectually rigorous, and oriented toward public-interest legal practice rather than corporate law.

The Hoffman maternal grandfather was a **New Deal-era public servant**, of the generation that built the post-1933 American administrative state. The family political tradition was therefore the established progressive-Democratic-aligned American legal-professional class — distinct, in its political and cultural orientation, from the Thiel-Klaus-Friedrich-apartheid-uranium-engineer family origins, from the Musk-Errol-South-African-apartheid-engineer family origins, and from the Sacks-South-African-Jewish-emigration family origins that the previous chapters and this chapter document.

The genuinely progressive Hoffman family-of-origin is documented. The inference that progressive family origins legitimise the subsequent political-economic activity is not accepted. The Hoffman household raised one of the principal financial backers of the contemporary American Democratic Party; the same Hoffman household also raised the figure whose Microsoft board position, OpenAI involvement, LinkedIn ownership, Jeffrey Epstein fundraising relationship, and broader Silicon Valley capital-class network make him operationally indistinguishable from the figures whose more conservative political alignments have been the subject of more sympathetic captured-press coverage on the progressive side and more critical coverage on the conservative side. The political alignment of Hoffman's donations is real; the structural alignment of his economic activity is also real; the two are separate questions.

## II. The Putney School to Oxford: the credentialled formation

Hoffman attended **The Putney School** in Vermont — a progressive boarding school in the New England-progressive-education tradition (founded 1935; the Putney School's curriculum emphasises arts integration, work programmes on the school farm, and self-directed learning in the John Dewey progressive-education tradition). The Putney environment was, by the standards of the elite American secondary school of the period, the most explicitly politically progressive of the major boarding schools.

He proceeded to **Stanford University**, where he completed a BS in **Symbolic Systems and Cognitive Science** in 1990. The Symbolic Systems major was the new interdisciplinary programme at Stanford that combined computer science, linguistics, philosophy, and psychology in a single undergraduate degree. The programme produced multiple subsequent Silicon Valley figures — including Mike Krieger (Instagram co-founder) and several others — and was, in the 1980s and 1990s, the principal Stanford undergraduate pathway for students interested in the cognitive-science approach to artificial intelligence that would, in the 2010s and 2020s, become the foundational discipline of the contemporary AI industry.

Hoffman was awarded a **Marshall Scholarship** to Oxford University, where he completed an MA in philosophy at **Wolfson College** in 1993. The Marshall Scholarship is one of the principal American-British academic-exchange programmes; Marshall Scholars are selected by the British government with explicit attention to demonstrated leadership potential and likely future influence on Anglo-American relations. The Oxford philosophy training has been a recurring theme in Hoffman's subsequent writing and his public-intellectual self-presentation; he is one of the most academically credentialed of the PayPal Mafia figures.

Hoffman's intellectual influences, in his own published statements:

- The British analytic-philosophy tradition (his Oxford training).
- The **American pragmatist tradition** (Dewey, James, Peirce).
- The Stanford cognitive-science approach to artificial intelligence.
- The **network-theory approach to social systems** — the proposition that the structural properties of social networks (rather than the individual properties of agents) determine the outcomes the networks produce.

The network-theory framework has been the principal intellectual contribution Hoffman has made through his published writing (the 2012 book *The Start-Up of You*, the 2018 *Blitzscaling*, the various essays). It is the intellectual framework within which Hoffman has operated his subsequent career — and the framework that, applied to the record of Hoffman's political-economy activity, explains the structural integration of his Democratic-aligned donor activity with the broader Silicon Valley capital-class network the previous chapters have documented.

## III. SocialNet, PayPal, and the early career

Hoffman joined **Apple Computer** briefly in the mid-1990s (eWorld product management) and **Fujitsu** briefly thereafter. The early-career corporate-employment period was, by Silicon Valley standards, conventional — Hoffman did not have a notable founding-or-senior-executive position in the period before his SocialNet venture.

**SocialNet** (1997-2000) was Hoffman's first founded company. The product was an online-dating-and-social-networking platform, anticipating by six years what would become Facebook and LinkedIn. SocialNet failed commercially — the company never achieved meaningful user adoption and was, by the time Hoffman joined PayPal in 2000, operationally moribund.

In **January 2000**, Hoffman joined **PayPal** as Executive Vice President. His principal responsibilities were business-development, regulatory, and government-affairs functions — that is, he was the senior PayPal executive responsible for the regulatory strategy that permitted PayPal to operate as an online payment processor without obtaining state-by-state money-transmitter licenses for the first years of its operation, and for the government-relations strategy that enabled PayPal's eventual exit to eBay. Hoffman was not on the engineering side of PayPal (that was Levchin) or on the operations side (that was Sacks); his role was the political-regulatory-business-development side that the press histories of PayPal have often under-emphasised but that was, as important as the engineering and operations functions to the company's eventual outcome.

Hoffman received **$60 million** from the 2002 eBay acquisition. The proceeds funded the subsequent LinkedIn venture and Hoffman's broader investment activity.

## IV. LinkedIn (2002-2016): the foundation of the personal fortune

**LinkedIn** was founded in **December 2002** by Hoffman with Allen Blue, Konstantin Guericke, Eric Ly, and Jean-Luc Vaillant. The product launched in **May 2003** as a professional-social-networking platform. The founding thesis: that the network-effect dynamics of consumer-social-networking (then emerging through Friendster and the other early platforms) would apply equally to professional networking, with the difference that the professional context would produce higher-value relationships and therefore higher-margin business models.

The thesis was operationally correct. LinkedIn grew steadily through 2003-2010 to 100 million users; the **19 May 2011 IPO** opened at $45 per share and closed the first day at $94.25, valuing the company at $9 billion. The IPO was, at the time, the largest US Internet IPO since the 2008 financial crisis — the event that signalled the beginning of the post-crisis Silicon Valley capital expansion that produced the subsequent decade's investment-and-valuation environment.

Hoffman served as CEO from 2003 to 2007, then as **Executive Chairman** from 2007 to the 2016 acquisition. The CEO transition in 2007 — to **Dan Nye** initially, then to **Jeff Weiner** in 2008 — was a Silicon Valley founder voluntarily transitioning operational leadership to professional management while retaining control through the Chairman role. The transition produced the stability that the 2011 IPO and subsequent growth required.

The **June 2016 Microsoft acquisition** of LinkedIn for **$26.2 billion in cash** was, at the time, the **largest Microsoft acquisition in history**. The transaction closed in December 2016. Hoffman received **$2.7 billion** from the transaction (his 11 percent personal stake at the acquisition price) — the foundation of his current personal net worth and his subsequent political-donor capacity.

As part of the acquisition, Hoffman **joined the Microsoft board of directors**. He remained on the Microsoft board through **June 2024**, when he stepped down to focus on his political activity and his Inflection AI ventures. The Microsoft board position is, the most consequential structural position of Hoffman's biography. During his eight-year tenure (December 2016 – June 2024), Microsoft completed:

- The **$13+ billion investment in OpenAI** (initial $1B 2019, additional $10B 2023, subsequent rounds).
- The **$68.7 billion acquisition of Activision Blizzard** (announced January 2022, closed October 2023).
- The major AI-product expansion that has, by 2026, made Microsoft one of the two or three most valuable companies in the world.

Hoffman's board position gave him direct decision-making influence over Microsoft's AI investment strategy during the period of OpenAI's emergence as the principal commercial AI company. The structural significance is documented: the same individual who was on the OpenAI board (until March 2023) was simultaneously on the Microsoft board overseeing the $13 billion investment that has made Microsoft the principal commercial beneficiary of OpenAI's products. The conflict-of-interest position was, by ordinary corporate-governance standards, major. The Microsoft board resolved it by Hoffman's eventual resignation from the OpenAI board in March 2023 — a resolution that arrived three years after Microsoft's initial $1 billion OpenAI investment and eight months after the Microsoft-OpenAI investment had become the principal commercial-AI partnership in the world.

## V. Greylock Partners (2009-present)

Hoffman joined **Greylock Partners** as a General Partner in **2009**. Greylock is one of the oldest and most consequential American venture-capital firms — founded in 1965 by Dan Gregory and Bill Elfers in Boston, subsequently expanded to Silicon Valley, with $3.5 billion in committed capital across its various funds at the time of Hoffman's joining. Greylock has, throughout its sixty-one-year history, been one of the principal patient-capital sources of the American technology industry.

Hoffman has been Greylock's principal interface with the consumer-Internet and social-networking investment categories. The Greylock portfolio under Hoffman's influence includes **Airbnb** (Greylock was an early lead investor; Hoffman is on the Airbnb board), **Facebook** (Greylock made a major pre-IPO investment), **Dropbox**, **Workday**, **Roblox**, **Coinbase**, **Discord**, **Figma**, and 200 other companies. The portfolio is, in aggregate, comparable in scale to the Andreessen Horowitz portfolio though much less politically active.

The structural position is documented. Hoffman is, through his Greylock partnership and through his various board positions (Microsoft 2016-2024, OpenAI 2015-2023, Airbnb, several others), one of the most operationally consequential figures in the contemporary American venture-capital environment. The captured-press framing has generally presented Greylock as the more "thoughtful" or "values-aligned" alternative to the more politically explicit Andreessen Horowitz; the documented operational record is that the two firms are, in their portfolio composition and their investment thesis, similar — the principal distinguishing characteristic is the more pronounced public political alignment of a16z's senior partners.

## VI. Inflection AI and the Microsoft "acqui-hire"

Hoffman co-founded **Inflection AI** in 2022 with **Mustafa Suleyman** (a co-founder of DeepMind, which Google had acquired in 2014) and **Karén Simonyan** (a senior DeepMind researcher). Inflection's principal product was **Pi**, a consumer-facing AI assistant positioned as a more "personal" alternative to ChatGPT. The company raised $1.5 billion in venture funding across 2022-2023 from investors including Microsoft, Reid Hoffman personally, Bill Gates personally, Eric Schmidt, and the various Silicon Valley AI-focused funds.

In **March 2024**, **Microsoft "acquired" Inflection AI in an unusual transaction structure**. The transaction involved hiring most of Inflection's technical staff (including Suleyman, who became CEO of Microsoft AI) and licensing Inflection's technology, without acquiring the corporate entity itself. Microsoft paid **$650 million** for the licensing and personnel transfer.

The transaction structure was, in the assessment of multiple antitrust regulators, **specifically designed to avoid antitrust review**. A conventional acquisition of Inflection AI by Microsoft — Microsoft acquiring the corporate entity, the founders selling their equity, the assets transferring as a unit — would have been subject to FTC and European Commission Merger Review. The licensing-plus-acqui-hire structure permitted Microsoft to obtain the economic benefits of the acquisition (the engineering talent, the technology) without triggering the regulatory review process. The **UK Competition and Markets Authority opened an investigation** of the transaction in April 2024 specifically on these grounds; the FTC opened a similar review.

Hoffman's role in the Inflection-Microsoft transaction is documented as serious: he was a co-founder of the company being "acquired" and simultaneously a director of the company doing the "acquiring." The conflict of interest was, in the standard captured-press framing, presented as a technicality that the Microsoft board's recusal process had managed. The observation is that **the principal structural integration of the venture-capital-funded AI sector with the principal commercial-AI Microsoft platform was executed in 2024 through a transaction in which Reid Hoffman was simultaneously a buyer and a seller**.

## VII. Hoffman and Jeffrey Epstein: the public record

The most operationally significant aspect of Hoffman's biography for this book's purposes is his documented relationship with **Jeffrey Epstein**, the convicted-sex-offender financier who died in federal custody on 10 August 2019.

The standard, per the dossier-treatment guidance, is to document the relationship at the depth the public record permits, in the same form applied to other principals' political-economy entanglements. The Epstein relationship is, in Hoffman's biography, structurally similar to the Errol-Musk-apartheid-mining-and-stepdaughter relationship in the Musk biography, the Thiel-Yarvin-funding relationship in the Thiel biography, and the Sam-Annie-Altman family-allegations situation in the Altman biography. Each is a documented aspect of the subject's personal-economic-political environment that the press has, in the standard pattern, either minimised or covered selectively. All of them are documented at the same depth.

The documented record on Hoffman and Epstein:

**The chronology**:

- **July 2013**: Per the **MIT Goodwin Procter report** (commissioned by MIT and released January 2020 after the September 2019 disclosures), Hoffman was a **member of the MIT Media Lab Advisory Council** during the period in which Joi Ito was the Media Lab's director and was soliciting donations from Epstein. The Goodwin Procter report documents a July 2013 Epstein visit to the MIT campus during which Ito escorted Epstein and met with Professor Ed Boyden and Professor Neil Gershenfeld; Hoffman was a member of the Advisory Council during this period.

- **2014**: Hoffman **visited Epstein's private island, Little St. James**, with Joi Ito. The visit is documented in the *Wall Street Journal*'s reporting and in Ito's own subsequent statement to the *Journal*, in which Ito described the trip as a "fundraising event" at his request. Documents subsequently obtained by the press indicated that Hoffman and Ito were planning a subsequent visit to the island later in 2014, with plans to travel from Palm Beach to the island for a weekend.

- **August 2015**: Hoffman **hosted a dinner in Palo Alto** in honour of MIT neuroscientist Ed Boyden. The dinner was attended by **Jeffrey Epstein, Elon Musk, Mark Zuckerberg, Peter Thiel, and Joi Ito**. The observation is that the dinner brought together, in one room, four of the principal figures whose biographies this book has documented as senior coalition partners of the new world order — Musk, Zuckerberg, Thiel, Hoffman — with Epstein in attendance. The dinner was first reported by *Vanity Fair* in July 2019, in the period of the broader Epstein-network-revelation cycle that followed Epstein's 6 July 2019 federal arrest.

- **July 2016**: Per the Goodwin Procter report, **Ito sought advice from Hoffman about whether to allow Epstein to attend a Media Lab conference** with "lots of people" who may "see him and maybe know he's involved." The report does not disclose Hoffman's advice. The Media Lab subsequently permitted Epstein's attendance.

- **16 March 2017**: Epstein wrote to Hoffman about creating a "donor advised fund." The verbatim Epstein text (preserved with original errors): "**a HUGE donor advised fund is an elegant solution to the cuts trump proposes to what some consider critical programs.**" The email was released as part of the November 2025 House Oversight Committee document release. The content is that, two months after the first Trump administration's inauguration, Epstein was proposing to Hoffman the creation of a major donor-advised-fund mechanism to offset proposed Trump-administration cuts to federal programs. The proposal — from a convicted sex offender to a Democratic-aligned billionaire, in the period when the broader Democratic-aligned philanthropic infrastructure was reorganising in response to the first Trump administration — is one of the more operationally consequential elements of the evidence.

- **2018**: Additional fundraising meetings with Epstein, per Hoffman's own subsequent corrections to his 2019 statement. The specific dates and content of those meetings have not been publicly disclosed.

**The "Disobedience Award" controversy**:

The MIT Media Lab gave **Jeffrey Epstein an "honorary orb"** in 2017 — a replica of the Lab's "Disobedience Award." The Disobedience Award was funded principally by Hoffman; Hoffman himself received an orb (in his capacity as the Award's funder); Epstein received one; **the following year, the same award went to the #MeToo movement founders** including Tarana Burke. The juxtaposition is in the public record and was extensively reported by *The Daily Beast*, *Vanity Fair*, and *Axios* in 2019.

The pattern is documented: the same individual (Hoffman) who funded the award that, in 2018, recognised the #MeToo movement leaders was, in 2017, the funder of the institution that gave the same award to one of the most documented sex-offender figures in the contemporary American record. The internal Media Lab communications produced by the Goodwin Procter report — including Ito's statement to a staffer that "there definitely is a lot of risk and I think about it a lot" — establish that the institution knew Epstein's status when it gave him the award. Hoffman was, on documented, a member of the Advisory Council during the period; whether he was specifically aware of the award decision is, on the available record, disputed.

**Hoffman's public statements**:

- **September 2019** (initial public response, to *Axios*): "My few interactions with Jeffrey Epstein came at the request of Joi Ito, for the purposes of fundraising for the MIT Media Lab. Prior to these interactions, I was told by Joi that Epstein had cleared the MIT vetting process, which was the basis for my participation. **My last interaction with Epstein was in 2015**. Still, by agreeing to participate in any fundraising activity where Epstein was present, **I helped to repair his reputation and perpetuate injustice**. For this, I am deeply regretful."

- **November 2025** (correction post on X, after the House Oversight Committee document release): "I only knew Jeffrey Epstein because of a fundraising relationship with MIT, which I very much regret. **In 2019 I told Axios the last meeting I had with him was in 2015 but I was mistaken**, as according to calendar entries I have become aware there were additional fundraising meetings in 2016 and 2018. These meetings were all coordinated by Joi Ito, then director of the MIT Media Lab."

- **November 2025** (continued): "Trump should release all of the Epstein files: every person and every document in the files. I want this complete release because it will show that the calls for baseless investigations of me are nothing more than political persecution and slander. **I was never a client of Epstein's** and never had any engagement with him other than fundraising for MIT."

**The Trump-administration investigation**:

After the November 2025 Epstein-files release, Trump publicly called for an investigation of Hoffman and called him a "**sleazebag**" in a Truth Social post. The Trump-administration DOJ subsequently opened an investigation. The investigation's direction is, as of May 2026, unclear — the politically motivated investigation of the principal Democratic political donor of the previous cycle by the administration he opposed is of the same character as the Trump-administration investigations of E. Jean Carroll (which Hoffman had funded), of Hillary Clinton (the "lock her up" framework), and of multiple other Trump-administration political opponents. The outcome of the investigation is not predicted at this stage; the documented facts on Hoffman's Epstein relationship and the administration's response are recorded.

**The core pattern**:

The underlying structural reality, beyond the specific Hoffman-Epstein facts, shows that the senior Silicon Valley capital-class network of the period included, as one of its operational fundraising-and-social structures, the **Jeffrey Epstein fundraising relationship with MIT Media Lab** — a relationship in which the principal Silicon Valley figures (Hoffman, Musk, Zuckerberg, Thiel, Bill Gates, the Sergey Brin / Eric Schmidt circle, Reid Hoffman) were systematically integrated through the Joi Ito intermediary and through the broader fundraising-and-personal-network mechanism Epstein operated. The Hoffman-Epstein relationship is not isolated. It is one element of a network whose other elements include the documented Bill Gates relationship with Epstein (which Chapter 19 will examine in detail), the documented Elon Musk relationship (which the August 2015 dinner partially documents), and the broader network of Silicon Valley figures whose names have appeared in the Epstein flight logs, the Epstein little-black-book, and the November 2025 House Oversight Committee document release.

No specific criminal conduct is alleged against any specific named individual beyond what the public record permits. The relationships, the meetings, the fundraising activity, and the documented institutional involvement are recorded at the depth the public record permits. The Hoffman-Epstein record is the most documented case in the contemporary Silicon Valley record; the broader pattern is the more operationally consequential observation.

## VIII. Hoffman's political activity: the bipartisan-and-partisan integration

Hoffman has been, since 2016, the **principal Democratic-aligned political donor of the Silicon Valley capital class**. The documented record of his political activity is and is the recorded case in which the book's argument about the bipartisan-and-partisan structure of the Silicon Valley capital class must be made most explicitly.

**The documented donations**:

- **2016 cycle**: Roughly **$7 million** in documented contributions to Democratic candidates and PACs.
- **2018 cycle**: Roughly **$20 million** to Democratic candidates, with particular focus on House and Senate flip-target races.
- **2020 cycle**: Roughly **$26 million in personal contributions**; additional $35 million through his funded organisations (Investing in US, AlphaTexa) and PACs.
- **2022 cycle**: Roughly **$17 million** in documented contributions.
- **2024 cycle**: Roughly **$20 million in personal contributions** to Kamala Harris and Democratic-aligned PACs. Hoffman was, the **single largest individual Democratic donor of the 2024 cycle**, exceeding the contributions of George Soros (who reduced his political-donation activity in 2024 in favour of generational transition to his son Alex Soros).

**Lawsuit funding**:

Hoffman has funded multiple lawsuits against Donald Trump, most consequentially the **E. Jean Carroll defamation case** that produced the **$83.3 million January 2024 verdict against Trump**. The funding was disclosed by Hoffman publicly through his lawyer; the substance is that Carroll's litigation, which produced both the May 2023 sexual-abuse verdict and the January 2024 defamation verdict, was financed by Hoffman's personal contributions to the litigation-funding mechanism. The Carroll case is, in retrospect, one of the most consequential pieces of pre-2024-election litigation against Trump; it produced the public record of a federal jury finding that Trump had sexually abused Carroll and had subsequently defamed her, and the $83.3 million damages award has been a continuing element of the Trump-litigation portfolio that the second Trump administration has been working to neutralise.

**The "Martyr" comment**:

In a 2024 interview, Hoffman made a comment that was widely quoted as expressing a wish that he had "made President Trump an actual martyr." Hoffman subsequently apologized: "I definitely regret having echoed the word martyr, which was pitched to me from the audience, and I echoed it." The comment was made in the period of intense political polarisation following the July 2024 Butler, Pennsylvania assassination attempt on Trump; it was, in the captured-press framing, an embarrassment to the Democratic-donor coalition. Hoffman is the principal donor; his stray comments are read carefully.

**The structural integration question**:

The book's argument about the bipartisan-and-partisan structure of the Silicon Valley capital class can be tested against the Hoffman record. The hypotheses:

**Hypothesis 1 (the co-opted press Democratic framing)**: Hoffman is the genuine Democratic Silicon Valley billionaire, operating in opposition to the right-wing Thiel-Musk-Sacks-Andreessen network. His political activity is principled opposition to the Trump-administration project.

**Hypothesis 2 (the press right-wing framing)**: Hoffman is a leftist saboteur using his Microsoft-LinkedIn-OpenAI capital to fund left-wing causes against the legitimate technology-industry political agenda. His political activity is destructive partisanship.

**Hypothesis 3 (the book's structural argument)**: Hoffman is the **portfolio-management Democratic node of an integrated Silicon Valley capital class** whose senior figures, regardless of their public partisan alignment, are united by their economic interest in the AI-and-platform-monopoly capital expansion the book has been documenting. The bipartisan donor structure ensures the network wins regardless of which party governs. Hoffman's specific Democratic donations are real; his structural participation in the Microsoft-OpenAI-Inflection-Greylock-Airbnb capital network is also real. The two are, complementary rather than contradictory.

The documented record supports Hypothesis 3 more strongly than Hypothesis 1 or Hypothesis 2. The structural features the documented record establishes:

- Hoffman's Microsoft board position 2016-2024 was the active mechanism by which the principal Democratic-aligned Silicon Valley billionaire was integrated with the principal commercial-AI partnership of the period. The OpenAI investment Microsoft made was, in terms, the same investment that Andreessen Horowitz, Sequoia, Founders Fund, and the other right-aligned firms participated in. The investment thesis was shared. The political alignment was different. The economic outcome was the same.
- Hoffman's funding of the E. Jean Carroll litigation was damaging to Trump personally but did not threaten the broader new-world-order capital programme. The Carroll case produced legal damages against Trump as an individual; it did not, threaten the AI-infrastructure capital build-out, the Stargate project, the DOGE federal restructuring, or any of the other policy outcomes the second Trump administration has executed.
- Hoffman's August 2015 Palo Alto dinner with Epstein, Musk, Zuckerberg, Thiel, and Ito is the documented case in which the Democratic-aligned Silicon Valley billionaire was, in the same room with the right-aligned billionaires, in the social-fundraising environment that the broader network operated through. The dinner is the working documentation that the partisan-Democratic and partisan-Republican Silicon Valley billionaires participate in the same fundraising and social networks regardless of their public partisan alignment.

The book's structural argument is therefore that the Hoffman-Democratic-donor activity is real but limited in its economic effect, and that the Hoffman-broader-Silicon-Valley-capital-network participation is the more operationally consequential aspect of his biography. Hoffman is, in this framing, the operational case of the bipartisan-and-partisan integration the book has been describing — the case in which the Democratic-aligned partisan activity coexists with the economic integration with the same capital class that the right-aligned partisan activity also represents. The economic programme is unified. The partisan presentation is divided. The division is, in the book's argument, the mechanism by which the capital class ensures its programme proceeds regardless of which party governs.

## IX. Hoffman's current position (May 2026)

Net worth (Forbes 2026 estimate): **$2.7 billion**.

Active positions: Greylock Partner; principal Democratic-aligned Silicon Valley political donor; subject of the Trump-administration DOJ investigation announced November 2025 in response to the Epstein-files release.

Recently departed positions: Microsoft Board (June 2024); OpenAI Board (March 2023); Inflection AI (sold to Microsoft March 2024).

Public writing: Author or co-author of *The Start-Up of You* (2012, with Ben Casnocha), *The Alliance* (2014), *Blitzscaling* (2018, with Chris Yeh), *Masters of Scale* (2021), *Impromptu: Amplifying Our Humanity Through AI* (2023, with GPT-4), *Superagency* (2025, with Greg Beato).

Hoffman is, in 2026, the most credentialed and most publicly intellectualised of the senior Silicon Valley capital-class figures. The combination of the Oxford philosophy training, the published book record, the Microsoft-LinkedIn-OpenAI corporate-board record, and the Democratic-donor political record produces, in the captured-press framing, a sympathetic figure of the "thoughtful billionaire" type.
