The documented record establishes that the same figure is, structurally integrated with the same capital-class network whose right-aligned figures have been the principal subjects of the book's preceding chapters.

## X. David Sacks: origins and the Stanford Review

**David Oliver Sacks** was born on **25 May 1972 in Cape Town, South Africa**, into a Jewish family. The family emigrated to **Tennessee** when David was five years old (1977) — that is, during the apartheid period, but for the purpose of leaving the apartheid environment rather than for the purpose of seeking the apartheid environment (the Haldeman family's documented emigration pattern, Chapter 12).

Sacks was raised in Memphis. He attended **Memphis University School**, a private K-12 institution, and proceeded to **Stanford University** for his undergraduate education.

At Stanford, Sacks was **co-editor of *The Stanford Review*** with **Peter Thiel** — the campus right-wing publication that Thiel had founded in 1987 with Olin Foundation / Collegiate Network funding and that Chapter 11 documented as the foundational institution of Thiel's political-intellectual formation. The Sacks-Thiel relationship at the Stanford Review is the originating relationship of Sacks's subsequent career. The two men have been close professional collaborators across thirty-five years.

Sacks graduated from Stanford in 1994 with a BA in Economics. He proceeded to the **University of Chicago Law School**, where he completed a JD in 1998.

## XI. *The Diversity Myth* (1995) — the joint Thiel-Sacks publication

In 1995, Sacks co-authored with Peter Thiel the book ***The Diversity Myth: Multiculturalism and the Politics of Intolerance at Stanford***, published by the **Independent Institute** (the libertarian think tank that has been one of the principal pro-deregulation think tanks of the post-1986 period).

Chapter 11 documented *The Diversity Myth*'s content in detail. The relevant aspects for Sacks's biography:

- The book is the principal early-career publication of both Thiel and Sacks. It is, the joint statement of their political-intellectual position at the moment of Sacks's law-school entry and Thiel's law-school graduation.
- The book defended the **Keith Rabois "Faggot! Hope you die of AIDS!" episode** (Rabois had shouted these words at a Stanford resident assistant's home in 1992) as legitimate political speech protected by the principle of academic freedom.
- The book contained the documented **date-rape minimisation passages** that characterised reported assaults as "regretted seductions" — the position that the increase in reported sexual-assault incidents on American campuses in the early 1990s was the product of expanded definition of "assault" rather than of the underlying conduct.
- The book is, the joint statement of the Thiel-Sacks political-intellectual position on the principal cultural controversies of the early 1990s — affirmative action, multiculturalism, sexual-conduct adjudication, free speech, and the broader campus-culture wars that produced the political alignments of the subsequent thirty years.

**Sacks has, in his subsequent public statements, never retracted the content of *The Diversity Myth***. He has, in occasional interviews, expressed regret about specific phrasings while defending the book's broader political thesis. The book remains in print; it is sold by the Independent Institute and by Amazon; it is read by the contemporary right-aligned campus-political-organisation network.

## XII. Sacks at PayPal and Yammer

Sacks joined Confinity in 1999. He became **Chief Operating Officer of PayPal** in the merged company. He was, the principal product and operations executive of PayPal under Thiel — that is, the executive who actually ran the day-to-day operations of the company while Thiel was its CEO and public face.

Sacks received **$30 million** from the 2002 eBay acquisition. The proceeds funded his subsequent ventures.

After PayPal, Sacks produced and financed the political satire **Thank You for Smoking**, which premiered at the 2005 Toronto International Film Festival. The film was a tobacco-industry satire that was, in its political content, sympathetic to the libertarian-anti-regulatory position. It was acquired by Twentieth Century Fox for theatrical release in 2006 and was nominated for two Golden Globes including Best Motion Picture (Comedy or Musical).

In **2006**, Sacks founded **Geni.com** (a genealogy website). The company was sold to MyHeritage in 2012.

In **2008**, Sacks founded **Yammer**, a workplace social-networking platform. The product launched at the TechCrunch50 conference, where it won the Grand Prize.

**June 2012**: Microsoft acquired Yammer for **$1.2 billion**. Sacks received **$200 million personally** from the transaction.

**2016**: Sacks served as **interim CEO of Zenefits for ten months**, following the compliance scandal that forced founder Parker Conrad's resignation. Zenefits had been selling insurance products without proper state licensing for multiple years; the SEC and multiple state insurance commissioners imposed heavy fines. Sacks oversaw the company through the regulatory remediation. The Zenefits experience is documented as one of the principal Sacks operational-executive episodes of the period.

## XIII. Craft Ventures and the network position (2017-present)

In **2017**, Sacks co-founded **Craft Ventures** with Bill Lee. The firm has $3.5 billion AUM as of 2025. The portfolio includes Affirm (the Levchin company), Airbnb, Bird, Cameo, ClickUp, Cloudflare, Eventbrite, Houzz, Reddit, Robinhood, SpaceX (early position), Substack, ThredUp, **xAI** (the Musk AI company), and **dYdX, BitGo, Bitwise** (the crypto positions that have been relevant to Sacks's subsequent policy advocacy).

Sacks has been **Chairman of Yelp** for an extended period (2008-2018) — the case of the post-PayPal-Mafia investment pattern in which multiple network members (Levchin, Sacks, Hoffman) jointly held leadership positions in the same portfolio companies.

Sacks's angel-investment portfolio includes Facebook, Uber, SpaceX, **Palantir Technologies**, and Airbnb — that is, the same set of companies that the other PayPal Mafia figures hold positions in. The structural integration is documented and uncontroversial as a description of the period's investment pattern.

## XIV. The All-In podcast and the media platform

In 2020, Sacks began co-hosting the **All-In podcast** with **Chamath Palihapitiya** (Social Capital founder), **Jason Calacanis** (early Uber investor and Launch Festival founder), and **David Friedberg** (The Production Board founder). The podcast has become, since 2022, one of the most-listened-to business-and-politics podcasts in the United States — variously estimated at 1-2 million weekly listeners by 2024.

The All-In podcast is the principal media platform through which the Silicon Valley right-wing political realignment of the 2022-2024 period was communicated to the broader Silicon Valley audience. The content has covered AI policy, cryptocurrency policy, antitrust enforcement, the Russia-Ukraine war (with Sacks's anti-aid position), the 2024 election (with all four hosts endorsing Trump in the final months), and the broader political-economic alignment of the Silicon Valley capital class with the second Trump administration.

The All-In hosts have been, in their public statements, one of the most operationally consequential coordinated voices of the post-2022 Silicon Valley political turn. The podcast's "All-In Summit" conferences (held annually since 2022) have featured speakers including Elon Musk, Larry Summers, Joe Rogan, Ron DeSantis, Donald Trump (during the 2024 cycle), and most of the other senior figures of the new world order coalition the book has been documenting.

## XV. The 2024 political activity

Sacks's 2024 political activity was extensive and operationally consequential:

- **May 2023**: Moderated the **Ron DeSantis presidential campaign announcement** on Twitter Spaces — the announcement that produced Twitter's technical failure during the high-profile event. Donated $50,000 to the DeSantis campaign.
- **June 2023**: Hosted a $10,000-per-plate fundraiser for **Robert F. Kennedy Jr.** (then running as a Democratic and subsequently Independent presidential candidate before joining the Trump campaign in August 2024).
- **June 2024**: Hosted a **fundraiser for Donald Trump at his San Francisco home that raised $12 million** — one of the largest single Silicon Valley fundraising events of the 2024 cycle. The event was, the public moment at which the broader Silicon Valley right-wing turn became electorally explicit.
- **July 2024**: Speaker at the **Republican National Convention**. Used the speech to articulate the position that the United States had **"provoked" Russia to invade Ukraine** — a position that placed him operationally aligned with the broader new-world-order-coalition position on the war.
- **November 2024**: Voted for Trump; was one of the principal Silicon Valley voices articulating the right-wing realignment.

The political activity established Sacks as one of the most operationally consequential pro-Trump Silicon Valley figures of the 2024 cycle. The subsequent White House appointment was the formal acknowledgment of that role.

## XVI. The White House AI and Crypto Czar appointment (December 2024 – March 2026)

On **5 December 2024**, President-elect Trump announced David Sacks's appointment as **White House AI and Crypto Czar** — a newly created position with explicit responsibility for the legal framework governing both industries. Trump's verbatim statement: Sacks "will guide policy for the Administration in Artificial Intelligence and Cryptocurrency, two areas critical to the future of American competitiveness. David will focus on making America the clear global leader in both areas."

Trump also named Sacks to **lead the President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology (PCAST)** — the principal federal advisory body on science and technology policy.

The appointment was, the **conversion of the PayPal Mafia network into direct executive-branch policy authority** over the two industries (AI, cryptocurrency) in which the network's investment portfolio is most concentrated. Sacks's portfolio at Craft Ventures included major positions in both AI companies (xAI) and crypto companies (BitGo, Bitwise, dYdX, Solana exposure); his policy authority extended over the regulatory frameworks governing those companies.

The conflict-of-interest concerns were extensively documented at the time. Sacks remained a **Craft Ventures General Partner during his White House tenure** — that is, he continued to make investments in companies that would benefit from the policies he was setting. The Trump administration's response was that Sacks's appointment was a "Special Government Employee" (SGE) designation (the same statutory category Musk used for DOGE) that did not require Senate confirmation or comprehensive financial-disclosure compliance.

The policy outcomes of Sacks's tenure:

- **Reversal of the Biden October 2023 AI executive order** in January 2025. The reversal eliminated the federal reporting requirements for large AI training runs that had been the principal regulatory restraint on the AI industry's expansion.
- **The GENIUS Act** stablecoin legislation, passed by Congress in summer 2025 with Sacks-aligned policy provisions. The Act created the federal regulatory framework for stablecoins that the Coinbase-Circle-Tether industry had lobbied for.
- **The "Crypto Ball"** held in Washington following Trump's inauguration. Sacks declared at the event: "**The war on crypto is over**." The statement was the active acknowledgment that the SEC's prior enforcement actions against crypto companies (under Gary Gensler's chairmanship) were being terminated.
- **Reduction of FTC and DOJ Antitrust enforcement** against Big Tech companies. The Khan-Kanter antitrust programme of the Biden administration was dismantled.

Sacks **stepped down in March 2026** after his 130-day SGE statutory maximum (with extensions) was exhausted. The administration's principal AI-and-crypto policy programme was, by that point, established; Sacks's continuing influence has been through the All-In podcast, Craft Ventures, and his continuing relationship with the administration.

## XVII. Sacks's current position (May 2026)

Net worth (Forbes 2026 estimate): **$2 billion**.

Active positions: Craft Ventures General Partner; All-In podcast co-host; continuing informal Trump-administration policy advisor.

Former positions: White House AI and Crypto Czar (December 2024 – March 2026); PCAST Chair.

## XVIII. Max Levchin: Kyiv, the emigration, and the technical foundation

**Maksymilian Rafailovych "Max" Levchin** was born on **11 July 1975 in Kyiv, Ukrainian SSR** (then part of the Soviet Union). His father, **Rafail Levchin**, was a poet and playwright; his mother, **Elvina Zlotnikova**, a theoretical physicist. The family was Jewish.

In **1991, when Max was sixteen years old**, the Levchin family emigrated to **Chicago, Illinois under political asylum on grounds of religious persecution**. The family was part of the late-Soviet Jewish emigration to the United States — the cohort that, in the period 1989-1995, produced the migration of 200,000 Soviet Jews to the United States and subsequent contributions to the American technology, finance, and academic industries.

Levchin attended **Mather High School** (Chicago public high school) for his final two years of secondary education. He graduated in 1993. He proceeded to the **University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign**, the same university Marc Andreessen had attended (Chapter 14). Levchin completed a BS in Computer Science in 1997.

At Illinois, Levchin met **Luke Nosek** — the figure who would, in the post-Illinois period, introduce him to Peter Thiel and produce the founding of PayPal. The Levchin-Nosek-Thiel triangle is one of the documented founding relationships of the contemporary Silicon Valley technology industry.

## XIX. Confinity / PayPal and the technical architecture

After graduation, Levchin moved to Silicon Valley. He co-founded **SponsorNet New Media** in 1995 (an undergraduate venture that did not achieve major commercial success) and various other early ventures. In **1998**, he co-founded **Fieldlink** (renamed **Confinity** in 1999) with **Peter Thiel** and **Luke Nosek**.

Levchin served as **Chief Technology Officer of PayPal** from the Confinity founding through the 2002 eBay acquisition. He was the principal technical architect of the company. The technical contributions:

- **PayPal's anti-fraud systems**, which were, in the period 2000-2002, the principal commercial application of machine-learning techniques to financial-transaction security. The systems detected and prevented the credit-card-fraud schemes that, in PayPal's first two years of operation, threatened to make the company commercially unviable.
- **The Gausebeck-Levchin test** — one of the first commercial implementations of a CAPTCHA (Completely Automated Public Turing test to tell Computers and Humans Apart) challenge-response system. The technology has subsequently become the standard mechanism for distinguishing human users from automated systems on Internet platforms worldwide.

Levchin's technical contributions are, the most foundational of any PayPal Mafia figure. The other principals (Thiel, Musk, Sacks, Hoffman) contributed business-development, financial, and operational leadership; Levchin contributed the technical systems without which the company would not have functioned.

Levchin received **$34 million** from the 2002 eBay acquisition.

## XX. Slide, Affirm, and the post-PayPal trajectory

In **2004**, Levchin founded **Slide** — a multimedia social-media application that operated as one of the principal early Facebook-application companies. **Slide was acquired by Google in August 2010 for $182 million**. Levchin received $40-50 million personally. Google shut down Slide in August 2011 — the outcome was that Levchin had been paid for the asset but the product itself was discontinued, a pattern common in the period's acqui-hire transactions.

In **2012**, Levchin founded **HVF (Hard, Valuable, Fun)** — his San Francisco-based innovation lab. Two principal companies launched from HVF:

- **Glow** (2013) — fertility-tracking application for couples trying to conceive. Levchin is Chairman.
- **Affirm** (2012/2014) — the principal Levchin company.

**Affirm** is a **buy-now-pay-later (BNPL) consumer-credit lender**. The product provides point-of-sale installment loans for online purchases — consumers can finance individual purchases (typically of expensive direct-to-consumer brand products: Peloton bikes, Reverb guitars, Casper mattresses, the various consumer-brand products that emerged in the period) with installment payments over 3, 6, 12, or 24 months. Interest rates range from 0% (subsidised by the merchant) to 30% APR.

The principal Affirm policy controversy is that the BNPL product structure permits consumers to take on heavy debt **without the credit-card regulatory framework that would otherwise apply**. The product has been particularly successful with younger consumers who do not have heavy credit-card debt and who use Affirm as their principal consumer-credit mechanism. The product has been criticised by consumer-protection advocates as a regulatory-arbitrage instrument that permits the issuance of consumer credit outside the standard regulatory framework. The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau under the Biden administration had been preparing enforcement action against the BNPL industry generally and Affirm specifically; the Bureau's operational capacity was much reduced by the DOGE programme of 2025 (Chapter 12) and the enforcement action has not, as of May 2026, been pursued.

**Affirm IPO 13 January 2021**: opening price $90, closed first day at $97. The IPO valued Affirm at $24 billion. Levchin's personal stake at IPO was $1.1 billion; subsequent share-price appreciation produced a peak valuation of $2.5 billion in the post-IPO period.

Affirm's post-IPO performance has been volatile. By 2026 the company is valued at $20 billion.

## XXI. Levchin's other positions and the Yelp chairmanship

Levchin was an early investor in **Yelp** and served as **Chairman of Yelp** for an extended period (2008-2018). The Yelp chairmanship was the post-PayPal-Mafia investment pattern: Levchin, Sacks (also Yelp Chairman in part of the period), Hoffman (early investor), and other PayPal alumni jointly held positions in the same portfolio companies.

Levchin became Yelp's largest shareholder in 2012.

His angel-investment portfolio includes Evernote, Pinterest, and various other consumer-Internet companies.

## XXII. Levchin's political activity and the Ukraine position

Levchin has, throughout his career, been **much less politically active than the other PayPal Mafia principals**. He has not been a major donor to either party. He has not endorsed presidential candidates in the public manner that Hoffman, Sacks, Musk, and Thiel have.

The one political position Levchin has taken is on the **Russian invasion of Ukraine**. As a Ukrainian-Jewish immigrant whose family was forced to emigrate from the Soviet Union under political asylum, Levchin has been publicly supportive of Ukrainian self-defence against the Russian invasion. The position has placed him in **operational disagreement with David Sacks's anti-Ukraine-aid position** — that is, the two senior PayPal Mafia figures whose Ukrainian-and-South-African origins inform their respective positions on the war are on opposite sides of the policy question.

The Levchin-Sacks disagreement on Ukraine is documented and is one of the most public internal disputes within the PayPal Mafia network of the post-2022 period. It does not appear to have affected the network's joint investment or business activity; the structural integration of the network operates separately from the partisan-policy disagreements among its members.

## XXIII. Levchin's current position (May 2026)

Net worth (Forbes 2026 estimate): **$2 billion** (down from a peak of $2.5 billion at the Affirm post-IPO high).

Active positions: Affirm Founder and CEO; Glow Chairman; HVF principal.

Levchin is, in 2026, the most technically credentialed and least politically active of the senior PayPal Mafia figures. His operational economic position is, in the financial-technology sector, comparable to that of the other PayPal Mafia principals; his political position is distinct from that of Thiel, Musk, Sacks, and Hoffman.

## XXIV. The structural mapping: the PayPal Mafia as the network

The PayPal Mafia is, **the documented network of fifteen individuals who held senior positions at Confinity, X.com, or the merged PayPal during the 1999-2002 period and who subsequently founded or led the principal companies of the post-2002 Silicon Valley wave**. The mapping is, by 2026, comprehensive:

- **Peter Thiel** — Founders Fund, Palantir, the political-operative network (Chapter 11).
- **Elon Musk** — SpaceX, Tesla, X/xAI, DOGE (Chapter 12).
- **Sam Altman** — OpenAI, Stargate, Worldcoin (Chapter 13 — joined OpenAI as a founding contributor and was operationally adjacent to the PayPal Mafia network through the Y Combinator-Thiel-Hoffman channel).
- **Reid Hoffman** — LinkedIn, Greylock, Microsoft board, Democratic political donor (this chapter).
- **David Sacks** — Yammer, Craft Ventures, White House AI and Crypto Czar (this chapter).
- **Max Levchin** — Affirm, Slide, Yelp (this chapter).
- **Ken Howery** — Founders Fund, US Ambassador to Sweden 2019-2021 (Chapter 11).
- **Luke Nosek** — Founders Fund, Gigafund (Chapter 11).
- **Roelof Botha** — Senior Partner at **Sequoia Capital** (since 2003). Botha is the PayPal Mafia integration with the principal traditional Silicon Valley venture-capital firm. South African by origin (like Musk, Sacks). Sequoia has, under Botha's senior partnership, been one of the principal investors in the new world order's portfolio companies — including Stripe, Klarna, and dozens of others.
- **Jeremy Stoppelman** — Co-founder of **Yelp** (2004).
- **Russel Simmons** — Co-founder of Yelp.
- **Jawed Karim, Steve Chen, Chad Hurley** — Co-founders of **YouTube** (2005, all three former PayPal employees). YouTube was acquired by Google in October 2006 for $1.65 billion; the YouTube acquisition is, the case in which three PayPal alumni founded the principal Internet-video platform of the subsequent twenty years.
- **Jack Selby** — Co-founder of Clarium Capital with Thiel; later partner at Thiel Capital.
- **Premal Shah** — Co-founder of Kiva.org.

The network is, the **structural mechanism by which the post-2002 Silicon Valley wave was generated**. The fifteen individuals who held senior positions at PayPal during 1999-2002 went on to found or lead the principal companies of the subsequent two decades — Tesla, SpaceX, LinkedIn, Yelp, YouTube, Yammer, Affirm, Slide, Palantir, Founders Fund, OpenAI, Glow, Craft Ventures, Greylock, Sequoia (through Botha), and fifty other consequential ventures.

The documented reality is that this is **not a coincidence**. The fifteen individuals were, at PayPal, the early-employee cohort that the founding-and-acquisition liquidity events of 2002 made wealthy. The wealth they accumulated funded the subsequent ventures. The network relationships they built funded the subsequent ventures. The shared political-intellectual formation (the Stanford Review-and-libertarian intellectual environment for the Thiel cohort; the broader Silicon Valley capital-class environment for all of them) produced the political-economic alignment that the book has been documenting across the preceding chapters.

The PayPal Mafia is, in this framing, the **central operational network of the contemporary American technological-capital-class formation**. The senior figures of the new world order are, with limited exceptions (Zuckerberg, Bezos, the Google founders, the Microsoft executives), either PayPal Mafia alumni or directly connected to the network through subsequent business relationships. The network is the structure; the structure has produced the political-economic outcomes the book has been documenting.



---

# Chapter 16: Jeff Bezos

In October 2024, Jeff Bezos blocked the *Washington Post* from endorsing a presidential candidate. He had purchased the newspaper in 2013 for $250 million in cash. In 2024 he overruled his editorial board. The man who owns the platform through which 100 million Americans receive their news decided that the newspaper would not tell them who it thought they should vote for. He then donated $1 million to the Trump inauguration fund.

**Jeffrey Preston Bezos** is, in May 2026, the third or fourth wealthiest individual in human history (Forbes 2026 estimates **$234 billion**). He is the founder and Executive Chairman of **Amazon** — $650 billion in 2025 revenue, $2.2 trillion in market capitalisation, 1.5 million employees worldwide. He is the founder and principal funder of **Blue Origin**, the second principal private space-launch company globally after SpaceX. And he is, through the 2018-2019 Saudi-Arabia-MBS phone-hacking-and-National-Enquirer-extortion episode, one of the most consequential modern intersections of US technology, Gulf sovereign-wealth, and tabloid-political-operative networks.

He is not a PayPal Mafia alumnus. His biography demonstrates that the structural pattern the prior chapters have traced is not a peculiarity of the PayPal-Stanford-Olin-Foundation lineage. It is a broader pattern of the American technological-capital class formation.

## I. Origins: Jacklyn Gise, the teenage mother, and Ted Jorgensen

Jeffrey Preston Bezos was born on **12 January 1964 in Albuquerque, New Mexico**. He was born as **Jeffrey Preston Jorgensen** — that is, with the surname of his **biological father, Ted Jorgensen**, a then-eighteen-year-old unicycle-and-bicycle shop worker who had married seventeen-year-old **Jacklyn Gise** shortly before Jeff's birth.

The Jorgensen-Gise marriage lasted two years. Jacklyn divorced Ted Jorgensen in 1965 or 1966, taking infant Jeff with her. **Ted Jorgensen had no further documented contact with his biological son**. He moved to Glendale, Arizona, where he operated a bicycle shop into his sixties. He was tracked down in 2012 by **Brad Stone**, the author of *The Everything Store* (Little, Brown, 2013) — by which time Jorgensen was 69 and operating Roadrunner Bike Center in Glendale; Stone's interview with Jorgensen is the record of Jorgensen's awareness of his biological son. Jorgensen told Stone that **he had not known what had become of Jeff**. He died in March 2015 without having met him.

The Jorgensen biological-paternity facts are documented in the public record (Stone's book, multiple subsequent biographical accounts). Jeff Bezos has, in his public biographical statements, **never explicitly addressed Ted Jorgensen** — the standard Bezos biographical framing treats Mike Bezos as the only father he had. The question of what relationship Bezos as an adult had or did not have with his biological father is not on the public record.

## II. Mike Bezos: the Cuban refugee and the Operation Pedro Pan adoption

In **1968**, Jacklyn Gise Jorgensen married **Miguel Ángel "Mike" Bezos** — a Cuban refugee who had arrived in the United States in 1962. Mike Bezos legally adopted Jeff in the period after the 1968 marriage. **Jeff Bezos's surname changed from Jorgensen to Bezos** in this period.

Mike Bezos's biography is one of the most operationally significant US-Cuban political-history programmes of the post-1959-revolution period:

- Mike Bezos was born in Cuba in 1945.
- He came to the United States in 1962 as part of **Operation Pedro Pan** — the CIA-State Department-Catholic Charities programme that, between December 1960 and October 1962, brought **14,000 unaccompanied Cuban children** to the United States. The programme was organised by the **Catholic Welfare Bureau of Miami** under Monsignor Bryan O. Walsh, with funding and coordination from the US State Department and operational support from the CIA. The programme was framed publicly as a humanitarian response to rumours circulating in Cuba that the Castro government intended to remove children from their parents' custody; the rumours were subsequently established to have been fabricated by anti-Castro-network propaganda in the immediate post-Bay-of-Pigs period.
- Mike Bezos was therefore part of the cohort that constituted the **anti-Castro Cuban-American formation** that became one of the principal Republican-Party voting blocs in Florida from the 1970s onward.
- He worked his way through the **University of Albuquerque**, became a **petroleum engineer at Exxon**, and was, by the early 1970s, an established member of the Albuquerque professional middle class.

The Mike Bezos biographical record is the verified case in which Jeff Bezos's adopted father was a product of one of the most explicit CIA-coordinated post-1959 operations of the period — the Pedro Pan programme. The observation is not that Mike Bezos was personally involved in any subsequent political-operational activity (he was a five-year-old child when he arrived in the US and his subsequent career was in petroleum engineering and the family-owned-business management of Jeff Bezos's wealth); the observation is that Jeff Bezos's family-of-origin was, on the adoptive-father side, structurally integrated with the CIA-coordinated anti-Castro Cuban-American network that has been one of the most consequential political-operative formations of the post-1959 American period.

## III. Lawrence Preston Gise: the AEC grandfather and the Texas ranch

The most operationally consequential family figure in Jeff Bezos's biography is his maternal grandfather, **Lawrence Preston Gise** (1915-1995).

Gise was a **senior career official at the United States Atomic Energy Commission (AEC)** — the federal agency that controlled the US nuclear-weapons development, production, and storage programme from 1946 (when the AEC was established by the Atomic Energy Act, taking over from the wartime Manhattan Project) through the AEC's dissolution in 1974. The AEC was, throughout its existence, the working agency of the US nuclear-weapons programme — the agency that managed the **Sandia National Laboratories** (Albuquerque), the **Los Alamos National Laboratory** (northern New Mexico), the **Hanford Site** (Washington State, plutonium production), and the **Nevada Test Site** (nuclear-weapons testing). The agency was, one of the most secretive and operationally consequential federal agencies of the post-1945 period.

Gise served as **director of the AEC's Albuquerque operations office** during the period covering major elements of the post-Manhattan-Project nuclear-weapons consolidation. The Albuquerque office was the hub of the AEC's nuclear-weapons-stockpile management programme; the Sandia National Laboratory adjacent to it was the principal weapons-engineering laboratory of the AEC. Gise's career at the AEC placed him in the senior career-civil-service tier of the US nuclear-weapons apparatus.

After his AEC retirement, Gise operated a **25,000-acre ranch near Cotulla, Texas**, in the brush country south of San Antonio. Jeff Bezos spent his summers at the ranch from age 4 to age 16 — that is, the formative summers of his childhood and adolescence were spent in the company of his AEC-career grandfather, on the 25,000-acre ranch. Bezos has, in adult interviews, repeatedly described the Gise ranch as the foundational environment of his work-ethic and engineering-doctrine formation; he has described his grandfather teaching him to repair windmills, slaughter cattle, and operate ranch equipment.

The observation is that **Jeff Bezos's family-of-origin was directly integrated with the senior US national-security apparatus through his maternal grandfather**. The standard captured-press biographical framing of Bezos has described Lawrence Gise as a "rancher" and "former AEC official"; the record is that Gise was a senior career-civil-service member of the US nuclear-weapons apparatus during the period of its peak operational activity. The grandfather-grandson relationship that the co-opted press framing has presented as a rural-American work-ethic-formation story is, in the underlying record, the case of an AEC-senior-career-official mentoring his grandson — the same grandson who would, four decades later, become the **principal contractor of the US intelligence community's cloud-computing infrastructure** (the 2013 CIA AWS contract, the subsequent JEDI / JWCC programmes). The continuity between Lawrence Gise's AEC career and Jeff Bezos's AWS-federal-contracting position is not, in the active record, accidental; it is multi-generational American national-security-apparatus integration with the contemporary technology industry.

## IV. Education and the D.E. Shaw period (1982-1994)

Bezos attended **Miami Palmetto Senior High School**, where he graduated as valedictorian in 1982. He won the Florida Silver Knight Award and was voted "Best Science Student."

He proceeded to **Princeton University**, where he completed a BSE in Electrical Engineering and Computer Science, *summa cum laude*, in 1986. He was elected to Phi Beta Kappa. His senior thesis was on chip-layout algorithms — a technical contribution to the foundational computer-engineering literature of the period.

After Princeton, Bezos worked at:

- **Fitel** (1986-1988) — a financial telecommunications start-up in New York.
- **Bankers Trust** (1988-1990) — where he rose to become the youngest Vice President in the company's history at that time.
- **D.E. Shaw & Co.** (1990-1994) — the quantitative-trading hedge fund founded by **David E. Shaw**. Bezos rose to **Senior Vice President at age 30**.

The **D.E. Shaw period is the documented foundational professional formation** of Bezos's subsequent career. David E. Shaw, a computer-science professor turned hedge-fund founder, had built D.E. Shaw into one of the principal quantitative-trading firms of the early 1990s — a firm whose distinctive operational doctrine combined high-frequency trading, statistical arbitrage, and major computer-science research investment. Bezos, at D.E. Shaw, was responsible for **studying business opportunities created by the emerging commercial Internet** — the firm had identified the Internet as a major commercial opportunity ahead of most of the broader financial-industry consensus, and Shaw had assigned Bezos to investigate the specific business categories that the Internet's emergence would enable.

Bezos produced an **internal D.E. Shaw report identifying online retail as a major commercial opportunity**. When the firm declined to pursue the opportunity at the scale Bezos thought warranted, Bezos left D.E. Shaw to pursue the opportunity personally. The documented founding of Amazon is therefore, **the personal pursuit by Bezos of the commercial opportunity D.E. Shaw had identified but declined to scale**. The Amazon founding is not the spontaneous-genius-in-a-garage story the press framing has constructed; it is a senior D.E. Shaw research-analyst executing the commercial opportunity his employer's research had identified.

## V. Amazon founding and the 1997 IPO

Bezos founded Amazon on **5 July 1994** in Bellevue, Washington. The company was initially named Cadabra Inc.; it was renamed Amazon.com by November 1994. The standard founding story: Bezos and his wife **MacKenzie Tuttle** (whom he had married in 1993; they had met at D.E. Shaw, where MacKenzie was a research associate) drove from New York to Seattle while drafting the business plan. They operated the company out of a garage in Bellevue.

Initial capital: **$250,000 from Mike and Jacklyn Bezos** — that is, from Bezos's adoptive father's Exxon retirement savings and the family's accumulated wealth. The family-investment foundation is documented in multiple subsequent retrospectives; Mike Bezos's stake at the 1997 IPO was reportedly worth **$300 million** (an 1,200-fold return on the original investment over three years).

The product-category selection (books) was made from a documented list of 20 categories Bezos analysed. Books offered: (1) a vastly larger catalogue than any physical bookstore could maintain (3 million titles in print, vs. 175,000 in the largest physical bookstore), (2) standardised products that did not require physical inspection before purchase, (3) the existence of two large wholesale distributors (Ingram, Baker & Taylor) whose inventories were already catalogued in computer-readable form, and (4) a relatively low average product price that reduced the financial risk of consumer fraud.

Amazon IPO **15 May 1997 at $18 per share**. First-day close: $23.50. Market capitalisation at IPO: **$438 million**. The IPO occurred at the very early stage of the dot-com bubble — two years after Netscape's foundational IPO (Chapter 14) and three years before the bubble peak.

Amazon survived the 2000-2002 dot-com collapse by raising **$2 billion in debt** during the late-1999 / early-2000 period — that is, immediately before the collapse, in the period when the bubble's terminal phase made debt financing available at minimal yields. The 2002 collapse killed the company's principal early-Internet competitors (Pets.com, Webvan, drkoop.com, eToys, dozens of others); Amazon's pre-collapse debt issuance gave it the working capital to survive the trough and emerge, by 2003, as the principal surviving online retailer.

## VI. The Amazon expansion (1997-2021) and the AWS pivot

The Amazon corporate-strategic record across 1997-2021 is one of the most operationally successful corporate-strategic-development trajectories of the contemporary American business record. The principal milestones:

- **1998**: Expanded beyond books to music, DVDs, video games, electronics — the case of the platform-economy doctrine being applied to retail. The strategic logic: the working infrastructure (warehouses, customer-service systems, payment-processing systems, recommendation engines) that Amazon had built for the books business could be applied to any retail product category at marginal incremental cost; the scale advantages would, over time, exclude smaller category-specific competitors.
- **1999**: Launched **Amazon Marketplace** — the third-party-seller platform. The Marketplace was the platform-monopoly doctrine being applied to retail: Amazon would, instead of competing directly with all merchants, host them on its platform and extract a percentage commission on all transactions. The Marketplace has, since 2015, generated more revenue than Amazon's first-party retail business.
- **2005**: Launched **Amazon Prime** — the loyalty/subscription programme. The Prime programme was, by 2025, the documented mechanism by which Amazon had locked in **200 million paying subscribers globally** at $139/year — that is, $28 billion in annual subscription revenue independent of product sales.
- **2006**: Launched **Amazon Web Services (AWS)** — the cloud-computing infrastructure platform that has, in the subsequent twenty years, become the most operationally significant single Amazon business unit. AWS was conceived, in the documented internal record, as a mechanism for monetising the internal infrastructure Amazon had built for its retail operations; the innovation was the recognition that the same elastic-computing-and-storage infrastructure could be offered to external customers as a service. AWS subsequently became the principal global cloud-computing infrastructure platform; by 2025 it generated **$110 billion in annual revenue and $35 billion in annual operating income** — that is, AWS alone was generating more operating profit than the entire Amazon retail business.
- **2007**: Launched Kindle (e-reader).
- **2014**: Launched Echo (Alexa smart speakers).
- **2017**: Acquired **Whole Foods** for **$13.7 billion** — the case of the platform-monopoly doctrine being extended to physical retail.
- **2019**: Acquired **Ring** (home surveillance) for $1 billion — the case of Amazon's entry into the consumer-surveillance market that has been one of the most operationally consequential post-2019 categories.
- **2021**: Acquired **MGM** for **$8.45 billion** — entertainment-content library acquisition that integrated Amazon Prime Video with the major-Hollywood-studio production network.
- **July 2021**: Bezos stepped down as Amazon CEO. **Andy Jassy** (previously the head of AWS) became CEO. Bezos retained the **Executive Chairman** role; the management of Amazon transitioned to Jassy.

The Amazon operational trajectory is, in the book's assessment, the most successful single corporate-strategic-development record of the post-1997 American period. The company has, over three decades, transformed from an online bookstore to the principal global retail platform, the principal cloud-computing infrastructure provider, one of the principal entertainment-content producers, the principal consumer-surveillance hardware provider (through Ring and Echo), and one of the principal global employers.

## VII. The federal-contracting integration: AWS as the principal cloud provider to the US national-security apparatus

The most operationally consequential aspect of Amazon's post-2006 business for the purposes of this book is the **integration of AWS with the US national-security apparatus** as the principal commercial cloud provider.

**2013**: AWS won a **$600 million CIA cloud-computing contract** — the foundational contract in the federal-government cloud-migration programme. The contract integrated AWS as the principal commercial cloud provider for the US intelligence community. The contract was, at the time of its award, the largest single classified federal IT contract; it produced the active architecture through which the CIA's analytical, communications, and storage infrastructure subsequently migrated to AWS.

The 2013 CIA AWS contract is the structural integration that Lawrence Preston Gise's AEC career had anticipated. The grandfather had been a senior career official at the AEC; the grandson, through Amazon, became the principal commercial cloud provider to the CIA. The continuity between the two positions — Gise's career-civil-service nuclear-weapons-apparatus position and Bezos's commercial-cloud-provider position for the intelligence community — is documented multi-generational integration of the Bezos family with the US national-security apparatus.

**2019**: The Pentagon **JEDI ($10 billion cloud-computing contract)** was awarded to Microsoft. Amazon sued the federal government in November 2019 claiming the award was politically motivated — that **Trump had "improperly pressured" the Pentagon to award the contract to Microsoft "to harm his perceived political enemy" Bezos**. Amazon's lawsuit was the documented case in which Bezos publicly accused Trump of corruption in federal contracting. The JEDI award was subsequently cancelled in 2021 and replaced by the multi-vendor **Joint Warfighting Cloud Capability (JWCC)** — a $9 billion contract awarded in December 2022 to Amazon, Google, Microsoft, and Oracle. The JWCC restructuring resolved the Bezos-Trump dispute by ensuring that all four major US cloud providers received Pentagon contracts.

**2024-2026**: AWS is the principal commercial cloud provider for the Department of Defense, the CIA, the NSA, and large portions of the federal civilian agencies. The Amazon-federal-contracting integration is, in 2026, one of the structurally foundational features of the US national-security cloud-computing architecture.

## VIII. Amazon's board: the structural mapping (May 2026)

The Amazon Board of Directors, as of May 2026, includes the following figures whose presence on the board documents the structural integration of Amazon with the broader new-world-order network:

- **Andy Jassy** — CEO, since July 2021.
- **Daniel Huttenlocher** — MIT computer-science professor; Dean of the MIT Schwarzman College of Computing. The board's principal academic-technical position.
- **Patty Stonesifer** — **former CEO of the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation** (2000-2008). Stonesifer's presence on the Amazon board is the **Gates Foundation-Amazon structural integration**.
- **Indra Nooyi** — former CEO of PepsiCo. The board's principal consumer-products-industry connection.
- **Edith Cooper** — former Goldman Sachs Global Head of Human Capital Management. The board's principal Wall Street connection.
- **Jonathan Rubinstein** — former Apple senior executive (one of the principal architects of the original iPod). The board's principal hardware-industry connection.
- **Brad Smith** — **Microsoft Vice Chair and President**. Smith joined the Amazon board in 2024 — the case of the **Big-Tech cross-board integration**. The same individual (Smith) who is the principal Microsoft external-affairs executive is also on the Amazon board; the two principal commercial cloud providers, ostensibly competitors, share board-level personnel.
- **Wendell Weeks** — Corning CEO. The board's principal materials-industry connection.
- **Andrew Ng** — Stanford AI professor; Coursera founder. The board's principal AI-industry position.
- **Keith Alexander** — **former Director of the National Security Agency (NSA), 2005-2014**; founder of IronNet Cybersecurity. Alexander's presence on the Amazon board is the **NSA-Amazon-AWS structural integration**.
- **Judith McGrath** — former MTV Networks chair. The board's principal entertainment-industry connection.

The board composition is structurally significant. The combination of **Keith Alexander (NSA), Brad Smith (Microsoft), Patty Stonesifer (Gates Foundation), and Andy Jassy (CEO)** documents the integration of Amazon with the US national-security apparatus, the principal competing cloud-computing platform, and the Gates philanthropic-and-investment network. The board is, the structural mechanism by which Amazon's decisions are integrated with the broader new-world-order network the book has been describing.

## IX. Blue Origin: the second private space-launch company

Bezos founded **Blue Origin in 2000** — that is, two years before Musk founded SpaceX (May 2002). Blue Origin is therefore the case of the first contemporary private space-launch company; the captured-press framing has, in the standard pattern, presented SpaceX as the pioneering company and Blue Origin as the subsequent competitor, but the chronological record is the reverse.

Blue Origin's operational trajectory has been much slower than SpaceX's. The principal vehicles:

- **New Shepard** — suborbital rocket; operational since 2015. Used for short suborbital flights with paying-passenger and research-payload cargoes.
- **New Glenn** — orbital heavy-lift rocket; **first successful orbital flight January 2025** (seventeen years after SpaceX's first successful Falcon 1 orbital flight in September 2008).

Blue Origin's CEO since 2023 is **Dave Limp** (former Amazon devices Senior VP, who oversaw the Echo / Alexa / Ring product lines). The Limp appointment was Bezos transferring senior Amazon executive talent to Blue Origin in the period after his July 2021 CEO transition; the transfer signalled Bezos's increased focus on Blue Origin as a personal-investment-and-strategic-priority.

Bezos has reportedly funded Blue Origin at **$1 billion per year from personal Amazon-share sales**. The consequence is that Bezos's personal stake in Amazon has declined over time as he has sold shares to fund Blue Origin, while his stake in Blue Origin has correspondingly increased.

Federal contracts: NASA Human Landing System contract (**$3.4 billion, 2023**) for Blue Origin's lunar-lander development; various Department of Defense contracts for launch services and satellite deployment.

**20 July 2021**: Bezos's first personal spaceflight on New Shepard — eleven days after Richard Branson's Virgin Galactic flight, in the period of the most intense billionaire-spaceflight cultural moment of the post-2020 period.

**14 April 2025**: Bezos's fiancée **Lauren Sánchez flew on a New Shepard mission with an all-female crew**. The flight was extensively covered in the press and was, the case of a billionaire's fiancée being given access to the company's product as a personal-life signalling event.

Blue Origin is, in 2026, the **second principal private space-launch company globally**. It operates at a tenth the launch cadence of SpaceX and generates a fifth the revenue. The competitive position is, in the book's assessment, structurally disadvantaged: SpaceX's first-mover advantage and the working momentum its higher launch cadence has generated have produced a market position that Blue Origin will likely not be able to displace.

## X. The Washington Post: acquisition and the early years

In **August 2013**, Bezos personally purchased **The Washington Post Company's newspaper assets** for **$250 million in cash**. The acquisition was a **personal investment by Bezos, not an Amazon transaction** — the legal-and-financial substance is that the *Post* is owned by Bezos personally through a holding company called Nash Holdings, not by Amazon.

The acquisition price was, in financial terms, a small fraction of Bezos's net worth at the time; for the Graham family (who had owned the *Post* through four generations from 1933 to 2013), the $250 million was the company's residual value after the steady decline of the print-newspaper-advertising business through the 2000s and the 2008 financial crisis.

Bezos's stated rationale for the acquisition: the institutional importance of an independent national newspaper, the opportunity to apply technology-driven transformation to a struggling print-newspaper business, and (in his own subsequent statements) his personal admiration for the *Post*'s historical record (the Watergate coverage, the Pentagon Papers, etc.).

The Graham family's decision to sell to Bezos was, by all documented accounts, a decision that the family had no operational path to maintaining the *Post* as a profitable independent business in the print-newspaper-decline environment of the 2010s. Donald Graham (then CEO of The Washington Post Company) has been publicly supportive of Bezos's subsequent stewardship.

**2013-2021** (under executive editor Marty Baron, publisher Fred Ryan):

The *Post* expanded during the Baron-Ryan period. Digital subscriptions grew from 100,000 to over 3 million by 2020. The newsroom expanded by 200 reporters. The *Post* won **18 Pulitzer Prizes** during the period.

The most operationally significant *Post* moment of the Baron period was the **February 2017 adoption of the slogan "Democracy Dies in Darkness"** — the slogan was explicitly framed as a response to the first Trump administration's adversarial relationship with the press, and it positioned the *Post* as one of the principal institutional opponents of the first Trump administration.

During the same period, the *Post* published the Khashoggi columns (Section XII below) that produced the Saudi-MBS-phone-hacking incident; the *Post* published serious investigative coverage of the Trump administration; the *Post* was, one of the most influential American newspapers documented of the 2016-2021 period.

## XI. The Will Lewis appointment and the editorial transition (January 2024)

In **January 2024**, Bezos appointed **Will Lewis** as Publisher and CEO of The Washington Post. The Lewis appointment was the beginning of the *Post*'s post-2024 editorial transition.

Will Lewis's biographical record is, in the documented public record, **problematic in ways that the co-opted press coverage of his appointment under-emphasised**:

- Lewis was editor of *The Wall Street Journal* (2014-2018) and CEO of Dow Jones (2014-2020).
- Lewis had been a senior News Corp executive in the United Kingdom during the **News of the World phone-hacking scandal** of 2011 — the scandal in which Rupert Murdoch's UK newspapers were established to have systematically hacked the voicemail of thousands of British citizens (including the murdered schoolgirl Milly Dowler, members of the royal family, politicians, and celebrities). The scandal produced the Leveson Inquiry and resulted in News of the World being closed in July 2011 and major criminal convictions of senior News Corp executives.
- **Lewis was implicated in the 2011 cover-up phase of the scandal**. Multiple subsequent reports (including the *Financial Times*, the *Press Gazette*, and the *Prospect* coverage) have documented Lewis's role in the destruction of evidence and the management of the corporate-response strategy. Lewis has denied wrongdoing; the record is that he was a senior News Corp UK executive during the cover-up period and was personally involved in the corporate decisions that have subsequently been the subject of multiple investigations and lawsuits.

The Lewis appointment was controversial within the *Post* newsroom. Multiple senior reporters opposed it. **Executive editor Sally Buzbee resigned in June 2024** — a resignation that was, in the press framing, presented as a management disagreement but that was, in the underlying record, a dispute over Lewis's editorial direction and over Lewis's documented personal involvement in the News Corp phone-hacking cover-up.

## XII. The Harris endorsement killing (October 2024)

The most operationally consequential single editorial decision in Bezos's twelve-year ownership of *The Washington Post* is the **October 2024 killing of the planned Kamala Harris presidential endorsement**.

The documented record:

- The *Post*'s editorial board had **drafted an endorsement of Vice President Kamala Harris** over Donald Trump for the 2024 presidential election. The endorsement had been written by editorial-page editor **David Shipley** and editorial-board members **Charles Lane** and **Stephen W. Stromberg**. The drafting process had occurred over several weeks; the editorial-page editor had told the board in the period before the killing that the endorsement was "on track."
- Per *Columbia Journalism Review* reporting (25 October 2024): Shipley had told the editorial board, in the drafting period, that "**this is something our owner has an interest in**."
- On **Friday, 25 October 2024 — eleven days before the 5 November election** — Will Lewis announced that the *Post* would not endorse a presidential candidate "in this election. Nor in any future presidential election." The announcement was framed as "returning to our roots of not endorsing presidential candidates."
- The *Post*'s own reporting (published the same day, by two staff reporters): "**The decision not to publish was made by The Post's owner — Amazon founder Jeff Bezos**, according to the same sources [briefed on the events]."
- Bezos had been **visiting Washington in the days before the announcement**; multiple sources confirmed his direct role in the decision.
- Lewis publicly denied that Bezos had read the drafted endorsement, saying Bezos "was not sent, did not read and did not opine on any draft." The denial was contradicted by the *Post*'s own reporting, by the editorial-page editor's statement to the board, and by the documented Bezos visit to Washington.

The response from former and current *Post* personnel was serious:

- **Marty Baron**, the former executive editor of the *Post* (2012-2021, the executive editor who had overseen the Watergate-era newspaper Bezos had acquired): "**This is cowardice, with democracy as its casualty.** Trump will see this as an invitation to further intimidate owner Jeff Bezos (and others). **Disturbing spinelessness at an institution famed for courage.**"
- **Robert Kagan**, *Post* editor-at-large: resigned in protest.
- **Bob Woodward and Carl Bernstein** (the legendary Watergate reporters): "We respect the traditional independence of the editorial page, but this decision 11 days out from the 2024 presidential election ignores the *Washington Post*'s own overwhelming reportorial evidence on the threat Donald Trump poses to democracy."
- **The Washington Post Guild** (the newsroom union): "The message from our chief executive, Will Lewis — not from the Editorial Board itself — makes us concerned that management interfered with the work of our members in Editorial."

The consequences:

- **Roughly 250,000 *Post* subscribers cancelled their subscriptions** in the week following the announcement — **10% of the paper's paid digital subscriber base**. The cancellation rate was the largest documented single-event subscriber loss in the *Post*'s history.
- The endorsement-killing was simultaneous with a similar incident at the **Los Angeles Times**, where owner **Patrick Soon-Shiong** had blocked a Harris endorsement. The LA Times's editorial-page editor Mariel Garza resigned in protest; the *Times* lost 18,000 subscribers (5% of its base).
- The two simultaneous endorsement-killings at the two principal newspapers owned by billionaires (the *Post* by Bezos, the *Times* by Soon-Shiong) were the documented operational case of **the billionaire-owners of the principal American newspapers exercising direct editorial control to prevent endorsements of the Democratic candidate** — in the closing days of the election, in the period when Trump's victory was operationally uncertain and the editorial endorsements would have been most consequential.

The book's structural argument: the endorsement-killing was not a journalistic decision; it was a **portfolio-management decision by Bezos** in anticipation of a potential Trump victory and the consequent risk of federal-retaliation against Amazon's federal-contracting position. The 2019 JEDI dispute (Section VII) had documented Bezos's awareness that the first Trump administration had been willing to interfere with federal contracting on political-personal-vendetta grounds; the 2024 endorsement-killing was Bezos taking the portfolio-management lesson and acting accordingly. The editorial-independence of the *Post* had, in the period after October 2024, been subordinated to the active requirement of protecting Amazon's federal-contracting position.

## XIII. The 2024-2025 political turn

The Bezos political-activity record of the 2024-2025 period documents the broader pattern the book has been describing across the previous chapters. The principal documented events:

- **December 2024**: Bezos personally donated **$1 million to the Trump inauguration committee** — the maximum permitted single contribution at the time. The donation was the Silicon Valley billionaire who, in 2019, had publicly accused Trump of corruption in federal contracting (the JEDI lawsuit) now personally funding Trump's second inauguration.
- **20 January 2025**: Bezos attended **Trump's inauguration, seated on the inauguration platform** with the other senior technology executives — **Mark Zuckerberg, Sundar Pichai, Tim Cook, Elon Musk, Sam Altman**. The photograph of the tech-billionaire row at the inauguration is one of the most operationally significant images of the new world order's public-self-presentation. The six senior figures of the contemporary American technology industry, in one row, at the inauguration of the President they had collectively (with the partial exception of Pichai and Cook) funded.
- **February 2025**: Bezos announced **editorial restrictions at the *Washington Post***: the opinion pages would, under new editorial guidelines, defend two "pillars" — "**personal liberties and free markets**" — and would not publish opinions that contradicted those pillars. The announcement was made by Bezos personally in an internal message to the *Post*'s staff that was subsequently leaked to the broader press.
- **Editorial-page editor David Shipley resigned** following the February 2025 announcement.

The February 2025 editorial-restrictions announcement is an **American newspaper owner imposing an explicit ideological restriction on the editorial pages of the newspaper he owns**. It is, structurally similar to the historical newspaper-baron behaviour of William Randolph Hearst in the early twentieth century — and structurally distinct from the post-1960s American norm of editorial-page independence from owner direction. The consequence is that *The Washington Post*, the newspaper that had adopted the slogan "Democracy Dies in Darkness" in February 2017 as a public commitment to journalistic independence against the first Trump administration, was, eight years later, formally restricted by its owner to publishing only opinions consistent with the "personal liberties and free markets" doctrine the second Trump administration's policy programme endorsed.

## XIV. The Saudi Arabia-MBS phone-hacking incident (2018-2020)

The most operationally consequential personal-life-and-political-economy intersection in Bezos's biography is his phone being hacked, allegedly via a WhatsApp message from Saudi Crown Prince **Mohammed bin Salman (MBS)**, and the subsequent **National Enquirer** publication of details of his extramarital relationship with Lauren Sánchez.

The book treats this episode at full depth because it is, **the case in which a senior Saudi state actor, a US tabloid-political-operative network, and a US technology billionaire were operationally integrated in a coordinated attack on the same billionaire's personal life and political-economic position**. The question of what the episode demonstrates about the working integration of Gulf sovereign-wealth, US political networks, and US technology-industry decision-making is at the centre of the book's broader argument.

### Background: the Khashoggi columns

In **September 2017**, the *Washington Post* (Bezos's newspaper) began publishing a series of opinion columns by **Jamal Khashoggi** — a Saudi journalist who had been a senior figure in Saudi state-aligned media and who had, in 2017, gone into self-imposed exile in the United States after his criticism of the Saudi government became operationally untenable in Saudi Arabia. Khashoggi's *Post* columns were critical of Saudi Arabia and of MBS personally. The columns were, one of the most consequential US-published critiques of the post-2017 MBS Saudi government during the period of the MBS-led "consolidation" of Saudi internal power (the November 2017 detention of 200 Saudi princes and businessmen at the Riyadh Ritz-Carlton, the various Saudi-internal-political-coercion operations of the period).

### April 2018: the Bezos-MBS dinner

In **April 2018**, Bezos and Mohammed bin Salman **exchanged phone numbers at a Hollywood dinner** during MBS's tour of US technology companies. MBS was, in the period, conducting a public-relations tour of the United States — meeting with technology executives, Hollywood figures, US government officials, and various other senior US figures — that was framed as the announcement of Saudi Arabia's "modernisation" programme under MBS's leadership. The Bezos-MBS phone-number exchange was a routine outcome of the period's social-networking environment.

### 1 May 2018: the hack

Per the **FTI Consulting forensic analysis** commissioned by Bezos (conducted between February and November 2019, published by *The Guardian* on 21 January 2020 and the full report by *Motherboard* on 23 January 2020), and per the **UN Special Rapporteurs Agnès Callamard and David Kaye** in their 22 January 2020 statement:

> "A message from the Crown Prince account was sent to Mr. Bezos through WhatsApp. The message is an encrypted video file. It is later established, with reasonable certainty, that the video's downloader infects Mr. Bezos' phone with malicious code."

The hack was concluded by the FTI report with "**medium to high confidence**" to have been the source of Bezos's subsequent compromised data. The hack resulted in "**dramatically higher amounts of data**" being transmitted from Bezos's phone in the months following the May 2018 message — the investigators concluded that several gigabytes of data had been exfiltrated.

The investigators identified **Saud al-Qahtani** — MBS's senior media advisor — as the likely operational executor of the hack. Saud al-Qahtani had, been implicated in the **murder of Jamal Khashoggi** in October 2018 (Section below).

### 2 October 2018: the Khashoggi assassination

On **2 October 2018**, Jamal Khashoggi entered the **Saudi consulate in Istanbul, Turkey** to obtain documents he required for his planned marriage to his Turkish fiancée Hatice Cengiz. He was **murdered and dismembered inside the consulate by a 15-person Saudi team**. The team included senior Saudi intelligence and military personnel; the murder was, in the subsequent forensic record, executed within two hours of Khashoggi's entry.

The CIA's subsequent assessment, declassified in February 2021, concluded that **MBS had personally ordered the killing**. The Saudi government has, throughout the period, denied that MBS personally ordered the killing; it has maintained that the murder was the unauthorised action of subordinate officials.

Following the Khashoggi murder, the *Washington Post* (Bezos's newspaper) much increased the volume and intensity of its critical coverage of Saudi Arabia and MBS personally. The *Post*'s Khashoggi-coverage programme was, one of the principal American media campaigns of accountability for the Khashoggi murder.

### November 2018: the WhatsApp image

Per the FTI Consulting report, in **November 2018** — that is, before the Bezos-Sánchez affair had become public — MBS sent Bezos a WhatsApp message containing an image of a woman who **closely resembled Lauren Sánchez**. The image was sent before the affair was publicly known. The implication, drawn explicitly by the FTI report and the UN Special Rapporteurs, was that **MBS had access to information about the affair before it was public** — that is, that the May 2018 hack had given the Saudi operation access to the personal communications that documented the affair.

### 9 January 2019: the National Enquirer publication

On **9 January 2019**, *The National Enquirer* published a story detailing Bezos's extramarital relationship with Lauren Sánchez, including text messages and explicit photographs.

The *Enquirer* was owned by **American Media Inc. (AMI)**, whose CEO was **David Pecker** — a long-time Trump ally and the executor of the **"catch-and-kill" operations** on behalf of Trump during the 2016 election. Pecker had, executed the **$150,000 payment to former *Playboy* model Karen McDougal** to suppress her allegations of an affair with Trump, the **$130,000 payment to adult-film performer Stormy Daniels** (executed by Trump's lawyer Michael Cohen with Pecker's coordination), and various other suppression operations. Pecker subsequently testified in the May 2024 New York v. Trump criminal trial that the catch-and-kill operations had been coordinated with the Trump 2016 campaign.

The National Enquirer's 9 January 2019 Bezos publication was therefore the operation of **a Trump-aligned tabloid-political-operative network** publishing the contents of Bezos's compromised personal communications.

### 30 January 2019: the Bezos divorce announcement

On **30 January 2019**, Bezos and his wife MacKenzie announced their divorce. The timing was related to the National Enquirer publication; the divorce had been operationally inevitable in the period after the affair's public disclosure.

### 7 February 2019: "No thank you, Mr. Pecker"

On **7 February 2019**, Bezos published a Medium post titled "**No thank you, Mr. Pecker**" in which he disclosed that AMI had attempted to extort him with the threat of publishing additional explicit photographs.

Bezos published the AMI's email demands verbatim. The emails included specific demands that Bezos publicly affirm that AMI's coverage of him "**was not politically motivated or influenced by political forces**." That is, AMI had explicitly demanded that Bezos publicly deny the existence of political motivations in the *Enquirer*'s coverage of him — the demand was the tabloid-political-operative network attempting to obtain a public denial of the political-motivation framework that was, in the underlying record, true.

The Bezos Medium post is the **documented case of a sitting CEO of a major US technology company publicly refusing to comply with a tabloid-extortion demand and publishing the extortion communications**. The post produced one of the most consequential public-relations moments of the period; it was, in the captured-press framing, a act of personal courage on Bezos's part. The consequence was that AMI was forced into the public-relations crisis position of having its extortion methods publicly documented; Pecker subsequently lost his position at AMI (he was forced out by AMI's owners in 2020).

### March 2019: the Gavin de Becker Daily Beast column

Bezos's chief of security **Gavin de Becker** subsequently published a *Daily Beast* column (30 March 2019) alleging that "**the Saudis had access to Bezos's phone**" and that the *National Enquirer*'s information had been obtained through the Saudi-linked hack. The de Becker column was the first public allegation of the Saudi-MBS-link in the *Enquirer* episode; it preceded the formal FTI Consulting report by eight months.

### AMI's denial and the Michael Sanchez counter-suit

AMI's response (March 2019): the only source for the *Enquirer*'s Bezos story was **Michael Sanchez** — Lauren Sánchez's brother — and there was "no involvement by any other third party whatsoever."

A year later (March 2020), Michael Sanchez sued AMI, claiming that when the *Enquirer* first contacted him they already had "**raunchy text messages and nude selfies**" of Bezos and Lauren Sánchez. Sanchez denied giving AMI the explicit photographs and accused AMI of having hacked Bezos's phone. The Sanchez lawsuit is the documented case in which AMI's stated source (Michael Sanchez) explicitly denied being the source of the most operationally damaging materials in the *Enquirer*'s publication — a denial that, if accurate, leaves the Saudi-MBS-hack as the likely actual source.

### February 2019: the MBS message

Per the FTI report, in February 2019 — **after** the *National Enquirer* story had been published — MBS sent Bezos a WhatsApp message urging him **not to believe everything**, after Bezos had been briefed on the phone about an internet campaign against him being conducted by Saudi-linked accounts. The message is documented as further circumstantial evidence of the MBS-network's operational awareness of the Bezos surveillance and of its political utility.

### The official US response: the FBI low-priority designation

The FBI investigated the alleged hack from 2019 through 2021. In **December 2021**, the FBI publicly stated it could not find proof to substantiate the claims that Saudi Arabia had hacked Bezos's phone, and "**considered an investigation into those allegations a low priority**."

The FBI's low-priority designation was inconsistent with the available forensic evidence — the FTI Consulting report had concluded with medium-to-high confidence that MBS's account had been the source of the hack, and the UN Special Rapporteurs had publicly endorsed the FTI findings — and is the recorded case in which the US national-security apparatus **declined to investigate the alleged Saudi hack of one of the most prominent US business executives**.

The context of the FBI's low-priority designation: the decision occurred during the same period in which the **Saudi-US security relationship was being maintained despite the Khashoggi murder**. The US government's operational determination, across the Trump first administration and the Biden administration, was that the strategic value of the Saudi-US relationship — the oil-pricing coordination, the Iran-containment programme, the various Middle East peace initiatives — exceeded the value of pursuing accountability for either the Khashoggi murder or the Bezos phone hack. The Bezos hack was, in this framework, **subordinated to the broader Saudi-US strategic relationship**.

The documented record establishes that a senior Saudi state actor (MBS), operating through his senior advisor (Saud al-Qahtani, who was simultaneously implicated in the Khashoggi murder), conducted a coordinated intelligence operation against one of the most prominent US business executives; the coordinated operation produced the disclosure of personal communications that were subsequently published by a Trump-aligned tabloid-political-operative network (AMI / David Pecker / *National Enquirer*); the US government declined to investigate.

The episode is **the integration of Gulf sovereign-wealth political-operative networks with US tabloid-political-operative networks against a US business executive** whose newspaper had been publishing critical coverage of the Gulf state actor.

## XV. The 2024-2026 reconciliation with Saudi Arabia

The question of what subsequently happened to the Bezos-MBS relationship is, the case in which Bezos has, in the post-2021 period, **normalised the relationship** with the Saudi government.

The principal documented evidence:

- **The Venice wedding** (27 June 2025): The guest list included **Queen Rania of Jordan and the Crown Prince of Jordan** but did not include MBS personally. The observation: Bezos's wedding in Venice — the most operationally significant personal-life event of the period — included Gulf-royal-family attendance.
- **Saudi PIF investments**: The **Saudi Public Investment Fund (PIF)** has, in the period 2022-2025, made major investments in Amazon-adjacent companies. The Saudi PIF's investments include positions in **Uber, Lucid Motors, multiple US-listed AI companies**, and major diversified equity exposure to the US technology sector. The PIF is, in 2026, one of the principal Gulf sovereign-wealth funds and is operationally controlled by MBS.
- **MBS's continuing US-government access**: MBS has, in the period 2022-2026, restored normal relations with the US government. The Biden administration's 2022 visit to Saudi Arabia (the Biden-MBS fist bump) was the Biden administration's operational decision to restore the relationship despite the Khashoggi murder. The second Trump administration has, in 2025-2026, expanded the relationship — the Saudi-US AI partnership (the **MGX investment fund** that is one of the four principal participants in the **Stargate Project**, Chapter 13) is one of the foundational structural features of the second Trump administration's AI-policy programme.

The operational outcome: the Bezos-MBS relationship has, in the period 2021-2026, been **subordinated to the broader US-Saudi strategic relationship and the AI-and-sovereign-wealth integration**. The 2018-2019 episode was a discrete operational event; the subsequent six years have been the case of the personal-political-economic relationship between the two parties being normalised in the context of the broader strategic environment.

## XVI. Personal life and the post-2019 trajectory

**MacKenzie Tuttle / Bezos / Scott** (born 1970, San Francisco) was Bezos's wife from 1993 to 2019. Princeton graduate (1992); novelist (*The Testing of Luther Albright*, 2005; *Traps*, 2013). Had worked at D.E. Shaw with Bezos. Was the **first Amazon employee**. The 2019 divorce produced a settlement in which MacKenzie received **25% of Bezos's Amazon stake** — at the time of the settlement, **$38 billion**.

MacKenzie subsequently took her middle name Scott as her surname. She has, since 2019, **given $18 billion to 2,500 charities**, principally through her **Yield Giving** initiative. MacKenzie Scott's giving is, by most analyses, the **largest and most effective single-individual contemporary philanthropic programme in the United States** — much larger as a fraction of underlying wealth than the Gates Foundation's giving, the Bezos Earth Fund's giving, or the various other senior-billionaire philanthropic programmes the book has documented. Scott has, in the period 2019-2026, given to **historically-Black colleges, Indigenous-led organisations, community-development financial institutions, and the broader infrastructure of US progressive civil society**. Her giving is a senior billionaire executing the philanthropic-disbursement programme the broader new-world-order capital class has declined to execute.

**Lauren Sánchez** (born 19 December 1969, Albuquerque, NM — coincidentally also Bezos's birthplace) — former TV news anchor (Fox's *Good Day LA* 2011-2017). Founder of **Black Ops Aviation** (aerial-cinematography production company). Helicopter pilot. Previously married to **Patrick Whitesell** (CEO of Endeavor / William Morris) from 2005 to 2019; two children with Whitesell.

**Engagement** May 2023; **wedding 27 June 2025 in Venice**.

The Venice wedding was estimated at **$40-55 million in total cost**. Roughly **200-250 guests**. Held at the basilica of **San Giorgio Maggiore** and the **Venetian Arsenal** (the venue was moved from the originally-planned Scuola Vecchia della Misericordia after protest planning by the **No Space for Bezos** coalition). Guests included **Bill Gates, Sam Altman, Ivanka Trump, Jared Kushner, Oprah Winfrey, Kim Kardashian, Leonardo DiCaprio, Tom Brady, Mick Jagger, Queen Rania of Jordan, the Crown Prince of Jordan**, and 200 others. **Roughly 90 private jets** were used to transport guests; the wedding generated an estimated €48 million in revenue for Venice airports and hospitality businesses.

The wedding produced major protest from **No Space for Bezos** — a coalition of Venetian organisations and Greenpeace and other groups that opposed the use of Venice as a setting for billionaire spectacles. A banner displayed in **Piazza San Marco** on 23 June 2025 read: "**If you can rent Venice for your wedding you can pay more tax.**" The protest produced major international media coverage; it was, one of the most consequential public protests of a billionaire personal-life event in the contemporary period.

## XVII. Bezos's philanthropic activity

The record on Bezos's philanthropic activity is, in the book's assessment, **much smaller as a fraction of underlying wealth than the co-opted press framing has presented**.

- **Bezos Earth Fund**: $10 billion committed in 2020 for climate and environmental causes. Disbursements through 2024-2025 have been $2.5 billion — that is, a quarter of the committed amount over five years.
- **Day 1 Fund**: Smaller; principal focus on homelessness and education. Disbursements through 2024-2025 $300 million.
- **Total Bezos philanthropic giving 2019-2026**: $5-6 billion. Bezos's underlying wealth over the same period: $100 billion to $234 billion. The giving has therefore been **2-5% of wealth over seven years**, against MacKenzie Scott's **60% of her starting wealth** over the same period.

The observation is that Bezos's philanthropic giving has been performative — the announcement of large commitments has been much larger than the active disbursements, and the giving has been much smaller as a fraction of wealth than the standard captured-press framing has presented. The contrast with MacKenzie Scott's giving is the evidence that the large-philanthropic-disbursement programme is operationally possible at the senior-billionaire scale; Bezos has chosen not to execute it.

## XVIII. Current position in 2026

This is what Jeff Bezos is, as of May 2026:
