# Chapter 18: Schmidt, Ellison, Fink — and the Structural Integration with Finance, Defense, and Media

The previous chapters described the PayPal Mafia network, Bezos, and Zuckerberg. The analysis turns to three figures whose biographies, taken together, document the **structural integration of the senior technology-industry-capital class with the principal financial-sector infrastructure, the US national-security apparatus, and the contemporary American media-industry consolidation**.

**Eric Emerson Schmidt** (born 1955) — the former Google CEO and Alphabet Executive Chairman, the founding Chairman of the Department of Defense's **Defense Innovation Board** (2016-2020), the Chairman of the **National Security Commission on Artificial Intelligence** (2019-2021), the founder of the **Special Competitive Studies Project** (2021-present), and the principal Silicon-Valley-to-Pentagon bridge of the post-2016 period. Schmidt is **the technology-industry executive whose post-corporate career has been the design of the US AI-and-national-security policy framework**.

**Lawrence Joseph Ellison** (born 1944) — the founder, Executive Chairman, and CTO of **Oracle Corporation**, the principal Trump-aligned senior technology executive of the pre-2024 period, the **financier of the Skydance-Paramount media acquisition** that consolidated CBS News, Paramount Pictures, and the broader Paramount-Skydance entertainment apparatus under his family's control, and **one of the four announced participants in the January 2025 Stargate Project** at the White House. On 10 September 2025, Ellison **briefly became the wealthiest person in the world** at an estimated net worth of $393 billion — the single largest one-day wealth increase on the public record of the Bloomberg Billionaires Index.

**Laurence Douglas "Larry" Fink** (born 1952) — the Chairman and CEO of **BlackRock**, the world's principal asset manager with **$12.5 trillion in assets under management** as of Q1 2026. BlackRock's combined direct-AUM-and-Aladdin-managed-third-party-AUM is **$32 trillion** — that is, **25% of all global investable assets pass through BlackRock's risk-management infrastructure**. Fink is **the integration of the principal global passive-investment infrastructure with the contemporary AI-and-defense-tech capital-deployment programme**.

The three figures, taken together, document the structural form of the contemporary American technological-capital-class formation. The chapter applies the dossier standard to each.

---

# Part One: Eric Schmidt

## I. Origins: Blacksburg, Virginia, and the academic-professional household

Eric Emerson Schmidt was born on **27 April 1955 in Washington, D.C.** His father, **Wilson Emerson Schmidt**, was a professor of economics at **Virginia Tech** — a Cold War academic-economics figure whose career included service as **Deputy Assistant Secretary for International Monetary Affairs in the Nixon Treasury Department** (1969-1970). Wilson Schmidt participated in the post-1971 Bretton Woods-collapse policy decisions that produced the contemporary international monetary system. His mother **Eleanor Schmidt** held a master's degree in psychology.

The family relocated to **Blacksburg, Virginia** when Eric was young; he was raised in the Virginia Tech university-town environment. More credentialed than the Andreessen or Zuckerberg families (Chapters 14, 17), less wealthy than the Thiel or Musk families (Chapters 11, 12), but unambiguously integrated with the senior US government-and-academic-policy network of the period.

**The senior-US-government-policy integration that has characterised Schmidt's post-2016 career was foreshadowed by his father's career.** Wilson Schmidt was a senior Treasury official in the Nixon administration; Eric Schmidt has been a senior advisor to multiple subsequent administrations across the AI-and-defense-policy environment. The structural pattern — academic-economics household to government-technology-policy architect — recurs throughout these biographical chapters.

## II. Princeton, Berkeley, and the early-career formation

Schmidt attended Yorktown High School in Arlington, Virginia, where he was valedictorian. He entered **Princeton University** in 1972 as an architecture student; he switched to electrical engineering and graduated with a BSEE in 1976.

He proceeded to the **University of California, Berkeley** for graduate work in computer science: MS 1979, **PhD Computer Science 1982**. His PhD dissertation addressed the problems of managing distributed software development — technical work that has been documented as a foundational contribution to the software-project-management literature of the period.

Schmidt's pre-Google career spanned twenty-five years across the principal technology companies of the 1976-2001 period:

- **Bell Labs** (briefly, 1976-1977): worked on early Unix systems.
- **Zilog** (briefly, late 1970s): microprocessor design.
- **Xerox PARC** (1979-1983): the legendary research laboratory in Palo Alto where large fractions of the foundational innovations underlying personal computing were developed (the graphical user interface, the Ethernet networking standard, laser printing, object-oriented programming). Schmidt was at PARC during the period of its peak operational significance.
- **Sun Microsystems** (1983-1997): joined as a software manager one year after Sun's founding; rose to **Chief Technology Officer**. Was a principal figure in the development and promotion of the **Java programming language** — the language that, in the post-1995 period, became the principal programming language for enterprise software and Internet applications. The Sun period is documented foundational executive-career formation of Schmidt's subsequent career.
- **Novell** (1997-2001): Chairman and CEO. Led the company through the post-NetWare period of decline. Novell's market position had been eroded by Microsoft's Windows NT; Schmidt's tenure was attempting to restructure a declining technology company in a market environment that did not permit recovery.

## III. Google (2001-2018): the seventeen-year construction of the platform

Schmidt joined **Google as CEO in August 2001** — the product of investor pressure. **Sequoia Capital and Kleiner Perkins**, the principal venture investors in Google's Series A round, had conditioned the funding on the recruitment of an experienced senior executive to provide "adult supervision" to the founders (Brin and Page, both 28).

Schmidt was the "adult supervision" hire. The Schmidt-Brin-Page **triumvirate structure** operated Google through the period of its transformation from a search-engine startup to the principal global advertising-and-information platform. The principal events of the Schmidt-CEO period (2001-2011):

- **2004**: Google IPO at $85 per share; closing first-day price $100.34; market capitalisation $23 billion. The second principal post-dot-com-collapse Internet IPO (after Salesforce.com 2004); it established the post-2000 Internet-company IPO framework.
- **2005**: Acquired **Android Inc.** for $50 million. The Android acquisition produced, in subsequent years, the principal global mobile-phone operating system.
- **2006**: Acquired **YouTube for $1.65 billion** in stock. The YouTube acquisition, executed when YouTube was 18 months old, has been documented as one of the most operationally successful acquisitions in contemporary American business history.
- **2007**: Launched **Android operating system**. By 2015, Android had captured 80% of the global smartphone operating-system market.
- **2008**: Launched **Chrome browser**. By 2020, Chrome had captured 65% of the global web-browser market.
- **2011**: Google acquired Motorola Mobility for $12.5 billion (subsequently sold to Lenovo at a loss; the principal value of the acquisition was the patent portfolio).

In **April 2011**, **Larry Page reclaimed the Google CEO position**; Schmidt transitioned to **Executive Chairman**. The transition was the founders' decision that, after ten years of "adult supervision," they had developed sufficient operational maturity to resume direct executive control.

**2015**: Google restructured into the **Alphabet Inc.** holding-company structure. Schmidt became **Executive Chairman of Alphabet**.

**December 2017**: Schmidt stepped down as Executive Chairman of Alphabet. He remained as Technical Advisor through 2020.

**April 2019**: Schmidt resigned from the Alphabet board.

The seventeen-year Schmidt-Google period is the foundational career-and-wealth formation from which his subsequent activity has proceeded. By 2018, Schmidt had accumulated personal wealth in the $15-20 billion range, the network connecting Silicon Valley with the Washington policy environment, and the credibility to operate as one of the principal technology-industry public figures of the period.

## IV. The Defense Innovation Board (2016-2020)

In **March 2016**, Schmidt was appointed by Secretary of Defense **Ashton Carter** as the **founding chairman of the Defense Innovation Board (DIB)** — per Carter's stated rationale, the structural mechanism for integrating Silicon Valley technology expertise with the Department of Defense's procurement and innovation programmes.

The DIB's membership during Schmidt's chairmanship included senior figures from Silicon Valley (Reid Hoffman, Marne Levine of Facebook), academia (Cass Sunstein of Harvard, multiple other senior figures), and the broader technology-policy environment. The Board's work included recommendations on Pentagon software-development practices, the **Ethical Principles for Artificial Intelligence** (adopted by the DoD in February 2020), and various procurement-reform proposals.

**The conflict-of-interest position**: Schmidt's DIB chairmanship overlapped with **Google's bidding for the Pentagon's Joint Enterprise Defense Infrastructure (JEDI) cloud contract** ($10 billion). The conflict was managed by Schmidt's stated screening of emails: "**There's a rule: I'm not allowed to be briefed about Google or Alphabet business as it relates to the Defense Department.**" The arrangement was far less rigorous than standard government-ethics frameworks require. Schmidt was simultaneously a senior Alphabet figure with major financial interest in Google's federal-contracting position and the chair of the advisory board on Pentagon-Silicon-Valley relations.

In **January 2017**, Schmidt was awarded the **Department of Defense Medal for Distinguished Public Service** by Secretary Carter. The medal is the highest civilian award the Department of Defense grants; its award to Schmidt was the senior US national-security apparatus formally recognising the Silicon-Valley-to-Pentagon bridge Schmidt had constructed.

Schmidt exited the DIB chairmanship in **November 2020**, after four and a half years.

## V. The National Security Commission on Artificial Intelligence (2019-2021)

The most operationally consequential of Schmidt's post-Google positions was his chairmanship of the **National Security Commission on Artificial Intelligence (NSCAI)** — a **congressionally chartered commission** established under the 2019 National Defense Authorization Act to make recommendations on AI in national security.

Schmidt was **chairman** of the NSCAI; **Robert O. Work** (former Deputy Secretary of Defense under both Obama and the first Trump administration) was vice-chair. The Commission's other members included senior figures from the technology industry, the national-security apparatus, and academia.

The NSCAI's **756-page final report** was released in **March 2021**. The principal recommendations:

1. **Leadership in AI is a national-defense priority** that requires major federal funding, policy support, and talent-development programmes.
2. The US should treat AI as the technological-competition foundation of the **strategic rivalry with China**.
3. The Pentagon should increase its AI investment to **at least $8 billion annually by 2025**.
4. The federal government should establish a **National AI Research Resource (NAIRR)** to provide academic researchers with access to the computational and data resources required for advanced AI research.
5. Major expansion of AI-related immigration programmes to recruit foreign AI talent.
6. Major restructuring of US export-control policies to constrain Chinese access to advanced semiconductor technology.

The NSCAI's recommendations have **structured the subsequent US AI policy environment**. The **CHIPS and Science Act (August 2022)**, the **National AI Research Resource pilot programme**, the **October 2022 export-control restrictions on advanced semiconductors to China**, the **October 2023 Biden AI executive order**, and the broader US AI policy framework all derive from the NSCAI's recommendations.

**Eric Schmidt, as a private citizen and former Google CEO, chaired the federal commission whose recommendations designed the US AI policy framework for the next five years.** The commission's structural integration of senior-technology-industry figures with the federal policy apparatus was the mechanism by which the technology industry's preferred policy framework became federal policy.

## VI. The Special Competitive Studies Project (2021-present)

In **2021**, Schmidt launched the **Special Competitive Studies Project (SCSP)** as the post-NSCAI continuation of the AI-and-national-security policy programme — the **principal post-NSCAI think tank for the China-AI-competition policy framework**.

The SCSP is **explicitly modelled on the 1950s Rockefeller Brothers Special Studies Project** — the Nelson Rockefeller-funded study programme of 1956-1960 that produced the **"missile gap" policy framework** that subsequently shaped Kennedy administration defence policy. The Rockefeller Special Studies Project's "missile gap" claim — that the Soviet Union had achieved nuclear-missile superiority over the United States — was, in retrospect, exaggerated; the policy programme it produced (the sharp increase in US strategic-missile production, the various counterforce-doctrine elaborations of the early 1960s) exceeded the actual underlying threat.

Schmidt's SCSP has been characterised by critics as the contemporary equivalent — an exaggerated framing of the China-AI-competition threat that produces policy outcomes (major federal AI investment, export-control restrictions, AI-industry deregulation) benefiting the senior US technology-industry actors who funded the project. The accuracy of the China-AI-competition framing is contested; the consequences of the SCSP's policy programme have been favourable to Schmidt's personal investment portfolio.

The SCSP's principal publications: *Mid-Decade Challenges to National Competitiveness* (2022), *Generative AI: The Future of Innovation Power* (2023), and various subsequent reports. Funded by the **Eric and Wendy Schmidt Fund for Strategic Innovation**, the SCSP has been Schmidt's principal personal-policy vehicle.

## VII. The military-technology investment portfolio

Since 2016, Schmidt's personal-investment portfolio has concentrated in **defense-and-national-security-aligned technology companies**:

- **Rebellion Defense** — defense-AI software company; Schmidt was the principal early investor. The company was the Silicon-Valley-to-Pentagon talent pipeline; CEO Chris Lynch had previously led the Defense Digital Service.
- **Istari** — Schmidt's defense-tech venture-capital firm. Funded multiple defense-AI companies.
- **Swift Beat** — Ukraine-defense-aligned drone company.
- **White Stork** — Ukrainian drone-development company that produced the **Merops counter-drone system**. Since 2023, Schmidt has invested directly in Ukraine-defense capacity-building through Ukrainian defense-tech companies.
- **SandboxAQ** — Palo Alto quantum-computing-and-AI company that spun off from Alphabet in 2022. Schmidt is Chairman.
- **Relativity Space** — aerospace-manufacturing company building 3D-printed rockets. Schmidt acquired a **controlling stake in March 2025** and took the **CEO position**.

The defense-tech investment portfolio is the case of **Schmidt's policy-advisory positions producing direct personal-investment benefits**. The NSCAI and SCSP recommended major federal AI investment in defense applications. Schmidt's personal investments are concentrated in the companies that would receive that investment.

## VIII. The Kissinger connection and *The Age of AI*

Schmidt was a close associate of **Henry Kissinger** in the period 2018-2023 (until Kissinger's death on **29 November 2023** at age 100). The Schmidt-Kissinger relationship is one of the most operationally consequential aspects of Schmidt's post-Google career.

The two **co-authored *The Age of AI: And Our Human Future*** (Little, Brown, 2021) with **Daniel Huttenlocher** (the MIT Schwarzman College of Computing Dean, separately an Amazon board member — Chapter 16). The book is the **integration of the contemporary technology-industry-capital-class with the senior Cold-War-era foreign-policy establishment**. Kissinger's *On China* (2011) framework — the proposition that the US-China relationship is the foundational geopolitical structure of the twenty-first century — and Schmidt's NSCAI China-AI-competition framework are **operationally continuous**: the Kissinger-era doctrine of US-China strategic competition has been updated, through Schmidt's policy work, into the contemporary AI-competition framework.

Kissinger personally advocated for AI policy during the final years of his life; his **April 2023 testimony before the US Senate Committee on Armed Services** at age 99 explicitly endorsed Schmidt's NSCAI recommendations and the broader China-AI-competition policy framework.

The Kissinger-Schmidt-Huttenlocher trio is the **multi-generational continuity of the senior US foreign-policy establishment from the Cold War era to the contemporary AI-policy environment**. The same framework — strategic competition with a designated adversary, major federal investment in the relevant technology, restriction of adversary access to critical technology — applied to nuclear weapons (1945-1991), then space and computing (1957-1991), then the Internet and biotechnology (1991-2015), then AI (2015-present). Schmidt is the contemporary executor of the policy framework Kissinger represented across his seventy-year career.

## IX. Schmidt's other contemporary positions

- **Schmidt Sciences** (co-founded 2024 with Wendy Schmidt) — principal philanthropic vehicle for science-funding.
- **Schmidt Futures** (co-founded 2017) — $1 billion committed.
- **Schmidt Ocean Institute** — ocean-research philanthropic vehicle.
- **Schmidt Family Foundation** — established 2006.
- **Co-owner of the Washington Commanders** (NFL franchise) — part of the **Josh Harris investment group that purchased the franchise for $6.05 billion in 2023**.

The Schmidt philanthropic portfolio is larger than those of Bezos, Musk, or Zuckerberg. Wendy Schmidt has been the principal operational manager of the Schmidt Family Foundation; her ocean-and-climate-research programmes rank among the most significant private climate-science-funding programmes globally.

## X. Schmidt's current position (May 2026)

- **Forbes 2026 net worth**: **$25-30 billion**.
- **CEO of Relativity Space** (since March 2025).
- **Chairman of SandboxAQ**.
- **Chairman of the Special Competitive Studies Project**.
- **Founding Chairman of the Defense Innovation Board** (former, 2016-2020).
- **Former Chairman of the National Security Commission on Artificial Intelligence** (2019-2021).
- **Member of the National Security Commission on Emerging Biotechnology**.
- **Co-owner of the Washington Commanders**.
- **Founder of Schmidt Futures, Schmidt Sciences, and the Schmidt Ocean Institute**.

Schmidt is the **technology-industry executive whose post-corporate career designed the US AI-and-national-security policy framework**. The line from Wilson Schmidt's Nixon Treasury position to Eric Schmidt's NSCAI chairmanship to the Kissinger collaboration to the Special Competitive Studies Project is sixty years of Schmidt-family integration with the senior US government-policy network.

---

# Part Two: Larry Ellison

## XI. Origins: the South Side of Chicago and the adoption

Lawrence Joseph Ellison was born on **17 August 1944, the Bronx, New York**, to **Florence Spellman** — a 19-year-old unmarried woman of Russian-Jewish descent. His biological father was an Italian-American US Army Air Corps pilot.

Florence gave Larry up for adoption to her aunt and uncle when he was nine months old, after he had contracted pneumonia and Florence could not provide adequate care. Larry was raised by his **adoptive parents Lillian "Lillie" and Louis Ellison** — Florence's aunt and uncle, who lived on the **South Side of Chicago**.

Louis Ellison was a real-estate-agent immigrant from the Crimean region. The Ellisons had immigrated through Ellis Island in the early 1900s and taken the surname "Ellison" from Ellis Island. Louis lost his Chicago real-estate investments in the Depression; by the time of Larry's childhood, the family was of modest economic position. Larry grew up in a two-bedroom apartment in the Jewish middle-class section of Chicago's South Shore neighbourhood.

The household was Reform-Jewish. Larry was bar mitzvah'd in 1957. In subsequent interviews, he has characterised his adoptive father Louis as cold and emotionally distant — Louis having told him, repeatedly, that **he would "never amount to anything."** The **Louis-Ellison-told-me-I-would-never-amount-to-anything narrative** has recurred across fifty years of biographical interviews. The adoptive-father-rejection experience is foundational to Ellison's subsequent business-and-personal trajectory.

Larry met his **biological mother Florence Spellman only once**, when he was 48 years old.

## XII. Education and the early-career formation

Ellison attended South Shore High School (Chicago public school) and entered the **University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign** in 1962. He **dropped out after his adoptive mother Lillie died of cancer in his sophomore year** (1964).

He attended the **University of Chicago** briefly in 1966 and dropped out. He has never completed an undergraduate degree.

In 1966, Ellison **moved to California**. He worked a series of programming jobs at the **Wells Fargo Bank, Fireman's Fund, and Amdahl Corporation** through the late 1960s and early 1970s. By the mid-1970s he had moved to **Ampex Corporation**.

**Ampex's first major project for Ellison was a database for the Central Intelligence Agency, code-named "Oracle"** — the project name that Ellison would take for his own company three years later. The CIA-Oracle-project connection is the **foundational federal-contracting relationship of Ellison's career**: Oracle Corporation's first major customer base was the US intelligence community, and the relational database was developed in major part for federal-government customers.

## XIII. The Oracle founding (1977) and the relational-database doctrine

In **1977**, Ellison co-founded **Software Development Laboratories (SDL)** with **Bob Miner** (Ellison's former supervisor at Ampex) and **Ed Oates**. Initial capital: $2,000 (of which $1,200 was Ellison's).

The technical foundation of Oracle was **Edgar F. Codd's 1970 paper "A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks"** — the IBM research paper that established the theoretical foundation of relational-database systems. The relational-database model had been developed at IBM's San Jose Research Laboratory through the 1970s under Codd's leadership; IBM had developed the principal experimental relational-database system (System R) but had not yet commercialised the relational-database model.

Ellison's insight was that **IBM had not commercialised the relational-database model**, leaving the commercial opportunity open for a startup that could implement it. The SDL team built the Oracle Database (initially named Oracle 2 — there was never a publicly released version 1) and shipped it commercially in **1979** — one year before IBM's own commercial relational-database product (DB2). The Oracle Database was the **first commercially successful relational-database product**; the company's early customer base (largely CIA and DoD) provided the scale that permitted Oracle to expand into the broader enterprise-software market.

The company was renamed **Relational Software, Inc.** in 1979, then **Oracle Systems Corporation** in 1982, then **Oracle Corporation** in 1995. Ellison was **CEO 1977-2014**; **Executive Chairman and CTO since 2014**.

## XIV. Oracle's principal trajectory (1986-2024)

The Oracle corporate trajectory across forty-nine years is one of the most consistent enterprise-software trajectories in contemporary American business history:

- **12 March 1986**: Oracle IPO (one day before Microsoft's IPO of 13 March 1986).
- **1990**: Oracle was nearly destroyed by an **accounting scandal** — revenue-recognition irregularities produced a large earnings restatement and a 70% decline in the stock price. Ellison subsequently rebuilt the company by terminating the senior sales-and-finance executives responsible for the irregularities and implementing more rigorous financial controls.
- **2005**: Acquired **PeopleSoft for $10.3 billion** in a **hostile takeover** that had been contested through eighteen months of litigation. The PeopleSoft acquisition was Ellison's aggressive corporate-warfare doctrine; the hostile-takeover playbook he developed in the PeopleSoft acquisition has been continued through David Ellison's 2025-2026 attempts to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery.
- **2010**: Acquired **Sun Microsystems for $7.4 billion**. The Sun acquisition gave Oracle the **Java programming language and the MySQL database**; both have subsequently been used to extract major licensing fees from the broader technology industry. Oracle's principal post-2010 litigation programme — including the **decade-long Oracle v. Google copyright lawsuit over the Java API** (2010-2021) — derives from the Sun acquisition's intellectual-property portfolio.
- **2014**: Ellison stepped down as CEO; **Mark Hurd and Safra Catz became co-CEOs**. Hurd died of cancer in October 2019; Catz remains as sole CEO.
- **2010s**: Oracle's competitive position declined through the cloud-computing transition — the company **missed the principal cloud-computing wave** (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud) and, by the late 2010s, the industry trade press characterised it as "the legacy database vendor whose principal business is locking customers into expensive on-premises database licenses."

The pre-2024 Oracle position was a declining enterprise-software company whose principal value derived from customer lock-in. The transformation occurred in 2024-2026, through the AI-infrastructure pivot.

## XV. The 2024-2025 AI pivot and the Stargate participation

In **September 2024**, Oracle's stock began a major rally on the company's announced positioning as a principal AI-infrastructure provider. Oracle had, over the post-2010 period, built major cloud-computing data-centre capacity (Oracle Cloud Infrastructure, OCI) that remained underutilised. The AI-training-and-inference workload — requiring much larger computing capacity than conventional database workloads — provided the use case for that underutilised OCI capacity.

**21 January 2025** (one day after Trump's inauguration): Ellison appeared at the **White House with Sam Altman (OpenAI) and Masayoshi Son (SoftBank) to announce the Stargate Project** — the **$500 billion AI-infrastructure investment programme** described in Chapter 13. Trump's verbatim characterisation: "**In the case of Larry, it's well beyond technology, he's sort of CEO of everything.**" Subsequent reporting read this as the working acknowledgment that Ellison's position in the second Trump administration exceeded the formal scope of his Oracle responsibilities.

**September 2025**: Oracle stock jumped 40% in a single day on the announcement of a **$300 billion deal with OpenAI for computing power**. The deal made OpenAI Oracle's largest customer — the largest single enterprise-software contract in contemporary American business history. **Ellison's wealth increased by $89 billion in a single day** — the **largest single-day wealth increase ever documented by the Bloomberg Billionaires Index**.

**10 September 2025**: Ellison briefly became **the wealthiest person in the world**, with estimated net worth of **$393 billion**. He subsequently fell back to second/third position behind Musk and Zuckerberg as Oracle stock corrected toward more realistic valuations of the OpenAI-Oracle contract's discounted cash flow.

**2026**: President Trump appointed Ellison to the **President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology (PCAST)** — the same body David Sacks had previously chaired (Chapter 15). The appointment was the formal integration of Ellison's position with the second Trump administration's science-and-technology policy apparatus.

## XVI. The Skydance-Paramount acquisition: the media-consolidation programme

**David Ellison** (Larry's son, born 1983) had founded **Skydance Media** in **2010** with **initial capital from Larry Ellison**. Skydance had operated through the 2010s as a Hollywood production company; its principal commercial successes were the post-2014 collaboration with Paramount on the *Mission: Impossible* franchise and the production of *Top Gun: Maverick* (2022, the highest-grossing film of that year).

The transformation of the Skydance position occurred in the 2024-2025 period through the **acquisition of Paramount Global**.

**January 2024**: Skydance first bid for Paramount Global. The bid was contested through eighteen months of regulatory-and-negotiation processes:

- **April 2024**: David Ellison personally donated **$929,600 to Joe Biden's reelection effort** — the case of heavy Democratic-aligned political activity during the period of the Paramount bid.
- **August 2024**: Skydance and Paramount announced the merger agreement; financed by Larry Ellison's family trust. The deal structure: the Ellison family trust would acquire a controlling stake in the combined entity (77.5% of voting interest); RedBird Capital Partners (which backs Skydance) would acquire the remaining voting interest. **Pinnacle Media, the Ellison family investment vehicle, would own 77.5 percent of the voting interest currently owned by Shari Redstone**.
- **2024-2025**: Lengthy regulatory-review process at the Federal Communications Commission (FCC), which had jurisdiction over the transfer of CBS-affiliate broadcast licenses.
- **November 2024 - July 2025**: Trump's lawsuit against CBS News over a Kamala Harris interview broadcast during the 2024 campaign produced major regulatory pressure on the deal. **Paramount settled the Trump lawsuit for $16 million in July 2025** — without any apology. The settlement was widely characterised in the trade press as a **portfolio-management decision by Paramount to clear the regulatory path for the Skydance acquisition**.
- **Late July 2025**: Skydance wrote to the FCC committing to "**a comprehensive review of CBS**" including the installation of an ombudsman to evaluate complaints about CBS coverage. The commitment was the platform-monopoly's prospective owner pre-committing to ideological restructuring of the news organisation as a condition of federal regulatory approval.
- **August 2025**: The Paramount-Skydance merger **closed**. **The Ellison family trust became the largest shareholder of the new Paramount Skydance Corporation**; David Ellison became operational head as CEO and Chairman.

The combined entity controls **CBS News, Paramount Pictures, MTV, Nickelodeon, Showtime, Comedy Central, the Paramount+ streaming service**, and the broader Paramount-Viacom content library — **one of the principal global entertainment-and-news-media platforms**.

### The Bari Weiss appointment

In **September 2025**, Paramount-Skydance hired **Bari Weiss** as **editor-in-chief of CBS News**. Weiss is the founder of **The Free Press** (a Substack-based publication that has been one of the principal post-2020 contrarian-journalism platforms) and has been one of the principal public critics of "woke journalism" in the post-2020 period.

The Weiss appointment was the **platform's ideological repositioning**. CBS News had been, throughout the post-1948 broadcast-television period, one of the principal mainstream-network news organisations; the Weiss appointment reoriented it toward the post-2020 contrarian-conservative journalism framework. **Free Press editorial staff have been integrated into CBS News editorial leadership** since September 2025; the editorial direction of CBS News now tracks The Free Press's documented editorial positions.

### The Warner Bros. Discovery bid

**October-December 2025**: Paramount-Skydance bid for Warner Bros. Discovery (the corporate parent of HBO, CNN, the Warner Bros. film studio, and the Discovery cable networks). The bid was **$111 billion**, with Larry Ellison's personal financing providing **$40.4 billion** of the equity component.

**Larry Ellison personally pitched Warner Bros. executives** on the deal — the case of the senior-billionaire-father personally pursuing the acquisition that would consolidate the principal American media-industry assets under his son's operational control.

**Warner Bros. Discovery rejected the bid in favour of Netflix's offer**. Trump publicly supported the Paramount-Skydance bid over Netflix's; the Trump statement that Skydance would give him "an additional $20 million worth of advertising, PSAs or similar programming" was widely characterised in the trade press as an explicit political-payoff demand. David Ellison declined to comment on Trump's claim.

The Warner Bros. Discovery rejection has not stopped the Paramount-Skydance media-consolidation programme. The trajectory of the post-August-2025 period has been the construction of a **vertically-integrated media-and-entertainment platform** that, per multiple subsequent analyses, is intended to rival Disney in entertainment scale and outpace Fox in ideological influence.

## XVII. The TikTok stake and the political-economic integration

**September 2025**: Trump announced a deal under which Oracle would obtain a sizeable minority stake in the US TikTok operations as part of the divestiture mandated by the **ByteDance-divestiture legislation** (signed by Biden in April 2024).

The TikTok deal structure: Oracle would provide cloud-computing infrastructure for the US TikTok operations and would obtain a major equity stake in the US-operating entity. The deal was negotiated through Ellison's direct Trump relationship; Trump's characterisation of Ellison at the announcement: "**Larry Ellison is one of them. He's involved. He's a great guy.**"

The significance: the TikTok deal gave Oracle access to **150 million US TikTok users' behavioural data** through the cloud-infrastructure contract. The combination of:

- **Oracle's existing federal-contracting infrastructure** (CIA, DoD, intelligence community)
- **The $300 billion OpenAI contract** (compute infrastructure)
- **The Paramount-Skydance media platform** (CBS News, Paramount+, broader entertainment apparatus)
- **The TikTok cloud-and-data position** (150 million US users)

produces the **most integrated single technology-and-media-and-surveillance-and-AI position of any contemporary American billionaire**. Structurally analogous to the Hearst newspaper empire of the early twentieth century, scaled to the platform-monopoly environment.

## XVIII. Ellison's political activity: the consistent Trump alignment

The Ellison political-activity record is **the most consistently Republican-aligned of the senior-billionaire technology class in the post-2012 period**.

**Through 2012**: bipartisan donor (donated to both parties).

**Since 2016**: Republican-aligned:

- One of the **top donors ($4 million by February 2016)** to **Conservative Solutions PAC**, the super PAC supporting Marco Rubio's 2016 presidential campaign.
- **2017**: Donated **$16.6 million to the Friends of the Israel Defense Forces (FIDF)** — the **largest donation in the organisation's history**. Ellison's verbatim statement: "**Since Israel's founding, we have called on the brave men and women of the IDF to defend our home.**"
- **2020 election**: Per *Washington Post* (May 2022), Ellison **participated in a conference call days after the 2020 election that focused on strategies for challenging the legitimacy of the vote**. Other participants on the call included **Fox News host Sean Hannity, Senator Lindsey Graham, Trump personal attorney Jay Sekulow, and James Bopp (attorney for True the Vote)**. The Washington Post cited court documents and a participant on the call. The post-election conference call is Ellison's direct participation in the operational planning for the 2020 election-result-contestation programme.
- **2024 cycle**: Heavy donations to Trump campaign and Trump-aligned PACs.
- **2025**: Ongoing role as principal Trump-administration AI-policy advisor; PCAST appointment.

The Ellison political-activity record is **much more consistent than the Musk, Andreessen, or Sacks records**. Ellison has been a Republican-aligned political donor for a decade; the post-2024 alignment is the **continuation, not the inception, of a long-standing political programme**.

## XIX. Ellison's personal life

- **Marriages**: Four prior marriages — Adda Quinn (1967-1974), Nancy Wheeler Jenkins (1977-1978), Barbara Boothe (1983-1986; mother of David and Megan), Melanie Craft (2003-2010), and Nikita Kahn (2015-2020). Currently married to **Jolin (Keren) Ellison** (née Zhu, alumna of University of Michigan); they have four children. Ellison is 80; Jolin Ellison is 50 years younger.
- **Children**: **David Ellison** (b. 1983) — Skydance / Paramount Skydance CEO; **Megan Ellison** (b. 1986) — founder of **Annapurna Pictures** (the independent film production company that has produced *Zero Dark Thirty*, *The Master*, *American Hustle*, and various other prestigious independent films); four younger children with Jolin.
- **Properties**: owns **98% of the Hawaiian island of Lānaʻi** (acquired June 2012 for $300 million). The Lānaʻi acquisition is one of the most major single private-island acquisitions in contemporary US history; Ellison has operated the island as a major private development project for fourteen years. He also owns extensive Malibu real estate; the **Porcupine Creek estate in Rancho Mirage, California** (the Trump-fundraiser-hosting residence); multiple yachts including the 192-foot *Musashi*; and other major residential and commercial real-estate holdings.

## XX. Ellison's stated political-economic views

The Ellison public-statement record on political-economic views is much more explicit than that of most other senior figures covered here. The principal quotations:

- On surveillance (Oracle investor presentation, 2024): "**Citizens will be on their best behavior, because we're constantly recording and reporting everything that's going on.**" One of the most explicit statements of the surveillance-society doctrine by any senior contemporary American technology executive.
- On Edward Snowden (2014): "**Snowden had yet to identify a single person who had been 'wrongly injured' by the NSA's data collection.**"
- On Israel (2017): "**Since Israel's founding, we have called on the brave men and women of the IDF to defend our home.**"

**Ellison's documented public statements on surveillance, intelligence-community deference, and Israeli national security are operationally aligned with the broader policy positions the post-2024 Trump administration has implemented.** He is the senior-billionaire technology executive whose political-economic positions most explicitly match the second Trump administration's policy programme.

## XXI. Ellison's current position (May 2026)

- **Forbes 2026 net worth**: **$201-203 billion** (sixth-richest globally as of early 2026; down from the September 2025 peak of $393 billion as Oracle stock has corrected).
- **Oracle Executive Chairman and CTO**.
- **Paramount Skydance controlling shareholder** (through the Ellison family trust).
- **Stargate Project principal participant**.
- **President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology member**.
- **TikTok US operations minority equity holder** (via Oracle).
- **The principal Trump-aligned senior technology executive of the post-2024 period**.

# Part Three: Larry Fink

## XXII. Origins: Van Nuys, California, and the shoe-store household

Laurence Douglas "Larry" Fink was born on **2 November 1952, Los Angeles, California**. His father, **Frederick Fink**, owned a shoe store in **Van Nuys** (San Fernando Valley, Los Angeles). His mother, **Lila Fink**, was an English professor at **California State University, Northridge**.

The household was Jewish, middle-class, and oriented toward educational achievement. Fink has characterised his Van Nuys childhood as the working-suburban-American-Jewish-family environment of the post-1945 Southern California period — comparable to the Andreessen rural-Wisconsin upbringing (Chapter 14) or the Zuckerberg Westchester upbringing (Chapter 17) in scale, but Southern Californian in cultural context.

## XXIII. UCLA and the early Wall Street career

Fink attended **Birmingham High School** in Van Nuys. He proceeded to the **University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA)**, where he completed a BA in Political Science (1974) and an MBA in **Real Estate** (1976).

The UCLA MBA in Real Estate is the foundation of Fink's subsequent career. His real-estate training prefigured what followed — throughout his early career, he was a **fixed-income-securities specialist** with deep expertise in mortgage-backed securities.

Fink joined **First Boston** in 1976 as a bond trader. He rose, over twelve years, to become **Head of the Taxable Bond Division and a member of the First Boston Management Committee**. At First Boston, **Fink helped pioneer the mortgage-backed securities (MBS) market** — the financial instruments that would, in the 1980s and 2000s, become foundational to American consumer-mortgage finance.

The Fink contribution at First Boston was the development of **collateralised mortgage obligations (CMOs)** — the structured-finance instruments that subdivided mortgage-backed securities into tranches with different risk-return characteristics. The CMO market, which Fink helped construct at First Boston in the early 1980s, subsequently grew to $9 trillion in outstanding securities by 2008 — that is, the structured-finance market Fink had helped create was the principal capital channel for the post-1980 American consumer-mortgage industry.

## XXIV. The 1986 First Boston loss and the BlackRock founding

In **1986**, Fink's bond-trading desk lost **$100 million in a single quarter** through an interest-rate hedging mistake. The loss ended his First Boston career; he was demoted from his senior position and subsequently left the firm.

The 1986 loss is the **foundational professional formation of Fink's career**. It taught him that the principal risk in financial-services firms is **inadequate risk-management infrastructure** — the kind that permits unidentified concentrations of risk to accumulate in portfolio positions. The BlackRock founding doctrine was structured around the principle of **comprehensive risk management as a core operational competence**.

In **1988**, Fink co-founded **BlackRock** with seven co-founders: **Robert Kapito, Susan Wagner, Barbara Novick, Ben Golub, Hugh Frater, Ralph Schlosstein, and Keith Anderson**. The new firm was established within **Blackstone Group** as a subsidiary; **Stephen Schwarzman** (Blackstone CEO) provided the initial capital.

The BlackRock founding doctrine: **fixed-income asset management with sophisticated risk-management technology**. The firm's principal operational competence would be the integration of portfolio management with risk-analysis software; the firm would charge management fees while limiting the risk of large-loss events through systematic risk-management protocols.

The Blackstone-BlackRock relationship continued until **1994**, when **BlackRock split from Blackstone** after Fink and Schwarzman disagreed over equity compensation structure. The split was acrimonious; Fink has subsequently described it as "a divorce." The family-name origins of the post-split entity: **Blackstone retained "stone"; the new firm took "rock"** (Schwarzman's joke that Fink "rocked" the Blackstone-BlackRock partnership).

**1999**: BlackRock IPO on the NYSE at **$14 per share**.

## XXV. BlackRock's expansion (1999-2024): from fixed-income manager to global-asset-management infrastructure

The BlackRock corporate trajectory across twenty-seven years is one of the most successful asset-management expansions in contemporary financial-services history:

- **2006**: Acquired **Merrill Lynch Investment Managers** for **$9.8 billion**. The acquisition doubled BlackRock's AUM from $500 billion to $1 trillion.
- **2008-2009 financial crisis**: BlackRock was the **principal contractor for the Federal Reserve's and Treasury's emergency-asset-management programmes**. The firm managed the wind-down of **Bear Stearns ($30 billion in assets), AIG ($180 billion in assets)**, and various other crisis-period portfolios. The 2008-2009 federal contracts were the **foundational government-relationship event of BlackRock's subsequent position**: the firm established itself as the principal contractor for federal emergency-financial-management programmes — a position maintained across the subsequent fifteen years.
- **June 2009**: Acquired **Barclays Global Investors for $13.5 billion**. The Barclays acquisition brought **iShares** — the principal exchange-traded-fund (ETF) franchise globally. The iShares acquisition **transformed BlackRock from a fixed-income firm into the principal global passive-investment manager**.
- **2010s**: Sustained AUM growth through the global passive-investment expansion. BlackRock's AUM passed $1 trillion in 2009, $4 trillion in 2014, $6 trillion in 2017, $8 trillion in 2020.
- **2024**: Acquired **Global Infrastructure Partners (GIP)** for **$12.5 billion** — BlackRock's expansion into private-infrastructure investment, gaining control of major global airport, port, pipeline, and energy infrastructure assets.
- **2024**: Acquired **HPS Investment Partners** for $12 billion (private credit). The HPS acquisition gave BlackRock major private-credit capacity.
- **2025**: Acquired **Preqin** (private-markets data) for $3.2 billion.

The cumulative effect of the 2024-2025 acquisitions has been the transformation of BlackRock from a principally passive-public-markets asset manager into a **comprehensive private-and-public-markets capital infrastructure** with major positions in infrastructure (through GIP), private credit (through HPS), and the broader alternative-investments market.

## XXVI. BlackRock's current operational scale (May 2026)

**BlackRock AUM as of Q1 2026**: **$12.5 trillion** — **by far the largest asset manager globally**; twice the AUM of the second-largest firm (Vanguard at $9 trillion). The figure represents **10% of all global investable assets**.

BlackRock is the **principal global passive-investment infrastructure**. Significant fractions of every major listed company globally are held by BlackRock-managed funds. The firm's stewardship voting (Section XXVII below) produces $3 trillion in voting rights at S&P 500 companies.

**Aladdin platform**: BlackRock's proprietary risk-management software. Aladdin (which stands for "**Asset, Liability, Debt, and Derivative Investment Network**") is used by **20,000 portfolio managers globally** (including large fractions of the world's largest pension funds, sovereign-wealth funds, central banks, and private-investment firms). **Aladdin manages $20 trillion in third-party assets** in addition to BlackRock's own $12.5 trillion.

The combined Aladdin-managed-plus-BlackRock-managed asset pool is **$32 trillion** — that is, **25% of all global investable assets pass through BlackRock's risk-management infrastructure**.

**BlackRock occupies a structural position in the contemporary global financial system that has no historical precedent.** The combination of $12.5 trillion in direct asset management and $20 trillion in Aladdin third-party services makes it the principal risk-management-and-capital-allocation infrastructure of global finance. Which assets to overweight, which to underweight, how to model risk, which voting positions to take at portfolio companies — these decisions are the principal mechanism by which global capital is allocated across asset classes, geographies, and industries.

## XXVII. BlackRock's stewardship voting and the corporate-governance position

BlackRock's iShares-and-other-ETF holdings produce **$3 trillion in voting rights at S&P 500 companies**. The stewardship team (60 staff under Vice Chair Sandy Boss) executes the proxy votes — the principal mechanism by which BlackRock influences boards and policies at the world's largest corporations.

**At every major listed company globally, BlackRock is one of the three or four largest shareholders** (along with Vanguard and State Street — the "Big Three" passive-investment managers that collectively control 20% of every major US listed company). The Big Three's combined voting position is the **principal contemporary corporate-governance structure** — far more consequential than dispersed individual shareholders or activist investors.

The Big Three vote in concert. On any major corporate-governance question at a US listed company, their combined position is typically determinative. The decisions are made through informal consultations with stewardship teams.

## XXVIII. The ESG controversy and the 2024 reversal

In **2018-2020**, Fink's annual letters to CEOs articulated the **"stakeholder capitalism" doctrine** — the proposition that corporate decision-making should consider environmental, social, and governance (ESG) factors alongside shareholder returns. The 2020 letter was particularly emphatic on climate-related risk; it argued that climate change represented a "structural, long-term crisis" that required corporate response.

The Fink ESG positioning was the principal global passive-investment manager publicly advocating for corporate-governance reform across the broader corporate landscape. The consequence: large fractions of US corporate boards adopted ESG-related governance frameworks in the 2020-2022 period in operational response to BlackRock's stated voting positions.

**2021-2023**: Republican-aligned political response. Multiple Republican state attorneys general (**Florida's James Uthmeier, Texas's Ken Paxton**, and others) accused BlackRock of "violating fiduciary duty" through ESG investment policies. Multiple Republican-controlled state pension funds (**Texas, Florida, Tennessee**, and others) **withdrew $4 billion in total from BlackRock**. The Republican argument: BlackRock's ESG positioning imposed a partisan-political-ideological framework on the pension-fund-investment decisions of Republican-state public employees.

**2024**: BlackRock reversed its ESG positioning:

- The **2024 annual letter abandoned the "stakeholder capitalism" framing**; it focused on "investing for retirement" as the firm's central operational mission.
- **February 2024**: BlackRock **withdrew from the Climate Action 100+ investor coalition** — the international investor coalition that had been one of the principal climate-related corporate-governance initiatives.
- BlackRock subsequently withdrew from various other ESG-related industry initiatives.

**2025-2026**: Fink's annual letters have emphasised **AI infrastructure, infrastructure investment, and the "tokenisation of every asset"** — the turn away from ESG and toward the new-world-order capital-deployment programme. The **2026 letter** ("**Own Assets or Miss the AI Wealth Boom**") explicitly framed AI-infrastructure investment as the principal long-term investment opportunity of the contemporary period.

The ESG reversal is the **case in which the principal global passive-investment manager reversed its corporate-governance positioning under coordinated Republican-state political pressure**. It has altered the corporate-governance environment at every major US listed company and has sharply reduced the institutional pressure for climate-related corporate-governance reform.

## XXIX. The AI Infrastructure Partnership (AIP) and the OpenAI-Microsoft-Nvidia integration

The most operationally consequential aspect of BlackRock's 2024-2026 strategic positioning is the **AI Infrastructure Partnership (AIP)** — the capital-deployment programme that has integrated BlackRock's passive-investment infrastructure with the OpenAI-Microsoft-Stargate AI buildout.

**September 2024**: BlackRock, **Global Infrastructure Partners**, **MGX (the UAE sovereign-wealth fund operationally controlled by Sheikh Tahnoun bin Zayed)**, and **Microsoft** announced the **AI Infrastructure Partnership (AIP)** — a **$30 billion initial fund commitment for AI-infrastructure investment**.

**March 2025**: AIP expanded to include **NVIDIA and xAI** (Musk's AI company). The expanded AIP was framed as the principal global AI-infrastructure capital channel.

**October 2025**: BlackRock announced a **$40 billion AI data-centre investment partnership** with Microsoft and the **MGX UAE sovereign-wealth fund**. The deal was the structural integration of BlackRock's passive-investment infrastructure with the OpenAI-Microsoft-Stargate AI buildout.

The combined AIP-plus-Stargate-plus-AI-data-centre capital deployment is **$600-700 billion across the 2025-2029 period** — the **largest single capital-deployment programme in contemporary American history outside the federal budget**.

**BlackRock has, through the AIP and the related partnerships, integrated its passive-investment infrastructure with the AI buildout at the principal capital-deployment scale.** The structural consequence: BlackRock's pension-fund clients, sovereign-wealth-fund clients, and broader passive-investment customer base are, through these programmes, **the principal capital sources for the AI-infrastructure buildout**. The contemporary American AI buildout is **financed by the passive-investment capital that BlackRock and the Big Three control on behalf of their pension-fund and sovereign-wealth-fund clients**.

## XXX. BlackRock's central bank and sovereign-wealth-fund relationships

BlackRock's institutional position is expanded by its **central-bank and sovereign-wealth-fund relationships**:

- **Central bank relationships**: BlackRock is a contractor to multiple central banks. The **Federal Reserve uses BlackRock for emergency-asset-management programmes** — most consequentially, the **2020 COVID-19 Federal Reserve corporate-bond-purchase programme was managed by BlackRock**. The **European Central Bank, Bank of Japan**, and various other central banks have BlackRock advisory relationships.
- **Sovereign-wealth-fund relationships**: BlackRock manages large portions of the **Norwegian Government Pension Fund Global, the Saudi Public Investment Fund, the Abu Dhabi Investment Authority (ADIA), the China Investment Corporation**, and most of the other principal sovereign-wealth funds globally.

**BlackRock is the principal commercial counterparty for the senior global central-bank and sovereign-wealth-fund infrastructure.** Since the 2008-2009 financial crisis, the firm has been integrated with the senior global monetary-and-sovereign-investment architecture.

## XXXI. Fink's political activity and the bipartisan integration

Fink has been **bipartisan** throughout his career. He has donated to both parties and served on policy councils under both Republican and Democratic administrations.

- **2008**: Considered as Treasury Secretary by both McCain and Obama transition teams.
- **2016**: Considered as Treasury Secretary by Trump transition team. Did not take the position.
- **2024-2025**: Closely aligned with the second Trump administration's AI-infrastructure-investment programme. The BlackRock-AIP-Stargate operational integration is the evidence of the alignment.
- **2025**: Appointed to the **President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology (PCAST)**.

The Fink political-activity record is the **bipartisan-policy-integration pattern documented throughout this book**. Across four decades, Fink has operated as a non-partisan figure whose principal political positioning is the protection of BlackRock's institutional position regardless of which party governs.

## XXXII. Fink's personal life and current position (May 2026)

- **Married Lori Fink (née Friedman) since 1974**. Three children: **Joshua Fink** (founder of Enso Capital Management), **Alexander Fink**, and **Elizabeth Fink**.
- Lives in New York City. Sizeable homes in **Aspen, Colorado**, and **North Salem, New York**.
- **Forbes 2026 net worth**: **$2.5-3 billion** (much smaller than the senior-billionaire technology class because Fink's wealth is primarily in BlackRock equity and the firm's executive-compensation pattern reflects the institutional-asset-management compensation structure rather than the founder-equity compensation structure of the technology companies).
- **Chairman and CEO of BlackRock**.
- **Manager of 25% of all global investable assets** through the combined BlackRock-AUM and Aladdin-managed-third-party assets.
- **Principal capital-deployment coordinator** for the AI Infrastructure Partnership and the broader new-world-order AI-buildout programme.
- **PCAST member**.

Fink is the **integration of the principal global passive-investment infrastructure with the contemporary AI-and-defense-tech capital-deployment programme**. BlackRock's $12.5 trillion AUM, the $32 trillion Aladdin-managed asset pool, the AIP capital-deployment programme, and the federal-government emergency-asset-management contract record produce a structural position in the global financial system with no historical precedent — one that operates outside the democratic-accountability framework applied to nominally public institutions.

# Part Four: The structural pattern

## XXXIII. The three figures and the network completion

The three figures Chapter 18 has documented — Schmidt, Ellison, Fink — share several structural features that, taken together, document the integration of the senior technology-industry-capital class with the broader financial-and-defense-industry infrastructure:

1. **Each is a senior-technology-or-finance executive whose post-corporate-founding career has been focused on the integration of his institutional position with the federal-government policy apparatus**. Schmidt through the DIB, NSCAI, and SCSP; Ellison through the Trump administration AI-and-media policy programme; Fink through the BlackRock-federal-reserve-and-Treasury contracting relationships.

2. **Each has operated, in the documented period, as a principal capital-deployment coordinator for the contemporary AI buildout**. Schmidt through the Stargate-adjacent defense-tech investment programme; Ellison through Oracle's $300 billion OpenAI contract and the Stargate participation; Fink through the AIP and the $40 billion AI-data-centre partnership.

3. **Each maintains major private positions in the AI-and-defense-technology investment categories** that the federal-policy programmes the same individuals have advised on are designed to support.

4. **Each aligned with the second Trump administration's policy programme in the post-2024 period**, regardless of prior political record. Schmidt was Democratic-aligned through 2024 but aligned with the AI-infrastructure-investment programme of the second Trump administration; Ellison has been the most consistently Republican-aligned of the three since 2016; Fink has been bipartisan and aligned with the AI-infrastructure programme through the AIP regardless of administration.

The three figures demonstrate the **structural integration of the senior technology-industry-capital class with the senior financial-and-defense-industry infrastructure** — the form of the contemporary American political-economic environment.
