# Chapter 20: Alex Karp — Palantir, Anduril, and the Surveillance Layer

The previous nine chapters dossiered the principal named figures of the techno-oligarchy: Thiel, Musk, Altman, Andreessen, the PayPal second tier, Bezos, Zuckerberg, Schmidt-Ellison-Fink, Gates. The evidence covers the man who runs the part of the network that is paid to watch people, and to recommend who should be killed or deported.

**Alexander Caedmon Karp**, co-founder and CEO of Palantir Technologies since 2003, is the public face of the surveillance layer the rest of the network produces, deploys, finances, and sells to governments. He is also one half of the structure: the other half is **Anduril Industries**, founded in 2017 by Palmer Luckey, Trae Stephens, Brian Schimpf, Matt Grimm, and Joe Chen — four of the five with prior Palantir tenure, seeded by Peter Thiel's Founders Fund. Palantir handles the data integration and the targeting recommendations. Anduril handles the drones, the surveillance towers, the autonomous interceptors, and the kill mechanism the targeting recommendations feed into. The two firms together are the instrument by which the AI capabilities documented in Chapters 13, 14, 17, 18, and 21 are being applied to live populations: immigrants inside the United States, Palestinians in Gaza, Ukrainians in the contested east, and the broader category of "targets" generated by the Western military-intelligence apparatus.

The evidence covers Karp at the same standard the book has applied to every preceding figure, and reports the Anduril structure as the adjacent vehicle. The book's position is plain: any AI infrastructure deployed without democratic control over the populations it targets is a danger to those populations and to democratic self-government as a system. Palantir and Anduril have, from 2003 to 2026, built and deployed that infrastructure. The work has been profitable. The people targeted by it have not been consulted.

## I. Origins, family, and the Philadelphia formation

Alexander Caedmon Karp was born on **2 October 1967 in New York City** and raised in Philadelphia. His father, **Robert Joseph Karp**, was a Jewish clinical pediatrician who practised in working-class Philadelphia neighbourhoods. His mother, **Leah Jaynes Karp**, was a Black artist. Both parents organised in civil-rights and labour-rights work across Karp's childhood. The Bloomberg Billionaires Index profile records that the parents "were both involved in social activism." Karp has confirmed this in his own interviews. He has described himself as Jewish; the Black-business press has named him the third-richest Black person in the United States. He carries both identities openly.

He has stated, in interviews dating from the Palantir period, that he is dyslexic and that reading was difficult for him as a child. Despite the dyslexia he became — by his own description and by every available interview — a heavy reader, three hours daily, principally in continental philosophy and political theory.

He attended **Central High School, Philadelphia**, the public magnet school whose alumni include Noam Chomsky. He graduated in 1985, the same school year that included a cohort of subsequent Ivy League and senior professional-class entrants. He was not from money; the family was middle-class Philadelphia professional. He has stated repeatedly, across decades of interviews, that he felt out of place at every institution he entered. The line he uses: *"I can navigate this place, but I am not a part of it."*

The Karp formation is one of very few American billionaire formations to combine a documented progressive-activist family of origin with the eventual role of building the deepest commercial surveillance infrastructure inside the contemporary state. Whether the progressive identity is operatively meaningful or whether it is the cover under which the surveillance work is performed is not adjudicated here. Both are recorded: the formation, and the work. Both are facts. The reader can hold them together.

What the formation does not produce, in Karp's case, is a documented public record of family allegation against him. The Annie-Altman question — the rule from section 9 of GUIDANCE that where a subject's own family member has, on the record, made public allegations against the subject, those allegations are documented in proportion to seriousness and public-record status — does not apply to Karp on the available record. There are no documented public allegations from his immediate family. He is unmarried and has no children. Both parents are now deceased (his father died after a long illness; his mother's death has been less widely reported). Absence of family allegation is not exoneration. The standard is what is on record, not what might be in private.

## II. Education and the Habermas formation

- **BA Philosophy, Haverford College** (1989). Liberal-arts foundation, no documented financial difficulty.
- **JD, Stanford Law School** (1992). It was at Stanford Law that Karp met **Peter Thiel**. The pair, in Thiel's account, argued constantly: Thiel the libertarian-leaning *Stanford Review* founder; Karp the self-identified socialist quoting Marx on alienated labour. Both have said they bonded over their shared discomfort with Stanford itself. Karp has called Stanford Law "the worst three years of my adult life." The friendship survived. It is the load-bearing relationship of his subsequent career.
- **PhD, neoclassical social theory, Johann Wolfgang Goethe University Frankfurt** (2002). Supervised by **Jürgen Habermas**, the leading living figure of the Frankfurt School. The dissertation examined aggression in the everyday life-world — how aggression structures social identity, drawing on Theodor Adorno's earlier work on the authoritarian personality. Karp spent close to a decade in Frankfurt. He is fluent in German, speaks French, and conducts half his daily working life in German with the Austrian assistants who travel with him.

The Habermas connection is the single most load-bearing intellectual fact about Karp. Habermas's lifelong philosophical project — the theory of communicative action, the rational reconstruction of the democratic public sphere, the defence of post-war Enlightenment universalism against postmodern relativism on the left and fascism on the right — is the philosophical opposite of what Palantir has subsequently built. Habermas's central argument is that democratic legitimacy requires public reasoning conducted under conditions of unforced consent. Palantir's product is the systematic elimination of unforced consent: the integration of personal data across institutional silos, on government contracts no affected population consented to, in service of targeting decisions made by intelligence services, immigration enforcement, and foreign militaries.

That Karp completed a doctorate under Habermas and then built the operative opposite of the Habermasian public sphere is the central biographical fact about him. The record is not softened here. He cannot have not understood, while working under Habermas across the late 1990s, what Habermas's framework required. He chose, after Frankfurt, to build the negation of that framework, and to be paid billions of dollars for it.

His published writing makes the inversion explicit. His 2025 book ***The Technological Republic: Hard Power, Soft Belief, and the Future of the West***, co-authored with Palantir Head of Corporate Affairs Nicholas Zamiska, argues a position incompatible with the Habermasian framework. The post-1960s American academic and tech-corporate elite, the book argues, has lost the cultural confidence to build. It has fragmented into identity-politics tribes. It must be replaced by an alliance of engineers and national-security professionals willing to wage hard-power competition against China. Palantir's own 2026 corporate summary of the book contains the line: ***"The postwar neutering of Germany and Japan must be undone."***

That sentence, published by a company whose CEO holds a Frankfurt doctorate from the philosopher most identified with the post-war German democratic settlement, is the verdict on what Karp's trajectory means. The student of Habermas calls for the undoing of the post-war German democratic settlement that Habermas spent six decades defending. The verdict writes itself.

## III. The founding — what 2003 actually was

The captured-press founding story has Palantir as a Silicon Valley fraud-detection startup that pivoted after 9/11 to counter-terrorism. The story is half-true. The half it omits is what made the company possible.

**Palantir was incorporated in Silicon Valley in 2003** by five co-founders: Peter Thiel, Alex Karp, Joe Lonsdale (Stanford CS), Stephen Cohen (Stanford CS), and Nathan Gettings (PayPal engineer). Karp was 35. He had no business or technical training. Thiel — Karp's friend from Stanford Law, by then post-PayPal and running Clarium Capital — chose Karp to run the company precisely because Karp had no Silicon Valley background. The early Palantir hires were former Stanford classmates, ex-PayPal engineers, and intelligence-community veterans introduced through Thiel's Washington network.

**The founding capital was the Central Intelligence Agency.** In 2005 Palantir received seed investment from **In-Q-Tel**, the CIA's venture-capital arm. Reported figures vary: BBC Science Focus cites $1.25 million; multiple subsequent sources cite a total commitment closer to $2 million across the first In-Q-Tel cycle. The figures matter less than the structure. In-Q-Tel exists, since 1999, for one operational purpose: to seed-fund companies the CIA wants to subsequently buy from, on faster timelines than the standard federal procurement cycle permits. Palantir was an In-Q-Tel investment first, a CIA contractor second, and a publicly disclosed Silicon Valley startup third. The CIA helped Palantir build its first product, **Palantir Gotham**, to CIA specifications. The CIA was the product's first customer, beginning in 2008.

The captured-press framing — Palantir as a private startup that subsequently attracted government interest — inverts the order of operations. The company was an arm of the US intelligence-procurement apparatus from its first quarter, dressed in the venture-capital legal form for reasons of speed and deniability. The "private startup" framing is the cover. Palantir is the cleanest documented contemporary case of a US intelligence-aligned private corporation built to launder intelligence procurement through Silicon Valley.

**The founding's structural lie**, in the book's terms, is the In-Q-Tel relationship presented as a normal early venture investment. Every contemporary technology company that grew through In-Q-Tel — and many did — has the same structural quality. Palantir is the case where the founders were most ideologically articulate about what they were doing and why. Thiel has stated, repeatedly across two decades, that Palantir was built to "defend the West" against terrorist threats. Karp has stated that the company exists to provide the West with the data-integration capability the West's intelligence services would otherwise lack. Neither has denied the CIA founding investment, or the CIA-customer founding role, because neither needs to deny it. The relationship is the company's competitive moat.

**The In-Q-Tel apparatus.** In-Q-Tel was founded in 1999 by the CIA, with founding direction from Norman Augustine (former Lockheed Martin CEO) and Gilman Louie (former Hasbro Interactive). The structure was deliberately chosen to evade the standard federal procurement timeline: rather than the CIA awarding contracts to existing companies, In-Q-Tel would take equity stakes in startups whose technology the CIA wanted to develop, accelerate that development through a structured engagement programme, and then enable the CIA and other intelligence agencies to buy the resulting products as commercial-off-the-shelf software. By 2026 In-Q-Tel has made over 800 investments. Its portfolio includes — beyond Palantir — companies such as **Keyhole Inc.** (acquired by Google in 2004, becoming Google Earth); **Recorded Future**; **MongoDB**; **Cloudera**; **Decibel.ai**; and a long list of senior contemporary commercial-data and AI-infrastructure firms. The In-Q-Tel structure is the recorded mechanism by which the senior US intelligence community has, across a quarter-century, seeded private companies into the commercial market with capabilities the intelligence community wanted as customer products. The captured-press framing of these companies as "private startups" obscures the structural relationship. Palantir is one of the largest and most operationally exposed cases.

The deeper structural point: the same intelligence-and-defence-procurement infrastructure that funded the Internet's early development (the DARPA-funded ARPANET network of the 1960s-1970s; the National Security Agency's funding of early cryptography research; the CIA-and-Navy funding of the early Silicon Valley semiconductor industry) has funded the contemporary AI capability buildout. The continuity is documented across six decades of US technology-policy literature. Palantir is the contemporary case where the founders have been most explicit about the continuity and most operationally integrated with it.

## IV. The corporate trajectory, 2003-2026

**2003.** Palantir Technologies incorporated in Silicon Valley. Five co-founders: Thiel, Karp, Lonsdale, Cohen, Gettings. Karp as CEO; Thiel as Chairman; the engineering co-founders building the original ontology system.

**2005.** In-Q-Tel seed investment. Gotham development begins around the technical concept of an "ontology" — a unified data model that integrates disparate intelligence sources (signals intelligence, human intelligence, financial-transaction data, communications metadata, biometric records) into a single queryable structure. Gotham's distinguishing feature is not analytical sophistication. It is integration. Other firms could analyse intelligence data; Palantir's product was that the previously siloed databases of the US intelligence community could be made jointly queryable. The integration was the product.

**2008.** Gotham deployed by the CIA. The same year, the company begins selling Gotham to other US federal agencies. The Defense Intelligence Agency, the FBI, and the NSA become customers across 2008-2010. The intelligence-community footprint grows organically through internal CIA referrals.

**2009.** ICE begins contracting with Palantir. The initial use case is tracking a Mexican drug-cartel network that had killed a US ICE agent. ICE remains a Palantir customer thereafter, and Palantir's ICE-contract revenue grows across the 2009-2026 period through both the Obama and Trump administrations. The contracts survive both 2018 and 2025 employee protests inside Palantir.

**May 2011.** Palantir software is reportedly used in the operation that killed Osama bin Laden. The reports are not denied by Palantir or by US intelligence; the company's value to the intelligence community is confirmed in the trade press from this point onward, and the subsequent contract growth reflects the confirmation.

**2013-2014.** Palantir's commercial platform, **Palantir Foundry**, is developed and launched. Foundry is the commercial version of the ontology architecture, sold to Fortune 500 customers as the integration layer for internal corporate databases. BP, Merck, Airbus, and a growing list of senior pharmaceutical and energy infrastructure firms become customers. The commercial growth funds further engineering across the subsequent decade; the government contracts remain the larger absolute revenue stream.

**2015.** Palantir's private valuation reaches $20 billion across multiple funding rounds totalling around $1.6 billion. Principal investors include Founders Fund (Thiel's vehicle), Tiger Global Management, and a list of additional VC firms. The 2015 round closes against the backdrop of growing trade-press scepticism about whether Palantir would ever achieve profitability; the company will not post a profitable year until 2023.

**2017-2018.** Internal employee protests against Palantir's ICE contracts. The protests are met with management reassurance that ICE's enforcement-and-removal arm is not the customer (Palantir's stated position is that it works with ICE's investigative arm only). The contracts continue. The internal-employee resistance does not move management.

**2019.** Palantir wins the US Army's **Distributed Common Ground System-Army (DCGS-A)** battlefield-intelligence platform contract, worth $876 million. The contract marks Palantir's transition from intelligence-community supplier to senior Pentagon platform-supplier. DCGS-A is the principal US Army battlefield-data-fusion system; the contract win is over the legacy defence prime that had previously held it.

**30 September 2020.** Palantir conducts a direct listing on the New York Stock Exchange at an opening market capitalisation above $15 billion. The direct-listing structure, rather than the standard IPO underwriting, reflects the founders' priority of retaining voting control through a three-class share structure including the supervoting **Class F shares** held by Karp, Thiel, and Cohen. The Class F structure gives the three founders voting control disproportionate to their economic equity stakes — the standard tech-founder mechanism by which public-market shareholder discipline is structurally evaded.

**2020-2022.** COVID-era contracting. Palantir is awarded multiple federal contracts for vaccine-distribution logistics, public-health data integration, and supply-chain monitoring. The contracts expand the HHS and CDC footprint and bring Palantir's tools into NHS England (the Foundry deployment on COVID data analysis became the active foundation of the later NHS Federated Data Platform contract).

**2022.** DHS awards Palantir a $139.3 million contract for "investigative case management operations, maintenance support services and custom enhancements" — the foundational ICE infrastructure the company has been building since 2009. The contract remains in force as of January 2026.

**November 2023.** Palantir wins the **NHS England Federated Data Platform** contract, valued at up to **£330 million over seven years**. The contract gives Palantir a central role in the integration of NHS patient data across hospital trusts in England. It is opposed at the point of award by **doctorsforthe.nhs**, the **British Medical Association**, the patient-advocacy group **Foxglove Legal**, **Open Rights Group**, and a cross-party group of MPs. The opposition focuses on three points: that Palantir's IDF and ICE contracting record makes the company an unsuitable steward of NHS data; that the procurement process narrowed the competitive field through specifications written around Palantir's Foundry product; and that no public consultation was held with the patient populations whose data the platform would integrate. The contract proceeds. By May 2026 the Federated Data Platform is operational across multiple NHS England trusts. The contract is the largest single foreign-government commercial contract in Palantir's history.

**The administration-relations record across 2017-2024.** Through the first Trump administration (2017-2021) Palantir's ICE relationship deepened despite the public-employee opposition. Through the Biden administration (2021-2025) Palantir's federal contracting grew in the intelligence and Pentagon channels even as the Biden DHS retained the Trump-era ICE contracts without modification. Through the second Trump administration (2025–) Palantir's contracting has accelerated, with the ImmigrationOS award, the USCIS contract, and the broader $900 million+ in federal awards in the first nine months. The pattern across both parties is consistent: Palantir's product is sufficiently embedded inside the federal data infrastructure that no incoming administration has been operationally able to displace it. The bipartisan portfolio-management strategy at the donor level has produced bipartisan procurement-continuation at the contracting level.

**Post-October 2023.** The Gaza period. Palantir publicly affirms its support for the IDF in posts on X and LinkedIn after the 7 October 2023 Hamas attack on Israel and the subsequent Israeli ground invasion of Gaza. The company's January 2024 board meeting is held in Tel Aviv as a deliberate public signal. Karp publicly defends the company's IDF work in multiple interviews. Independent reporting (The Nation's April 2024 investigation by James Bamford; +972 Magazine's contemporaneous reporting on the IDF's **Gospel** and **Lavender** AI-targeting systems) documents that Palantir's IDF relationship includes the supply of advanced data-mining and targeting capability. Palantir, in its January 2024 public statement, denies specific involvement with the Lavender database while confirming its IDF partnership in other defence and intelligence contexts.

By April 2024 the official Gaza Health Ministry death toll exceeds 30,000 Palestinians killed since 7 October 2023. By January 2026 the Berkeley Political Review reports the toll has exceeded 70,000 dead. The Lancet's January 2025 estimate, accounting for indirect deaths from infrastructure destruction, famine, and untreated disease, puts the total at 186,000 excess deaths. The +972 Magazine investigation, sourced from six serving IDF intelligence officers, documents that Lavender marked 37,000 Palestinian men as suspected Hamas operatives in the first months of the war, with the IDF accepting civilian-casualty ratios of up to 20 civilians killed per junior Hamas target — a ratio with no precedent in the post-1949 Geneva Conventions framework. Palantir's wider IDF partnership is one American AI-defence company providing targeting infrastructure to a foreign military that the International Court of Justice, in its January 2024 provisional-measures ruling on South Africa v. Israel, found there was plausible cause to investigate for genocide. Palantir has continued the partnership.

**2024.** *The Economist* names Karp **CEO of the Year**. Palantir's stock rises over 600% across the calendar year. Karp sells **$1.88 billion** of his Palantir stock in 2024, including **$1.4 billion in the four weeks around the November 2024 presidential election**. He is, by fiscal-year reporting, the highest-paid CEO of a publicly traded US company by "compensation actually paid," at **$6.8 billion** — a figure inflated by the post-2017 stock-grant accounting rules but reflecting genuine wealth extraction from the company.

**17 April 2025.** ICE awards Palantir a **$30 million sole-source contract** to develop the **Immigration Lifecycle Operating System (ImmigrationOS)** — a platform to give ICE "near real-time visibility" on self-deportations, to streamline identification and apprehension of removal-prioritised individuals, and to make deportation logistics more efficient. The contract is awarded without competitive bidding. The ICE justification document states that Palantir is the "only source" capable of building the system. The contract is structured as a modification to the existing sole-source ICM deal, which by 2026 has grown to over **$145 million** cumulative. The ImmigrationOS prototype is delivered in September 2025; the contract runs through September 2027. ImmigrationOS pulls data from the IRS, the Social Security Administration, passport databases, and Automated License Plate Recognition records to build profiles of deportation targets. The accuracy of the classifications is unknown; no independent audit of ImmigrationOS has been permitted. The ACLU has documented that the system is being used to target US citizens, lawful permanent residents, and others whose immigration status the system has misclassified.

**December 2025.** Palantir wins a separate contract with **US Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS)**. With this contract, Palantir holds infrastructure on both the enforcement arm (ICE) and the benefits arm (USCIS) of US immigration administration.

**2025.** Total Palantir federal contracting since January 2025: over **$900 million** (per *NYT* August 2025 reporting cited by the American Immigration Council).

**Late 2025.** Palantir market capitalisation peaks above **$400 billion** before settling at **$325 billion** by May 2026. Karp's personal net worth peaks above **$18 billion** at the 2025 high. Fiscal 2025 revenue: **$4.475 billion** (56% year-over-year). 2026 guidance: **$7.65-7.66 billion**. Karp has sold over **$3 billion** of Palantir stock since the 2020 IPO. He buys a **3,700-acre compound near Aspen, Colorado for $120 million** in 2025. In June 2025, through an LLC, he buys a **$46 million home on Miami's San Marino Island**. He retains 2.5% of the company directly, plus 27.5 million vested and exercisable options.

The corporate trajectory is the network's clearest single case study. A company seeded by the CIA in 2005 became, by 2026, a $325 billion public-market enterprise whose largest customers remain the US intelligence community, the Pentagon, ICE, the IDF, the Ukrainian Armed Forces, and the UK Ministry of Defence — with a growing commercial customer base in the Fortune 500. The founding investor and the eventual largest customer are the same institution. The legal-financial wrapping has changed; the structural relationship has not.

## V. Ideology in Karp's own words

The dossier standard requires the subject's own published statements on the political-economic questions the book is examining. Karp has been unusually voluble for a CEO at his level of operational exposure. The principal documents are:

**1. *The Technological Republic* (February 2025).** Co-authored with Nicholas Zamiska. The book's specific operational programme: rebuild the American defence-industrial base around software-defined, Silicon-Valley-pace development; reverse the post-war neutering of Japan and Germany; treat AI development as a winner-take-all geopolitical competition with China; reject the diversity-equity-inclusion framework that the book argues has displaced engineering competence inside the American technology and academic elites. The book is, in genre, a manifesto for a new American military-industrial alliance between Silicon Valley and the Pentagon. On the book tour, Karp said: ***"I love the idea of getting a drone and having light fentanyl-laced urine spraying on analysts that tried to screw us."*** The statement was made publicly, on the record, at a book promotion event in February 2025. It remains in the public record. He has not retracted it.

**2. The *Atlantic* essay (February 2025).** Karp writes that Palantir ***"serves our most significant and vital needs, including those of U.S. defense and intelligence agencies, instead of merely catering to the consumer."*** The essay positions Palantir's defence-contractor identity as morally superior to the consumer-internet companies (Google, Facebook) whose data-collection practices Karp has, separately, criticised — though Palantir's own data-integration practices on government contracts dwarf anything Google or Facebook has done in the consumer space.

**3. The Ukrainian targeting statement (multiple interviews, 2023-2024).** Karp's verbatim statement that Palantir is ***"responsible for most of the targeting in Ukraine,"*** and that ***"from the moment the algorithms set to work detecting their targets [i.e., people] until these targets are prosecuted [i.e., killed]... no more than two or three minutes elapse."*** The quote was relayed by Bruno Macaes, former senior Portuguese official, after a tour of Palantir's London headquarters. The word "prosecuted" is documented military-AI terminology; in the working context Karp used it, it means "killed." Karp described, voluntarily and to a journalist, the reality that his company's AI compresses the time between target identification and target killing to two or three minutes. He was not asked to make the quote. He volunteered it.

**4. The "tactical nuclear weapons" comparison (multiple 2023-2024 interviews).** Karp: ***"The power of advanced algorithmic warfare systems is now so great that it equates to having tactical nuclear weapons against an adversary with only conventional ones."*** A senior US defence-industry CEO publicly describing his own commercial product as the equivalent of tactical nuclear weapons. In any earlier era of US defence-industry communications, the statement would have been suppressed by the corporate communications staff. Karp made it deliberately, repeatedly, across multiple interviews. It is his characterisation of his own product.

**5. The earnings-call rhetoric.** Karp's quarterly earnings letters use phrases such as ***"tectonic shift,"*** ***"commodity cognition,"*** ***"we are an n of 1."*** The Q4 2025 letter announced that Palantir's Rule of 40 score (a SaaS industry blended growth-and-margin metric) had reached 127%, and stated: ***"Last quarter, our U.S. revenue grew 93% year-over-year and U.S. commercial revenue grew 137% year-over-year."*** The financial communications are philosophical-mystical in tone — a deliberate inversion of normal CEO disclosure register. The inversion is part of the company's brand-building inside the financial-press infrastructure.

**6. The short-seller "fentanyl" remark (February 2025).** Karp's stated love of ***"burning the short sellers"*** and his comment that he would like to spray ***"light fentanyl-laced urine"*** on Palantir's short-seller analysts is the kind of public statement that would produce immediate regulatory and reputational consequence at any other senior US public company. Palantir's stock has continued to appreciate. The statement remains undeleted from the public record. The SEC has not commented.

**7. The self-description.** He has called himself, in successive interviews and across the *Watching You* documentary, ***"a socialist,"*** ***"progressive but not woke,"*** ***"a neo-Marxist,"*** ***"Jewish but not religious."*** He has called Palantir's work ***"the most important thing happening in technology."*** He has compared himself to a Talmudic scholar.

The Karp self-presentation, on the public record, serves a specific operational purpose. The progressive identity inoculates the company against the criticism it would otherwise face from the centre-left coalition that has historically opposed mass-surveillance contracting. The neo-Marxist self-description gives Karp a register in which to criticise other technology billionaires (the consumer-internet executives) while operating the deepest commercial surveillance infrastructure in the US state. The Talmudic-scholar framing gives him an intellectual register that the financial press has been willing to take at face value. The persona has worked. It is part of the company's competitive moat.

**8. The Davos and Munich appearances (2023-2025).** Karp speaks at the World Economic Forum's Davos meeting and at the Munich Security Conference every year since 2022. The Davos statements have been more cultural: ***"The West is at war with itself culturally,"*** ***"we are losing because our elite class doesn't believe in the West,"*** ***"the engineering class is the last serious class in America."*** The Munich Security Conference statements have been more operational: at Munich 2024 he called for ***"a wartime mentality"*** inside the US defence-industrial base; at Munich 2025 he stated that ***"the Ukrainian war has been won technologically by software, not hardware, and the Western governments that don't understand this are going to lose the next war."*** The Munich Security Conference is co-organised by the German Ministry of Defence and is the senior contemporary European transatlantic defence-policy convening. Karp's keynote position there is one of the senior visible signs of his political integration into the German and broader European policy elite.

**9. The *Watching You* documentary (Klaus Stern, 2024-2025).** The film maps Karp's German political network. It is the most detailed publicly available record of his European political integration: the meetings with Annalena Baerbock (then Foreign Minister), with Friedrich Merz (then CDU leader, subsequently Chancellor), with the senior figures of the German tech-policy and intelligence-services establishment, and with the Atlantik-Brücke leadership. Karp chose not to participate in the documentary. The film proceeds without him. Klaus Stern uses footage of Karp's public speeches and interviews supplemented by interviews with former Palantir employees, senior European politicians, and the Dutch MEP Sophie in 't Veld, whose work tracking Palantir's EU engagement has been the principal contemporary European parliamentary check on the company. The film was broadcast on the senior German public-broadcaster ARD in late 2024 and on its successor channels in 2025.

## VI. The political activity — donor by donor

Karp's personal political-donation record, on the documented FEC filings, is the cleanest single case of the network's bipartisan portfolio-management strategy operating at the level of an individual donor. The pattern:

- **2020 cycle.** Karp publicly backed and donated to Joe Biden's presidential campaign. The specific 2020 contribution amount has not been publicly aggregated in the recent CNN and *Politico* analyses; the level was in the high six figures, in line with Karp's subsequent 2023 figure.
- **2023.** **$360,000** to the Biden-Harris joint fundraising committee.
- **August 2024.** Karp publicly endorses **Kamala Harris** for the 2024 presidential election in interviews with *Fortune* and the *New York Times*. He describes himself as "progressive but not woke" and states that Thiel's vocal Trump support has "made it harder to get things done" at Palantir.
- **Q4 2024.** **$1 million to MAGA Inc.** — the principal pro-Trump super PAC — made after the public Harris endorsement and before the November 2024 election. Reported in CNN's February 2026 analysis of FEC filings.
- **2025.** At least **$1.3 million** in additional contributions to Republican PACs and committees, including six-figure cheques to the National Republican Congressional Committee (NRCC) and to PACs aligned with Senator John Cornyn and the broader Republican congressional leadership. Reported in CNN's February 2026 coverage and in concurrent *Politico* and *Washington Examiner* reporting on FEC filings.

The pattern is not contradiction. It is portfolio management. Karp endorsed Harris in public, donated to the Harris-Biden infrastructure, and simultaneously gave $1 million to the principal pro-Trump super PAC before the same election. The network funds both coalitions. Palantir's federal-contract pipeline expanded under both the Biden and Trump administrations. The endorsements and the donations track different audiences: the public endorsements address the senior centre-left coalition that has been Palantir's recruiting base for engineering talent; the donations track the network's commercial interest in whichever administration is about to take office.

**Karp's own framing.** In *Financial Times* interviews during the 2024 cycle, Karp said ***"political correctness in the party is a huge problem, the Democrats can't yet understand the cost of it,"*** and that ***"people who innovate are fleeing"*** the industry because of regulatory pressure. The statements were made in the context of his public Harris endorsement. They were also the framing under which the $1 million MAGA Inc. donation was conducted. Karp positions himself as the Democratic-aligned-but-pragmatic CEO whose loyalty is contingent on the Democratic Party's adoption of the network's preferred policy framework. The donations reflect the contingency.

**Thiel's parallel position.** Peter Thiel — Palantir's Chairman of the Board since 2003 and largest individual shareholder — is the documented principal funder of the Vance 2022 Ohio Senate campaign ($15 million committed), of the broader Trump 2024 political infrastructure (Thiel "largely sat out" the 2024 election cycle per CNN's February 2026 reporting, then resumed senior-level Republican giving across 2025), and of the senior figures of the post-2024 Trump administration. Thiel sold around **$1.5 billion** of Palantir stock across 2024, including the same election-week window in which Karp sold his $1.4 billion. The combined founder-stock-sale volume — $3.4 billion in 2024 alone — represents one of the largest documented founder-stock-extraction events in the contemporary technology-industry record.

**The combined Karp-Thiel donor record across 2024-2025**, on the published FEC analyses: well over **$3 million** in personal contributions to Republican-aligned PACs, plus the additional Palantir-corporate political activity (FEC-disclosed and the larger non-FEC-disclosed lobbying expenditures), plus the Founders Fund and broader Thiel-vehicle commitments. The total represents one of the largest concentrated private-political-spending records of any single corporate node in the contemporary American technology industry.

## VII. The board, the lieutenants, and the structural partners

**Palantir's board (May 2026).** Per Palantir's 2025 proxy statement and the subsequent SEC filings:

- **Peter Thiel** — Chairman since 2003. Co-founder of PayPal, founder of Founders Fund, Palantir's largest individual shareholder. Network detail in Chapter 11.
- **Alex Karp** — CEO and Director since 2003.
- **Stephen Cohen** — Co-founder, President, Director since 2003. Stanford CS graduate; principal early architect of the Palantir ontology system. Cohen has received the largest internal Palantir share grants alongside Karp, including the coordinated 20 February 2026 grants of 168,004 Class A and 375,000 Class B shares.
- **Alexander Moore** — Director. Senior Palantir executive across the European and international expansion since the company's early period.
- **Spencer Rascoff** — Director since 2020. Co-founder and former CEO of Zillow; appointed at the 2020 direct listing.
- **Alexandra Schiff** — Director. Senior US national-security policy figure; previously at the Council on Foreign Relations.
- **Lauren Friedman Stat** — Director.
- **Eric Woersching** — Director; Founders Fund partner.

The board is, by composition, dominated by Palantir co-founders and Founders Fund partners. The supervoting Class F structure ensures that Karp, Thiel, and Cohen retain operational voting control above their economic equity stakes. The voting structure is disclosed in Palantir's SEC filings; it is the standard tech-founder mechanism for evading public-market shareholder discipline.

**The senior executive bench.** Position as of May 2026:

- **Shyam Sankar** — President. Received 168,004 Class A and 375,000 Class B shares on 20 February 2026 in a coordinated grant alongside Karp and Cohen. The company's recent disclosures position him as Karp's successor or co-leader. Born in Lagos, Nigeria to Tamil Indian parents who emigrated to escape political instability; subsequent family relocation to the United States. Cornell undergraduate (Industrial Engineering); Stanford Master's (Management Science and Engineering). Joined Palantir in 2006. Rose through the engineering ranks, became Chief Operating Officer in 2017, President in 2024. His career has paralleled the company's rise; his stock holdings have made him a billionaire on paper. His 2024 House Armed Services Committee testimony — calling for a "Manhattan Project for AI-defense" — was the public statement that signalled his political-positioning beyond the Palantir CEO succession question.
- **Stephen Cohen** — Co-founder, President, Director since 2003. Stanford Computer Science. The principal early architect of the Palantir ontology system; the engineering lead through whom the original CIA-customer integration was built. Holds major supervoting Class F shares alongside Karp and Thiel. Less publicly visible than Karp; reportedly the engineering manager inside the company through 2020.
- **Ryan Taylor** — Senior executive. SEC Form 4 filings record him as a major shareholder; an early-Palantir engineering hire whose career has paralleled the company's rise. Less publicly profiled than Sankar or Cohen.
- **Jeffrey Buckley** — Chief Accounting Officer. Joined Palantir 2018 from the Big Four accounting world.
- **Joe Lonsdale** — Co-founder, departed operational role in 2009 but remains a shareholder and Founders Fund-adjacent through his own venture vehicle **8VC**. Born 1982 in Fremont, California. Stanford CS. Founder of **OpenGov** (civic-data software, founded 2012), **Affinity** (relationship-intelligence software), and a list of related civic-tech firms. Bay Area political donor across both Republican and Democratic candidates; senior member of the Thiel-network economic infrastructure. Married to Tayler Cox, formerly of the Thiel Foundation. Has personally funded the **University of Austin** (UATX), the post-2021 conservative-aligned alternative to standard US higher education; sits on the UATX board alongside Bari Weiss and other senior figures from the contemporary heterodox-academic network. His 2009 departure from Palantir followed a sexual-misconduct lawsuit from a Stanford student named Ellie Clougherty; the case was settled in 2016 with no admission of liability. The case is the documented public allegation in the Lonsdale record.
- **Akash Jain** — Engineering leadership; the principal current architect of the AIP (Artificial Intelligence Platform) commercial product line. Joined Palantir as an early engineer; led the development of the AIP product that has driven Palantir's commercial revenue growth from 2023 onward.

**The senior Palantir DC office.** Staffed across the 2003-2026 period by Pentagon, intelligence-community, and senior Republican and Democratic congressional-staff alumni. The DC office is the principal government-affairs operation; its head has reported directly to Karp since the post-2015 period. Known DC office leadership figures across the documented period include: **Doug Philippone** (former Special Operations veteran, head of Palantir's defence-and-intelligence sales through 2020); **Courtney Bowman** (Global Director of Privacy and Civil Liberties Engineering, the public-policy interface with civil-liberties critics); **Eliano Younes** (head of European Government for Palantir, the principal liaison with the UK, German, and wider European defence-and-intelligence customers); **Wendy Anderson** (former senior Pentagon official, joined Palantir 2017, the principal interface with the senior DoD policy staff). The DC office's contemporary footprint, by May 2026, includes a multi-floor presence in downtown Washington and a senior staff list that overlaps with the post-2017 Pentagon and intelligence-community alumni network.

**The structural corporate partnerships**, May 2026:

- **AWS** — Palantir's principal cloud-infrastructure provider, embedded across Foundry and Gotham deployments.
- **Microsoft Azure** — partnership extended in 2024 to cover joint defence-customer deployment.
- **Anthropic** — partnership announced 2024; Claude models available through Palantir's AIP platform for defence and intelligence customers (full detail in Chapter 21).
- **OpenAI** — partnership announced mid-2025; covers similar defence-customer integration.
- **NVIDIA** — GPU-infrastructure partner; the Q1 2026 AIP rollout was largely built on NVIDIA hardware.

Palantir sits at the integration layer between the senior contemporary American frontier-AI laboratories, the senior cloud-infrastructure providers, the senior chip-infrastructure provider, and the senior US-and-allied military-intelligence customers. The company is the central commercial node through which the AI capabilities documented across the preceding chapters are converted into deployable targeting, surveillance, and analysis products for the customer class the book has named.

## VIII. The cultural and intellectual apparatus

The Karp-and-Palantir cultural-intellectual apparatus is, on the record, less institutionally developed than the Thiel apparatus mapped in Chapter 11. Karp does not personally fund a parallel to ACTS17 or to the broader Thiel network of intellectual nonprofits. The Palantir-specific cultural infrastructure consists of four principal vehicles:

**The Karp public-intellectual persona.** Karp positions himself as a philosopher-CEO. He gives long interviews on senior contemporary American political podcasts (Joe Rogan, Lex Fridman, the senior cable-news interview slots); he publishes books and *Atlantic* essays; he speaks at the Munich Security Conference and at Bilderberg. The personal-brand infrastructure functions as the cultural translation layer between Palantir's commercial-government activity and the senior American political-and-cultural elite. The persona is part of the product.

**The Atlantik-Brücke and German political network.** Karp's German-political integration is the distinctive feature of his cultural apparatus. The 2024-2025 Klaus Stern documentary *Watching You: The World of Palantir and Alex Karp* mapped Karp's network with Annalena Baerbock, Friedrich Merz, the senior CDU figures, and the broader contemporary German political establishment. **Atlantik-Brücke** is one of the principal post-war German-American transatlantic nonprofit organisations; founded in 1952 by Eric Warburg, John McCloy, and others, it operates as the senior transatlantic-elite networking infrastructure. Karp is a member of its steering layer; his German fluency and decade in Frankfurt make him uniquely positioned among senior American technology executives to operate inside German political-elite circles. Sophie in 't Veld, the Dutch MEP who has tracked Palantir's EU engagement, has documented that when the EU delegation visited Washington in 2019, the only private company they contacted was Palantir.

**The Bilderberg steering committee.** Karp is one of around forty members of the Bilderberg Meeting steering committee — the body that decides who attends the annual closed-door conferences. The 2025 Bilderberg meeting included a secret side-meeting between Karp, Thiel, and Swedish Prime Minister **Ulf Kristersson**. Kristersson was not on the official Bilderberg attendee list; the meeting became a Swedish political scandal when it was reported in the post-Bilderberg press. Karp's position on the steering committee gives him a direct role in shaping who is invited to and present at the principal contemporary transatlantic elite-policy convening.

**The Palantir cultural artefacts.** The company's *Lord of the Rings*-derived naming convention is the verified cultural signature of the network. Palantir is named for Tolkien's *palantíri* — the seeing stones that allow remote viewing. Founders Fund's logo is *Lord of the Rings*-derived. Anduril is named for Aragorn's reforged sword from the same text. The naming is not incidental. It is the explicit framing the founders have adopted: that they are restoring fallen institutions to their rightful power. The framing is reactionary in the precise political-philosophical sense; the post-war democratic settlement is the fallen institution that the network is, in its self-conception, restoring. Karp's "postwar neutering of Germany and Japan must be undone" line is the same argument made in policy register.

## IX. The 2025-2026 federal-contracting boom and what it has produced

The 2025-2026 federal-contracting boom is what the book has named across the preceding chapters: the technology-industry-capital class has captured the political apparatus and is now executing the commercial extraction the capture enables.

**The contract numbers, summarised:**
- Palantir total federal contracts since January 2025: over **$900 million** (per *NYT* August 2025 reporting)
- ICE Investigative Case Management infrastructure: over **$145 million** cumulative
- ImmigrationOS: **$30 million** (April 2025; prototype delivered September 2025; runs through September 2027)
- USCIS contract: dollar amount not publicly disclosed (December 2025)
- US Army DCGS-A platform: **$876 million** original 2019 award, ongoing
- IDF partnership: dollar amount not publicly disclosed; partnership ongoing and expanded post-October 2023
- Ukrainian Armed Forces partnership: dollar amount not publicly disclosed; ongoing
- UK MoD partnership and NHS England Federated Data Platform: up to **£330 million** over seven years (November 2023)
- Anduril total federal-contract pipeline: over **$6 billion** by end of 2025

**Inside the United States.** ICE deportations under the Trump 2025-2026 enforcement programme have run at multiples of the prior administration's pace. ImmigrationOS gives ICE near-real-time visibility on the population the agency is targeting. The Lavender-equivalent question — how the AI's classification errors translate into people's lives — is unanswered in the public record because no independent audit of ImmigrationOS has been permitted. The system is producing classifications that are converted into deportations of US-resident populations. The populations targeted include US citizens, lawful permanent residents, and others whose immigration status the system has misclassified. The ACLU has documented multiple cases of US citizens flagged by the system and subjected to extended detention before the error was resolved. The error rate is not public.

**Outside the United States.** The Gaza death toll: between October 2023 and January 2026, over 70,000 Palestinians killed by direct violence (per Berkeley Political Review's reading of the Gaza Health Ministry figures and Airwars analysis), and 186,000 total excess deaths per the Lancet's January 2025 estimate. The IDF's AI-targeting systems — Lavender, Gospel, *Where's Daddy?* — operating alongside Palantir's wider IDF partnership, produced what the United Nations, the International Court of Justice (in its January 2024 provisional-measures ruling on South Africa v. Israel), and senior international-humanitarian-law specialists have described as the most disproportionate civilian-casualty rate of any contemporary urban-combat operation. The +972 Magazine documentation, sourced from six serving IDF intelligence officers, records that the AI-targeting systems expanded the kill-list to junior operatives and accepted civilian-casualty ratios of up to 20 civilians killed per junior Hamas target.

Palantir has, across 2024-2026, denied specific involvement with Lavender, denied responsibility for IDF targeting decisions, and stated its "human rights due diligence" framework. The denials are precise on Lavender — the specific named database — but not on the underlying targeting infrastructure. The IDF, in its own statements, has confirmed Palantir is among its principal AI infrastructure partners. The company's denials are treated as the kind of authorised account section 3 of GUIDANCE warns against: an account produced by a party whose continued commercial relationship with the IDF depends on the IDF's continued willingness to contract with the company. The denials are read for what they admit (the underlying IDF partnership) and disregarded for what they self-serve (the active separation from Lavender, which cannot be independently verified).

## X. The current 2026 position — what Karp controls, what he is worth, what he is doing

**Net worth.** Between $9 billion and $18 billion across the volatile 2024-2026 range, depending on Palantir's stock price. Forbes and Bloomberg both placed the 2025 peak above $18 billion. Among the world's 200 wealthiest people; the third-richest Black person in the United States. Holdings: 2.5% of Palantir directly (Class A, B, and F shares), plus 27.5 million vested and exercisable options. Liquid cash, after the $3 billion of Palantir stock sales since the 2020 IPO and adjustment for taxes and the recent property purchases, sits between $1.5 and $2 billion.

**Properties.** A 3,700-acre compound near Aspen, Colorado ($120 million, 2025). A $46 million home on Miami's San Marino Island (June 2025, through an LLC). The Lyman, New Hampshire primary residence — a remote rural property where he skis up to five hours a day in winter. Properties variously reported in Vermont, Norway, Alaska, and several European locations. Karp's joke that he owns ***"ten cross-country ski huts"*** is publicly documented; the underlying property portfolio matches the joke.

**Operational position.** CEO of Palantir Technologies. Co-managing director of Palantir Technologies GmbH (Frankfurt). Board member of Palantir Technologies UK Ltd. Member of the Bilderberg Meeting steering committee. Member of Atlantik-Brücke. Member of the Munich Security Conference. Former board member of Axel Springer SE (2018-2020) and BASF (until 2020). Member of the Business Council. Author, with Nicholas Zamiska, of *The Technological Republic*.

**Active threat.** Karp's role is the central commercial node through which the AI capabilities documented across the preceding chapters are converted into targeting, surveillance, and deportation infrastructure deployed against living populations. The active threat is not Karp personally — he is one operator among many, and the network would replace him if he were removed — but the integration he has built. By 2026, Palantir is a private corporation whose commercial product line holds:

- The capacity to identify and locate any US resident at the granularity its data-integration infrastructure permits, which on the ImmigrationOS contracting documents is close to total — every IRS record, every Social Security record, every passport record, every ALPR record, every state driver's-licence record that has been brought into the federal system.
- The capacity to generate targeting recommendations for kinetic military operations at timescales of two to three minutes per target, on the CEO's own published statement about the Ukrainian deployment.
- Integration with the senior frontier-AI laboratories' outputs through the Anthropic, OpenAI, AWS, Azure, and NVIDIA partnerships — meaning the contemporary state-of-the-art language models are now operationally accessible inside Palantir's targeting and surveillance products.
- Operation under sole-source government contracts awarded without competitive bidding, with no independent audit access, and with no meaningful regulatory recourse available to the populations targeted.

The active threat, in plain terms: this capability now exists as a deployable commercial product, owned by five people at the senior level (Karp, Thiel, Cohen, Sankar, Lonsdale), staffed by 4,000 Palantir employees plus an order of magnitude more across the partner-firm network, supported by $325 billion of public-market valuation, and integrated into the kill chains of three contemporary military campaigns (Gaza, Ukraine, the broader US counter-terror programme) and into the deportation infrastructure of the US Department of Homeland Security. The capability did not exist twenty-three years ago. It is now in production at scale. The build-out period is over. The deployment period is the present.

## XI. The Anduril question — the network's drone-and-autonomous-weapons vehicle

Anduril is operationally distinct from Palantir but structurally adjacent. The shared infrastructure is Founders Fund, the shared Palantir alumni among its founding team, and the same Pentagon-customer base. The two firms together form the contemporary American defence-tech integration that the book has been naming.

**The founding.** Anduril was incorporated on **20 April 2017** by five co-founders: **Palmer Luckey** (founder of Oculus VR, sold to Facebook for around $2 billion in 2014), **Trae Stephens** (Founders Fund partner, ex-Palantir, ex-State Department), **Brian Schimpf** (ex-Palantir, the company's CEO from incorporation), **Matt Grimm** (ex-Palantir, COO), and **Joseph Chen** (engineering co-founder). Four of the five had Palantir tenure. Stephens had been at Palantir from 2008 to 2014 before moving to Founders Fund; Schimpf had joined Palantir straight from Cornell undergraduate and risen to senior engineering leadership.

The founding episode was Luckey's 2016 firing from Facebook after his $10,000 donation to a pro-Trump meme operation (Nimble America) was reported by *The Daily Beast*. Luckey had the capital from the Oculus sale, the public profile from Oculus, and an explicit desire to build defence products as a political statement about what he believed the US lacked. Stephens had been arguing inside Founders Fund since at least 2014 that the US defence-industrial base had structurally failed to adopt software-native methods and that venture-backed defence-tech startups would displace the legacy primes — Lockheed Martin, Raytheon, Northrop Grumman, Boeing, General Dynamics — across the next decade.

Stephens was recruiting for the company before Luckey left Facebook. The founding round was led by Founders Fund. Brian Singerman ran the seed round. Luckey, Stephens, and Grimm pitched the founding pitch to Palantir's senior team; Brian Schimpf joined as the fifth co-founder and CEO. Joseph Chen joined the engineering team.

**The product trajectory.** Anduril's first product was the **Sentry** AI surveillance tower — autonomous radar-and-camera surveillance for border-security applications. The first deployment was on a Texas rancher's private land in June 2018, informally tested before any government contract; the first paid deployment came shortly after, from US Customs and Border Protection. Sentry remains operational along the US-Mexico border in 2026.

Subsequent products: **Ghost** autonomous reconnaissance drone (using NVIDIA Jetson processing for on-board AI, designed for radio silence); **Roadrunner** twin-turbojet counter-drone interceptor; **Anvil** counter-UAS system; **Dive-LD** undersea autonomous vehicle; **Altius** loitering munition; **Barracuda** cruise-missile-class munition; **Fury** aircraft, selected by the US Air Force in 2024 as one of two contractors for the **Collaborative Combat Aircraft (CCA)** programme alongside General Atomics. The CCA selection is the central 2024 procurement event: Anduril, founded seven years earlier, beat the incumbent General Atomics for one of the two US Air Force loyal-wingman drone contracts. The selection is what *Fortune*'s May 2026 coverage describes as the moment Anduril ***"transitioned from disruptor to incumbent."***

The product line runs on **Lattice**, the company's AI command-and-control software platform. Lattice integrates sensor data from cameras, radars, satellites, and drones into a single fused-data picture; it provides autonomous decision-support and tasking for unmanned systems; it exposes its capabilities via an open SDK so third-party hardware can integrate. The platform is the company's principal software moat. Anduril's marketing position is that legacy defence primes are "hardware-defined and software-enabled," while Anduril is "software-defined and hardware-enabled." Palmer Luckey's stated principle: ***"only solve in hardware what must be solved in hardware. Everything else? Solve for free in software."***

**The funding trajectory.** Founders Fund led the seed and Series A. Subsequent rounds drew Andreessen Horowitz, Lux Capital, General Catalyst, and Valor Equity Partners. The December 2022 Series E closed at a **$8.48 billion** valuation, raising $1.48 billion. Subsequent rounds: a 2023 round at around $14 billion; a mid-2024 round at $20 billion; the **Series G in mid-2025** at around **$30 billion**, with Founders Fund leading a $1 billion+ commitment as part of a $2.5 billion total raise. By end of 2025 the company had secured over **$6 billion** in government contracts worldwide.

**The political alignment.** Anduril is explicitly Republican-aligned through Luckey's documented Trump support, through Stephens's role on the 2016 Trump defense transition team and the Defense Innovation Board, and through Founders Fund's broader political alignment. Brian Schimpf, the CEO, identifies as a "minority-opinion Democrat" — pro-institution but supportive of accountability. The founders' team is bipartisan in stated composition but the company's political alignment, in practice, is Republican-leaning across the 2017-2026 period.

Luckey's personal positions: ***"We need to transition from being the world police to being the world gun store."*** ***"There's no moral high ground to making a land mine that can't tell the difference between a school bus full of children and Russian armor."*** ***"There is more AI in a Tesla than any military aircraft."*** Stephens, on the legacy primes: ***"The people that love the [US] defense primes most are the Chinese Communist Party."*** Luckey, on the moral framework: ***"If we are going to ethically engage in warfare, [then] there is no moral high ground in leaving this problem to existing prime contractors that are incompetent at many things, and totally unable to do other things."***

**Luckey personally.** Born 1992 in Long Beach, California. Home-schooled. Built the prototype Oculus headset in his parents' garage at 18. Sold Oculus to Facebook for around $2 billion in 2014; fired from Facebook in 2017 after the Nimble America donation reporting. Personal net worth around $3.5 billion per Forbes November 2025. Also operates **Jetson** (personal aviation) and **ModRetro** (retro-gaming electronics). Wears Hawaiian shirts and flip-flops at congressional hearings as a deliberate political statement. Unmarried, lives in Newport Beach, California.

**The structural conclusion.** Anduril is the network's drone-and-autonomous-weapons vehicle, sitting beside Palantir, operating in the same Founders-Fund-anchored, Pentagon-customer-base environment. The two firms together represent the documented capture of US-and-allied defence procurement by a Silicon Valley-funded network whose senior figures hold the supervoting control of both companies, the political donations to both major US parties, the press ownership the book has documented elsewhere (Bezos / *Washington Post*; David Ellison / CBS through Skydance; the broader senior-American press infrastructure), and now the deployment of AI-targeting systems against live populations in three theatres.

**The Founders Fund defence-tech portfolio (May 2026).** Beyond Palantir and Anduril, Founders Fund holds positions across an explicit defence-tech investment thesis Stephens articulated from 2014 onward. The principal portfolio companies, by company and function:

- **Shield AI** — autonomous fighter-jet AI; the Hivemind AI pilot product; founded 2015 by Brandon Tseng (former Navy SEAL) and Ryan Tseng. 2024 valuation $2.7 billion. Customer base includes the US Air Force and US Marine Corps.
- **Hadrian** — automated CNC machining factories producing precision aerospace and defence parts; founded 2020 by Chris Power. Series B 2023 at over $500 million valuation.
- **Saronic** — autonomous unmanned surface vessels (drone boats); founded 2022 by Dino Mavrookas and Rob Lehman. Series B in 2024 at $1.5 billion valuation.
- **Vannevar Labs** — AI-enabled intelligence analysis software for US intelligence customers; founded 2019 by Brett Granberg (ex-Palantir). 2024 valuation over $700 million.
- **Castelion** — hypersonic weapons systems for the US Department of Defense; founded 2022 by Andrew Kreitz and Bryon Hargis. Both founders SpaceX alumni.
- **Apex Space** — modular satellite manufacturing for US defence and intelligence customers; founded 2022. Series B 2024.
- **Rebellion Defense** — defence AI software; founded 2019 by Chris Lynch (former director of the Defense Digital Service), with Nicole Camarillo and Oliver Lewis. Customer base includes the US Air Force, US Army, and UK Ministry of Defence. Founders Fund led the Series B.
- **EpiSci** — autonomous-aircraft AI; subsequently acquired by Applied Intuition in 2025.

The portfolio represents, by May 2026, the most concentrated venture-capital position in the contemporary American defence-tech market. Founders Fund's combined defence-portfolio commitments since 2017 sit at over $5 billion. Its returns on Palantir and Anduril alone have crossed $40 billion in paper gains. The fund's documented thesis — that venture-backed startups can displace the legacy primes — has, by 2026, become the consensus position inside the US defence-acquisition apparatus. The legacy primes (Lockheed Martin, Raytheon Technologies, Northrop Grumman, Boeing, General Dynamics) have lost a measurable share of US defence-procurement spending to the new Founders-Fund-aligned wave; Anduril alone took the IVAS programme from Microsoft and the CCA programme (partly) from General Atomics. The portfolio is the practical mechanism by which the network has, across a decade, converted the post-2014 thesis into an operating procurement reality.

**The cross-portfolio governance structure.** Several Founders Fund partners and Thiel-network senior figures sit on multiple portfolio-company boards. Trae Stephens sits on the Anduril board and on several Founders-Fund-portfolio defence-company boards. Joe Lonsdale (Palantir co-founder, now operating through 8VC) sits on related defence-company boards. The cross-portfolio governance is the practical realisation of what Thiel articulated in his 2014 book *Zero to One*: that the contemporary venture-capital structure permits coordinated capital allocation across a defined network of related companies, with the cross-portfolio governance functioning as the coordinating mechanism. Palantir, Anduril, and the wider Founders Fund defence-portfolio are the most operationally exposed contemporary example.

## XII. The verdict

The surveillance layer the book has been documenting is operationally complete. Palantir is its commercial home. Karp is its public face. Anduril is its drone-and-autonomous-weapons sibling. The capabilities built across 2003-2026 — the integration of personal-data silos into queryable ontologies; the deployment of AI-targeting recommendations at two-to-three-minute timescales; the sole-source contracting that has placed the product line beyond competitive-market discipline; the political-and-press capture that has prevented effective regulatory constraint — are the consequence of twenty-three years of coordinated network work.

The book's dossier standard treats Karp at the same depth as every other named figure. The Habermas doctorate, the self-described socialism, the Black maternal heritage, the cold-water-swimming meditation-teaching philosopher-CEO persona — these are the cover under which the working work has been done. The work is the construction of the surveillance instrument the contemporary American state has bought, deployed, and is now using to identify, track, target, and remove the populations it has chosen to target. The work is performed for profit. The contracts are awarded without competitive bidding. The corporation operates outside democratic oversight. The technical infrastructure is opaque to the populations it targets. The error rate is not publicly verifiable. On the available evidence — the +972 Lavender investigation; the documented ImmigrationOS data-quality problems; the Gaza casualty figures; the 186,000 excess-death estimate — the error rate is far higher than the company's public statements imply.

Karp's published comparison of Palantir-class systems to tactical nuclear weapons is, by the book's standard, both true and an admission. The systems have the scale of weapons of mass destruction. They are owned by a private corporation whose CEO has named that scale on the public record. They are deployed without the international legal framework that has, since 1945, attempted to constrain weapons of mass destruction. They are deployed by the political coalition the network has funded into power. The evidence records the structure, names the operator, and reaches the conclusion the record requires: the surveillance layer is built, the populations it is being deployed against have no effective recourse, the build-out period is over, and the deployment period is the present.
