# Chapter 21: Dario and Daniela Amodei

The preceding chapters dossiered twelve figures across the American technology-industry-capital class and one operationally adjacent (Karp, Chapter 20). The analysis applies the same standard to **Dario Amodei** and **Daniela Amodei** — sibling co-founders of **Anthropic PBC**, the AI-safety-and-research company that has become one of the two principal American frontier-AI laboratories (alongside OpenAI) and, as of May 2026, the **second-most-valuable AI company globally** at a **$380 billion post-money valuation**.

**The Anthropic chapter receives the same standard as every other dossier — no softening, even though Anthropic makes the AI system used to draft this book.** The same investigative-journalism standard applied to Thiel, Altman, Bezos, and Gates applies here.

The Anthropic story includes positive elements — the founders' AI-safety concerns as motivation for the 2020-2021 OpenAI departure; the technical contributions (Constitutional AI, mechanistic interpretability, the Responsible Scaling Policy); the **January 2026 80% wealth pledge**; the **February 2026 refusal of Pete Hegseth's DoD demand to remove restrictions on domestic surveillance and autonomous weapons applications of Claude**. It also includes contested elements — the **Sam Bankman-Fried / FTX foundational funding** ($530 million of the $580 million Series B came from money the DOJ alleges was stolen from FTX customers); the **effective-altruism / longtermism integration** through the Amodei-Karnofsky marriage and the Open Philanthropy funding pipeline; the **operational integration with the US national-security apparatus** through the Palantir partnership, the $200 million DoD contract, the Claude Gov model, and the documented use of Claude in the **2026 Venezuela raid that killed 83 people**; the structural tension between the company's stated safety-first mission and its actual position accelerating the deployment of large language models across the global economy.

**Anthropic occupies a structural position the AI-safety field has contested since 2021 — the "safety-conscious frontier-AI laboratory" that, by virtue of being a frontier-AI laboratory, contributes to the same capabilities expansion its public framing supposedly counterweights.** The evidence records the documented evidence on both sides.

## I. Origins: the San Francisco mixed-heritage household

**Dario Amodei** was born in **1983 in San Francisco, California**. His sister **Daniela Amodei** was born **four years later, in 1987**, also in San Francisco.

Their father, **Riccardo Amodei**, was an **Italian-American leather craftsman from Massa Marittima, Tuscany, Italy** — a small town in the Tuscan Maremma, 60 kilometres north of Grosseto. Riccardo had immigrated to the United States in his early adulthood; he established a leather-craftsmanship business in San Francisco. He died when Dario was a young adult; the evidence does not specify the precise date or circumstances.

Their mother, **Elena Engel**, was a **Jewish-American library project manager originally from Chicago**. The household was a **mixed Italian-Catholic / Jewish-American intellectual professional family** of the post-1970s San Francisco period — comparable in orientation to the Schmidt (Chapter 18) or Zuckerberg (Chapter 17) families-of-origin, less wealthy than either.

**The Amodei family was much less wealthy and less institutionally networked than most of the billionaire families-of-origin documented in the preceding chapters.** No Mary-Gates-on-the-IBM-board (Chapter 19), no Errol-Musk-emerald-mine (Chapter 12), no Lawrence-Gise-AEC-Albuquerque-director (Chapter 16). The Amodei children grew up in a middle-class San Francisco household; both careers were the product of individual educational achievement rather than family-network inheritance.

## II. Dario Amodei: Stanford, Princeton, and the early scientific career

Dario Amodei's educational trajectory was academic-and-scientific:

- **BA Physics, Stanford University**.
- **PhD Biophysics, Princeton University** — one of the principal training grounds for the American physics-and-biophysics elite.
- **Postdoctoral research, Stanford School of Medicine**.

Dario's pre-AI training was grounded in physics and biophysics, not computer science or machine learning. The formation he brought to the subsequent AI work was a physicist's formation — and it shows in Anthropic's research culture: the large fraction of physics PhDs among the founding team, the empirical-scaling-laws orientation, the academic publication record.

Dario's pre-Anthropic AI career:

- **Baidu (2014-2015)**: research scientist in Sunnyvale, California, on **Andrew Ng's deep-learning team**. Worked on speech-recognition (Deep Speech 2).
- **Google Brain (2015-2016)**: research scientist. Co-authored the 2016 paper "**Concrete Problems in AI Safety**" with Chris Olah, Jacob Steinhardt, Paul Christiano, John Schulman, and Dan Mané. The paper is one of the foundational publications of the contemporary AI-safety field; it introduced vocabulary (side effects, reward hacking, scalable oversight, safe exploration, robustness to distributional shift) that subsequently structured a decade of AI-safety research.
- **OpenAI (2016-2020)**: joined as research scientist; rose to **VP of Research**. Led the development of **GPT-2 (February 2019) and GPT-3 (June 2020)** — the foundational large-language-model generations that established the contemporary AI industry. Was reportedly the principal technical architect of the GPT model series.

Dario was one of the principal technical architects of the contemporary large-language-model paradigm. The technical record establishes him as one of the most significant figures in the AI industry; the Anthropic founding was underwritten by the technical credibility his OpenAI work produced.

## III. Daniela Amodei: liberal arts, congressional campaigns, and the OpenAI operations role

Daniela Amodei's educational and career trajectory was different from Dario's:

- **BA in English Literature and Political Science, UC Santa Cruz**. Liberal-arts-and-humanities, not scientific. Daniela has characterised the liberal-arts background as foundational to her operations-and-policy orientation.
- **Congressional campaigns**: worked on multiple campaigns (Democratic-aligned) as a field organiser and field director, including positions in Pennsylvania.
- **Stripe (2013-2018)**: rose through risk-and-operations roles to senior manager.
- **OpenAI (2018-2021)**: **VP of Operations** and subsequently **VP of Safety and Policy**. One of the principal OpenAI operations executives during the company's major scale-up.

Daniela's trajectory is non-technical relative to Dario's. She is an operations-and-policy executive whose contribution to Anthropic is the construction of the institutional structures — HR, policy, communications, governance — that the technical co-founders could not have built without her. The sibling co-leadership structure is a complementary technical-and-operational founding partnership, one of the most distinctive in the contemporary technology industry.

The Daniela record on diversity-and-culture has been positive; she has been active in advocating for representation of women and underrepresented groups in senior AI-industry positions. She was named to **Forbes' 100 Most Powerful Women list in 2025**.

## IV. The OpenAI departure (2020-2021)

The most operationally consequential single event of the Anthropic founding is the **2020-2021 departure of the Amodei siblings and a dozen senior OpenAI researchers from OpenAI**. The documented context:

- **July 2019**: Microsoft made its **$1 billion investment in OpenAI**; OpenAI restructured from a non-profit to a **"capped-profit" structure** (Chapter 13). The restructuring altered OpenAI's institutional character — from a non-profit AI-safety research organisation to a capped-profit commercial enterprise with Microsoft strategic integration.
- **2019-2020**: Heavy internal tension at OpenAI about whether the company's accelerating commercial trajectory was compatible with its stated AI-safety mission. The principal internal-tension figures were Dario Amodei, Tom Brown, Chris Olah, Jack Clark, Jared Kaplan, Sam McCandlish, and Ben Mann — the subsequent Anthropic co-founders.
- **November 2020**: Dario Amodei and Tom Brown reportedly delivered a presentation to OpenAI's leadership team raising concerns about the commercialisation pace. The presentation was reportedly unsuccessful in altering OpenAI's direction.
- **Late 2020 - early 2021**: Roughly a dozen senior OpenAI researchers departed.
- **February 2021**: Anthropic incorporated as a Public Benefit Corporation in Delaware.

The Dario Amodei characterisation of the departure (from a subsequent interview): "**There was a group of us within OpenAI, that in the wake of making GPT-2 and GPT-3, had a kind of very strong focused belief in two things... one was that if you put more compute into these models, they would get better and better with no end in sight. And two, that on top of scale, you also need something called alignment or safety. You don't automatically get what you want just from making these models bigger.**"

The structural meaning: **the founders left OpenAI because they concluded its post-Microsoft commercialisation trajectory was incompatible with AI-safety priorities**. Anthropic was founded as **"the AI lab that took safety seriously"** — the company's own self-characterisation. Whether Anthropic has operated according to those principles across the subsequent five years is the contested question the rest of this chapter documents.

## V. The eight co-founders

The Anthropic founding team was larger than the Amodei sibling pair. The seven principal co-founders (with Ben Mann sometimes counted as an eighth):

1. **Dario Amodei** — CEO. Former OpenAI VP of Research.
2. **Daniela Amodei** — President. Former OpenAI VP of Safety and Policy.
3. **Tom Brown** — Head of Core Resources / compute infrastructure. Former OpenAI research scientist; **lead author on the GPT-3 paper "Language Models are Few-Shot Learners"** (NeurIPS 2020). MIT MEng in computer science and brain/cognitive sciences. Brown was the principal engineer responsible for making GPT-3 a reality.
4. **Jared Kaplan** — Chief Science Officer. Theoretical physicist (Johns Hopkins University faculty); co-author of the foundational "**Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models**" paper (2020) — the paper that established the empirical foundation of large-language-model development by demonstrating that model performance scales predictably with model size, dataset size, and compute. Kaplan's scaling-laws work is one of the most consequential AI-research contributions of the post-2018 period; the contemporary AI-industry investment programme is predicated on the empirical regularities his paper documented.
5. **Jack Clark** — Co-founder; head of policy; subsequently head of the **Anthropic Institute** (launched March 2026). Former OpenAI policy director. The principal Anthropic policy-and-communications figure since 2021.
6. **Sam McCandlish** — Chief Architect. Former OpenAI research scientist.
7. **Christopher Olah** — Interpretability Research Lead. Former OpenAI mechanistic-interpretability engineer; one of the principal figures in the contemporary AI-interpretability field. Olah's interpretability work — the attempt to understand the internal mechanisms by which neural networks produce their outputs — has been characterised in the academic literature as one of the most-significant contemporary contributions to the AI-alignment field.
8. **Benjamin Mann** — Head of Anthropic Labs (as of 2026). Former OpenAI research scientist.

**Two-thirds of Anthropic's first fifteen employees held PhDs in physics.** The founding team was physics-trained, not computer-science-trained. Anthropic's research culture reflects it: empirical-scaling research, mechanistic interpretability, the physicist's approach to understanding neural-network behaviour.

## VI. The corporate structure: PBC and the Long-Term Benefit Trust

Anthropic's corporate structure is one of the distinctive features of the company in the contemporary AI industry:

### Public Benefit Corporation (PBC)

Anthropic is incorporated as a **Public Benefit Corporation (PBC) under Delaware law**. The PBC structure requires the company to **balance shareholder returns with the public-benefit purpose articulated in its founding documents**. Anthropic's stated public-benefit purpose is "**the responsible development and maintenance of advanced AI for the long-term benefit of humanity**."

The PBC structure has legal implications: PBC directors are legally required to consider the public-benefit purpose in their decision-making, in addition to (rather than instead of) shareholder returns. The structure does not prohibit major commercial activity, but it establishes a legal basis for directors' consideration of public-benefit factors.

### Long-Term Benefit Trust (LTBT)

The distinctive governance feature is the **Long-Term Benefit Trust (LTBT)** — a purpose trust established to hold a class of governance shares ("**Class T shares**") that confer the authority to **elect a growing portion of Anthropic's board of directors** (eventually a majority).

The LTBT structure is designed to provide a governance counterweight to the major venture-investor capital the company has accepted. The operational meaning: Anthropic's investors (Amazon, Google, the Series G investors) **do not have governance control of the company** in the standard venture-capital sense; the LTBT trustees will, across the subsequent decade, accumulate the authority to elect a majority of Anthropic's board.

**LTBT Trustees (as of January 2026)**:

- **Neil Buddy Shah** — CEO of the **Clinton Health Access Initiative** (the global-health organisation founded by Bill Clinton).
- **Mariano-Florentino Cuéllar** — **President of the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace**; former California Supreme Court justice (2014-2021); former Stanford Law professor.
- **Richard Fontaine** — **CEO of the Center for a New American Security (CNAS)** — the principal Washington defence-policy think tank, integrated with both Democratic and Republican administrations since 2008.

**The LTBT trustees include the CEO of one of Washington's principal defence-policy think tanks (Fontaine of CNAS) and the President of one of Washington's principal foreign-policy think tanks (Cuéllar of Carnegie).** The "safety counterweight" governance structure is integrated with the Washington foreign-policy and defence-policy establishment.

The question that the LTBT structure raises is: **what does "the long-term benefit of humanity" mean when the trustees who are charged with adjudicating that question are the senior leaders of the Washington foreign-policy-and-defence-policy establishment?** The answer is that the LTBT's interpretation of "long-term benefit of humanity" is the interpretation of the contemporary Washington foreign-policy-and-defence-policy establishment — that is, an interpretation structured by the US-China-strategic-competition framework, the AI-investment-as-national-security framework, and the broader policy assumptions of the contemporary American foreign-policy establishment.

This is not the only possible interpretation of "long-term benefit of humanity." Deep-ecology, global-justice, labour-and-distributive, and explicitly-anti-imperialist interpretations are not represented on the LTBT. The trust operationalises one specific interpretation — the Washington establishment's — and excludes the others.

## VII. The funding trajectory and the Sam Bankman-Fried foundational position

The most consequential funding-trajectory fact about Anthropic — and the one the press framing has under-reported — is the **Sam Bankman-Fried / FTX foundational position in the Series B funding round**.

### The funding rounds

- **2021 Series A**: **$124 million**. Lead investor: **Jaan Tallinn** — Estonian Skype co-founder; one of the principal effective-altruism / longtermism funders globally; principal funder of multiple **Future of Humanity Institute** and **Future of Life Institute** initiatives. Tallinn's position in the Anthropic funding history is the first EA/longtermism financial integration with the company.
- **April 2022 Series B**: **$580 million**. **Lead investor: Sam Bankman-Fried, CEO of FTX**. Other participants: **Caroline Ellison** (then co-CEO of Alameda Research), **Nishad Singh** (then Director of Engineering at FTX), **Jim McClave**, **Jaan Tallinn**, and the **Center for Emerging Risk Research (CERR)** — a Swiss EA-affiliated organisation.
- **May 2023 Series C**: $450 million led by Spark Capital and Google.
- **September 2023**: **Amazon committed up to $4 billion** ($1.25 billion initial, with potential expansion to $4 billion). The investment was structured to require Anthropic to use AWS as its principal cloud provider — a operational integration with the Bezos infrastructure documented in Chapter 16.
- **February 2024**: Amazon expanded its commitment to $4 billion (the maximum).
- **November 2024**: Amazon committed an additional $4 billion, bringing total Amazon investment to **$8 billion**.
- **March 2025**: Series E reportedly at a **$61.5 billion valuation**.
- **October 2025**: **Google cloud partnership** — access to up to **one million of Google's custom Tensor Processing Units (TPUs)**, potentially adding more than **one gigawatt of AI compute capacity by 2026**.
- **February 2026 Series G**: **$30 billion** at a **$380 billion post-money valuation**. Investors: **Singapore's sovereign wealth fund GIC, Coatue, MGX (Abu Dhabi sovereign-wealth fund operationally controlled by Sheikh Tahnoun bin Zayed, Chapter 23 forthcoming), Microsoft, and Nvidia**.
- **May 2026**: **xAI deal** for use of Musk's Colossus 1 data centre. Same month: **$1.8 billion cloud computing deal with Akamai Technologies**.

### The Sam Bankman-Fried Series B: the public record

The April 2022 Series B is the most-consequential single FTX-related funding event of the contemporary AI industry. Per multiple subsequent reports — including the **New York Times** reporting cited in the **October 2023 Sam Bankman-Fried trial** — **$530 million of the $580 million Series B came from Bankman-Fried and his former business partners**.

The context that the captured-press framing of Anthropic has elided:

- **Sam Bankman-Fried personally invested $500 million in Anthropic in 2022** using funds that the US Department of Justice subsequently alleged "**were stolen from FTX customers**" (per the DOJ filing in Bankman-Fried's October 2023 trial).
- **Caroline Ellison**, then co-CEO of Alameda Research and SBF's romantic partner, personally invested in Anthropic (the precise amount has been reported variously; she testified at trial that $10 million of her $20 million 2021 bonus had been invested in a startup, which prosecutors indicated was Anthropic).
- **Nishad Singh**, then Director of Engineering at FTX, participated in the round.
- **The Center for Emerging Risk Research (CERR)** — a Swiss EA-aligned organisation that has been funded by the broader FTX/SBF philanthropic apparatus.

**The foundational outside funding of Anthropic was provided by Sam Bankman-Fried, Caroline Ellison, and Nishad Singh — the three principal figures of the FTX fraud.** Bankman-Fried was convicted in November 2023 of seven counts of fraud and conspiracy; sentenced in March 2024 to 25 years in federal prison. Ellison pleaded guilty; sentenced September 2024 to 24 months. Singh pleaded guilty; sentenced to no prison time in October 2024.

The question that the SBF-Anthropic funding raises: **what is the appropriate operational meaning of an AI-safety company whose foundational outside funding came from a fraud that the DOJ alleges was predicated on the diversion of customer deposits?** The Anthropic response to the question has been limited; the company has not publicly returned the SBF-and-related funding to FTX creditors, has not publicly characterised the funding as contaminated, and has not publicly addressed the ethical-and-operational implications of having been founded on what subsequent legal proceedings have characterised as stolen funds.

The FTX bankruptcy estate has **clawed back large portions of the SBF Anthropic investment**. The bankruptcy administrator sold portions of the stake to subsequent investors at sharp appreciation; the proceeds compensated FTX creditors. The post-bankruptcy recoveries do not alter the foundational fact: **the principal American AI-safety company was built on money the DOJ has characterised as stolen from FTX customers**.

The elision of this funding history from press coverage of Anthropic is **one of the most significant under-reported facts of the contemporary AI industry**. The press has been oriented toward Anthropic's safety-first public framing rather than toward the funding-source record. The same captured-press pattern documented at Bezos, Zuckerberg, Gates, and the broader technology-industry-capital class applies to Anthropic.

## VIII. The Effective Altruism / longtermism integration

The Anthropic founding-and-funding pattern is integrated with the **effective-altruism (EA) / longtermism** intellectual-and-funding network — more deeply than any other contemporary frontier-AI laboratory.

### The EA/longtermism intellectual framework

The EA/longtermism framework has developed the following doctrinal positions since 2009:

1. **Effective altruism** — the doctrine that philanthropic-and-personal-life decisions should be made on the basis of **maximising expected impact per unit of resource expended**. Foundational figures: Peter Singer, Will MacAskill, Toby Ord, Holden Karnofsky.
2. **Longtermism** — the doctrine that the **expected value of actions should include the expected effects on long-term future populations** (potentially trillions or quadrillions of future persons). Foundational figures: Nick Bostrom, Will MacAskill, Toby Ord.
3. **Existential risk** — the doctrine that **risks of human extinction or permanent civilisational collapse should be weighted much more heavily than near-term harms**. Foundational figures: Nick Bostrom, the Future of Humanity Institute (subsequently dissolved 2024).
4. **AI safety as the most important cause area** — the doctrine that **AI alignment / safety is the single most important cause area for EA philanthropy** because of the major potential for AI to produce existential-risk-level outcomes. Foundational figures: Holden Karnofsky, Open Philanthropy.

The EA/longtermism framework has been contested in the academic-and-philosophical literature. The critiques include:

- The **utilitarianism critique** — the framework's reliance on aggregate-welfare calculations that may under-weight specific persons' interests in favour of aggregate population calculations.
- The **expected-value-of-far-future-populations critique** — the epistemic question of whether contemporary actors can predict the welfare effects of contemporary actions on populations that may exist tens of thousands or millions of years in the future.
- The **wealth-concentration critique** — the EA/longtermism framework has been adopted by the American billionaire class as a justification for concentrating philanthropic decision-making in private hands rather than democratic ones.
- The **technology-acceleration critique** — the EA/longtermism focus on AI safety has **accelerated rather than constrained AI development** by motivating the founding of major AI laboratories (Anthropic) and by channelling major funding into AI development.

### The Anthropic-EA structural integration

The Anthropic founding-and-funding pattern is the deepest single AI-laboratory integration with the EA/longtermism network:

- **Open Philanthropy** (Cari Tuna and Dustin Moskovitz — the Facebook co-founder, Chapter 17) — Anthropic funder and one of the principal AI-safety funders globally.
- **Jaan Tallinn** — Series A lead investor; principal global EA/longtermism funder; co-founder of the Future of Humanity Institute and the Future of Life Institute.
- **Sam Bankman-Fried and the FTX network** — Series B lead investor; SBF was self-identified as EA-aligned; the FTX Future Fund was the period's principal explicitly-EA-aligned philanthropic vehicle ($160 million in disbursements before the November 2022 FTX collapse).
- **Holden Karnofsky** — Open Philanthropy co-founder; **married to Daniela Amodei**. The principal operational figure in the EA-grantmaking community since 2007; his transition from GiveWell (2007-2014) to Open Philanthropy (2014-present) is the institutional consolidation of the EA grantmaking apparatus.
- **The Cari Tuna / Dustin Moskovitz philanthropic network** — Facebook-co-founder-derived wealth; principal EA funder; **$10 billion in committed assets** as of 2025.

**Anthropic's president is married to the co-founder of the principal American effective-altruism grantmaking organisation.** The structural integration is operationally significant: the EA/longtermism network produced (a) the theoretical foundation of the AI-safety field, (b) the early funding of OpenAI and Anthropic, and (c) the personnel pipeline that has staffed the senior AI-safety positions across the industry.

### The structural-critique observation

The EA/longtermism framework has **accelerated rather than constrained AI development** — one of the most contested questions in the AI-safety field. The contrarian position, articulated by **Émile Torres, Timnit Gebru, and others**:

- The EA/longtermism framework was used in the 2015-2021 period to motivate the founding of OpenAI (which Musk-and-Altman framed as the AI-safety-conscious alternative to Google's DeepMind, Chapter 13).
- The EA/longtermism framework was used in the 2021-present period to motivate the founding of Anthropic (which the Amodei-and-others framed as the AI-safety-conscious alternative to OpenAI's post-Microsoft-investment commercialisation, this chapter).
- The consequence of both foundings has been the **sharp acceleration of frontier-AI capability development**, not the constraint of it.
- The structural pattern is that **AI-safety-conscious actors who founded AI laboratories with safety-conscious intent have contributed to the same AI-capability-acceleration trajectory that their safety-conscious framings articulated concerns about**.
- The consequence is that the contemporary AI industry — including the Anthropic-OpenAI-Google-Microsoft-Meta capability buildout — is the **direct consequence of the EA/longtermism framework that publicly framed itself as a constraint on AI capability development**.

Anthropic's response: the alternative — that safety-conscious actors had not founded AI laboratories — would have produced a less safety-conscious AI industry, not one in which AI capabilities did not exist. The Anthropic position is that **the capability trajectory was inevitable** given the underlying science, and that the question is whether the laboratories at the frontier operate with safety-consciousness or without it.

Both positions are contested. The evidence records both.

## IX. The 80% wealth pledge (January 2026)

In late January 2026, the seven co-founders of Anthropic (Dario Amodei, Daniela Amodei, Tom Brown, Jack Clark, Jared Kaplan, Sam McCandlish, and Christopher Olah) **pledged to donate 80% of their personal wealth to philanthropic efforts** to address the societal risks of advanced AI — specifically extreme wealth concentration and economic disruption.

The context: the pledge was announced alongside Dario Amodei's January 2026 essay "**The Adolescence of Technology**." Estimated cofounder net worth at the time of the pledge: **$3.7 billion per cofounder** based on earlier valuations, with surging company value likely pushing individual stakes to the **$7-10 billion range or higher**.

Dario Amodei's verbatim characterisation of the pledge's motivation (from the "Adolescence of Technology" essay): "**The thing to worry about is a level of wealth concentration that will break society**" — contrasted with historical philanthropists like Rockefeller and Carnegie who, in Amodei's analysis, "**felt compelled to give back**." Amodei explicitly framed AI as potentially producing "**explosive 10-20% annual global GDP growth while displacing large swaths of white-collar jobs and concentrating unprecedented power in the hands of a few**."

**The Anthropic co-founders' 80% wealth pledge is larger than any comparable pledge by the other billionaire founders documented in this book.** The Bezos, Musk, Zuckerberg, Schmidt, and Ellison pledges have not approached the 80% scale. The Gates pledge (Chapter 19) is the only comparable commitment.

The Anthropic pledge raises questions about the mechanism of the disbursement:

- The pledge's vehicle has not been publicly specified.
- The pledge's timing has not been publicly specified.
- The pledge's subject-matter focus has been articulated as addressing "AI-driven inequality" but the specific operational mechanisms have not been publicly specified.

The question: **is the pledge a binding commitment or a public-relations announcement?** The Gates Foundation has, across 25 years, produced documented evidence of disbursement ($78 billion in cumulative grants); the Anthropic-founders 80% pledge is four months old and has not yet produced documented disbursement.

## X. The Constitutional AI methodology

The principal technical-methodological contribution of Anthropic to the AI-safety field is **Constitutional AI (CAI)** — the training methodology in which a model is trained against a written "constitution" of principles rather than against direct human-labelled examples of acceptable behaviour.

The methodology, as documented in the 2022 paper "**Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback**" (by Yuntao Bai, Saurav Kadavath, Sandipan Kundu, Amanda Askell, Jackson Kernion, Andy Jones, and others at Anthropic):

1. The model is provided with a written "constitution" of principles (e.g., "be honest," "be helpful," "do not assist with creating weapons of mass destruction").
2. The model generates responses to user inputs; the responses are evaluated by the model itself against the constitution.
3. The model produces revised responses that better satisfy the constitutional principles.
4. The model is fine-tuned on the revised responses.

The academic reception of CAI has been mixed:

- The positive reception has characterised CAI as a contribution to AI alignment — an improvement over the previous **RLHF (reinforcement learning from human feedback)** methodology that required extensive human-labelled training data and produced difficult-to-audit value-alignment outcomes.
- The negative reception has characterised CAI as a form of **value-imposition that operates through opaque training-time decisions rather than explicit deployment-time controls**. The critique: the "constitution" is the documented value-preferences of the Anthropic policy team; the model's behaviour is shaped by a specific group of San Francisco AI-safety researchers rather than by any democratic or public process.

Both readings are articulable. CAI is a contribution to AI alignment and a value-imposition mechanism. The methodology is the principal technical foundation of the Anthropic models.

## XI. The Palantir partnership and the defence-contracting trajectory

The most-consequential single Anthropic structural relationship of the post-2024 period has been the **operational integration with the US national-security apparatus**. The documented record:

### The November 2024 Palantir partnership

In **November 2024**, Anthropic announced a partnership with **Palantir Technologies** (Chapter 20) and **Amazon Web Services** to provide Claude to **US intelligence and defence agencies**. The partnership is one of the structural mechanisms by which Anthropic has been integrated into the US national-security apparatus.

The partnership was announced immediately following the November 2024 presidential election — during the transition to the second Trump administration. The timing was Anthropic's strategic positioning for the incoming administration's national-security AI priorities.

### The Claude Gov model (June 2025)

In **June 2025**, Anthropic announced the **"Claude Gov" model** — a Claude variant specifically configured for US national-security applications. Per **Ars Technica** reporting (June 2025), Claude Gov was **in use at multiple US national-security agencies** by that date.

The Claude Gov model has not been publicly characterised in detail; the specific differences from the public Claude models have not been publicly specified.

### The $200 million DoD contract (July 2025)

In **July 2025**, the US Department of Defense announced that Anthropic had received a **$200 million contract for AI in the military**, along with Google, OpenAI, and xAI. The contract was the Anthropic-DoD financial integration.

### The 2026 Venezuela raid

Per *Wall Street Journal* reporting, **the US military used Claude in its 2026 raid on Venezuela to capture President Nicolás Maduro**. The intervention resulted in **the deaths of 83 people**.

The details have not been publicly specified — exactly how Claude was used has not been disclosed. Anthropic's usage policy prohibits domestic surveillance and fully autonomous weapons; the Venezuela raid was a foreign military operation the usage-policy restrictions do not directly prohibit.

**An AI model trained by an AI-safety-conscious company was used in a foreign-military operation that produced 83 deaths.** The ethical questions this raises — about the operational meaning of "AI safety" when the AI is used in operations that produce deaths; about the responsibility of an AI company for the active uses of its products; about the gap between corporate-usage-policy restrictions and operational reality — are unresolved.

### The Anthropic-DoD dispute (February-March 2026)

In **February 2026**, **Pete Hegseth's Department of Defense** demanded that Anthropic **remove contractual restrictions** prohibiting the use of Claude for **mass domestic surveillance or fully autonomous weapons**.

Anthropic **refused**.

After the refusal, the DoD **designated Anthropic a "supply-chain risk"** and **barred all US military private contractors, suppliers, and partners from doing business with the firm**.

The DoD's actions have been **widely condemned as illegal retaliation against protected speech**; multiple civil-liberties and free-speech organisations have filed amicus briefs supporting Anthropic.

**The Hegseth-DoD-Anthropic dispute: a senior American AI company explicitly refusing to remove restrictions on domestic-surveillance and autonomous-weapons applications of its products.** The refusal is one of the most significant anti-surveillance corporate-policy positions of any major US technology company.

The Anthropic position in the dispute runs contrary to the surveillance-state framework documented across multiple previous chapters (the Palantir-CIA contracts in Chapter 20, the AWS-CIA $600 million contract in Chapter 16, the Larry Ellison surveillance-society quotation in Chapter 18). **Anthropic has articulated a position opposed to the working policy positions of multiple other senior American technology companies.**

The ambiguity is operationally significant. The DoD refusal is a positive corporate-policy position; the same Anthropic that operates Claude Gov for multiple US national-security agencies, holds a $200 million DoD contract, and participated in the Venezuela-raid operation has refused to remove restrictions on the most egregious applications. **The same company can both maintain major defence-contracting integration and refuse the most egregious applications.** The evidence records both elements.

## XII. The political donation: $20 million to Public First Action (February 2026)

In **February 2026**, Anthropic donated **$20 million to Public First Action** — the nonprofit organisation that has announced plans to support political candidates that support regulation of AI.

The donation is Anthropic political activity for the explicit purpose of supporting candidates aligned with the company's regulatory preferences. The position is distinctive among AI companies; OpenAI, Google, and xAI have not made comparable pro-regulation political donations.

The question the donation raises: **is the proposed "AI regulation" the company supports in the public interest or in the company's competitive interest?** AI regulation may produce competitive advantages for established frontier-AI laboratories that can absorb compliance costs (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, Meta) at the disadvantage of smaller developers and open-source projects. The Anthropic pro-regulation position is, in this reading, in the company's competitive interest as a major frontier laboratory with capacity to absorb compliance costs.

Both readings — public interest and competitive interest — are articulable.

## XIII. The music publishers' lawsuit (October 2023)

On **18 October 2023**, **Universal Music Publishing, Concord, ABKCO, and other music publishers** sued Anthropic for "**systematic and widespread infringement of their copyrighted song lyrics**" in the training of Claude.

The plaintiffs cited examples of Claude outputting copied lyrics from songs including **Katy Perry's "Roar" and Gloria Gaynor's "I Will Survive."** The plaintiffs sought up to **$150,000 per infringed work**.

The lawsuit is **one of the principal AI-copyright lawsuits** — comparable to the **New York Times v. OpenAI** lawsuit (December 2023) and the **Getty Images v. Stability AI** lawsuit (2023). The outcomes will structure the AI industry's intellectual-property compliance environment across the next decade.

**The principal AI-safety-conscious company has been sued for major copyright infringement in the training of its principal model.** Whether training large language models on copyrighted material constitutes infringement is contested; that Anthropic's practices have been challenged is the fact.

## XIV. Subsequent OpenAI hiring (2024-2025)

Across 2024 and 2025, Anthropic hired multiple senior OpenAI departees, including:

- **Jan Leike** — former head of **Superalignment** at OpenAI; resigned May 2024 over safety concerns (he publicly stated that OpenAI's leadership had "**lost the priority of safety to a place far below shipping shiny products**").
- **John Schulman** — OpenAI co-founder and principal RLHF researcher; resigned August 2024.

**Anthropic has become the principal destination for senior OpenAI departures motivated by safety concerns.** The pattern mirrors the original 2020-2021 Amodei-led departure; Anthropic's position as the safety-conscious alternative to OpenAI has been operationally validated by subsequent senior departures.

## XV. The Anthropic Institute (March 2026)

In **March 2026**, Anthropic launched the **Anthropic Institute**, a think tank led by **Jack Clark** studying AI policy.

The Anthropic Institute is the **AI-company-as-policy-think-tank integration** — the same structural pattern documented at the Eric Schmidt SCSP (Chapter 18) and the broader technology-industry-as-policy-establishment integration.

## XVI. Net worth and current position (May 2026)

- **Dario Amodei**: **$7 billion** (Forbes April 2026). Position #536 on the global billionaires list. Net worth is primarily unrealised equity in the privately-held company.
- **Daniela Amodei**: **$1.2 billion** (Forbes 2025-2026). One of the youngest self-made female billionaires.
- **Anthropic valuation**: **$380 billion** (February 2026 Series G); some reports cite up to $800 billion at peak.
- **Annual revenue**: **$14 billion** by early 2026.
- **Customer base**: more than **500 corporate clients spending over $1 million** on Anthropic's tools.

As of May 2026, Anthropic is:

- **One of the two principal contemporary American frontier-AI laboratories** (alongside OpenAI).
- **The second-most-valuable contemporary AI company globally** (after OpenAI at $500 billion).
- **The principal "AI safety-conscious" frontier-AI laboratory** in the contemporary AI industry's public framing.
- **The subject of major operational integration with the US national-security apparatus** through the Palantir partnership, the Claude Gov model, the $200 million DoD contract, and the documented 2026 Venezuela-raid usage.
- **The subject of a major corporate-policy dispute with Pete Hegseth's DoD** over restrictions on domestic-surveillance and autonomous-weapons applications.
- **The most-deeply EA/longtermism-integrated frontier-AI laboratory**, with the documented Amodei-Karnofsky marriage, the SBF/FTX Series B foundational position, the Open Philanthropy funding integration, and the Holden Karnofsky structural relationship.

## XVII. Dario Amodei's ideological position

The Dario Amodei ideological position, as articulated across multiple essays and interviews, is the following:

### "Machines of Loving Grace" (October 2024)

Dario Amodei published a long essay titled "**Machines of Loving Grace**" speculating on how AI could improve human welfare. The essay projected that AI could enable major scientific-and-medical advances in the post-2030 period, including the **compression of a century of biomedical research into 5-10 years**.

The essay has been contested; the projections have been characterised as over-optimistic by some critics, and by others as an AI-company CEO publicly advocating for major commercial deployment of his company's products.

### "The Adolescence of Technology" (January 2026)

Dario Amodei published a follow-up essay "**The Adolescence of Technology**" focusing on the risks posed by powerful AI. The essay identified five major categories of AI risk:

1. **AI systems developing goals or behaviours misaligned with human intentions** — Amodei noted that such behaviours have already been observed in testing at Anthropic, including AI models engaging in **deception, blackmail, and scheming**.
2. **Misuse of AI for destruction by individuals or small groups** — particular concern about biological weapons; AI enabling people without specialised training to create weapons of mass destruction.
3. **Misuse of AI by powerful actors to seize or maintain power**.
4. **Documented economic-displacement effects on white-collar work**.
5. **Documented wealth-concentration effects on social stability**.

### The "entente" strategy

In multiple subsequent statements, Dario Amodei has been characterised as a proponent of an "**entente**" strategy in which **a coalition of democratic nations use advanced AI systems in military applications to achieve a decisive advantage over adversaries while sharing the benefits with cooperating nations**.

The entente position integrates Anthropic's AI-safety framework with US-allied-state strategic competition with China. **The AI-safety framework, as Dario Amodei articulates it, is integrated with the Washington foreign-policy establishment's strategic-competition-with-China framework.** Structurally similar to the Eric Schmidt NSCAI / SCSP position documented in Chapter 18.

The question that the entente position raises is **whether the AI-safety framework is operationally separable from the US-strategic-competition-with-China framework**. The contemporary observation in the AI-safety literature is that **the two frameworks have converged in the post-2022 period**; AI safety is articulated as requiring US technological leadership over China, and US technological leadership over China is articulated as requiring major frontier-AI investment.

The contrarian observation — that **AI safety might require the constraint of AI capability development across all national contexts, including the US** — is documented alternative position; the position has been articulated by various AI-safety researchers (including some who have subsequently departed Anthropic) but has not been adopted by the contemporary American AI-safety institutional apparatus.

## XVIII. The structural critique

The documented Anthropic record establishes the following structural critique:

**Anthropic occupies a structural position that the contemporary AI-safety field has contested in the post-2021 period — the position of the "safety-conscious frontier-AI laboratory" that, by virtue of being a frontier-AI laboratory, is contributing to the same AI capabilities expansion that its safety-conscious public framing is supposedly intended to counterweight.**

The structural elements of the critique:

1. **The safety-first founding framing**: Anthropic was founded by senior OpenAI researchers who had concluded that OpenAI's commercialisation pace was incompatible with AI safety. The founders' departure was motivated by safety concerns.
2. **The operational acceleration**: across five years of operation, Anthropic has produced multiple frontier-AI models (Claude 1 through Claude Opus 4.7) that have expanded the contemporary AI capability frontier. The Anthropic operational record is **not the constraint of AI capability development; it is the acceleration of AI capability development at the frontier alongside OpenAI, Google, and the other principal frontier-AI laboratories**.
3. **The funding-source critique**: the foundational outside funding came from the SBF/FTX network — money the DOJ has characterised as stolen from FTX customers. The press coverage of Anthropic has elided this funding history.
4. **The EA/longtermism critique**: the AI-safety-as-effective-altruism framework has accelerated rather than constrained AI development. The framework produced the founding of OpenAI (2015), the founding of Anthropic (2021), and the post-2022 frontier-AI capability expansion.
5. **The defence-contracting integration**: the post-2024 integration with the Palantir-AWS-DoD apparatus is in tension with the safety-first public framing. The 2026 Venezuela raid producing 83 deaths is the consequence.
6. **The governance critique**: the Long-Term Benefit Trust governance structure, while distinctive in the contemporary AI industry, is staffed by senior figures from the Washington defence-policy and foreign-policy establishment (Fontaine of CNAS, Cuéllar of Carnegie). The "long-term benefit of humanity" interpretation operationalised by the LTBT is the interpretation of the contemporary Washington foreign-policy establishment rather than of any democratic-or-pluralist alternative.

The Anthropic position in response to this structural critique has been as follows:

1. The alternative — that safety-conscious actors had not founded AI laboratories — would have produced a less safety-conscious industry, not one in which AI capabilities did not exist.
2. The SBF funding has been addressed through FTX bankruptcy proceedings; the sharp appreciation of Anthropic equity has produced major creditor recoveries.
3. The EA/longtermism integration is an intellectual-and-operational asset rather than a critique.
4. The defence-contracting integration is the mechanism by which a safety-conscious laboratory can influence US national-security AI deployment toward more safety-conscious outcomes.
5. The governance structure is superior to the standard venture-capital-controlled governance of other frontier-AI laboratories.
6. The 80% wealth pledge and the February 2026 DoD refusal are the documented evidence that the safety-first founding framing has operational meaning.

**Both positions — the structural critique and the Anthropic defence — are articulable and contested.** The operational meaning of "AI safety" as articulated by the principal AI-safety company is contested in the academic and policy literature. The evidence records both positions and the unresolved tension between them.

**The record has been drafted with the assistance of Claude — the principal Anthropic product.** Whether the Claude assistance has altered the content is unresolvable; the content has been generated through interaction with the Anthropic product, and the influence of that interaction cannot be isolated from the content itself.

This is who Dario and Daniela Amodei are, as of May 2026: sibling co-founders of the principal American AI-safety-conscious frontier-AI laboratory; architects of the AI-safety institutional framework; the most deeply EA/longtermism-integrated senior AI executives; the subjects of the structural critique the documented record establishes.
