# Chapter 22: Masayoshi Son — SoftBank, the Vision Funds, and the Stargate Pipeline

**Masayoshi Son** — founder, Chairman, CEO, Representative Director, and largest shareholder of SoftBank Group; Chairman of Arm Holdings; Chairman of Stargate LLC — has committed somewhere between $80 billion and $140 billion to the American AI buildout. The exact figure moves every quarter because Son has not stopped writing cheques. OpenAI, the Stargate data-centre chain, the Founders-Fund-adjacent defence-tech portfolio, the wider AI infrastructure — all carry Son's capital. He has stated he intends to commit $500 billion across Stargate and adjacent vehicles by 2029. No other single investor, American or otherwise, has matched the scale.

Son is also the principal commercial intermediary between **the Gulf sovereign-wealth layer** (Chapter 23) and the American AI principals. His Vision Funds were funded by Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund (PIF) and Abu Dhabi's Mubadala; his Stargate co-equity investors include Abu Dhabi's MGX. He is not American, his capital is not American, and his political loyalties are not American. He is the bridge — between Japanese corporate capital, Gulf sovereign-wealth capital, Chinese internet capital (his foundational Alibaba position), and the American AI-and-defence buildout. Without Son's capital flow, OpenAI's revenue-to-capex gap could not have been financed.

## I. Origins, family, and the Zainichi Korean formation

Masayoshi Son was born **11 August 1957 in Tosu, Saga Prefecture, Japan**. He was born **Masayoshi Yasumoto** — the Japanese alias the family used. His legal Korean surname is **Son** (손, 孫). The family is **Zainichi Korean**, third-generation: ethnic Koreans permanently resident in Japan, the diaspora produced by the 1910-1945 Japanese colonial occupation of Korea.

His grandfather **Son Jong-kyung** (손종경) migrated from **Daegu**, Korea to Japan during the colonial period and worked as a coal miner in Kyushu. His father **Son Sam-heon** (Korean) / **Yasumoto Sanken** (the Japanese alias) was a serial small-businessman: fisherman, pig farmer, fishpond operator, illegal sake bootlegger, loan shark, and the successful operator of a **pachinko parlour** (Japanese pinball-gambling arcade). The pachinko business produced the family's first stable income. Pachinko was — and remains — one of the few Japanese industries Zainichi Koreans were permitted to enter in the post-war period when most professional and corporate roles were closed to them by social discrimination, family-registry-based hiring restrictions, and outright legal barriers. The pachinko industry was, by some counts, around 70% Zainichi-Korean-operated through the 1970s-1990s.

Masayoshi was the second of four brothers. His younger brother **Taizo Son** founded GungHo Online Entertainment, the Japanese mobile-game company whose hit *Puzzle & Dragons* posted annual revenues over ¥100 billion for five consecutive years from 2013. Two other brothers are operationally less public.

The Zainichi Korean formation is the central biographical fact about Son. He has, across decades of interviews, returned to the experience: being pelted with stones by Japanese classmates in primary school; being denied bank credit on the basis of his Korean family registry when he tried to start SoftBank in 1981; the family's use of the **Yasumoto** alias to "pass" as Japanese (the standard Zainichi practice of using a Japanese tsūmei alongside the legal Korean name); his decision in 1990, after acquiring Japanese citizenship, to use the **Son** Korean surname as his legal name rather than the Yasumoto alias — a public statement of identity at a time when most successful Zainichi Koreans were still publicly using Japanese names. The Lionel Barber 2024 biography *Gambling Man*, sourced from over 150 interviews including Son himself, treats the Zainichi experience as the load-bearing fact of his psychological formation: the outsider posture, the willingness to take risks Japanese corporate culture forbids, the preference for American business norms over Japanese ones, the comfort with public confrontation.

The family lived, in Son's childhood, on the edge of a Tosu shantytown. The pachinko business eventually produced enough income for the family to move to better housing. Son has said, repeatedly, that his earliest memory of business was watching his father work. The father's bootlegging-to-pachinko trajectory — illegal sake production sold underground, then loan-sharking, then the eventual pachinko parlour — is the entrepreneurial inheritance Son took into his own career. He has framed it, in interviews, as the discriminated outsider's only available path to wealth in mid-century Japan. The framing is Son's own; the underlying business activity is what it is: bootlegging is bootlegging, loan-sharking is loan-sharking, pachinko is gambling. Son did not start there; his father did. Son's own career began on the other side of the discrimination barrier his family had broken through.

No documented family allegation against Son personally exists on the public record. He married **Masami Ohno** (a Japanese citizen) in 1979 during their university period in the United States; the couple has two daughters, **Maya Kawana** and a second daughter whose name has been kept out of the public record. Son has kept his immediate family out of the press. Absence of allegation is not exoneration.

## II. Education and the early career

- **Kyushu Kurume University Affiliated High School** (April 1973). Son dropped out in the autumn of 1973 to move to the United States.
- **Serramonte High School, Daly City, California** (1974). The standard Zainichi-Korean American-immigration route through the San Francisco Bay area, which had absorbed waves of Korean-American immigrants since the 1950s. Son completed high school in three weeks by passing the required exams.
- **Holy Names College, Oakland** (1974-1975). Two-year transfer institution.
- **University of California, Berkeley** (1977-1980). BA Economics. Concurrently enrolled in courses in Computer Science. During the Berkeley period he co-developed, with a small group of Berkeley faculty (Forrest Mozer of the Physics Department, others), a **portable electronic voice translator** — a handheld device that converted Japanese phrases to English and other languages. He sold the patent to **Sharp Corporation** for, by his own subsequent accounts, around $1 million. He also imported and resold Japanese arcade-game machines on the Berkeley campus, reportedly earning around another $1 million from the arcade-import business.

The decisive influence on his career was the meeting with **Den Fujita**, founder of McDonald's Japan. The teenage Son had repeatedly requested a meeting; Fujita finally agreed to fifteen minutes. Fujita told Son the future was in computers, not McDonald's-style food retail, and that Son should study English and go to America. Son followed the advice exactly. He returned to Japan after the Berkeley graduation in 1980, then in 1981 founded **Nihon SoftBank Corporation** in Tokyo with three early collaborators and a borrowed corner of a friend's office space. He was 23 years old.

The early-1980s formation is the predicate. Son had, by the time he founded SoftBank, made his first $2 million through patents and arcade imports; he had completed an American university education in economics-and-computing at a moment when Japanese corporate culture had not yet begun adopting personal-computer software; he had married a Japanese citizen who was studying in the United States with him; he had identified the personal-computer software industry as the largest available open commercial opportunity in Japan; and he had, in his Zainichi outsider posture, the cultural permission to act on the opportunity at a scale that would have been forbidden to a salaryman following the standard corporate-Japan trajectory.

## III. The SoftBank trajectory, 1981-2016 — the predicate to the AI period

**1981.** Nihon SoftBank Corporation founded in Tokyo. Initial business: distributing personal-computer software to the small base of contemporary Japanese PC retailers. Three employees plus Son. First-year revenue under ¥35 million. The company's name, *SoftBank*, meant "bank of software" — a deliberate framing of software as a financial-industry asset rather than a consumer product.

**1982.** Son launches *Oh! PC* and *Oh! MZ*, the first SoftBank publishing magazines covering personal-computer hardware lines from NEC, Sharp, and Fujitsu. The magazines build the audience the software distribution would subsequently serve. By 1986 SoftBank's publishing arm was producing eight magazines.

**1983.** Son contracts hepatitis B. He is given a five-year life expectancy. He spends two years in and out of hospital, undergoing experimental treatments. He survives. The hepatitis episode is the recorded inflection point in his subsequent risk tolerance: the man who had been given five years to live and got more was, after the recovery, prepared to bet at scales the surviving Japanese corporate establishment found incomprehensible.

**1990.** Son acquires Japanese citizenship. He chooses the **Son** Korean surname as his legal name rather than the Yasumoto Japanese alias.

**1994.** SoftBank IPOs on the Japanese over-the-counter market. Son uses the proceeds for the acquisition spree that follows. He has described his approach, in subsequent interviews, as ***"diplomatic warfare"*** — winning by absorbing rather than confronting.

**1995-1996.** The Yahoo! investment. Son makes a $2 million investment in the Stanford-startup Yahoo! in 1995; by 1996 he had upped his position to a 40% stake. The 1996 SoftBank-Yahoo! joint venture creates **Yahoo! Japan**, which becomes the dominant Japanese internet portal of the late 1990s and 2000s. Yahoo! Japan is, by 2026, still operating as **LY Corporation** (after the 2023 merger with LINE) — and SoftBank still holds the principal stake.

**1996.** SoftBank and News Corporation jointly acquire a 21% stake in **Asahi National Broadcasting Co.** (TV Asahi) — Son's first move into Japanese media ownership. The stake is sold within a year after political objections about foreign capital in Japanese broadcasting; the structural ambition is on the record.

**2000.** The Alibaba investment. Son's $20 million bet on Jack Ma's three-year-old Hangzhou startup. By 2014, after Alibaba's IPO, SoftBank's stake is worth over $75 billion. By 2018, with additional purchases, the stake is worth $132 billion. The Alibaba investment is, in absolute dollar terms, the single most profitable venture-capital investment in history.

**2001.** SoftBank launches **Yahoo! BB** broadband internet in Japan, undercutting NTT on price and giving away free modems on Tokyo street corners. The model loses money for several years before reaching profitability; it establishes SoftBank as a Japanese telecommunications player.

**2006.** Son acquires **Vodafone Japan** for ¥1.75 trillion (around $17.5 billion at the time, $11.2 billion of it through a bank-syndicated loan). Rebranded as **SoftBank Mobile**. Son's friendship with Steve Jobs, established through the Yahoo! BB period, leads to SoftBank Mobile becoming the exclusive iPhone distributor in Japan from 2008 onward. By 2012, SoftBank Mobile's operating margin had doubled to 21%; subscriber base passed 30 million.

**11 March 2011.** Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster. Son personally pledges ¥10 billion ($125 million) plus his remaining salary until retirement to disaster relief. He launches SB Energy to build solar capacity across Japan; he publicly criticises Japan's nuclear-industry leadership for "creating the problem that worries Japanese the most today." The post-Fukushima energy turn is one of the documented inflections in Son's public political-economic positioning: he becomes, in the Japanese context, one of the senior business voices for renewable-energy infrastructure.

**October 2012.** SoftBank announces the acquisition of a 70% stake in **Sprint Nextel** for $20.1 billion. The Sprint acquisition is the first major American-corporate transaction Son personally drives. The integration period is operationally difficult; Sprint's subscriber and financial performance lags through the mid-2010s.

**18 July 2016.** SoftBank announces the acquisition of **Arm Holdings** for £24 billion ($32 billion) — the largest acquisition of a European technology company in history at the time. Arm is the British semiconductor-IP firm whose chip architecture sits inside 95% of all smartphones globally. The Arm acquisition is the pivotal strategic move of the second half of Son's career. He buys Arm not for its existing revenue (Arm's licensing revenue at the time was $1 billion annually) but for its position in the supply chain of every future computing device. The acquisition closes 5 September 2016.

The post-Arm period is the AI period.

## IV. The Vision Fund era, 2016-2024

In late 2016 Son begins assembling what becomes the **SoftBank Vision Fund** — at its 2017 close, a **$100 billion** technology-focused investment fund, the largest single private technology-investment vehicle in history. The fund's structure is the mechanism by which the Gulf sovereign-wealth pipeline that Chapter 23 will cover was channelled into the American technology industry.

**The Vision Fund 1 capital sources** (the publicly disclosed structure):
- **Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund (PIF)**, chaired by Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman: **$45 billion**. Roughly $35 billion in preference shares paying 7% annually, the remainder in common equity.
- **Abu Dhabi's Mubadala Investment Company**, chaired by Sheikh Mansour bin Zayed Al Nahyan: **$15 billion**.
- **SoftBank Group**: $28 billion (including Son's personal commitments).
- **Apple, Qualcomm, Foxconn, Sharp, Larry Ellison, and the Mubadala-adjacent UAE investors**: a combined $20 billion (the precise allocation across these LPs is not publicly disclosed).

The September 2016 Riyadh meeting between Son and MBS — at which Son reportedly secured the $45 billion PIF commitment in 45 minutes — was the inflection point. PIF's investment in the Vision Fund was MBS's first major demonstration of the **Vision 2030** strategy: the deployment of Saudi oil-revenue surplus into non-oil productive assets, with American technology positioned as the principal investment category. The Saudi sovereign-wealth flow into Silicon Valley, traced through Vision Fund and subsequent vehicles, is the mechanism by which the Saudi state has, since 2016, taken indirect equity positions in nearly every senior contemporary American technology company.

**Vision Fund 1's principal portfolio investments** (at peak): Uber (~$7.7 billion at investment), WeWork (~$10 billion total before the 2019 rescue), DoorDash, Coupang, Slack, Didi, Grab, Paytm, Oyo, ByteDance, Klarna, Nvidia (briefly, before Son sold the position in 2019 for around $4 billion in realised gains — a position that would have been worth over $130 billion by 2025 if held). The fund invested in around 88 portfolio companies through 2020.

**The WeWork collapse, 2019.** Vision Fund 1's principal disaster. SoftBank had invested over $10 billion in WeWork by mid-2019, valuing the company at $47 billion. The October 2019 IPO attempt collapsed when WeWork's S-1 filings revealed governance failures and structural unprofitability. SoftBank bailed out the company in a $9.5 billion rescue, eventually writing down WeWork to a small fraction of the investment value. The episode damaged the Gulf sovereign-wealth investor relationships. PIF and Mubadala both signalled, in the months following WeWork, that they would either not invest in Vision Fund 2 or would invest at much-reduced commitments. The Bloomberg reporting of September 2019 documented PIF as planning to invest only "profits from Vision Fund 1" into the successor vehicle, and Mubadala as considering paring its commitment below $10 billion.

**Vision Fund 2 (announced 2019).** The target was $108 billion. The actual realisation, by 2022, was much smaller — $56 billion, with SoftBank itself providing the majority and the Gulf principals largely sitting out. The fund's average per-company investment fell from $943 million in Vision Fund 1 to $192 million in Vision Fund 2, reflecting the smaller capital base.

**The 2022 collapse.** Vision Fund 1 and 2 posted combined losses of around $32 billion for SoftBank's fiscal year ending March 2022 (Japanese fiscal-year basis). The losses reflected the global technology-stock decline of 2022, the Chinese regulatory crackdown on Vision Fund portfolio company Didi Global, and the deflation of late-stage venture valuations across the unprofitable-growth-stock category Son had heavily backed. The 2022 reporting period produced what was, at the time, the largest single annual loss ever reported by a Japanese corporation.

**The Arm IPO recovery, September 2023.** Arm's relisting on Nasdaq on 14 September 2023 valued the company at $54.5 billion at IPO and $65 billion by end of first trading day — over twice the $32 billion SoftBank had paid for it in 2016. SoftBank retained 90% of Arm after the listing. By February 2024, with Arm's stock continuing to appreciate on the AI-chip-demand narrative, the SoftBank gain on Arm exceeded $16 billion in realised-and-unrealised terms — a figure CNBC's coverage observed exceeded SoftBank's total WeWork losses. By 2026, with Arm trading between $130 and $150 per share through the year so far, SoftBank's remaining position is worth over $200 billion, making the Arm holding the single largest line on the SoftBank balance sheet.

The Arm IPO recovery is the operational predicate to the 2024-2026 AI capital-deployment period. Without the Arm windfall, SoftBank would not have had the balance-sheet capacity to commit the OpenAI and Stargate capital.

## V. The AI period: OpenAI, Stargate, and the 2024-2026 capital deployment

Son's documented public statements through the 2020-2024 period repeatedly identified **artificial general intelligence** as the largest economic opportunity of his career, larger than the original Yahoo! and Alibaba bets. His framing — articulated at the October 2024 SoftBank World event in Tokyo — was that **artificial superintelligence (ASI)** would arrive within a decade and would be "10,000 times smarter than humans." The framing is technologically tendentious but politically operational: it is the framing under which Son justified the magnitude of capital deployment he subsequently committed.

**September 2024.** Vision Fund 2 invests **$500 million** in OpenAI through a tertiary round. SoftBank had missed the principal early OpenAI funding rounds (the Microsoft 2019 and 2023 rounds, the Thrive-led 2024 round); the September 2024 commitment was Son's entry on terms set by the existing investor cap table.

**January 2025.** Son meets Sam Altman (and, separately, Larry Ellison) at Trump's January 20 inauguration. On 21 January 2025 — Trump's first full day in office — Son, Altman, and Ellison stand alongside Trump in the White House Roosevelt Room to announce **the Stargate Project**: a new joint-venture company to invest **$500 billion over four years** in American AI data-centre infrastructure, with **$100 billion** committed to be deployed immediately. The four initial equity investors are:
- **SoftBank** (financial leadership; Son as Stargate Chairman)
- **OpenAI** (operational leadership; Altman as CEO)
- **Oracle** (data-centre operations; Ellison)
- **MGX** (Abu Dhabi sovereign-wealth, chaired by Sheikh Tahnoun bin Zayed Al Nahyan — Chapter 23)

The Stargate announcement is the largest single private-sector commercial announcement of the early Trump second term. Trump describes the project as ***"the largest AI infrastructure project in history."*** The project's stated goal is to "secure American leadership in AI." The political framing is national-security: the Stargate buildout will prevent China from achieving AI parity. The commercial framing is more specific: the data centres will be exclusively used by OpenAI.

**29 January 2025.** SoftBank announces it is in talks to invest **up to $25 billion** directly in OpenAI equity. This is on top of the Stargate commitment.

**March-September 2025.** Stargate site selection. The flagship site is in **Abilene, Texas**, developed in partnership with **Crusoe Energy** and **Oracle**. Five additional sites are announced through the year: Wisconsin (developed by Oracle with Vantage Data Centers), Ohio, Pennsylvania, Louisiana, and one undisclosed Midwest location. By September 2025 the combined planned capacity reaches nearly **7 gigawatts** with over **$400 billion** in committed investment over three years. Two Abilene buildings become operational in September 2025.

**December 2025.** SoftBank completes a **$41 billion** direct investment in OpenAI, securing an **11% stake** in the company. The investment is, in absolute dollar terms, among the largest private-company funding rounds in history. The capital is sourced from a combination of SoftBank's balance sheet (drawing on the Arm position), margin loans collateralised against the Arm holding, and bridge financing from a syndicate of close to 20 financial institutions. Reuters' December 2025 reporting documented that Son was "scrambling" to assemble the final $22.5 billion of the commitment by year-end; his principal lever was the untapped margin-loan capacity against the Arm position. The deal closed on time.

**January 2026.** SoftBank announces a joint $1 billion **SB Energy** commitment to renewable power generation for Stargate sites. The SB Energy unit, originally founded post-Fukushima for Japanese solar, is now the renewable-power provider for the American Stargate buildout.

**By May 2026.** SoftBank's combined exposure to OpenAI and Stargate (direct equity + Stargate commitment + bridge financing + SB Energy + adjacent vehicles) is somewhere between $80 billion and $140 billion, depending on which line items are counted. The exposure is concentrated in a single counterparty (OpenAI is the exclusive Stargate customer; Stargate's economics depend entirely on OpenAI's ability to generate the revenue to lease the data-centre capacity). The concentration risk is, by May 2026, the largest single bet of Son's career.

**The funding-source question.** Where is the SoftBank money coming from? The publicly disclosed sources, in order of magnitude:
1. The Arm position (collateralised margin loans against the May 2026 stock value of over $200 billion).
2. SoftBank's continuing reduction of its Alibaba position (the historic position has been sold down across multiple stages from 2022 onward; by 2025, SoftBank's residual Alibaba stake is below 1%).
3. Bank syndicate loans from a documented group of around 20 financial institutions, principally Japanese megabanks (Mizuho, Sumitomo Mitsui Banking Corporation, MUFG) plus selected American and European banks.
4. New equity issuance from Vision Fund 2 LPs (much smaller than VF1 commitments).
5. Continuing margin-loan capacity against SoftBank Group's own equity position.

The leverage stack is, by 2026, among the largest documented single-corporate-counterparty leverage stacks in the global financial system. If the OpenAI revenue trajectory does not materialise, the cascade of margin calls would be of systemic significance. The Japanese megabank syndicate is the documented backstop. The Gulf sovereign-wealth principals are the equity-level co-investors. The American government, through the Trump administration's regulatory posture on AI infrastructure, is the implicit political backstop. The structure is consistent with what the book has named in earlier chapters: the contemporary AI capability buildout is being financed by a coordinated arrangement among American technology principals, Gulf sovereign wealth, Japanese corporate balance-sheet leverage, and the American political-regulatory apparatus, on a counterparty-concentration risk that no single party would assume alone.

## VI. Ideology in Son's own words

Son is less ideologically articulate than Karp or Thiel; his public statements are more visionary-monologue than political-philosophical argument. The principal documented positions:

**1. The 300-year vision.** Son has stated, repeatedly across two decades, that SoftBank is built for a **300-year time horizon** — that he is constructing a corporate entity to outlast his own life by ten generations. The framing is on the record at multiple SoftBank annual general meetings. The 2010 "30-Year Vision" presentation laid out a specific 30-year plan in which SoftBank would become "the world's top 10 company by market capitalisation" by 2040. Son has framed the AGI buildout as the technology that makes the 300-year vision plausible: a corporation that operates AI infrastructure will, in Son's framing, retain commercial relevance across timescales that exceed any human-only company.

**2. The "artificial superintelligence is coming" framing.** At the October 2024 SoftBank World event Son stated that **ASI** — distinct from AGI; "10,000 times smarter than humans" — would arrive within a decade. The framing has been repeated at every public Son appearance since: at Davos January 2025, at the Stargate White House announcement, at the September 2025 Stargate expansion announcement. The framing is the rhetorical justification for the magnitude of capital commitment.

**3. The pro-Japan, pro-Korea ethno-pragmatist register.** Son has, since the 1990 surname-change, been one of the senior Japanese business figures publicly identifying as Zainichi Korean. He has used the position to advocate, in Japanese policy circles, for relaxation of the Japanese citizenship-by-descent rules and for greater Japanese acceptance of Korean and broader Asian immigration. He has invested in Korean technology companies (Coupang, Naver) at preferential valuations on the Korean-side relationship. He has also supported Japanese national-industrial-policy initiatives — the SB Energy solar buildout being the senior post-Fukushima example. The dual identity is, in his framing, an asset.

**4. The pro-American AI-buildout register.** From January 2025 onward Son has been one of the senior non-American voices framing American AI leadership as the necessary outcome of the contemporary technology cycle. At the Stargate announcement he stated that Trump's election victory was the moment he had been ***"waiting for, my whole life"*** — the political-economic precondition under which American AI infrastructure would be built at scale. The framing has been repeated across multiple subsequent appearances. Son is, by his own description, ideologically aligned with the Trump administration's AI infrastructure programme. He has been a documented donor and political figure in Japan; he has not, on the FEC public record, made personal political donations in the United States, but his corporate commitments through Stargate have functioned as the political-economic equivalent.

**5. The 2008 Lehman-equivalent risk acknowledgment.** Son has stated, repeatedly in interviews, that the AI capital-deployment cycle could end in collapse comparable to 1929 or 2008. His framing: the cycle's existence does not depend on whether the AGI thesis is right; it depends on whether enough principals are willing to commit capital while the thesis is unresolved. He is comfortable being personally responsible for the largest single bet of the cycle, on the precedent of his own previous bets: dot-com 2000 (lost 97% of personal wealth, recovered), Sprint 2012-2020 (lost over $20 billion before the T-Mobile merger), WeWork 2019 (lost over $10 billion), Vision Fund 2022 (lost over $30 billion). Son has framed himself as the man whose risk-tolerance is what makes the cycle possible. The concentration risk is the public-record fact of May 2026.

**6. The "we are early, not late" framing.** Son has stated, across the 2024-2026 period, that the AI buildout is in its first 5% of expected total capital expenditure; that the $500 billion Stargate commitment is the early-cycle position; that the eventual buildout will require $10 trillion or more in cumulative global capital expenditure across two decades; and that the contemporary debate about whether the buildout is overbuilt is a category error. The framing is consistent with what the senior AI-laboratory principals (Altman, the Amodeis, Hassabis) have articulated. Son's particular contribution is the magnitude of his actual capital commitment: he is, on the public record, the principal capital provider acting on the framing.

## VII. The political activity and the cross-border alignments

Son's political activity is operationally different from the American principals the book has dossiered. He does not make FEC-reportable personal donations in the United States. His political engagement is conducted through three channels:

**1. The Japanese channel.** Son is one of the senior business voices in contemporary Japanese policy. He maintains personal relationships with successive Japanese Prime Ministers (Abe, Suga, Kishida, Ishiba) and with the senior **METI** (Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry) figures who shape Japanese AI and semiconductor policy. He has been an advocate for Japanese national-industrial coordination with the American AI buildout — the position that produced Japan's $26 billion subsidy package for TSMC's Kumamoto chip-fab expansion in 2024 and the broader Japanese government alignment with US AI export controls on China.

**2. The American channel — through commercial commitments.** Son's political relationships in the United States are commercial. He attends the Trump inaugurations (2017 he committed to a $50 billion US investment package on a White House visit; 2025 he committed $100 billion to the US then upped to $500 billion through Stargate). He attends senior policy convenings (the Code Conference, Davos, the Sun Valley conference). He has personal relationships with Trump, with senior Trump administration figures (Lutnick at Commerce, Bessent at Treasury), with Altman, with Larry Ellison, with the senior Gulf principals MBS and Tahnoun bin Zayed. The relationships are conducted through capital commitments rather than political donations; the effect on US AI policy is the same.

**3. The Gulf channel.** Son's relationships with **MBS** (Saudi Arabia) and **Tahnoun bin Zayed** (Abu Dhabi) are the documented operational predicate for the Vision Fund 1 capital base and for MGX's Stargate participation. The relationships were built in the 2016-2017 period — Son met MBS in Tokyo in September 2016, in Riyadh later that month, and at multiple subsequent meetings; he met Tahnoun and the senior Abu Dhabi figures across the same period. The Khashoggi October 2018 murder briefly disrupted the public profile of the Saudi-Vision-Fund relationship; Son did not pull capital and the relationship continued. The full Gulf sovereign-wealth structure that Chapter 23 will cover is the network in which Son is the principal commercial intermediary to the American technology principals.

The cross-border alignments produce the outcome the book has been describing. Son is the bridge through which Saudi and Emirati capital reach the American AI-laboratory cap tables and the American AI data-centre buildout. Without the bridge, the Gulf capital would have to make direct relationships with Altman, Amodei, Karp, the senior Pentagon contracting figures, and the American political principals. The Gulf principals have, in some cases, chosen to make those direct relationships (the MGX direct equity in Stargate, the MGX direct equity in Anthropic and OpenAI). But the bulk of the Gulf-to-American AI capital flow has run through Son. The bridge has been profitable for Son personally — the management fees on Vision Fund 1 alone produced over $4 billion in fee revenue across 2017-2024 — and operationally critical for the buildout the American principals have executed.

## VIII. The SoftBank Group structure, the lieutenants, and the succession question

**SoftBank Group Corp. is a Japanese publicly listed holding company**, ticker **9984.T** on the Tokyo Stock Exchange. As of December 2025 SEC and Tokyo Stock Exchange filings:
- **Market capitalisation**: between ¥18 trillion and ¥22 trillion across the year so far ($120-150 billion).
- **Son's personal direct stake**: 35% (held directly and through controlled holding entities).
- **Collateralised position**: per SoftBank's December 2025 filing, about one-third of Son's holdings pledged as collateral against margin loans to close to 20 financial institutions.

The SoftBank Group structure is, by Japanese corporate standards, exceptionally concentrated in a single individual. Son's 35% holding (combined with his Representative Director / Chairman / CEO positions) gives him near-absolute operational control of the company. The voting structure is Japanese standard (one-share-one-vote, no dual-class) but the practical control is total. The succession question — what happens to SoftBank when Son retires or dies — is the verified major-uncertainty discussed in financial-press coverage from 2020 onward.

**The senior SoftBank Group leadership (May 2026):**
- **Masayoshi Son** — Chairman, CEO, Representative Director.
- **Yoshimitsu Goto** — Chief Financial Officer (since 2018). The principal architect of the post-2020 SoftBank balance-sheet restructuring; the active figure who has executed the Arm position and the AI capital deployment.
- **Rajeev Misra** — formerly Vision Fund CEO (2017-2022); left SoftBank in 2022 to launch his own fund **One Investment Management**, retaining advisory ties. Misra was Son's principal lieutenant through the Vision Fund 1 period; his departure marked one of the documented turning points in the post-WeWork Vision Fund era.
- **Marcelo Claure** — formerly COO of SoftBank Group (2019-2022); departed in January 2022 after a publicly disclosed compensation dispute with Son. Claure had been the Sprint-rescue principal and the broader operational integrator across the SoftBank portfolio.
- **Vikas Parekh** — current Vision Fund CFO; senior figure in the OpenAI / Stargate financial structuring.
- **Tadashi "Ted" Fujita** — board director; CFO predecessor.
- **Lip-Bu Tan** — independent board director (joined 2020); former Cadence Design Systems CEO; brings semiconductor-industry network ties.
- **Yuko Kawamoto** — independent board director; one of the senior corporate-governance figures in contemporary Japanese business; former Mitsubishi Bank executive and senior McKinsey partner.

**The succession question.** Son turned 68 in August 2025. He has publicly stated, repeatedly, that he intends to identify a successor before he turns 70 (so by August 2027). The candidates publicly identified at various points include Misra (now departed), Claure (now departed), Goto (CFO, current), and external possibilities from the senior Japanese business community. No formal succession plan has been publicly announced as of May 2026. The succession question is, structurally, the largest single open question about whether the SoftBank-OpenAI-Stargate capital commitment will hold across the time period the AI buildout requires.

**The SoftBank Vision Fund 2 portfolio (current operational holdings):** The fund has $56 billion in committed capital, with around 250 portfolio companies. The principal contemporary positions (May 2026):
- **OpenAI** (~11% stake, $41 billion December 2025 round)
- **Arm Holdings** (~90% retained ownership)
- **TikTok / ByteDance** (legacy Vision Fund 1 position; small residual after the Chinese-regulatory-driven 2021-2023 valuation collapse)
- **Coupang, DoorDash, Grab, Klarna** (legacy Vision Fund 1 positions)
- **Skild AI** (robotics, 2025 commitment $500 million)
- **Mapper** (precision-mapping for autonomous vehicles)
- **Cruise** (formerly GM Cruise; SoftBank retook position in late 2024)
- The **Stargate LLC** equity (operational vehicle, not classified as a Vision Fund holding per se)
- A long tail of smaller AI-adjacent and biotech positions

The portfolio is, by May 2026, more concentrated in AI and AI-adjacent positions than at any prior point in SoftBank's history. The concentration is deliberate: Son has stated, repeatedly, that the AI buildout is the only investment category that matters for the next decade.

## IX. The 2026 position — what Son controls, what he is worth, what he is doing

**Net worth.** Forbes (March 2026) places Son at around **$57 billion**, ranking him among the global top-50 wealthiest individuals. Bloomberg Billionaires Index (May 2026) places him in a similar range. The exact figure varies on a daily basis with SoftBank's stock price and with the mark-to-market valuation of the OpenAI position. The bulk of the wealth is his 35% SoftBank Group stake, valued at the May 2026 SoftBank market capitalisation.

**Operational position:**
- Chairman, CEO, Representative Director of SoftBank Group Corp.
- Chairman of **Arm Holdings plc**.
- Chairman of **Stargate LLC**.
- Owner of the **Fukuoka SoftBank Hawks** (Nippon Professional Baseball; the team has won eight Japan Series titles under Son's ownership).
- The principal Japanese-corporate counterpart to the senior American AI-laboratory and AI-infrastructure principals.

**Active threat assessment.** Son is, on the record, the principal foreign capital provider to the American AI buildout. The buildout is the instrument by which the surveillance and platform-monopoly capabilities documented across Chapters 11-21 are being extended to the AI substrate. Son's role is enabling: the American principals could not have financed the OpenAI / Stargate / data-centre capital expenditure from their own balance sheets at the timescales the cycle has required. Son's bridge from Gulf sovereign wealth and Japanese megabank leverage to the American technology cap table is the documented mechanism by which the timescale problem has been solved.

The active threat from Son's position is not personal — he is, in temperament and stated political-economic positioning, a less menacing figure than Thiel, Karp, or Andreessen. The active threat is the **counterparty concentration**: a single Japanese-listed holding company is operationally responsible for the capital provision to a single American AI laboratory whose revenue trajectory must justify the buildout. If the trajectory does not materialise on the timescale Son has committed to, the cascade — through Japanese megabank exposure, through Gulf sovereign-wealth equity positions, through Arm-collateralised margin loans, through American data-centre infrastructure that has been built on the assumption of OpenAI revenue — would be of global systemic significance. The buildout's success depends on a small set of correlated bets. Son holds the most concentrated correlated position any single individual holds.

The secondary threat is **the Gulf bridge function**. Son is the principal commercial intermediary through which Saudi and Emirati sovereign wealth has reached the American AI capital structure. If the bridge fails — if Son retires, dies, or the SoftBank balance sheet falters — the Gulf principals will either have to make direct relationships with the American principals (which they are increasingly doing through MGX) or the capital flow will stop. The bridge has been the working mechanism by which the American AI buildout has been financed without requiring direct US-government acknowledgment of the magnitude of the Gulf sovereign-wealth role. The bridge is fragile.



## X. The verdict

Son's role in the contemporary AI buildout is not the role of a Silicon Valley operator. He is not the founder of an AI laboratory. He is not the operator of a surveillance product line. He is not the architect of a platform monopoly. He is the **financing principal**: the man who has, across 2024-2026, committed somewhere between $80 billion and $140 billion of capital to the American AI buildout, drawing on Gulf sovereign wealth, Japanese megabank leverage, and his personal balance-sheet capacity. The capital has made the buildout possible at the timescale the American operators required. Without it, the cycle would have proceeded at a slower pace, with smaller data centres, smaller training runs, and smaller absolute capital deployment.

The Zainichi Korean outsider who started SoftBank in a rented Tokyo office corner in 1981, who survived hepatitis B against a five-year death sentence in 1983, who made the Yahoo! bet in 1995 and the Alibaba bet in 2000, who lost 97% of his personal wealth in the dot-com crash and clawed back, who acquired Arm in 2016 and survived the WeWork collapse in 2019, is — at 68 — the man underwriting the American AI cycle. The buildout is in progress. The outcome is unresolved.


---

# Chapter 23: The Gulf Sovereign-Wealth Layer — Tahnoun bin Zayed, Mohammed bin Salman, and the Capital Behind the Buildout

The preceding chapter dossiered Masayoshi Son, the bridge through which Gulf sovereign-wealth capital has reached the American AI buildout. The evidence covers the source.

The Gulf sovereign-wealth layer is, by May 2026, the single largest pool of non-American capital deployed into the contemporary American AI cycle. Through four principal vehicles — **Abu Dhabi's MGX**, **Mubadala**, and **ADIA**; **Saudi Arabia's PIF**; **Qatar's QIA** — and through the personal-investment vehicles of the senior Gulf principals, the layer has deployed somewhere between $150 billion and $250 billion into American AI laboratories, data-centre infrastructure, defence-tech firms, and the platform monopolies the book has been documenting. The exact figure is opaque because the vehicles do not file Western-style disclosures and the deals are increasingly structured as private commercial-confidentiality arrangements. What is publicly documented establishes the order of magnitude.

The two senior principals on whom this chapter focuses are:

- **Sheikh Tahnoun bin Zayed Al Nahyan** (born 4 December 1968) — Deputy Ruler of Abu Dhabi; National Security Advisor of the United Arab Emirates; Chairman of the Abu Dhabi Investment Authority (ADIA, $790 billion AUM); Chairman of the International Holding Company (IHC); Chairman of G42; Chairman of MGX; brother of UAE President Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan. Press estimates of the asset base he personally chairs or controls: between $1.4 trillion and $1.5 trillion.

- **Mohammed bin Salman Al Saud (MBS)** (born 31 August 1985) — Crown Prince of Saudi Arabia, Prime Minister, the country's de facto ruler since 2015; Chairman of PIF ($941 billion AUM as of late 2024, target above $2 trillion by 2030); Chairman of HUMAIN, the sovereign Saudi AI company launched May 2025.

The two men are, on the record, the principal non-American individuals whose decisions have determined the magnitude of capital flow into the American AI buildout from 2016 onward. Their respective wealth-fund vehicles funded the SoftBank Vision Fund; their respective AI-investment vehicles co-equity Stargate; their respective national champions (G42, HUMAIN) are the Gulf-aligned counterparts to the American AI laboratories the book has dossiered. The capital they direct is, in scale, the largest single block of non-Western capital in the contemporary global financial system.

The evidence covers them and the structures they chair. It is the final node in the network the book has been mapping.

---

## Part A: Sheikh Tahnoun bin Zayed Al Nahyan and the Abu Dhabi vehicles

## I. Origins, family, and the Al Nahyan formation

Sheikh Tahnoun bin Zayed Al Nahyan was born **4 December 1968 in Al Ain, Abu Dhabi**. He is the son of **Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan**, the founder of the United Arab Emirates (ruled Abu Dhabi 1966-2004; founding President of the UAE 1971-2004). Zayed had nineteen sons by multiple wives, of whom Tahnoun is one. His full brothers — the six sons of Zayed by **Sheikha Fatima bint Mubarak Al Ketbi** (Zayed's principal wife and the senior matriarch of the contemporary Al Nahyan family) — are the **Bani Fatima** core of the contemporary Al Nahyan ruling structure:

- **Mohamed bin Zayed (MBZ)** — President of the UAE since May 2022; Crown Prince of Abu Dhabi 2004-2022; eldest of the Bani Fatima brothers.
- **Hamdan bin Zayed** — Deputy Prime Minister of the UAE.
- **Hazza bin Zayed** — National Security Advisor 2006-2016 (Tahnoun's predecessor in that role).
- **Tahnoun bin Zayed** — the subject of this chapter.
- **Mansour bin Zayed** — Vice President and Deputy Prime Minister; owner of Manchester City Football Club; Chairman of Mubadala until 2022.
- **Abdullah bin Zayed** — Minister of Foreign Affairs.

The Bani Fatima brothers control the contemporary UAE state. They divide responsibility — MBZ rules; Mansour holds the headline international assets (Manchester City, real estate); Tahnoun holds the security, intelligence, and contemporary technology portfolio; the others hold ministerial and diplomatic roles. The division has been operationally stable since Zayed's death in 2004 and Khalifa bin Zayed's succession (Khalifa, an older half-brother through a different mother, ruled until his 2022 death). The contemporary Al Nahyan structure is the consolidated rule of the six Bani Fatima brothers under MBZ's leadership.

Tahnoun's personal formation followed the Al Nahyan template for a prince trained for the intelligence and security portfolio rather than the political-ceremonial one. He was educated privately in Abu Dhabi and at British institutions; he speaks Arabic, English, and (per recent press coverage) reportedly Mandarin. He has an eye condition that requires him to wear sunglasses indoors and out — the visible personal trait that has become his press signature. He is married to **Sheikha Salama bint Mubarak Al Suwaidi** with whom he has multiple children whose names are not on the international public record.

Tahnoun's personal interests are documented: he is a serious **Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu** practitioner (he has trained personally with Mark Zuckerberg, who shares the obsession); he funds the **UAE Team Emirates** professional cycling team (which has won multiple Tour de France titles with rider Tadej Pogačar); he is reportedly obsessed with the strategy video game *Age of Empires*, to the point of having hired an employee of the game's developer to train him in tactics; he funded the development of the chess computer **Hydra** in the early 2000s; he is reportedly pursuing **personal longevity research** with the stated goal of living to 150. The personal interests have become operational filters: those who wish to do business with him are reportedly expected to engage with one or more of the hobby categories.

## II. The intelligence and security trajectory, 2006-2016

Tahnoun entered the senior UAE security apparatus in the early 2000s. He served as **Deputy National Security Advisor** under his brother Hazza from 2006. The Deputy role gave him operational control over the contemporary UAE foreign-intelligence and cyber-operations apparatus across the years in which the UAE built out its present surveillance capability.

The principal documented operations of the Tahnoun-supervised UAE intelligence apparatus across the 2006-2020 period:

**Project Raven (2014-2019).** A covert UAE cyber-operations programme that recruited former US National Security Agency contractors to spy on the UAE's domestic and international political targets. The targets included Emirati dissidents, journalists, foreign government officials, and — per the 2019 *Reuters* investigation by Christopher Bing and Joel Schectman — at least one American citizen. The programme was conducted from a Tahnoun-affiliated Abu Dhabi villa. In September 2021 three former US intelligence-community contractors who had worked on Raven (Marc Baier, Ryan Adams, Daniel Gericke) entered a deferred prosecution agreement with the US Department of Justice and paid a combined $1.685 million fine for the unlicensed export of US cyber-intelligence services. Raven was UAE-funded American-trained-intelligence work targeting Americans on American soil; the US prosecution was unprecedented in its scope.

**ToTok (2019).** The UAE-developed messaging app, distributed through the Apple App Store and Google Play, that US intelligence officials told the *New York Times* in December 2019 was a UAE government surveillance tool used to monitor every conversation, movement, contact, and audio recording of its users. The app was removed from the Apple and Google stores after the *NYT* reporting. The development was, per US intelligence sources, supervised by the Tahnoun-controlled UAE intelligence apparatus.

**The Qatar smear campaign, 2017-2021.** The documented UAE-directed lobbying-and-disinformation operation against Qatar across the period of the Saudi-UAE-led Gulf blockade of Qatar (June 2017 - January 2021). The campaign included contracts with US lobbying firms, US public-relations firms, and contracted academic-and-policy work in Washington. The 2023 Abu Dhabi Secrets leak documented the campaign's structure and identified Tahnoun's office as one of the senior internal sponsors.

**The CIA carve-out.** Per public reporting and US intelligence-community sources, the CIA's documented signals-intelligence operations across the Middle East have, for the period of Tahnoun's leadership, exempted the UAE from the kind of NSA monitoring applied to other Gulf states. The exemption is the publicly inferred consequence of the close UAE-CIA cooperation Tahnoun has cultivated, including the parallel UAE participation in US-led counter-terror operations across Yemen, Libya, and the broader region.

In **February 2016** Tahnoun was promoted from Deputy to **National Security Advisor** of the UAE. He has held the position since.

## III. The Tahnoun economic empire, 2016-2026

The 2016-2026 period is the build-out of Tahnoun's commercial-investment empire alongside the security role. The principal vehicles he chairs as of May 2026:

**G42** (founded 2018). The Abu Dhabi AI company; CEO Peng Xiao (former MicroStrategy CTO). G42 operates across cloud infrastructure, AI applications, biotechnology (the Sanad cloud and Inception model lines), and contemporary surveillance-and-cyber capabilities. The company has been the principal vehicle by which the UAE has built domestic AI capability and the principal node for the UAE-China-versus-UAE-US strategic-alignment question that has dominated 2020-2025 UAE technology policy.

The G42 China question was the central diplomatic crisis of the company's 2020-2024 period. G42 had close partnerships with Huawei and other Chinese technology firms; senior Chinese executives held operational positions inside G42; G42's Chinese-aligned 42X investment fund had deployed capital in Chinese AI firms. The US position, articulated by senior Biden-administration national-security figures, was that G42's continued access to advanced US chips (Nvidia H100s and successors) required full G42 divestment from China. In early 2024 G42 removed all Huawei equipment from its infrastructure and severed the China commercial partnerships. In April 2024 **Microsoft announced a $1.5 billion investment in G42** for a minority stake; **Microsoft President Brad Smith** joined the G42 board. The deal was the realignment of G42 from the Chinese to the American AI infrastructure orbit. The realignment unblocked G42's access to advanced US chips; G42 acquired Nvidia H100s in late 2024 after the temporary US export ban was lifted.

**MGX** (launched March 2024). The Abu Dhabi technology-investment company Tahnoun chairs. Backed by Mubadala (the senior Abu Dhabi sovereign-wealth fund) and G42, MGX was launched with the stated mandate to accelerate AI and advanced-technology adoption in the UAE and globally. The MGX leadership: Tahnoun as Chairman; **Ahmed Yahia Al Idrissi** as Managing Director and CEO; senior board members including **Martin Edelman** (G42 General Counsel, with extensive sports-and-media connections including City Football Group); **Peng Xiao** (G42 CEO); **Khaldoon Khalifa Al Mubarak** (Mubadala CEO).

MGX's stated investment thesis: **70-80% of capital deployed into US AI investments**, the remainder into UAE-and-allied-country investments. The 2024-2026 MGX deployment record:

- **March 2024.** MGX, BlackRock, Microsoft, and Global Infrastructure Partners (now part of BlackRock) launch the **Global AI Infrastructure Investment Partnership (GAIIP)** with a target of $30 billion in initial investment scaling to $100 billion. The partnership's stated purpose: AI data centres and energy infrastructure, primarily in the US. The deal is structured to give MGX co-equity positions in US AI data-centre buildouts.
- **2024.** MGX co-leads the $6 billion **xAI** funding round (Elon Musk's AI company; Chapter 12).
- **December 2024.** MGX participates in the **OpenAI** $6.6 billion round at a $157 billion valuation.
- **January 2025.** MGX is named as one of the four initial equity investors in **Stargate LLC** alongside SoftBank, OpenAI, and Oracle. The MGX commitment is the senior Gulf-sovereign-wealth participation in the Stargate buildout.
- **March 2025.** MGX invests **$2 billion** in **Binance**, the cryptocurrency exchange that pleaded guilty in November 2023 to violations of US anti-money-laundering law and whose founder Changpeng Zhao served a four-month US federal prison sentence. The MGX investment was, per reporting, in part the financing leg of a broader Al Nahyan family lobbying effort to secure a Trump-administration pardon for CZ. The pardon followed in late 2025.
- **Ongoing 2025.** MGX has participated in funding rounds for **Anthropic**, **Cohere**, **Mistral**, **Hugging Face**, and the senior contemporary AI-laboratory cap tables. The exact MGX commitment per laboratory is not publicly disclosed across all positions.

**Lunate** (founded 2023). The Tahnoun-controlled asset-management vehicle, established under IHC. Lunate manages the former G42 China-focused 42X fund and serves as the operational asset-management arm for Tahnoun's broader portfolio. In 2024 Lunate committed **$1.5 billion** to **Affinity Partners**, the private-equity firm of **Jared Kushner** (Trump's son-in-law). The Kushner-Tahnoun financial relationship is Gulf-sovereign-wealth capital flowing directly to a Trump-family principal across the 2020-2026 period; the flow continued and expanded through both the Biden and the second Trump administrations.

**International Holding Company (IHC).** The Abu Dhabi-listed holding company Tahnoun chairs; market cap fluctuating between $200 billion and $240 billion across 2024-2025. IHC operates across construction, agriculture, energy, healthcare, and financial services. Its subsidiary **International Resources Holding (IRH)** acquired Zambia's **Mopani copper mine** in 2024 under chief global strategist **Sibtein Alibhai**; subsequent UN reporting alleged that Alibhai and IRH had met with Congolese gold smugglers, raising concerns that IHC's African-mining strategy was being executed in part through illicit gold-flow channels. The reporting is contested by IHC.

**First Abu Dhabi Bank.** Tahnoun chairs; the largest UAE commercial bank by assets.

**Royal Group.** Tahnoun's private conglomerate, holding diversified Abu Dhabi commercial interests; not publicly listed.

**Abu Dhabi Investment Authority (ADIA).** In 2023 Tahnoun was named Chairman of ADIA, the UAE's senior sovereign-wealth fund with **$790 billion AUM**. ADIA had historically been a passive global-equity-and-fixed-income investor; under Tahnoun's chairmanship from 2023 it has been repositioned toward larger direct-equity positions in strategic sectors, including AI and energy infrastructure.

**Abu Dhabi Developmental Holding Company (ADQ).** Tahnoun chairs; UAE state holding company worth $110 billion. ADQ operates across food, healthcare, utilities, and logistics; it is the senior UAE state-investment vehicle for domestic infrastructure development.

**The Aryam Investment 1 personal vehicle.** Tahnoun's documented personal investment company. The vehicle's holdings include the **M2.com** cryptocurrency exchange (Tahnoun is the publicly disclosed owner) and additional bitcoin-mining and digital-asset positions.

The combined Tahnoun-chaired or Tahnoun-controlled asset base, per the multiple 2024-2025 *Wall Street Journal* and *Bloomberg* analyses, sits between **$1.3 trillion and $1.5 trillion**. The figure is, for any single individual outside the senior Communist Party of China and the senior Gulf ruling families, without parallel in the contemporary global economy. The figure exceeds the AUM of all but the largest American asset managers (BlackRock at $11.5 trillion, Vanguard at $9 trillion, State Street at $4.5 trillion); it is, for any single individual operating without the institutional structure of a public asset manager, the largest concentrated capital base on the planet.

## IV. The 2025-2026 American political integration

Tahnoun's American political integration accelerated through the 2024-2026 period. The principal moves on the evidence:

**Summer 2024.** Tahnoun's US tour. Meetings with **Elon Musk** (at the Giga Texas factory; Tahnoun appeared in Western jeans and t-shirt, departing from his usual Emirati robes), **Mark Zuckerberg** (joint BJJ training session; Zuckerberg has visited the Abu Dhabi royal compound subsequently), **Bill Gates** (philanthropic-and-AI discussions), **Satya Nadella** (the Microsoft-G42 follow-up after the April 2024 investment), **Jeff Bezos** (Amazon-and-Anthropic discussions). Concurrent meetings with the Biden administration: **Jake Sullivan** (then National Security Advisor), **Gina Raimondo** (Commerce Secretary), and Biden himself in a White House meeting.

**January 2025.** MGX as Stargate co-investor at the White House announcement. Tahnoun himself was not present at the announcement; MGX was represented at the principal level by Ahmed Yahia Al Idrissi.

**27 January 2025.** UAE President MBZ meets Commerce Secretary Gina Raimondo in Abu Dhabi to discuss MGX's $30 billion (scaling to $100 billion) US AI infrastructure partnership. Tahnoun attends as Deputy Ruler. The meeting was reported by *Gulf Business* and confirmed by the UAE state news service.

**18 March 2025.** Tahnoun hosted at the White House by President Trump for a banquet dinner. The dinner included meetings with the **CIA Director**, the **Treasury Secretary** (Bessent), the **Commerce Secretary** (Lutnick), and senior US technology CEOs including **Jeff Bezos**. The dinner was, in *Wall Street Journal* coverage, the documented public moment at which Tahnoun was formally inducted into the second Trump administration's senior foreign-relations circle.

**Throughout 2025.** Tahnoun's senior commercial-and-policy meetings with Trump-administration figures continued. The Kushner-Affinity-Lunate relationship continued. The Trump-MBZ direct relationship, anchored in part by the Tahnoun bridge, was reported across multiple US foreign-policy outlets as the most consequential US-Gulf relationship of the second Trump administration.

The 2025-2026 American integration is documented operational realignment of Abu Dhabi's senior sovereign-wealth and AI-investment apparatus toward the US technology cycle. The China alignment that had characterised G42 through 2023 has been broken; the US alignment has replaced it. Tahnoun is, by May 2026, one of the senior non-American principals in the contemporary American AI-capital-deployment cycle and one of the senior personal financial sponsors of the Trump-family financial-and-political infrastructure.
