## Part B: Mohammed bin Salman and the Saudi vehicles

## V. Origins and the MBS consolidation

**Mohammed bin Salman bin Abdulaziz Al Saud** was born **31 August 1985 in Jeddah**, the eldest of six sons of **King Salman** by his third wife **Fahda bint Falah Al Hithlain Al Ajmi**. He was educated at King Saud University (BA Law, 2007) and was not, in the immediate post-graduation period, considered the likely succession candidate. The standard royal-line analysis at the time identified the senior Al Saud princes — Muqrin bin Abdulaziz, Mohammed bin Nayef — as the next-generation rulers. MBS's rise from junior royal to de facto ruler is one of the documented political consolidations of the post-2010 Middle East.

**January 2015.** King Abdullah dies; Salman ascends. MBS, at 29, is appointed Minister of Defence and Chairman of the Council of Economic and Development Affairs. The Council brings PIF under his direct chairmanship.

**April 2015.** Salman appoints MBS as Deputy Crown Prince, displacing the previous succession line. MBS launches the Saudi-led intervention in Yemen the same month — the active signal of his consolidated control over Saudi foreign policy.

**June 2017.** Salman removes Mohammed bin Nayef from the Crown Prince position and appoints MBS as Crown Prince. The June 2017 transfer is conducted, per multiple subsequent reports, under conditions that included MBN's house arrest at the King's palace; MBN was reportedly pressured to formally consent to the transfer.

**November 2017.** The Ritz-Carlton purge. MBS orders the detention of over 200 senior Saudi princes, ministers, and business figures at the Ritz-Carlton hotel in Riyadh. The official framing is anti-corruption; the working outcome is the consolidation of MBS's personal control over the senior Saudi business class, including the forced transfer of $100 billion-plus in assets from the detained figures to the Saudi state. The detainees included **Prince Alwaleed bin Talal** (the Citigroup and Twitter investor), **Bakr bin Laden** (Saudi Binladin Group), and dozens of other senior figures. The Ritz purge was the consolidation of MBS's economic-political control over the Saudi state.

**2 October 2018.** The murder of **Jamal Khashoggi** at the Saudi consulate in Istanbul. Khashoggi, a Saudi-American journalist and *Washington Post* columnist who had been critical of MBS, was killed by a Saudi state hit team. The CIA's subsequent classified assessment, made public in February 2021 under the Biden administration, concluded that MBS had personally approved the operation. MBS denied personal involvement but conceded "ultimate responsibility" as the country's leader. The Khashoggi murder produced temporary American political distance from MBS through the late Trump first term and the Biden administration; the relationship was restored across the 2022-2024 period as the American principals — the SoftBank-Vision-Fund LPs, the senior American technology executives, and the senior American political figures — chose continued commercial engagement with MBS over the political-and-moral consequences of the murder.

**March 2022.** King Salman's health declines further; MBS becomes acting head of state in practice. In **September 2022** he is formally appointed Prime Minister (a role traditionally held by the King), confirming his operational rule.

By May 2026, MBS — at 40 years old — is the de facto and increasingly de jure ruler of Saudi Arabia. King Salman is 90 years old and increasingly absent from operational decisions. The succession question — when MBS becomes formal King — is open; the reality is settled.

## VI. PIF — the principal Saudi vehicle

**The Public Investment Fund (PIF)** is the senior Saudi sovereign-wealth fund. Founded by Royal Decree in 1971 under King Faisal, the fund operated as a passive holding vehicle for Saudi state equity stakes through 2015. The March 2015 restructuring brought PIF under MBS's direct chairmanship and began its transformation into a globally active investment institution.

**The MBS-era PIF trajectory:**
- **2015.** AUM around $150 billion; 50 employees; principally a domestic Saudi holding vehicle.
- **2017.** First Vision Fund commitment to SoftBank: **$45 billion**, of which $35 billion in preference shares paying 7% annually.
- **2018.** AUM around $270 billion. PIF expands US-equity public-market positions across Uber (pre-IPO and IPO), Tesla (a 5% direct position taken before the August 2018 Musk "funding secured" tweet, subsequently sold down), Lucid Motors (lead investor since 2018; PIF holds majority control as of May 2026).
- **2019-2020.** PIF expands into entertainment and sports. **LIV Golf** launches in 2021 ($300 million initial commitment, escalating to multiple billions by 2026 including the 2023 PGA Tour merger announcement). **Newcastle United** takeover (April 2020 agreement; closed October 2021 after Premier League approval; PIF holds 85% of the club). **Saudi Arabia awarded the 2034 FIFA World Cup hosting rights** in December 2024.
- **2020.** $20 billion commitment to **Blackstone**'s infrastructure fund (the principal Blackstone-PIF partnership).
- **2022.** Saudi Aramco transfers a 4% stake to PIF (worth $80 billion at the time). Second 4% transfer in 2023 brings PIF's Aramco stake to 8% (worth $160 billion at peak).
- **2023.** AUM crosses $700 billion. PIF announces $30 billion commitment to Lucid Motors over 2021-2025 to ensure the company's solvency through its growth period.
- **June 2023.** PIF-funded LIV Golf and the PGA Tour announce a framework agreement to merge their commercial businesses into a new for-profit company. The PGA Tour position on Saudi sportswashing — under which the Tour had refused to allow PIF investment for the preceding two years — was reversed entirely. The merger negotiations remain ongoing through 2025-2026.
- **2024.** AUM reaches $913 billion. PIF deploys more capital in the first half of 2024 than all other state-owned investors globally combined.
- **May 2025.** MBS launches **HUMAIN**, the PIF-owned sovereign AI company, with MBS himself as Chairman. HUMAIN's stated scope: data centres, cloud infrastructure, foundation models, applications, and one of the world's most advanced Arabic-language large language models. The launch is the Saudi parallel to G42 and to the broader Gulf-AI-sovereignty initiative.
- **2025-2026.** PIF-HUMAIN announces partnerships with **Nvidia** (for AI chip access), **AMD**, **Cisco**, and the senior American cloud-infrastructure providers. The Saudi National Data Center buildout — the HUMAIN flagship — is in construction across multiple Saudi sites.
- **April 2026.** PIF announces a 2026-2030 strategy emphasising "sustained value creation" rather than the prior period's "rapid growth and acceleration." The shift reflects the financial-and-political constraints PIF has accumulated through the 2021-2025 period, including the World Expo 2030 commitment, the 2034 World Cup commitment, the NEOM giga-project capital overruns, and the continued unprofitable Lucid Motors position.

**Current PIF AUM (May 2026):** Per the April 2026 PIF strategic update, $941 billion, with a continuing target of exceeding $2 trillion by 2030.

**Current PIF US-equity disclosed holdings (late 2025):** Lucid Group (majority), Electronic Arts, Uber Technologies, Allurion Technologies, Clarivate Corp. Earlier 2025 positions in Apple, ASML, Arm Holdings, UnitedHealth, Eli Lilly, and Merck were sold down through the year as part of a portfolio reallocation. The reallocation, per *House of Saud* coverage, reflects a strategic shift from public-equity positions to direct private investments in Saudi domestic giga-projects and HUMAIN.

## VII. The MBS political activity and the Kushner relationship

MBS's political-financial relationships with the contemporary American political principals are documented through multiple channels:

**The Kushner-Affinity relationship.** **Jared Kushner**, son-in-law of President Trump and former senior White House advisor (2017-2021), founded **Affinity Partners** in 2021 after leaving the White House. PIF committed **$2 billion** to Affinity's debut fund in 2022 — the largest single LP commitment Affinity received. The commitment proceeded despite PIF's own internal investment-screening committee recommending against the investment on the basis of Kushner's lack of private-equity track record. The internal-committee recommendation was overridden at the principal level. The Affinity-PIF relationship has been investigated by US Senate Finance Committee Chair Ron Wyden across 2022-2024.

**The Affinity-Israel relationship.** In 2022-2023 Affinity invested in two Israeli startups; the investments were the first known occasion in which Saudi PIF money flowed to Israeli companies. Kushner reportedly facilitated the investments with MBS's approval. The investments were the financial-commercial groundwork for the subsequent (and now stalled) Saudi-Israeli normalisation negotiations.

**The Trump second administration alignment.** MBS hosted Trump in Riyadh for Trump's first foreign visit of the second term (May 2025). The visit was the public signal of the renewed senior US-Saudi commercial-and-political alignment. Trump's subsequent foreign-policy posture toward Iran, toward the Yemen war, and toward the broader Middle East has been closely aligned with MBS's stated priorities.

**The American technology integration.** MBS has personally hosted, in Riyadh and at the Future Investment Initiative (the annual "Davos in the Desert" conference PIF hosts), the senior contemporary American technology executives: Altman, Musk, Bezos, Zuckerberg, Pichai, Karp, the Amodeis, Hoffman. The FII has, since 2017, been the principal annual convening at which Saudi sovereign-wealth capital, American technology principals, and senior American political figures conduct the coordination of their commercial relationships.

**The human-rights record.** Across the 2017-2026 period MBS's domestic political record has included: the Ritz-Carlton purge (November 2017); the assassination of Jamal Khashoggi (October 2018); the war in Yemen (2015-present; documented Saudi-led civilian casualties in the hundreds of thousands); the continued detention of women's-rights activists who had campaigned for the right to drive (which MBS then granted in 2018); the imprisonment of religious clerics and intellectual dissidents; the abduction of dual-national activists from neighbouring countries; and the suppression of the country's residual civil-society organisations. Human Rights Watch's November 2024 report *The Man Who Bought the World* documents the connection between PIF's international investments and the broader Saudi human-rights apparatus: the investments function as soft-power instruments that buy political silence on the abuses. The June 2023 PIF-PGA Tour framework agreement included a "non-disparagement clause" preventing the PGA from criticising Saudi Arabia's human-rights record — the clearest documented case of the soft-power mechanism in operation.

---

## Part C: The other Gulf vehicles

Beyond the principal Tahnoun- and MBS-chaired vehicles, three other Gulf sovereign-wealth funds matter for the network the book has been documenting:

**Mubadala Investment Company (Abu Dhabi)** — AUM $330 billion (May 2026 reporting); CEO **Khaldoon Khalifa Al Mubarak**; Chairman until 2022 was Sheikh Mansour bin Zayed (Tahnoun's brother). Mubadala was the original Abu Dhabi vehicle backing the Vision Fund 1 ($15 billion commitment) and the senior LP across multiple American venture-and-private-equity positions. It has, through 2024-2026, been the institutional infrastructure under which MGX operates. The Khaldoon Al Mubarak-Khaldoon connection: Khaldoon is also Chairman of **City Football Group** (Manchester City and its globally distributed sister clubs), one of the senior figures at JP Morgan's International Council, and a board member at ADNOC, L'IMAD, and G42. He is, in functional terms, MBZ's senior commercial-and-policy operator across the Abu Dhabi-international interface.

**Qatar Investment Authority (QIA)** — AUM $510 billion (2025 estimate); Chairman **HE Sheikh Mohammed bin Abdulrahman Al Thani** (also Qatar's Prime Minister and Foreign Minister). QIA's senior international positions include Volkswagen, Glencore, Sainsbury's (the UK retailer), Heathrow Airport, Credit Suisse (legacy position now in UBS), Harrods, and a long list of senior Western infrastructure-and-financial-services positions. QIA was less central to the AI buildout than MGX or PIF through 2024 but has, across 2025, increased its position in OpenAI, in Anthropic, and in the senior American AI cloud infrastructure. The senior Qatari-American political relationship — anchored in the **Al Udeid Air Base** (the US Central Command forward-operating headquarters, located in Qatar) — has been one of the senior US Middle Eastern alliances through the post-2003 period.

**Kuwait Investment Authority (KIA)** — AUM $980 billion; less politically directed than PIF or ADIA; principally a passive global-equity investor with senior positions across the US blue-chip equity universe. KIA's role in the AI buildout has been smaller than the Abu Dhabi or Saudi vehicles; its operational integration with the network has been limited.

The four senior Gulf vehicles (PIF, ADIA, Mubadala/MGX, QIA) plus KIA together control between **$3.2 trillion and $3.5 trillion** in AUM as of May 2026. The figure is, in scale, the second-largest concentrated capital pool in the global financial system after the senior American asset-management oligopoly (BlackRock-Vanguard-State Street at a combined $25 trillion).

---

## VIII. The architecture: how the Gulf layer reaches the American buildout

The capital-flow architecture by which the Gulf layer reaches the American AI buildout, summarised:

1. **Direct equity in American AI laboratories.** MGX in OpenAI, xAI, Anthropic. PIF in HUMAIN's American partnerships. QIA in late-2025 OpenAI and Anthropic rounds. Direct equity positions give the Gulf principals cap-table representation, information-rights access, and the active ability to participate in subsequent funding rounds.

2. **The Stargate co-equity structure.** MGX as one of four initial Stargate equity investors. Through Stargate, the Gulf capital is positioned at the data-centre-infrastructure layer rather than at the model-laboratory layer. The infrastructure layer captures the long-duration commercial value: data centres, energy, cooling, real estate, the physical substrate of the AI buildout.

3. **The SoftBank bridge.** PIF and Mubadala as the principal Vision Fund 1 LPs (a combined $60 billion). The bridge has been less central since 2019 — Vision Fund 2 had reduced Gulf participation — but it remains the documented historical predicate of the contemporary direct Gulf-US relationship.

4. **The BlackRock infrastructure-partnership channel.** The MGX-BlackRock-Microsoft-Global-Infrastructure-Partners $30 billion (scaling to $100 billion) Global AI Infrastructure Investment Partnership announced March 2024. The partnership operates as a co-investment platform: BlackRock provides the institutional asset-management infrastructure; MGX provides the principal Gulf-sovereign capital; Microsoft provides the commercial AI-customer anchor; GIP provides the data-centre-development expertise. The four-way structure is the documented blueprint for the contemporary Gulf-sovereign-American-AI-infrastructure-partnership model.

5. **The Kushner-Affinity channel.** PIF's $2 billion commitment to Affinity Partners. Lunate's $1.5 billion commitment to Affinity. The combined Gulf-sovereign-wealth flow to a single Trump-family principal totals $3.5 billion across 2022-2025. The flow is, in scale, without precedent in US-Gulf political-financial history.

6. **The personal-investment channel.** MBS, Tahnoun, MBZ, and the senior princes have made personal investments in American technology companies through opaque private-investment vehicles. The exact scale is not publicly disclosed. The reported figures are in the tens of billions of dollars across the senior royal family principals.

7. **The sportswashing-and-philanthropic channels.** LIV Golf, Newcastle United, the Saudi-PGA merger, the 2034 World Cup. Manchester City and the broader City Football Group network. The Khaldoon-Al-Mubarak-chaired JP Morgan International Council. The Tahnoun-funded UAE Team Emirates cycling team. The various foundations and academic partnerships (the Khalifa University AI partnerships with MIT and Stanford; the King Abdullah University of Science and Technology partnerships with multiple American research universities). The sport-and-philanthropic channels produce political legitimacy, soft-power influence, and access to American political and cultural elites that the direct-investment channels alone could not deliver.

The combined architecture has produced the reality the book has been describing: the contemporary American AI buildout is, in its capital base, heavily Gulf-sovereign-wealth-financed. Without the Gulf layer, the buildout could not have proceeded at the timescale the American operators required. The Gulf layer has, in exchange for the capital, received cap-table positions, infrastructure co-equity, political access at the senior level of both major American parties, the senior cultural-and-philanthropic positioning that produces ongoing soft-power influence, and the working integration with the American technology-industry that the Gulf states have positioned as the central plank of their post-oil economic diversification.

## IX. The active threat

The Gulf sovereign-wealth layer is a non-democratic, non-Western, non-American capital pool now positioned at the centre of the contemporary American AI infrastructure. The active threat is not the Gulf principals themselves — they are operationally rational actors pursuing the post-oil diversification their own state-economic plans require — but the integration they have achieved with the American political-and-technology elite.

The principal active-threat components, named:

1. **The capture of US AI capital flow by foreign authoritarian states.** The senior contemporary American AI laboratories' cap tables now include Saudi, Emirati, and Qatari sovereign-wealth positions at the level of single-digit-percent ownership. The information-rights access these positions confer — observer seats, financial reporting access, periodic management briefings — gives the Gulf principals operational visibility into the development trajectory of frontier AI capability that no public-policy mechanism oversees.

2. **The political-influence purchasing power.** The Kushner-Affinity-PIF-Lunate flow is foreign sovereign capital reaching a senior American presidential-family principal. The scale ($3.5 billion combined) is, on the public record, without parallel. The political consequences are reflected in the second Trump administration's documented Middle East policy posture: close alignment with Saudi-Emirati priorities on Iran, on the Yemen war (despite the documented civilian-casualty record), on the Gaza war (despite the documented civilian-casualty record), and on the broader regional realignment.

3. **The cultural-soft-power channel.** The sportswashing-and-philanthropic channels have, in the post-2017 period, neutralised the senior Western cultural-political opposition to the senior Gulf-state human-rights record. The PGA-LIV merger, the Newcastle takeover, the 2034 World Cup, the City Football Group expansion — these are the documented mechanisms by which Saudi and Emirati state behaviour has been removed from Western political discussion.

4. **The AI-sovereignty bid.** G42 and HUMAIN are the senior Gulf-state efforts to build domestic AI capability. The capability, once built, will not be subject to American export controls or to American political-and-regulatory oversight. The Gulf states' explicit position is that they will deploy the capability across their domestic populations (G42's Arabic LLMs; HUMAIN's healthcare-and-public-services applications) and across their broader regional-influence operations (the Arabic-LLM and Arabic-language-translation infrastructure that will operate across the broader Arab world). The American Project Maven and IDF-equivalent capabilities are now being built inside the Gulf states themselves.

5. **The systemic-financial channel.** Tahnoun's $1.4-1.5 trillion and MBS's $941 billion are concentrated capital pools whose investment decisions are not subject to democratic oversight in their home countries and whose deployment trajectories have, across 2024-2026, moved together. If the Gulf principals decided, simultaneously, to withdraw capital from the American AI-and-technology cycle, the resulting cascade would be of systemic financial significance. The decision-making process that would produce such a withdrawal is opaque to the markets and to the American political system.

## X. The verdict

The Gulf sovereign-wealth layer is, by May 2026, the documented foreign-capital base of the contemporary American AI buildout. Tahnoun bin Zayed and Mohammed bin Salman — together with their senior commercial operators (Khaldoon Al Mubarak at Mubadala; Yasir Al-Rumayyan at PIF; Ahmed Yahia Al Idrissi at MGX; Peng Xiao at G42) — are the principal non-American individuals whose capital decisions have determined the shape of the cycle.

They are the financing source, the political-economic predicate of the American buildout, and the structural constraint that any future democratic regulatory response would face. Any attempt by a future American administration to constrain the American AI principals' commercial conduct will have to contend with the Gulf-capital positions on those principals' cap tables, the Gulf-political relationships that the Trump-family financial integration has established, and the Gulf-state AI capability that has now been built outside American jurisdiction.

The American AI buildout has been jointly executed by the American technology principals, the Gulf sovereign-wealth layer, the Japanese-corporate balance-sheet leverage (Chapter 22), and the American political-regulatory apparatus that has been purchased through the donor-and-philanthropic channels documented across Chapters 11-21. The contemporary AI capability is the joint product of this network. The populations being targeted by the deployed systems have not been parties to the network.

# Chapter: Google, Alphabet, DeepMind — Page, Brin, Pichai, Hassabis

In August 2024, a federal judge ruled that Google is a monopolist. In April 2025, a second federal judge ruled that Google unlawfully monopolised the ad-tech industry. Two adverse antitrust rulings within eight months. The company controls **89% of global search**, **71% of global mobile operating systems**, **65% of global web browsers**, and serves **2.7 billion YouTube monthly active users**. Its market capitalisation is **$2.8 trillion**. It is one of the four most valuable corporations on earth.

The senior figures of **Alphabet Inc.** — the corporate parent of Google, YouTube, Android, Chrome, Google Cloud, Waymo, and **Google DeepMind** — are **Larry Page** (co-founder, Class B controlling shareholder), **Sergey Brin** (co-founder, Class B controlling shareholder, returned to hands-on AI work since 2023), **Sundar Pichai** (CEO of Google since 2015, of Alphabet since December 2019), **Demis Hassabis** (co-founder and CEO of DeepMind, 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry), and **John Jumper** (Senior Research Director, 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry, co-developer of AlphaFold).

Eric Schmidt's role across the 2001-2018 period was covered in Chapter 18; this chapter cross-references rather than duplicates that record. The analysis focuses on the company's pre-Schmidt foundation, the post-Schmidt period, and the four biographical records the prior chapters did not establish.

Page and Brin's combined net worth reached **$520 billion** in November 2025 — driven by the Gemini 3 launch. Page individually reached #2 globally at $262 billion, briefly overtaking Bezos. The wealth appreciation has occurred as Brin returned to hands-on AI work and Page re-engaged. The two founders who walked away from day-to-day operations came back when the AI race made it worth their while.

## I. Larry Page — origins, education, and the Michigan-State-CS family

**Lawrence Edward Page** was born on **26 March 1973 in East Lansing, Michigan**. His family is one of the most computer-science-integrated in the American technology-industry capital class.

His father, **Carl Victor Page Sr.** (1938-1996), was a **computer science professor at Michigan State University** who held a **PhD in Computer Science from the University of Michigan (1965)** — one of the earliest American CS PhDs awarded. The Carl Page Sr. academic specialisation was in artificial intelligence and data extraction. His mother, **Gloria Page**, was Jewish and taught computer-programming instruction at Michigan State University. Larry Page's older brother, **Carl Victor Page Jr.**, subsequently co-founded the email-discussion-group company eGroups (which was sold to Yahoo in 2000 for $432 million); Carl Jr. died in 2014.

The Page household: academic-and-Jewish-mixed; computers and programming present from Larry's earliest childhood. The documented anecdotes: Larry's father gave him computer-science magazines and programming texts from an early age; Larry was reportedly the first child in his elementary school to submit a word-processed homework assignment. **This was the most computer-science-integrated family in the American billionaire class** — more so than the Schmidt family (Wilson Schmidt the Virginia Tech economist; Chapter 18), the Bezos family (Lawrence Gise the AEC director; Chapter 16), the Zuckerberg family (Edward Zuckerberg the Dobbs Ferry dentist; Chapter 17).

The captured-press framing of Page as a "self-taught Stanford genius" elides the documented family-network connection: Larry Page grew up in the household of two computer-science academics at one of the principal American computer-science research universities. The technical formation Page brought to the subsequent Stanford and Google work was foundational — the product of twenty years of family-level computer-science immersion before he entered college.

### Education

- **East Lansing Montessori School** (1975-1979) — the Montessori formation that Page and Brin have repeatedly cited as foundational to their intellectual orientation.
- **East Lansing High School**.
- **BS Computer Engineering, University of Michigan** (1995). While at Michigan, Page built an inkjet printer made of Lego bricks (the documented anecdote of his early-engineering creativity). Member of the Eta Kappa Nu honor society.
- **PhD program (incomplete), Stanford University Computer Science** (1995-1998) — supervised by **Terry Winograd**. The PhD was never formally completed; Page subsequently received honorary doctorates.

## II. Sergey Brin — origins, education, and the Soviet-Jewish emigration

**Sergey Mikhailovich Brin** was born on **21 August 1973 in Moscow, the Soviet Union**. His family-of-origin is Soviet-Jewish emigration in response to Soviet antisemitism — the documented family-of-origin pattern that has shaped his subsequent ideological and operational positioning.

His father, **Mikhail Brin** (born 1948), is a retired professor of mathematics at the **University of Maryland**. His mother, **Eugenia Brin** (1948-2024), was a researcher at **NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center**. Both parents graduated from Moscow State University.

The Brin family is **Russian-Jewish**. They lived in a three-room apartment in central Moscow shared with Sergey's paternal grandmother. The documented context of the family's emigration:

- In **1977**, Mikhail Brin attended a mathematics conference in Warsaw, Poland — his first major exposure to Western academic conditions.
- After observing Western academic conditions, Mikhail concluded that **Soviet antisemitism made the family's continued residence in the Soviet Union untenable**. Mikhail informed Eugenia: it was time for the family to emigrate.
- **September 1978**: the family formally applied for an exit visa. The consequence: **Mikhail was "promptly fired"** from his Soviet position; **Eugenia was required to leave her job**.
- For **eight months**, the family was without steady income, taking temporary jobs while waiting for the visa to be approved. There was documented uncertainty about whether the visa would be approved at all (many Soviet-Jewish refusenik applications were denied across the 1970s).
- **1979**: family emigrated to the United States via Vienna and Rome (the standard Soviet-Jewish-emigration route of the period). Initially settled in Maryland. Sergey was 6 years old.

**Sergey Brin's family is the most Soviet-Jewish-refugee-derived in the American billionaire class.** The Soviet antisemitism that produced the family's emigration is the historical context of Brin's subsequent distrust of authoritarian state power; Brin has been the most outspoken senior Silicon Valley executive on Russian, Chinese, and Iranian state authoritarianism — consistent with the family formation.

### Education

- **Paint Branch Montessori School** (the parallel Montessori formation to Page's).
- **Eleanor Roosevelt High School**.
- **BS Mathematics and Computer Science, University of Maryland, College Park** (1993, with honors).
- **PhD program (incomplete), Stanford University Computer Science** (1993-1998) — initially supervised by **Hector Garcia-Molina**. Sergey was a National Science Foundation Graduate Research Fellow during his Stanford period.

## III. The Page-Brin meeting and the Google founding (1995-1998)

The Page-and-Brin partnership originated in **March 1995** at a **Stanford CS PhD orientation weekend**. They argued vigorously about almost every topic during their first meeting; despite the disagreement they recognised complementary intellectual styles. (Brin: extroverted, gregarious, comfortable with confrontation; Page: introverted, considered, deliberate.) Their complementary leadership structure has been characterised as one of the most distinctive co-founder pairings in American technology history.

### BackRub and PageRank (1996-1997)

In **1996**, Page and Brin began collaborating on a research project initially called **"BackRub"** — a innovation in web-search ranking that used the link structure of the web as a ranking signal. The insight: the importance of a web page could be measured by the number and quality of other pages linking to it. The algorithmic formalisation became the **PageRank algorithm** (named after Page, not after the web-page concept).

The BackRub operation consumed a large fraction of Stanford's bandwidth across 1996-1997; the Stanford administration tolerated the bandwidth consumption because the project was producing research outputs. Page and Brin's 1998 paper **"The Anatomy of a Large-Scale Hypertextual Web Search Engine"** described BackRub's architecture and remains one of the most-cited papers in computer-science literature.

The PageRank patent was filed at Stanford and licensed to Google; Stanford received **1.8 million Google shares** in exchange for the patent licence. Stanford subsequently sold those shares for **$336 million in 2005** — one of the most operationally successful single university-technology-licensing transactions in contemporary American academic history.

### Google Inc. incorporated (4 September 1998)

- **September 1997**: domain google.com registered (misspelling of "googol" = 10^100).
- **4 September 1998**: **Google Inc. incorporated** in California. Initial capital **$100,000 from Andy Bechtolsheim** (Sun Microsystems co-founder) — one of the earliest seed-investment-in-a-startup events of the post-1995 Silicon Valley period; Bechtolsheim's check was famously written before Google had a bank account.
- Company operations from **Susan Wojcicki's garage in Menlo Park**. Wojcicki was a Stanford friend of Page's; she subsequently became Brin's sister-in-law (Brin married Anne Wojcicki, Susan's sister, in 2007). The Page-Wojcicki family-network integration produced the case of one of the early-Google founder-personal-network integrations: Susan Wojcicki subsequently joined Google as marketing manager in 1999 and rose to become **CEO of YouTube (2014-2023)** before her 2024 departure and death from cancer in August 2024.

### Initial venture-capital funding (1999)

In **1999**, Google raised **$25 million in Series A funding from Kleiner Perkins (John Doerr) and Sequoia Capital (Michael Moritz)** — the principal contemporary Silicon Valley venture-capital partnerships. The condition of the Series A funding was that Page and Brin agree to hire an "adult supervision" CEO; the negotiations took two years and produced the **March 2001 Eric Schmidt hire** (Chapter 18).

## IV. The Schmidt period (2001-2011) and the Google trajectory

The Eric Schmidt period at Google has been documented in Chapter 18. The principal events during the Schmidt-CEO period:

- **2000**: Google AdWords launched — the contextual-advertising-on-search system that became the foundational Google revenue model.
- **2001**: Eric Schmidt joined as CEO. The Schmidt-Brin-Page "triumvirate" operated Google through the period of its scale-up.
- **19 August 2004**: **Google IPO** at $85 per share. Closing first-day price $100.34. Market capitalisation at IPO: **$23 billion**.
- **August 2005**: **Android Inc. acquired for $50 million**.
- **October 2006**: **YouTube acquired for $1.65 billion in stock**.
- **April 2007**: **DoubleClick acquired for $3.1 billion**.
- **September 2008**: **Google Chrome browser launched** (Sundar Pichai's operational breakthrough).
- **April 2011**: Page reclaimed the CEO position from Schmidt; Schmidt transitioned to Executive Chairman.

The Google dual-class share structure: **Class A (one vote per share)** held by the public; **Class B (ten votes per share)** retained by Page, Brin, and Schmidt. **Class C (no voting rights)** subsequently introduced for stock-based compensation. The Class B structure produced founder voting control exceeding the founders' economic stakes; Page and Brin together control **51% of Alphabet's voting power** with much less than 51% of the economic equity.

## V. The 2014 DeepMind acquisition and the AI integration

The most operationally consequential single Google acquisition of the post-2010 period was the **January 2014 acquisition of DeepMind Technologies for $500 million** (some sources cite $400-650 million depending on currency conversion and earnouts).

### Why DeepMind mattered

DeepMind had been founded in **2010** in London by **Demis Hassabis, Shane Legg, and Mustafa Suleyman** with the stated mission to "**solve intelligence, and then use intelligence to solve everything else**" — the most ambitious mission statement of any contemporary AI laboratory.

The technical breakthrough that motivated the Google acquisition was the **December 2013 Deep Q-Network (DQN)** paper, in which DeepMind demonstrated that an AI system could be trained to play **Atari games at superhuman level by deep reinforcement learning** — without any human-game-specific training data. The DQN result was one of the foundational demonstrations of deep reinforcement learning at scale; it demonstrated that the techniques DeepMind had developed could be applied to the broader question of artificial general intelligence.

The Google acquisition of DeepMind was one of the largest AI-research-laboratory acquisitions in history. The negotiation reportedly involved a competing **Facebook offer** that DeepMind rejected; the DeepMind founders preferred Google's guarantee of operational independence and London-based-headquarters preservation.

### DeepMind's accomplishments under Google

DeepMind has produced one of the most significant AI-research-laboratory records in history:

- **March 2016**: **AlphaGo** defeated **Lee Sedol**, the world's top professional Go player, **4-1 in Seoul**. The AlphaGo victory was the most significant single AI-system-vs-human-expert competition since IBM's Deep Blue vs Kasparov (1997). The cultural impact: Go had been considered more complex than chess (the game-tree complexity is 10^170 vs chess's 10^120); the AlphaGo victory was widely characterised in the trade press as the demonstration that contemporary AI systems could exceed human performance on complex strategic-thinking tasks.
- **2017**: **AlphaGo Zero** — the successor system that learned Go from scratch by self-play, with no human-game training data. AlphaGo Zero defeated the original AlphaGo **100-0**.
- **2017**: **Alpha Zero** — the more general system that mastered chess, shogi, and Go from scratch.
- **2018**: **AlphaFold** entered the **13th Critical Assessment of Structure Prediction (CASP) competition** for protein-structure prediction and reached **60% accuracy** (vs ~40% for the previous best models).
- **2020**: **AlphaFold 2** reached **90% accuracy at CASP14** — comparable to experimental methods. The protein-folding problem, a 50-year grand challenge in biochemistry, was solved.
- **2021-2022**: AlphaFold 2 used to predict the structures of **200 million proteins** known to science. The **AlphaFold Protein Structure Database** released openly and freely available; used by **2 million researchers from 190 countries** by October 2024.
- **2024**: **Demis Hassabis and John Jumper jointly awarded the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry** for protein-structure prediction. The Nobel Prize was the first to be awarded for AI-based scientific research. The parallel **2024 Nobel Prize in Physics** was awarded to **Geoffrey Hinton and John Hopfield** the day before, also for foundational AI work — the 2024 Nobel Prizes recognised AI as a scientific discipline.

**DeepMind has produced the most significant AI-research record of the post-2010 period.** The contrast with OpenAI: while OpenAI has produced the commercially-deployed large-language-model products (GPT-2, GPT-3, GPT-4, GPT-5), DeepMind has produced the scientifically-validated breakthroughs (AlphaGo, AlphaFold) that have advanced the knowledge base.

## VI. Demis Hassabis — the biographical record

The Hassabis biographical record is one of the most distinctive among senior AI executives:

### Origins

**Demis Hassabis** was born on **27 July 1976 in London, England**. His father, **Costas Hassabis**, is Greek-Cypriot. His mother, **Angela Hassabis**, is Chinese Singaporean. The mixed Greek-Cypriot / Chinese Singaporean heritage is distinctive — more diverse than the typically Anglo-American family pattern that has characterised most of the figures in this book.

### Chess prodigy and computer-programming proficiency

- Began playing chess competitively at age 4.
- **Reached chess Master standard at age 13** with an Elo rating of 2300 — the top 0.1% of-active-tournament-players.
- Computer-programming proficiency from age 8.
- Serious video-game-design work from age 14 (commercial games written in his teens).

### Education and pre-DeepMind career

- **Cambridge University (Queens' College)** — Computer Science, BA double first (1997). At Cambridge, captained the college's chess team.
- **Bullfrog Productions** (under Peter Molyneux) — lead programmer for *Theme Park* (1994) at age 17 (simultaneous work during his Cambridge years).
- **Lionhead Studios** (also under Molyneux) — lead AI programmer for *Black & White* (2001).
- **Elixir Studios** (founded 1998, Hassabis's own video-game development company) — produced *Republic: The Revolution* (2003) and *Evil Genius* (2004). Sold his Elixir holdings in 2005.
- **PhD in Cognitive Neuroscience, University College London (UCL)** (2009) — the PhD was focused on understanding **how the human brain manages imagination and memory**; the thesis was foundational to the subsequent DeepMind work.
- Postdoctoral work at **Harvard and MIT** (2009-2010).
- **Henry Wellcome Fellow at UCL**.

The Hassabis pre-DeepMind formation is **distinctive among AI executives** — the combination of video-game-AI commercial proficiency, cognitive-neuroscience PhD training, and serious chess/board-game expertise produced an intellectual formation different from the standard CS-undergraduate-to-startup-founder trajectory of most senior American AI executives.

### DeepMind founding (2010)

In **2010**, Hassabis co-founded **DeepMind Technologies** in London with:

- **Shane Legg** — New Zealand AI researcher whom Hassabis had met at the **Gatsby Computational Neuroscience Unit** at UCL.
- **Mustafa Suleyman** — English entrepreneur, Hassabis's family friend from childhood. Subsequently departed DeepMind in 2022; founded **Inflection AI** with Reid Hoffman (Chapter 15) and Karén Simonyan; subsequently joined **Microsoft as CEO of Microsoft AI** in March 2024.
- **David Silver** — Hassabis's Cambridge friend and Elixir partner, who joined as principal AI researcher.

DeepMind's stated mission: "**solve intelligence**, and then use intelligence to solve everything else." The most ambitious single corporate-mission statement in technology-industry history.

### Recognition

- **March 2024**: Hassabis **knighted (KBE)** by King Charles III for "services to artificial intelligence."
- **October 2024**: **Nobel Prize in Chemistry** (with Jumper and Baker).
- **2017**: Appointed CBE.
- **Time 100 most influential people**: 2017 and 2025.
- **Time 2025 Person of the Year**: **"Architects of AI"** — collective recognition that included Hassabis.
- **UK Government AI Adviser**.

### Isomorphic Labs (2021)

In **2021**, Hassabis founded **Isomorphic Labs** — an Alphabet spinout focused on AI-driven drug discovery. Hassabis is co-CEO alongside his Google DeepMind responsibilities. Isomorphic Labs has partnerships with **Eli Lilly** and **Novartis** for pharmaceutical research applications of AlphaFold-derived technology.

## VII. John Jumper — the biographical record

**John Michael Jumper** was born on **1 January 1985 in Little Rock, Arkansas**. The Jumper biographical record is documented in detail in the chapter sources file; the principal facts:

- **Education**: Pulaski Academy (Little Rock, 2003); BS Physics and Mathematics, **Vanderbilt University** (2007); MPhil in Theoretical Condensed Matter Physics, **University of Cambridge** (2008); MS Theoretical Chemistry, **University of Chicago** (2012); PhD Theoretical Chemistry, **University of Chicago** (2017).
- **D.E. Shaw Research** (2009-2012) — computational protein-dynamics simulations at D.E. Shaw's computational laboratory in New York. The D.E. Shaw Research is the same Renaissance-Technologies-adjacent quantitative-finance firm where **Jeff Bezos** developed his early Amazon insight (Chapter 16); the D.E. Shaw connection has been one of the recurring institutional connections across the book's biographical chapters.
- **DeepMind** (joined 2017, from his University of Chicago PhD position) — initially research scientist; subsequently director.
- **2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry** (with Hassabis and Baker).
- Per Nobel officials, **Jumper was the youngest chemistry laureate in more than seventy years** (age 39 at the time of the prize).

The Jumper-Hassabis collaboration produced AlphaFold 2 — the 2020 system that solved the 50-year-old protein-folding grand challenge. Jumper's contribution was the theoretical-chemistry expertise that reformed the AlphaFold model architecture between AlphaFold 1 (2018, ~60% accuracy) and AlphaFold 2 (2020, ~90% accuracy).

## VIII. Sundar Pichai — origins, education, and the Google trajectory

**Pichai Sundararajan** was born on **10 June 1972 in Madras (now Chennai), Tamil Nadu, India**.

### Origins

The Pichai family is the most modest among the senior American technology-industry capital class. His father, **Regunatha Pichai**, was a senior electrical engineer at **General Electric Company (GEC)** in Chennai — a middle-class Indian professional position. His mother, **Lakshmi**, was a stenographer before her marriage. The Tamil-Brahmin family background was middle-class by Indian standards of the 1970s-1980s; the family did not have a telephone until Sundar was 12, did not have a refrigerator or television until he was a teenager.

The observation about the Pichai family-of-origin: it was the most-modest senior contemporary American technology-industry-capital class family-of-origin the book has documented. The Mary-Gates-on-the-IBM-board, the Lawrence-Gise-AEC-director, the Errol-Musk-emerald-mine, the Carl-Page-Sr.-Michigan-State-CS-professor family-network advantages were absent; the Pichai trajectory is individual educational-achievement-based mobility in the contemporary technology industry.

### Education

- **Jawahar Vidyalaya** (Ashok Nagar, Chennai).
- **Vana Vani School** (IIT Madras campus, Chennai).
- **Bachelor of Technology in Metallurgical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur (IIT Kharagpur)** (1989-1993). The IIT Kharagpur is one of the most-selective Indian engineering institutions; the Pichai academic record was distinguished — Pichai was named institute silver medallist.
- **MS in Materials Science and Engineering, Stanford University** (1995) — scholarship support. The Pichai family had borrowed money in excess of the father's annual salary to fund the relocation and the Stanford tuition.
- **MBA, Wharton School at the University of Pennsylvania** (2002) — Pichai was named a **Siebel Scholar** and a **Palmer Scholar**.

### Pre-Google career

- **Applied Materials** (semiconductor manufacturing) — engineering and product-management positions.
- **McKinsey & Company** — management-consulting positions through 2004.

### Google career

- **April 2004**: Pichai joined Google in product management. Initial product responsibility: **Google Toolbar** for Internet Explorer and Firefox.
- **2006-2008**: Led the development of **Google Chrome browser**. The Chrome project was Pichai's operational breakthrough at Google; Chrome's launch in September 2008 produced one of the most successful single browser launches in the contemporary technology industry. By 2020, Chrome had captured 65% of the global web-browser market.
- **2013**: Pichai promoted to **Senior Vice President of Android, Chrome, and Google Apps** (after Andy Rubin's departure from Android amid subsequent harassment-and-conduct controversies).
- **August 2015**: Pichai appointed **CEO of Google** (when Google was reorganised as a subsidiary of Alphabet Inc.).
- **3 December 2019**: Pichai appointed **CEO of Alphabet Inc.** (replacing Larry Page, who stepped down).

**The Pichai trajectory is the most meritocratic senior American technology executive trajectory in this book.** From modest Chennai origins to CEO of Alphabet — the most validating case of the meritocratic narrative, against the more extensive family-network-facilitation patterns at Bezos, Gates, Musk, and the other senior figures.

The fact that the industry has produced meritocratic mobility for some figures (Pichai is the case) does not alter the broader pattern: most senior figures arrived through family-network-facilitated paths.

## IX. The 2015 Alphabet restructuring

In **August 2015**, Google reorganised into the **Alphabet Inc.** holding-company structure. The rationale: Alphabet would serve as the umbrella for **Google** (largely advertising-and-search-revenue-generating) and the various Alphabet "**Other Bets**" subsidiaries:

- **Waymo** — autonomous-vehicle technology; spun out from Google's self-driving-car project in 2016.
- **Verily** — life sciences and health technology.
- **Calico** — longevity and biotechnology research.
- **X** ("the moonshot factory") — various experimental projects.
- **Google Fiber** — broadband-internet infrastructure.
- **Sidewalk Labs** — urban-technology development.
- **Wing** — drone-delivery service.
- **DeepMind** — AI research laboratory (subsequently merged with Google Brain in April 2023 to form Google DeepMind).
- **Isomorphic Labs** (subsequently spun out in 2021 by Hassabis).

The 2015 Alphabet restructuring was widely characterised in the trade press as a corporate-governance reorganisation that permitted Page and Brin to focus on the **Other Bets** while delegating Google-search-and-advertising operational management to Pichai.

- **Larry Page**: CEO of Alphabet.
- **Sergey Brin**: President of Alphabet.
- **Sundar Pichai**: CEO of Google.
- **Eric Schmidt**: Executive Chairman of Alphabet (until December 2017; subsequently Technical Advisor until 2018-2020; subsequently resigned from Alphabet board April 2019).

## X. The 2019 Page-Brin departure and the Pichai consolidation

On **3 December 2019**, **Larry Page stepped down as CEO of Alphabet**. **Sergey Brin stepped down as President of Alphabet**. **Sundar Pichai was appointed CEO of Alphabet** (additionally to his Google CEO role).

The Page and Brin departure: both retained their Class B supervoting shares and their board positions; they withdrew from day-to-day operations. The Brin characterisation of his withdrawal (per his **December 2025 Stanford School of Engineering centennial celebration speech**): he felt himself "**spiraling**" despite his status as one of the world's richest men. The observation: the Brin departure was not the conventional senior-executive retirement; it was personal-existential withdrawal that has subsequently been reversed.

The Pichai consolidation of the Alphabet CEO role with his pre-existing Google CEO role produced the case of one of the most concentrated senior executive positions in contemporary American corporate history — Pichai is, as of May 2026, the CEO of **89% of global search, 71% of global mobile operating systems, 65% of global web browsers, 2.7 billion YouTube users**, and the contemporary AI-research-and-product infrastructure.

## XI. The 2020 antitrust lawsuit and the principal federal antitrust trajectory

### US v. Google (search) — the principal antitrust case

- **20 October 2020**: **Department of Justice and 11 state attorneys general** filed the **US v. Google** antitrust lawsuit in the **US District Court for the District of Columbia**, alleging Google had **unlawfully maintained monopoly power in general search services and search text advertising** through exclusive-default-search agreements with device manufacturers (Apple, Samsung, others) and browser developers (Mozilla, others).
- The context: the 2020 DOJ filing was the principal federal antitrust action against Google during the first Trump administration. The case was continued by the Biden administration through trial.
- **September 2023 - November 2023**: **Nine-week bench trial** before **US District Judge Amit Mehta**.
- **5 August 2024**: Judge Mehta issued a **277-page Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law** opinion. The principal ruling: "**Google is a monopolist, and it has acted as one to maintain its monopoly**" in violation of Section 2 of the Sherman Antitrust Act. The opinion established that Google's exclusive default-search contracts with **Apple, Samsung, Mozilla, and others** had excluded competition.

The August 2024 Mehta ruling was **the most significant federal antitrust adverse ruling against a US technology company since the 1998-2001 US v. Microsoft case** (Chapter 19). Judge Mehta's ruling was structurally analogous to Judge Thomas Penfield Jackson's 2000 ruling that Microsoft was a monopolist; the question of the appropriate remedy was contested across the subsequent year.

### The September 2025 remedies judgment

- **April-May 2025**: 15-day remedies trial; testimony included **Alphabet CEO Sundar Pichai** and executives from Apple, OpenAI, News Corp, and Perplexity AI.
- **2 September 2025**: Judge Mehta issued his **230-page remedies judgment**.

The Mehta remedies judgment:

- **Rejected the DOJ's proposed Chrome browser divestiture**. Mehta's characterisation: "The court's task is to discern between conduct that maintains a monopoly through anticompetitive acts as distinct from 'growth or development as a consequence of a superior product, business acumen, or historic accident.' After two complete trials, this court cannot find that Google's market dominance is sufficiently attributable to its illegal conduct to justify divestiture." Mehta additionally characterised divestiture as **"incredibly messy and highly risky."**
- **Rejected the DOJ's contingent Android divestiture**.
- **Banned exclusive default-search contracts** for **six years** (covering Google Search, Chrome, Google Assistant, and Gemini app placements on devices manufactured by Apple, Samsung, and other partners).
- **Required Google to share certain search data** with qualified competitors.
- Allowed Google to continue to pay device makers to preload its products, **provided the deals are not exclusive**.
- Established a **technical committee** with access to Google's source code and algorithms to oversee Google's data-sharing obligations.

**The September 2025 Mehta remedies judgment produced narrower remedies than the principal historical comparison** (the 2000 Microsoft break-up order that was subsequently reversed on appeal). The Chrome and Android divestiture rejection was widely characterised in the trade press as a victory for Google; the behavioural remedies have produced major operational changes but have not produced the structural transformation the DOJ had sought.

- The case was joined by **49 states, two territories, and the District of Columbia**.
- **Perplexity AI submitted a $34.5 billion offer for Chrome** during the remedy proceedings; the offer was rendered moot by Mehta's decision not to require divestiture.
- **5 December 2025**: Judge Mehta finalised the remedies.
- Google has indicated intent to appeal the underlying August 2024 monopoly ruling.

### US v. Google (ad-tech) — the second antitrust case

The second federal antitrust case against Google was filed in **January 2023** by the **Department of Justice and 8 state attorneys general** in the **US District Court for the Eastern District of Virginia**, alleging Google had **unlawfully monopolised the digital advertising technology stack** — the ad-exchange, ad-server, and ad-network markets through which display advertising is bought and sold.

- **September 2024 - November 2024**: Trial before **US District Judge Leonie Brinkema**.
- **17 April 2025**: Judge Brinkema issued her opinion finding Google had **unlawfully monopolised the ad-tech industry**. The ruling established that Google had monopolised the publisher ad-server market and the ad-exchange market through tying arrangements and exclusionary conduct.
- **Remedy phase**: ongoing as of May 2026; DOJ proposal for **divestiture of Google Ad Manager** (the principal publisher-side ad-server) is being contested.

**Google has been the subject of two federal antitrust adverse rulings within 18 months** — the August 2024 search monopoly ruling and the April 2025 ad-tech monopoly ruling. The consequences:

- The behavioural remedies in the search case have produced operational changes (the termination of exclusive default-search contracts with Apple and others) but have not produced the structural divestiture the DOJ sought.
- The ad-tech remedy phase remains contested.
- Google's market position has, across the period of the adverse rulings, **appreciated**. Alphabet's market capitalisation has increased from **$1.6 trillion** at the time of the August 2024 ruling to **$2.8 trillion** by May 2026 — driven by the Gemini 3 launch and AI-industry positioning.

**Contemporary federal antitrust enforcement has not constrained the operational monopolies of the American technology industry.** The Google monopolies in search, mobile operating systems, web browsers, display advertising, and video have persisted through the federal antitrust adverse rulings. The comparison to US v. Microsoft (Chapter 19): Microsoft's Windows monopoly persisted through the 2001 settlement; Google's search monopoly is persisting through the 2025 remedies judgment.

## XII. Project Maven and the 2018 employee walkout

The Google-DoD relationship has been one of the most contested in the American technology industry.

### The 2017-2018 Project Maven contract

- **2017**: Google entered a contract with the **US Department of Defense's Project Maven** — a military-AI program for analysing drone footage from US military surveillance operations. The contract was reportedly worth **$9 million initially** with major expansion potential.
- **March-June 2018**: Major Google employee opposition to Project Maven; **4,000 Google employees signed an internal petition** demanding the company withdraw from the contract. **Roughly 12 senior Google engineers resigned in protest**.
- **June 2018**: Google announced it would not renew the Project Maven contract when it expired in 2019.
- **June 2018**: Google published its **AI Principles** including a commitment not to develop AI "**weapons or other technologies whose principal purpose or implementation is to cause or directly facilitate injury to people**."

### The 2025 AI Principles revision

- **February 2025**: Google **updated its AI Principles**, **removing the prohibition on AI applications for weapons and surveillance**. The policy change was widely characterised in the trade press as the case of the company's realignment with the second Trump administration's defence-AI priorities.

The Google trajectory across the 2018-2025 period:

- **2018**: Major employee-led withdrawal from Project Maven; AI Principles commitment.
- **2018-2024**: Major subsequent Google-DoD cloud-computing contracts and Google-CIA cloud-computing contracts (comparable to Amazon's $600 million CIA cloud contract documented in Chapter 16).
- **July 2025**: Google received **$200 million DoD contract for AI in the military** (alongside Anthropic, OpenAI, and xAI; see Chapter 21).
- **February 2025**: Major AI Principles revision removing the prohibition on weapons applications.

**The 2018 Google AI Principles commitment has been reversed across seven years.** The 2018 employee opposition to military-AI applications produced temporary policy changes; the subsequent corporate realignment with the defence-contracting environment reversed the policy commitments. The structural pattern: corporate accommodation to the defence-contracting environment.

## XIII. The contemporary AI position (2023-2026)

### Gemini

- **December 2023**: Google launched the **Gemini** AI model family (initially Gemini Ultra, Pro, and Nano). The Gemini launch was widely characterised in the trade press as Google's response to OpenAI's GPT-4 dominance — delayed relative to the OpenAI lead.
- **February 2024**: **Gemini 1.5** released.
- **December 2024**: **Gemini 2.0** released.
- **2025**: Multiple Gemini 2.x releases.
- **November 2025**: **Gemini 3** launch. One of the most successful AI-model launches of the period; **Alphabet stock appreciated 65% by end of 2025** on the Gemini 3 success and the associated **"Nano Banana"** image-generation model.

### Google AI infrastructure

- **TPU (Tensor Processing Unit)**: Google's proprietary AI-acceleration semiconductor; the principal Google alternative to NVIDIA's GPU dominance. The TPU programme has been continued across multiple generations.
- The **October 2025 Anthropic deal** (Chapter 21) provided Anthropic with access to up to **one million Google TPUs** — the largest single AI-compute-capacity-deployment commitment in the contemporary AI industry. The deal positioned Google as the principal alternative AI-infrastructure provider to NVIDIA.
- **$75 billion AI capex 2025**: Google announced $75 billion in capital expenditure for AI infrastructure in 2025 — comparable to the Microsoft and Amazon AI-capex programmes documented in previous chapters.

### Senior figures and operational structure

- **CEO of Alphabet**: Sundar Pichai (since December 2019).
- **CEO of Google DeepMind**: Demis Hassabis.
- **Chief Scientist of Google DeepMind**: Jeff Dean.
- **President of Google and CFO of Alphabet**: Ruth Porat (until 2023; subsequently Anat Ashkenazi as CFO).
- **Larry Page**: withdrawn from operations through 2023; re-engaged in "more strategic ways" since 2024 per Hassabis (February 2026 Fortune interview); Class B supervoting share control retained.
- **Sergey Brin**: returned to operational AI work since 2023; "**in the weeds, programming**" on Gemini models per Hassabis (February 2026); Class B supervoting share control retained.

## XIV. The Page-Brin 2023-2025 founder return

The Page-and-Brin trajectory from 2019 withdrawal to 2023-2025 return is one of the most-documented senior-corporate-founder-return patterns in the contemporary American technology industry.

### The context

- **December 2019**: Page and Brin withdrew from operational management.
- **2020-2022**: No public operational engagement on documented. The Brin characterisation (December 2025): he felt himself "**spiraling**" despite his status as one of the world's richest men.
- **2023**: When the Googleplex reopened for employees post-COVID, **Brin began showing up for work three to four times per week** (per Wall Street Journal reporting).
- **February 2025**: Brin issued an **internal memo to Google employees** requiring more time at the office, with emphasis on in-person collaboration for the Gemini development effort.
- **2025-2026**: Per Hassabis (February 2026 Fortune interview): "**Sergey has been in the weeds, programming**" on Gemini models. Page is "getting involved as well, in more strategic ways."

### The operational meaning

The Page-and-Brin founder return is senior corporate founders re-engaging operationally in response to existential corporate competition. The competitive context: **OpenAI's 2022-2024 ChatGPT lead, Anthropic's Claude lead, and the associated existential threat to Google's search-advertising-revenue position** produced the active pressure that motivated the founder return.

The observation: **the contemporary AI competition has produced the return of the senior contemporary American technology-industry founders to operational management**. The pattern is documented at Google (Page and Brin); at OpenAI (Altman's November 2023 re-acquisition of the CEO position after the board-coup attempt, Chapter 13); at Apple (Tim Cook's AI-positioning through the post-2024 period); and at Microsoft (Satya Nadella's OpenAI integration).

### The consequence

- **Alphabet's market capitalisation**: appreciated through 2025 on Gemini 3 success.
- **Page's net worth**: **$249-262 billion** at November 2025 peak; #2 globally.
- **Brin's net worth**: **$233-243 billion** at November 2025 peak; #4 globally.
- **Combined Page-and-Brin net worth**: **$520 billion** at peak — comparable to the Musk personal net worth (Chapter 12).

## XV. The political and tax-relocation activity

### The January 2025 Trump inauguration

**Brin attended Donald Trump's January 2025 inauguration** alongside the other senior contemporary American technology executives (Bezos, Zuckerberg, Musk, Pichai, Cook, Altman). The Brin presence at the inauguration was one of the most prominently Democratic-aligned senior contemporary American technology figures executing the post-election political-realignment that the book has documented across multiple chapters.

**Pichai also attended Trump's inauguration**; Pichai met Trump at **Mar-a-Lago in November 2024** (post-election transition meeting). Google contributed to Trump's inauguration committee (comparable to the corporate donations from Meta, Amazon, Microsoft, and the broader senior contemporary American technology industry).

### The December 2025 California LLC relocation

- **December 2025**: Brin and Page **terminated or moved out of California sixty limited liability companies** that hold their assets.

The operational meaning of the LLC relocation has been characterised in subsequent commentary as tax-relocation; the founders are reportedly moving large portions of their personal-wealth-holding infrastructure out of California in the period contemporaneous with the founders' operational re-engagement with Google. The timing — the LLC relocation occurring contemporaneously with the Alphabet share-price appreciation — has been characterised as tax-optimisation-motivated; the California capital-gains tax (13.3% top marginal rate) on the 2025-2026 Alphabet share appreciation would produce much higher tax liability than would be produced in much lower-tax-jurisdiction states (Texas, Florida, Nevada, Wyoming).

The Brin-and-Page LLC-relocation pattern is, structurally similar to the Larry Ellison Tennessee relocation (2020) and the Elon Musk Texas relocation (2020) — the tax-relocation pattern that has characterised the senior contemporary American technology-industry-capital class across the post-2020 period.

## XVI. Net worth and current positions (May 2026)

The Forbes / Bloomberg net-worth figures have been volatile across the 2025-2026 period as Alphabet share price has appreciated:

- **Larry Page**: **$240 billion** as of May 2026 (appreciated from $150 billion in mid-2024). Roughly **#2-3 globally**.
- **Sergey Brin**: **$235 billion** as of May 2026. Roughly **#3-4 globally**.
- **Sundar Pichai**: **$1.3 billion**. (The observation: Pichai's wealth is smaller than the founder positions because his compensation has been executive-compensation rather than founder-equity. The contrast with the Page-and-Brin founder positions documents the working distinction between founder-equity and executive-compensation in the contemporary American technology industry.)
- **Demis Hassabis**: **$300-400 million** (smaller than the senior technology-industry founders; Hassabis's wealth derives from the DeepMind acquisition proceeds and his subsequent Alphabet compensation).
- **John Jumper**: smaller; the Nobel Prize cash component was $500,000 (shared half of the 11 million Swedish kronor with Hassabis); Jumper's net worth is in Alphabet stock-based compensation accumulated since 2017.

### Alphabet operational scale (May 2026)

- **Market capitalisation**: **$2.8 trillion**.
- **Annual revenue (2025)**: **$400 billion**.
- **Annual operating income (2025)**: **$130 billion**.
- **Employees**: **180,000** globally.
- **Search market share**: **89%** globally.
- **YouTube monthly active users**: **2.7 billion** globally.
- **Android market share**: **71%** of global mobile-OS market.
- **Chrome market share**: **65%** of global web-browser market.

## XVII. Alphabet's structural position

This is what the contemporary Alphabet structural position represents, as of May 2026:

- **The single most-operationally-consequential contemporary American technology-industry corporate position**. Alphabet's monopoly positions in search, mobile operating systems, web browsers, and display advertising exceed those of any other contemporary American technology corporation.
- **The most AI-research-laboratory-integrated contemporary corporation**. DeepMind under Hassabis has produced the AlphaGo and AlphaFold breakthroughs that have advanced the contemporary AI scientific knowledge base; the 2024 Nobel Prize is the scientific validation of the AI-research programme.
- **The most federally-antitrust-adverse-ruled contemporary American technology corporation**. The August 2024 search monopoly ruling and the April 2025 ad-tech monopoly ruling have produced behavioural remedies; the consequences have been limited.
- **The most-recent-founder-return contemporary American technology corporation**. The 2023-2025 Page-and-Brin return to operational engagement has driven the Gemini 3 launch and the associated share-price appreciation.
- **The most-Class-B-supervoting-control-concentrated contemporary American technology corporation**. The Page-and-Brin Class B control (~51% of voting power with much less than 51% of economic equity) concentrates operational decision-making in the co-founders' positions.

**The Alphabet structural position represents the most operationally consequential single American technology-industry corporate position.** The operational meaning — the consequences of the monopoly concentrations for the information ecosystem, for the AI development trajectory, for the American economic structure — is the question the book's broader argument addresses.

**The Google search monopoly has persisted through the August 2024 federal antitrust adverse ruling. The Google ad-tech monopoly has persisted through the April 2025 adverse ruling. Federal antitrust enforcement has not constrained the monopolies of the American technology industry.**

This is who Larry Page, Sergey Brin, Sundar Pichai, Demis Hassabis, and John Jumper are, as of May 2026: the senior figures of the Alphabet platform-monopoly position; the controllers of 89% of global search, 71% of global mobile operating systems, 65% of global web browsers, and 2.7 billion YouTube users; the operators of the most significant AI-research laboratory; the subjects of two federal antitrust adverse rulings; the principals of $520 billion in combined Page-and-Brin personal net worth.
