# Sources — Chapter 7: The Arithmetic

## Compound interest and exponential growth

- **Rule of 72**: doubling time = 72 / interest rate. At 3% interest, money doubles every 24 years; at 5%, every 14.4 years; at 7%, every 10.3 years. Pure arithmetic, traceable to Luca Pacioli's 1494 *Summa de Arithmetica*.
- **Einstein, attributed**: "Compound interest is the eighth wonder of the world. He who understands it, earns it; he who doesn't, pays it." The attribution to Einstein is disputed — first traceable to a 1983 *New York Times* ad — but the principle stands independently of the quote.
- **The "penny doubled for 30 days" demonstration**: $0.01 doubled every day for 30 days reaches $5,368,709.12. The same arithmetic applied to a 30-year mortgage at 7% requires repayment of 2.4 times the principal; at 30 years on a sovereign debt rolled at the same rate, the amount required for repayment exceeds the original loan.

## Frederick Soddy (1877–1956)

- Nobel laureate in chemistry, 1921 (for work on isotopes and radioactive decay).
- *Wealth, Virtual Wealth and Debt: The Solution of the Economic Paradox*, 1926.
- Central argument: real wealth is constrained by the laws of thermodynamics (you cannot create energy or matter from nothing); financial wealth (debt, money) is a mathematical abstraction that compounds exponentially. The two diverge over time, and the divergence is the source of every recurring financial crisis.
- Specific policy recommendations (later adopted, in part, by the 1933 Chicago Plan economists): 100% reserve requirements, abolition of private money creation through fractional reserve lending, currency issued only by sovereign authority.
- Soddy's work was dismissed in his lifetime as the eccentricity of a chemist meddling in economics. It has been reassessed since the 1970s, and Soddy is now recognised as one of the founders of ecological economics (Herman Daly cited Soddy as his principal influence).

## Thomas Piketty's r > g

- *Capital in the Twenty-First Century* (Belknap/Harvard, 2014; French original 2013).
- Central finding: the rate of return on capital (r) has, across the historical record from antiquity to the present, normally exceeded the rate of economic growth (g).
- Historical estimates: r ≈ 4–5% across centuries; g ≈ 1–1.5% across centuries (Piketty pp. 73, 93, 208, 358–59).
- The exception: 1914–1975, when r was suppressed by two world wars, the Great Depression, capital destruction, and post-war progressive taxation. This exception, the "Golden Age" of compressed inequality, is what the post-war generation mistook for the normal condition of capitalism. It was an anomaly.
- Mathematical consequence: if r > g indefinitely, then capital's share of national income rises, labour's share falls, and wealth concentrates at the top, as a matter of arithmetic. No moral or political variables required. This is what has happened since 1980. Inequality has returned to pre-1914 levels.
- Piketty's empirical work, drawn from tax records and national accounts across 20 countries over 250 years, is the largest dataset in the history of inequality economics. The trend is not disputable.
- The 2025 Carlos Góes paper (arXiv 2505.01521) challenged whether r > g causally drives inequality using panel VAR techniques. This is a methodological critique of the causal mechanism, not of the underlying r/g facts. The r/g facts are not in serious dispute among economists who have worked with the data.

## Aristotle on usury

- *Politics* Book I, chapter 10 (1258a-b): "The most hated sort [of money-getting], and with the greatest reason, is usury, which makes a gain out of money itself, and not from the natural use of it. For money was intended to be used in exchange, but not to increase at interest. And this term usury (τόκος), which means the birth of money from money, is applied to the breeding of money because the offspring resembles the parent. Wherefore of all modes of getting wealth this is the most unnatural."
- This argument was the basis of the Christian, Jewish, and Islamic prohibitions on usury between 300 CE and 1500 CE.
- The argument was not displaced by a counter-argument. It was displaced by Calvin (1545, *De usuris*), who permitted commercial interest while continuing to condemn usurious lending to the poor, and then progressively eroded across the seventeenth century as the financial revolution made the prohibition practically impossible to enforce against the merchant class.
- The Islamic prohibition on *riba* (interest) is still in force across one-third of the world's population.

## The arithmetic of debt creation

- In a fractional-reserve banking system, money is created when banks make loans. The borrower receives the principal; the bank receives an interest-bearing asset.
- The interest, however, is not created by the loan. The borrower must obtain it elsewhere, which means somewhere else in the economy another loan must be made.
- The system therefore requires perpetual expansion of credit to service existing credit. Steady-state operation is structurally impossible; the system either expands or collapses.
- This is documented in elementary economics textbooks but rarely framed in these terms. The clearest popular treatment is the Bank of England's own working paper *Money creation in the modern economy* (Quarterly Bulletin Q1 2014), which acknowledges that "loans create deposits" — that is, that commercial banks create money by lending it into existence.

## The Triffin Dilemma applied domestically

- Robert Triffin's 1960 critique was about reserve currency. Applied to domestic monetary systems, the same logic gives the *debt-money dilemma*: a debt-issued money supply requires permanent debt expansion to provide the medium of exchange for the next cycle of production. To pay down debt collectively is to contract the money supply, which collapses output, which makes existing debts unpayable, which collapses the system. The system can grow or fail; it cannot rest.

## Herman Daly and ecological economics

- *Steady-State Economics* (1977); *For the Common Good* with John Cobb (1989).
- Argued, building on Soddy, that an economy embedded in a finite biosphere cannot grow exponentially forever. The growth requirements imposed by compound interest therefore guarantee ecological breakdown over a finite time horizon.
- Daly's work has been confirmed by the Limits to Growth modelling (Meadows et al., 1972), which has tracked closely with empirical reality across fifty years (Graham Turner, CSIRO, 2008 and 2014 updates).

## Game-theoretic locks

- **Libya, 2011**: Muammar Gaddafi had announced plans to introduce a pan-African gold-backed dinar to replace the dollar and CFA franc for African oil and trade settlement. NATO bombed Libya beginning March 2011; Gaddafi was killed October 2011. Documented in Hillary Clinton's State Department emails released under FOIA: French intelligence advised that Gaddafi's gold-dinar plan was "a threat to the financial security of the world." (Clinton email, 2 April 2011, declassified 31 December 2015.)
- **Iraq, 2000**: Saddam Hussein announced in September 2000 that Iraqi oil-for-food transactions would be conducted in euros rather than dollars. The decision was implemented in November 2000. The 2003 US invasion reversed the euro conversion in June 2003 within weeks of regime change. The euro-conversion decision is documented in UN Oil-for-Food programme records.
- **Iran, 2018**: continuing US sanctions, partly in response to Iran's establishment of an oil bourse trading in non-dollar currencies (the Iranian Oil Bourse, opened 2008 on Kish Island).
- **Venezuela, 2018**: Maduro launched the petro cryptocurrency partly to bypass dollar sanctions. US sanctions intensified.

The pattern: any sovereign attempt to exit the dollar system produces immediate severe consequences. The system is therefore not voluntary at the sovereign level either.

## Sources

- Frederick Soddy, *Wealth, Virtual Wealth and Debt* (1926; reprinted Omni Publications 1961).
- Thomas Piketty, *Capital in the Twenty-First Century* (Belknap/Harvard, 2014).
- Thomas Piketty, *Capital and Ideology* (Belknap/Harvard, 2020).
- Herman Daly and John Cobb, *For the Common Good* (Beacon, 1989).
- Donella Meadows et al., *The Limits to Growth* (1972, updates 1992, 2004).
- Graham Turner, "A Comparison of The Limits to Growth with 30 years of reality" (CSIRO, 2008); updated 2014.
- David Graeber, *Debt: The First 5,000 Years* (2011).
- Michael Hudson, *And Forgive Them Their Debts* (2018).
- Steve Keen, *Debunking Economics* (2011).
- Aristotle, *Politics* Book I, chapter 10 (c. 350 BCE).
- Bank of England, "Money creation in the modern economy," Quarterly Bulletin Q1 2014.
- Bank for International Settlements working papers on r > g implications.
- Hillary Clinton State Department emails released under FOIA, particularly the 2 April 2011 email on Libya gold-dinar plan.
- Donald Trump-era and prior Treasury sanctions data on Iran, Venezuela.

# Sources — Chapter 8: The Cult

## The Big Three asset managers

**BlackRock**: founded 1988 by Larry Fink. Assets under management $11.6 trillion at 31 December 2024 (BlackRock Form S-3ASR, FY2025 filing).
- CEO: Larry Fink (founder, chairman).
- Aladdin (Asset, Liability, Debt and Derivative Investment Network): risk management platform. As of December 2025, BlackRock reports **$25 trillion** in assets managed on the Aladdin platform (Wikipedia, *Aladdin (BlackRock)*; BusinessStats March 2026; Central Banking magazine).
- Aladdin client base: over 1,000 organisations including central banks, sovereign wealth funds, pension funds, insurance companies, asset managers. Named clients: Bank of Israel (since 2019, $208B FX), CalPERS ($260B), Deutsche Bank (€900B), Prudential plc ($700B), Microsoft Treasury (via Azure partnership).
- During 2008 crisis: US government placed $130 billion in toxic assets with BlackRock for management. Federal Reserve gave BlackRock the $1.25 trillion mortgage-backed securities repurchase mandate.

**Vanguard**: founded 1975 by John C. Bogle. As of 2024, $10 trillion AUM. The world's largest mutual fund company. CEO: Salim Ramji (from July 2024; previously Mortimer "Tim" Buckley).
- Privately owned by its mutual fund holders (an unusual mutual structure).
- Largest or second-largest shareholder in 90% of S&P 500 companies.

**State Street**: $4.7 trillion AUM (2024). CEO: Ronald O'Hanley. The third of the "Big Three" American passive-index asset managers.

**Combined**: the Big Three hold 20-25% of typical large-cap US equities. They are the largest shareholders in 88% of S&P 500 companies. They are, in effect, the dominant owners of corporate America.

## Horizontal shareholding

- Eric Posner (University of Chicago Law) and Glen Weyl, *Radical Markets* (Princeton, 2018): documented that when the same investors hold large positions in competing firms, competition decreases.
- Einer Elhauge (Harvard Law), "Horizontal Shareholding," *Harvard Law Review* 129 (2016): the legal-economic argument that common ownership of competing firms is an antitrust violation under the Clayton Act.
- Empirical studies: José Azar, Martin Schmalz, Isabel Tecu, "Anticompetitive Effects of Common Ownership," *Journal of Finance* (2018) — found that common ownership in the US airline industry raised airfares by 3-7%.

## Private equity

- **Blackstone**: founded 1985 by Stephen Schwarzman and **Peter G. Peterson** (the same Peterson who pushed the Nixon Shock plan in January 1971; see Ch 5). World's largest alternative asset manager. AUM $1.1 trillion (2024). CEO: Schwarzman; President: Jonathan Gray.
- **KKR**: founded 1976 by Jerome Kohlberg, Henry Kravis, and George Roberts. The original leveraged-buyout firm. AUM $550 billion (2024). Co-CEOs: Joseph Bae and Scott Nuttall.
- **Apollo Global Management**: founded 1990 by Leon Black, Joshua Harris, and Marc Rowan. AUM $700 billion. CEO since 2021: Marc Rowan. **Leon Black**: founder and former CEO; resigned in 2021 after disclosure that he had paid Jeffrey Epstein $158 million for "tax and estate planning advice" between 2012 and 2017 (Apollo Independent Review, Dechert LLP, January 2021).
- **Carlyle Group**: founded 1987 by David Rubenstein, Stephen Norris, and others in Washington, DC. AUM $426 billion (2023). The intelligence-and-defense PE firm:
  - **Frank Carlucci** (former CIA Deputy Director under Carter; Reagan National Security Adviser; Reagan Defense Secretary): joined 1989 as Chairman. The man who brought Carlyle into defence contracting.
  - **James A. Baker III** (Reagan Treasury Secretary; Bush Sr. Secretary of State; Bush Sr. Chief of Staff): senior counselor.
  - **George H.W. Bush** (former CIA Director, VP, President): senior adviser 1994-2003.
  - **John Major** (former UK PM): European chairman 1997-2004.
  - **Arthur Levitt** (former SEC chairman): senior adviser.
  - **The Bin Laden family**: through the Saudi Binladin Group, invested in Carlyle until October 2001, when they were quietly bought out after the Bush-Bin Laden connection became politically embarrassing.
  - **Carlyle's 11 September 2001 annual investor conference**: held at the Ritz-Carlton in Washington DC. Shafiq bin Laden (half-brother of Osama) was present with George H.W. Bush, James Baker, Frank Carlucci, David Rubenstein, on the day the WTC towers fell.

## Hedge funds

- **Citadel**: founded 1990 by Ken Griffin. AUM ~$66B (2024). Griffin's net worth ~$45B (Forbes 2024). Major political donor (Republican).
- **Renaissance Technologies**: founded 1982 by Jim Simons. Medallion Fund — the most successful hedge fund in history. **Robert Mercer**, co-CEO 2010-2017 — funded Cambridge Analytica, Breitbart News, the 2016 Trump campaign through donations to PACs run by Steve Bannon. The data-operation pipeline of the 2016 election was Mercer money.
- **Bridgewater Associates**: founded 1975 by Ray Dalio. World's largest hedge fund by AUM (~$170B). Dalio's "principles" doctrine; Dalio's relationship with the Chinese government (extensive holdings in mainland China).
- **Elliott Management**: founded 1977 by Paul Singer. ~$70B AUM. Famous for sovereign-debt vulture investments (Argentina, Republic of Congo, Puerto Rico). Major Republican donor.
- **D.E. Shaw**: founded 1988 by David Shaw. ~$60B AUM. Notable for being where Larry Summers, Jeff Bezos worked.
- **Millennium Management**: founded 1989 by Israel "Izzy" Englander. ~$70B AUM.

## The Goldman Treasury pipeline

Goldman Sachs alumni in senior US government and global central banking positions:

- **Robert Rubin**: Goldman CEO 1990-92; US Treasury Secretary 1995-99; Citigroup senior counsellor 1999-2009.
- **Henry "Hank" Paulson**: Goldman CEO 1999-2006; US Treasury Secretary 2006-09 — the man who ran TARP.
- **Steven Mnuchin**: Goldman partner 1994-2002; Trump Treasury Secretary 2017-21.
- **Gary Cohn**: Goldman President 2006-16; Trump National Economic Council Director 2017-18.
- **Steve Bannon**: Goldman M&A 1984-90 before politics; Trump senior strategist 2017.
- **Neel Kashkari**: Goldman 2002-06; ran TARP under Paulson; President of Minneapolis Fed 2016-present.
- **Mark Carney**: Goldman 1990-2003; Governor of the Bank of Canada 2008-13; Governor of the Bank of England 2013-20; **Prime Minister of Canada from March 2025**.
- **Mario Draghi**: Goldman managing director 2002-2005; Bank of Italy governor 2006-11; **President of the European Central Bank 2011-19**; Prime Minister of Italy 2021-22.
- **William Dudley**: Goldman partner 1986-2007; President of New York Fed 2009-18.

**Goldman's role in the 2001 Greek euro entry**: Goldman Sachs structured currency swaps (cross-currency swaps with non-market rates) that allowed Greece to disguise €2.8 billion of debt from EU Maastricht criteria assessments. Documented by Spiegel, Bloomberg, and the New York Times in 2010 after the swaps became public. Goldman's fee was $300 million. The deal helped Greece into the euro and helped Goldman make a major profit; the Greek public paid the cost of the subsequent debt crisis.

## Think-tank and policy layer

- **World Economic Forum (Davos)**: founded 1971 by Klaus Schwab. Now coordinates 1,000 corporate members (membership starts at $60,000/year, "strategic partners" pay $500,000+). The Young Global Leaders programme has placed alumni in senior positions across governments (Macron, Merkel, Trudeau, Ardern, Jacinda; Sebastian Kurz; in policy circles globally). Schwab's *The Great Reset* (2020) and *The Fourth Industrial Revolution* (2016) — explicit publications about coordinating technocratic governance globally. The argument for coordination is openly stated; the question is whose interests are coordinated.
- **Trilateral Commission**: founded 1973 by David Rockefeller and Zbigniew Brzezinski. Three regions: North America, Europe, Japan. Coordinated transatlantic and trans-Pacific elite policy from the 1970s.
- **Council on Foreign Relations**: founded 1921. American foreign-policy establishment. Members include the senior leadership of nearly every major American institution.
- **Bilderberg Meetings**: annual, founded 1954 by Prince Bernhard of the Netherlands. Roughly 130 attendees per year, by invitation only, under Chatham House rules. No public minutes. Senior politicians, central bankers, corporate executives, and media figures.
- **Atlas Network**: see Chapter 6 — coordinates ~500 free-market think tanks globally.
- **Aspen Institute**: founded 1949 by Walter Paepcke. Aspen Strategy Group, Aspen Security Forum — bipartisan coordination on national-security policy.

## PayPal Mafia and techno-oligarchs

**The PayPal Mafia**: founders and senior employees of PayPal who, after eBay's 2002 acquisition for $1.5B, dispersed across Silicon Valley to found or invest in subsequent firms.

- **Peter Thiel**: co-founder PayPal 1998; founder Palantir 2003 (with seed funding from CIA's In-Q-Tel); founder Founders Fund 2005; early Facebook investor (received $2,200 worth of stock for $500,000 in 2004, sold for $1 billion at IPO); major funder of JD Vance's 2022 Ohio Senate campaign (now Vice President of the United States); long-time funder of Curtis Yarvin (writing as Mencius Moldbug) and the neoreactionary "Dark Enlightenment" movement that advocates replacing American liberal democracy with corporate-authoritarian governance.
- **Elon Musk**: co-founder of Zip2 (sold 1999); joined X.com (later PayPal) 1999. Family background: father Errol Musk owned, at various periods in the 1980s, a half-share in a Zambian emerald mine. Net worth $400 billion as of 2025, making him the wealthiest individual in human history. Owns Tesla, SpaceX, X (Twitter), xAI, the Boring Company, Neuralink. Effective director of the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) under the second Trump administration, January-May 2025.
- **Reid Hoffman**: co-founder LinkedIn 2002; OpenAI board member 2019-23. Funder of Democratic politics. The PayPal Mafia is bipartisan.
- **David Sacks**: COO PayPal 1999-2002; founder Yammer (sold to Microsoft 2012); host of *All-In* podcast; AI and crypto czar in the second Trump administration from 2025.
- **Max Levchin**: co-founder PayPal; founder Affirm.
- **Roelof Botha**: PayPal CFO; senior partner Sequoia Capital.
- **Keith Rabois, Jeremy Stoppelman, Chad Hurley, Steve Chen, Jawed Karim** (the last three founded YouTube), **Russel Simmons** (Yelp), **Premal Shah** (Kiva).

**Other techno-oligarchs**:

- **Marc Andreessen**: co-founder Netscape; co-founder Andreessen Horowitz (a16z) 2009. AUM $45B. Author of "The Techno-Optimist Manifesto" (2023) — explicit ideological text advocating accelerationism and dismissing concerns about AI safety, technological inequality, and democratic oversight. Major Trump donor 2024.
- **Sam Altman**: founder Y Combinator (chairman 2014-19); co-founder OpenAI 2015 (with Musk, Hoffman, others); CEO OpenAI from 2019. Also founder of Worldcoin — biometric identification project that collects iris scans from 6 million people globally (as of 2025).
- **Alex Karp**: co-founder Palantir Technologies 2003 (with Thiel and others). Palantir's contracts: ICE deportation operations (US, ~$200M+ since 2014); UK National Health Service ($330M, 2023); Israel Defense Forces (Gaza operations, undisclosed value, 2023-25); US Army battlefield AI (~$800M, 2024).
- **Larry Ellison**: founder Oracle 1977. Owns 98% of the Hawaiian island of Lanai (purchased 2012, ~$300M). Major Republican donor. Net worth $200B.
- **Jeff Bezos**: founder Amazon 1994; founder Blue Origin 2000; owner of *The Washington Post* (purchased 2013 for $250M). Amazon Web Services (AWS) signed a $600M contract with the CIA in 2013 to build the agency's cloud infrastructure; this was the founding contract of the AWS government-cloud business which is now $30B/year.
- **Mark Zuckerberg**: founder Facebook 2004 (now Meta). Owns 56% of Meta voting shares. Effective sole controller of the company that operates Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, and Threads — combined 4 billion monthly active users.
- **Eric Schmidt**: Google CEO 2001-11; chairman 2011-15. Chair of the National Security Commission on Artificial Intelligence 2018-21 — wrote the policy framework for US government AI deployment, including the recommendations that became the AI/military integration programmes now in operation.

## The Epstein-Wexner-Maxwell network — documented elements

This section uses only documented and corroborated elements.

- **Leslie Wexner**: founder of L Brands (Victoria's Secret, Bath & Body Works, The Limited). Net worth $6B. Met Jeffrey Epstein in 1986. In 1989 granted Epstein **power of attorney** over his $1.4 billion business empire and his charitable foundation. Gifted Epstein the Manhattan townhouse at 9 East 71st Street, valued at $77M at time of transfer (free of cost; Epstein paid Wexner $0). Documented in court filings, the New York Times investigation (July 2019), and the Apollo Independent Review (January 2021).
- **The Mega Group**: founded 1991 by Wexner and Charles Bronfman (Seagram). Pro-Israel billionaires' coordination group. Maria Farmer (Epstein abuse survivor, named in court documents) testified that the Mega Group was associated with the Epstein blackmail operation.
- **Robert Maxwell** (1923-1991): father of Ghislaine Maxwell. British media mogul, owner of the Daily Mirror and the New York Daily News. Worked for British intelligence (MI6) during and after WWII. Confirmed Mossad asset per Gordon Thomas and Martin Dillon, *Robert Maxwell: Israel's Superspy* (2002), and the BBC documentary series *House of Maxwell* (2022). Died falling from his yacht near the Canary Islands in November 1991 under circumstances never resolved. Buried on the Mount of Olives in Jerusalem; eulogy by Israeli Prime Minister Yitzhak Shamir; attended by at least six current or former Israeli intelligence chiefs.
- **Ghislaine Maxwell**: Robert Maxwell's youngest daughter. Moved to New York after her father's death and the collapse of his publishing empire. Met Epstein in 1991 or 1992. Convicted by federal jury in December 2021 of five counts including sex trafficking of minors; sentenced June 2022 to 20 years; eligible for release 2037.
- **Acosta and the "intelligence" claim**: Alexander Acosta, the US Attorney who approved Epstein's 2008 non-prosecution agreement, was reported by journalist Vicky Ward (*Daily Beast*, July 2019) to have told Trump transition officials during his 2017 vetting for Labor Secretary that he had been told to "back off" the Epstein case because Epstein "belonged to intelligence." Acosta denied this under oath to the House Oversight Committee in October 2025. Steve Bannon was named as a likely source for the original Ward reporting. Acosta resigned as Labor Secretary in July 2019 after public outcry over the original Epstein plea deal.
- **The 2008 Florida non-prosecution agreement**: 18-month sentence on state charges, served in county jail with 12-hour daily work release. Approved over the objections of FBI investigators and federal prosecutors who had identified at least 30 victims. The deal also granted immunity to "co-conspirators" — unnamed in the document. The arrangement was kept from the victims themselves, which a federal court later ruled violated the Crime Victims Rights Act. Investigative reporting that reopened the case: Julie K. Brown, *Miami Herald*, "Perversion of Justice" series, November 2018.
- **Ehud Barak**: former Israeli Prime Minister and Defence Minister. Met Epstein on 36 occasions between 2013 and 2017 (documented through visitor logs at Epstein's Manhattan townhouse and through photographs). Received $2.3 million from the Wexner Foundation in 2004, described as a payment for "research."
- **Steven Hoffenberg**: Epstein's former business partner in the Towers Financial Ponzi scheme (Hoffenberg convicted 1995, served 18 years). Said publicly before his 2022 death that Epstein had repeatedly told him he had Mossad ties.
- **2024 court document releases**: hundreds of pages of Epstein-related court documents were released in January 2024, naming 170 individuals who had contact with Epstein. The list included Bill Clinton, Donald Trump, Bill Gates, Leon Black, Stephen Hawking, Prince Andrew, and many others — political figures across both American parties, billionaires, academics, intelligence figures, royalty.
- **2025 DOJ "no further releases" memo**: in July 2025 the US Department of Justice and FBI issued a joint memo stating that no further Epstein-related documents would be released, that there was no "client list," and that Epstein's death in August 2019 had been a suicide. This memo broke with public commitments made by the Trump administration during the 2024 campaign that Epstein-related files would be released in full. The memo split the Trump coalition. As of May 2026 the matter remains a live political controversy with ongoing congressional investigation.

## AI consolidation

- **OpenAI**: founded 2015 by Sam Altman, Elon Musk, Reid Hoffman, Greg Brockman, Ilya Sutskever, Wojciech Zaremba, John Schulman. Originally non-profit; converted to capped-profit structure 2019; further restructuring to for-profit announced 2025. Microsoft has invested $13 billion. Valuation $500 billion as of late 2025.
- **Anthropic**: founded 2021 by Dario Amodei, Daniela Amodei, and others from OpenAI. Investors include Google, Amazon (combined ~$8 billion), Spark Capital, others. Valuation $170 billion (early 2025). **This book is being drafted using Claude, Anthropic's AI assistant.** See author's note at chapter close.
- **Google DeepMind**: Alphabet subsidiary, formed 2023 by the merger of Google Brain and DeepMind (UK, acquired 2014).
- **xAI**: founded by Elon Musk 2023. Operates Grok. Valuation $80 billion (2024).
- **Meta AI**: Mark Zuckerberg's Meta has invested heavily in LLaMA and AGI labs.
- **Microsoft AI**: in addition to OpenAI investment, internal AI division headed (until 2025) by Mustafa Suleyman (former DeepMind co-founder).

**The pattern**: every major Western AI lab is owned by or largely funded by five corporate parents (Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Meta, plus Musk personally), all of which are themselves majority-owned by the Big Three asset managers. The technology that is being deployed to manage labour, information, surveillance, and increasingly governance at population scale is owned by the same dozen people who own the rest of the consolidated oligarchy.

# Sources — Chapter 9: The Capture of Knowing

## University capture

- **Koch Foundation academic funding**: Charles Koch's foundations fund 350 universities. The largest single recipient is **George Mason University**, which hosts the Koch-funded **Mercatus Center** (founded 1980, named "Mercatus" in 1999, ~$10M+/year Koch funding) and the Antonin Scalia Law School (renamed in 2016 after a $30 million combined Koch/anonymous donation).
- **Florida State University 2008 contract**: revealed by *St. Petersburg Times* in 2011. Charles Koch Foundation gave FSU's economics department $1.5 million in exchange for veto power over hiring decisions through an advisory board with Koch representatives. The contract was published online; FSU subsequently revised the arrangement under public pressure but the funding model continued.
- **Sackler family academic funding**: pre-OxyContin and through the OxyContin years, Sackler money funded major academic institutions globally. The Sackler galleries, libraries, and institutes at Yale, Harvard, Columbia, Tufts (which housed the Sackler School of Graduate Biomedical Sciences), Cornell, Oxford, Cambridge, Edinburgh, the Smithsonian, the Metropolitan Museum, the Tate, the Louvre, the Guggenheim, the National Portrait Gallery. After 2019, most institutions removed the Sackler name following public protest organised by photographer Nan Goldin (PAIN: Prescription Addiction Intervention Now).
- **MIT-Epstein**: MIT Media Lab took $7.5 million from Jeffrey Epstein after his 2008 conviction. **Joi Ito**, director of the MIT Media Lab, resigned 8 September 2019 after Ronan Farrow's *New Yorker* exposé documented that Ito and senior MIT administrators (including President L. Rafael Reif) had knowingly accepted Epstein's donations while concealing the source. The subsequent MIT investigation by **Goodwin Procter** (December 2019 report) confirmed that Ito and several MIT officials had treated Epstein as a "disqualified" donor while continuing to take his money and direct it through Ito's lab and through other channels including the lab's general fund.
- **Larry Summers**: Harvard president 2001-2006; resigned after votes of no confidence over his comments on women in science and his handling of the Andrei Shleifer Russian-economic-reform scandal (Shleifer was found liable for fraud in federal court after using a Harvard-led USAID contract to enrich himself and his wife through Russian privatisation investments). Subsequent positions: Director, National Economic Council 2009-2010 (Obama); part-time at D.E. Shaw (~$5.2M/year, one day/week); board member of OpenAI 2023-present.

## Replication crisis

- **Psychology**: the Reproducibility Project: Psychology (Open Science Collaboration, 2015) attempted to replicate 100 published psychology studies. Only **39 of 100** replicated; 36% of replications produced statistically significant results. *Science*, 28 August 2015, "Estimating the reproducibility of psychological science."
- **Cancer biology**: Begley and Ellis (2012): former Amgen researcher C. Glenn Begley reported in *Nature* that only **6 of 53** "landmark" preclinical cancer studies could be replicated. *Nature* 483, 531-533, 29 March 2012, "Drug development: Raise standards for preclinical cancer research."
- **The Reinhart-Rogoff Excel error**:
  - 2010: Carmen Reinhart and Kenneth Rogoff (both Harvard) published *Growth in a Time of Debt* in *American Economic Review Papers and Proceedings*. Headline finding: countries with debt-to-GDP ratio over 90% have average GDP growth of -0.1%.
  - Cited by US House Budget Chairman **Paul Ryan**, UK Chancellor of the Exchequer **George Osborne**, EU Economic Commissioner **Olli Rehn**, and the IMF as scientific justification for austerity programmes affecting hundreds of millions of people across the Western world.
  - April 2013: **Thomas Herndon** (PhD student at UMass Amherst) requested the original Excel spreadsheet from Reinhart and Rogoff and discovered three errors:
    1. **Coding error**: Reinhart and Rogoff had failed to extend an averaging formula to include all rows in their Excel spreadsheet. Australia, Austria, Belgium, Canada, and Denmark — five of twenty countries — had been excluded from the calculation.
    2. **Selective data exclusion**: years of data showing Australia, Canada, and New Zealand with simultaneously high debt and high growth were omitted.
    3. **Inappropriate weighting**: equal weighting of country-years vs equal weighting of countries.
  - Corrected calculation: countries with debt-to-GDP over 90% have average GDP growth of **+2.2%**, not -0.1%. The "90% cliff" did not exist.
  - Herndon, Ash, and Pollin, "Does High Public Debt Consistently Stifle Economic Growth? A Critique of Reinhart and Rogoff," *Cambridge Journal of Economics* 38 (2014).
  - The austerity policies the original paper had justified continued. The Greek bailout terms, the UK austerity programme, the EU stability-and-growth pact — none were reversed. Greek unemployment reached 27%, UK life expectancy declined for the first time since the Second World War, 130,000 additional UK deaths between 2012 and 2019 have been attributed to austerity in subsequent studies (UCL/Bristol).
- **Pharma publication bias**: half of clinical trial results unpublished or selectively published. Ben Goldacre, *Bad Pharma* (2012). The AllTrials campaign documents the systematic suppression of negative trial results.
- **Marcia Angell**: former editor of the *New England Journal of Medicine* (1999-2000). In *The Truth About the Drug Companies* (2004) and in subsequent essays in the *New York Review of Books*, Angell documented that the major pharmaceutical companies systematically distort published research through study design choices, selective publication, ghostwriting of academic articles, and direct payment to academic researchers.

## FDA capture

- **Prescription Drug User Fee Act (PDUFA)**: enacted 1992. FDA permitted to collect "user fees" from drug companies to accelerate the review of New Drug Applications.
- **Growth of industry funding share**:
  - FY1993: ~7% of FDA's prescription drug program funded by industry.
  - FY2007: ~20% of total FDA budget funded by industry.
  - FY2017: per NEJM, industry pays **75% of the costs of FDA's scientific review of drugs**.
  - FY2022: user fees account for **66% of the human drugs program budget** (HHS ASPE 2023 report).
  - FY2025: industry user fees totalled $3.3 billion, **~49% of total FDA budget**, ~65% of drug-evaluation programmes.
- **The structural consequence**: the FDA's drug-review function is now majority-funded by the industry it regulates. Reviewers' salaries, in 2018, were already ~80% industry-funded (Darrow et al. 2020).
- **PDUFA also expanded industry's role in policy**: PDUFA reauthorisations every five years have included industry-negotiated provisions on review timelines, meeting access, expedited approval pathways, and dissemination rules. The industry now formally negotiates the terms of its own regulation (PMC 2022).

## Curtis Wright and the OxyContin case

- **The drug**: OxyContin (oxycodone hydrochloride extended-release), made by Purdue Pharma. The Sackler family's signature product.
- **Purdue's marketing claim** (the falsehood that drove the epidemic): the extended-release mechanism made OxyContin less addictive than other opioids. There was no scientific evidence for this claim. It was instead a marketing pitch.
- **Curtis Wright IV**: FDA medical review officer for the OxyContin New Drug Application. The sole reviewer.
- **The hotel-room meeting**: 31 January – 2 February 1995. Three Purdue executives and Wright met for three days at a hotel near FDA offices in Rockville, Maryland. Wright allowed Purdue executives to help draft his official FDA Medical Officer's Review (MOR), including approving the wording for the package insert that read: "Delayed absorption, as provided by OxyContin tablets, is believed to reduce the abuse liability of the drug." The claim had no supporting evidence in the application.
- **Approval**: OxyContin approved by FDA December 1995 with the false abuse-liability language in the label, on Wright's sign-off.
- **The revolving door**: Wright resigned from FDA in 1996. Joined Adolor briefly. In **1998**, hired by Purdue Pharma as Executive Director of Medical Research at $379,000/year — **three times his FDA salary**.
- **The 2006 DOJ memo**: a federal prosecution memo prepared in 2006 recommended criminal charges against the Sackler family and senior Purdue executives. The Justice Department declined to bring the charges. The memo was sealed. It was released, partially, in 2019 through Senator Maggie Hassan's office and *ProPublica* reporting.
- **The 2007 plea**: three senior Purdue executives (not the Sacklers personally) pleaded guilty to federal misdemeanour misbranding charges. Purdue paid $634.5 million. No prison time. The Sacklers personally kept billions in dividends extracted from the company in the preceding decade.
- **The opioid death toll**: between 1999 and 2024, **727,000 Americans died from opioid overdoses**, with prescription opioids the gateway for the majority. OxyContin's market success defined the prescription wave; the subsequent transition to heroin and then fentanyl was the second and third waves.
- **The 2020 settlement**: Purdue Pharma agreed in October 2020 to plead guilty to three federal felonies and pay $8.3 billion. The Sackler family agreed to pay $4.5 billion personally in exchange for civil immunity, then increased to $6 billion in 2022 after the bankruptcy court rejected the original deal. **No member of the Sackler family has ever been criminally prosecuted.** The family remains wealthy.
- **McKinsey & Company**: paid $573 million in February 2021 to settle state attorneys general claims that McKinsey had advised Purdue on how to "turbocharge" OxyContin sales, including by paying distributors rebates for overdoses ($14,810 per overdose customer in one 2017 PowerPoint slide). No McKinsey partner was prosecuted.
- **Patrick Radden Keefe**, *Empire of Pain: The Secret History of the Sackler Dynasty* (Doubleday, 2021): the standard journalistic account.

## The Gates Foundation layer

- **Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation**: endowment $80 billion (2024). The world's largest private philanthropic foundation. Founded 2000.
- **GAVI (the Vaccine Alliance)**: founded 2000 with $750 million Gates seed funding; now ~$10 billion in committed Gates funding. GAVI is the principal vehicle for Gates-coordinated global vaccine deployment.
- **The Imperial College COVID model (March 2020)**: Neil Ferguson's epidemiological model at Imperial College London, which on 16 March 2020 projected 510,000 UK deaths and 2.2 million US deaths if no mitigation was undertaken. The model became the immediate basis for the UK national lockdown of 23 March 2020 and similar lockdowns globally. **Imperial College London receives Gates Foundation funding** — between 2015 and 2024, Imperial received over $185 million in Gates Foundation grants across various programmes including the Vaccine Impact Modelling Consortium and the Abdul Latif Jameel Institute for Disease and Emergency Analytics. The Ferguson model's code was later criticised by software engineers (after May 2020 partial release on GitHub) for poor structure and non-deterministic behaviour. The model's parameter choices (R₀, infection fatality rate, asymptomatic transmission) were not subjected to standard pre-publication peer review before being used as the basis for global policy.
- **AGRA (Alliance for a Green Revolution in Africa)**: launched 2006 by the Gates and Rockefeller Foundations; Gates Foundation contributed $700 million through 2020. AGRA promoted commercial seed and fertiliser inputs to African smallholder farmers as the solution to food insecurity.
  - **2020 Tufts University evaluation**: the "False Promises" report by the Tufts University Global Development and Environment Institute, evaluating AGRA's 14 years of operation in its 13 target countries. The report found:
    - Productivity gains: 18% over 12 years for AGRA's target staple crops, well below AGRA's stated goal of doubling productivity.
    - Number of severely undernourished people: **increased by 30%** in the AGRA target countries during the programme.
    - Indicators of rural poverty in target countries: deteriorated relative to non-target African countries.
  - The Gates Foundation continued funding AGRA after the Tufts report.
- **Columbia Journalism Review, August 2020**: *Journalism's Gates Keepers* by Tim Schwab. Documented that the Gates Foundation had given more than $250 million to media organisations including NPR, BBC, the Guardian, the New York Times, the Atlantic, ProPublica, El País, Univision, NBC, the Financial Times, and many smaller outlets. The grants funded specific reporting projects on global health, education, and development. Schwab documented that these funded reports often did not disclose the Gates Foundation's funding role when covering Gates Foundation activities, and that journalists at funded outlets faced career incentives to avoid critical coverage of Gates Foundation programmes.

## COVID origins and the Proximal Origin paper

- **Anthony Fauci**: Director of the National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases (NIAID) 1984-2022. NIAID is a division of the National Institutes of Health (NIH).
- **EcoHealth Alliance**: New York-based non-profit, run by **Peter Daszak**. Received $3.7 million in NIAID grants 2014-2019 for research on bat coronaviruses, of which $600,000 was subcontracted to the **Wuhan Institute of Virology** (WIV).
- **Gain-of-function research**: the 2014 NIH gain-of-function moratorium had paused federally funded research that would enhance the transmissibility or pathogenicity of pandemic-potential pathogens. EcoHealth Alliance's grant included gain-of-function-adjacent research that, in October 2021, the NIH itself acknowledged in a letter from NIH Principal Deputy Director Lawrence Tabak had produced a chimeric coronavirus that showed enhanced replication in humanised mice.
- **The Proximal Origin paper**: Andersen, Rambaut, Lipkin, Holmes, Garry, "The proximal origin of SARS-CoV-2," published in *Nature Medicine* on 17 March 2020. The paper concluded that SARS-CoV-2 was "not a laboratory construct" and dismissed lab origin. The paper was used throughout 2020 and 2021 as the principal scientific basis for the public consensus that lab leak was a "debunked conspiracy theory."
- **The 2023 Slack disclosures**: in July 2023 the House Select Subcommittee on the Coronavirus Pandemic released Slack and email messages from the Proximal Origin authors, dating from February and early March 2020 — that is, during the drafting of the paper. In private, several of the authors expressed significant doubt about natural origin. Kristian Andersen wrote on 2 February 2020: "The unusual features of the virus make up a really small part of the genome (<0.1%) so one has to look really closely at all the sequences to see that some of the features (potentially) look engineered." Robert Garry wrote: "I really can't think of a plausible natural scenario." Andrew Rambaut wrote: "from a (natural) evolution and ecology perspective… given the scenarios proposed I cannot favour one outcome over the other." The paper that was published on 17 March 2020 expressed none of these doubts.
- **The 2024 House Subcommittee report** documented that Fauci had personally convened and influenced the drafting of the Proximal Origin paper in late January and early February 2020. The paper was used to suppress the lab-leak hypothesis publicly while its authors privately retained doubt.
- **The shift in official assessment**:
  - February 2023: US Department of Energy concluded with "low confidence" that the pandemic most likely arose from a laboratory leak (*Wall Street Journal* 26 February 2023).
  - March 2023: FBI Director Christopher Wray confirmed publicly that the FBI had assessed the lab-leak hypothesis as the most likely origin with "moderate confidence" (his agency's assessment had been classified since 2021).
  - January 2025: the CIA, under newly appointed Director John Ratcliffe, updated its assessment to "low confidence" lab-leak as the most likely origin, reversing the agency's previous "no determination" position.

## Media consolidation

- **The Bagdikian count**: in 1983, when Ben Bagdikian published the first edition of *The Media Monopoly*, 50 corporations controlled 90% of American mass media. In his 2004 revision *The New Media Monopoly*, the number was 5: Time Warner, Disney, Murdoch's News Corporation, Viacom, and Bertelsmann.
- **2026 status**: after further consolidation (the AT&T-Time Warner merger and subsequent divestiture; the Disney-Fox transaction 2019; the Skydance-Paramount merger 2024; the Murdoch family realignment 2023), five companies control 90% of US media: Disney, Comcast (NBCUniversal), Warner Bros. Discovery, Paramount/Skydance (now David Ellison-controlled), and News Corp/Fox (Murdoch).
- **Local newspaper collapse**: from 2005 to 2024, the United States lost 2,200 local newspapers — about a third of all titles operating in 2005. The replacements (Alden Global Capital's strip-mining model, McClatchy bankruptcy, Gannett's contraction) have accelerated the decline. Roughly 70 million Americans now live in a "news desert" or near-desert (Northwestern Local News Initiative annual reports).

# Sources — Chapter 10: The Consensus Future

All claims verified 12 May 2026.

## The capital commitments

- **Microsoft**: Brad Smith (Vice Chair and President), blog post 3 January 2025: Microsoft will spend "$80 billion in fiscal year 2025" on AI-enabled data centres.
- **Google (Alphabet)**: Q4 2024 earnings call, 4 February 2025. CFO Anat Ashkenazi and CEO Sundar Pichai: $75 billion capital expenditure for 2025, principally on AI infrastructure.
- **Meta**: Q4 2024 earnings call, 29 January 2025. Mark Zuckerberg: $60-65 billion capex for 2025, "vast majority" AI-related.
- **Amazon**: Q4 2024 earnings call, 6 February 2025. CEO Andy Jassy: AWS capex "$100 billion" for 2025, dominated by AI infrastructure.
- **Combined Big Four hyperscaler 2025 capex**: **$320 billion** in a single year.
- **Stargate Project**: announced 21 January 2025 at the White House. OpenAI, SoftBank, Oracle, MGX. $500 billion over 4 years; $100 billion deployed immediately. SoftBank chairman Masayoshi Son: chairman. First site: 1 million sq ft, Abilene, Texas.
- **xAI**: $6B Series B May 2024; $5B Series C November 2024; $6B Series D December 2024. Building "Colossus" data centre in Memphis, Tennessee — 100,000 Nvidia H100 GPUs operational December 2024, planned expansion to 1 million GPUs (Musk announcement, December 2024).
- **G42 (UAE)**: $1.5B Microsoft investment in April 2024. G42 chairman: Sheikh Tahnoun bin Zayed Al Nahyan, UAE National Security Adviser and brother of UAE President Mohammed bin Zayed.
- **Saudi PIF**: ~$40B AI fund announced March 2024 in partnership with Andreessen Horowitz (Bloomberg, 19 March 2024).
- **Total announced AI infrastructure capex 2024-2029**: by industry estimates from Goldman Sachs Research and Bain & Co. analysis (Q1 2025), on the order of **$1.4-2 trillion**.

## The labour displacement predictions

### Goldman Sachs (March 2023)

- Authors: Joseph Briggs and Devesh Kodnani.
- Title: *The Potentially Large Effects of Artificial Intelligence on Economic Growth*, 26 March 2023.
- Findings:
  - **300 million full-time jobs** globally exposed to AI automation.
  - **Two-thirds of US occupations** exposed to some degree of automation.
  - 25-50% of workload replaceable in exposed occupations.
  - **18% of global work** could be computerised on an employment-weighted basis.
  - 46% of office and administrative support work automatable.
  - 44% of legal work automatable.
  - 37% of architecture and engineering work automatable.
  - 7% increase in global GDP over 10 years projected.

### IMF (January 2024)

- Staff Discussion Note SDN/2024/001, *Gen-AI: Artificial Intelligence and the Future of Work*, 14 January 2024. Authors: Cazzaniga, Jaumotte, Li, Melina, Panton, Pizzinelli, Rockall, Tavares.
- IMF Managing Director Kristalina Georgieva blog post, *AI Will Transform the Global Economy*, same date.
- Findings:
  - **Nearly 40% of global jobs** exposed.
  - **60% of jobs in advanced economies** exposed (UK 70%, US 60%).
  - 40% in emerging markets, 26% in low-income countries.
  - Half of exposed jobs face displacement risk; the other half face augmentation.
  - That is, **30% of all advanced-economy jobs face displacement**.
  - Georgieva: "In most scenarios, AI will likely worsen overall inequality."

### McKinsey Global Institute (June 2023; July 2023; May 2024; November 2025)

- *The economic potential of generative AI: The next productivity frontier*, 14 June 2023: $2.6-4.4 trillion in annual value across 63 use cases; half of today's work activities automatable between 2030 and 2060 (midpoint 2045), accelerated ~10 years by generative AI.
- *Generative AI and the future of work in America*, 26 July 2023: 30% of US work hours automatable by 2030 (up from 21.5% pre-generative-AI baseline). 12 million occupational transitions in the US by 2030 — low-wage workers face up to 14× more transitions than high-wage workers.
- *A new future of work: The race to deploy AI and raise skills in Europe and beyond*, May 2024: 27% of European work hours automatable by 2030 alongside the 30% US figure.
- *Agents, Robots, and Us: Skill Partnerships in the Age of AI*, November 2025: 57% of work hours already technically automatable with currently demonstrated technology.

### OECD (2024)

- *OECD Employment Outlook 2024*, July 2024: 27% of work in OECD countries at high risk of automation, highest exposure in professional, technical, and managerial occupations.

### Acemoglu critique (May 2024)

- Daron Acemoglu (MIT, 2024 Nobel laureate in Economics), *The Simple Macroeconomics of AI*, NBER Working Paper 32487, May 2024: projects much smaller GDP impact (0.06% per year, totalling 0.93-1.6% over 10 years) and finds that the productivity gains are concentrated in a narrow set of cognitive tasks. Acemoglu's view is the dissenting position within mainstream economics, but it does not contradict the displacement predictions — it only contradicts the GDP-growth projections that the industry uses to justify the displacement.

## The CEO statements

- **Sam Altman** (OpenAI CEO), World Government Summit, Dubai, 12 February 2024: "If the world economy grows at 3% a year, that's $3 trillion of new economic activity created every year. If we have AGI, that goes way up." Repeated formulations across 2024 interviews.
- **Sam Altman**, *The Intelligence Age* blog post, 23 September 2024: "It is possible that we will have superintelligence in a few thousand days; it may take longer, but I'm confident we'll get there."
- **Dario Amodei** (Anthropic CEO), *Machines of Loving Grace*, October 2024 essay: "powerful AI" by 2026; "compressed 21st century" of progress in 5-10 years following; specific projections include cures for most diseases, doubling of human healthy lifespan, ending of poverty in low-income countries, solving most mental illness.
- **Elon Musk**, VivaTech conference, Paris, 23 May 2024: "Probably none of us will have a job. In a benign scenario, probably none of us will have a job."
- **Demis Hassabis** (Google DeepMind CEO), *Wired* interview May 2024: AGI "in the next 5 to 10 years."
- **Geoffrey Hinton** (2024 Nobel Prize in Physics for foundational AI work), CBS *60 Minutes* October 2023 and BBC interviews 2024: 50% probability AI exceeds human intelligence within 5-20 years; 10-20% existential risk to humanity.
- **Yann LeCun** (Meta Chief AI Scientist), dissenting position: current LLM architecture cannot achieve AGI; new architectures required. LeCun does not dispute the displacement timeline, only the AGI timeline.

## Stargate Project details

- **Announced**: 21 January 2025, White House Roosevelt Room. Trump's second day in office.
- **Principals**: OpenAI, SoftBank Group, Oracle Corporation, MGX (Abu Dhabi sovereign vehicle).
- **Investment**: $500 billion over 4 years; $100 billion deployed immediately.
- **Chairman**: Masayoshi Son (SoftBank).
- **Operating partner**: OpenAI.
- **Finance partner**: SoftBank.
- **First site**: 1 million-square-foot data centre, Abilene, Texas. Under construction at announcement.
- **Stated jobs**: 100,000.
- **Trump**: "the largest AI infrastructure project in history."
- **Altman**: "the most important project of this era."
- **Ellison**: "the beginning of a golden age."
- **Son to Trump, publicly**: "We wouldn't have decided to do this unless you won."

## The political-administration personnel

The second Trump administration's AI policy team is drawn from the same circle that owns the AI companies:

- **JD Vance**, Vice President. Funded into the Senate by Peter Thiel ($15M+ in PAC contributions, 2022). Thiel's protégé since 2016. Worked at Thiel's Mithril Capital before founding Narya Capital (Thiel investor).
- **David Sacks**, "AI and Crypto Czar" (announced 5 December 2024). PayPal Mafia founder; co-founder of Yammer (sold Microsoft 2012). Major Trump donor 2024.
- **Elon Musk**, head of the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) January-May 2025. Cut 220,000 federal positions. Departed May 2025 after the Trump-Musk public falling-out.
- **Marc Andreessen**, informal adviser. Hosted multiple Trump transition meetings at his Atherton property. Major Trump donor 2024.
- **Larry Ellison**, principal of Stargate, attended Trump inauguration, his son David Ellison's Skydance Media acquired Paramount/CBS December 2024 with Trump-administration regulatory approval.
- **Stephen Miran**, Chair of the Council of Economic Advisers (from January 2025). Worked at Hudson Bay Capital before appointment.
- **Howard Lutnick**, Commerce Secretary (from January 2025). Cantor Fitzgerald CEO before appointment. Cantor Fitzgerald is a major investor in Tether and in the crypto industry that Sacks now regulates.
- **Scott Bessent**, Treasury Secretary (from January 2025). Hedge fund manager (Key Square Group); Soros Fund Management chief investment officer 2011-2015.

The administration is, in its economic-policy senior personnel, almost entirely composed of senior figures from the financial-technology layer the book has described.

# Sources — Chapter 11: Peter Thiel

All claims verified 12 May 2026.

## Family and origins

- **Peter Andreas Thiel**: born 11 October 1967, Frankfurt am Main, West Germany.
- **Klaus Friedrich Thiel** (1938-2022): Peter's father. Chemical engineer. Worked at various mining companies in the US and South Africa, including a uranium-mining operation in then-South African-administered South West Africa (modern Namibia).
- **Susanne Thiel**: Peter's mother.
- **Brother**: Patrick Thiel.
- **The Rössing uranium mine** (Erongo region, Namibia, near Swakopmund): operated by Rio Tinto Zinc; the largest open-pit uranium mine in the world by the 1970s. Operated in contravention of UN Security Council Resolution 366 (1974) and UN Council for Namibia Decree No. 1 (1974), both of which prohibited the extraction of Namibian resources during the illegal South African occupation. The mine supplied uranium to the apartheid South African nuclear weapons programme (which developed six nuclear devices, dismantled in 1989 and disclosed in 1993) and to Western utilities through sanctions-busting arrangements brokered partly through the Rössing consortium's complex ownership structure (Rio Tinto majority, Iranian government minority pre-1979, German and Japanese utility offtake agreements).
- **The Thiel family movement**: Frankfurt 1967 → Cleveland, Ohio 1968 (Klaus Thiel applied for US citizenship) → South Africa and South West Africa in the early 1970s → return to United States, settling in Foster City, California, in 1977.
- **Peter Thiel's schooling during the Namibia period**: changed elementary schools seven times. Attended Pridwin School in Johannesburg (a non-denominational Christian school) in the early 1970s, then a German-language school in Swakopmund from 1975 to 1977. The Swakopmund German community was, in the 1970s and 1980s, known for continued Nazi sympathies — New York Times reporting from 1976 documented that "Heil Hitler" greetings and Nazi salutes were still common in the town. Souvenir shops sold Nazi-themed merchandise into the 1980s (cited in Namibian Sun, 30 August 2025, citing journalist Phil van Niekerk).

Sources for the Klaus Thiel uranium connection:
- *Queers in History* 2025: "His father, Klaus Friedrich Thiel (1938–2022), was a chemical engineer employed by various mining companies in US as well as South Africa and South West Africa (now Namibia)—both then under the apartheid racial system."
- Namibian Sun, 30 August 2025: "How Namibia shaped Silicon Valley's most contrarian mind" — confirms Klaus Thiel worked at Rössing uranium mine and Peter attended German school in Swakopmund.
- *New Internationalist* "Hall of Infamy: Peter Thiel," 4 March 2026.
- Jim Stewartson, "Peter Thiel's Radioactive Childhood," 4 April 2025.
- Johnny Vedmore, "Schwab and Thiel – Nuclear Apartheid," 1 October 2025.

## Stanford and ideological formation

- **Stanford University**: undergraduate philosophy 1985-89, J.D. from Stanford Law School 1992.
- **The Stanford Review**: founded by Thiel in 1987 with funding from the Olin Foundation and the Collegiate Network. The Review was the principal Stanford right-wing student publication and became the recruiting ground for what would become the PayPal Mafia. Subsequent Review editors include David O. Sacks (now Trump's AI and Crypto Czar) and Keith Rabois (Founders Fund, OpenStore).
- **René Girard**: French Stanford literary theorist whose theory of "mimetic desire" — that humans copy each other's desires — has been Thiel's principal philosophical influence. Thiel attended Girard's seminars at Stanford in the late 1980s and has cited Girard's work in every long-form interview since.
- **Opus Dei**: the Catholic conservative organisation maintained a presence at Stanford in the 1980s through Arne Panula, the priest who directed the Opus Dei University House at Stanford. Thiel was a regular attendee (Jim Stewartson reporting; confirmed by *The Atlantic* profile 2023).

## *The Diversity Myth* (1995)

- Co-authored with **David O. Sacks**. Published by the Independent Institute, 1995, then re-issued 1996.
- Subtitle: *"Multiculturalism" and the Politics of Intolerance at Stanford* (1995 edition); later editions: *Multiculturalism and Political Intolerance on Campus*.
- Foreword by Elizabeth Fox-Genovese.
- **The Rabois incident**: the book contains a defence of Keith Rabois (then a Stanford Law student, now a long-time member of Thiel's circle) for shouting "Faggot! Faggot! Hope you die of AIDS!" at a Stanford resident assistant outside the dorm where the RA lived. Thiel and Sacks framed Rabois's outburst as legitimate political speech and treated the university's response as evidence of "political correctness."
- **The date rape passages**: the book includes a chapter casting doubt on the credibility of women who report rape after intoxication. The passages discuss a specific 1991 Stanford case in which a 17-year-old freshman was raped while intoxicated; Thiel and Sacks wrote that "[a]lthough [the alleged perpetrator] was clearly guilty of serving alcohol to an underage woman and taking advantage of her resulting lack of judgement, there was no sexual assault." A separate passage: "But since a multicultural rape charge may indicate nothing more than belated regret, a woman might 'realize' that she had been 'raped' the next day or even many days later. Under these circumstances, it is unclear who should be held responsible. If the alcohol made both of them do it, then why should the woman's consent be obviated any more than the man's? Why is all blame placed on the man?"
- **Thiel's 2016 retraction** (provided to Ryan Mac of Forbes after the passages were unearthed by Julie Carrie Wong of *The Guardian*): "More than two decades ago, I co-wrote a book with several insensitive, crudely argued statements." Sacks issued a similar statement.

## Confinity / PayPal

- **Confinity** founded December 1998 by Thiel and Max Levchin. Initial product: cryptography for handheld devices. Pivoted to payments.
- **Confinity merged with X.com** (Elon Musk's online bank) in March 2000 to form what was renamed **PayPal** later that year.
- **Thiel was CEO** of PayPal from October 2000.
- **eBay acquired PayPal** in October 2002 for $1.5 billion. Thiel personally received $55 million from the sale, becoming the first significant Silicon Valley tech wealth of his cohort.
- **The PayPal Mafia** is the network of Confinity/X.com/PayPal alumni who, after the eBay sale, used their proceeds to fund the next generation of Silicon Valley companies: Musk (Tesla, SpaceX), Hoffman (LinkedIn), Levchin (Affirm, Yelp), Sacks (Yammer, Craft), Botha (Sequoia), Chen-Hurley-Karim (YouTube), Rabois, Howery, Nosek, McClure. The network has consistently maintained, through to the present, the role of a coordinated investment-and-political circle. Their joint investment vehicle, **Founders Fund**, was established by Thiel in 2005.

## Palantir Technologies

- **Founded 2003** by Thiel with Alex Karp (Thiel's Stanford Law School classmate, then living in Frankfurt as a PhD philosophy student), Nathan Gettings (PayPal engineer), Joe Lonsdale (Stanford undergraduate at the time, *Stanford Review* editor), Stephen Cohen (Stanford undergraduate).
- **Initial funding**: Thiel personally bankrolled $30 million of the initial capital. **In-Q-Tel**, the venture capital arm of the Central Intelligence Agency (established 1999 by CIA Director George Tenet), invested $2 million in 2004 — Palantir's first external institutional investment.
- **Karp installed as CEO** in 2004.
- **Founding mission** (per Karp and Thiel public statements): "defending the West." Palantir's first major contract was with the CIA, where its software replaced the British i2 Analyst's Notebook as the agency's principal analytics platform.
- **Subsequent contracts**: FBI, NSA, US Army, ICE (Immigration and Customs Enforcement, where Palantir's Falcon and Investigative Case Management systems support deportation enforcement), Department of Defense, US Special Operations Command, Israeli Defense Forces (where Palantir's systems are integrated with the IDF's targeting infrastructure including the Lavender and Gospel programmes used in Gaza), and the UK NHS (Federated Data Platform, £330M contract awarded November 2023).
- **Post-Trump-Vance growth**: Palantir's federal contracts doubled in the first year of the second Trump administration — from $541 million in 2024 to over $970 million by mid-2025. The US Army contract has a $10 billion ceiling.
- **Palantir as the technical foundation of the new order**: by 2026, Palantir is the principal data-fusion platform integrating federal-agency data across HHS, IRS, SSA, DHS, and the intelligence community. Critics have described the integration as the technical infrastructure of a domestic-surveillance state. The integration is being executed under Trump-administration executive orders (the March 2025 EO on "data sharing").

## Founders Fund and Facebook

- **Founders Fund**: founded 2005 by Thiel, Ken Howery, and Luke Nosek. Now manages ~$11 billion across multiple funds. Principal positions include SpaceX, Palantir, Stripe, Anduril, Airbnb (early stage), and (historically) Facebook.
- **Facebook investment, August 2004**: Thiel was the first outside investor in Facebook, investing $500,000 for 10.2 percent of the company (becoming the first member of the board after the Zuckerbergs themselves). Thiel sold most of his Facebook stake between 2012 (post-IPO) and 2017, realising $1 billion in total returns from the original $500,000 investment. Thiel remained on the Facebook (now Meta) board until February 2022.

## The Cato 2009 essay

- **"The Education of a Libertarian"**, *Cato Unbound*, 13 April 2009.
- The opening sentence: "I remain committed to the faith of my teenage years: to authentic human freedom as a precondition for the highest good. I stand against confiscatory taxes, totalitarian collectives, and the ideology of the inevitability of the death of every individual. For all these reasons, I still call myself 'libertarian.' But I must confess that over the last two decades, **I have changed radically on the question of how to achieve these goals. Most importantly, I no longer believe that freedom and democracy are compatible.**"
- The women-and-welfare passage: "The 1920s were the last decade in American history during which one could be genuinely optimistic about politics. Since 1920, **the vast increase in welfare beneficiaries and the extension of the franchise to women — two constituencies that are notoriously tough for libertarians — have rendered the notion of 'capitalist democracy' into an oxymoron.**"
- Editor's follow-up: after public backlash, Thiel issued a clarifying note that Cato published alongside the original: "While I don't think any class of people should be disenfranchised, I have little hope that voting will make things better."
- Thiel's prescription in the essay: libertarians should "escape" politics rather than reform it, via three "frontiers" — cyberspace, outer space, and seasteading. Thiel funded all three: he co-founded PayPal (the cyber-currency project), he was the early major outside investor in SpaceX (Musk's space-launch company), and he funded the Seasteading Institute (founded by Milton Friedman's grandson Patri Friedman in 2008, with seed capital from Thiel).

## Curtis Yarvin / Mencius Moldbug

- **Curtis Guy Yarvin** (born 1973): software engineer, writer under the pen name "Mencius Moldbug." Founder of the **Dark Enlightenment** / **neo-reactionary (NRx)** movement.
- Wrote the blog *Unqualified Reservations* from 2007 to 2014.
- Core doctrine: "neocameralism" — the proposal that nation-states should be reorganised as joint-stock corporations, with the country's territory and population as the corporation's assets and a single "CEO" sovereign in place of elected government. Yarvin has explicitly argued for replacing American democracy with this corporate-monarchical structure, with the President as "CEO" with absolute executive authority. He has also written favourably about historical authoritarian models (Frederick the Great, Napoleon III, Singapore under Lee Kuan Yew).
- **Thiel funded Yarvin's startup Tlon** (the corporate vehicle for the Urbit decentralised computing project) through Founders Fund, beginning 2013. Thiel also personally gave $100,000 to Tlon co-founder John Burnham in 2011.
- **Yarvin's claim to be "coaching" Thiel**: per BuzzFeed reporting on Milo Yiannopoulos's leaked emails, Yarvin told Yiannopoulos in mid-2016: "I watched the election at his house, I think my hangover lasted into Tuesday. **He's fully enlightened, just plays it very carefully.**" Yarvin has consistently described Thiel as among his most important political-ideological interlocutors. Max Chafkin, in his 2021 Thiel biography *The Contrarian*, described Yarvin as the "house political philosopher" for Thiel's circle.
- **Yarvin in the second Trump administration**: attended Trump's inaugural gala on 20 January 2025 as, per Politico's reporting, an "informal guest of honor" because of his "outsize influence over the Trumpian right." Has claimed to have given staffing recommendations to **Michael Anton**, the State Department Director of Policy Planning in the second Trump administration. JD Vance discussed Yarvin's influence on his thinking in a July 2024 podcast interview.
- **Andreessen connection**: Marc Andreessen has called Yarvin a "friend," invested in Tlon, and urged people to read Yarvin's work.
- **The structural significance**: Yarvin's neocameralism doctrine — that the United States should be operated as a corporation under a CEO-sovereign — is the doctrine being operationalised by the Trump-Musk-Vance administration. The DOGE programme (Musk, January-May 2025) was an explicit attempt to apply the corporate-restructuring model to the federal government, including the elimination of 220,000 federal positions, the dismantling of USAID, and the consolidation of federal data systems on Palantir infrastructure. The doctrine is now being applied in practice.

## The Gawker case

- **2007**: Gawker (specifically Owen Thomas at the affiliated Valleywag) published an article headlined "Peter Thiel is totally gay, people," outing Thiel. The article was accurate; Thiel has not contested its factual claims and subsequently became publicly out, including speaking at the 2016 Republican National Convention as the first openly gay convention speaker since the 2000 Jim Kolbe speech.
- **2012**: Gawker's A.J. Daulerio published a two-minute extract from a 30-minute sex tape of Terry Bollea (Hulk Hogan) without Bollea's consent.
- **2013**: Bollea filed *Bollea v. Gawker* in Florida state court. Bollea's lawyer was Charles Harder of Los Angeles.
- **The secret funding**: **Thiel secretly funded the lawsuit** with $10 million in legal expenses. The arrangement was brokered through Australian businessman Aron D'Souza, who had been a Thiel associate since 2011. The funding was not disclosed to Gawker's lawyers or to the court. Forbes magazine (Ryan Mac) revealed Thiel's role in May 2016.
- **March 2016**: jury awarded Bollea $140.1 million ($115M compensatory, $25M punitive).
- **June 2016**: Gawker Media filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy, unable to post the appeal bond. Nick Denton personally filed for bankruptcy in August 2016. Gawker.com was shut down. Univision purchased the remaining Gawker properties (Deadspin, Jezebel, Gizmodo, Kotaku, Lifehacker) for $135 million.
- **The precedent**: Thiel's secret funding of a third-party lawsuit to bankrupt a media organisation that had previously written about him established the template that has been used multiple times since (Mike Lindell-funded suits, various billionaire-funded suits against critical media outlets, the Dominion v. Fox News framework). The chilling effect on American investigative journalism is now and documented in successive Knight Foundation and Reporters Without Borders annual reports.

## Trump 2016 endorsement and the $1.25M post-*Access Hollywood* donation

- **2016 Republican National Convention** (Cleveland, 21 July 2016): Thiel spoke from the convention stage, explicitly endorsing Trump and identifying himself as a "proud" gay Republican.
- **8 October 2016**: the *Access Hollywood* tape was released, showing Trump bragging about sexual assault. Several major donors withdrew or paused support.
- **15 October 2016**: Thiel donated $1.25 million to Trump-affiliated PACs and the Trump campaign — a public show of support specifically timed to counter the *Access Hollywood* fallout.
- **Trump transition role**: Thiel was a member of Trump's transition executive committee (November 2016 onward). Per Trump's own subsequent statements, Thiel chose which technology CEOs would be invited to the December 2016 Trump Tower meeting (the so-called "Tech Summit"). The invitees: Tim Cook (Apple), Larry Page (Alphabet), Sheryl Sandberg (Facebook), Jeff Bezos (Amazon), Satya Nadella (Microsoft), Ginni Rometty (IBM), Safra Catz (Oracle), Elon Musk (Tesla/SpaceX), Brian Krzanich (Intel), Chuck Robbins (Cisco), Eric Schmidt (Alphabet), Alex Karp (Palantir).

## The JD Vance pipeline

- **2011**: Thiel speaks at Yale Law School. **JD Vance**, then a Yale Law student, attends. Vance later credits Thiel's talk with "steering me away from a career in big law and instead pursuing entrepreneurship."
- **2014-2016**: Vance graduates Yale Law, briefly works at Sidley Austin, drafts *Hillbilly Elegy* (published June 2016).
- **2016**: Thiel hires Vance at **Mithril Capital** (Thiel's global venture capital firm). Vance reportedly rarely appears at the Mithril office; spends most of his time promoting *Hillbilly Elegy*.
- **2017**: Vance leaves Mithril for Steve Case's Revolution.
- **2019**: Vance co-founds **Narya Capital** in Cincinnati, Ohio, with backing from **Thiel, Eric Schmidt, and Marc Andreessen**. Thiel provided at least 15 percent of the initial capital.
- **2019**: Vance converts to Roman Catholicism, with Thiel as principal influence (Vance's published account). Vance's previous private description of Trump as "America's Hitler" is later disclosed.
- **February 2021**: Thiel personally introduces Vance to Trump at Mar-a-Lago.
- **March 2021**: Thiel donates $10 million to **Protect Ohio Values** (a super PAC supporting Vance's potential Senate run).
- **July 2021**: Vance announces Senate candidacy.
- **2022**: Thiel's total contributions to Vance's Senate campaign reach **$15 million** — the largest single-donor contribution to any individual Senate campaign in American history at that point. (Thiel separately donated $20 million to Blake Masters's Arizona Senate run; Masters lost.)
- **3 May 2022**: Vance wins Republican primary with 32 percent.
- **8 November 2022**: Vance wins Senate seat, defeating Tim Ryan 53-47.
- **2024**: Trump's vice-presidential selection process. Per *New York Times* reporting, **Thiel privately lobbied Trump for Vance**, with Musk, David Sacks, and Tucker Carlson also pressing for the selection. Trump's sons Donald Jr. and Eric also advocated Vance. Trump announced Vance as VP nominee 15 July 2024.
- **20 January 2025**: Vance sworn in as 50th Vice President of the United States.

The JD Vance career — from Yale Law student in 2011 to Vice President of the United States in 2025 — was funded, mentored, networked, introduced to the principal, sponsored at every electoral stage, and lobbied into the VP slot by a single man: Peter Thiel. The biography of the sitting Vice President is, in every operational respect, an extension of the biography of the man this chapter is about.

## The 2024 disengagement (apparent)

- Thiel told *The Atlantic* (Barton Gellman, March 2024) that he had decided not to donate directly to Trump's 2024 campaign and was disappointed in Trump's first-term performance. The Atlantic interview was widely reported as Thiel "stepping back" from political donations.
- This apparent disengagement is, on the record, hollow. While Thiel did not directly fund the Trump 2024 campaign, his indirect influence operated through:
  - **JD Vance**'s VP candidacy (Thiel-sponsored).
  - **David Sacks** as principal Trump fundraiser within Silicon Valley (Thiel's PayPal Mafia colleague since 1998).
  - **Marc Andreessen** as principal organiser of the Silicon Valley pro-Trump shift (Thiel's long-standing investment partner).
  - **Continued Founders Fund and Palantir activity** that benefits directly from Trump-administration contracts.
- The pattern is the standard pattern of senior figures: nominal disengagement combined with operational continuation through the network. Thiel's net worth has grown by $20 billion since the 2024 election, primarily through the Palantir share-price appreciation. His structural position has strengthened, not weakened.

All claims verified 12 May 2026.

## Palantir Technologies — Board of Directors (current, 2025)

The Palantir board, per the company's April 2025 proxy statement (DEF 14A, SEC filing):

- **Peter Thiel** — Chairman of the Board. President, Thiel Capital LLC. Partner, Founders Fund LLC.
- **Alexander C. Karp** — CEO and Co-Founder.
- **Stephen Cohen** — President, Secretary, and Co-Founder.
- **Alexander Moore** — Partner at 8VC (Joe Lonsdale's firm).
- **Alexandra Wolfe Schiff** — Freelance writer and journalist; daughter of novelist Tom Wolfe; former Wall Street Journal reporter (2004-2005, 2013-2020); joined Palantir board July 2020 immediately upon resigning from WSJ.
- **Lauren Friedman Stat** — Chief Executive Officer of Stat and Co LLC; previously Chairwoman of the Board, Valkyrie Intelligence LLC; previously CEO of Figa Jewelry.
- **Eric Woersching** — Private consultant for early-stage software companies; former General Catalyst partner.
- **Spencer Rascoff** — Zillow co-founder; joined the board June 2020; subsequently departed (no longer listed on current proxy).

## Palantir voting structure — the Founder Voting Trust

Per the 2025 proxy filing: Palantir has a three-class share structure (Class A, Class B, Class F). All Class F supervoting shares — which carry 49.999999% of the total voting power on most matters — are held in a Founder Voting Trust established by Peter Thiel, Alex Karp, and Stephen Cohen, with Wilmington Trust as trustee. This structure guarantees that the three Founders retain nearly 50% of all voting power on the company, regardless of how many of their shares they personally sell. Investors who buy Palantir stock are buying a non-voting (or near-non-voting) share of a company whose actual control is held in trust by three private individuals.

## Palantir senior executives (non-board)

- **Shyam Sankar** — Chief Technology Officer and Executive Vice President. Employee #13 (joined 2006). Cornell BS Electrical Engineering, Stanford MS Management Science. Now also Chairman of Ginkgo Bioworks. Trustee at Hudson Institute. Has testified before the House Select Committee on Strategic Competition with the Chinese Communist Party and the Senate Armed Services Subcommittee on Cybersecurity. Became Palantir's fifth billionaire in July 2025; net worth $1.3B per Bloomberg. The first non-founder Palantir employee to reach billionaire status.
- **David Glazer** — CFO and Treasurer (since 2013). Santa Clara BA History, Emory JD.
- **Ryan Taylor** — Chief Revenue Officer and Chief Legal Officer. Stanford MS Management Science.

Subsequently founded Formation 8 (with Brian Koo), then **8VC** (~$5 billion AUM as of 2025). Major Republican political donor. Founded the right-wing think tank Cicero Institute. Lonsdale's 8VC partner Alexander Moore now sits on Palantir's board.
- **Nathan Gettings** — Co-founder. PayPal engineer originally. Left for robotics ventures.
