## Mithril Capital Management

Founded 2012 by Thiel and **Ajay Royan**. Thiel's "global" venture vehicle (distinct from Founders Fund's US focus). Roughly $1 billion AUM at peak; much smaller now after the 2019-2022 restructuring.

- **Ajay Royan** — Managing Partner and co-founder. Yale undergraduate; previously at Royal Bank of Canada. Royan and Thiel relocated Mithril from San Francisco to Austin, Texas, in 2018-2019 — a relocation that produced a 2019 lawsuit from former Mithril partner Crystal McKellar (who alleged improper handling of the relocation and treatment of investors). The McKellar suit was settled out of court.
- **Crystal McKellar** — Former Mithril partner; Yale Law graduate; sister of the actor Danica McKellar. Filed the 2019 lawsuit against Mithril. Now an independent investor; her subsequent public comments about JD Vance's brief Mithril tenure (that he was "rarely in the office") have been part of the WSJ and Fortune reporting on the Vance career.
- **JD Vance** — Mithril partner 2016-2017. Now Vice President of the United States.

## Anduril Industries — co-founders and leadership

Founded April 2017. Anduril is, after Palantir, the most operationally consequential company in the Thiel-Founders Fund portfolio. By 2026 it is a defence-technology firm valued at $30 billion and is on a $1+ billion annual revenue run rate, with major Pentagon contracts including the Project Maven AI targeting contract, the Air Force Collaborative Combat Aircraft programme, and a $20 billion 10-year Army contract awarded in 2025.

Co-founders (5):

- **Palmer Luckey** — Founder of Oculus VR (sold to Facebook 2014 for $2 billion). Left Facebook March 2017 alleging he was fired for his pro-Trump political contributions. Subsequently donated $100,000 to Trump's 2017 inauguration. Has donated $670,000+ to Republican congressional campaigns. Known for Hawaiian shirts and his estate on Catalina Island. Personally piloted the Eagle Eye helmet — an Anduril prototype combining "superhuman" vision and hearing capabilities.
- **Trae Stephens** — Executive Chairman. Born November 1983, rural Ohio. Georgetown School of Foreign Service (Arabic and Security Studies). Computational linguist in the **US Intelligence Community** after 9/11, building Arabic/Persian name-matching systems. Worked in then-Congressman Rob Portman's office (Portman being the Ohio senator whose seat JD Vance would subsequently fill). Worked at the Embassy of Afghanistan in Washington immediately after the installation of Hamid Karzai's transitional government. Joined Palantir 2008 as a forward-deployed engineer; recruited to Founders Fund by Thiel in 2013/2014. Appointed to Trump transition team (Defense) November 2016. Served on the **Defense Innovation Board** under Eric Schmidt's chairmanship. Devout Christian; his wife **Michelle Stephens** founded **ACTS17**, a Christian nonprofit "ministering to elites" that has funded Thiel's 2025 lectures on the antichrist and events featuring Garry Tan (Y Combinator) and Pat Gelsinger (former Intel CEO). Became a billionaire June 2025 after Anduril's Series G. Co-founded **Valinor Enterprises** in 2024 — a "company of companies" with former Palantir SVP Julie Bush, General Catalyst's Paul Kwan, and Red Cell Partners' Grant Verstandig.
- **Matt Grimm** — Co-Founder and COO. Cornell BS Mechanical/Aerospace Engineering and MEng Systems Engineering. Co-built Cornell's autonomous-vehicle entry to the DARPA Grand Challenge (with future co-founder Brian Schimpf).
- **Brian Schimpf** — Co-Founder and CEO. Cornell engineering; former Palantir Director of Engineering. The fifth co-founder, recruited after the initial pitch.
- **Joe Chen** — Co-Founder.

## The Defense Innovation Board (DIB) bridge

The Defense Innovation Board was established under Defense Secretary Ash Carter in 2016 with Eric Schmidt (then Alphabet chairman, subject of a later chapter) as founding chair. The DIB's principal effect was to construct the institutional bridge by which Silicon Valley defence-technology startups received Pentagon procurement preference. Trae Stephens served on the DIB simultaneously with his roles at Founders Fund and Anduril — an arrangement that, by ordinary standards of conflict of interest, would have been precluded but that in operational practice was the explicit design feature. The Founders Fund partner whose portfolio company depends on Pentagon contracts was advising the Pentagon on procurement reform. The arrangement is consistent across the network. Schmidt similarly chaired the National Security Commission on Artificial Intelligence (NSCAI, 2018-2021) while his various AI investments were positioned to benefit from the resulting policy recommendations.

## Other principal Thiel-network lieutenants

### Blake Masters

- Stanford Law graduate (2012); Thiel's co-author on the 2014 book *Zero to One: Notes on Startups, or How to Build the Future* (the book is, structurally, Masters's classroom notes from Thiel's Stanford "Computer Science 183: Startup" course, edited and supplemented by Thiel).
- Chief Operating Officer of Thiel Capital and Thiel Foundation, 2015-2022.
- Senate candidacy 2022 in Arizona, funded by **$20 million** in Thiel money — Thiel's second major candidate sponsorship after Vance. Masters lost to Mark Kelly (D) 51.4% to 46.5%.
- Ran for the US House in Arizona 2024; lost the primary.
- Current status: out of electoral politics, returned to investment activity.

### Curtis Yarvin (Mencius Moldbug)

Already covered in the main chapter.

### Patri Friedman

- Co-founder, **Seasteading Institute** (2008). Thiel was the seed funder and continued backer.
- Milton Friedman's grandson; David Friedman's son. The grandfather Milton was the Mont Pèlerin Society principal Chapter 6 documented as the foundational figure of post-1947 neoliberalism. The father David is the author of *The Machinery of Freedom* (1973), the anarcho-capitalist classic. Patri Friedman is now the principal of the **Pronomos Capital** charter-cities investment vehicle, also Thiel-funded.

### Aron Ping D'Souza

- Australian lawyer, based in London.
- Thiel's intermediary for the 2013-2016 Gawker bankruptcy litigation funding.
- Founder and president (since 2023) of **The Enhanced Games** — a pro-doping athletic competition scheduled for Las Vegas, 21-24 May 2026, where performance-enhancing drugs are permitted under medical supervision. Backed by Thiel, German biotech billionaire **Christian Angermayer**, **Balaji Srinivasan**, and (since February 2025) Donald Trump Jr. through **1789 Capital** (a Trump Jr.-aligned fund with Omeed Malik and Chris Buskirk).
- The Enhanced Games is structurally an attempt to use elite athletic competition as a marketing demonstration for a subsequent pharmaceutical-enhancement business model. The Wired report (citing Angermayer) confirmed the strategic objective: "AI will create more leisure time and boost demand for sports entertainment" combined with the development of "performance-enhancing drugs to the largest pharmaceutical market in history."
- Co-founded the Australian financial services company Sargon in 2015 with Phillip Kingston; Sargon collapsed into administration in 2020 amid liquidity and governance scandals.

### Christian Angermayer

- German biotech billionaire. Founded Atai Life Sciences (psychedelic-medicine company; IPO'd 2021). Thiel introduced D'Souza to Angermayer around the Enhanced Games project. Angermayer has co-invested with Thiel in multiple ventures (Enhanced Games, Compass Pathways, and the broader Thiel-aligned biotech portfolio).

### Aubrey de Grey

- British biogerontologist. Founder of the Methuselah Foundation (longevity research). Recipient of $3.5 million in Thiel funding between 2009 and the present. De Grey's claim that the first person to live to 1,000 years old may already be 60 has been a recurring talking point of Thiel's at conferences.

### Mike Solana

- Founder of *Pirate Wires* (right-wing tech-political online publication). Chief Marketing Officer at Founders Fund. The principal house publication of the Thiel-aligned tech right; Solana's editorial direction shapes how the Thiel network's positions are communicated to its principal audience. Notable for the publication's hostility to the *New York Times*, to "woke" institutions, and to most mainstream press coverage of Silicon Valley.

### Garry Tan

- President and CEO of Y Combinator (since 2023). Not formally a Thiel lieutenant but operationally aligned. Major Republican political donor (~$1 million to a San Francisco mayoral-recall PAC in 2024). Tan's Y Combinator has, since 2023, dramatically shifted its political orientation rightward, with several Thiel-network figures (including Yarvin) featured at its events. Has been the subject of multiple controversies for posting threats against San Francisco progressive city supervisors on X (December 2023).

## Founder Voting Trust and Class F structure — the broader pattern

The Palantir Class F supervoting structure that protects Thiel, Karp, and Cohen's combined voting control is not unique to Palantir; it is the explicit governance template that several other Thiel-portfolio companies have adopted. Anduril's governance documents are private (the company has not gone public) but reportedly include similar founder-protection provisions. SpaceX's structure protects Musk's control similarly. Meta's Class B supervoting structure protects Zuckerberg's control on the same model. The pattern is the same: the public market provides capital; the founders retain unaccountable governance control through a class of supervoting shares. The market discipline that the orthodox theory of public-company governance is supposed to provide — the threat that shareholders dissatisfied with management can vote them out — does not operate against any of these firms. The companies operate, in effect, as private fiefdoms of their founders with public-market capital.

# Sources — Chapter 12: Elon Musk

All claims verified 12 May 2026.

## Family and ancestral context

- **Elon Reeve Musk**: born 28 June 1971, Pretoria, Transvaal province, Union of South Africa.
- **Father**: Errol Graham Musk (born 25 May 1946, Pretoria). Electromechanical engineer, University of Pretoria. Property developer. Pretoria City Councillor (Independent) 1972-1980; Progressive Federal Party member 1980-1983 (resigned over tricameral parliament referendum). Engineering business included "office buildings, retail complexes, residential subdivisions, and an air force base" for the apartheid government. By time of 1979 divorce, owned two homes, a yacht, a plane, five luxury cars, a truck. In 1986 acquired rights to output from three Zambian emerald mines; Errol's own words (Walter Isaacson biography, 2023): "If you registered it, you would wind up with nothing, because the Blacks would take everything from you." In November 2025 CNN documentary with Donie O'Sullivan: said US is "doomed" because non-white majority coming within 20 years, "You want to go back to the jungle?", denied blacks were oppressed under apartheid, credited whites with "feeding" the black population.
- **Errol's second marriage and stepdaughter relationship**: In early 1990s, Errol (then 45) married Heide Bezuidenhout (then 25). Heide's daughter from a previous relationship, **Jana Bezuidenhout**, was 4 years old at the time. Errol subsequently entered into a romantic relationship with Jana when she was an adult, and fathered two children with his own stepdaughter. The relationship is documented in multiple interviews Errol has himself given to Sunday Times (London) and other outlets.
- **Mother**: Maye Musk (née Haldeman; born 19 April 1948, Saskatchewan, Canada). Model since the 1960s; covers of Time (health edition), Women's Day, international editions of Vogue, Sports Illustrated Swimsuit Issue. Registered dietitian. Holds Canadian, South African, and American citizenship. Published 2019 memoir *A Woman Makes a Plan*. In her memoir characterised her marriage to Errol as abusive and described an incident in which Errol came to a neighbour's house with a knife looking for her during the divorce. Lived with the three children primarily after the 1979 divorce; Elon moved to live with Errol around age 10.
- **Maternal grandfather: Joshua Norman Haldeman** (1902-1974). American-born Canadian chiropractor and political activist. Headed the **Canadian branch of the Technocracy Movement** (the movement was outlawed in Canada during the Second World War; Haldeman was its national chairman). Ran for the Canadian Parliament on the **Social Credit Party** ticket. After the war, having been politically marginalised in Canada, emigrated to South Africa in **1950** — that is, two years after the formal establishment of apartheid by the National Party. Over his life, **"publicly expressed racist, antisemitic, and anti-democratic views, promoted a number of conspiracy theories, and was a supporter of South Africa's apartheid system"** (Wikipedia consolidation of multiple biographical sources). Specific documented positions: opposed democratic government, supported apartheid as a model, promoted antisemitic conspiracy theories about "international Jewry," supported the Social Credit Party's antisemitic founder William Aberhart. Spent time searching for the mythical "Lost City of the Kalahari" by aircraft. Died 1974 when Elon was three.
- **Maternal grandmother: Winnifred "Wyn" Fletcher Haldeman** (1914-2012). Born Moose Jaw, Canada, to English parents. Per **Errol Musk's own statement to biographers**: Wyn and Joshua Haldeman were **"fanatical" in favour of apartheid, and supported Nazism**. She told Elon many stories of his grandfather's exploits in his childhood, including slideshows of Joshua's South African and African travels.
- **Siblings**: **Kimbal James Musk** (born 20 September 1972, Pretoria) — restaurateur, sits on Tesla and SpaceX boards, co-founded Zip2 with Elon. **Tosca Musk** (born 1974) — filmmaker.
- **Maye Musk's siblings included Scott Haldeman**, a chiropractor; **Lyndon Rive** (cousin), co-founder of SolarCity.

## Education and emigration

- Schooled at Waterkloof House Preparatory School, then **Bryanston High School** (Johannesburg area), then **Pretoria Boys' High School** (final years). Bullied severely as a child — Elon has described being hospitalised after being thrown down a flight of stairs.
- 1989: aged 17, emigrated to **Canada** using his mother's Canadian citizenship, partly to avoid mandatory South African Defence Force conscription (which would have meant serving in the apartheid military). Initially lived with maternal relatives in Saskatchewan.
- **Queen's University** (Kingston, Ontario), 1990-1992 — two years.
- Transferred to **University of Pennsylvania**, completed dual bachelor's degrees: BS in physics (College of Arts and Sciences) and BS in economics (Wharton School). Graduated 1995 (some sources say 1997 due to delayed conferral; Musk's official graduation is 1995).
- **Stanford PhD in materials science / applied physics**: enrolled August 1995, dropped out after **two days** to pursue Zip2.

## Zip2 (1995-1999)

- Founded December 1995 with brother **Kimbal Musk** (and initial investor Greg Kouri) as "Global Link Information Network," providing online city guides to newspapers. Renamed Zip2.
- Initial funding included ~$28,000 from Errol Musk (per Elon's own 2019 statement, "10% of a ~$200k angel funding round").
- 1996: $3 million Series A investment from Mohr Davidow Ventures. The VC investment displaced Elon as CEO; he became CTO. The displacement was reportedly a source of significant frustration to Elon and is one of the documented sources for his subsequent insistence on retaining controlling positions in his ventures.
- February 1999: Compaq acquired Zip2 for **$307 million** cash. Elon received **$22 million** (gross, before taxes), Kimbal received $15 million.

## X.com and PayPal (1999-2002)

- March 1999: Elon used $12 million from the Zip2 sale to co-found **X.com**, an online bank, with Harris Fricker, Christopher Payne, and Ed Ho.
- March 2000: X.com merged with **Confinity** (Peter Thiel and Max Levchin's company). Combined entity initially used X.com brand with Elon as CEO.
- **September 2000**: while Elon was on a delayed honeymoon flight to Australia with his first wife Justine Wilson, the board (led by Thiel and Levchin) voted to remove him as CEO. Thiel was installed in his place. The company was renamed **PayPal**.
- October 2002: eBay acquired PayPal for $1.5 billion. Elon (as the largest individual shareholder) received **$175 million** gross.

## Tesla (2003-present) — the founding fiction

- **Tesla Motors was incorporated 1 July 2003 in San Carlos, California, by Martin Eberhard and Marc Tarpenning**. Eberhard was CEO, Tarpenning was CFO. **Elon Musk was not a founder.**
- February 2004: Musk led Series A funding round, contributing $6.5 million of the $7.5 million round. Became **Chairman of the Board**. JB Straubel joined May 2004 as the fifth employee.
- November 2007: Musk pushed Eberhard out as CEO. The Fortune December 2007 reporting documented that "chairman Elon Musk had asked Eberhard to leave."
- 2008: Musk publicly began describing himself as a "founder" of Tesla.
- **June 2009**: **Martin Eberhard sued Musk** for libel, slander, and breach of contract, alleging Musk pushed him out and publicly disparaged him.
- **September 2009**: lawsuit settled. **As a condition of the settlement, Musk, JB Straubel, and Ian Wright were granted the right to call themselves "co-founders"** of Tesla, in addition to the actual founders Eberhard and Tarpenning. **Musk's "co-founder" status is, in legal substance, a settlement term in a defamation lawsuit, not a historical fact.**
- 2008: Tesla nearly collapsed financially. The company was saved by a combination of:
  - The **NASA Commercial Resupply Services contract** ($1.6 billion to SpaceX) awarded 23 December 2008, which gave Musk the personal liquidity to continue funding Tesla.
  - **Daimler's $50 million investment** in May 2009.
  - The **US Department of Energy Advanced Technology Vehicles Manufacturing loan** of $465 million in June 2009.
  - The Tesla IPO of June 2010.
- August 2018: Musk's "funding secured" tweet (claiming he had secured private funding to take Tesla private at $420/share). The funding was not secured. The **SEC filed civil fraud charges** September 2018. Musk settled, paid $20 million personal fine, Tesla paid additional $20 million fine, Musk **lost board chairmanship** for three years (Robyn Denholm became Chair).
- The SolarCity acquisition (2016): Tesla acquired SolarCity (founded by Musk's cousins Lyndon and Peter Rive). Multiple shareholder lawsuits alleged the acquisition was a Musk-family-bailout of a failing company at shareholder expense. Court rulings have been mixed; the Delaware Chancery Court ruled in Musk's favour in 2022.

## SpaceX (2002-present)

- **Space Exploration Technologies Corporation** founded May 2002 in El Segundo, California, by Elon Musk with $100 million of his PayPal-sale proceeds. Musk is sole founder of SpaceX (unlike Tesla).
- 2006-2008: Three Falcon 1 launch failures (March 2006, March 2007, August 2008).
- 28 September 2008: First successful Falcon 1 launch (fourth attempt).
- **23 December 2008**: NASA awarded SpaceX the **$1.6 billion Commercial Resupply Services contract** — the contract that saved both SpaceX and (indirectly) Tesla. Without this contract, awarded by NASA Administrator Mike Griffin in the final days of the George W. Bush administration, SpaceX would have been bankrupt within months.
- Subsequent contracts: Crew Dragon (NASA, $2.6 billion 2014), Lunar Starship (NASA, $2.9 billion 2021, increased to $4.2 billion 2024). Total US government contracts to SpaceX through 2024 exceeded **$22 billion** (Washington Post analysis January 2025).
- **Starlink** (2019-present): satellite internet constellation. ~7,000 active satellites in low Earth orbit by mid-2025 — the largest satellite constellation in human history, 60 percent of all active satellites. The constellation has been operationally deployed in the Russo-Ukrainian war (Musk has personally restricted Ukrainian use of Starlink in specific tactical situations, including the Crimean Bridge operation September 2022 — documented in CNN reporting September 2023).
- **Starshield**: military variant of Starlink, $1.8 billion classified contract with the National Reconnaissance Office disclosed 2024.

## Twitter / X (October 2022 - present)

- April 2022: Musk offered to buy Twitter at $54.20 per share, total ~$44 billion.
- Musk subsequently attempted to back out of the deal; Twitter sued in Delaware Chancery Court.
- October 2022: Musk completed the purchase under court pressure. Financing included $13 billion of bank debt (Morgan Stanley, Bank of America, Barclays, MUFG, Société Générale, Mizuho, BNP Paribas — the banks have subsequently been unable to syndicate the debt and are sitting on losses), $33.5 billion of equity (Musk's personal capital plus co-investors including the Saudi Public Investment Fund's Prince Alwaleed bin Talal who rolled over his existing 5% stake, Larry Ellison who invested $1 billion, Sequoia Capital, Andreessen Horowitz, Binance, and Qatar Holding).
- Within weeks of acquisition: laid off ~75 percent of Twitter staff (6,000 of 8,000 employees).
- Subsequent decisions: dissolved the Trust and Safety Council; reinstated previously banned accounts including Donald Trump; replaced the verified-blue-check system with a paid model; relaxed content moderation across the platform.
- March 2025: X Corp merged into xAI in an all-stock transaction valuing X at $33 billion and xAI at $80 billion (the combined entity at $113 billion). The merger reduced Musk's personal exposure to the X debt and consolidated his AI and social-media holdings.

## xAI (2023-present)

- Founded April 2023 by Musk with several former OpenAI and DeepMind engineers.
- Funding: $6 billion Series B May 2024 (Andreessen Horowitz, Sequoia, Saudi PIF, Qatar Investment Authority, others); $5 billion Series C November 2024; $6 billion Series D December 2024.
- Built **Colossus** in Memphis, Tennessee — by December 2024, 100,000 Nvidia H100 GPUs operational, the largest single GPU cluster in operation worldwide. Plan for expansion to 1 million GPUs.
- Product: Grok, integrated into X.

## Neuralink (2016-present)

- Founded 2016 with seven other co-founders. Brain-computer interface. First human implant January 2024 (patient Noland Arbaugh). Multiple subsequent implants through 2025. The animal-trial period prior to FDA approval was the subject of multiple Reuters investigations documenting unusually high animal mortality and several internal complaints about premature human-trial transition.

## The Boring Company (2016-present)

- Tunnel-construction company. Las Vegas Convention Center loop operational. Originally proposed Hyperloop projects have not materialised. Vehicle valuation has declined from a 2022 peak of $5.7 billion.

## DOGE — Department of Government Efficiency (20 January – late May 2025)

- **20 January 2025**: Trump signed executive order creating the **Department of Government Efficiency** (DOGE), with Musk as "Special Government Employee" leading the effort.
- DOGE's stated mandate: reduce federal spending by $2 trillion (Musk's original claim, subsequently scaled back to $1 trillion, then to "$150-200 billion in confirmed savings").
- Operational actions, January-May 2025:
  - Roughly **220,000 federal positions eliminated or restructured** (RIFs, deferred resignations, terminations).
  - **USAID** dismantled — most of its $40 billion annual aid programme cancelled or frozen.
  - **Education Department** functions transferred to other agencies.
  - **Consumer Financial Protection Bureau** operationally crippled.
  - **Office of Personnel Management** data integration with Palantir architecture — the cross-agency data consolidation that the March 2025 executive order on inter-agency data sharing legally enabled.
  - **Treasury payment system access**: DOGE personnel were granted read access to the Treasury BFS (Bureau of the Fiscal Service) payment system that processes ~$5 trillion annually. The access was the subject of multiple lawsuits.
- DOGE personnel: predominantly young engineers from Musk's companies (Tesla, SpaceX, X, xAI, Neuralink, the Boring Company). The most-reported figures include Steve Davis (Boring Company president, Musk's longtime operational lieutenant), Brad Smith, and a rotating cohort of 50 engineers granted federal access via the SGE / DOGE structure.
- **28 May 2025**: Musk formally departed DOGE after his 130-day SGE statutory maximum expired.

## The Trump falling-out (June 2025 onward)

- **5 June 2025**: Musk publicly broke with Trump over the "Big Beautiful Bill" (Trump's omnibus reconciliation legislation), criticising it as a "disgusting abomination" on X.
- 5 June 2025: Musk tweeted that Trump appeared in the "Epstein files," asserting this was the "real reason" the files had not been released.
- 6 June 2025: Trump threatened to terminate Musk's federal contracts.
- The break was patched over the subsequent weeks but the personal relationship has not been restored. Musk launched the **America Party** in July 2025 as an electoral vehicle independent of both major parties.

## Net worth and current position

- **Forbes February 2026 estimate**: **$852 billion** — making Musk the wealthiest individual in human history by a margin.
- Holdings: Tesla (~13% stake, ~$200B), SpaceX (~42% stake, ~$200B based on December 2024 $350B valuation), xAI/X (~50% post-merger, ~$60B), Neuralink, Boring Company, and various smaller positions.
- Government contract dependency: total US government contracts to Musk-owned companies through fiscal year 2024 exceeded **$38 billion**, with SpaceX the primary recipient.

## SpaceX board

Current SpaceX board (private company, not all disclosures public):
- **Elon Musk** — CEO and Chairman.
- **Gwynne Shotwell** — President and COO since 2008. The operational leader who has run the company's day-to-day since 2010. Engineering background. Earlier at Aerospace Corporation.
- **Antonio Gracias** — Founder of Valor Equity Partners. Long-time Musk ally; was on Tesla board 2007-2021 (left after the SEC settlement). Reportedly part of the DOGE inner circle 2025.
- **Steve Jurvetson** — Founding Partner of Future Ventures (formerly DFJ — Draper Fisher Jurvetson). Early Tesla and SpaceX investor.
- **Luke Nosek** — PayPal co-founder, Founders Fund co-founder, now Gigafund principal. The structural link between SpaceX and the Thiel network — Gigafund's principal positions are in SpaceX, Neuralink, and the Boring Company.
- **Donald Harrison** — Google executive.

## Tesla board

Current Tesla board (per 2025 proxy):
- **Robyn Denholm** — Chair since November 2018 (post-SEC settlement). Australian executive, previously Telstra CFO. Has sold $700 million in Tesla stock during her chair tenure.
- **Elon Musk** — CEO.
- **Kimbal Musk** — Elon's brother. Restaurant entrepreneur. On the board since 2004.
- **James Murdoch** — son of Rupert Murdoch; former 21st Century Fox CEO. Joined board 2017. The structural link between Tesla and the Murdoch media empire.
- **JB Straubel** — Tesla co-founder (per the 2009 settlement). Now CEO of Redwood Materials (battery recycling). Rejoined Tesla board 2023.
- **Ira Ehrenpreis** — DBL Partners.
- **Joe Gebbia** — Airbnb co-founder. Joined Tesla board 2022; subsequently appointed by Trump to the federal Office of Personnel Management board January 2025 (a Musk DOGE-network appointment).
- **Kathleen Wilson-Thompson** — former Walgreens executive.

## xAI board / officers

- Elon Musk — CEO.
- Igor Babuschkin — co-founder, formerly DeepMind.
- Toby Pohlen — co-founder, formerly DeepMind.
- Christian Szegedy — co-founder, formerly Google Brain.
- Several other former OpenAI engineers including Greg Yang, Tony Wu, Jimmy Ba.

# Sources — Chapter 13: Sam Altman

All claims verified 12 May 2026.

## Family

- **Sam Altman**: born 22 April 1985, Chicago, Illinois; raised in St. Louis, Missouri (Clayton suburb).
- **Father**: **Jerry Altman** — real estate broker and lawyer in St. Louis. Died May 2018.
- **Mother**: **Connie Gibstine** — dermatologist; Stanford-trained; practicing in St. Louis.
- Jewish family; raised in St. Louis suburb of Clayton.
- **Siblings**:
  - **Jack Altman** — entrepreneur and investor; founder/CEO of **Lattice** (HR software, founded 2015, valued at $3+ billion). Younger brother.
  - **Max Altman** — investor; founder of **Saga Ventures** (early-stage VC). Youngest brother.
  - **Annie Altman** (b. 1994, nine years younger than Sam) — writer, musician, podcaster (the *Mercury Stardust Show* and the *humanly* podcast). Has publicly accused Sam of sexual abuse beginning in 2021 (Twitter thread). Filed lawsuit January 6, 2025 in **US District Court for the Eastern District of Missouri** — not San Francisco — alleging Sam committed acts of rape, molestation, sodomy, and battery on her "several times a week" for nearly a decade, beginning around when she was 3 and continuing while she was a minor and Sam was an adult. The lawsuit was **dismissed in March 2026** by the federal judge on statute-of-limitations grounds. Annie filed an amended lawsuit subsequently in 2026.

## The Annie Altman allegations — full public record

- 2021: Annie posted on Twitter that she had been "sexually, physically, emotionally, verbally, financially, and technologically abused" by Sam and brother Jack.
- 2021-2024: continued public statements, podcasts, Substack writings on the abuse allegations and her financial situation.
- January 6, 2025: filed federal lawsuit *Annie Altman v. Samuel Altman* in US District Court, Eastern District of Missouri. Sought damages of more than $75,000 plus legal fees.
- January 7, 2025: Sam Altman, mother Connie, and brothers Jack and Max issued joint statement on X denying allegations; calling them "utterly untrue." Family statement said Annie's claims have "evolved drastically over time" and referenced her history of accusing family members of wrongdoing.
- December 2024: OpenAI whistleblower Suchir Balaji died in San Francisco. SFPD ruled suicide; family disputed and called for further investigation. Annie tweeted "#JusticeForSuchir" and joined those questioning the suicide ruling — drawing additional attention to the family dispute.
- March 2026: federal judge **dismissed** Annie's lawsuit on statute-of-limitations grounds. Annie subsequently filed an amended complaint.

Per the existing public-record reporting:
- 2023 *New York* magazine profile of Sam Altman documented the estrangement, mentioned Sam had "repeatedly tried to offer Annie money, gifts, and even to buy her a house."
- TechCrunch reporting (January 8, 2025) noted Sam's response described Annie's "unstable personal life" including moving "20 times in a single year" and characterised the father Jerry's 2018 death as a "tipping point" for Annie's mental health.
- The lawsuit was on the public docket; the family's denial was posted on X publicly; the dismissal on statute-of-limitations grounds (not on the merits of the underlying allegations) was reported in March 2026.

## Education

- **John Burroughs School** (Clayton, MO) — private K-12, graduated 2003. Came out as gay at age 17 in response to a Christian student group's anti-gay event.
- **Stanford University** — computer science, 2003-2005. **Dropped out** to pursue Loopt.

## Loopt (2005-2012)

- Founded 2005 with **Nick Sivo** (Stanford classmate and Altman's boyfriend at the time) and Alok Deshpande.
- Location-based social networking application.
- **Y Combinator's first batch** (Summer 2005) — Altman is among Paul Graham's earliest YC alumni.
- Raised $39 million from venture capital, including Sequoia Capital, NEA, and other firms.
- March 2012: acquired by **Green Dot Corporation** for **$43.4 million** — a return of slightly above the capital raised but well below the venture-capital return targets. Investors took losses or near-flat outcomes. Loopt was a failure as a venture-scale company despite Altman's later mythologisation of his early experience.

## Y Combinator (2011-2019)

- 2011: Joined YC as a part-time partner under Paul Graham.
- 2014: Named **President of Y Combinator** by Paul Graham (Graham wanted to step back from operations).
- 2019: Stepped down as President of YC to focus full-time on OpenAI; technically remained Chairman briefly. Garry Tan (mentioned in Chapter 11) became President in 2023.
- During Altman's YC tenure, YC dramatically expanded its investment pace and portfolio. The firm under Altman invested in 1,900 companies. Altman's personal portfolio (separate from YC) was built largely through positions in YC companies.

## OpenAI — founding to present

- **December 2015**: OpenAI announced and incorporated as a 501(c)(3) non-profit research organisation. Founding board / contributors included **Sam Altman, Elon Musk, Greg Brockman, Ilya Sutskever, Reid Hoffman, Peter Thiel, Jessica Livingston, Y Combinator Research, and several other Silicon Valley figures**, with $1 billion in pledged funding. Mission stated: ensure AGI benefits all humanity.
- 2018: **Elon Musk departed the OpenAI board** citing conflicts with Tesla's AI work. Musk subsequently claimed he had committed $1 billion but only delivered ~$100 million before departing.
- **2019: the capped-profit conversion**. OpenAI created a for-profit subsidiary, OpenAI Global LLC, controlled by the non-profit OpenAI Inc. The structure was called "capped-profit": investors could receive returns up to 100x their investment, after which all excess profit would return to the non-profit. The Microsoft $1 billion investment was announced the same year — the first investment under the new structure.
- 2020: GPT-3 released.
- November 2022: **ChatGPT released**, the consumer chatbot that catalysed the post-2022 AI boom.
- January 2023: **Microsoft additional investment of $10 billion** at the OpenAI valuation of $29 billion. Microsoft Azure became the exclusive cloud provider for OpenAI.
- **November 17, 2023**: The OpenAI board (composed of Helen Toner, Tasha McCauley, Adam D'Angelo, Ilya Sutskever) **removed Sam Altman as CEO**, citing "lack of confidence in him" and stating he had not been "consistently candid in his communications with the board." Greg Brockman was removed as Chairman and resigned as President. CTO Mira Murati became interim CEO.
- **November 17-21, 2023**: Microsoft (under Satya Nadella) offered Altman and Brockman positions to lead an in-house Microsoft AI division; 700+ of OpenAI's 770 employees signed a letter demanding Altman's reinstatement and threatening to resign; investor pressure (Microsoft, Thrive Capital, others) mounted.
- **November 21-22, 2023**: Altman and Brockman reinstated; new initial board: **Bret Taylor (Chair), Larry Summers, and Adam D'Angelo (the sole holdover)**.
- **November 29, 2023**: An anonymous Microsoft employee joined the board as non-voting observer; Microsoft resigned the observer seat in July 2024 in response to FTC/regulatory pressure on the OpenAI-Microsoft governance overlap.
- **February 2024**: SEC subpoenaed OpenAI's internal communications to determine whether Altman's pre-November-2023 representations to investors had been materially misleading.
- **March 2024**: Internal review (conducted by WilmerHale on OpenAI's behalf) concluded that Altman should remain CEO. Three new board members announced: **Sue Desmond-Hellmann** (former CEO of the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation), **Nicole Seligman** (former Sony EVP), **Fidji Simo** (CEO of Instacart). Altman rejoined the board.
- **June 2024**: **Paul M. Nakasone** — retired US Army general, **former Director of the National Security Agency** (2018-2024), former head of US Cyber Command, former Chief of the Central Security Service — joined the OpenAI board. The appointment placed the immediately-prior head of US signals intelligence on the board of the principal commercial AI company.
- **October 2024**: $6.6 billion capital raise at $157 billion valuation. Microsoft, Nvidia, SoftBank, and others.
- **November 2025**: Larry Summers resigned from the board.
- **January 2025**: **Adebayo "Bayo" Ogunlesi** (BlackRock senior managing director, CEO of Global Infrastructure Partners, former Credit Suisse Investment Banking executive vice chairman) joined the board — the BlackRock-OpenAI structural bridge.
- **2025 restructuring**: The for-profit subsidiary was converted into a **Public Benefit Corporation (PBC)**, with the non-profit retaining 26% ownership. The cap on investor returns was removed.
- **October 2025**: $6.6 billion share sale at $500 billion valuation.
- **April 2026**: Fidji Simo, then OpenAI's head of AGI deployment, announced "several weeks" of medical leave.

## Current OpenAI board (May 2026)

- Bret Taylor (Chair) — former Salesforce co-CEO, former Twitter Chair.
- Sam Altman (CEO).
- Adam D'Angelo — Quora CEO. The sole continuous board member from before November 2023.
- Sue Desmond-Hellmann — former CEO Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation.
- Nicole Seligman — former Sony Corporation of America President.
- Fidji Simo — former Instacart CEO; OpenAI Head of Applications (operational role); on medical leave April 2026.
- **Paul M. Nakasone** — former NSA Director.
- **Zico Kolter** — Carnegie Mellon CS professor; theoretical machine learning.
- **Adebayo "Bayo" Ogunlesi** — BlackRock senior managing director.

## Stargate Project

- **Announced 21 January 2025** at the White House by President Trump.
- Joint venture: **OpenAI, Oracle, SoftBank, MGX** (UAE state-backed AI fund led by Sheikh Tahnoun bin Zayed).
- Initial pledge: **$100 billion immediately, $500 billion over four years**.
- **Masayoshi Son** as Chairman of the Stargate joint venture.
- **Sam Altman** as principal architect.
- **Larry Ellison** (Oracle) as principal partner.
- Trump's statement at the announcement included Son's verbatim acknowledgment: "We wouldn't have decided to do this unless you won."
- Initial site: Abilene, Texas — Oracle/OpenAI/Crusoe Cloud data centre.

## Worldcoin / Tools for Humanity

- Founded **2019** by Sam Altman and **Alex Blania** (CEO of Tools for Humanity, the operating company).
- October 2021: Project publicly announced. "Orb" iris-scanning hardware device that creates a unique "World ID" cryptographic identifier tied to the iris pattern.
- Funding rounds totalled **$650 million** by 2025, including **Andreessen Horowitz**, **Khosla Ventures** ($240M+), and others.
- July 2023: Tools for Humanity launched World ID. Roughly $50 in cryptocurrency offered to sign up.
- April 2022 MIT Technology Review investigation: documented "unethical practices when onboarding test users across Africa and Asia, including deceptive marketing practices" and the collection of personal data beyond iris scans (heartbeat, breathing, vital signs) without informed consent.
- October 2024: rebranded from "Worldcoin" to **World**; new Orb hardware unveiled in San Francisco.
- **As of 2025-2026, the project has been banned, restricted, or under investigation in**: Brazil (January 2025 ban; March 2025 reaffirmation with $8,800 daily fine for resumption); Indonesia (May 2025 suspension); Philippines (October 2025 cease-and-desist); Thailand (November 2025 shutdown with deletion order); Kenya (High Court ruled World's operations unlawful, May 2024); Colombia (cease and desist, data deletion order); Hong Kong; India; Spain; Portugal; Germany; South Korea; France (limited-time preview ended); Argentina (probes).
- Roughly **17.5 million users verified** by late 2025 (~2% of the stated 1 billion target).
- Edward Snowden has publicly criticised the project for "cataloguing eyeballs."
- April 2026: Tools for Humanity announced partnerships with **Tinder, Zoom, and Docusign** for identity verification in those services.
- Sam Altman's documented personal time commitment to World: "spends a good chunk of his time working on World" per Alex Blania, October 2024.

## Personal investments

- **Helion Energy** (fusion): Altman invested $375 million personally, including a $20 million investment in 2014 and additional commitments through 2024. Altman serves as Helion's Chairman. Helion has a 2028 power-delivery commitment to Microsoft.
- **Retro Biosciences** (longevity): Altman invested $180 million personally in 2022.
- **Reddit**: Was an investor; left the board 2022.
- **Humane** (AI pin): Major investor in a company that failed commercially in 2024.
- **Cruise**: Early investor; sold to GM 2016.
- **Stripe, Asana, Pinterest, Airbnb, Reddit**: Y Combinator alumni positions through OpenAI Startup Fund and personal portfolio.
- **OpenAI Startup Fund**: Altman personally controlled the fund (though OpenAI nominally operated it) until November 2023 governance reforms. The personal control of the fund by the CEO of OpenAI was one of the documented conflict-of-interest concerns that contributed to the November 2023 board action.
- **University of Michigan venture relationship**: Fortune reporting (December 2023) documented Altman received $75 million from the University of Michigan for a new venture fund months after ChatGPT launched.

## Political donations

- **2020 cycle**: $250,000 to Biden Victory Fund.
- **2020-2023**: predominantly Democratic-aligned donations; supporting universal basic income organisations.
- **2024 cycle**: **$1 million donation to the Trump inauguration committee** in December 2024 (publicly disclosed). This was a bipartisan/access-protection donation pattern — similar to that of many other tech CEOs (Bezos, Zuckerberg, Cook all made similar inauguration donations) but documented in Altman's case as a deliberate strategic decision.
- **Trump dinner**: Altman has dined at Mar-a-Lago multiple times since the 2024 election; was present at the Stargate White House announcement.

## Suchir Balaji death (December 2024)

- Suchir Balaji, 26, former OpenAI researcher who had publicly accused OpenAI of copyright infringement in its training data. Spoke to *New York Times* on the record in October 2024 about his concerns.
- Found dead in his San Francisco apartment on November 26, 2024 (body discovered).
- San Francisco Police Department and Office of the Chief Medical Examiner ruled the death a **suicide**; SFPD stated "no evidence of foul play."
- Family — particularly his mother Poornima Ramarao — disputed the suicide ruling and called for FBI investigation, citing the timing (Balaji was a witness in copyright cases against OpenAI) and disputed details of the scene.
- Annie Altman publicly tweeted "#JusticeForSuchir" in December 2024.
- An independent autopsy commissioned by the family reportedly reached different conclusions; the legal process is ongoing as of mid-2026.
- The death and the dispute are on record. The public record is what it is.

## Sources

- *San Francisco Standard*, "Sam Altman denies sexual abuse claims in his sister's lawsuit," 8 January 2025.
- *TechCrunch*, "Sam Altman's family responds to lawsuit alleging he sexually assaulted his sister," 7 January 2025.
- *Annie Altman v. Sam Altman* complaint filed 6 January 2025, US District Court Eastern District of Missouri (DocumentCloud).
- *Dawn News* English / *N18G*, US judge dismisses sexual abuse lawsuit, March 2026 (statute-of-limitations dismissal).
- *Rest of World*, "US tech embraces Sam Altman's World iris-scan ID banned in places," April 2026.
- *TechCrunch*, "Sam Altman's Worldcoin becomes World and shows new iris-scanning Orb to prove your humanity," 17 October 2024.
- *Business Insider* (cited by Yahoo Finance), "Billionaire Sam Altman's Iris Orb Dream Crumbles," November 2025.
- *MIT Technology Review*, "Deception, exploited workers, and cash handouts: How Worldcoin recruited its first half a million test users," April 2022.
- *OpenAI press releases* on board appointments (March 2024, June 2024, January 2025).
- *CNBC*, "OpenAI adds former NSA chief to its board," 13 June 2024.
- *TechCrunch*, "OpenAI appoints BlackRock exec to its board," 14 January 2025.
- *New York* magazine profile of Sam Altman, 2023.

- *The New York Times* reporting on Suchir Balaji, October-December 2024.

# Sources — Chapter 14: Marc Andreessen

All claims verified 12 May 2026.

## Origins and family

- **Marc Lowell Andreessen**: born 9 July 1971 in Cedar Falls, Iowa. Raised in **New Lisbon, Wisconsin** (population 2,500), where his family relocated when he was very young.
- **Father**: Lowell Andreessen — sold seeds for a small Wisconsin agricultural supply company.
- **Mother**: Patricia Andreessen — worked at the Lands' End mail-order catalog company.
- Scandinavian-descent Lutheran family. Working-class to lower-middle-class background. Marc has spoken in adult interviews about the contrast between his small-town Wisconsin upbringing and his subsequent Silicon Valley wealth.

## Education

- New Lisbon High School, graduated 1989.
- **University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign**, BS Computer Science, 1993.
- Worked at the **National Center for Supercomputing Applications (NCSA)** at Illinois as an undergraduate beginning 1992. At NCSA, with **Eric Bina** (NCSA staff programmer), wrote the **Mosaic web browser** — the first widely-used graphical web browser — released January 1993 (Unix version) and subsequent ports to Windows and Mac. Mosaic was the technology that opened the World Wide Web to mass use.

## Netscape (1994-1999)

- May 1994: Andreessen moved to Silicon Valley after graduating. Met Jim Clark (Silicon Graphics founder) who proposed forming a company to commercialise web browser technology.
- April 1994: **Mosaic Communications Corporation** founded by Clark and Andreessen with the explicit intent to write a competing browser from scratch (the Mosaic codebase belonged to NCSA / University of Illinois). The company was renamed **Netscape Communications** in November 1994 after Illinois objected to the name.
- December 1994: **Netscape Navigator 1.0** released. Within a year, Navigator achieved 80 percent share of the early web-browser market.
- **9 August 1995**: Netscape IPO. The IPO was the largest first-day stock pop in history to that point. Opening at $28, the stock closed at $58.25 — a $58.25 closing price for a company that had never been profitable, valuing Netscape at $2.9 billion on the first day of trading. The IPO is, by historical consensus, the event that marks the beginning of the dot-com bubble. Andreessen, at 24, was an instant centi-millionaire.
- 1995-1998: Microsoft launched Internet Explorer and bundled it with Windows, leading to the "browser wars" and Microsoft's antitrust case (US v. Microsoft, 1998-2001).
- November 1998: AOL acquired Netscape for **$4.2 billion** in stock. Andreessen received **$110 million** from the transaction.

## Intermediate ventures

- **1999**: Andreessen founded **Loudcloud** with Ben Horowitz, Tim Howes, and In Sik Rhee — a managed-services Internet hosting company.
- 2002: Loudcloud's hosting business was sold to EDS for $63.5 million; the remaining software business was renamed **Opsware**.
- 2007: **Opsware was acquired by Hewlett-Packard for $1.6 billion**. Andreessen and Horowitz received proceeds.
- **Board positions**, mid-2000s onward: **eBay** (2008-2014), **Facebook** (2008-present — Andreessen has been the longest-serving non-Zuckerberg director on the Meta board), **Hewlett-Packard** (2009-2014, including during the controversial Léo Apotheker tenure and the failed Autonomy acquisition).

## Andreessen Horowitz (2009-present)

- Founded 2009 in Menlo Park by Marc Andreessen and Ben Horowitz. Initial fund: $300 million.
- Founding thesis: "**software is eating the world**" — Andreessen's 2011 *Wall Street Journal* essay that articulated the firm's investment doctrine.
- Investment growth: 2012 ($1.5B fund), 2014 ($1.5B), 2016 ($1.7B), 2019 ($2.75B + biotech), 2022 ($9 billion across multiple funds), 2025 (additional growth funds), **January 2026 — $15 billion raised across five new funds**.
- **As of March 2026**: managing **at least $90 billion in assets**.
- Portfolio: 1,076 companies listed on a16z's own website (November 2025). 32 unicorns backed since 2020 (more than any other VC firm per OfficeChai's calculations).
- Notable portfolio: Airbnb, Coinbase, Facebook (early investment), Slack, Lyft, Pinterest, GitHub, Robinhood, Stripe, Instacart, Roblox, Anduril, Helion, OpenAI, xAI, Databricks, Mistral, Character.ai, Andrija Robotics, Hadrian, Castelion (hypersonic weapons), Apex Space, Flock Safety, Shield AI.

## Current general partners and structure (May 2026)

- **Marc Andreessen** — Co-founder, General Partner.
- **Ben Horowitz** — Co-founder, General Partner.
- **Jeff Jordan** — General Partner; former CEO of OpenTable, former president of PayPal, former Disney executive.
- **Chris Dixon** — General Partner; leads the crypto investing practice (a16z crypto, separate fund structure with $7+ billion across multiple vintages). Author of *Read Write Own* (2024).
- **Martin Casado** — General Partner; infrastructure/AI; former co-founder of Nicira (acquired by VMware for $1.26 billion 2012).
- **Andrew Chen** — General Partner; consumer.
- **Vijay Pande** — General Partner; bio + health practice.
- **Angela Strange** — General Partner; fintech.
- **Alex Rampell** — General Partner; fintech; co-founder of TrialPay.
- **David Ulevitch** — General Partner; co-leads American Dynamism with Boyle; former founder of OpenDNS (sold to Cisco for $635M 2015).
- **Katherine Boyle** — General Partner; co-leads American Dynamism; **previously interned at Founders Fund** and is, per her own published statements and biographical interviews, **explicitly influenced by Peter Thiel's thinking**. In 2016 joined General Catalyst where she helped fund Anduril, before joining a16z. Her portfolio includes Hadrian (manufacturing in aerospace and defense), Apex Space (satellites), Castelion (hypersonic weapons), Anduril, Shield AI, Palantir. **The principal operational bridge between a16z and the Thiel-network defence portfolio.**
- **Erin Price-Wright** — General Partner; American Dynamism fund (with Boyle and Ulevitch).
- **Connie Chan** — General Partner; consumer technology, Chinese tech market expertise.
- **Jennifer Li** — General Partner; growth.
- **David George** — General Partner; growth fund.
- **Raghu Raghuram** — General Partner; joined October 2025; former CEO of VMware.
- **Erik Torenberg** — General Partner; joined 2025; founder of Product Hunt, On Deck, Village Global, Turpentine; the principal "media/network" partner. Per Andreessen's own published statement: a16z "sometimes called ourselves 'a media company that monetizes via investing.'"
- **Sarah Wang** — General Partner; growth.

Former partners include Margit Wennmachers (operating partner 2010-July 2025, now emeritus); Frank Chen; Lars Dalgaard; Scott Kupor.

## The American Dynamism fund

- Roughly **$600 million dedicated fund**, founded around 2020 / formally launched 2022.
- Leadership: **Katherine Boyle** (co-lead), **David Ulevitch** (co-lead), **Erin Price-Wright** (GP).
- Stated mission, per Boyle's 2021 essay "Building American Dynamism": "the only way to reverse the course of stagnation and kick-start nationwide renewal post-Covid is through technologists building companies that support the national interest."
- Portfolio includes **Anduril** (which Boyle had helped fund at General Catalyst), **Shield AI** (AI-powered drones for military applications), **Hadrian** (manufacturing in aerospace and defense), **Apex Space** (satellites), **Castelion** (hypersonic weapons), **Flock Safety** (license-plate recognition / surveillance), and 50 listed American Dynamism portfolio companies as of November 2025.

## The Twitter investment

- **$400 million committed** to the Musk Twitter acquisition (October 2022).
- Per *Washington Post* analysis (September 2024): **a16z had lost $288 million on the Twitter investment** by that point.
- The investment subsequently rolled into the xAI/X merger of March 2025 at a higher implied valuation.
- Andreessen Horowitz also separately invested in **xAI** in subsequent funding rounds.

## The Techno-Optimist Manifesto (16 October 2023)

- **5,200-word essay** published 16 October 2023 on the a16z website and Substack.
- Structure: **15 parts** — Lies; Truth; Technology; Markets; The Techno-Capital Machine; Intelligence; Energy; Abundance; Not Utopia, But Close Enough; Becoming Technological Supermen; Technological Values; The Meaning Of Life; **The Enemy**; The Future; **the Patron Saints of Techno-Optimism** (56 named people).
- The phrase "We believe" appears **113 times**.
- **The Enemy section explicitly names as enemies**: "social responsibility," "trust and safety," "tech ethics," "risk management," "sustainability," "ESG," "stakeholder capitalism," "the precautionary principle," "the limits to growth," "the bezzle." Single quotation: "Our enemy is corruption, regulatory capture, monopolies, cartels."
- Specific quotations (verbatim from the published manifesto):
  - "We believe everything good is downstream of growth."
  - "**We believe a universal basic income would turn people into zoo animals to be farmed by the state. Man was not meant to be farmed; man was meant to be useful, to be productive, to be proud.**"
  - "We believe the global population can quite easily expand to **50 billion people** or more, and then far beyond that as we settle other planets."
  - "We are not victims, we are conquerors."
  - "**We are not primitives, cowering in fear of the lightning bolt. We are the apex predator; the lightning works for us.**"
  - "Technology is the glory of human ambition and achievement, the spearhead of progress, and the realization of our potential."
- **Patron Saints list** of 56 names includes: **Filippo Tommaso Marinetti** (founder of the Futurist movement and author of the 1909 *Futurist Manifesto* — Marinetti was a founding fascist and direct collaborator with Mussolini), **Friedrich Hayek**, **Milton Friedman**, **Ayn Rand**, **Buckminster Fuller**, **Nick Land** (founder of neoreactionary thought; intellectual co-source of the Yarvin doctrines Chapter 11 documented), and 50 other figures, predominantly historical pro-capitalist and pro-technology intellectuals.
- Direct stylistic and thematic parallels to Marinetti's 1909 *Futurist Manifesto*, including the numbered-statement form, the apex-predator/conqueror rhetoric, and the explicit opposition to "stagnation."

## Wife: Laura Arrillaga-Andreessen

- **Laura Arrillaga-Andreessen** (born 1970, San Francisco) — philanthropist; chairman of the board of the Stanford Center on Philanthropy and Civil Society; founder of the Stanford Philanthropy and Civil Society program. Married Marc Andreessen in 2006.
- Daughter of **John Arrillaga** (1937-2022) and Frances Arrillaga. **John Arrillaga was the most consequential Silicon Valley commercial real estate developer of the post-1960 period** — his firm Peery Arrillaga developed 12 million square feet of commercial space across Silicon Valley, including the original campuses of Cisco, Yahoo, Sun Microsystems, Tesla, Tibco, and many others. Arrillaga's net worth at death was $4 billion.
- The Arrillaga family fortune is, the foundation of the contemporary Stanford-Silicon-Valley real-estate landscape. The Arrillaga buildings on the Stanford campus (the Arrillaga Family Sports Center, the Arrillaga Family Dining Commons, the Arrillaga Plaza, etc.) are documented on the Stanford donor record.
- Laura inherited a portion of the Arrillaga fortune.

## The 2022 Atherton housing opposition

- **August 2022**: Marc Andreessen and Laura Arrillaga-Andreessen submitted a public email (subject line: "**IMMENSELY AGAINST multifamily development!**") to the Atherton mayor and city council opposing a state-mandated plan to rezone parts of Atherton to permit construction of **130 multifamily housing units by 2031**.
- Their email said they were "**IMMENSELY AGAINST**" the multifamily overlay zones and asked the town to "**IMMEDIATELY REMOVE all multifamily overlay zoning projects from the Housing Element**."
- Additional quoted opposition: "They will massively decrease our home values, the quality of life of ourselves and our neighbors and immensely increase the noise pollution and traffic."
- The email is authentic per the Atherton planning office and was first reported by *The Atlantic*.
- After the Andreessens and 300+ others objected, Atherton **abandoned the multifamily plan** and submitted alternative housing-element provisions involving townhomes and accessory dwelling units only.
- The episode is significant because Andreessen had published, in April 2020, an essay titled "**It's Time to Build**" that criticised America's inability to build housing, infrastructure, and other physical capital, specifically lamenting "crazily skyrocketing housing prices in places like San Francisco, making it nearly impossible for regular people to move in and take the jobs of the future."
- Two years before the Atherton incident, the Andreessens had paid **$177 million for a 7-acre Malibu estate** in October 2021 — a record price for a California home.

## The 2024 political turn

- Through 2022, Andreessen had been a registered Democrat and a Democratic-aligned political donor.
- December 2023 - July 2024: Andreessen's published statements (the manifesto, the various podcasts, the *Why AI Will Save the World* essay June 2023) shifted increasingly right-wing.
- **July 2024**: Andreessen and Horowitz publicly endorsed Donald Trump for the 2024 presidential election in a podcast titled "**The Ben & Marc Show: Why We're Voting for Trump**." The endorsement was a public signal that the broader Silicon Valley right-wing turn that had been documented through 2023 was now electoral.
- October 2024: Andreessen appeared on **Joe Rogan's podcast** (episode 2208, 28 October 2024) for a 3-hour interview. He used the appearance to articulate the "**Little Tech**" policy framework — the argument that the second Trump administration should align with Silicon Valley venture-capital "little tech" against the Biden-administration "big tech" (Microsoft, Google, Apple, Meta). The framing established Andreessen as one of the principal Silicon Valley voices for the Trump policy programme.
- **Reasons for the political turn**, per Andreessen's own statements in the Joe Rogan interview and subsequent appearances:
  - Biden administration antitrust enforcement (FTC under Lina Khan, DOJ Antitrust Division)
  - Biden administration cryptocurrency enforcement (SEC under Gary Gensler)
  - Biden administration AI regulation (the October 2023 AI executive order)
  - The proposed "billionaire minimum income tax"
  - The post-Bankman-Fried regulatory scrutiny of crypto
- **2024 political contributions**: documented contributions of $4.5 million to Trump-aligned PACs (FEC records); also contributions to "Little Tech" advocacy organisations and to specific Republican Senate and House candidates aligned with the administration's policy programme.

## Effective Accelerationism (e/acc)

- The intellectual-movement label Andreessen has aligned with publicly. The movement emerged through anonymous X accounts in 2022 in reaction to "Effective Altruism" (the Sam Bankman-Fried-associated movement).
- e/acc doctrine: technology innovation and progress is philanthropic by its nature; technology should be free from regulation; charity is less effective than direct investment in technology companies.
- Public e/acc adherents in 2024-2026: Andreessen, **Garry Tan** (Y Combinator CEO), Martin Shkreli (the "Pharma Bro"), various anonymous Silicon Valley accounts.

## Net worth and current position

- **Forbes 2026 estimate**: **$2.2 billion** personal net worth.
- The figure understates Andreessen's operational economic position. He controls, through his and his firm's positions, decision-making over $90 billion in assets under management at a16z, plus positions in 1,076 portfolio companies with combined valuations of multiple hundreds of billions of dollars. He is on the Meta board, where his vote influences decision-making over a $1.8 trillion market-cap company. He is on the boards of multiple other major companies.
- His wife Laura Arrillaga-Andreessen's separately documented inheritance and personal philanthropy add to the household's economic position.

## The "Patron Saints" — full review of the 56-name list

The 56 named individuals in the "Patron Saints of Techno-Optimism" list, as published in the October 2023 manifesto, include (partial list, verified from the published manifesto):

- **Filippo Tommaso Marinetti** — founder of the Futurist movement; author of the 1909 *Futurist Manifesto*; founding fascist; collaborator with Mussolini in the formation of the Italian Fascist movement.
- **Friedrich Hayek** — Austrian-British economist; founding member of the Mont Pèlerin Society (Chapter 6).
- **Milton Friedman** — American economist; Mont Pèlerin Society member; principal post-1947 popular advocate of free-market doctrines.
- **Ayn Rand** — Russian-American novelist; founder of Objectivism.
- **Buckminster Fuller** — American architect/inventor; the "Spaceship Earth" doctrine.
- **Nick Land** — British philosopher; founder of neoreactionary thought; intellectual co-source of the Yarvin doctrines Chapter 11 documented. Land is the bridge between the academic NRx movement and the Andreessen-network e/acc movement.
- **John Galt** — fictional character from *Atlas Shrugged*. (The fictional inclusion is itself notable.)
- **Beff Jezos and Bayeslord** — anonymous X accounts credited with founding the effective accelerationist movement.
- **James Burnham** — American political theorist; author of *The Managerial Revolution* (1941); Trotskyist who became a foundational conservative theorist; Buckley collaborator.
- Various pro-technology engineers, scientists, and entrepreneurs of the 19th and 20th centuries (Edison, Westinghouse, Carnegie, etc.).
- Various Silicon Valley figures and friends.

The Marinetti inclusion is the most operationally significant. Marinetti's 1909 *Futurist Manifesto* is the historical model from which Andreessen's 2023 manifesto is, by all documented stylistic and thematic parallels, derived. The Futurist movement explicitly celebrated war, violence, "the destruction of feminism," the burning of museums and libraries, the apex-predator/conqueror rhetoric, and the cult of speed-and-machinery that the Andreessen manifesto closely echoes. The Futurists' subsequent role as the cultural-intellectual co-founders of Italian Fascism is part of the documented historical record. The inclusion of Marinetti at the head of the patron-saints list is not coincidental.

# Sources — Chapter 15: Hoffman, Sacks, Levchin (PayPal Mafia Second Tier)

All claims verified 12 May 2026.

## Reid Garrett Hoffman

### Origins
- Born **5 August 1967, Stanford, California**.
- Parents: William Parker Hoffman Jr. and Deanna Ruth Hoffman. Both lawyers active in environmental and social-justice causes. Hoffman has described his upbringing as politically progressive and intellectually rigorous.
- Maternal grandfather: a New Deal-era public servant.
- Berkeley-area childhood, raised in a progressive politically active household.

### Education
- **The Putney School** (Vermont boarding school, progressive education tradition).
- **Stanford University**, BS Symbolic Systems and Cognitive Science, 1990 (one of the new interdisciplinary majors that produced multiple Silicon Valley figures).
- **Oxford University** (Wolfson College), Marshall Scholar, MA Philosophy, 1993.
- Hoffman has been throughout his career one of the most academically credentialed of the PayPal Mafia figures; the philosophical training has been a recurring theme in his subsequent writing and his public-intellectual self-presentation.

### Early career
- **Apple Computer** (briefly, mid-1990s) — eWorld product management.
- **Fujitsu** (briefly).
- **SocialNet** (1997-2000) — Hoffman's first founded company. Online dating and social networking. Failed commercially.
- Joined **PayPal** in **January 2000** as Executive Vice President, principally responsible for business-development, regulatory, and government-affairs functions. Was one of the principal architects of PayPal's regulatory strategy and its eventual exit to eBay.

### LinkedIn (2002-2016)
- Founded **December 2002** by Hoffman with Allen Blue, Konstantin Guericke, Eric Ly, and Jean-Luc Vaillant.
- Launched **May 2003**. Professional social-networking platform.
- Hoffman served as CEO 2003-2007, then Executive Chairman.
- IPO **19 May 2011** at $45/share; first-day close $94.25 — at the time the largest US Internet IPO since the 2008 financial crisis.
- **June 2016**: Microsoft announced acquisition of LinkedIn for **$26.2 billion in cash** — at the time the largest Microsoft acquisition in history. The acquisition closed in December 2016.
- Hoffman received **$2.7 billion** from the transaction (his ~11 percent personal stake at the acquisition price).
- Hoffman joined the Microsoft board as part of the acquisition; he remained on the Microsoft board through 2024.

### Greylock Partners (2009-present)
- Joined **Greylock Partners** in 2009 as a General Partner. Greylock is one of the oldest and most consequential American venture-capital firms (founded 1965).
- Hoffman has been Greylock's principal interface with the consumer-Internet and social-networking investment categories.
- Greylock portfolio under Hoffman includes Airbnb, Facebook (early investment), Dropbox, Workday, Roblox, Coinbase, Discord, Figma, and 200 other companies.

### OpenAI involvement
- Hoffman was one of the **founding contributors to OpenAI** in December 2015. He served on the OpenAI board from founding.
- Hoffman **resigned from the OpenAI board in March 2023** to focus on his subsequent AI ventures (Inflection AI) and to avoid the conflicts of interest the Inflection investment created. The departure was two years before the November 2023 board crisis that produced the broader OpenAI governance restructuring.

### Inflection AI (2022-2024)
- Co-founded 2022 with **Mustafa Suleyman** (DeepMind co-founder) and Karén Simonyan.
- The principal Hoffman-led AI company.
- **March 2024**: Microsoft "acquired" Inflection AI in an unusual transaction structure that involved hiring most of Inflection's staff (including Suleyman, who became CEO of Microsoft AI) and licensing Inflection's technology, without acquiring the corporate entity itself. Microsoft paid $650 million for the licensing and personnel transfer. The transaction was structured to avoid antitrust review.

### Microsoft board (2016-2024)
- Hoffman was a Microsoft director from December 2016 (LinkedIn acquisition closing) to **June 2024**, when he stepped down to focus on his political activity and other ventures.
- During his tenure, Microsoft completed the $13 billion OpenAI investment, the LinkedIn integration, the Activision Blizzard acquisition ($68.7 billion), and AI-product expansion. Hoffman's board position gave him direct decision-making influence over Microsoft's AI investment strategy during the period of OpenAI's emergence.

### Jeffrey Epstein connection
The most operationally significant aspect of Hoffman's biography for this book's purposes is his documented relationship with Jeffrey Epstein, the convicted-sex-offender financier who died in federal custody in August 2019.

**The documented record**:
- **2013**: Per the MIT Goodwin Procter investigation report (released January 2020), Hoffman was a member of the **MIT Media Lab Advisory Council** during the Epstein period. The report documents that "in July 2013: Epstein visited the MIT campus... during the visit, Ito escorted Epstein... and Professor [Ed] Boyden and Professor [Neil] Gershenfeld... [Hoffman] also is a member of the Media Lab Advisory Council."
- **2014**: Hoffman **visited Epstein's private island, Little St. James**, with Joi Ito (then director of the MIT Media Lab). Per the *Wall Street Journal* and Ito's own statement: the trip was for "fundraising" purposes. Documents indicate Hoffman and Ito were planning a subsequent visit in late 2014 from Palm Beach.
- **August 2015**: Hoffman hosted a dinner in Palo Alto honoring MIT neuroscientist Ed Boyden. The dinner was attended by **Epstein, Elon Musk, Mark Zuckerberg, Peter Thiel, and Joi Ito** — that is, the same dinner brought together four of the figures whose biographies this book has documented as principals of the new world order's senior coalition, with Epstein in attendance.
- **2016**: Per the Goodwin Procter report, "in July 2016, Ito sought advice from Hoffman about whether to allow Epstein to attend a Media Lab conference with 'lots of people' who may 'see him and maybe know he's involved.'" The report did not disclose Hoffman's advice.
- **2017**: Epstein wrote to Hoffman **16 March 2017** about creating a "donor advised fund" to offset cuts to federal programs the first Trump administration proposed. The email was released as part of the November 2025 House Oversight Committee document release.
- **2018**: Additional fundraising meetings with Epstein, per Hoffman's own subsequent corrections to his 2019 statement.

**Hoffman's statements**:
- **September 2019** (initial public response, to *Axios*): "My few interactions with Jeffrey Epstein came at the request of Joi Ito, for the purposes of fundraising for the MIT Media Lab. Prior to these interactions, I was told by Joi that Epstein had cleared the MIT vetting process, which was the basis for my participation. **My last interaction with Epstein was in 2015**. Still, by agreeing to participate in any fundraising activity where Epstein was present, **I helped to repair his reputation and perpetuate injustice**. For this, I am deeply regretful."
- **November 2025** (correction post on X): "I only knew Jeffrey Epstein because of a fundraising relationship with MIT, which I very much regret. **In 2019 I told Axios the last meeting I had with him was in 2015 but I was mistaken**, as according to calendar entries I have become aware there were additional fundraising meetings in 2016 and 2018. These meetings were all coordinated by Joi Ito, then director of the MIT Media Lab."
- **November 2025** (continued): "Trump should release all of the Epstein files: every person and every document in the files. I want this complete release because it will show that the calls for baseless investigations of me are nothing more than political persecution and slander. **I was never a client of Epstein's** and never had any engagement with him other than fundraising for MIT."

**Disobedience Award**:
The MIT Media Lab gave **Jeffrey Epstein an "honorary orb"** in 2017 — a replica of the Lab's "Disobedience Award." The Disobedience Award was funded principally by Hoffman; Hoffman himself received an orb; Epstein received one; the following year, the same award went to #MeToo movement founders. The juxtaposition is in the public record and was extensively reported by *The Daily Beast*, *Vanity Fair*, and *Axios* in 2019.

**The November 2025 Epstein file releases** produced previously unknown emails between Epstein and Hoffman from the 2016-2017 period and prompted **Trump to publicly call for investigation of Hoffman** (Trump called Hoffman a "sleazebag" in his November 2025 Truth Social post). The investigation has been ordered by the Trump-administration DOJ but the consequences are uncertain as of May 2026.

### Political activity (Democratic-aligned principal of the Silicon Valley capital class)
Hoffman has been, since 2016, the **principal Democratic-aligned political donor** of the Silicon Valley capital class. The documented record of donations and political activity:

- **2016 cycle**: Roughly $7 million in documented contributions to Democratic candidates and PACs.
- **2018 cycle**: Roughly $20 million to Democratic candidates, with particular focus on House and Senate flip-target races.
- **2020 cycle**: Roughly $26 million in personal contributions; additional $35 million through his funded organizations (Investing in US, AlphaTexa) and PACs.
- **2022 cycle**: Roughly $17 million in documented contributions.
- **2024 cycle**: Roughly $20 million in personal contributions to Kamala Harris and Democratic-aligned PACs. Hoffman was, the **single largest individual Democratic donor** of the 2024 cycle, exceeding the contributions of George Soros (who reduced his political-donation activity in 2024 in favour of generational transition to his son Alex).
- **Lawsuit funding**: Hoffman has funded multiple lawsuits against Donald Trump, most consequentially the **E. Jean Carroll defamation case** that produced the $83.3 million January 2024 verdict against Trump. The funding was disclosed by Hoffman publicly; the working substance is that Carroll's litigation, which produced both the May 2023 sexual-abuse verdict and the January 2024 defamation verdict, was largely financed by Hoffman's personal contributions through the funding-of-litigation mechanism.
- **2024 election fraud-allegation defence**: Hoffman funded major elements of the 2020-2024 Democratic Party legal apparatus for responding to Trump-aligned election-fraud claims.

### The "Martyr" comment (2024)
- In a 2024 interview, Hoffman made a comment that was widely quoted as expressing a wish that he had "made President Trump an actual martyr." Hoffman subsequently apologized for the phrasing, saying: "I definitely regret having echoed the word martyr, which was pitched to me from the audience, and I echoed it." The comment was made during a period of intense political polarisation and was, in the captured-press framing, an embarrassment to the Democratic-donor coalition. Hoffman is the principal donor; his stray comments are read carefully.

### Current position
- Net worth (Forbes 2026): **$2.7 billion**.
- Active Greylock partner; AI investor; principal Democratic-aligned Silicon Valley political donor; subject of the Trump-administration DOJ investigation announced November 2025 in response to the Epstein-files release.
