## David Oliver Sacks

### Origins
- Born **25 May 1972, Cape Town, South Africa**.
- Jewish family. Emigrated to **Tennessee** (United States) with his family when he was five years old (1977).
- Raised in Memphis. South African origin, like Musk, but the family had emigrated specifically to leave the apartheid environment.

### Education
- **Memphis University School** (private K-12).
- **Stanford University**, BA Economics, 1994. **Co-editor of *The Stanford Review*** with Peter Thiel during the period — the same campus right-wing publication that produced Thiel's first ideological-formation outputs (Chapter 11).
- **University of Chicago Law School**, JD, 1998.

### *The Diversity Myth* (1995)
- Co-authored with Peter Thiel: *The Diversity Myth: Multiculturalism and the Politics of Intolerance at Stanford* (Independent Institute, 1995).
- The book is the principal early-career publication of both Thiel and Sacks. Chapter 11 documented the book's content in detail — including the **Keith Rabois "Faggot! Hope you die of AIDS!" episode** that the book defended as legitimate political speech, and the various **date-rape passages** that minimised reported assaults as "regretted seductions."
- Sacks is, with Thiel, co-author of the public record of those passages.
- Sacks has, in his subsequent public statements, **never retracted the content of *The Diversity Myth***. He has, in occasional interviews, expressed regret about specific phrasings while defending the book's broader political thesis.

### PayPal (1999-2002)
- Joined Confinity 1999. Became Chief Operating Officer of the merged PayPal company.
- Was, the principal product and operational executive of PayPal under Thiel.
- Received $30 million from the 2002 eBay acquisition.

### Yammer (2008-2012)
- Founded **2008**. Workplace social networking platform.
- **Sold to Microsoft June 2012 for $1.2 billion**. Sacks received $200 million personally.

### Zenefits (2016)
- **Interim CEO** of Zenefits for ten months in 2016, following the compliance scandal that forced founder Parker Conrad's resignation. Zenefits had been selling insurance products without proper state licensing; the SEC and multiple state insurance commissioners imposed fines. Sacks oversaw the company through the regulatory remediation.

### Craft Ventures (2017-present)
- Co-founded 2017 with Bill Lee.
- Roughly $3.5 billion AUM as of 2025.
- Portfolio: Affirm, Airbnb, Bird, Cameo, ClickUp, Cloudflare, Eventbrite, Houzz, Reddit, Robinhood, SpaceX (early), Substack, ThredUp, xAI.
- **dYdX, BitGo, Bitwise** (crypto positions, with Sacks involvement in the crypto-policy advocacy that has produced his White House appointment).

### Yelp board / chairmanship
- Sacks was Chairman of Yelp from 2008 to 2018. Yelp was, in the post-PayPal-Mafia investment pattern, one of the principal joint investments of the Levchin-Sacks-Hoffman group.

### Personal investments
Angel investments include Facebook, Uber, SpaceX, **Palantir Technologies**, Airbnb. Sacks is, like the other PayPal Mafia figures, structurally invested across the network's principal companies.

### Geni.com and Yammer entrepreneurship
- 2006: Founded Geni.com (genealogy website). Sold in 2012.

### Film production
- Sacks has been an active film producer. *Thank You for Smoking* (2005) — produced and financed by Sacks; satirical political film. *Dalíland* (2023) — biopic about Salvador Dalí.

### Political activity
- Through 2016: Sacks was politically active but did not have a clear partisan alignment. He had donated to candidates of both parties.
- 2020 cycle: contributions to multiple candidates.
- **2023**: Sacks **moderated the May 2023 Ron DeSantis presidential campaign announcement on Twitter Spaces** — the announcement that produced Twitter's technical failure during the high-profile event. Donated $50,000 to DeSantis.
- **June 2023**: Hosted a $10,000-per-plate fundraiser for **Robert F. Kennedy Jr.** (then running as a Democratic and subsequently Independent presidential candidate).
- **June 2024**: Hosted a fundraiser for **Donald Trump at his San Francisco home that raised $12 million** — one of the largest single Silicon Valley fundraising events of the 2024 cycle.
- **July 2024**: Speaker at the **Republican National Convention**. Used the speech to articulate the position that the US had "provoked" Russia to invade Ukraine.
- **2024 election**: Voted for Trump; was one of the principal Silicon Valley voices articulating the right-wing realignment.

### Russia / Ukraine position
- Sacks has, since October 2022, been one of the principal Silicon Valley public voices opposing US military assistance to Ukraine.
- His position has been that the US "provoked" Russia to invade Ukraine, that NATO expansion was the proximate cause of the war, and that US Ukraine assistance is a waste of taxpayer resources that should be redirected to domestic priorities.
- The position is consistent with the broader new-world-order-coalition position on the war (the Musk Crimean Bridge restriction, the Vance opposition to Ukraine aid, the Trump-administration peace negotiations with Putin) and is structurally connected to the broader geopolitical realignment the second Trump administration has been executing.

### All-In Podcast (2020-present)
- Co-hosts with **Chamath Palihapitiya** (Social Capital), **Jason Calacanis** (early Uber investor), and **David Friedberg** (The Production Board).
- One of the most-listened-to business and politics podcasts in the United States since 2022.
- The principal media platform through which the Silicon Valley right-wing political realignment of the 2022-2024 period was communicated to the broader Silicon Valley audience.

### The White House AI and Crypto Czar appointment
- **5 December 2024**: President-elect Trump announced Sacks's appointment as **White House AI and Crypto Czar**.
- The role: "guide policy for the Administration in Artificial Intelligence and Cryptocurrency" — a newly created position with explicit responsibility for the legal framework governing both industries.
- Trump also named Sacks to **lead the President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology** (PCAST).
- The appointment was, **the conversion of the PayPal Mafia network into direct executive-branch policy authority** over the two industries (AI, crypto) in which the network's investment portfolio is most concentrated.
- The conflict-of-interest concerns were extensively documented at the time. Sacks remained a Craft Ventures General Partner during his White House tenure — that is, he continued to make investments in companies that would benefit from the policies he was setting. The Trump administration's response was that Sacks's appointment was a "Special Government Employee" (SGE) designation (similar to Musk's DOGE arrangement) that did not require Senate confirmation or comprehensive financial-disclosure compliance.
- **Stepped down March 2026** after his 130-day SGE statutory maximum (with extensions) was exhausted.

### Current position
- Craft Ventures General Partner.
- Former White House AI and Crypto Czar (Dec 2024 – March 2026).
- Continuing co-host of *All-In* podcast.
- Net worth (Forbes 2026): **$2 billion**.

---

## Maksymilian Rafailovych "Max" Levchin

### Origins
- Born **11 July 1975, Kyiv, Ukrainian SSR** (then part of the Soviet Union).
- Father: Rafail Levchin — poet and playwright.
- Mother: Elvina Zlotnikova — theoretical physicist.
- Jewish family. The family emigrated to **Chicago, Illinois in 1991** when Max was sixteen, under political asylum on grounds of religious persecution.
- The Levchin family is late-Soviet Jewish emigration to the United States, of the cohort that produced large fractions of the post-Soviet American technology and finance industries.

### Education
- **Mather High School** (Chicago public high school), graduated 1993.
- **University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign**, BS Computer Science, 1997.

### Pre-PayPal ventures
- **SponsorNet New Media** (1995) — undergraduate venture.
- Moved to Silicon Valley after graduation. Met Peter Thiel through **Luke Nosek** (Levchin's college friend at Illinois who had subsequently moved to the Valley).

### PayPal / Confinity (1998-2002)
- Co-founded **Fieldlink** in 1998 with Thiel and Nosek. Renamed **Confinity** in 1999.
- Served as **Chief Technology Officer** of PayPal from the Confinity founding through the eBay acquisition.
- Principal technical architect of PayPal's anti-fraud systems.
- **Co-creator of the Gausebeck-Levchin test** — one of the first commercial implementations of a CAPTCHA challenge-response system, the technology that has subsequently become the standard mechanism for distinguishing human users from automated systems on Internet platforms.
- Received **$34 million** from the 2002 eBay acquisition.

### Slide (2004-2010)
- Founded 2004. Multimedia social-media application.
- Operated as one of the principal early Facebook-application companies.
- **Acquired by Google in August 2010 for $182 million**. Levchin received $40-50 million personally.
- Google shut down Slide in August 2011 — the outcome was that Levchin had been paid for the asset but the product itself was discontinued, a pattern common in the period's acqui-hire transactions.

### HVF (Hard, Valuable, Fun) (2012-present)
- Levchin's San Francisco-based innovation lab. Founded 2012.
- Two principal companies launched from HVF:
  - **Glow** (2013) — fertility-tracking application for couples trying to conceive. Levchin is Chairman.
  - **Affirm** (2012/2014) — the principal Levchin company, see below.

### Affirm (2012-present)
- Founded 2012; commercially launched 2014.
- **Buy-now-pay-later (BNPL)** consumer-credit lender. Provides point-of-sale loans for online purchases.
- The product structure: consumers can finance individual purchases (typically of expensive consumer goods — Peloton bikes, Reverb guitars, Casper mattresses, the various direct-to-consumer brands that emerged in the period) with installment payments over 3, 6, 12, or 24 months. The interest rates range from 0% (subsidised by the merchant) to 30% APR.
- The principal Affirm policy controversy is that the BNPL product structure permits consumers to take on debt without the credit-card regulatory framework that would otherwise apply. The product has been particularly successful with younger consumers who do not have credit-card debt and who use Affirm as their principal consumer-credit mechanism. The product has been criticised by consumer-protection advocates as a regulatory-arbitrage instrument that permits the issuance of consumer credit outside the standard regulatory framework.
- **Affirm IPO 13 January 2021**: opening price $90, closed first day at $97. The IPO valued Affirm at $24 billion.
- Affirm's post-IPO performance has been volatile. By 2026 the company is valued at $20 billion.

### Yelp involvement
- Levchin was an early investor in Yelp and served as **Chairman of Yelp** (in the period during which Sacks was also on the board).
- Became Yelp's largest shareholder in 2012.

### Personal investments
- Evernote, Pinterest, Yelp, multiple others.
- Much smaller personal investment portfolio than Hoffman or Sacks.

### Political activity
- Levchin has, throughout his career, been much less politically active than the other PayPal Mafia principals. He has not been a major donor to either party.
- Has spoken publicly about his Ukrainian-Jewish origins; was, in 2022-2024, one of the few PayPal Mafia figures publicly supportive of Ukrainian self-defense against Russian invasion (a position that placed him in operational disagreement with Sacks's anti-Ukraine position).

### Current position
- Affirm Founder and CEO.
- Glow Chairman.
- Net worth (Forbes 2026): **$2 billion** (down from a peak of $2.5 billion at the Affirm post-IPO high).

---

## The PayPal Mafia — the structural mapping

The "PayPal Mafia" is the documented network of 12-15 individuals who held senior positions at Confinity, X.com, or the merged PayPal during the 1999-2002 period and who subsequently founded or led the principal companies of the post-2002 Silicon Valley wave. The members, with their post-PayPal trajectories:

- **Peter Thiel** — Founders Fund, Palantir, the political-operative network (Chapter 11).
- **Elon Musk** — SpaceX, Tesla, X/xAI, DOGE (Chapter 12).
- **Reid Hoffman** — LinkedIn, Greylock, Microsoft board, Democratic political donor (this chapter).
- **Max Levchin** — Affirm, Slide, Yelp (this chapter).
- **David Sacks** — Yammer, Craft Ventures, AI and Crypto Czar (this chapter).
- **Ken Howery** — Founders Fund, US Ambassador to Sweden 2019-2021 (Chapter 11).
- **Luke Nosek** — Founders Fund, Gigafund (Chapter 11).
- **Roelof Botha** — Senior Partner at **Sequoia Capital** (since 2003); Sequoia's principal in the modern period. Botha is the PayPal Mafia integration with the principal traditional Silicon Valley venture-capital firm.
- **Jeremy Stoppelman** — Co-founder of **Yelp** (2004).
- **Russel Simmons** — Co-founder of Yelp.
- **Jawed Karim** — Co-founder of **YouTube** (2005, with Steve Chen and Chad Hurley). The PayPal-YouTube founding lineage is one of the most consequential consumer-Internet companies (YouTube) being founded by PayPal alumni.
- **Steve Chen** — Co-founder of YouTube. Chinese-Taiwanese immigrant. PayPal engineer 1999-2005.
- **Chad Hurley** — Co-founder of YouTube. PayPal designer.
- **Jack Selby** — Co-founder of **Clarium Capital** with Thiel; later partner at Thiel Capital.

The structural mapping has been extensively documented in popular Silicon Valley histories (Sarah Lacy's *Once You're Lucky, Twice You're Good*, 2008; Jimmy Soni's *The Founders*, 2022; the various PayPal documentaries) and is uncontroversial as a description. The observation is that the same fifteen individuals have, over the past two decades, executed the principal founding and senior-investment positions of the principal companies of the post-2002 Silicon Valley wave. The network is the structure; the structure is the network.

# Sources — Chapter 16: Jeff Bezos

All claims verified 12 May 2026.

## Origins and family

- **Jeffrey Preston Bezos** (originally Jeffrey Preston Jorgensen): born **12 January 1964 in Albuquerque, New Mexico**.
- **Mother**: **Jacklyn Gise Jorgensen Bezos** — was **17 years old** at his birth (a teenage mother who had married his biological father shortly before). She subsequently divorced Ted Jorgensen and remarried Miguel "Mike" Bezos in 1968.
- **Biological father**: **Ted Jorgensen** (1944-2015), a unicycle-and-bicycle shop owner in Albuquerque. He had no further contact with Jeff after the divorce; he was tracked down by author Brad Stone in 2012 (then 69, running a bike shop in Glendale, Arizona) for the book *The Everything Store*; Jorgensen said he had not known what had become of his son. He died March 2015.
- **Adoptive father (legal father from 1968)**: **Miguel Ángel "Mike" Bezos** (born 1945) — a **Cuban refugee who arrived in the United States in 1962 as part of Operation Pedro Pan**, the CIA-State-Department-Catholic-Charities programme that brought 14,000 unaccompanied Cuban children to the US between 1960-1962 in the period after the Bay of Pigs invasion. Mike Bezos worked his way through the University of Albuquerque, became a petroleum engineer at Exxon, and was Jacklyn's second husband. He legally adopted Jeff (and Jeff's surname became Bezos).
- **Maternal grandfather**: **Lawrence Preston Gise** (1915-1995). This is the most operationally consequential family figure in Jeff Bezos's biography. Gise was a senior career official at the **US Atomic Energy Commission (AEC)** — the agency that controlled US nuclear-weapons development, production, and storage from 1946 through the AEC's dissolution in 1974. Gise was **director of the AEC's Albuquerque operations office** during the period covering major elements of the postwar nuclear-weapons programme. He subsequently retired to operate a **25,000-acre ranch near Cotulla, Texas**, where Jeff Bezos spent his summers from age 4 to 16. The Gise ranch was the foundational childhood-and-adolescence environment.
- **Maternal grandmother**: Mattie Gise.
- **Siblings**: Christina Bezos and Mark Bezos (both younger; both children of Jacklyn and Mike).

The Gise maternal-grandfather connection is the recorded case in which Bezos's family-of-origin was directly integrated with the senior US national-security apparatus — the AEC was the agency that controlled the nuclear weapons programme, and Gise was a senior AEC career official during the post-Manhattan-Project consolidation period.

## Education

- Miami Palmetto Senior High School, valedictorian, class of 1982. Florida Silver Knight Award winner. Voted "Best Science Student."
- **Princeton University**, BSE Electrical Engineering and Computer Science, summa cum laude, 1986. Phi Beta Kappa. Senior thesis on chip layout algorithms.
- Initially considered theoretical physics; switched to computer science after concluding (per his own account) that he was not going to be among the very best physicists.

## Pre-Amazon career

- 1986-1988: **Fitel** (financial telecommunications start-up, New York). 
- 1988-1990: **Bankers Trust** (rose to youngest VP).
- 1990-1994: **D.E. Shaw & Co.** — the quantitative-trading hedge fund founded by David E. Shaw. Bezos rose to **Senior Vice President at age 30**. The D.E. Shaw period is Bezos's training in the quantitative-finance-and-arbitrage techniques that subsequently became Amazon's foundational operational doctrine. At D.E. Shaw, Bezos was responsible for studying business opportunities created by the early Internet; he produced an internal report identifying online retail as a major commercial opportunity. When the firm declined to pursue the opportunity at the scale Bezos thought warranted, Bezos left to found Amazon.

## Amazon founding (1994)

- Founded **5 July 1994** in **Bellevue, Washington**, originally as Cadabra Inc.; renamed **Amazon.com** by November 1994.
- The standard founding story: Bezos drove from New York to Seattle with his wife MacKenzie (then MacKenzie Tuttle, now MacKenzie Scott), drafting the business plan in the car. Operated out of a garage in Bellevue. Initial capital **$250,000** from Mike and Jacklyn Bezos (Mike Bezos's Exxon retirement savings). The $250,000 family investment is documented in multiple subsequent retrospectives; Mike Bezos's stake at the 1997 IPO was reportedly worth $300 million.
- Initial focus on books was selected from a list of 20 product categories Bezos analyzed. Books offered: (1) a vastly larger catalogue than any physical bookstore could maintain, (2) standardised products that did not require physical inspection before purchase, (3) the existence of two large wholesale distributors (Ingram, Baker & Taylor) whose inventory was already catalogued, and (4) a relatively low average product price that reduced the financial risk of consumer fraud.

## Amazon IPO (1997)

- IPO **15 May 1997** at $18 per share. First-day close: $23.50. Market capitalisation at IPO: **$438 million**.
- The 1997 IPO is the documented event after which Bezos became wealthy; it preceded the dot-com bubble peak by three years.
- Amazon survived the 2000-2002 dot-com collapse by raising $2 billion in debt during the late-1999 / early-2000 period; the survival permitted the subsequent two-decade expansion.

## Amazon expansion (1997-2021)

- 1998: Expanded beyond books to music, DVDs, video games, electronics.
- 1999: Launched Amazon Marketplace (third-party seller platform).
- 2002: Launched Amazon Web Services (initial release of web-services infrastructure).
- 2005: Launched **Amazon Prime** (the loyalty/subscription programme that has been one of the foundational operational innovations of the platform).
- **2006**: Launched **AWS** (Elastic Compute Cloud, Simple Storage Service). AWS subsequently became the principal global cloud-computing infrastructure platform.
- 2007: Launched Kindle.
- 2009: Acquired Zappos for $1.2 billion.
- 2014: Launched Echo (Alexa smart speakers).
- 2017: Acquired **Whole Foods** for **$13.7 billion**.
- 2019: Acquired Ring (home surveillance) for $1 billion.
- 2021: Acquired **MGM** for **$8.45 billion** (entertainment-content library).
- **July 2021**: Bezos stepped down as Amazon CEO; **Andy Jassy** (previously head of AWS) became CEO. Bezos retained the **Executive Chairman** role.

## Federal government contracts and the national-security apparatus

- **2013**: AWS won a **$600 million CIA cloud computing contract** — one of the foundational contracts in the federal-government cloud-migration programme. The contract integrated AWS as the principal commercial cloud provider for the US intelligence community.
- **2019**: The **Pentagon JEDI ($10 billion cloud computing contract)** was awarded to Microsoft. Amazon sued the federal government in November 2019 claiming the award was politically motivated — that Trump had "improperly pressured" the Pentagon to award the contract to Microsoft "to harm his perceived political enemy" Bezos. Amazon's lawsuit was the documented case in which Bezos publicly accused Trump of corruption in federal contracting.
- **2021**: JEDI was cancelled. Replaced by the **Joint Warfighting Cloud Capability (JWCC)** — a multi-vendor contract worth $9 billion that was awarded to Amazon, Google, Microsoft, and Oracle in December 2022.
- **2024-2026**: AWS is the principal commercial cloud provider for the Department of Defense, the CIA, the NSA, and large portions of the federal civilian agencies.

## Amazon's current operating scale (2026)

- Revenue (2025 full year): **$650 billion**.
- AWS annual revenue: **$110 billion** (the principal profit centre of the company).
- Employees: **1.5 million worldwide**.
- Market capitalisation (May 2026): **$2.2 trillion**.

## Amazon board (May 2026)

- **Andy Jassy** (CEO, since July 2021).
- **Daniel Huttenlocher** (MIT computer-science professor, Schwarzman College Dean).
- **Patty Stonesifer** — **former CEO of the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation** (2000-2008). Stonesifer's presence on the Amazon board is the Gates Foundation-Amazon integration.
- **Indra Nooyi** — former CEO of PepsiCo.
- **Edith Cooper** — former Goldman Sachs executive.
- **Jonathan Rubinstein** — former Apple senior executive.
- **Brad Smith** — Microsoft Vice Chair and President (joined Amazon board 2024 — the case of the Big-Tech cross-board integration).
- **Wendell Weeks** — Corning CEO.
- **Andrew Ng** — Stanford AI professor, Coursera founder.
- **Keith Alexander** — **former Director of the National Security Agency** (2005-2014), founder of IronNet Cybersecurity. Alexander's presence on the Amazon board is the NSA-Amazon-AWS integration.
- **Judith McGrath** — former MTV Networks chair.

The board composition documents the structural integration of Amazon with the national-security apparatus (Alexander = NSA), the Gates Foundation (Stonesifer), and Microsoft (Smith).

## Blue Origin (2000-present)

- Founded **2000** by Bezos — the first contemporary private space-launch company. Predates SpaceX (2002) by two years.
- Headquartered in Kent, Washington.
- CEO: **Dave Limp** (former Amazon devices senior VP; appointed CEO 2023).
- Principal vehicles:
  - **New Shepard** — suborbital rocket; operational since 2015.
  - **New Glenn** — orbital heavy-lift rocket; first successful orbital flight **January 2025**.
- **Federal contracts**: NASA Human Landing System contract ($3.4 billion, 2023); various Department of Defense contracts.
- Personal funding: Bezos has reportedly funded Blue Origin at $1 billion per year from his personal Amazon-share sales.
- **20 July 2021**: Bezos's first personal spaceflight on New Shepard.
- **14 April 2025**: Bezos's fiancée Lauren Sánchez flew on a New Shepard mission with an all-female crew.

Blue Origin is the second principal private space-launch company globally (after SpaceX), though it operates at one-tenth the launch cadence and revenue scale.

## The Washington Post acquisition (2013-present)

- **August 2013**: Bezos purchased **The Washington Post Company's newspaper assets** for **$250 million in cash**. The acquisition was a personal investment by Bezos, **not an Amazon transaction**.
- Bezos's stated rationale: the institutional importance of an independent national newspaper, the operational opportunity to apply technology-driven transformation to a struggling print-newspaper business.
- 2013-2021: Under executive editor **Marty Baron** and publisher Fred Ryan, the *Post* expanded sharply; digital subscriptions grew from 100,000 to over 3 million by 2020. The *Post* won 18 Pulitzer Prizes during the Baron period.
- The *Post* adopted the slogan "**Democracy Dies in Darkness**" in February 2017 — explicitly framed as a response to the first Trump administration.

## Will Lewis appointment (January 2024)

- **January 2024**: Bezos appointed **Will Lewis** as Publisher and CEO of The Washington Post. Lewis had been editor of *The Wall Street Journal* (2014-2018) and CEO of Dow Jones (2014-2020); his tenure at News Corp had been associated with **the UK phone-hacking scandal** at News Corp's UK newspapers, in which Lewis had been a senior figure during the 2011 cover-up phase.
- The Lewis appointment was controversial within the *Post* newsroom. Multiple senior reporters opposed it on grounds of Lewis's phone-hacking-cover-up involvement; multiple senior departures followed (executive editor Sally Buzbee resigned June 2024).

## The Harris endorsement killing (October 2024)

- The most documented case of Bezos imposing editorial restrictions on the *Post* concerns the **2024 presidential election endorsement**.
- The *Post* editorial board had **drafted an endorsement of Vice President Kamala Harris** over Donald Trump. The endorsement had been written by editorial-page editor **David Shipley** and editorial board members **Charles Lane** and **Stephen W. Stromberg**. Per *Columbia Journalism Review* reporting and the *Post*'s own subsequent acknowledgment, the editorial-page editor had told the board that "this is obviously something our owner has an interest in" during the drafting period.
- **Friday, 25 October 2024** (eleven days before the election): Will Lewis announced that the *Post* would not endorse a presidential candidate "in this election. Nor in any future presidential election." The announcement was framed as "returning to our roots of not endorsing presidential candidates."
- The *Post*'s own reporting (published the same day, by two staff reporters): "**The decision not to publish was made by The Post's owner — Amazon founder Jeff Bezos**, according to the same sources."
- The decision came as **Bezos was visiting Washington in the days before the announcement**; multiple sources confirmed Bezos's direct role.
- Lewis publicly denied that Bezos had read the drafted endorsement, saying Bezos "was not sent, did not read and did not opine on any draft." The denial was contradicted by the *Post*'s own reporting and by the editorial-board members who had been told that the owner "has an interest in" the decision.
- **Marty Baron**, the former executive editor of the *Post* (2012-2021, the executive editor who oversaw the Watergate-era newspaper Bezos acquired): "**This is cowardice, with democracy as its casualty**." Baron added: "Trump will see this as an invitation to further intimidate owner Jeff Bezos (and others). Disturbing spinelessness at an institution famed for courage."
- **Robert Kagan**, *Post* editor-at-large: resigned in protest.
- **Roughly 250,000 *Post* subscribers cancelled their subscriptions** in the week following the announcement — 10% of the paper's paid digital subscriber base.
- The endorsement-killing was simultaneous with a similar incident at the **Los Angeles Times**, where owner **Patrick Soon-Shiong** had blocked a Harris endorsement. The LA Times's editorial-page editor Mariel Garza resigned in protest.

## The 2024-2025 political turn

- **December 2024**: Bezos personally donated **$1 million to the Trump inauguration committee** — the maximum permitted single contribution at the time.
- **20 January 2025**: Bezos attended **Trump's inauguration**, **seated on the inauguration platform** with the other senior technology executives (Mark Zuckerberg, Sundar Pichai, Tim Cook, Elon Musk, Sam Altman). The photograph of the tech-billionaire row at the inauguration is one of the most operationally significant images of the new world order's public-self-presentation.
- February 2025: Bezos announced editorial restrictions at the *Washington Post*: the opinion pages would, under new editorial guidelines, defend two "pillars" — "personal liberties and free markets" — and would not publish opinions that contradicted those pillars. The editorial-page editor David Shipley resigned.
- The February 2025 announcement is an *American newspaper owner imposing an explicit ideological restriction on the editorial pages of the newspaper*. It is, structurally similar to the historical newspaper-baron behaviour of William Randolph Hearst in the early twentieth century — and structurally distinct from the post-1960s American norm of editorial-page independence from owner direction.

## The phone-hacking incident (2018-2020)

The most operationally consequential personal-life-and-political-economy intersection in Bezos's biography is his phone being hacked, allegedly via a WhatsApp message from Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, and the subsequent National Enquirer publication of details of his extramarital relationship with Lauren Sánchez.

**Background**:
- **September 2017**: Bezos's *Washington Post* began publishing a series of opinion columns by **Jamal Khashoggi** — a Saudi journalist who had become a US-based contributor to the *Post* and who was critical of Saudi Arabia and the Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman.
- **April 2018**: Bezos and Mohammed bin Salman exchanged phone numbers at a Hollywood dinner during MBS's tour of US technology companies.

**The hack**:
- **1 May 2018**: Per UN Special Rapporteurs Agnès Callamard and David Kaye and per the FTI Consulting forensic analysis commissioned by Bezos: "A message from the Crown Prince account was sent to Mr. Bezos through WhatsApp. The message is an encrypted video file. It is later established, with reasonable certainty, that the video's downloader infects Mr. Bezos' phone with malicious code."
- The forensic analysis (conducted by FTI Consulting between February and November 2019, published by *The Guardian* on 21 January 2020 and the full report by *Motherboard* on 23 January 2020) concluded with "**medium to high confidence**" that Bezos's phone had been hacked via the MBS-account video file.
- The hack resulted in "**dramatically higher amounts of data**" being transmitted from Bezos's phone in the months following the May 2018 message. The investigators concluded that several gigabytes of data had been exfiltrated.
- The investigators identified **Saud al-Qahtani** — MBS's senior media advisor — as the likely operational executor of the hack. Saud al-Qahtani had been implicated in the **murder of Jamal Khashoggi** in the Saudi consulate in Istanbul on 2 October 2018.

**The Khashoggi assassination**:
- **2 October 2018**: Khashoggi was murdered and dismembered in the Saudi consulate in Istanbul by a 15-person Saudi team. The CIA's subsequent assessment concluded that MBS had personally ordered the killing. The Khashoggi murder produced one of the most consequential US-Saudi diplomatic crises of the post-2000 period.
- Following the murder, the *Washington Post* (Bezos's newspaper) increased sharply the volume and intensity of its critical coverage of Saudi Arabia and MBS personally.

**The National Enquirer episode**:
- **9 January 2019**: *The National Enquirer* published a story detailing Bezos's extramarital relationship with Lauren Sánchez, including text messages and explicit photographs.
- The *Enquirer* was owned by **American Media Inc. (AMI)**, whose CEO was **David Pecker** — a long-time Trump ally and the executor of the "catch-and-kill" operations on behalf of Trump during the 2016 election (the Stormy Daniels payment, the Karen McDougal payment, etc.).
- **30 January 2019**: Bezos and his wife MacKenzie announced their divorce.
- **7 February 2019**: Bezos published a Medium post titled "**No thank you, Mr. Pecker**" in which he disclosed that AMI had attempted to extort him with the threat of publishing additional explicit photographs. Bezos published the AMI's email demands verbatim; the emails included specific demands that Bezos publicly affirm that AMI's coverage of him "**was not politically motivated or influenced by political forces**." The Bezos Medium post is a sitting CEO of a major US technology company publicly refusing to comply with a tabloid-extortion demand and publishing the extortion communications.
- Bezos's chief of security **Gavin de Becker** subsequently published a *Daily Beast* column (March 2019) alleging that "**the Saudis had access to Bezos's phone**" and that the National Enquirer's information had been obtained through the Saudi-linked hack.
- AMI's response (March 2019): the only source for the *Enquirer*'s Bezos story was **Michael Sanchez** — Lauren Sánchez's brother — and there was "no involvement by any other third party whatsoever."
- A year later (March 2020), Michael Sanchez sued AMI, claiming that when the *Enquirer* first contacted him they already had "raunchy text messages and nude selfies" of Bezos and Lauren Sánchez. Sanchez denied giving AMI the explicit photographs and accused AMI of having hacked Bezos's phone.

**The November 2018 image**:
- Per the FTI Consulting report, in **November 2018** — that is, before the Bezos-Sánchez affair had become public — MBS sent Bezos a WhatsApp message containing an image of a woman who resembled Lauren Sánchez. The image was sent before the affair was publicly known. The implication, drawn explicitly by the FTI report and the UN Special Rapporteurs, was that MBS had access to information about the affair before it was public — that is, that the May 2018 hack had given the Saudi operation access to the personal communications that documented the affair.

**The February 2019 message**:
- Per the FTI report, in February 2019 — after the National Enquirer story had been published — MBS sent Bezos a WhatsApp message urging him **not to believe everything**, after Bezos had been briefed on the phone about an internet campaign against him being conducted by Saudi-linked accounts. The message is documented as further circumstantial evidence of the MBS-network's operational awareness of the Bezos surveillance and of its political utility.

**The official US response**:
- The FBI investigated the alleged hack from 2019 through 2021. **December 2021**: The FBI publicly stated it could not find proof to substantiate the claims that Saudi Arabia had hacked Bezos's phone, and "considered an investigation into those allegations a low priority."
- The FBI's low-priority designation was inconsistent with the available forensic evidence (the FTI Consulting report had concluded with medium-to-high confidence that MBS's account had been the source of the hack) and is the verified case in which the US national-security apparatus declined to investigate the alleged Saudi hack of one of the most prominent US business executives — a decision that occurred during the same period in which the Saudi-US security relationship was being maintained despite the Khashoggi murder.

## Bezos's philanthropic activity

- **Bezos Earth Fund** ($10 billion committed 2020). Major grants for climate and environmental causes through 2024-2025.
- **Day 1 Fund** (homelessness and education). Much smaller than the Earth Fund.
- Per various analyses: Bezos's actual philanthropic disbursements have been much smaller than the committed amounts, and much smaller as a fraction of his wealth than MacKenzie Scott (who has, since the 2019 divorce, given away $18 billion to 2,500 charities).

## Personal life and the post-2019 trajectory

- **MacKenzie Tuttle / Bezos / Scott**: Bezos's wife from 1993 to 2019. Princeton graduate; novelist. Worked at D.E. Shaw with Bezos. Was the first Amazon employee. The 2019 divorce produced a settlement in which MacKenzie received **25% of Bezos's Amazon stake** — at the time of the settlement, **$38 billion**. MacKenzie subsequently took her middle name Scott as her surname; she has, since 2019, given $18 billion to 2,500 charities, principally through her **Yield Giving** initiative. MacKenzie Scott's giving is, by most analyses, the largest and most effective single-individual contemporary philanthropic programme in the United States.
- **Lauren Sánchez** (born 19 December 1969, Albuquerque, NM — coincidentally also Bezos's birthplace). Former TV news anchor (Fox's *Good Day LA* 2011-2017). Founder of Black Ops Aviation (aerial-cinematography production company). Helicopter pilot. Previously married to **Patrick Whitesell** (CEO of Endeavor / William Morris) from 2005 to 2019. Two children with Whitesell.
- **Engagement** May 2023; **wedding 27 June 2025 in Venice**.
- The Venice wedding was estimated at **$40-55 million in total cost**. Roughly 200-250 guests. Held at the basilica of San Giorgio Maggiore and the Venetian Arsenal. Guests included **Bill Gates, Sam Altman, Ivanka Trump, Jared Kushner, Oprah Winfrey, Kim Kardashian, Leonardo DiCaprio, Tom Brady, Mick Jagger, Queen Rania of Jordan, the Crown Prince of Jordan**, and 200 others. **Roughly 90 private jets** were used to transport guests.
- The wedding produced protest from **No Space for Bezos** — a coalition of Venetian organisations and Greenpeace and other groups that opposed the use of Venice as a setting for billionaire spectacles. A banner displayed in Piazza San Marco read: "**If you can rent Venice for your wedding you can pay more tax.**"

## Current position (May 2026)

- Net worth (Forbes 2026, most recent estimate): **$234 billion** (third or fourth wealthiest individual globally; behind Musk, Ellison, Zuckerberg in various rankings).
- Active positions: **Executive Chairman of Amazon**; **Founder and Funder of Blue Origin**; **Owner of The Washington Post**; **Chair of the Bezos Earth Fund**.
- Continuing involvement with the Trump administration through the inauguration-committee donation, the inauguration attendance, and the documented post-January 2025 editorial restrictions at the *Washington Post*.

# Sources — Chapter 17: Mark Zuckerberg

All claims verified 12 May 2026.

## Origins and family

- **Mark Elliot Zuckerberg**: born **14 May 1984** in White Plains, New York.
- Raised in **Dobbs Ferry, New York** (a small affluent village in Westchester County, 25 miles north of Manhattan).
- **Father**: **Edward "Ed" Zuckerberg** (born 1953) — practicing dentist; ran his dental practice "Dr. Z's" out of the family home in Dobbs Ferry. The practice was, by all documented accounts, a small private dental practice serving the affluent Westchester clientele.
- **Mother**: **Karen Kempner Zuckerberg** (born 1958) — psychiatrist by training; left her practice to manage her husband's dental practice and to raise the children.
- Jewish family. The Zuckerbergs trace their ancestry to Austrian, German, and Polish Jewish lineages. Mark was bar mitzvah'd in 1997; the bar mitzvah was *Star Wars*-themed, a detail that has been repeatedly cited in subsequent biographical profiles as illustrative of the family's accommodation of Mark's particular interests.
- **Siblings**: three sisters — **Randi Zuckerberg** (born 1982; former Facebook marketing director, subsequently entrepreneur and author), **Donna Zuckerberg** (classicist; PhD in Classics from Princeton 2014; subsequent writer on the men's rights movement and antiquity), and **Arielle Zuckerberg** (venture-capital investor at Long Journey Ventures).

The Zuckerberg family-of-origin is, the contemporary American Jewish upper-middle-class professional family — both parents medical professionals, four children all educated at elite universities, an affluent suburban household.

## Education

- **Ardsley High School** (briefly, 1998-2000) — public high school in Westchester County.
- **Phillips Exeter Academy** (2000-2002) — the New Hampshire boarding school that has been one of the principal feeders to Harvard and the broader Ivy League since its 1781 founding. Zuckerberg attended Exeter on partial scholarship. While at Exeter he excelled in classical literature, mathematics, and computer science; he developed (with Adam D'Angelo, a future OpenAI board member and Quora founder) the early music-recommendation software **Synapse Media Player**, which received recruitment offers from Microsoft and AOL in 2002.
- **Harvard University** (2002-2004) — entered as a computer-science and psychology dual major; dropped out in his sophomore year to focus on Facebook. Zuckerberg received an **honorary degree** from Harvard in 2017, where he delivered the commencement address.

## The Facebook founding (2003-2004)

The Facebook founding chronology, per the documented court records, public statements, and subsequent biographical accounts:

**October 2003**: Zuckerberg created **Facemash** — a website that displayed two Harvard student photographs (obtained by hacking Harvard's residential-house directories) and asked users to rate which was "hotter." The site received 22,000 visits in its first four hours; Harvard administration shut it down within days. Zuckerberg was charged with breach of security, violating copyrights, and violating individual privacy; he faced expulsion but received only academic probation.

**November 2003**: Cameron Winklevoss, Tyler Winklevoss, and Divya Narendra approached Zuckerberg to work on their **HarvardConnection** project (later renamed **ConnectU**) — a social-networking site they had been developing since December 2002. Zuckerberg agreed to write the site's code.

**4 February 2004**: Zuckerberg launched **TheFacebook.com** from his Harvard dorm room. The site was, structurally similar to the HarvardConnection project; the Winklevoss brothers and Narendra subsequently alleged that Zuckerberg had deliberately stalled their project while building TheFacebook from their idea.

**Co-founders**: Eduardo Saverin (provided initial $1,000 capital for site hosting), Dustin Moskovitz (engineering co-founder), Chris Hughes (communications), and Andrew McCollum (designer). Zuckerberg retained the largest equity share.

**ConnectU lawsuit (2004-2008)**: The Winklevoss-Narendra-ConnectU group sued Zuckerberg in September 2004 alleging breach of contract, copyright infringement, and theft of trade secrets. The case settled in **June 2008** for **$65 million** ($20 million cash plus Facebook shares valued at $45 million at the time; the shares subsequently appreciated to $300 million by Facebook's 2012 IPO).

**Eduardo Saverin dilution (2005)**: In a 2005 corporate restructuring, Zuckerberg engineered the dilution of Saverin's stake from 30 percent to 0.03 percent. Saverin sued in 2009; the case was settled out of court with terms that restored Saverin's equity to 5 percent of Facebook. The Saverin episode is the subject of David Fincher's 2010 film *The Social Network*, based on Ben Mezrich's book *The Accidental Billionaires*.

**Sean Parker** (Napster co-founder) was Facebook's first president (2004-2005); resigned after a cocaine-possession arrest at a beach house with Facebook interns present (no charges filed). Parker's later book and various interviews confirmed that he and Zuckerberg explicitly designed Facebook's UX around **dopamine reward loops** to maximise engagement — what Parker has subsequently called a "social-validation feedback loop... exactly the kind of thing that a hacker like myself would come up with, because you're exploiting a vulnerability in human psychology."

## Facebook's first investments and the corporate trajectory (2004-2012)

- **September 2004**: Peter Thiel made his **$500,000 angel investment** in Facebook (Chapter 11), in exchange for 10 percent of the company. Thiel's stake was, the first venture-capital investment in the company.
- **2005**: Accel Partners led a $12.7 million Series A round at a $87.5 million valuation.
- **2006**: Greylock Partners and Meritech Capital led a $25 million Series B round.
- **2007**: Microsoft invested $240 million for a 1.6 percent stake — valuing Facebook at $15 billion. The Microsoft investment was, the foundational moment of the Microsoft-Facebook strategic relationship that has subsequently shaped multiple Big Tech-policy decisions.
- **2008**: Sheryl Sandberg joined as Chief Operating Officer. Sandberg, previously VP of Online Sales at Google, was the active executive hire that converted Facebook from a product into a business. Sandberg remained COO until June 2022.
- **2010**: Goldman Sachs investment at $50 billion valuation.
- **2012 IPO**: **18 May 2012**, Nasdaq, opening price $38 per share, valuing the company at $104 billion. The IPO was, at the time, the largest US Internet IPO. The first-day trading was characterised by technical failures at Nasdaq and declines below the $38 offering price; the stock did not recover to $38 until August 2013.

## Major acquisitions

- **April 2012**: Acquired **Instagram** for $1 billion (cash plus stock; final value $1 billion at closing). Instagram had 30 million users, 13 employees, and zero revenue at the time of acquisition. The acquisition is, in retrospect, one of the most operationally successful acquisitions in the contemporary American business record — Instagram has subsequently grown to 2 billion monthly active users and $50 billion in annual advertising revenue.
- **February 2014**: Acquired **WhatsApp** for **$19 billion** (cash plus stock). WhatsApp had 450 million users and 55 employees at the time of acquisition. The acquisition was, at the time, the largest acquisition of a venture-capital-backed company in history.
- **March 2014**: Acquired **Oculus VR** for **$2 billion** — Facebook's entry into virtual-reality hardware. Oculus subsequently became the foundation of Meta's "metaverse" investment programme.
- **October 2021**: The corporate parent company **renamed from "Facebook, Inc." to "Meta Platforms, Inc."** — the rebrand explicitly framed as the working pivot toward virtual-reality / metaverse / mixed-reality investment. The rebrand was widely criticised at the time as a public-relations response to the **Frances Haugen whistleblower disclosures** (September 2021), which had documented Facebook's internal awareness of Instagram's psychological harms to teenage users.

## The principal scandals and crises

### Cambridge Analytica (2018)
- **March 2018**: *The Observer* and *The New York Times* published the **Cambridge Analytica scandal**: Cambridge Analytica, a UK-based political-consulting firm that had been engaged by the Trump 2016 campaign and by the Brexit "Leave.EU" campaign, had obtained the personal data of **87 million Facebook users without their consent** through a personality-quiz app (Aleksandr Kogan's "thisisyourdigitallife" app). The data had been used for psychographic micro-targeting of political advertisements.
- The scandal produced congressional testimony by Zuckerberg in **April 2018** before the US Senate and House. The hearings were the case of Zuckerberg's first major public appearance under hostile questioning; his performance was characterised by extensive deflection and the documented "I'll have my team get back to you" responses.
- **July 2019**: FTC imposed a **$5 billion fine** on Facebook — at the time the largest privacy-related fine in US history. The fine was smaller as a fraction of Facebook's wealth than the underlying violations would have suggested; the FTC settlement was, in subsequent commentary, characterised as a portfolio-management decision by Facebook to settle the regulatory exposure at a manageable cost.

### The 2016 election interference
- The documented Russian Internet Research Agency operation on Facebook during the 2016 US presidential election produced 80,000 pieces of content reaching 126 million Americans. Facebook's internal awareness of the operation was, per the Frances Haugen documents and the Mueller Report, earlier than Facebook's public acknowledgment.
- Zuckerberg's initial response (November 2016): the suggestion that Russian-influenced misinformation on Facebook had affected the election outcome was "a pretty crazy idea." Zuckerberg subsequently apologised for the dismissal.

### The Rohingya genocide (2017-2018)
- Facebook's platform was, per the **UN Human Rights Council fact-finding mission report (August 2018)**, a "**determining role**" in the incitement of the Rohingya genocide in Myanmar. The platform's content-moderation systems, which operated almost entirely in English, had failed to detect and remove Burmese-language anti-Rohingya hate speech that had preceded and accompanied the Myanmar military's August 2017 ethnic-cleansing operation against the Rohingya Muslim minority.
- The Rohingya genocide produced 25,000 deaths and 750,000 refugees. Facebook's role was, in the UN's analysis, operationally significant; the platform's failure to invest in Burmese-language content moderation was the platform-monopoly's externalities producing one of the most consequential mass-atrocity events of the contemporary period.
- Facebook subsequently banned the Myanmar military's senior commanders from the platform (August 2018) — the first time the platform had banned the leadership of a national military. The ban occurred after, not before, the genocide.

### The Frances Haugen disclosures (September-October 2021)
- **September 2021**: *The Wall Street Journal* published the **"Facebook Files"** — a series of investigative reports based on internal Facebook documents leaked by **Frances Haugen**, a former Facebook product manager on the company's Civic Integrity team.
- The disclosures documented:
  - Facebook's internal research had established that Instagram was harmful to teenage girls' mental health — specifically that 13.5% of teenage girls said Instagram made suicidal thoughts worse and 17% said it made eating disorders worse.
  - Facebook's content-moderation systems were much less effective than the company had publicly claimed.
  - Facebook's algorithmic-amplification mechanisms had been documented internally as amplifying outrage, anger, and divisive content because that was what produced the highest engagement.
  - Facebook's internal investigation of the January 6, 2021 Capitol insurrection had documented the platform's role in coordinating the attack.
- Haugen testified before the US Senate on **5 October 2021**. The testimony was, one of the most operationally consequential whistleblower events of the period.

### The January 6, 2021 Trump ban
- Following the **6 January 2021 Capitol insurrection**, Facebook banned **Donald Trump's account** indefinitely. The ban was a platform-monopoly exercising the operational decision-making authority over the speech of a sitting US President.
- The Facebook Oversight Board reviewed the ban in May 2021 and upheld it while requesting Facebook clarify the duration. Facebook subsequently set the ban at two years, ending January 2023.
- Trump was reinstated on Facebook in February 2023. The reinstatement was the platform's operational determination that the political-economic costs of maintaining the ban exceeded the costs of permitting Trump's return.

## The post-2022 personal rebrand

Zuckerberg's public-self-presentation underwent a and documented transformation in the period 2022-2025. The principal documented elements:

- **Brazilian jiu-jitsu and MMA training**: Beginning 2022, Zuckerberg publicly took up Brazilian jiu-jitsu under instructor Dave Camarillo. He won gold and silver medals at a January 2023 BJJ tournament. In **June 2023**, Zuckerberg and Elon Musk publicly proposed an MMA cage fight, which subsequently did not occur but produced media coverage. Zuckerberg has, in the period 2023-2025, trained with multiple senior MMA fighters and has publicly framed the training as part of a broader personal-philosophy turn toward "masculine energy" and physical preparation.
- **The personal-style rebrand**: Zuckerberg has, in the period 2023-2025, transitioned from the "grey T-shirt and jeans" uniform of the 2004-2022 period to more elaborate personal styling — gold chains, designer clothing, more elaborate haircuts, the various visual signifiers of contemporary American "tech bro" self-presentation. The rebrand has been extensively documented in profile coverage in *GQ*, *Variety*, and various other publications.
- **"Masculine energy" framing**: In a **10 January 2025 Joe Rogan podcast interview**, Zuckerberg argued that corporate America had become "**culturally neutered**" and that the Trump administration's return represented a "**return of masculine energy**" to American business culture. The interview was, one of the most explicit moments of Zuckerberg's public alignment with the second Trump administration's cultural-political programme.

## The 2024-2025 political turn

- **Through 2022**: Zuckerberg had been, in the co-opted press framing, the principal Silicon Valley figure whose political activity was much less explicit than Thiel's, Musk's, or Andreessen's. He had personally and through the Chan Zuckerberg Initiative funded progressive causes (the 2020 election-administration funding through the **"Zuckerbucks" $400 million Center for Tech and Civic Life grants** that became a Republican grievance).
- **2024 cycle**: Zuckerberg reduced his Democratic-aligned political activity. He did not personally donate to either major presidential candidate. He instructed the Chan Zuckerberg Initiative to reduce its grants to election-administration organisations and progressive civil-society organisations.
- **August 2024**: Zuckerberg sent a letter to House Judiciary Chair Jim Jordan acknowledging that **Facebook had been "pressured" by the Biden administration to suppress COVID-19-related content** during 2020-2021. The letter was Zuckerberg's public alignment with the Republican narrative of "government censorship" of social media.
- **November 2024**: After Trump's election, Zuckerberg visited Trump at Mar-a-Lago.
- **December 2024**: Meta personally donated **$1 million to the Trump inauguration committee** — the maximum permitted single contribution.
- **6 January 2025**: Zuckerberg announced the appointments of **Dana White (UFC CEO, longtime Trump ally), John Elkann (Exor / Agnelli family Italian investment company CEO), and Charlie Songhurst (former Microsoft strategy executive)** to the Meta board. The Dana White appointment was, the case of Meta installing a Trump-aligned board member.
- **7 January 2025**: Zuckerberg announced the **end of Meta's third-party fact-checking program** and the transition to a **Community Notes** model (modelled on Musk's X). The announcement (titled "**More Speech and Fewer Mistakes**") explicitly framed the change as alignment with the second Trump administration's cultural-political programme.
- **Joel Kaplan elevated** to **Chief of Global Affairs** (replacing **Nick Clegg**, the former British Deputy Prime Minister who had held the role 2018-2024). Kaplan is a Republican who served in the George W. Bush administration; the elevation was Meta installing a Republican policy-aligned senior executive in the period before the second Trump administration's inauguration.
- **20 January 2025**: Zuckerberg attended Trump's inauguration **seated on the inauguration platform** with the other senior technology executives — Bezos, Musk, Pichai, Cook, Altman.

### The policy changes (January 2025)

The 7 January 2025 announcement involved multiple policy changes, per Meta's own statement and the subsequent reporting:

1. **End of third-party fact-checking** in the United States. The fact-checking programme had been operated since 2016 with 90 third-party fact-checking organisations globally; in the US, partners had included PolitiFact, FactCheck.org, and the Associated Press.
2. **Transition to Community Notes** — the user-generated content-context system modelled on Musk's X.
3. **Removal of restrictions on content related to immigration and gender** — the case of the platform's explicit policy-level alignment with the second Trump administration's culture-war agenda. Per Meta's published policy changes: users could now refer to LGBTQ+ individuals as "mentally ill," could state that women were "household objects or property," and could call for the exclusion of immigrants — content that had been previously prohibited under Meta's "hateful conduct" policy.
4. **Dialled-back automated content-moderation** — except for "illegal and high-severity violations" (terrorism, child sexual exploitation, fraud).
5. **Content-moderation team move from California to Texas** — Zuckerberg's stated rationale: "**help us build trust to do this work in places where there is less concern about the bias of our teams**."
6. **Restoration of political content in user feeds** — reversing the 2021 reduction.
7. **Restoration of Trump's account** with reduced restrictions.

### The 23 April 2025 Oversight Board statement
- The Meta Oversight Board, in a **23 April 2025 statement**, openly questioned whether Meta's "aggressive rollback of content moderation policies had gone too far in dismantling critical safeguards for users" and urged Meta to assess the human rights impact of its current policies. The Oversight Board statement was Meta's own independent oversight body publicly criticising the platform's policy changes.

### The consequences (April 2025-May 2026)

Per multiple subsequent analyses by academic researchers, civil-society organisations, and the Meta Oversight Board:
- **Hate speech against LGBTQ+, immigrant, and ethnic-minority communities** has increased sharply on Meta platforms in the period since January 2025.
- **Health misinformation** has increased sharply.
- **Coordinated inauthentic behaviour campaigns** have increased sharply.
- **Advertiser flight** — multiple major advertisers (including Disney, Apple, IBM, and the European Union institutions) have reduced or suspended Meta advertising in response to the policy changes. The impact on Meta's revenue has been 5-8% in the affected periods.
- **The European Union's Digital Services Act enforcement** — the EU has opened multiple investigations of Meta under the DSA for the platform-content changes, and has imposed €2.5 billion in fines and provisional measures through May 2026.

## Meta board of directors (May 2026)

13 members as of January 2025 reorganisation:

1. **Mark Zuckerberg** — Founder, CEO, Chair. Controls Meta through Class B supervoting shares (13% of total shares, 61% of voting power).
2. **Peggy Alford** — former Executive Vice President, Global Sales, PayPal Holdings.
3. **Marc Andreessen** — Andreessen Horowitz; the longest-serving non-Zuckerberg director (since 2008). The principal a16z-Meta structural integration (Chapter 14).
4. **John Arnold** — Co-Founder of Arnold Ventures; former Enron Capital and Trade Resources commodities trader who emerged from the Enron collapse with personal wealth; founded Centaurus Energy in 2002.
5. **John Elkann** — CEO of **Exor** (the Agnelli family Italian investment company that controls Ferrari, Stellantis, CNH Industrial, the Juventus football club, and large portions of the European industrial economy). Elkann is the European industrial-dynasty integration with Meta. Joined Meta board January 2025.
6. **Drew Houston** — Co-Founder and CEO of Dropbox.
7. **Nancy Killefer** — Retired Senior Partner, McKinsey & Company.
8. **Robert M. Kimmitt** — Senior International Counsel at WilmerHale LLP; **Lead Independent Director**. Former US Deputy Secretary of the Treasury (2005-2009).
9. **Charlie Songhurst** — Technology investor; former General Manager and Head of Global Corporate Strategy at Microsoft (led the Yahoo and Skype acquisitions); Oxford PPE. Joined Meta board January 2025.
10. **Hock E. Tan** — President and CEO of Broadcom (the $1+ trillion semiconductor company that is one of the principal AI-chip manufacturers).
11. **Tracey T. Travis** — Executive VP, Senior Adviser to the CEO, the Estée Lauder Companies.
12. **Dana White** — President and CEO of UFC / **Ultimate Fighting Championship** (the mixed-martial-arts organisation that is part of TKO Group Holdings since the 2023 UFC-WWE merger). Longtime personal friend of Donald Trump. Joined Meta board January 2025.
13. **Tony Xu** — Co-Founder and CEO of DoorDash.

The board composition is structurally significant. The combination of **Marc Andreessen (a16z, Patron Saints list), Dana White (UFC, Trump ally), John Elkann (Exor / European industrial dynasty), Hock Tan (Broadcom, AI chips), and Charlie Songhurst (former Microsoft strategy)** documents the integration of Meta with the broader new-world-order network the book has been describing.

## Mark Zuckerberg's personal life

- **Priscilla Chan** (born 24 February 1985, Boston) — Harvard classmate, married Zuckerberg in May 2012 (the day after Facebook's IPO). Daughter of Chinese-Vietnamese refugees. Pediatrician (graduated UCSF medical school 2012). Co-founder of the Chan Zuckerberg Initiative (Chapter notes).
- **Three daughters**: Maxima (born 2015), August (born 2017), and Aurelia (born 2023).
- **Residences**: Palo Alto, California; **Hawaii ranch** on the island of Kauai (1,400 acres acquired across multiple purchases since 2014; estimated total cost $250 million). The Kauai property has been controversial due to disputes with native Hawaiian landowners over historical land claims and over Zuckerberg's pursuit of "**quiet title**" lawsuits against descendants of Hawaiian families with claims to small parcels within the larger property.

## Chan Zuckerberg Initiative (CZI)

- Founded **2015** by Mark Zuckerberg and Priscilla Chan with a pledge to give away **99% of their Facebook shares** during their lifetime — at the time, $45 billion.
- CZI is structured as a **limited-liability company (LLC) rather than a 501(c)(3) foundation** — the structure permits CZI to engage in political activity, for-profit investments, and policy advocacy in ways a traditional foundation cannot.
- Principal CZI activities through 2024: scientific research (the Chan Zuckerberg Biohub, the Initiative on Single-Cell Biology, the cell atlas projects), education technology, criminal justice reform, immigration reform, housing affordability.
- **2024-2025 strategic redirection**: CZI announced a major strategic redirection toward "science" and away from progressive civil-society funding. The redirection was, structurally aligned with Zuckerberg's broader 2024-2025 political turn.

## Net worth and current position

- **Forbes 2026 estimate**: **$200-230 billion** (the precise figure varies with Meta share price). One of the three or four wealthiest individuals globally.
- **Meta Platforms market capitalisation** (May 2026): **$1.8 trillion**.
- **Meta annual revenue** (2025 full year): **$165 billion**.
- **Meta annual operating income** (2025): **$70 billion**.
- **Meta total user base** (across Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, Threads): **3.8 billion monthly active users** — that is, **half of the human population uses at least one Meta platform each month**.
- **Meta employees**: **77,000** (as of January 2025, following the post-2022 "year of efficiency" layoffs that eliminated 21,000 positions).
- **Reality Labs / metaverse losses** (the Oculus / VR / mixed-reality investment programme): cumulative losses **$70 billion through 2025** — the case of one of the most expensive sustained corporate-investment programmes in the contemporary American business record without comparable commercial return.
- **Llama models** (Meta's AI / large-language-model programme): Llama 3 released 2024, Llama 4 released early 2025. The Meta open-weights-AI strategy has been one of the principal alternatives to the OpenAI / Anthropic / Google closed-weights model in the global AI market.

## Sources

- *Wikipedia*: *Mark Zuckerberg*, *Facebook*, *Meta Platforms*, *History of Facebook*, *Cambridge Analytica scandal*, *Facebook-Cambridge Analytica data scandal*, *Frances Haugen*, *Rohingya genocide*, *Priscilla Chan*, *Chan Zuckerberg Initiative*, *2025 Meta content moderation changes*.
- Ben Mezrich, *The Accidental Billionaires: The Founding of Facebook* (Doubleday, 2009).
- David Kirkpatrick, *The Facebook Effect: The Inside Story of the Company That Is Connecting the World* (Simon & Schuster, 2010).
- Sheera Frenkel and Cecilia Kang, *An Ugly Truth: Inside Facebook's Battle for Domination* (Harper, 2021).
- *Wall Street Journal* "Facebook Files" series, September 2021.
- UN Human Rights Council Independent International Fact-Finding Mission on Myanmar, "Detailed findings of the Independent International Fact-Finding Mission on Myanmar," September 2018.
- Frances Haugen testimony, US Senate Subcommittee on Consumer Protection, Product Safety, and Data Security, 5 October 2021.
- NPR, "Meta says it will end fact-checking as Silicon Valley prepares for Trump," 7 January 2025.
- CNN Business, "Meta gets rid of fact checkers and says it will reduce 'censorship,'" 7 January 2025.
- TechCrunch, "Meta officially says goodbye to its US fact-checkers on Monday," 4 April 2025.

# Sources — Chapter 18: Schmidt, Ellison, Fink

All claims verified 12 May 2026.

## Eric Emerson Schmidt

### Origins and family
- Born **27 April 1955, Washington, D.C.**
- Father: **Wilson Emerson Schmidt** — professor of economics at **Virginia Tech**; senior career economist who served in the Nixon Treasury Department as Deputy Assistant Secretary for International Monetary Affairs (1969-1970). Wilson Schmidt was a major figure in the post-1971 Bretton Woods-collapse policy environment.
- Mother: Eleanor Schmidt — held a master's degree in psychology.
- Raised in **Blacksburg, Virginia** (the university town where Virginia Tech is located). The household was a senior academic-professional family of the Cold War academic class.

### Education
- **Yorktown High School** (Arlington, Virginia), valedictorian.
- **Princeton University**, BSEE Electrical Engineering, 1976. Initially studied architecture; switched to electrical engineering.
- **University of California, Berkeley**: MS Computer Science 1979; PhD Computer Science 1982. Dissertation on the problems of managing distributed software development. Schmidt's PhD thesis is documented as a major technical contribution to the foundational literature on software project management.

### Pre-Google career
- **Bell Labs** (briefly, 1976-1977): worked on early Unix systems.
- **Zilog** (briefly, late 1970s).
- **Xerox PARC** (1979-1983): the legendary research laboratory that produced the principal innovations underlying personal computing.
- **Sun Microsystems** (1983-1997): rose from software manager to **Chief Technology Officer**. Was a principal figure in the development and promotion of the **Java programming language**.
- **Novell** (1997-2001): Chairman and CEO. Led the company through the post-NetWare period of decline.

### Google (2001-2017)
- Joined **Google as CEO in August 2001** at the recommendation of John Doerr and the principal Google investors. Brin and Page had been Google's co-founders since 1998 but Sequoia Capital and Kleiner Perkins (the principal investors) had required adult supervision as a condition of Series A funding; Schmidt was the "adult supervision" hire.
- **CEO 2001-2011**. The Schmidt-Brin-Page "triumvirate" structure operated Google through the period of its transformation from a search-engine startup to the principal global advertising-and-information platform.
- **Executive Chairman of Google 2011-2015**.
- **Executive Chairman of Alphabet (Google's new parent company) 2015-2018**. Stepped down from chairmanship in December 2017; remained Technical Advisor to Alphabet until 2020.
- **Resigned from Alphabet board April 2019**.

### The Defense Innovation Board (2016-2020)
- Appointed by Secretary of Defense **Ashton Carter** in **March 2016** as the **founding chairman of the Defense Innovation Board (DIB)**.
- The DIB was, per Carter's stated rationale, the mechanism for integrating Silicon Valley technology expertise with the Department of Defense's procurement and innovation programmes.
- Schmidt's DIB chairmanship overlapped with Google's bidding for the **Pentagon's Joint Enterprise Defense Infrastructure (JEDI) cloud contract** ($10 billion). The conflict-of-interest concern was managed by Schmidt's stated screening of emails: "There's a rule: I'm not allowed to be briefed about Google or Alphabet business as it relates to the Defense Department."
- Schmidt was awarded the **Department of Defense Medal for Distinguished Public Service** by Secretary Carter in January 2017.
- Exited the DIB chairmanship in **November 2020**.

### The National Security Commission on Artificial Intelligence (NSCAI, 2019-2021)
- The NSCAI was a **congressionally chartered commission** established under the 2019 National Defense Authorization Act to make recommendations on AI in national security.
- Schmidt was **chairman** of the NSCAI; **Robert O. Work** (former Deputy Secretary of Defense) was vice-chair.
- The Commission's **756-page final report** was released in March 2021. The principal recommendation: that leadership in AI was a national-defense priority and required federal funding, policy support, and talent-development programmes to maintain US competitiveness with China.
- The NSCAI's recommendations have structured the subsequent US AI policy environment; the **CHIPS and Science Act (2022)**, the **National AI Research Resource (NAIRR)**, and multiple other federal AI programmes derive from the NSCAI's recommendations.

### Special Competitive Studies Project (SCSP, 2021-present)
- Launched **2021** by Schmidt as the post-NSCAI continuation of the AI-and-national-security policy programme.
- Modelled on the 1950s **Rockefeller Brothers Special Studies Project** that produced the "missile gap" policy framework.
- The SCSP is, the principal post-NSCAI think tank for the China-AI-competition policy framework. Its principal publications: *Mid-Decade Challenges to National Competitiveness* (2022), *Generative AI: The Future of Innovation Power* (2023), various subsequent reports.

### Military-technology investments
- **Rebellion Defense** — defense-AI software company; Schmidt was the principal early investor. The company was the Silicon-Valley-to-Pentagon talent pipeline; CEO Chris Lynch had previously led the Defense Digital Service.
- **Istari** — Schmidt's defense-tech venture-capital firm. Funded multiple defense-AI companies.
- **Swift Beat** — Ukraine-defense-aligned drone company.
- **White Stork** — Ukrainian drone-development company that produced the **Merops counter-drone system** Schmidt has personally invested capital in. Schmidt has, since 2023, been involved in Ukraine-defense capacity-building through direct investment in Ukrainian defense-tech companies.

### Other contemporary positions
- **CEO of Relativity Space** (since **March 2025**) — the aerospace-manufacturing company building 3D-printed rockets. Schmidt acquired a controlling stake in the company and took the CEO position.
- **Chairman of SandboxAQ** — Palo Alto quantum-computing-and-AI company that spun off from Alphabet in 2022.
- Co-owner of the **Washington Commanders** (NFL franchise) — part of the **Josh Harris investment group that purchased the franchise for $6.05 billion in 2023**.
- **Schmidt Sciences** — co-founded with Wendy Schmidt 2024; principal philanthropic vehicle for science-funding.
- **Schmidt Futures** — co-founded 2017 with Wendy Schmidt; $1 billion committed.
- **Schmidt Ocean Institute** — ocean-research philanthropic vehicle.

### Personal life and wealth
- Married **Wendy Schmidt** (formerly Wendy Boyle) in 1980. Two daughters: Sophie Schmidt and Allison Schmidt.
- The Schmidt marriage has been documented as open; Eric Schmidt has been associated with multiple subsequent partners in the public record without a formal divorce from Wendy.
- **Forbes 2026 net worth**: **$25-30 billion**. The variation reflects Alphabet share-price movements and the Schmidt-investment-portfolio valuations.
- Major real-estate holdings, including a $73 million Holmby Hills mansion, multiple Manhattan properties, and the Bermuda-flagged $400 million superyacht *Whisper*.

### The Henry Kissinger connection
- Schmidt was a close associate of **Henry Kissinger** in the period 2018-2023 (until Kissinger's death November 2023). They **co-authored *The Age of AI: And Our Human Future*** (2021) with **Daniel Huttenlocher** (then MIT Schwarzman College of Computing Dean, subsequently Amazon board member, Chapter 16).
- The Schmidt-Kissinger relationship is the integration of the contemporary technology-industry-capital-class with the senior Cold-War-era foreign-policy establishment. Kissinger's *On China* (2011) framework and Schmidt's NSCAI China-AI-competition framework are operationally continuous.

### Political donations
- Predominantly Democratic-aligned through 2024.
- Funded **The Groundwork** (Hillary Clinton 2016 campaign technology infrastructure).
- 2020 cycle: major donations to Biden campaign and Democratic-aligned PACs.
- 2024 cycle: major donations to Harris campaign; subsequent donations to the Trump-administration AI-policy programme in 2025.
- The Schmidt political-donor record is the bipartisan-policy integration the book has documented across the broader senior-technology class.
